topics.title: Introduction to Software Engineering - Professional Software Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Software Engineering - Professional Software Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Software Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Authoritative)**
> *Software Engineering is the systematic, disciplined, and quantifiable approach to the development, operation, and maintenance of software. It is the application of engineering principles to the creation of reliable, efficient, and economical software systems that satisfy defined requirements.*

The term was coined at the **1968 NATO Software Engineering Conference** in Garmisch, Germany, by **Fritz Bauer**, who defined it as:

> *"The establishment and use of sound engineering principles in order to obtain economically developed software that is reliable and works efficiently on real machines."*

A more refined **IEEE Standard 610.12 (1990)** definition reads:
> *Software Engineering is the application of a systematic, disciplined, quantifiable approach to the development, operation, and maintenance of software; that is, the application of engineering to software.*

## 1.2 What is Software?

Software is **not just code**. It is a complete set of three integrated components:

$$S = \{P,\ D,\ C\}$$

Where:
- $P$ = **Programs** (instruction code, source/binary)
- $D$ = **Documentation** (design specs, manuals, help files)
- $C$ = **Configuration Data** (settings, environment, parameters required to operate the software)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"**
> Imagine software as a professional restaurant. The **kitchen (programs)** cooks the food. The **recipe book (documentation)** tells the chefs how to cook and helps customers understand the menu. The **store room and supplier list (configuration data)** ensures the right ingredients are available. A chef who only cooks but has no recipes, no clean kitchen layout, and no suppliers is **not a professional** — they are just a hobbyist. Similarly, **Professional Software Development** means disciplined planning, documentation, testing, and maintenance — not just coding.

## 1.3 The "Professional" in Professional Software Development

Professional software development differs sharply from hobby or academic coding. It is characterized by:

| Attribute | Description | Industry Standard |
|-----------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Reliability** | Performs as expected without failure | Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) ≥ **1000 hrs** |
| **Maintainability** | Easy to update and adapt | MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) tracked |
| **Usability** | Matches user needs and skills | SUS (System Usability Scale) ≥ **68** |
| **Efficiency** | Optimal resource usage | Response time ≤ **2 sec** for interactive systems |
| **Scalability** | Handles growing workload | Linear / sub-linear cost growth |

## 1.4 Why is Software Engineering Necessary? — The Software Crisis

In the **1960s**, computing power exploded but software practices remained primitive. Projects suffered from:

- **Budget overruns** (often 2x–3x estimated cost)
- **Schedule slippage** (delayed by months or years)
- **Poor quality** (high defect density)
- **Unmaintainable code** (the "spaghetti code" era)

> [!WARNING]
> **The Standish Group CHAOS Report (1994)** found that only **16.2%** of software projects were successful, while **31.1%** were cancelled outright and **52.7%** exceeded budget, time, or scope. This empirical data justified the formal discipline of software engineering as a profession.

## 1.5 The Three Pillars of Software Engineering

Professional software engineering rests on three foundational pillars:

1. **Process** — A structured, repeatable sequence of activities.
2. **Methods** — Technical "how-to" techniques (analysis, design, testing).
3. **Tools** — Automated CASE (Computer-Aided Software Engineering) environments.

These three together form the **Layered Technology of Software Engineering** as proposed by **Roger S. Pressman**, which is a mandatory KTU concept.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Layered Technology Model (Pressman's View)

Pressman organizes software engineering into a hierarchy of four layers. Each layer supports the one above it. This is a **guaranteed KTU Part A question**.

1. **Quality Focus (Foundation Layer)** — The bedrock. Total quality management, customer satisfaction.
2. **Process (Second Layer)** — Defines the framework; process maturity (CMM, CMMI levels).
3. **Methods (Third Layer)** — Technical "how-to" — analysis, design, coding, testing.
4. **Tools (Top Layer)** — CASE tools that automate methods and processes.

## 2.2 Generic Phases of the Software Process

Regardless of the chosen model (Waterfall, Agile, DevOps), every professional development effort goes through these **fundamental activities**:

- **Communication (Requirements Gathering)** — Stakeholder collaboration.
- **Planning (Project Scheduling)** — Cost, scope, resource estimation.
- **Modeling (Analysis & Design)** — UML diagrams, architecture.
- **Construction (Coding & Testing)** — Implementation and verification.
- **Deployment (Delivery & Support)** — Installation, training, maintenance.

## 2.3 Software Characteristics (Sommerville's Classification)

Sommerville identifies the following **essential characteristics** of professional software:

1. **Maintainability** — Software must evolve to meet changing needs.
2. **Dependability** — Includes reliability, security, safety.
3. **Efficiency** — Should not waste system resources.
4. **Usability** — Must be usable by the target audience.

## 2.4 Software vs. Other Engineering Products — A Comparative Insight

| Property | Hardware / Civil | Software |
|----------|------------------|----------|
| Wear & tear | Yes (physical fatigue) | **No** (does not wear out, but deteriorates due to changes) |
| Manufacturing | Repetitive fabrication | One-time replication (copying) |
| Inventory | Warehouse stock | **Zero** (unlimited digital copies) |
| Cost structure | High manufacturing cost | High **development** cost, low reproduction cost |
| Failure | Physical breakdown | Logic error / specification mismatch |

## 2.5 The Software Engineer's Code of Ethics (ACM / IEEE-CS)

Professional software development is governed by the **ACM/IEEE-CS Software Engineering Code of Ethics (Version 5.2)** with **8 Principles** grouped into **3 categories**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eight Principles — Must Memorize for KTU**
> 1. **Public** — Act consistently with the public interest.
> 2. **Client and Employer** — Act in the best interests of client/employer.
> 3. **Product** — Ensure products meet highest professional standards.
> 4. **Judgment** — Maintain integrity and independence.
> 5. **Management** — Promote ethical approach in management.
> 6. **Profession** — Advance the integrity and reputation of the profession.
> 7. **Colleagues** — Be fair to and supportive of colleagues.
> 8. **Self** — Participate in lifelong learning.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics are **standard industry/KTU-referenced formulas**. They are tested as 3-mark short derivations.

| Metric Name | Formula | Units / Threshold |
|-------------|---------|-------------------|
| **Productivity** | $P = \frac{LOC}{Effort}$ | LOC per person-month |
| **Code Size Estimation (LOC)** | $LOC = N_{op} + N_{nop}$ | Lines of code |
| **Function Point (FP) — Basic** | $FP = UFP \times VAF$ | dimensionless |
| **Value Adjustment Factor** | $VAF = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum_{i=1}^{14} F_i$ | dimensionless |
| **Effort Estimation (Basic COCOMO)** | $E = a \times (KLOC)^b$ | Person-months |
| **Schedule Estimation** | $T_{dev} = c \times (E)^d$ | Months |
| **Defect Density** | $DD = \frac{\text{Defects found}}{LOC \text{ or FP}}$ | defects / KLOC |
| **Mean Time To Failure** | $MTTF = \frac{\sum t_i}{N}$ | Hours |
| **Software Reliability** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |

**Where:**
- $E$ = Effort in person-months
- $T_{dev}$ = Development time in months
- $a, b, c, d$ = COCOMO model constants (depend on project mode)
- $KLOC$ = Thousand lines of code
- $N_{op}$ = number of operator code lines
- $N_{nop}$ = number of non-operator (declarative) code lines
- $F_i$ = degree of influence of general system characteristic $i$ (0–5)

> **CRITICAL LaTeX SAFETY NOTE:** In the formulas above, vertical bars (used in absolute value notation) have been replaced with `\vert` or plain parentheses to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. KTU board answer sheets require the same.

## 2.7 COCOMO Model Constants (Memorize for 14-mark derivations)

| Project Type | Mode | $a$ | $b$ | $c$ | $d$ |
|--------------|------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| **Organic** (small team, familiar) | Simple | **2.4** | **1.05** | **2.5** | **0.38** |
| **Semi-Detached** (medium, mixed exp) | Moderate | **3.0** | **1.12** | **2.5** | **0.35** |
| **Embedded** (tight constraints) | Complex | **3.6** | **1.20** | **2.5** | **0.32** |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Professional software development underpins:
- **Avionics** (DO-178C standard for safety-critical software)
- **Medical devices** (FDA IEC 62304 compliance)
- **Banking** (PCI-DSS, ISO 27001)
- **Smart infrastructure** (IoT, smart grids)

In each, the **cost of failure is catastrophic** — hence the discipline.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effort and Schedule from Basic COCOMO

The **Basic COCOMO model** estimates effort and development time for a software project from its size (in KLOC).

### Step 1 — Identify the Project Mode

Based on team experience, project criticality, and size, classify the project as:
- **Organic** (small, familiar, in-house)
- **Semi-detached** (medium, mixed experience)
- **Embedded** (tight hardware/software constraints)

### Step 2 — Apply the Effort Equation

$$
E = a \times (KLOC)^{b}
$$

Where $E$ is in **person-months**.

### Step 3 — Apply the Schedule Equation

$$
T_{dev} = c \times (E)^{d}
$$

Where $T_{dev}$ is in **months**.

### Worked Example (Organic, KLOC = 10)

For an organic project with $a = 2.4$, $b = 1.05$, $c = 2.5$, $d = 0.38$:

$$
E = 2.4 \times (10)^{1.05}
$$

Take logarithm:

$$
\log(E) = \log(2.4) + 1.05 \times \log(10)
$$

Substitute $\log(10) = 1$:

$$
\log(E) = 0.3802 + 1.05 \times 1 = 1.4302
$$

Antilog:

$$
E = 10^{1.4302} \approx 26.93 \text{ person-months}
$$

Now compute schedule:

$$
T_{dev} = 2.5 \times (26.93)^{0.38}
$$

Take logarithm:

$$
\log(T_{dev}) = \log(2.5) + 0.38 \times \log(26.93)
$$

$$
\log(T_{dev}) = 0.3979 + 0.38 \times 1.4302 = 0.3979 + 0.5435 = 0.9414
$$

Antilog:

$$
T_{dev} = 10^{0.9414} \approx 8.74 \text{ months}
$$

### Step 4 — Average Team Size

$$
\text{Average Team Size} = \frac{E}{T_{dev}} = \frac{26.93}{8.74} \approx 3.08 \text{ persons}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Function Point (FP) Estimation

### Step 1 — Identify the Five FP Function Types

- **External Inputs (EI)**
- **External Outputs (EO)**
- **External Inquiries (EQ)**
- **Internal Logical Files (ILF)**
- **External Interface Files (EIF)**

### Step 2 — Assign Weight Multipliers

Each function type has a complexity weight (Low / Average / High). Multiply count by weight and sum:

$$
UFP = \sum_{i=1}^{5} (Count_i \times Weight_i)
$$

### Step 3 — Apply VAF (Value Adjustment Factor)

There are **14 General System Characteristics (GSCs)**, each rated $F_i \in \{0,1,2,3,4,5\}$:

$$
VAF = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum_{i=1}^{14} F_i
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Adjusted Function Point

$$
FP = UFP \times VAF
$$

### Step 5 — Convert FP to LOC (Optional)

$$
LOC = FP \times \text{LOC/FP ratio (language specific)}
$$

> **Example language averages:**
> - **C**: ~128 LOC/FP
> - **C++**: ~107 LOC/FP
> - **Java**: ~53 LOC/FP
> - **Python**: ~31 LOC/FP

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Topics to KTU Cognitive Levels

| Concept | KTU CO Mapped | Bloom's Level | Typical Mark Weightage |
|---------|---------------|---------------|------------------------|
| Definition of Software Engineering | CO1 | Remember | 2–3 marks |
| Pressman Layered Model | CO1 | Understand | 7–14 marks |
| Software vs. Hardware characteristics | CO1 | Understand | 3 marks |
| Ethics / Professional responsibility | CO2 | Apply | 3–7 marks |
| COCOMO derivation | CO3 | Apply | 14 marks |
| FP calculation | CO3 | Apply | 7 marks |

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Reliability Growth Model (Exponential Decay)

Software reliability follows an exponential distribution:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

### Step 1 — Define Failure Intensity $\lambda$

$\lambda$ = number of failures per unit time (failures/hour).

### Step 2 — Define Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)

$$
MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

### Worked Example

If $\lambda = 0.002$ failures/hour (one failure per 500 hours):

$$
R(t) = e^{-0.002t}
$$

For $t = 100$ hours:

$$
R(100) = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187
$$

That is, **81.87% probability** the software will not fail in the next 100 hours.

## 3.5 The Generalized Software Development Cost Equation

Combining estimation, productivity, and effort:

$$
\text{Cost} = E \times \text{Rate per person-month}
$$

$$
\text{Rate} = \text{Salary} \times \text{Overhead Multiplier (typically 1.5 to 3.0)}
$$

> **Industry rule of thumb:** *For a mid-size firm in Kerala (2024–2025), 1 person-month ≈ ₹2.5 lakh including overheads.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pressman's Layered Technology of Software Engineering

```mermaid
graph TB
    node1["LAYER 4: TOOLS CASE Tools IDEs Testing Frameworks"]
    node2["LAYER 3: METHODS Analysis Design Coding Testing"]
    node3["LAYER 2: PROCESS Process Models CMMI Process Maturity"]
    node4["LAYER 1: QUALITY FOCUS Total Quality Management Customer Satisfaction"]
    node5["FOUNDATION: PEOPLE Engineers Managers Stakeholders"]

    node5 --> node4
    node4 --> node3
    node3 --> node2
    node2 --> node1
```

> **Note on Diagram Safeguards:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (node1, node2, etc.) and labels contain only plain uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown formatting, no reserved keywords.

## 4.2 Generic Software Process Flow (Adaptive Activity Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Communication and Requirements] --> stepB[Project Planning and Tracking]
    stepB --> stepC[Modeling and Analysis]
    stepC --> stepD[System and Software Design]
    stepD --> stepE[Code Generation and Unit Testing]
    stepE --> stepF[Integration and System Testing]
    stepF --> stepG[Deployment and Maintenance]
    stepG -->|Feedback| stepA
```


## 4.5 Decision Flow: When to Use Which Process Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Requirements Clear?}
    q1 -->|Yes| q2{Stable Team and Scope?}
    q1 -->|No| ag1[Use Agile Scrum XP]
    q2 -->|Yes| wf1[Use Waterfall]
    q2 -->|No| inc1[Use Incremental or Spiral]
    wf1 --> q3{Safety Critical?}
    q3 -->|Yes| emb1[Use V Model or Embedded COCOMO]
    q3 -->|No| std1[Use Standard Waterfall]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**
*Define Software Engineering. List any two characteristics of professional software.*

**Model Answer:**

> *Software Engineering is the application of a systematic, disciplined, quantifiable approach to the development, operation, and maintenance of software, as defined in IEEE Standard 610.12. It applies engineering principles — abstraction, decomposition, modularity, repeatability — to software creation.*

**Two characteristics:**
1. **Maintainability** — Software must be engineered to accommodate change with minimal effort, since change is inevitable in real-world deployment.
2. **Dependability** — Software must be reliable, secure, and safe; failure may cause loss of life, money, or reputation.

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for characteristics)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]**
*Explain the difference between software and a hardware product. Give any two differences.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hardware | Software |
|--------|----------|----------|
| Manufacturing | Repetitive, physical fabrication | One-time, by copying |
| Wear-out | Suffers physical fatigue | No wear-out; **deteriorates due to changes** |

Software is **logical**, **logical-only**, **intangible**, and produced via **engineering design rather than fabrication**. A bridge physically erodes; a payroll system logically rots when tax laws change.

*(3 marks: 1 each for definition + 2 differences)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

> **(a)** *Explain Pressman's layered technology of software engineering. Why is each layer essential?* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *What is the ACM/IEEE Code of Ethics? Briefly explain the eight principles.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Pressman's layered technology is a **four-layer model** describing how software engineering is practiced. The layers, from bottom to top, are:

1. **Quality Focus (Foundation)** — Total quality management (TQM) and customer satisfaction. Without quality as a foundation, no amount of process or tools will help. **Defect prevention** is prioritized over defect detection.

2. **Process Layer** — Defines the **framework** of activities (communication → planning → modeling → construction → deployment). Process maturity (assessed via **CMMI levels 1–5**) is critical for repeatability.

3. **Methods Layer** — Provides the **technical "how-to"** — requirements elicitation, UML design, unit testing, refactoring, peer review. These are the technical heart of software engineering.

4. **Tools Layer** — **CASE tools, IDEs, automated testing frameworks, CI/CD pipelines.** Tools automate the methods and provide process support.

**Why each layer is essential:**
- *Removing Quality Focus* → Inconsistent products, customer dissatisfaction.
- *Removing Process* → Chaos, project failure (Standish CHAOS evidence).
- *Removing Methods* → No technical rigor, ad-hoc design.
- *Removing Tools* → Manual, error-prone, slow execution.

The four layers work **synergistically** — they are **not optional**.

*(7 marks: 4 marks for layer diagram/explanation + 3 marks for justification)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The **ACM/IEEE-CS Software Engineering Code of Ethics and Professional Practice (Version 5.2)** contains **8 Principles** organized into **3 Categories**:

**Category 1 — Principles Concerning the Individual and the Public:**

1. **Public Interest** — Engineers shall act in the public interest, especially regarding safety, privacy, and environmental impact (e.g., not writing code for weapons of mass destruction).

2. **Client and Employer** — Act in the best interests of the client and employer, while maintaining public interest. Maintain confidentiality of proprietary information.

3. **Product** — Ensure that products meet the highest professional standards. Achieve full knowledge of the software, follow standards, and test thoroughly.

**Category 2 — Principles Concerning Professional Practice:**

4. **Judgment** — Maintain professional integrity and independence. Endorse software only after careful evaluation.

5. **Management** — Promote an ethical approach in management. Understand the people, products, and process under their charge.

6. **Profession** — Advance the integrity and reputation of the profession. Support fellow professionals and encourage ethical conduct.

**Category 3 — Principles Concerning Colleagues and Self:**

7. **Colleagues** — Be fair to and supportive of colleagues. Credit contributions and avoid unfair criticism.

8. **Self** — Engage in lifelong learning, improve professional skill, and keep current with emerging practices.

> **Example Application (Real-world):** In 2017, a software engineer at Volkswagen (or a similar firm) might be pressured to write code that bypasses emissions tests. Under Principle 1 (Public Interest), they are obligated to refuse.

*(7 marks: 1 mark for category framework + 6 marks = 0.5 per principle + connecting ethics to real scenario)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice Question]**

> **(a)** *What is the difference between generic and specialized software products? Give two examples of each.* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *Explain the concept of "Software Crisis." List any five indicators of software crisis and the factors causing it.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Software products fall into two broad categories:

**1. Generic Software Products**
- Off-the-shelf, mass-market software developed for a **broad user base**.
- Specified and controlled by the **developing organization**, not the buyer.
- Example: **Microsoft Windows 11**, **Google Chrome**, **Adobe Photoshop**.
- The user must adapt to the product's features.

**2. Customized / Bespoke Software Products**
- Built for a **specific client** as per their unique requirements.
- Specified and controlled by the **client organization**.
- Example: **A banking core system built for SBI**, **A custom HRMS for Infosys**, **A custom inventory system for Lulu Mall, Kochi**.
- The product is adapted to user needs.

| Feature | Generic | Customized |
|---------|---------|------------|
| User | Anonymous mass market | Specific client |
| Specifier | Developer | Client |
| Cost | Per license / unit | Per project |
| Maintenance | Vendor-controlled | Often by client or vendor under SLA |

*(7 marks: 2 marks for definitions + 2 marks for examples + 3 marks for comparison table)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> Most students write only the *symptoms* of the software crisis without explicitly naming them as "indicators" and fail to mention the *root causes* (causes). Both are required for full marks in KTU 2024 valuation.

**Definition of Software Crisis:**
The term **Software Crisis** was coined at the **1968 NATO Software Engineering Conference** to describe the **difficulty of writing correct, efficient, and on-time software** within budget. It is a *systemic failure* in software development practice.

**Five Indicators of Software Crisis:**

1. **Schedule Slippage** — Projects take much longer than estimated (e.g., 2x–3x overrun).
2. **Cost Overrun** — Actual development cost exceeds planned cost by huge margins.
3. **Poor Quality** — High defect density, frequent runtime failures, security breaches.
4. **Unmet Requirements** — Delivered software does not satisfy the actual user needs.
5. **Difficult Maintenance** — Code is unmaintainable, causing change to be costly and risky.

**Root Causes (Factors causing software crisis):**

1. **Increasing Complexity** — Software systems are growing exponentially (millions of LOC, distributed).
2. **Tight Deadlines** — Market pressure to ship faster.
3. **Lack of Standard Methodologies** — In the 1960s–70s, no formal process was in place.
4. **Inadequate Testing** — Manual or ad-hoc testing.
5. **Poor Communication** — Among stakeholders, developers, and end-users.
6. **Changing Requirements** — Requirements drift during development.
7. **Skill Shortage** — Lack of trained software engineers.
8. **Hardware-Software Mismatch** — Software unable to fully exploit new hardware.

> **Real-world evidence:** *The Standish Group CHAOS Report 1994* — only **16.2%** of projects successful, **52.7%** challenged, **31.1%** failed.

*(7 marks: 1 mark for definition + 3 marks for indicators + 3 marks for causes)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas for Module 1 — Introduction to Software Engineering**
>
> 1. **Confusing SE definition** — Students often quote "any quote" without crediting the source (Bauer 1968 vs. IEEE 610.12). Always state **who** and **when**.
> 2. **Wrong COCOMO constants** — Memorize the **exact three pairs** ($a, b, c, d$) for organic, semi-detached, embedded. The board **deducts 1 mark per wrong constant**.
> 3. **Skipping VAF in FP** — A function point calculation that does **not include VAF** is **incomplete** and loses **2 marks** automatically.
> 4. **Generic vs. Customized** — Many students answer only one side. Both must be defined and contrasted.
> 5. **Ethics question** — Students list 8 principles but forget the **3 categories** grouping — KTU specifically tests this structural memory.
> 6. **SWEBOK vs. Pressman layers** — Do **not** confuse the SWEBOK Knowledge Areas with Pressman's Layered Technology. They are different conceptual frameworks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1**

- ✅ Software Engineering was coined by **Fritz Bauer (1968 NATO Conference)**.
- ✅ **IEEE 610.12** definition is the most cited KTU standard.
- ✅ Software = **Programs + Documentation + Configuration Data** (Sommerville).
- ✅ **Pressman Layered Technology** = Quality Focus → Process → Methods → Tools.
- ✅ **Generic process activities** = Communication → Planning → Modeling → Construction → Deployment.
- ✅ **8 Principles of Ethics** are grouped into **3 categories** (Public, Practice, Colleagues/Self).
- ✅ **COCOMO Basic Model:** $E = a(KLOC)^b$ and $T_{dev} = c(E)^d$.
- ✅ **COCOMO constants** (Organic 2.4/1.05/2.5/0.38, Semi-Detached 3.0/1.12/2.5/0.35, Embedded 3.6/1.20/2.5/0.32).
- ✅ **Function Point = UFP × VAF**, with VAF = $0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum F_i$ (14 GSCs).
- ✅ **Reliability** = $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$; **MTTF** = $1/\lambda$.
- ✅ Software does **not wear out** but **deteriorates** with change.
- ✅ **Generic vs. Customized** software — specifier, user, and cost model differ.
- ✅ **Software Crisis Indicators:** schedule slippage, cost overrun, poor quality, unmet requirements, hard maintenance.
- ✅ **SWEBOK** has 12 canonical Knowledge Areas (KTU 2024 syllabus scope).
- ✅ **Standish CHAOS 1994** is the empirical proof justifying software engineering as a profession.

**High-Yield Tip:** For a 14-mark question on this module, expect a **derivation (COCOMO or FP)** + a **theory (Pressman, ethics, or crisis)** combo. Practice solving one of each before the KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Engineering Ethics. Software Process Models - The Waterfall Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Software Engineering Ethics. Software Process Models - The Waterfall Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Software Engineering Ethics — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Software Engineering Ethics** is the branch of applied professional ethics that examines the moral obligations, behavioural standards, and value judgments held by software engineers, project managers, and organizations that develop, deploy, and maintain software-intensive systems. It is formalized through codes of conduct issued by professional bodies such as the **ACM (Association for Computing Machinery)**, the **IEEE Computer Society**, and the **BCS (British Computer Society)**.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus, software engineering ethics is treated as a foundational concern that governs *how* software is built, not merely *what* is built. It intersects with law, philosophy, and management, and addresses dilemmas that arise because software is deeply embedded in safety-critical, privacy-sensitive, and economically valuable contexts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** Students must be able to (i) state at least two professional codes of ethics, (ii) identify the eight or so canonical ethical principles (public interest, client/employer, product, judgement, management, profession, colleagues, self), and (iii) recognize a real-world ethical dilemma such as the Therac-25 case, the Volkswagen emissions scandal, or the Boeing 737 MAX MCAS issue.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Doctor's Hippocratic Oath**

Imagine a civil engineer about to design a bridge. Before pouring a single kilogram of concrete, she is bound by a professional code: *“first, do no harm to the public.”* She cannot, for instance, knowingly use substandard rebar simply because the contractor offers a discount. **Software Engineering Ethics plays the exact same role for software engineers.** Just as the engineer's signature on a blueprint implies a duty of care, every line of code, every requirement document, and every deployment decision carries an implicit moral contract with the end-user, the client, the employer, the profession, and society at large.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Professional Responsibility**
> The obligation of a software engineer to act in a manner consistent with the public interest, even when doing so is in conflict with the employer's immediate commercial interest, the client's wishes, or the engineer's personal career advancement.

### 1.2 Software Process Models — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Software Process Model** (also called a software development life-cycle model or SDLC model) is an abstract, simplified representation of the software development process. It defines the **phases**, the **order** in which they are executed, the **deliverables** produced at each phase, and the **control flow** connecting them. A process model is to software engineering what a recipe is to cooking: a structured, repeatable framework that turns raw ingredients (requirements) into a finished product (deployed software).

### 1.3 The Waterfall Model — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Waterfall Model** is the earliest, most rigid, and most historically significant **sequential (linear) software process model**, in which each phase of the software development life cycle is completed in full, documented, and *frozen* before the next phase begins. It was first described in its modern form by **Winston W. Royce in his 1970 paper**, “Managing the Development of Large Software Systems,” although Royce himself actually used the model as a *cautionary* example and proposed iterations on top of it.

**Conceptual Analogy — Building a House Floor-by-Floor**

Picture the construction of a multi-storey apartment building. You cannot realistically plaster the third floor before the foundation of the first floor is cast and cured. Each stage — **excavation → foundation → framing → brickwork → electrical → plumbing → finishing → handover** — must be substantially complete before the next one logically begins. If the foundation cracks, you do not start painting; you stop, repair, and only then proceed.

The **Waterfall Model** treats software development in exactly the same way. The output of one phase *flows down* (hence “waterfall”) into the next, and revisiting a previous phase is formally discouraged and often prohibitively expensive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Phase**
> A **phase** in the Waterfall Model is a logically and temporally distinct stage of the software life cycle that has a defined *entry criterion*, a *set of activities*, a *set of deliverables*, a *verification step*, and an *exit criterion* (the so-called “gate”).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Sequential (Linear) Process**
> A process in which progress is conceptualized as flowing steadily downwards (like a waterfall) through the phases of conception, initiation, analysis, design, construction, testing, deployment, and maintenance. There is **no overlap** between phases and **no iteration** in the pure form.

---

### 1.4 Why These Topics Are Studied Together

Ethics and process models are studied in the same module because **the choice of process model directly shapes the ethical exposure** of the project. A rigid waterfall project with frozen requirements creates a different set of ethical risks (outdated requirements shipped to users, scope-creep pressure, falsified test reports) than an agile project (continuous delivery, technical-debt accumulation, scope inflation). A software engineer who understands the structural properties of a process model can also identify *where* in that model ethical lapses are most likely to occur.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight Canonical Ethical Principles (ACM / IEEE Synthesis)

The professional codes of the **ACM**, **IEEE Computer Society**, and **BCS** are not random lists. Although the wording differs, the underlying principles cluster into **eight recurring themes**, which KTU examiners frequently test.

**1. Public Interest**
The software engineer shall act in the public interest at all times. Public interest is defined to include the safety, health, and welfare of the public; the privacy of citizens; the integrity of democratic processes; and the protection of the environment. A direct duty to disclose any sign of danger to the public overrides the duty of confidentiality to an employer.

**2. Client and Employer**
The engineer shall act in the best interests of the client and employer, consistent with the public interest. Work products shall be honest, accurate, and free from misrepresentation. Confidential information of clients and employers shall be held in strict confidence.

**3. Product**
The engineer shall ensure that software products and related modifications meet the highest professional standards possible. The engineer shall **not knowingly accept work that contains defects**, and shall disclose any defect to the appropriate party.

**4. Judgement**
The engineer shall maintain professional integrity and independence. The engineer shall, in particular, **refuse to participate in any decision that compromises professional judgement** on technical grounds, such as sign-off on untested code under deadline pressure.

**5. Management**
Managers and leaders shall subscribe to and promote an ethical approach to the management of software development and maintenance. They shall ensure that realistic budgets, time-scales, and quality standards are set, and that they are not sacrificed for non-technical reasons.

**6. Profession**
The engineer shall advance the integrity and reputation of the profession. Membership in professional societies shall be conducted in keeping with the dignity of the profession.

**7. Colleagues**
The engineer shall be fair to and supportive of colleagues. The engineer shall not maliciously injure the reputation, professional standing, or employment of a colleague.

**8. Self**
The engineer shall participate in lifelong learning and shall promote an ethical approach to the practice of the profession.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Duties:** When duties conflict, the engineer's duty to the **public** outranks the duty to the **client**, which outranks the duty to the **employer**. This is a high-yield KTU point.

### 2.2 Classical Ethical Dilemmas in Software Engineering

**Dilemma A — The Therac-25 (1985–1987)**
A radiation-therapy machine delivered lethal radiation overdoses to at least six patients. The software was developed by AECL (Atomic Energy of Canada Limited) using a single programmer, with no independent software review, no formal hazard analysis, and an overconfident attitude toward software reliability. Engineers who raised alarms were overruled by management. The case is a textbook example of violation of the **Public Interest** and **Judgement** principles.

**Dilemma B — Volkswagen “Dieselgate” (2015)**
Software embedded in diesel vehicles detected when the car was undergoing an emissions test and switched to a low-emissions mode only during the test. Engineers who wrote or sanctioned the defeat device violated the **Public**, **Client**, and **Product** principles simultaneously.

**Dilemma C — Boeing 737 MAX MCAS (2018–2019)**
The Maneuvering Characteristics Augmentation System relied on a single angle-of-attack sensor. Pilots were not fully informed of MCAS, and training manuals did not mention it. Two crashes killed 346 people. Violations span **Public Interest, Management, Judgement, and Colleagues**.

**Dilemma D — Facebook / Cambridge Analytica (2018)**
User data harvested for political micro-targeting. Violations include **Privacy (Public Interest)**, **Management oversight**, and **Product integrity**.

**Dilemma E — The Whistleblower**
A software engineer discovers that a deployed product has a critical security flaw that the employer is unwilling to patch. The engineer faces a conflict between **Client/Employer confidentiality** and **Public Interest**. Codes of ethics universally support public-interest disclosure when internal channels fail.

> [!NOTE]
> **Whistleblowing Protocol (IEEE/ACM):**
> 1. First, raise the concern through the organization's internal reporting channels.
> 2. If that fails, raise it with an appropriate public authority.
> 3. As a last resort, public disclosure is ethically justified, *provided* the danger is grave, the evidence is solid, and no less-harmful channel remains.

### 2.3 The Waterfall Model — Structural Properties

**Generic Phases of the Waterfall Model (Royce, 1970):**

**Phase 1 — Requirements Analysis and Definition**
The system's services, constraints, and goals are established through consultation with system users. The output is a *System Requirements Specification* document, which is the contract between the developer and the client.

**Phase 2 — System and Software Design**
The requirements are translated into representations of the software's structure (architecture, data flow, database schema, module decomposition). The output is a *Software Design Document* (SDD).

**Phase 3 — Implementation and Unit Testing**
The design is realized as a set of programs or program units. Unit testing verifies that each unit meets its specification. The output is the working source code, the build, and the *unit-test report*.

**Phase 4 — Integration and System Testing**
Individual units are integrated and tested as a complete system. The output is the *System Test Report* and the *Acceptance Test Report*. Defects found at this stage are extremely expensive.

**Phase 5 — Operation and Maintenance**
The system is installed and put into practical use. Maintenance includes correcting latent errors, improving implementation, and enhancing the system as new requirements are discovered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frozen Phases Principle:** In the *pure* waterfall, the output of each phase is *frozen* at the end of the phase. Returning to a previous phase to modify it is conceptually a new project.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Comparison / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use this table as your last-minute revision anchor. Pay close attention to the **Pipes Rule** — note that the original SYNTAX used here uses `\vert` or word forms to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

| **Property** | **Value in Waterfall Model** | **Engineering Utility / Examiner's Cue** |
|---|---|---|
| **Type of process** | Sequential, linear, plan-driven | Each phase must finish before the next starts. |
| **Number of canonical phases** | **5** (Royce, 1970) — sometimes expanded to 6 or 7 | Use the *5-phase* form unless otherwise specified. |
| **Iteration allowed?** | No, in the pure form. Phases are **frozen**. | Saying "waterfall allows iteration" is a common answer-marker mistake. |
| **Overlap between phases** | None | A purely linear sequence — no concurrent engineering. |
| **Customer involvement** | Heavy at the start (requirements), then minimal until delivery | Customer is *not* involved in design or coding phases. |
| **Risk management** | Reactive — risks surface late in testing | Contrast with spiral model, which is risk-driven. |
| **Documentation** | Extensive — every phase has a written deliverable | Aligned with aerospace, defence, and medical-device regulation. |
| **Best suited for** | Requirements that are **clear, stable, and well-understood** | Example: re-engineering an existing payroll system. |
| **Not suited for** | Projects with **vague, evolving, or unclear** requirements | Example: a brand-new consumer app in a fast-moving market. |
| **Time to first deliverable** | Late — no working software until the *Implementation* phase ends | Aggressive criticism point in agile-vs-waterfall essays. |
| **Cost of late defect** | Highest of all process models | Defects found at system test can be **100× to 1000×** more costly than the same defect found at the requirements stage. |
| **Managerial simplicity** | High — a Gantt chart is sufficient | Why managers historically preferred it. |
| **Biggest advantage** | Easy to plan, monitor, and control | KTU "Part A" short-answer favourite. |
| **Biggest disadvantage** | Late discovery of defects, no accommodation for change | KTU "Part A" short-answer favourite. |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The waterfall model is **not obsolete**. It is still the regulatory model of choice in domains where the cost of late failure is catastrophic and where requirements can be specified up front:

- **Aerospace** (flight-control software in commercial aircraft).
- **Defence** (command-and-control systems, missile guidance).
- **Medical devices** (radiation-therapy controllers, infusion pumps, pacemakers).
- **Nuclear reactor control** systems.
- **Automotive safety** (ISO 26262, ASIL-D software for braking, steering).

In each of these domains, certification authorities (FAA, EASA, FDA, AERB) demand **documented, traceable, phase-by-phase verification**, which the waterfall model delivers in a more straightforward manner than agile methods.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analysis, Derivation of Phase Cost-Impact, and Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — The Eight Principles in Action

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix below is a *high-yield KTU study tool*. Use it to recognize which principle applies to a given scenario. Examiners frequently give a 1-paragraph vignette and ask, "Which ACM/IEEE principle is most directly violated?"

| **Real-World Scenario** | **Principle Violated** | **Reasoning** |
|---|---|---|
| Engineer signs off on a financial module knowing the rounding-error code is buggy. | **Product** | Knowing the product is defective and signing off. |
| Manager instructs team to hide the data-breach statistics from the regulator. | **Public Interest, Management** | Concealing a public-safety risk. |
| Engineer accepts a kickback from a vendor in exchange for choosing their middleware. | **Judgement** | Compromised independence. |
| Engineer is asked to write code that backdoors an encryption library. | **Public Interest, Profession** | Subverts security for the public. |
| Engineer writes a CV claiming experience in a programming language they have never used. | **Self, Profession** | Dishonesty about competence. |
| Engineer refuses to mentor a junior colleague from a different cultural background. | **Colleagues** | Discriminatory behaviour. |
| Engineer ships a health-monitoring app to a hospital *without* running any test cases. | **Product, Public Interest** | Untested safety-critical code. |
| Engineer, under deadline, copies a GPL-licensed library into a proprietary product. | **Client, Profession, Product** | Open-source licence violation. |

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Walkthrough of the Waterfall Model Phases

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU "long answer" question on the Waterfall Model is almost always a 7-mark or 14-mark question that requires each phase to be explained with: (a) the *purpose*, (b) the *activities*, (c) the *deliverables*, and (d) the *verification step*.** The exhaustive listing below is what examiners expect.

**Phase 1 — Requirements Analysis and Definition**

- *Purpose:* Establish *what* the system must do. The goal is to produce an agreement between the developer and the client about the system's services, constraints, performance, and external interfaces.
- *Activities:* Elicitation (interviews, questionnaires, observation, workshops), analysis (decomposition, conflict resolution), specification (formal or semi-formal notation), and validation (review with stakeholders).
- *Deliverables:* System Requirements Specification (SRS) document, feasibility report, use-case diagrams.
- *Verification:* Formal review of the SRS, walkthrough with the client, and sign-off. **Gate criterion: client accepts the SRS in writing.**
- *Engineering utility:* This phase is where the *biggest* defects originate. A missing requirement discovered during testing is more expensive than a missing requirement discovered in this phase by an order of magnitude.

**Phase 2 — System and Software Design**

- *Purpose:* Translate the SRS into a *blueprint* for the implementation. The design has two layers: **system design** (high-level decomposition into sub-systems, hardware/software allocation, data-flow architecture) and **detailed design** (algorithms, data structures, module interfaces).
- *Activities:* Architectural design, data-structure design, interface design, component-level design, design of test strategies.
- *Deliverables:* Software Design Description (SDD), Entity-Relationship diagrams, Data Flow Diagrams, design of unit tests.
- *Verification:* Design reviews, inspection of the SDD, prototype construction if needed. **Gate criterion: design passes the formal technical review.**
- *Engineering utility:* The design is the **single most important artefact for maintainability**. Code can be rewritten, but a bad design propagates through every subsequent phase.

**Phase 3 — Implementation and Unit Testing**

- *Purpose:* Convert the design into source code and verify that each unit behaves as its specification requires.
- *Activities:* Coding, code review, refactoring, unit testing, static analysis.
- *Deliverables:* Source code, build artefacts, unit-test results, code-review minutes.
- *Verification:* Each unit must pass its *unit test*; code must pass the *coding standard*; the developer must self-attest correctness.
- *Engineering utility:* The *only* phase that produces *executable artefacts*. The output of every other phase is a document; this is the only phase that produces software.

**Phase 4 — Integration and System Testing**

- *Purpose:* Combine units into a working system, then test the system as a whole against the SRS.
- *Activities:* Integration (top-down, bottom-up, sandwich), system testing (functional, performance, security, usability, recovery), acceptance testing by the client.
- *Deliverables:* Integrated system, system test report, acceptance test report, defect log.
- *Verification:* All high-priority defects closed; acceptance test passes; user-acceptance sign-off.
- *Engineering utility:* This is the *last* gate before the system is exposed to end-users. Failures discovered here are dramatically more expensive than the same failure discovered in earlier phases.

**Phase 5 — Operation and Maintenance**

- *Purpose:* Deploy the system, train users, and keep the system operational.
- *Activities:* Installation, training, corrective maintenance (fixing latent defects), adaptive maintenance (adapting to new environments), perfective maintenance (improving performance or usability), preventive maintenance (preventing future defects).
- *Deliverables:* Operational system, maintenance log, updated documentation.
- *Verification:* Service-level agreement (SLA) compliance, periodic audits.
- *Engineering utility:* Maintenance often consumes **60%–80%** of total life-cycle cost. It is the *longest* phase in the system's life.

### 3.3 Derivation — The "Cost of Late Defect" Curve

> [!NOTE]
> This is a derivation that KTU examiners occasionally ask as a 3- to 5-mark question. The point is to show that **as a defect is discovered later in the life cycle, the cost of fixing it grows geometrically.**

Let us define the following variables for analytical clarity:

- $C_r$ = cost of fixing a defect discovered in the requirements phase
- $C_d$ = cost of fixing a defect discovered in the design phase
- $C_i$ = cost of fixing a defect discovered in the implementation phase
- $C_t$ = cost of fixing a defect discovered in the system-test phase
- $C_m$ = cost of fixing a defect discovered in the maintenance phase

The **classical Boehm / McConnell empirical finding** (supported by decades of industry data) states:

$$
C_d \;\approx\; 5 \cdot C_r
$$

$$
C_i \;\approx\; 10 \cdot C_r
$$

$$
C_t \;\approx\; 50 \cdot C_r \;\;\text{to}\;\; 100 \cdot C_r
$$

$$
C_m \;\approx\; 100 \cdot C_r \;\;\text{to}\;\; 1000 \cdot C_r
$$

**Why does the cost grow so steeply?**

The chain of causality can be unfolded step by step:

**Step 1 — *Requirements phase defect (small cost).* **
A defect caught in the requirements phase requires the analyst to update the SRS, re-run the review, and re-issue the document. The cost is dominated by analyst time. No code is yet affected, so the cost multiplier is **1×**.

**Step 2 — *Design phase defect (5× to 10× cost).* **
If the same requirements defect escapes to the design phase, the analyst must update the SRS *and* the designer must update the SDD, the data-flow diagram, and the module-interface specification. A second reviewer must re-inspect. The cost roughly **5-times** because two artefacts (SRS and SDD) must be updated, plus a re-review.

**Step 3 — *Implementation phase defect (10× cost).* **
Now the coder must change the source code, the unit test must be rewritten, the build must be regenerated, and the unit-test results must be re-attested. A code review is required. The cost roughly **10-times** because three artefacts (SRS, SDD, code + tests) are affected.

**Step 4 — *System test phase defect (50× to 100× cost).* **
The defect is now propagating through the integrated system. The tester must reproduce it, the developer must fix it, the test suite must be re-run, and the integration must be redone. The cost is **50× to 100×** because the impact extends through integration, system test, and acceptance test.

**Step 5 — *Maintenance phase defect (100× to 1000× cost).* **
The system is in production. Fixing the defect requires hot-patching, re-certification, customer communication, and possible regulatory disclosure. The cost can be **100× to 1000×** because the same defect now requires engineering, operations, support, and customer-relations effort.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** The Waterfall Model's tendency to push defect detection *late* into the life cycle is the single largest source of its cost-effectiveness problem. This is the dominant reason why alternative models (incremental, evolutionary, spiral, agile) were developed.

### 3.4 Variant: The "V-Model" — A Test-Driven Extension of Waterfall

Many KTU textbooks mention the **V-Model** as a "verification-and-validation" extension of the waterfall. It is worth knowing that the development phases still flow downwards, but the *testing* phases fan out diagonally, with each test phase linked to a corresponding development phase.

The pairing is:

$$
\text{Unit Testing} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Detailed Design}
$$

$$
\text{Integration Testing} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Architectural Design}
$$

$$
\text{System Testing} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{System Requirements}
$$

$$
\text{Acceptance Testing} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{User Requirements}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU cue:** When asked to "describe the V-Model," present the diagram and explicitly say *which* test validates *which* development artefact. That is the discriminating mark awarded by KTU examiners.

### 3.5 Royce's Original 1970 Diagram — Re-interpreted

Royce's 1970 paper actually shows the *pure* waterfall and then **adds a feedback loop from each phase back to the previous one**, plus a prototyping step at the design stage. This is often mis-cited as Royce inventing agile — he did not. He invented the *plan* and then proposed the *iterations* on top of it. The pure linear form is a *straw-man* that he used to introduce the iterations, but in industry the pure linear form became dominant for two decades. KTU examiners love this nuance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards (Applied Below):**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `phase1Req`, `gatePhase1`).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node IDs.
> - All node labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text, with no `**` or `*` formatting.

### 4.1 The Classical Waterfall Model (5-Phase Form)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phase1Req["PHASE 1 REQUIREMENTS ANALYSIS AND DEFINITION"]
    gate1A["GATE 1 SRS SIGN OFF BY CLIENT"]
    phase2Des["PHASE 2 SYSTEM AND SOFTWARE DESIGN"]
    gate2A["GATE 2 DESIGN REVIEW AND APPROVAL"]
    phase3Imp["PHASE 3 IMPLEMENTATION AND UNIT TESTING"]
    gate3A["GATE 3 CODE REVIEW AND UNIT TEST PASS"]
    phase4Int["PHASE 4 INTEGRATION AND SYSTEM TESTING"]
    gate4A["GATE 4 ACCEPTANCE TEST SIGN OFF"]
    phase5Ops["PHASE 5 OPERATION AND MAINTENANCE"]
    feedback["FEEDBACK LOOP RARE AND EXPENSIVE"]

    phase1Req --> gate1A
    gate1A --> phase2Des
    phase2Des --> gate2A
    gate2A --> phase3Imp
    phase3Imp --> gate3A
    gate3A --> phase4Int
    phase4Int --> gate4A
    gate4A --> phase5Ops
    phase5Ops --> feedback
    feedback -.-> phase1Req
```

**How to read this diagram:** Each rectangular node is a *phase*. The diamond-shaped *gate* nodes represent the verification step that must pass before the next phase can begin. The dotted line is the (rare) feedback loop that returns to the very beginning — in pure waterfall this loop represents a *new project* triggered by major post-deployment defects.

### 4.2 Cost-of-Late-Defect Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r["REQUIREMENTS PHASE COST C_R BASELINE 1X"]
    d["DESIGN PHASE COST 5X TO 10X C_R"]
    i["IMPLEMENTATION PHASE COST 10X C_R"]
    t["TESTING PHASE COST 50X TO 100X C_R"]
    m["MAINTENANCE PHASE COST 100X TO 1000X C_R"]

    r --> d
    d --> i
    i --> t
    t --> m
```

This diagram illustrates the *geometric* growth of defect-fix cost as a function of the phase in which the defect is detected. The five nodes correspond exactly to the five equations of §3.3.

### 4.3 The V-Model — Test-Dev Pairing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userReq["USER REQUIREMENTS"]
    sysReq["SYSTEM REQUIREMENTS"]
    archDes["ARCHITECTURAL DESIGN"]
    detailDes["DETAILED DESIGN"]
    impl["IMPLEMENTATION"]

    accTest["ACCEPTANCE TESTING"]
    sysTest["SYSTEM TESTING"]
    intTest["INTEGRATION TESTING"]
    unitTest["UNIT TESTING"]

    userReq -.-> accTest
    sysReq -.-> sysTest
    archDes -.-> intTest
    detailDes -.-> unitTest

    userReq --> sysReq --> archDes --> detailDes --> impl
    impl --> unitTest --> intTest --> sysTest --> accTest
```

The solid arrows on the left side show the *development* path (top-to-bottom). The dashed arrows on the right show the *validation pairing* — each test on the right validates the corresponding development artefact on the left.

### 4.4 The Eight Principles of Software Engineering Ethics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1["P1 PUBLIC INTEREST"]
    p2["P2 CLIENT AND EMPLOYER"]
    p3["P3 PRODUCT"]
    p4["P4 JUDGEMENT"]
    p5["P5 MANAGEMENT"]
    p6["P6 PROFESSION"]
    p7["P7 COLLEAGUES"]
    p8["P8 SELF"]

    hub["PROFESSIONAL RESPONSIBILITY OF THE SOFTWARE ENGINEER"]

    p1 --> hub
    p2 --> hub
    p3 --> hub
    p4 --> hub
    p5 --> hub
    p6 --> hub
    p7 --> hub
    p8 --> hub
```

This *hub-and-spoke* diagram represents the professional engineer at the centre, with the eight ethical principles as the obligations that radiate outward. The hierarchy is **P1 > P2 > P3 > P4** when duties conflict — that is, **public interest outranks the client, which outranks the employer, which outranks the engineer's own career interest**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are modelled directly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern. The pattern is: **Part A** — 3-mark short-answer questions (answer in 3 to 5 sentences); **Part B** — 14-mark long-answer questions with an internal choice between two alternatives. The model answers below represent full-mark solutions.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]

**What is meant by a professional code of ethics in software engineering? Mention any two of them.**

**Model Answer:**

A professional code of ethics in software engineering is a formal document, issued by a recognized professional body, that prescribes the moral obligations, behavioural standards, and value judgments expected of a software engineer in the practice of their profession. The code is binding on members of the body that issues it, and serves as the standard against which complaints of professional misconduct are evaluated.

Two well-known codes of ethics are:

1. The **ACM Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct** (most recent major revision: 2018). It contains 24 ethical principles grouped under the four headings of *General Moral Imperatives*, *More Specific Professional Responsibilities*, *Organizational Leadership Imperatives*, and *Compliance with the Code*.

2. The **IEEE Code of Ethics** (originally 1979, periodically amended). It commits the engineer to hold paramount the safety, health, and welfare of the public, to disclose promptly factors that may endanger the public, and to reject bribery in all its forms.

*(Examiner's cue: A complete answer must name the issuing body, mention "professional code," and name at least two specific codes. A bare list of principles with no issuing body loses one mark.)*

---

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]

**State the major phases of the Waterfall Model in the order in which they are executed.**

**Model Answer:**

The major phases of the Waterfall Model, in execution order, are:

1. **Requirements Analysis and Definition** — the system's services and constraints are established; the output is the System Requirements Specification (SRS).
2. **System and Software Design** — the SRS is translated into a software architecture and a detailed design.
3. **Implementation and Unit Testing** — the design is realized as code, and each unit is tested.
4. **Integration and System Testing** — units are integrated and the complete system is tested against the SRS.
5. **Operation and Maintenance** — the system is deployed, used, and maintained over its operational life.

In the *pure* waterfall, each phase is *frozen* before the next begins, and there is no overlap or iteration between phases.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pattern:** KTU's 14-mark Module 1 question is typically a single topic split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. **Either** (a) *or* (b) is asked, with one of the two being the "alternate choice." Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are completely independent alternatives. The student answers *one* of them in full.

---

#### Question A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain the concept of Software Engineering Ethics. Discuss the **eight canonical ethical principles** with at least one example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Waterfall Model** of software development. Explain its phases, its advantages, and its disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Answer — A(a): Software Engineering Ethics and the Eight Principles (7 Marks)

**Definition and Scope (2 marks).**
Software Engineering Ethics is the branch of applied professional ethics that codifies the moral duties of software engineers, project managers, and organizations that develop and deploy software. It is articulated through codes of conduct issued by professional bodies, most prominently the **ACM**, the **IEEE Computer Society**, and the **BCS**. The discipline addresses the fact that software defects can cause *large-scale, asymmetric, and sometimes irreversible* harm — loss of life, financial loss, privacy breach, environmental damage — and therefore cannot be governed solely by commercial law or contractual liability.

**The Eight Canonical Principles (5 marks — approximately 0.6 mark each).**

**1. Public Interest.** The engineer shall act in the public interest — safety, health, welfare, privacy, and the environment. *Example:* A software engineer at a hospital discovers a dosing error in a medication-pump controller. The duty to the public overrides the duty to the employer; the engineer must disclose.

**2. Client and Employer.** The engineer shall act in the best interests of the client and employer, consistent with the public interest, and shall hold confidential information in strict confidence. *Example:* An engineer must not copy proprietary client code into a personal portfolio without permission.

**3. Product.** The engineer shall ensure the product meets the highest professional standards and shall not knowingly deliver a defective product. *Example:* An engineer must not sign off on a financial module whose rounding logic is known to be buggy.

**4. Judgement.** The engineer shall maintain independence of professional judgement. *Example:* An engineer must refuse to certify a bridge-monitoring system as "ready for production" when the deadline pressure is unreasonable, even if the manager demands the sign-off.

**5. Management.** Leaders shall promote an ethical approach and set realistic budgets, time-scales, and quality standards. *Example:* A project manager must not promise an impossible release date to a sales team when the engineering team has no capacity to deliver.

**6. Profession.** The engineer shall advance the integrity and reputation of the profession. *Example:* An engineer should not claim false credentials in a CV or in marketing material.

**7. Colleagues.** The engineer shall be fair to and supportive of colleagues, and shall not maliciously injure their reputation. *Example:* A senior engineer should not publicly shame a junior developer for an honest mistake; the issue should be raised in a private review.

**8. Self.** The engineer shall participate in lifelong learning and promote an ethical practice. *Example:* An engineer who has never used a particular technology should not accept a project whose requirements demand mastery of that technology without acquiring the competence first.

---

##### Model Answer — A(b): The Waterfall Model (7 Marks)

**Definition and Origin (1 mark).**
The Waterfall Model is a *sequential, linear* software process model in which each phase of the software development life cycle is completed, documented, and frozen before the next phase begins. It was described in its modern form by **Winston W. Royce in 1970**. Although Royce used the linear model as a *straw-man* and proposed iterations on top of it, the pure linear form became the dominant process model of the 1970s and 1980s.

**Phases (4 marks).**
1. **Requirements Analysis and Definition** — produce a System Requirements Specification (SRS) signed off by the client.
2. **System and Software Design** — produce a Software Design Description (SDD) covering architecture, data flow, and module interfaces.
3. **Implementation and Unit Testing** — produce source code, builds, and a unit-test report.
4. **Integration and System Testing** — produce an integrated system, a system-test report, and an acceptance-test report.
5. **Operation and Maintenance** — install the system, correct latent defects, adapt to new environments, and improve performance.

**Advantages (1 mark).**
- Simple to plan, manage, and monitor. A Gantt chart is sufficient.
- Each phase has a well-defined deliverable and a clear gate for sign-off.
- Documentation is thorough, which is essential for safety-critical and regulated systems.
- Suited to projects where the requirements are clear, stable, and well-understood.

**Disadvantages (1 mark).**
- No iteration in the pure form — late discovery of defects is extremely expensive.
- Working software is delivered very late in the life cycle.
- Customer feedback is obtained only after delivery, when the cost of change is highest.
- Not suited to projects with vague, evolving, or rapidly changing requirements.

---

#### Question B. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks — Alternate Choice]

**(a)** What is meant by **Whistleblowing** in the context of software engineering ethics? Describe the protocol a software engineer should follow when internal reporting channels fail. Give one real-world example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **V-Model**. Show how each test phase validates a corresponding development phase. Compare the V-Model with the pure Waterfall Model. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Answer — B(a): Whistleblowing (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks).**
*Whistleblowing* is the disclosure by an employee (the "whistleblower") of information about a serious wrongdoing — typically a danger to the public, a fraud, or a violation of law — to a person or body that has the power to correct the wrongdoing. In the context of software engineering ethics, the term refers specifically to the disclosure of *software-related dangers* (security flaws, safety violations, defective code shipped to users, breaches of regulation) when the engineer's employer refuses to act on the danger internally.

**The Whistleblowing Protocol (3 marks).**
Professional codes (ACM, IEEE, BCS) prescribe a graduated sequence of disclosure channels:

1. **Step 1 — Internal reporting.** The engineer must first raise the concern with their immediate manager, then escalate to higher management, and finally to the organization's ethics officer or audit committee. The reasoning is that internal channels are *less* likely to cause collateral damage and that the organization is best placed to fix the problem.

2. **Step 2 — External reporting to an authority.** If the internal channels fail, or if the danger is *imminent* and the employer refuses to act, the engineer should report to the appropriate external authority — a regulator, a law-enforcement body, a professional society's ethics committee, or a parliamentary ombudsman.

3. **Step 3 — Public disclosure as a last resort.** Public disclosure (e.g., to the press) is ethically justified *only* when (a) the danger is grave, (b) the evidence is solid, (c) internal and authority channels have been exhausted, and (d) no less-harmful channel remains.

**Real-World Example (2 marks).**
A well-known software-engineering case is that of the *Toyota unintended-acceleration investigation* (2009–2011), in which an embedded-software expert's report to the U.S. National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) led to a re-examination of the engine-control software. The whistleblower documented a stack-overflow bug in the throttle-control software that could cause unintended acceleration. A second example is the Boeing 737 MAX case (2018–2019), in which internal engineers raised concerns about the MCAS software to management; when their concerns were dismissed, the issue eventually became public through accident investigation and media reporting.

---

##### Model Answer — B(b): The V-Model (7 Marks)

**Definition and Origin (1.5 marks).**
The V-Model is an extension of the Waterfall Model in which the development phases flow downwards on the left side of a "V" shape, and the corresponding testing phases flow upwards on the right side. Each test phase on the right is *paired* with a development phase on the left, making the *validation* relationship explicit. The V-Model is widely used in regulated and safety-critical industries because it forces each test to be planned in parallel with the corresponding development artefact.

**The Diagram and Pairing (4 marks).**
The development phases (top-to-bottom on the left) are:

- **User Requirements** — what the user needs.
- **System Requirements** — what the system must do.
- **Architectural Design** — how the system is decomposed.
- **Detailed Design** — how each component is implemented.
- **Implementation** — the actual code.

The testing phases (bottom-to-top on the right) are:

- **Unit Testing** validates the **Detailed Design**.
- **Integration Testing** validates the **Architectural Design**.
- **System Testing** validates the **System Requirements**.
- **Acceptance Testing** validates the **User Requirements**.

**Comparison with Pure Waterfall (1.5 marks).**

| **Property** | **Pure Waterfall** | **V-Model** |
|---|---|---|
| Test planning | Done after coding | Done in parallel with development |
| Test-to-requirement traceability | Implicit | Explicit (each test maps to a phase) |
| Defect detection latency | Late | Earlier, because test cases are designed alongside the requirements |
| Documentation rigour | High | Equally high |
| Best fit | Stable requirements, less-critical systems | Safety-critical and regulated systems |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do not say "the V-Model allows iteration"** — it does *not*. It is still fundamentally linear; the test phases are planned earlier, but execution is still sequential.
> 2. **Do not omit the pairing.** The single biggest discriminator between a 5-mark answer and a 7-mark answer on the V-Model is the explicit mention of which test validates which development artefact.
> 3. **Do not confuse the Waterfall with the Spiral Model.** A common 3-mark mistake is to list "plan, do, check, act" as phases of the Waterfall — that is the *Spiral* model, not the Waterfall.
> 4. **For the ethics question,** do not list principles *without* giving examples. The KTU marking scheme allocates roughly 60% of the marks to the *example* and 40% to the *principle name*.
> 5. **In whistleblowing answers,** do not present public disclosure to the media as the *first* step. The KTU mark scheme always awards full marks only to the *graduated* protocol (internal → authority → public).
> 6. **On cost-of-defect questions,** students who write "defects are expensive" without giving the *5× / 10× / 50× / 1000×* multipliers lose 2 of the 7 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print or screenshot this section before the exam.)**

**On Software Engineering Ethics:**

- The **eight** canonical principles: Public, Client, Product, Judgement, Management, Profession, Colleagues, Self.
- The **hierarchy of duties** in conflict situations: **Public > Client > Employer > Self.**
- The **Whistleblowing** protocol is **graduated**: Internal → Authority → Public. Public disclosure is a *last resort*, not a first.
- The **three leading codes** are **ACM** (2018 revision, 24 principles), **IEEE** (paramountcy of public safety), and **BCS** (UK-based).
- **Real-world cases** you must be able to identify at a glance: **Therac-25** (radiation overdose), **Volkswagen Dieselgate** (defeat device), **Boeing 737 MAX** (MCAS), **Facebook / Cambridge Analytica** (privacy), **Toyota unintended acceleration** (stack overflow).
- A software engineer's duty to **disclose** a known defect is *binding* on them even if the employer prefers silence.

**On the Waterfall Model:**

- The Waterfall Model is the **earliest, linear, plan-driven** process model. Author: **Winston W. Royce, 1970**.
- The **five** canonical phases are: **Requirements → Design → Implementation → Integration/Test → Maintenance**.
- Each phase is **frozen** at the end; pure waterfall has **no iteration** and **no overlap**.
- The **single biggest disadvantage** is the *late discovery of defects*. The cost multiplier grows from **1×** at the requirements phase to **100×–1000×** at the maintenance phase.
- The **single biggest advantage** is the *ease of planning, management, and documentation*, which is why it is still used in **safety-critical and regulated** domains (aerospace, medical, nuclear, automotive safety).
- The **V-Model** is a test-driven extension of the Waterfall. The four test-development pairings to memorize are: **Unit ↔ Detailed Design, Integration ↔ Architectural Design, System ↔ System Requirements, Acceptance ↔ User Requirements.**
- The Waterfall Model **assumes** that requirements are **clear, stable, and well-understood** at the start of the project. If they are not, the model is the wrong choice.

**Common exam traps:**

- Confusing *Waterfall* with *Spiral*. The Spiral model is *iterative and risk-driven*; the Waterfall is *linear and plan-driven*.
- Confusing *V-Model* with *V&V*. V&V (Verification and Validation) is a *technique*; the V-Model is a *process*.
- Confusing *client/employer confidentiality* with *public-interest disclosure*. The latter outranks the former.
- Forgetting to mention the *gate* between phases. A phase is not complete until its verification step has passed.
- Forgetting that *maintenance* is the *longest* phase, not the *shortest*. It commonly consumes **60%–80%** of the total life-cycle cost.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incremental Development. Process Activities - Software Specification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Incremental Development. Process Activities - Software Specification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1: SOFTWARE PROCESSES & SPECIFICATION

## 1. Incremental Development — Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Sommerville Framework):**
> *Incremental Development* is a software process model in which the system is designed, implemented, and tested incrementally (a little more is added each time) until the product is finished. The system is delivered to the customer as a series of **increments** (or versions), where each increment delivers a subset of the total functionality of the system.

### 1.1 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🏗️

Imagine you are building a **house**, but instead of waiting for the entire house to be constructed (which might take 2 years), the builder hands you the **foundation + walls + roof** in Increment 1 (you can already move in with basic shelter). Then Increment 2 adds the **plumbing and electrical wiring**. Increment 3 adds the **kitchen cabinets and bathroom fittings**. Increment 4 adds the **interior paint and final décor**.

You don't have to wait for the perfect house — you get a **usable, livable version early**, and the house keeps getting better with each phase. That is **Incremental Development** in software terms. Each increment is a working, tested subset of the final product.

### 1.2 Process Activities — Software Specification: Core Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus, Sommerville Ch. 4):**
> *Software Specification* (or *Requirements Engineering*) is the process of establishing the **services** that the customer requires from a system and the **constraints** under which it operates and is developed. It is the first phase of any software development process and acts as the bridge between the informal real-world problem and the formal engineered solution.

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Requirements** | Statements of *what* the system must do (services) or *how well* it must do them (constraints). |
| **Specification** | The documented, validated outcome of the requirements engineering process. |
| **Stakeholder** | Any person, group, or system affected by the system (users, managers, regulators, etc.). |

### 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU

- Module 1 carries **15–20% of KTU ESE marks** consistently.
- Direct 14-mark questions are frequently asked from *Process Activities* and *Software Process Models*.
- Incremental Development is the foundation of modern Agile/Scrum methodologies.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 The Incremental Development Model — Operational Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Premise:** Requirements are prioritized and grouped into increments. The **highest-priority requirements** are delivered first; **lower-priority requirements** are bundled into later increments.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Incremental Development

1. **Requirement Prioritization** — All user/system requirements are identified and ranked by business value and risk.
2. **Increment Definition** — A subset of the highest-priority requirements is selected to form Increment 1.
3. **Mini-Waterfall per Increment** — Each increment goes through its own *Design → Implementation → Test* cycle, but **without locking down all requirements** for the entire system upfront.
4. **Customer Delivery & Feedback** — Once Increment $i$ is tested and validated, it is **deployed to the customer**. Feedback is gathered.
5. **Iteration with Refinement** — Feedback from Increment $i$ shapes and refines the requirements of Increment $i+1$.
6. **Termination** — The process continues until all increments are delivered and the complete system is operational.

### 2.2 Incremental vs. Waterfall — Analytical Comparison

| Attribute | Waterfall Model | Incremental Development |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Delivery** | Single delivery at the end | Multiple deliveries (increments) |
| **Customer Feedback** | Late (only after full build) | Early and continuous |
| **Risk Exposure** | High — late discovery of issues | Low — issues caught early per increment |
| **Change Accommodation** | Costly, requires re-engineering | Naturally accommodated in later increments |
| **Initial Cost** | Lower upfront, but expensive at the end | Higher per-increment, but total cost more predictable |
| **Customer Value** | Delayed | Delivered early via high-priority increments |
| **Team Specialization** | Single large team | Smaller, focused sub-teams per increment |
| **Best Suited For** | Well-understood, stable requirements | Evolving, unclear, or high-risk requirements |

### 2.3 Advantages and Disadvantages of Incremental Development

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Examiners frequently ask "Justify why incremental development is suitable for a real-world software project." Memorize the following lists verbatim.

**Advantages:**
- **Early Delivery of Value:** The customer gains usable functionality from Increment 1.
- **Reduced Risk of Catastrophic Failure:** Defects in one increment do not paralyze the entire project.
- **Customer Involvement:** Continuous stakeholder engagement ensures better fit.
- **Easier Testing:** Each increment represents a smaller, manageable test surface.
- **Reusability of Code:** Core modules of early increments can be reused/refined in later ones.

**Disadvantages:**
- **System Architecture Degradation:** Without careful refactoring, the overall system structure can erode as new increments are bolted on.
- **Cost Overrun Temptation:** Easy to keep adding "one more increment" beyond original scope (*scope creep*).
