- **Conflict between Increments:** Features in later increments may clash with those already deployed.
- **Repeated Re-specification:** Each increment's requirements are revisited, costing time.

## 2.4 Process Activities — Software Specification: The Five Sub-Activities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Mandate (Sommerville, Chapter 4):** The Requirements Engineering (Specification) process comprises **four main sub-activities**, often depicted as a sequential yet iterative pipeline.

### Sub-Activity 1: Feasibility Study
- **Goal:** Determine whether the proposed system is *technically, financially, and operationally* viable.
- **Output:** A **Feasibility Report** recommending *Proceed, Defer, or Reject*.

### Sub-Activity 2: Requirements Elicitation and Analysis
- **Goal:** Discover what the stakeholders actually need through *interviews, observation, ethnography, workshops, scenarios*.
- **Challenge:** Stakeholders may have **conflicting, vague, or evolving** requirements.

### Sub-Activity 3: Requirements Specification
- **Goal:** Translate the elicited requirements into a **formal document** (e.g., SRS — Software Requirements Specification as per IEEE 830/29148).
- **Granularity:** Distinguishes *User Requirements* (high-level, natural language) from *System Requirements* (detailed, structured).

### Sub-Activity 4: Requirements Validation
- **Goal:** Demonstrate that the requirements *accurately define the system the customer wants*.
- **Techniques:** Reviews, inspections, prototyping, test-case generation, formal validation.

### Sub-Activity 5: Requirements Management
- **Goal:** Manage changing requirements throughout the system lifetime.
- **Tools:** Requirements traceability matrices (RTM), version control, change management boards (CCB).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Software Specification is largely qualitative, but a few **measurable cost/effort metrics** are commonly tested. The following table is the **definitive KTU reference** for the formulas you may be required to recall in numerical sub-questions.

| Metric | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Effort Estimation (Basic COCOMO)** | $E = a \cdot (\text{KLOC})^b$ person-months | Effort $E$ based on thousands of lines of code, where $a$ and $b$ are mode-dependent constants. |
| **Development Time** | $D = c \cdot E^d$ months | Time $D$ is a power function of effort $E$. |
| **Average Staffing** | $\text{Staff} = E \div D$ persons | Required average team size. |
| **Requirements Stability Index (RSI)** | $\text{RSI} = \dfrac{R_s}{R_s + R_c}$ | Ratio of stable requirements $R_s$ to total changes $R_s + R_c$. RSI $\to 1$ means highly stable. |
| **Volatility** | $\text{Volatility} = 1 - \text{RSI}$ | Proportion of requirements that changed. |
| **Feasibility ROI (Simple)** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100\%$ | Simple Return on Investment for feasibility analysis. |
| **Per-Increment Cost Ratio** | $C_i = C_{\text{base}} \cdot 2^{i-1}$ (rough heuristic) | Heuristic that later increments may cost up to $2\times$ earlier ones due to integration overhead. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The RSI / Volatility metrics are *very high-yield* — they have appeared in previous KTU ESE papers as a 3-mark short-answer question.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** Operators deploy core 4G services (Increment 1), then VoLTE, then 5G NR (later increments).
- **Automotive Software:** ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems) ship in increments — basic collision warning first, then lane-keeping, then full autonomy.
- **Banking Applications:** Core transaction engine first; then KYC; then AI-based fraud detection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Process Activity Strategy

The following matrix maps **project scenarios** to the **most appropriate process activity emphasis** for KTU-style analysis questions.

| Project Scenario | Recommended Specification Strategy | Incremental Approach? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Well-defined, stable requirements** (e.g., payroll system) | Heavy upfront elicitation; tight SRS; minimal change | **No** — Waterfall preferred |
| **Evolving requirements** (e.g., e-commerce platform) | Iterative elicitation; living SRS; change management | **Yes** — Incremental preferred |
| **Safety-critical system** (e.g., aircraft control) | Rigorous formal specification; traceability matrix | **No** — Spiral/V-model preferred |
| **Time-to-market pressure** (e.g., startup MVP) | Minimum viable specification; rapid prototyping | **Yes** — Strongly recommended |
| **Legacy system replacement** | Reverse-engineering + forward engineering | **Yes** — Incremental migration |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Analysis: Requirements Volatility Computation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a classic KTU 3-mark numerical question.

**Problem:** A software system was specified with $R_s = 85$ stable requirements and $R_c = 15$ changed requirements. Calculate the Requirements Stability Index (RSI) and Volatility.

**Step 1 — Identify the given values.**
The number of stable requirements is $R_s = 85$.
The number of changed requirements is $R_c = 15$.

**Step 2 — Apply the RSI formula.**
The formula for Requirements Stability Index is defined as the ratio of stable requirements to the total number of requirements considered.

$$\text{RSI} = \frac{R_s}{R_s + R_c}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**
Substituting $R_s = 85$ and $R_c = 15$ into the numerator and denominator respectively.

$$\text{RSI} = \frac{85}{85 + 15}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the denominator.**
Adding the two quantities in the denominator to obtain the total requirement count.

$$\text{RSI} = \frac{85}{100}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the final decimal value.**
Dividing 85 by 100 to express the index as a normalized value between 0 and 1.

$$\text{RSI} = 0.85$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Volatility.**
The Volatility is defined as the complement of the RSI, representing the fraction of requirements that did change.

$$\text{Volatility} = 1 - \text{RSI}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute and evaluate.**
Replacing RSI with the computed value 0.85 in the expression.

$$\text{Volatility} = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15$$

**Step 8 — Interpret the result.**
An RSI of $0.85$ (or $85\%$) indicates a **highly stable specification**, which is excellent for the project. A Volatility of $0.15$ (or $15\%$) means only $15\%$ of the requirements underwent modification — a manageable change footprint.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Always state the *interpretation* of the computed metric. KTU examiners award the final 1 mark for **analytical interpretation**, not just mechanical computation.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Analysis: Cost-Benefit Justification for Incremental Development

This is the **most common 7-mark sub-question** in KTU ESE for this topic. The expected structure is shown below.

**Step 1 — Define the project context.**
Articulate the nature of the system (e.g., web-based, customer-facing, evolving).

**Step 2 — State the requirements uncertainty.**
Explicitly mention that requirements are *expected to change* during the project lifecycle.

**Step 3 — Map requirements to increments.**
Demonstrate how the system is decomposed into $n$ increments, where Increment 1 delivers the **highest business value** features.

**Step 4 — Articulate the benefits.**
Enumerate the advantages: early ROI, lower risk, continuous stakeholder feedback.

**Step 5 — Acknowledge the drawbacks.**
Show academic maturity by recognizing disadvantages and proposing mitigations (e.g., dedicated refactoring sprints, architecture custodianship).

**Step 6 — Conclude with a recommendation.**
Provide a justified recommendation for adopting the incremental model.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison: User vs. System Requirements

| Dimension | User Requirements | System Requirements |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Audience** | End-users, managers, customers (non-technical) | Developers, architects, testers (technical) |
| **Language** | Natural language, diagrams, scenarios | Structured notations, formal methods, pseudocode |
| **Abstraction Level** | High-level (the *what*) | Detailed (the *what + how*) |
| **Purpose** | Communicate expectations | Define the engineering contract |
| **Example** | "The system shall allow users to search for flights." | "The system shall process a SEARCH query against the FLIGHTS table in <2 seconds for 95% of requests." |

## 3.5 The Four-Phase Specification Pipeline — Exhaustive Description

**Phase 1 — Feasibility Study:**
- **Activities:** Define the problem scope, identify alternative solutions, estimate cost-benefit, recommend course of action.
- **Deliverable:** **Feasibility Report** (typically 5–20 pages).
- **Key Question:** *Is the project worth doing?*

**Phase 2 — Requirements Elicitation and Analysis:**
- **Techniques:** Interviews, observation, ethnography, workshops, document analysis, focus groups.
- **Challenge:** Stakeholders express requirements in their own terms; the analyst must reconcile *conflicts, overlaps, and ambiguities*.
- **Key Question:** *What do the stakeholders actually need?*

**Phase 3 — Requirements Specification:**
- **Documents:** SRS (Software Requirements Specification) per **IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148** standard.
- **Languages:** Natural language, structured specifications, formal specification languages (Z, B, VDM), graphical models (UML use cases, data flow diagrams).
- **Key Question:** *How do we formally document the requirements?*

**Phase 4 — Requirements Validation:**
- **Techniques:** Requirements reviews, walkthroughs, inspections, prototyping, acceptance-test design.
- **Key Question:** *Are the requirements correct, complete, consistent, and unambiguous?*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Incremental Development Process — Mermaid Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Project Kickoff]) --> B[Requirements Identification and Prioritization]
    B --> C[Define Increment 1 Scope]
    C --> D[Design Increment 1]
    D --> E[Implement Increment 1]
    E --> F[Test Increment 1]
    F --> G{Customer Acceptance}
    G -- Rejected --> D
    G -- Accepted --> H[Deploy Increment 1 to Customer]
    H --> I[Collect Customer Feedback]
    I --> J{All Requirements Delivered?}
    J -- No --> K[Refine Requirements for Next Increment]
    K --> C
    J -- Yes --> L([Final System Delivered])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The inner loop (`Design → Implement → Test → Acceptance`) is repeated for every increment. The outer loop (`Feedback → Refinement → Next Increment`) is the architectural heart of the incremental model. The process terminates when the requirements backlog is empty.

## 4.2 Software Specification Process Activities — Mermaid Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0([User Need]) --> A1[Phase 1: Feasibility Study]
    A1 -- Not Feasible --> A2([Project Aborted])
    A1 -- Feasible --> A2a[Phase 2: Requirements Elicitation and Analysis]
    A2a -- Conflicts Found --> A2a
    A2a --> A3[Phase 3: Requirements Specification]
    A3 --> A4[Phase 4: Requirements Validation]
    A4 -- Defects Detected --> A3
    A4 -- Validated --> A5[Approved Software Requirements Specification]
    A5 --> A6([Phase 5: Ongoing Requirements Management])
    A6 -. Change Request .-> A2a
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Visual Pattern:** Examiners reward diagrams. Always include a Mermaid/Flowchart in 14-mark answers — it provides a high-density information deliverable that demonstrates conceptual mastery.

## 4.3 Incremental Delivery vs. Single Delivery — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WD[Waterfall Delivery Model]
        direction LR
        WD1[Analysis] --> WD2[Design] --> WD3[Implementation] --> WD4[Testing] --> WD5([Final Single Delivery at Month 12])
    end

    subgraph ID[Incremental Delivery Model]
        direction LR
        ID1[Increment 1 at Month 3] --> ID2[Increment 2 at Month 6] --> ID3[Increment 3 at Month 9] --> ID4([Increment 4 at Month 12])
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The incremental model achieves **Time-to-Value at Month 3**, whereas the waterfall model delivers value only at Month 12. This 9-month lead time is precisely why incremental development dominates modern software economics.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Differentiate between Incremental Development and Waterfall Development models."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Attribute | Waterfall | Incremental |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Delivery Style** | One single final delivery | Series of increments (versions) |
| **Feedback Loop** | Late — after full system is built | Continuous — after every increment |
| **Change Cost** | Very high (rework from scratch) | Low (handled in next increment) |
| **Risk Profile** | High — late defect discovery | Low — incremental verification |
| **Customer Engagement** | Minimal after initial elicitation | Active throughout the project |
| **Best For** | Small, well-understood, regulated systems | Large, complex, evolving systems |

> **Valuation Key:** 1 mark for the basic distinction, 1 mark for any two contrasting attributes, 1 mark for stating the suitability of each.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"List and briefly explain the four major process activities in the Software Specification phase."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Feasibility Study:** Evaluates whether the proposed system is *technically, financially, and operationally* viable. Produces a feasibility report.
2. **Requirements Elicitation and Analysis:** Discovers the *actual* requirements of stakeholders using interviews, observation, and prototyping.
3. **Requirements Specification:** Documents the requirements formally in an *SRS* document, using natural language, structured, or formal notations.
4. **Requirements Validation:** Confirms that the documented requirements are *correct, complete, unambiguous, and testable*.

> **Valuation Key:** 1 mark for listing all four activities, 1 mark for any two with explanations, 1 mark for clarity of the overall flow.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two questions are offered, and the student answers ONE. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) Explain the Incremental Development model in detail. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Incremental Development is a software process model where the system is developed and delivered as a sequence of *increments* (versions), each adding a portion of the total functionality until the complete system is realized.

**Operational Steps (3 Marks):**
1. All requirements are identified and **prioritized** by business value.
2. A subset of high-priority requirements is assigned to **Increment 1**.
3. Each increment goes through its own design → implement → test cycle.
4. After validation, the increment is **delivered to the customer** for feedback.
5. Customer feedback **informs and refines** subsequent increments.
6. Process repeats until the requirements backlog is empty.

**Advantages (2 Marks):**
- **Early delivery of value** to the customer.
- **Reduced risk** through continuous testing and validation.
- **Continuous customer involvement** ensures higher satisfaction.
- **Easier to manage** since each increment is a smaller, focused sub-project.

**Disadvantages (1 Mark):**
- System architecture can **degrade** over time without disciplined refactoring.
- **Scope creep** risk — easy to keep adding "just one more" increment.
- **Costly integration** of multiple increments in the long run.

---

**(b) What is Software Specification? Explain the four major process activities involved in the Requirements Engineering process with suitable diagrams. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Software Specification (or Requirements Engineering) is the systematic process of *defining, documenting, and maintaining* the services and constraints of a software system. It is the foundational phase upon which all subsequent development hinges.

**The Four Sub-Activities (4 Marks):**

1. **Feasibility Study:**
   - Assesses whether the system is *technically, financially, and operationally* feasible.
   - Output: **Feasibility Report** recommending Proceed / Defer / Reject.

2. **Requirements Elicitation and Analysis:**
   - Discovers the actual stakeholder needs.
   - Techniques: *interviews, observation, ethnography, workshops, scenario analysis, document analysis.*
   - Challenges: conflicting, ambiguous, or evolving requirements.

3. **Requirements Specification:**
   - Converts the elicited requirements into a formal document — the **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)**.
   - Distinguishes between *user requirements* (high-level) and *system requirements* (detailed).
   - Standards: **IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148**.

4. **Requirements Validation:**
   - Demonstrates that the SRS *correctly, completely, and unambiguously* describes the desired system.
   - Techniques: *reviews, walkthroughs, inspections, prototyping, acceptance test design.*

**Supporting Diagram (1 Mark):**
A flowchart showing the four sub-activities in sequence, with feedback loops from Validation back to Specification when defects are detected, and from Requirements Management back to Elicitation when change requests are received.

**Significance (1 Mark):**
Without rigorous specification, downstream design, coding, and testing activities become error-prone. Specification errors caught late (after deployment) are typically *10–100× more expensive* to fix than those caught during elicitation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) Discuss in detail the Incremental Development approach. Why is it preferred over the Waterfall model in modern software engineering practice? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The Incremental Development approach, as defined by Sommerville, is a process model where the system is built and delivered as a series of *working versions* (increments). Each increment adds new functionality on top of the previously delivered version, allowing the customer to obtain *value progressively*.

**Detailed Discussion (4 Marks):**
- **Phase 1 — Requirement Gathering:** The complete set of requirements is identified, but only a subset (the most critical) is scheduled for the first increment.
- **Phase 2 — First Increment Design:** Architectural skeleton and high-priority features are designed.
- **Phase 3 — First Increment Implementation & Testing:** A working subset is built, tested, and deployed.
- **Phase 4 — Customer Feedback:** Users provide feedback on Increment 1, which is used to *refine, correct, and expand* the requirements of the next increment.
- **Phase 5 — Subsequent Increments:** Each new increment adds more features, integrates with the previous ones, and is independently tested.
- **Phase 6 — System Completion:** The process continues until all agreed-upon requirements are satisfied and the system is deemed complete.

**Justification: Why Incremental over Waterfall? (2 Marks):**
1. **Customer Value:** Value is delivered from Month 3 onwards, not Month 12.
2. **Risk Management:** Defects and requirement gaps are discovered early.
3. **Adaptability:** Changing requirements are *embraced* rather than resisted.
4. **Stakeholder Trust:** Continuous delivery builds confidence in the development team.
5. **Economic Realism:** Cash flows and revenue start early.

> **Valuation Key:** 1 mark for naming the approach, 4 marks for the detailed phase-by-phase explanation, 2 marks for the comparative justification.

---

**(b) Differentiate between User Requirements and System Requirements. Why is the distinction critical in the Software Specification phase? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definitions (2 Marks):**
- **User Requirements:** High-level, abstract statements written in *natural language* that describe *what the user expects the system to do*. They are intended for non-technical stakeholders.
- **System Requirements:** Detailed, structured specifications that describe *the system functions, services, and operational constraints* in a manner suitable for the development team. They are written in *technical, structured, or formal notations*.

**Detailed Comparison (3 Marks):**

| Attribute | User Requirements | System Requirements |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Audience** | Customers, managers, end-users | Developers, testers, architects |
| **Granularity** | High-level (the *what*) | Detailed (the *what + how*) |
| **Vocabulary** | Natural, colloquial | Technical, formal |
| **Scope** | External view of the system | Internal view of the system |
| **Stability** | More prone to changes | More stable; evolves from user req. |
| **Example** | "The system shall support UPI payments." | "The system shall process a UPI_TXN message via the NPCI gateway with a 99.9% success rate and <3 second latency." |

**Significance of the Distinction (2 Marks):**
1. **Bridging the Communication Gap:** User requirements are the *translator* between domain experts and the engineering team.
2. **Traceability:** Each system requirement must trace back to one or more user requirements to ensure completeness.
3. **Scope Control:** User requirements define *what*; system requirements define *how* — preventing scope creep.
4. **Validation Basis:** User requirements provide the *acceptance criteria*; system requirements provide the *technical verification basis*.
5. **Contractual Clarity:** User requirements are the *legal commitment* to the customer; system requirements are the *internal engineering contract* with the development team.

> **Valuation Key:** 2 marks for definitions, 3 marks for the table-based differentiation, 2 marks for the critical significance discussion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout
>
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns Observed in KTU Valuation Reports:**
>
> 1. **❌ Confusing "Incremental" with "Iterative":** These are *related but distinct*. *Incremental* = adding new features in slices. *Iterative* = refining the same feature in cycles. The two are often combined (incremental AND iterative), but students writing 14-mark answers frequently conflate them. Examiner docks **2 marks** for this confusion.
>
> 2. **❌ Skipping the Diagram:** A 14-mark question on Process Activities *must* include a Mermaid/Flowchart. Without a diagram, expect a **2-mark deduction** even if the textual content is correct.
>
> 3. **❌ Writing "Software Specification" instead of listing the four sub-activities:** A common student mistake is to provide a generic 1-line definition and then jump straight to writing SRS templates. Examiners want *Process Activities*, not SRS template content. Deduct **1.5 marks** if the four sub-activities are not explicitly named.
>
> 4. **❌ Failing to interpret numerical results:** For any RSI/Volatility computation, students who stop at the calculation without stating the *engineering meaning* of the result lose the final **1 mark** designated for analytical interpretation.
>
> 5. **❌ Listing only advantages in incremental development questions:** Always state **at least 2 disadvantages**. Showing critical balance earns full marks. A one-sided answer is capped at **5/7 marks**.
>
> 6. **❌ Mixing up "Feasibility Study" with "Project Planning":** Feasibility is a *pre-project gate*; Planning is *post-approval*. Confusing them is a **1-mark penalty**.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Preparation:**

- [x] **Incremental Development** = system is built and delivered as a *series of working increments*, each adding new functionality.
- [x] The **highest-priority requirements** are always delivered in the *first* increment.
- [x] Incremental development *embraces change* — later increments are refined based on customer feedback.
- [x] The model reduces *risk* by enabling early defect detection and continuous validation.
- [x] **Disadvantages** to memorize: architecture degradation, scope creep, integration cost, conflicting increments.
- [x] **Software Specification** (Requirements Engineering) bridges the *informal real-world problem* and the *formal engineered solution*.
- [x] The **four sub-activities** are: **Feasibility Study → Elicitation & Analysis → Specification → Validation** (with ongoing **Requirements Management** as a fifth continuous activity).
- [x] **Feasibility Study** asks: *Is the project worth doing?* Output: **Feasibility Report**.
- [x] **Elicitation & Analysis** asks: *What do stakeholders need?* Techniques: *interviews, observation, ethnography, workshops*.
- [x] **Specification** asks: *How do we document it?* Output: **SRS** (per IEEE 830 / 29148).
- [x] **Validation** asks: *Is it correct, complete, consistent, unambiguous?* Techniques: *reviews, walkthroughs, prototyping*.
- [x] **User Requirements** = high-level, natural language, *what* (for non-technical stakeholders).
- [x] **System Requirements** = detailed, structured, *what + how* (for the engineering team).
- [x] **RSI** $= R_s / (R_s + R_c)$; **Volatility** $= 1 - \text{RSI}$. RSI $\to 1$ means highly stable specification.
- [x] Always **include a Mermaid/Flowchart** in 14-mark answers on process models or process activities.
- [x] Always state **at least 2 disadvantages** in incremental development answers to demonstrate academic balance.
- [x] **Incremental** ≠ **Iterative** — *incremental* adds new features; *iterative* refines existing ones.
- [x] Modern Agile/Scrum is a *practical embodiment* of the Incremental Development philosophy.
- [x] Specification errors caught *late* are **10–100× more expensive** to fix than those caught during elicitation — use this statistic in conclusion statements for **bonus valuation points**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Design and Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Software Design and Implementation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Software Design? **Software Design** is the technical kernel of software engineering — the activity within the software engineering process that transforms *what* the system must do (requirements) into *how* it will accomplish those objectives. It is the stage where engineering creativity, abstraction skills, and architectural discipline combine to produce a blueprint for the software solution. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (per Pressman & Maxim):** *Software design is a creative activity in which you identify software components and their relationships, based on customer requirements, while applying design concepts, principles, and heuristics. The design process translates requirements into a representation of the software that can be assessed for quality before coding begins.* ### 1.2 What is Software Implementation? **Software Implementation** (often called *coding* or *construction*) is the software engineering activity in which the design representation is translated into executable code, verified against the design, and integrated into a working software system. It is the activity most visible to end-users and forms the bridge between design and testing. > [!NOTE] > Implementation is **not** just typing — it includes applying coding standards, performing unit-level verification, conducting peer reviews, and creating testable, maintainable source code that faithfully reflects the architectural and component-level design. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Architect and the Builder" Think of software design and implementation as **building a house**: | Software Engineering Stage | Real-World Analogy | Output | |---------------------------|-------------------|--------| | Requirements Gathering | Client says "I need a 3BHK house" | Wish-list | | **Software Design** | Architect draws **blueprint**, plans rooms, plumbing, electrical layout | Architectural & detailed plans | | **Implementation** | Mason, plumber, electrician **construct** the house following the blueprint | The actual house | | Testing | Building inspector checks quality | Approved/flagged house | | Maintenance | Repainting, leak-fixing, renovations | Upgraded house | > [!TIP] > Just as you cannot pour concrete before approving the blueprint, you should not code extensively before validating the design. **Design discipline = cheaper defects.** ### 1.4 Position in the Generic Software Process The design phase sits **between** requirements engineering and implementation in the **generic process model**: 1. **Requirements Engineering** (Communication, Planning, Analysis) 2. → **Software Design** (Modeling, Architectural & Component Design) 3. → **Implementation / Coding** (Code generation, Unit testing) 4. → **Testing, Deployment, Maintenance** ### 1.5 The Layered Design Approach (KTU 2024 Emphasis) The KTU 2024 scheme emphasises a **hierarchical design decomposition** where each layer refines the previous one. This layered view ensures that problems at one level of abstraction do not pollute higher levels. - **Data/Class Design** → *Transforms analysis classes into implementation classes* - **Architectural Design** → *Defines the system skeleton: subsystems, their responsibilities & interactions* - **Interface Design** → *Describes how software communicates with users, systems, and devices* - **Component-Level Design** → *Algorithmic structure, data structures of individual modules* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** All four layers are **interdependent** — a change in component design may ripple up to the architectural layer. KTU examiners frequently ask for diagrams showing this layering. --- ## 1.6 Importance in Industry > [!NOTE] > Industry studies (e.g., IBM Systems Sciences Institute) consistently show that **design-phase defects cost 10×–100× less to fix** than implementation or post-deployment defects. Hence, the investment in quality design is non-negotiable in professional practice. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Design Process — Stepwise Logic The design activity is **iterative**, not linear. Each step refines and feeds back to the previous one. ### Step 1: Review and Refine the Software Architecture - Begin with the *Data Design* (define major data abstractions, then refine into class hierarchies). - Move to the *Architectural Design* (identify architectural style: layered, client-server, microservice, pipe-filter). - Decompose into subsystems and allocate responsibilities. ### Step 2: Build Patterns-Driven Components - Apply **design patterns** (Gang-of-Four: Creational, Structural, Behavioural). - Choose **off-the-shelf components** where appropriate to avoid re-inventing. - Develop user-interface components that map naturally to user tasks. ### Step 3: Create Detailed Component Models - For each component, specify: - Processing logic (algorithms) - Data structures - Interface contracts (signatures, pre/post conditions) - Collaboration protocols (sequence/communication diagrams) ### Step 4: Quality-Driven Refinement (the "Why" Loop) Refine using **quality attributes** as the *fitness function*: - **Functional correctness**, **usability**, **reliability**, **performance**, **maintainability**, **portability**. ### Step 5: Inspection & Verification - Conduct **formal technical reviews (FTRs)** of the design model. - Walk-throughs and inspections catch defects before a single line of code is written. > [!TIP] > KTU questions often ask: *"Why is the design step iterative?"* — The answer lies in Step 4: each refinement reveals flaws that necessitate revisiting earlier layers. --- ## 2.2 Design Concepts (The Foundational Principles) > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU syllabus **mandates** these concepts under Module 1. Memorise both definition and one-line application. | # | Concept | Definition | Design Application | |---|---------|-----------|--------------------| | 1 | **Abstraction** | Hiding lower-level details to focus on essential features | Procedural, Data, Control abstractions | | 2 | **Architecture** | Overall structure of software — components, connectors, constraints | Subsystem decomposition, control flow | | 3 | **Patterns** | Reusable solution to recurring problem in context | MVC, Observer, Factory, Strategy | | 4 | **Modularity** | Division of software into discrete named modules | High cohesion, low coupling | | 5 | **Information Hiding** | Hide implementation details behind interfaces | Encapsulation, data abstraction | | 6 | **Functional Independence** | Single-mindedness of a module (high cohesion + low coupling) | One function = one module | | 7 | **Refinement** | Successive elaboration of details at each level | Stepwise refinement (Wirth) | | 8 | **Refactoring** | Reorganising code/design without changing external behaviour | Eliminating redundancy, improving structure | ### 2.3 Modularity — The Most Quantifiable Concept Modularity is the **single most leveraged attribute** of software design. KTU frequently tests the relationship between number of modules, cost, and defect density. > [!NOTE] > **Cost vs Modules Curve:** As the number of modules increases, **development cost decreases** (parallel work, easier debugging) **but integration cost increases** (more interfaces to test). There is an **optimal number of modules** that minimises total cost. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!WARNING] > **Formatting Note:** All absolute-value and "divided-by" expressions use `\vert`, `\mid`, or `\big/` to keep the markdown table well-formed. **Never** use the bare `|` inside a table cell. | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning of Variables | Unit / Range | |---|---------|----------------------|----------------------|--------------| | 1 | **Coupling** (qualitative rank) | $C = \text{type and strength of inter-module connection}$ | $C \in \{\text{None}, \text{Data}, \text{Stamp}, \text{Control}, \text{External}, \text{Common}, \text{Content}\}$ | Categorical | | 2 | **Cohesion** (qualitative rank) | $H = \text{strength of intra-module functional bonding}$ | $H \in \{\text{Coincidental}, \text{Logical}, \text{Time}, \text{Procedural}, \text{Communicational}, \text{Sequential}, \text{Functional}\}$ | Categorical | | 3 | **Total Cost of Software** | $T_c = D_c + I_c + M_c$ | $D_c$ = development cost, $I_c$ = integration cost, $M_c$ = maintenance cost | Money / Person-month | | 4 | **Cost vs. Modules** | $T_c(n) = D_c(n) + I_c(n)$ where $D_c \downarrow$ and $I_c \uparrow$ with $n$ | $n$ = number of modules | Money | | 5 | **Software Reuse Ratio** | $R_r = \dfrac{\text{lines reused}}{\text{total lines of code}}$ | Fraction of reused code | Dimensionless $\left[0,1\right]$ | | 6 | **Mean Time To Failure** | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate | Hours | | 7 | **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$ | $MTTR$ = mean time to repair | Dimensionless $\left[0,1\right]$ | | 8 | **Cyclomatic Complexity** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components in control flow graph | Integer $\geq 1$ | | 9 | **Design Stability Index** (qualitative) | $S_d = \dfrac{\text{modules unchanged after review}}{\text{total modules reviewed}}$ | Ratio of stable modules | Dimensionless $\left[0,1\right]$ | | 10 | **Fan-in / Fan-out** | $FI = $ callers, $FO = $ callees | Useful for spotting over-centralised modules | Integer | > [!TIP] > **Coupling ranking — least → most costly:** *None < Data < Stamp < Control < External < Common < Content*. KTU always tests this in order. **Content coupling** = worst (direct modification of another's data). > [!TIP] > **Cohesion ranking — worst → best:** *Coincidental < Logical < Temporal < Procedural < Communicational < Sequential < Functional*. Aim for **Functional** cohesion always. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility These concepts are not academic — they drive **concrete engineering decisions**: - **Abstraction** → API contract design (e.g., REST endpoints, Java interfaces). - **Modularity** → Microservices in cloud-native systems. - **Information Hiding** → `private` fields, encapsulation in OOP. - **Patterns** → Spring Boot (Factory + DI), React (Observer state), Kafka (Pub-Sub). - **Refactoring** → Continuous improvement in Agile sprints. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** *Functional independence* is treated as a **measurable** goal. The two key measurable dimensions are **cohesion (high)** and **coupling (low)**. They are not independent: increasing cohesion typically reduces coupling. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Why "More Modules" is Not Always Better Let us formally derive the existence of an **optimal module count** $n^*$. ### Step 1: Define the two opposing cost curves. - **Development cost per module** decreases as $n$ increases (due to **division of labour**, parallel work, and reduced individual complexity). - **Integration cost** increases with $n$ (more interfaces, more wiring, more inter-module tests). ### Step 2: Choose a mathematical model. A standard textbook approximation (used in KTU reference material) is: $$ D_c(n) = k_1 \cdot \dfrac{1}{n^{\alpha}} $$ $$ I_c(n) = k_2 \cdot n^{\beta} $$ where $k_1, k_2$ are positive constants, $\alpha, \beta \in (0, 1)$ are sensitivity exponents. ### Step 3: Total cost function. $$ T_c(n) = D_c(n) + I_c(n) = k_1 \cdot n^{-\alpha} + k_2 \cdot n^{\beta} $$ ### Step 4: Minimise $T_c(n)$ by differentiating with respect to $n$ and setting equal to zero. $$ \dfrac{dT_c}{dn} = -\alpha \, k_1 \, n^{-\alpha - 1} + \beta \, k_2 \, n^{\beta - 1} = 0 $$ ### Step 5: Solve the derivative equation. $$ \alpha \, k_1 \, n^{-\alpha - 1} = \beta \, k_2 \, n^{\beta - 1} $$ $$ \dfrac{\alpha \, k_1}{\beta \, k_2} = n^{\beta - 1} \cdot n^{\alpha + 1} = n^{\alpha + \beta} $$ ### Step 6: Solve for the optimal $n$. $$ n^{*} = \left( \dfrac{\alpha \, k_1}{\beta \, k_2} \right)^{\dfrac{1}{\alpha + \beta}} $$ ### Step 7: Interpretation. - If $k_1$ is large (development is expensive), $n^*$ increases → modularise more. - If $k_2$ is large (integration is expensive), $n^*$ decreases → use fewer, larger modules. - The exponent $\dfrac{1}{\alpha + \beta}$ moderates how strongly $k_1, k_2$ influence $n^*$. > [!NOTE] > This derivation is **not a KTU compulsory proof** but is a high-yield analytical answer when asked *"Discuss the cost versus modularity trade-off with a mathematical justification."* --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Cyclomatic Complexity of a Control Flow Graph Cyclomatic complexity $V(G)$ is one of the most-cited design metrics. Its derivation uses graph theory (Euler's formula). ### Step 1: Begin with Euler's formula for a connected planar graph. $$ V - E + F = 2 $$ For a flow graph, the outer face represents the *outside world*, so $F = P + 1$ where $P$ is the number of connected components (usually 1 for a single routine). ### Step 2: Substitute and simplify. $$ N - E + (P + 1) = 2 $$ $$ E - N + 1 = P - 1 $$ ### Step 3: Add 1 to both sides to get the standard form. $$ E - N + 2 = P + 1 $$ $$ V(G) = E - N + 2P $$ ### Step 4: Alternative form using predicates. $$ V(G) = \pi + 1 $$ where $\pi$ is the number of predicate (decision) nodes in the flow graph. ### Step 5: Worked numerical example. Consider a flow graph with $N = 6$ nodes, $E = 9$ edges, $P = 1$ connected component. $$ V(G) = 9 - 6 + 2(1) = 5 $$ Equivalently, if the flow graph has 4 predicate nodes: $V(G) = 4 + 1 = 5$. Both match. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Threshold Rule (Industry standard):** *If $V(G) > 10$, the component is too complex and must be re-designed.* KTU examiners often cite this rule. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Design Concepts The matrix below cross-references design concepts against quality attributes. This is a high-yield KTU chart: | Design Concept →
Quality ↓ | Abstraction | Architecture | Patterns | Modularity | Information Hiding | Functional Independence | |-------------------------------|:-----------:|:------------:|:--------:|:----------:|:------------------:|:-----------------------:| | **Correctness** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | | **Maintainability** | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | | **Reusability** | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | | **Testability** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | | **Performance** | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | | **Security** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | | **Portability** | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Legend: ✓ = supports, ✓✓ = strongly supports, ✓✓✓ = critical enabler, ✗ = no direct effect. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Answers:** *Modularity* and *Functional Independence* are the only concepts that broadly improve **all** quality attributes (except raw performance, which often *suffers* slightly due to function-call overhead). --- ## 3.4 Worked Example — Evaluating a Module's Coupling & Cohesion **Module M** performs the following operations when called: 1. Reads a record from file A. 2. Calculates an employee's gross salary. 3. Updates a global tax-rate table. 4. Prints a payslip. ### Analysis - **(1), (2), (3), (4)** are unrelated to one another — this is **Coincidental Cohesion** (worst kind). - **(3) Updates a global table** — this is **Common Coupling** (any module can modify that table — a hazard). - **(4) Prints output** — this is **Content/External coupling** if it directly manipulates the printer buffer. ### Redesign Split into four modules: - `ReadEmployee` (functional cohesion — single read operation). - `ComputeGross` (functional cohesion — pure calculation). - `UpdateTaxTable` (controlled via parameter, no globals — eliminates Common coupling). - `PrintPaySlip` (pass `data` by parameter — reduces Content coupling to Data coupling). > [!TIP] > **KTU trap:** Students often confuse **Common coupling** with **Control coupling**. *Common* = shared global data. *Control* = passing flags to alter behaviour of callee. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Software Design Process The following Mermaid block diagram captures the full **end-to-end design and implementation pipeline** as a functional architecture. It deliberately avoids physical layout (which cannot be rendered in Mermaid) and emphasises the *information flow* between design activities. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Requirements Specification] --> B[Data Class Design] B --> C[Architectural Style Selection] C --> D[Subsystem Decomposition] D --> E[Component Level Design] E --> F[Interface and UI Design] F --> G[Design Review and Inspection] G --> H{Defects Found?} H -- Yes --> I[Iterative Refinement Loop] I --> B I --> C I --> D I --> E I --> F H -- No --> J[Implementation Phase] J --> K[Coding with Standards] K --> L[Unit Testing] L --> M[Integration Testing] M --> N[Software Product] N --> O[Maintenance and Evolution] O -. feedback .-> A ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The top half (A → G) is the **Design Phase**. - The gate `H` is a **quality gate** — design review decides whether to loop back or proceed. - The bottom half (J → N) is the **Implementation Phase**. - The dotted feedback line `.->` shows **maintenance** feeding new requirements back into the cycle. --- ## 4.2 Coupling vs Cohesion — Comparative Block Diagram The following diagram maps the **seven levels** of coupling and cohesion side-by-side, from best to worst. This is a frequently-asked KTU diagram. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BEST["Best"] C1[Data Coupling] C2[Stamp Coupling] C3[Control Coupling] C4[External Coupling] C5[Common Coupling] C6[Content Coupling] C7[No Direct Coupling] end subgraph BEST2["Best to Worst"] H1[Functional Cohesion] H2[Sequential Cohesion] H3[Communicational Cohesion] H4[Procedural Cohesion] H5[Temporal Cohesion] H6[Logical Cohesion] H7[Coincidental Cohesion] end ``` > [!NOTE] > In Mermaid, subgraphs render in declaration order top-to-bottom. The reader should mentally label each block by its index (1 = best, 7 = worst in both columns). This is the standard KTU evaluation ladder. --- ## 4.3 Layered Design View (Block Architecture) This diagram captures the **four design layers** (data, architecture, interface, component) and shows how they refine a single requirement specification. ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Requirements Model] --> L1[Layer 1: Data and Class Design] R --> L2[Layer 2: Architectural Design] R --> L3[Layer 3: Interface Design] R --> L4[Layer 4: Component Level Design] L1 --> V[Validation against Quality Attributes] L2 --> V L3 --> V L4 --> V V --> Q{Quality Gate} Q -- Pass --> I[Implementation] Q -- Fail --> R ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why four layers, not three or five?** The four layers correspond to four *kinds* of design knowledge: data abstractions, structural topology, communication contracts, and algorithmic detail. KTU 2024 explicitly tests this four-layer division. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` > **Q1.** Differentiate between **coupling** and **cohesion** in software design. Why are they considered two sides of the same coin? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Coupling** is the measure of *inter-module* interdependence — it describes how strongly one module is connected to another. *Lower coupling is better.* - **Cohesion** is the measure of *intra-module* functional strength — it describes how closely the responsibilities inside a single module are related. *Higher cohesion is better.* - They are two sides of the same coin because **increasing the cohesion of a module automatically reduces its coupling to other modules**. A module that does one thing well does not need to share data, control, or content with many other modules. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students swap the definitions. *Cohesion = inside a module, Coupling = between modules.* Examiners deduct 1 mark instantly for this reversal. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` > **Q2.** What is **information hiding**? Explain its role in achieving functional independence. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Information hiding** (Parnas, 1972) is the design principle that the *implementation details* of a module — its data structures, algorithms, internal decisions — must be hidden from other modules. Only the module's **interface** (signatures, contracts) is exposed. - **Role in functional independence:** 1. Forces designers to specify clean **public interfaces**. 2. Decouples the *what* (interface) from the *how* (implementation), so each module can evolve independently. 3. Reduces *propagation of changes* — internal modifications do not ripple across the system. 4. Enables **separate compilation, testing, and replacement** of modules, supporting maintainability. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Do not equate information hiding with "data hiding" alone. Information hiding covers **all** internal details, including algorithms and control flow. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, candidates answer **one full question** (with sub-parts a and b). Two alternatives provided below. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` > **Q(A). (a)** Explain the **software design process** in detail. Describe the layered approach to design with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)** > > **Q(A). (b)** Discuss in detail the **seven levels of cohesion** in software design. For each level, give one example and indicate the design quality. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q(A)(a): The software design process is a **multi-step, iterative engineering activity** that translates requirements into a verifiable, high-quality design representation. The standard KTU-aligned process is: 1. **Review and refine the software architecture** — Begin by reviewing the data design (data abstractions, class hierarchies) and architectural design (subsystems, control flow, allocation of responsibilities). Refine iteratively. 2. **Apply design patterns and reuse** — Identify recurring design problems and apply established *Gang-of-Four patterns* (Creational, Structural, Behavioural). Reuse existing components to reduce cost and risk. 3. **Construct component-level models** — For each module, specify processing logic, data structures, interface contracts, and collaboration protocols using UML diagrams (class, sequence, state). 4. **Refine using quality attributes** — Use functional correctness, reliability, usability, performance, and maintainability as the *fitness function*. Iterate until acceptable. 5. **Conduct formal technical reviews (FTRs)** — Use walkthroughs and inspections to detect defects *before* implementation begins. **The Layered Approach** (refer to Section 4.3 diagram): - **Layer 1 — Data/Class Design:** Translates analysis classes into implementation classes; refines data abstractions and identifies class hierarchies. - **Layer 2 — Architectural Design:** Establishes the overall structure — subsystem decomposition, control style, deployment topology. - **Layer 3 — Interface Design:** Specifies human-machine interfaces (UI) and inter-module interfaces (APIs, contracts). - **Layer 4 — Component-Level Design:** Defines the algorithmic and data structure details of each module. > All four layers undergo **continuous refinement** and are validated against quality attributes before transitioning to implementation. *(7 marks: 4 marks process + 3 marks layered diagram and explanation)* #### Model Solution for Q(A)(b): | # | Cohesion Level | Description | Example | Quality | |---|---------------|-------------|---------|---------| | 1 | **Functional** | All elements contribute to a *single, well-defined task*. | `computeTax(income)` returns tax. | **Excellent** | | 2 | **Sequential** | Output of one element is input to the next. | `readRecord → validate → writeLog`. | **Very Good** | | 3 | **Communicational** | Elements operate on the same input data or produce the same output. | `formatReport(empData)` + `printReport(empData)`. | **Good** | | 4 | **Procedural** | Elements must be executed in a fixed order, but are otherwise unrelated. | `init → readUser → startEngine`. | **Moderate** | | 5 | **Temporal** | Elements are executed at the *same time* (e.g., startup, shutdown). | `initLogger(); loadConfig(); openDB();` | **Weak** | | 6 | **Logical** | Elements perform similar functions, selected by a control flag. | `process(input, mode)` where `mode` picks one of many. | **Poor** | | 7 | **Coincidental** | Elements are arbitrarily grouped; no meaningful relationship. | A module that reads, prints, updates DB, sends mail. | **Worst** | **Design Quality Direction:** Functional → Coincidental is *decreasing* quality. Always target Functional cohesion; flag Logical and Coincidental modules for refactoring. *(7 marks: 1 mark introduction + 6 marks table with examples)* > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often miss the *Sequential vs Communicational* distinction. *Sequential = pipe of data; Communicational = same data set used but no pipe.* Examiners look for this subtlety. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]` > **Q(B). (a)** Explain the **seven levels of coupling** with examples. State which is the most desirable and which is the least desirable form. **(7 marks)** > > **Q(B). (b)** What is **modularity**? Derive the relationship between cost and number of modules. Justify the existence of an optimal number of modules. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q(B)(a): | # | Coupling Level | Description | Example | Quality | |---|---------------|-------------|---------|---------| | 1 | **No Direct Coupling** | Modules communicate *only* through parameters; no shared data, no flags. | `sort(arr, comparator)` — pure data. | **Best** | | 2 | **Data Coupling** | Parameters are simple data items passed by value. | `add(a, b)` returns sum. | **Good** | | 3 | **Stamp Coupling** | A whole data *record* is passed, but only a few fields are used. | Passing full `Customer` object when only `id` is needed. | **Acceptable** | | 4 | **Control Coupling** | A *flag* or control variable dictates internal flow of callee. | `printReport(report, isSummary)`. | **Bad** | | 5 | **External Coupling** | Modules share an *external data format* (e.g., file layout, protocol). | Both modules parse the same XML schema. | **Bad** | | 6 | **Common Coupling** | Modules share *global variables*. | Two modules both write to `globalCounter`. | **Worse** | | 7 | **Content Coupling** | One module *directly modifies* the internals of another. | Module A reaches into Module B's private data. | **Worst** | - **Most desirable:** No Direct Coupling / Data Coupling. - **Least desirable:** Content Coupling. - **Design rule:** Always refactor Content, Common, and External couplings into Data or Stamp couplings. *(7 marks: 6 marks table + 1 mark summary)* #### Model Solution for Q(B)(b): **Definition (1 mark):** *Modularity* is the property of a software system that has been decomposed into a set of cohesive, loosely-coupled modules. Each module is a named, addressable, separately compilable unit of code. **Cost vs. Number of Modules (6 marks derivation):** - **Development cost** $D_c(n)$ per module **decreases** with $n$ because smaller modules are easier to design, code, test, and assign to developers in parallel. - **Integration cost** $I_c(n)$ **increases** with $n$ because more modules means more interfaces to wire, test, and validate. - **Total cost** $T_c(n) = D_c(n) + I_c(n)$ exhibits a **U-shaped curve** with a unique minimum at $n^*$. Using the analytical model (from Section 3.1): $$ D_c(n) = k_1 \, n^{-\alpha}, \quad I_c(n) = k_2 \, n^{\beta} $$ $$ T_c(n) = k_1 \, n^{-\alpha} + k_2 \, n^{\beta} $$ Setting $\dfrac{dT_c}{dn} = 0$: $$ n^{*} = \left( \dfrac{\alpha \, k_1}{\beta \, k_2} \right)^{\dfrac{1}{\alpha + \beta}} $$ **Justification of $n^*$:** - If $n < n^*$ → modules are too large; subdivide further to reduce $D_c$. - If $n > n^*$ → modules are too granular; consolidate to reduce $I_c$. - At $n = n^*$ → total cost is minimised and parallel development is optimised. - The trade-off is fundamental and **cannot be eliminated**, only balanced. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** When asked for a derivation, **never skip the differentiation step**. Many students jump from the cost equation to the conclusion without showing $\dfrac{dT_c}{dn} = 0$. This costs 2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-minute revision bullet sheet — read this 30 minutes before the exam.** ### Foundational Definitions - **Software Design:** *Transforms requirements into a verifiable representation of the software, identifying components, relationships, and structure.* - **Software Implementation:** *Translates design representation into executable code, following standards and unit-test discipline.* - **Abstraction:** *Hiding lower-level details to focus on essential features.* - **Architecture:** *Overall structure — components, connectors, constraints.* - **Pattern:** *Proven, reusable solution to a recurring problem in a context.* - **Modularity:** *Decomposition into discrete, named, addressable units.* - **Information Hiding:** *Concealing implementation details behind a public interface.* - **Functional Independence:** *High cohesion + low coupling of every module.* - **Refinement:** *Successive elaboration of detail across design levels.* - **Refactoring:** *Reorganising code/design without altering external behaviour.* ### Coupling Order (Best → Worst) `None / Data → Stamp → Control → External → Common → Content` *(memorise verbatim)* ### Cohesion Order (Worst → Best) `Coincidental → Logical → Temporal → Procedural → Communicational → Sequential → Functional` *(memorise verbatim)* ### Key Metrics - **Cyclomatic Complexity:** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ or $V(G) = \pi + 1$. - **Industry threshold:** $V(G) > 10$ → refactor. - **Availability:** $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$. - **Reuse ratio:** $R_r = \dfrac{\text{reused lines}}{\text{total lines}}$. ### Four Design Layers (Data → Architecture → Interface → Component) - Each layer refines the previous; all are validated against **quality attributes**. ### Common Traps - **Content coupling** ≠ **Common coupling** — *direct modification* vs *shared global data*. - **Sequential cohesion** ≠ **Communicational cohesion** — *pipe of data* vs *same dataset*. - **Information hiding** ≠ **encapsulation** alone — covers algorithms, not just data. - **Refinement** ≠ **Refactoring** — *top-down design* vs *behaviour-preserving restructure*. ### Top Three Examiner Favourite Questions 1. *"Explain the seven levels of coupling with examples."* (Always comes.) 2. *"What is functional independence? Why is it desirable?"* (Conceptual + metrics.) 3. *"Discuss the cost vs. modularity trade-off."* (Derivation-type question.) > **All the best for your KTU 2024 Semester Exam! 🚀**

topics.title: Software Validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Software Validation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 – Software Validation

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU / IEEE 2024 Alignment)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Validation** is defined as the process of evaluating software during or at the end of the development life cycle to determine whether it satisfies the specified **business requirements, user needs, and intended use**. It answers the question: *"Are we building the right product?"*

The **IEEE 1012 Standard for Software Verification and Validation** formally defines:

- **Verification:** *"The process of evaluating a system or component to determine whether the products of a given development phase satisfy the conditions imposed at the start of that phase."* (i.e., *Are we building the product right?*)
- **Validation:** *"The process of evaluating a system or component during or at the end of the development process to determine whether it satisfies specified requirements."* (i.e., *Are we building the right product?*)

> [!NOTE]
> In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 OBE framework)**, Software Validation is mapped to **Course Outcome CO1 / CO2** of the Software Engineering syllabus, requiring students to demonstrate the ability to *apply verification & validation techniques* on real-world software projects.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect building a hospital**:

- **Verification** is like checking the **blueprints at every stage** — does the foundation match the drawing? Are the walls plumb? Are the electrical layouts per code? *You are confirming that each phase is built correctly.*
- **Validation** is like **walking a patient through the finished hospital** — can the patient find the reception easily? Does the signage work? Are the wards comfortable? *You are confirming that the final product actually serves its intended purpose.*

> [!TIP]
> A product can be **verified correctly** (built right) but still **fail validation** (wrong product). A bridge can be structurally perfect (verified) but connect the wrong two cities (invalid). This is the single most important distinction KTU examiners test.

### Key Terminology & Metrics (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Defect** | A flaw in software that *may* cause failure |
| **Error** | A human mistake that produces a defect |
| **Failure** | Manifested deviation of software from expected behavior |
| **Bug** | Informal synonym for defect |
| **Reliability** | Probability of failure-free operation for a specified time |
| **MTTF** | Mean Time To Failure (in hours) |
| **Defect Density** | Defects per thousand lines of code (KLOC) |
| **Test Coverage** | Percentage of code/requirements exercised by tests |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners **deduct marks** if students interchange "error," "defect," and "failure." Memorize the chain: **Human makes Error → Error creates Defect → Defect causes Failure**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 1.2 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 1.2.1 The V-Model of Software Validation (Extension of Waterfall)

The **V-Model** (also called the *Verification & Validation Model*) is the canonical KTU-diagram for this module. Each development phase on the left leg has a corresponding testing phase on the right leg.

**Left Leg – Development (Verification):**
1. Requirements Analysis → User Requirements
2. System Design → System Architecture
3. Architectural Design → High-Level Design
4. Module Design → Detailed Design
5. Coding → Source Code

**Right Leg – Testing (Validation):**
1. Unit Testing (validates Module Design)
2. Integration Testing (validates Architectural Design)
3. System Testing (validates System Design)
4. Acceptance Testing (validates User Requirements)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The V-Model is **NOT a separate life cycle** — it is the testing extension of the Waterfall model. The horizontal line at the bottom represents the **coding phase**, which is the inflection point.

---

### 1.2.2 Levels of Software Testing (KTU Mandatory)

| Test Level | Who Performs | What It Validates | Granularity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit Testing** | Developer | Individual functions/methods/modules | Fine |
| **Integration Testing** | Developer / QA Team | Interaction between modules | Medium |
| **System Testing** | Independent QA Team | Complete integrated system | Coarse |
| **Acceptance Testing** | End User / Client | Business & user requirements | Coarse |

**Sub-categories of Integration Testing:**
- **Top-Down Integration** → Stubs used for lower modules; depth-first/breadth-first traversal.
- **Bottom-Up Integration** → Drivers used for upper modules; clusters built first.
- **Sandwich (Hybrid) Integration** → Combination of top-down and bottom-up; uses both stubs and drivers.

---

### 1.2.3 Types of Software Testing

**A. Functional Testing**
- Unit Testing
- Integration Testing
- System Testing
- Acceptance Testing (UAT – User Acceptance Testing, Alpha, Beta)
- Smoke Testing, Sanity Testing
- Regression Testing
- Recovery Testing, Security Testing

**B. Non-Functional Testing**
- Performance Testing (Load, Stress, Spike, Volume)
- Usability Testing
- Compatibility Testing
- Reliability Testing
- Localization / Internationalization Testing

**C. Structural / White-Box Testing**
- Statement Coverage
- Branch (Decision) Coverage
- Cyclomatic Complexity based path testing
- Condition / Multiple-Condition Coverage

**D. Functional / Black-Box Testing**
- Equivalence Partitioning
- Boundary Value Analysis
- Decision Table Testing
- State Transition Testing
- Use-Case Testing
- Error Guessing

---

### 1.2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **frequently asked in KTU Numerical/Analytical questions**. Memorize the boundary conditions and units.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V(G) = E - N + 2$ | Cyclomatic complexity from Control Flow Graph | $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $V(G)$ = independent paths |
| 2 | $V(G) = P + 1$ | Cyclomatic complexity from predicate nodes | $P$ = number of predicate (decision) nodes |
| 3 | $V(G) = R$ | Cyclomatic complexity = number of enclosed regions in CFG | $R$ = regions |
| 4 | $\text{Defect Density} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Size (KLOC)}}$ | Bugs per thousand lines of code | KLOC = Kilo Lines of Code |
| 5 | $\text{Test Coverage (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{Items Tested}}{\text{Total Items}} \times 100$ | Coverage metric | Items = statements, branches, paths |
| 6 | $\text{Statement Coverage} = \dfrac{\text{Statements Executed}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100$ | White-box metric | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Branch Coverage} = \dfrac{\text{Branches Executed}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100$ | White-box metric | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Mean Time To Failure | Hours |
| 9 | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | Hours |
| 10 | $\text{Reliability} \, R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential reliability | $\lambda$ = failure rate |
| 11 | $\text{Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)} = \dfrac{\text{Defects Removed in Phase}}{\text{Defects Present at Start}} \times 100$ | Process metric | Percentage |

> [!TIP]
> All three cyclomatic-complexity formulas ($E - N + 2$, $P + 1$, and $R$) **must yield the same value** for the same graph. KTU questions often ask you to verify this consistency.

---

### 1.2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **aerospace and medical-device industry** (ISO 26262, DO-178C, IEC 62304), V&V is legally mandated. The V-Model is the de-facto standard because every requirement on the left **must be traceable to a test on the right** — this is called **Bidirectional Traceability**. KTU graduates working in embedded systems, automotive software, or medical software routinely use this matrix.

---

### 1.2.6 Comparison Matrix: Verification vs. Validation (Frequently Asked 6-Mark Question)

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| **Question Answered** | Are we building the product right? | Are we building the right product? |
| **Phase** | During development (in-process) | After development (end-process) |
| **Activity Type** | Static (reviews, walkthroughs, inspections) | Dynamic (execution-based testing) |
| **Inputs** | Plans, designs, code, requirements | Executable code / system |
| **Outputs** | Inspection reports, review logs | Test results, defect reports |
| **Tools** | Checklists, static analyzers | Test runners, automation frameworks |
| **Performed By** | Developers, QA, peers | Testers, end users |
| **Cost of Fix** | Low (early in cycle) | High (late in cycle) |
| **Bohm's View** | "Building the system right" | "Building the right system" |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 1.3 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Worked Example 1: Cyclomatic Complexity using Control Flow Graph (CFG)

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 6/7-Mark Question):**

> Compute the **Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G)$ of the following pseudocode using the Control Flow Graph method. Verify your answer using the **three independent formulas**.

```
1. PROCEDURE CalcGrade(marks)
2.   BEGIN
3.     IF marks >= 90 THEN
4.        grade = 'A'
5.     ELSE IF marks >= 60 THEN
6.        grade = 'B'
7.     ELSE IF marks >= 40 THEN
8.        grade = 'C'
9.     ELSE
10.       grade = 'F'
11.    END IF
12.    PRINT grade
13. END
```

---

**Step 1 – Identify the Nodes (N) of the CFG**

A node represents either a **process statement** or a **decision (predicate) point**. Let us label them:

- Node 1 → Entry point
- Node 2 → `IF marks >= 90` (Decision)
- Node 3 → `grade = 'A'`
- Node 4 → `ELSE IF marks >= 60` (Decision)
- Node 5 → `grade = 'B'`
- Node 6 → `ELSE IF marks >= 40` (Decision)
- Node 7 → `grade = 'C'`
- Node 8 → `grade = 'F'`
- Node 9 → `PRINT grade`
- Node 10 → Exit point

$$
\therefore N = 10
$$

---

**Step 2 – Identify the Edges (E) of the CFG**

Edges are directed arrows showing flow of control:

1 → 2, 2 → 3, 2 → 4, 3 → 4, 4 → 5, 4 → 6, 5 → 6, 6 → 7, 6 → 8, 7 → 8, 8 → 9, 9 → 10

$$
\therefore E = 12
$$

---

**Step 3 – Apply Formula 1: $V(G) = E - N + 2$**

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2
$$

$$
V(G) = 12 - 10 + 2
$$

$$
V(G) = 4
$$

---

**Step 4 – Verify using Formula 2: $V(G) = P + 1$**

The predicate (decision) nodes are at lines 3, 5, 7 — three IF/ELSE IF statements.

$$
P = 3
$$

$$
V(G) = P + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4
$$

---

**Step 5 – Verify using Formula 3: $V(G) = R$ (Regions)**

Draw the CFG and count enclosed regions plus the outer region:

- Region 1: `grade = 'A'`
- Region 2: `grade = 'B'`
- Region 3: `grade = 'C'`
- Region 4: outer (everything else)

$$
V(G) = R = 4
$$

---

**Step 6 – Interpret the Result**

$$
V(G) = 4
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU Answer:** The cyclomatic complexity of the procedure is **4**. This means the **minimum number of independent test cases** required to exercise every path in the program is **4**. For a KTU 7-mark question, present this as the *Minimum Test Case Count = 4*.

---

### Worked Example 2: Defect Density Calculation (Frequently Asked 3/4-Mark Numerical)

**Problem:** A software project has 45,000 lines of code. During the testing phase, **135 defects** are detected. Compute the **Defect Density**.

**Step 1 – Convert LOC to KLOC:**

$$
\text{Size} = \frac{45000}{1000} = 45 \text{ KLOC}
$$

**Step 2 – Apply the Defect Density Formula:**

$$
\text{Defect Density} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Size (KLOC)}}
$$

$$
\text{Defect Density} = \frac{135}{45} = 3.0 \text{ defects / KLOC}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Industry benchmark for **well-tested software**: 0.5 to 2.0 defects/KLOC. A value of 3.0 indicates the module has **higher-than-acceptable defect concentration** and requires additional testing.

---

### Worked Example 3: Statement and Branch Coverage (White-Box Metric Derivation)

**Problem:** Consider the following snippet with 2 decision statements and 1 compound condition.

```
IF (a > 0 AND b > 0) THEN
    x = 1
ELSE
    x = 0
```

**Step 1 – Statement Count:** There are **3 statements** in the path: `IF`, assignment to `x=1`, assignment to `x=0`. The `IF` itself counts as 1.

**Step 2 – Branch Count:** Two branches (THEN, ELSE) → 2 branches.

**Step 3 – Apply Coverage Formulas:**

$$
\text{Statement Coverage} = \frac{\text{Statements Executed}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Branch Coverage} = \frac{\text{Branches Executed}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A test set that covers 100% of branches *automatically* covers 100% of statements, but the **converse is not true**. KTU exams love this property — write it down in your own words.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 1.4 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 1.4.1 The V-Model of Software Validation

```mermaid
graph TD
    REQ["User Requirements"] --> SYS["System Design"]
    REQ2["Acceptance Test Plan"]
    SYS --> ARC["Architectural Design"]
    SYS2["System Test Plan"]
    ARC --> MOD["Module Design"]
    ARC2["Integration Test Plan"]
    MOD --> COD["Coding"]
    MOD3["Unit Test Plan"]
    COD --> UNIT["Unit Testing"]
    MOD3 --> UNIT
    UNIT --> INT["Integration Testing"]
    ARC2 --> INT
    INT --> SYS3["System Testing"]
    SYS2 --> SYS3
    SYS3 --> ACC["Acceptance Testing"]
    REQ2 --> ACC
    ACC --> DEL["Delivered Product"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each *Test Plan* on the right is prepared **in parallel** with its corresponding development phase on the left. This is the core principle of the V-Model and the source of most 14-mark KTU questions.

---

### 1.4.2 Verification & Validation Relationship Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    VV["V AND V ENGINEERING"]
    VV --> V["VERIFICATION"]
    VV --> VA["VALIDATION"]
    V --> V1["Are we building the product RIGHT?"]
    V --> V2["Static Techniques"]
    V --> V3["Reviews / Walkthroughs / Inspections"]
    V --> V4["Done During Development"]
    VA --> VA1["Are we building the RIGHT product?"]
    VA --> VA2["Dynamic Techniques"]
    VA --> VA3["Testing / Execution"]
    VA --> VA4["Done After Build"]
```

---

### 1.4.3 Test Pyramid — Levels of Testing Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    TOP["Acceptance Testing — User Validates Business Needs"]
    MID["System Testing — Independent QA Validates Full System"]
    CORE["Integration Testing — Validates Module Interactions"]
    BASE["Unit Testing — Developer Validates Individual Units"]
    TOP --> MID
    MID --> CORE
    CORE --> BASE
```

> [!TIP]
> The pyramid shape is intentional: **the volume of tests at the base is huge**, but each test is fast, cheap, and isolated. The tip (acceptance) is **small in volume** but **highest in business value**. This is the principle KTU examiners expect in 7-mark "testing strategy" answers.

---

### 1.4.4 Test Process Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Test Planning"] --> B["Test Case Design"]
    B --> C["Test Environment Setup"]
    C --> D["Test Execution"]
    D --> E["Defect Reporting"]
    E --> F{"Defect Fixed?"}
    F -- No --> D
    F -- Yes --> G["Test Closure"]
    G --> H["Test Metrics Analysis"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This loop represents the **iterative nature of testing**. KTU frequently asks: *"Why is testing iterative?"* The answer lies in the **re-execution of failed test cases** (regression) after each defect fix.

---

### 1.4.5 White-Box vs. Black-Box Testing — Architectural Contrast

```mermaid
graph TB
    WB["WHITE BOX TESTING"]
    WB --> WB1["Internal Code Knowledge Required"]
    WB --> WB2["Tests Internal Structure"]
    WB --> WB3["Techniques: Statement Branch Path Coverage"]

    BB["BLACK BOX TESTING"]
    BB --> BB1["No Code Knowledge Required"]
    BB --> BB2["Tests Functional Behavior"]
    BB --> BB3["Techniques: Equivalence Boundary Decision Table"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 1.5 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**Differentiate between Verification and Validation. State two activities for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Building the product **right** | Building the **right** product |
| Timing | During development (static) | After build (dynamic) |
| Activities | Reviews, inspections, walkthroughs, static analysis | Unit testing, integration testing, system testing, UAT |

> **Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the timing distinction, 1 mark for the activity list.**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**What is Regression Testing? Why is it necessary after every defect fix?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Regression testing is the re-execution of **previously passed test cases** to ensure that **new code changes (defect fixes or enhancements) have not broken existing functionality**. It is necessary because a defect fix in one module may **unintentionally introduce new defects** in related modules — this phenomenon is called *side-effect regression*. Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the re-execution concept, 1 mark for the side-effect justification.

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **V-Model of software development** with a neat diagram. List the four development phases on the left leg and the corresponding testing phases on the right leg. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain in detail the **White-Box Testing** and **Black-Box Testing** techniques. List at least three techniques under each category. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The V-Model is an extension of the Waterfall model in which **development and testing phases are planned in parallel**. Each development phase on the left leg has a corresponding validation phase on the right leg, joined by a coding phase at the bottom.

**Development Leg (Left):**
1. **Requirements Analysis** – gathering user requirements.
2. **System Design** – defining the overall architecture.
3. **Architectural / Detailed Design** – specifying modules and interfaces.
4. **Coding** – implementing modules.

**Validation Leg (Right):**
1. **Unit Testing** – validates individual modules.
2. **Integration Testing** – validates module interactions.
3. **System Testing** – validates the complete integrated system against specifications.
4. **Acceptance Testing** – validates the system against user / business needs.

The key principle is **bidirectional traceability**: every requirement on the left is traceable to a test on the right. This makes defect attribution easier and ensures completeness of validation.

*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 1.4.1 for the schematic. 5 marks for diagram + 2 marks for the textual explanation.)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**White-Box Testing (Structural Testing):** The tester has full knowledge of the internal code structure. Test cases are designed to exercise specific paths, branches, and conditions.

Techniques:
- **Statement Coverage** – ensure every executable statement runs at least once.
- **Branch (Decision) Coverage** – ensure every decision branch is taken.
- **Cyclomatic Complexity-based Path Testing** – derive the minimum number of independent paths.
- **Condition / Multiple-Condition Coverage** – test combinations of Boolean sub-conditions.

**Black-Box Testing (Functional Testing):** The tester treats the software as an opaque box; only inputs and outputs matter.

Techniques:
- **Equivalence Partitioning** – divide inputs into equivalent classes.
- **Boundary Value Analysis** – test values at, just inside, and just outside boundaries.
- **Decision Table Testing** – for complex business rules.
- **State Transition Testing** – for systems with finite states.
- **Error Guessing** – intuition-based test design.

*(Award 3 marks for white-box explanation, 3 marks for black-box explanation, 1 mark for the comparison.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Style]**

**(a)** What is **Cyclomatic Complexity**? Compute the cyclomatic complexity for the following control flow graph and state the **minimum number of test cases** required. *(7 Marks)*

**Given:** $E = 14$ edges, $N = 11$ nodes, $P = 3$ predicate nodes.

**(b)** Explain **Equivalence Partitioning** and **Boundary Value Analysis** with a suitable example. Differentiate between them. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Cyclomatic Complexity, denoted $V(G)$, is a software metric that quantifies the **number of linearly independent paths** through a program's source code. It was proposed by Thomas J. McCabe in 1976.

**Method 1 — Edge-Node Formula (2 Marks):**

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2
$$

$$
V(G) = 14 - 11 + 2
$$

$$
V(G) = 5
$$

**Method 2 — Predicate Formula (2 Marks):**

$$
V(G) = P + 1
$$

$$
V(G) = 3 + 1
$$

$$
V(G) = 4
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The two methods give different values (5 vs 4).** This is a common KTU exam pitfall. The examiner may have designed the CFG with the expectation that the *correct* value is **5**, and a student who blindly applies $P + 1$ without verifying will lose 2 marks. The verdict: always cross-verify all three formulas; if they disagree, recount the edges and predicate nodes carefully — usually one method is being misapplied.

**Conclusion (2 Marks):** The **minimum number of independent test cases** required to cover all paths equals the **highest verified value** of $V(G)$ — i.e., **5 test cases**. (The minimum test case count is **equal to the cyclomatic complexity**.)

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**
