> **Final Exam-Day Tip:** Spiral Model questions almost always need a **diagram + 4 quadrants + 1 real-world example**. Agile questions almost always need **2 specific methods + 1 comparison**. Memorize the comparison table in Section 2.4 — it has answered every KTU comparison question asked in the last 5 years.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Agile Manifesto - Values and Principles. Agile Development Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Agile Manifesto - Values and Principles. Agile Development Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: The Agile Manifesto

> [!NOTE]
> **Agile Software Development** is an *umbrella term* for a set of frameworks and practices based on the values and principles expressed in the **Agile Manifesto (2001)**. It is a **lightweight, iterative, and incremental** approach to software engineering that emphasizes **adaptive planning, evolutionary delivery, and continuous improvement**.

The **Agile Manifesto** was authored in **February 2001** by **17 software practitioners** at Snowbird, Utah (USA). It is the foundational charter of Agile Software Development and consists of:

1. **4 Core Values**
2. **12 Supporting Principles**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Manifesto does **not reject** the items on the right; it only states that the items on the **left are valued *more***. This is a critical distinction frequently tested in KTU exams.

### 1.2 The Four Core Values (Verbatim from the Manifesto)

| # | We Value (Higher) | Over (Lower) |
|---|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Individuals and Interactions** | Processes and Tools |
| 2 | **Working Software** | Comprehensive Documentation |
| 3 | **Customer Collaboration** | Contract Negotiation |
| 4 | **Responding to Change** | Following a Plan |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "Planning a Road Trip with Friends"** 🛣️
>
> Imagine you and three friends decide to drive from Kerala to Leh. The **Waterfall** approach is: lock the entire route 6 months in advance, book every hotel, and refuse to deviate even if a landslide blocks the road.
>
> The **Agile** approach is: agree on the *destination*, pack the car, and start driving. Every morning at breakfast, you check the map, ask the team, *"What's the best next stop?"*, and adjust. If a road is closed, you re-route *immediately* without filing a change-request form. The **goal (Leh)** stays constant; the **path** is negotiable.
>
> - **Individuals & interactions** → The people in the car matter more than the GPS device.
> - **Working software** → Actually *reaching* a beautiful viewpoint beats a perfect 50-page itinerary.
> - **Customer collaboration** → The travellers (customers) give daily feedback on food, photos, fatigue.
> - **Responding to change** → When it snows, you change the plan; you don't stick to the July plan blindly.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Key Terms (KTU Vocabulary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Terms to Memorize:**
> - **Sprint / Iteration**: A short (typically **1–4 weeks**) time-boxed development cycle.
> - **Product Backlog**: A *prioritized* list of features/stories.
> - **Velocity**: Number of story points completed per sprint (measured in **story points/sprint**).
> - **Burn-down Chart**: A graph of remaining work vs. time.
> - **Daily Stand-up (Scrum)**: A **15-minute** synchronization meeting.
> - **User Story**: A requirement expressed as: *"As a \<user\>, I want \<feature\>, so that \<benefit\>."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The 12 Principles of the Agile Manifesto (Detailed)

> [!NOTE]
> These 12 principles are derived directly from the 4 values. KTU frequently asks students to **list and explain** at least 4–6 principles. Memorize the keywords in **bold**.

**Principle 1 — Highest Priority: Customer Satisfaction via Early & Continuous Delivery**
- The primary measure of success is delivering *valuable* software to the customer **early and continuously**.
- Justification: Customers gain ROI sooner; feedback loop shortens.

**Principle 2 — Welcome Changing Requirements, Even Late in Development**
- Agile processes are designed to **harness change** for the customer's competitive advantage.
- Contrast with Waterfall where late changes are penalized.

**Principle 3 — Deliver Working Software Frequently (Weeks, Not Months)**
- Preferred timescale: between **2 weeks and 2 months**.
- Encourages short feedback cycles.

**Principle 4 — Business People & Developers Must Work Together Daily**
- Eliminates the "us vs. them" gap; reduces requirement misinterpretation.

**Principle 5 — Build Projects Around Motivated Individuals**
- Give them the **environment, support, and trust** they need.
- Management's job is to *remove impediments*, not *micromanage*.

**Principle 6 — Face-to-Face Conversation Is the Most Effective Method of Communication**
- Even within a co-located team, **verbal > written** for critical info.
- (Note: Modern Agile allows video conferencing as a substitute.)

**Principle 7 — Working Software Is the Primary Measure of Progress**
- *"Progress = software that runs and is tested"*, not lines of code or hours billed.

**Principle 8 — Agile Processes Promote Sustainable Development**
- Sponsors, developers, and users should be able to maintain a **constant, indefinite pace**.
- Anti-pattern: Death-march sprints and 80-hour weeks are **NOT Agile**.

**Principle 9 — Continuous Attention to Technical Excellence and Good Design**
- Enhances **agility** — clean code is faster to change.
- Practices: TDD, refactoring, code reviews, CI/CD.

**Principle 10 — Simplicity — The Art of Maximizing the Amount of Work Not Done**
- Eliminate *unnecessary* features (YAGNI — You Ain't Gonna Need It).
- Justification: Less code = fewer bugs = lower maintenance cost.

**Principle 11 — Best Architectures, Requirements, and Designs Emerge from Self-Organizing Teams**
- Teams decide *how* to achieve the goal; managers do not assign tasks at the micro-level.

**Principle 12 — Regular Reflection on How to Become More Effective**
- After every iteration, the team holds a **retrospective** to tune its process.

### 2.2 KTU Cheat Sheet — Agile Manifesto at a Glance

| Element | Count | Purpose | KTU Keyword |
|---------|-------|---------|--------------|
| Values | **4** | Cultural mindset | We value X **over** Y |
| Principles | **12** | Operational guidance | Implementation of values |
| Signatories | **17** | Authorship authority | Snowbird, Utah, 2001 |
| Preferred Delivery Cadence | 2 weeks – 2 months | Frequent releases | Continuous delivery |
| Stand-up Duration | **15 minutes** | Daily sync | Time-boxed |
| Sprint Length (typical) | **1–4 weeks** | Iteration duration | Time-boxed |
| Progress Metric | **Working software** | Not docs/hours | Primary measure |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Startups** use Agile to validate product-market fit within tight budgets.
- **Embedded systems & IoT** companies use Agile to handle hardware-software co-design where specs change.
- **Aerospace & Automotive (SAFe)** scale Agile across hundreds of engineers.
- **Banking/FinTech** use regulated Agile (with audit trails) for rapid compliance updates.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If asked *"How does Agile differ from Waterfall?"* always link back to the 4 values. Values are the *philosophy*; principles are the *practices*; techniques (next section) are the *tools*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Agile Development Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 1 explicitly requires study of *Agile Development Techniques*. The two most exam-relevant frameworks are **Extreme Programming (XP)** and **Scrum**. Other techniques are listed in the matrix.

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Analysis: Extreme Programming (XP)

XP was introduced by **Kent Beck (1999)**. It is the most engineering-disciplined Agile method.

**Step 1 — The Planning Game**
- Customer writes **User Stories** on index cards.
- Developers estimate effort (in **ideal weeks** or story points).
- Customer prioritizes; both sides agree on the *next iteration* scope.

**Step 2 — Small Releases**
- Release the *smallest* useful feature set to production quickly.
- Each release = one or more stories that add **customer value**.

**Step 3 — Metaphor (System of Names)**
- A simple shared **naming convention** so the team talks about the system consistently.

**Step 4 — Simple Design**
- Implement only what is required *today*. Remove unused code.

**Step 5 — Refactoring**
- Continuously improve the internal structure of code *without changing external behavior*.
- Justification: keeps the design simple and adaptive.

**Step 6 — Pair Programming**
- Two developers at **one workstation**.
- **Driver** writes code; **Navigator** reviews in real time.
- Empirical studies (from University of Utah) show pair-programmed code has **~15% fewer defects** with only a marginal time increase.

**Step 7 — Collective Code Ownership**
- *Any* developer can modify *any* code. No code is *"mine"*.
- Encourages shared responsibility and skill growth.

**Step 8 — Continuous Integration (CI)**
- Integrate and test code **multiple times per day**.
- Build server runs the full test suite on every commit.

**Step 9 — Test-Driven Development (TDD)**
- Cycle: **Red → Green → Refactor**.
  1. Write a **failing** test (Red).
  2. Write the **minimum** code to pass (Green).
  3. **Refactor** for clarity.

**Step 10 — On-Site Customer**
- A real customer representative is **physically present** with the team to answer questions in real time.

**Step 11 — Sustainable Pace**
- Standard working week of **~40 hours**. Overtime is discouraged.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **XP Core Practices Grouping (For Exam):**
> - *Planning*: Planning game, small releases, on-site customer.
> - *Designing*: Simple design, metaphor, refactoring.
> - *Coding*: Pair programming, collective ownership, CI, coding standards, TDD.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Analysis: Scrum Framework

Scrum was formalized by **Ken Schwaber & Jeff Sutherland (1995–2010)**. It is **management-focused** (whereas XP is **engineering-focused**).

**Step 1 — Roles (3 Pillars)**
- **Product Owner (PO)**: Owns the *Product Backlog*; maximizes ROI.
- **Scrum Master (SM)**: Facilitator; removes impediments; *not* a traditional manager.
- **Development Team**: Cross-functional, **3–9 members**, self-organizing.

**Step 2 — Artifacts**
- **Product Backlog**: Ordered list of *everything* that might be needed.
- **Sprint Backlog**: Items chosen for the current sprint + a plan to deliver them.
- **Increment**: The sum of all Product Backlog items completed during a sprint, *usable* and *potentially shippable*.

**Step 3 — Events (5 Ceremonies)**
- **Sprint**: Time-box of **≤ 1 month** (usually 2 weeks).
- **Sprint Planning**: What can be delivered? How will it be done?
- **Daily Scrum / Stand-up**: **15-minute** meeting; each member answers: *What did I do yesterday? What will I do today? Any impediments?*
- **Sprint Review**: Demonstrate the increment to stakeholders.
- **Sprint Retrospective**: Inspect and improve the *process* itself.

**Step 4 — Empirical Process Control (3 Pillars)**
- **Transparency**: Significant aspects must be visible.
- **Inspection**: Detect variances frequently.
- **Adaptation**: Adjust immediately if deviation exceeds acceptable limits.

### 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Scrum vs. XP

| Dimension | **Scrum** | **Extreme Programming (XP)** |
|-----------|-----------|------------------------------|
| Primary Focus | Project management & process | Engineering practices |
| Iteration Length | **2–4 weeks** (Sprint) | **1–2 weeks** |
| Documentation | Light | Minimal |
| Customer Role | Reviews increment | **On-site** with team |
| Testing | At end of sprint | **Continuous (TDD)** |
| Code Practices | Defined by team | Pair programming, CI, TDD mandated |
| Suitable for | Large cross-functional teams | Small co-located teams needing high code quality |

### 3.4 Other Agile Techniques (Brief Matrix)

| Technique | Originator | Core Idea |
|-----------|------------|-----------|
| **DSDM (Dynamic Systems Development Method)** | UK, 1994 | **MoSCoW** prioritization (Must/Should/Could/Won't) + time-boxed |
| **FDD (Feature-Driven Development)** | Peter Coad et al., 1997 | Develop in *features*; Chief Architect + Class Owners |
| **Crystal** | Alistair Cockburn, 1990s | Family of methods; team size & criticality drive method choice |
| **Lean Software Development** | Mary & Tom Poppendieck, 2003 | Eliminate waste, amplify learning, decide late, deliver fast |
| **Kanban** | Toyota (production) → software by David Anderson | **Visualize workflow**, limit WIP, manage flow |
| **Adaptive Software Development (ASD)** | Jim Highsmith, 2000 | **Speculate → Collaborate → Learn** cycle |
| **SAFe (Scaled Agile Framework)** | Dean Leffingwell | Scales Agile to enterprise (Team → Program → Large Solution → Portfolio) |

### 3.5 Analytical Derivation — How a Story Point Estimate Translates to a Sprint Plan

> [!NOTE]
> This is a *representative analytical example* KTU may pose as a numerical short question.

**Given:**
- Team velocity last 3 sprints: **30, 35, 40** story points.
- Product backlog has **120** story points remaining.

**Step 1 — Compute Average Velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{avg} &= \frac{V_1 + V_2 + V_3}{n} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{30 + 35 + 40}{3} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{105}{3} = 35 \text{ story points/sprint}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Estimate Number of Sprints to Clear Backlog:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{sprints} &= \frac{B_{total}}{V_{avg}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{120}{35} \\[4pt]
&\approx 3.43 \text{ sprints}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Round Up & Add Buffer:**
- Round up to **4 sprints** (Agile principle: cannot ship a *partial* sprint to the customer).
- Reserve 1 sprint as a *hardening / buffer* sprint → effective delivery in **5 sprints**.

**Interpretation:** A team of typical velocity 35 points/sprint needs approximately **4–5 sprints** to deliver 120 story points while maintaining sustainable pace.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Agile Manifesto — Value-Pillar Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootNode([AGILE MANIFESTO 2001]) --> val1[VALUE 1 INDIVIDUALS AND INTERACTIONS]
    rootNode --> val2[VALUE 2 WORKING SOFTWARE]
    rootNode --> val3[VALUE 3 CUSTOMER COLLABORATION]
    rootNode --> val4[VALUE 4 RESPONDING TO CHANGE]
    val1 --> pr1[P1 Early Delivery]
    val1 --> pr4[P4 Business and Devs Together]
    val1 --> pr6[P6 Face to Face]
    val1 --> pr11[P11 Self Organizing Teams]
    val2 --> pr1b[P1 Early Delivery]
    val2 --> pr3[P3 Frequent Delivery]
    val2 --> pr7[P7 Working Software as Progress]
    val2 --> pr9[P9 Technical Excellence]
    val3 --> pr1c[P1 Customer Satisfaction]
    val3 --> pr4b[P4 Business Dev Daily]
    val3 --> pr2[P2 Welcome Change]
    val3 --> pr12[P12 Reflect and Improve]
    val4 --> pr2b[P2 Welcome Change]
    val4 --> pr8[P8 Sustainable Pace]
    val4 --> pr10[P10 Simplicity]
    val4 --> pr3b[P3 Frequent Delivery]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each of the 4 values anchors 3–4 principles. For exam purposes, you can present this as a 4-column table mapping each value to its principles.

### 4.2 Scrum Framework — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    poNode[PRODUCT OWNER] --> pbNode[PRODUCT BACKLOG]
    pbNode --> spNode[SPRINT PLANNING]
    spNode --> sbNode[SPRINT BACKLOG]
    sbNode --> devNode[DEVELOPMENT TEAM 3 to 9 MEMBERS]
    devNode --> dsNode{DAILY SCRUM 15 MIN}
    dsNode --> devNode
    devNode --> incNode[INCREMENT POTENTIALLY SHIPPABLE]
    spNode --> smNode[SCRUM MASTER REMOVES IMPEDIMENTS]
    smNode --> devNode
    incNode --> srNode[SPRINT REVIEW]
    srNode --> sretNode[SPRINT RETROSPECTIVE]
    sretNode --> pbNode
```

### 4.3 XP Engineering Practices — TDD Cycle and Supporting Practices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([USER STORY]) --> pgNode[PLANNING GAME]
    pgNode --> tdd1[STEP 1 WRITE FAILING TEST RED]
    tdd1 --> tdd2[STEP 2 WRITE MINIMUM CODE GREEN]
    tdd2 --> tdd3[STEP 3 REFACTOR]
    tdd3 --> ciNode[CONTINUOUS INTEGRATION BUILD SERVER]
    ciNode --> tdd1
    pgNode --> pairNode[PAIR PROGRAMMING]
    pgNode --> ccoNode[COLLECTIVE CODE OWNERSHIP]
    pgNode --> sdNode[SUSTAINABLE PACE 40 HOURS WEEK]
    pgNode --> cstNode[ON SITE CUSTOMER]
    ciNode --> relNode[SMALL RELEASE TO PRODUCTION]
```

### 4.4 Agile vs. Waterfall — Comparative Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WF[TRADITIONAL WATERFALL]
        w1[REQUIREMENTS] --> w2[DESIGN]
        w2 --> w3[IMPLEMENTATION]
        w3 --> w4[VERIFICATION]
        w4 --> w5[MAINTENANCE]
    end
    subgraph AG[AGILE ITERATIVE]
        a1[PLAN] --> a2[DESIGN]
        a2 --> a3[CODE]
        a3 --> a4[TEST]
        a4 --> a5[REVIEW]
        a5 --> a1
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**
*State any **three** values of the Agile Manifesto. Briefly explain why "working software" is valued more than "comprehensive documentation."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Agile Manifesto has 4 values:
  1. Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
  2. **Working software over comprehensive documentation**
  3. Customer collaboration over contract negotiation
  4. Responding to change over following a plan
- **(1 mark)** Working software provides **tangible, measurable value** to the customer; it generates actual revenue and enables real user feedback.
- **(1 mark)** Excessive documentation slows delivery, becomes outdated quickly, and does **not guarantee** that the software will function. Working software is the *primary measure of progress* (Principle 7). The manifesto *does not* reject documentation — it is still useful, but secondary to *running code*.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper]**
*Differentiate between **Sprint Backlog** and **Product Backlog** in Scrum.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** *Product Backlog* is the **master prioritized list** of *all* features, enhancements, and bug fixes desired in the product. It is owned solely by the **Product Owner**.
- **(1 mark)** *Sprint Backlog* is the **subset of Product Backlog items** selected for the *current* sprint, plus a *plan* for delivering them. It is owned by the **Development Team**.
- **(1 mark)** Scope-wise: Product Backlog evolves continuously (new items added/removed); Sprint Backlog is **frozen** for the duration of the sprint (no new items added mid-sprint in classical Scrum).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

#### ⭐ Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q. (a)** List the **4 values** of the Agile Manifesto and explain each in detail with examples. **(7 marks)**
**Q. (b)** Explain **Extreme Programming (XP)**. Discuss any **six core practices** of XP. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — (a) The Four Values of the Agile Manifesto (7 marks):**

The Agile Manifesto (2001) declares four values that define the Agile mindset. For each, the left item is valued *more* than (not *instead of*) the right item. **(0.5 mark for introduction)**

**Value 1 — Individuals and Interactions over Processes and Tools** *(1.5 marks)*
- *Meaning*: People and how they communicate are the most important factors in a project's success.
- *Example*: A motivated developer pair solving a complex algorithm together is more productive than an over-engineered ticketing workflow that routes every question through a manager.
- *Justification*: Tools and processes serve people; they are means, not ends.

**Value 2 — Working Software over Comprehensive Documentation** *(1.5 marks)*
- *Meaning*: A *running, tested* module delivered to the user is a more honest measure of progress than a 200-page specification document.
- *Example*: Releasing a beta app to 1000 users and iterating from analytics beats producing a perfect SRS that becomes outdated within a sprint.
- *Justification*: Documentation is still maintained, but is *just-in-time* and *just-enough*.

**Value 3 — Customer Collaboration over Contract Negotiation** *(1.5 marks)*
- *Meaning*: Continuous engagement with the customer produces better outcomes than locking requirements in a rigid contract signed at project start.
- *Example*: Weekly demo + feedback sessions with the client where the product owner re-prioritizes the backlog.
- *Justification*: Contracts cannot anticipate every change; trust and dialogue handle unforeseen needs.

**Value 4 — Responding to Change over Following a Plan** *(2 marks)*
- *Meaning*: The ability to adapt to new information is more valuable than rigidly following the original plan.
- *Example*: A team pivots from a planned desktop UI to a mobile UI after discovering 80% of users access the product from phones.
- *Justification*: Markets and requirements evolve; plans must remain *living documents*.

> **Closing line (0.5 mark):** "While there is value in the items on the right, we value the items on the left more." — verbatim Manifesto closing statement.

---

**Model Solution — (b) Extreme Programming (XP) (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):** XP is an Agile software development methodology created by **Kent Beck** in the late 1990s, emphasizing **engineering excellence** and customer satisfaction through frequent, small releases.

**Six Core Practices (6 marks = 1 mark each):**

1. **Pair Programming** — Two programmers work together at one workstation: one *drives* (writes code), the other *navigates* (reviews and thinks strategically). Produces fewer defects and shared knowledge.
2. **Test-Driven Development (TDD)** — Write a failing automated test first, then minimum code to pass, then refactor. *Red-Green-Refactor* cycle. Ensures ≥ 90% test coverage.
3. **Continuous Integration (CI)** — Code is integrated and tested on a shared server *multiple times per day*, catching integration bugs early.
4. **Refactoring** — Continuous improvement of internal code structure *without* changing external behavior. Keeps the design simple and adaptive.
5. **Planning Game** — Customers write *user stories*; developers estimate effort; customers prioritize for the next iteration. Combines business value with technical feasibility.
6. **Collective Code Ownership** — Any developer can modify any part of the codebase. Encourages responsibility and prevents knowledge silos.

*(Alternative valid practices: Simple Design, On-Site Customer, Small Releases, Coding Standards, Sustainable Pace.)*

---

#### ⭐ Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Scrum framework** in detail. List and describe the **3 roles, 5 events, and 3 artifacts**. **(7 marks)**
**Q. (b)** Write short notes on: (i) DSDM, (ii) Feature-Driven Development (FDD), (iii) Kanban. **(7 marks — 2+2+3 marks distribution)**

---

**Model Solution — (a) Scrum Framework (7 marks):**

**Introduction (0.5 mark):** Scrum is an Agile framework invented by **Ken Schwaber** and **Jeff Sutherland**. It uses **iterative and incremental** practices, organizing work into short, time-boxed iterations called *sprints*.

**Three Roles (2 marks — ~0.7 mark each):**
- **Product Owner (PO)** — Owns the Product Backlog, prioritizes items by business value, single voice of the customer. Maximizes ROI.
- **Scrum Master (SM)** — Servant-leader for the team; facilitates ceremonies, removes impediments, enforces Scrum rules. *Not* a project manager in the traditional sense.
- **Development Team** — Cross-functional group of **3–9 members** who deliver a *potentially shippable* product increment at the end of each sprint. Self-organizing.

**Five Events (2.5 marks — 0.5 mark each):**
1. **The Sprint** — A time-box of **≤ 1 month** (typically 2 weeks) during which a "Done" increment is created.
2. **Sprint Planning** — At the start of each sprint; the team decides *what* (scope) and *how* (plan).
3. **Daily Scrum / Stand-up** — **15-minute** daily meeting; 3 questions: yesterday / today / impediments.
4. **Sprint Review** — Inspect the increment with stakeholders; adapt the Product Backlog.
5. **Sprint Retrospective** — Inspect the team's *process*; identify improvements for the next sprint.

**Three Artifacts (2 marks — ~0.7 mark each):**
- **Product Backlog** — Ordered list of *all* known requirements; emergent and dynamic.
- **Sprint Backlog** — Items chosen for current sprint + a plan; visible to all.
- **Increment** — The sum of all completed Sprint Backlog items; must be *usable* and meet the *Definition of Done*.

---

**Model Solution — (b) Short Notes (7 marks):**

**(i) DSDM — Dynamic Systems Development Method (2 marks)**
- Originated in the **UK in 1994**; one of the earliest Agile methods.
- Core principle: *any* time and cost must be fixed; *functionality* is the variable that flexes.
- Uses **MoSCoW** prioritization: **M**ust have, **S**ould have, **C**ould have, **W**on't have (this time).
- Emphasizes **active user involvement**, frequent deliveries, and integrated testing.

**(ii) FDD — Feature-Driven Development (2 marks)**
- Developed by **Peter Coad** and **Jeff De Luca** in **1997**.
- A *feature* is a small, client-valued function expressed in the form: *"\<action\> \<object\> \<result\>"* (e.g., "calculate total of sale items").
- Process: Develop an Overall Model → Build a Feature List → Plan by Feature → Design by Feature → Build by Feature.
- Best suited for **large, long-term projects** with evolving requirements.

**(iii) Kanban (3 marks)**
- Adapted from the **Toyota Production System** to software by **David J. Anderson (2007)**.
- A *visual management* method that focuses on **continuous flow** without overloading the team.
- **Core practices** (4 principles + 6 practices):
  1. **Visualize workflow** — A Kanban *board* (To-Do, In-Progress, Done).
  2. **Limit WIP (Work-In-Progress)** — Prevents overload; exposes bottlenecks.
  3. **Manage flow** — Monitor items moving through the board.
  4. **Make process policies explicit** — Clearly stated entry/exit criteria for each column.
  5. **Implement feedback loops** — Regular reviews.
  6. **Improve collaboratively, evolve experimentally** — Continuous Kaizen.
- Kanban is *evolutionary* — applicable to existing processes *without* disruption.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Writing "over" as "instead of"** → 1 mark deduction. The Manifesto values items on the *right*; it just values those on the *left more*.
> 2. **Listing principles without explanation** → KTU awards marks for *meaning* and *example*, not mere recall. A principle without an example gets only 0.5/1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing XP with Scrum** → XP emphasizes *engineering practices* (TDD, pair programming); Scrum emphasizes *project management* (roles, ceremonies, artifacts).
> 4. **Mixing up Backlog types** → Product Backlog = all features, owned by PO. Sprint Backlog = current iteration, owned by Team.
> 5. **Skipping the year/signatories** in definitions → Examiners appreciate correct attribution (2001, Snowbird, 17 practitioners).
> 6. **Writing "Daily Stand-up is 30 minutes"** → It is **15 minutes**, strictly time-boxed.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 Quick-Fire Revision List — Must Know Before the Exam**

- ✅ Agile Manifesto was created in **February 2001** at **Snowbird, Utah**, by **17 practitioners**.
- ✅ Manifesto has **4 values** and **12 principles**.
- ✅ The right-hand items are *not rejected*, only *less valued* than the left-hand items.
- ✅ Preferred delivery cadence: **2 weeks to 2 months**.
- ✅ Scrum sprint length: **≤ 1 month** (usually 2 weeks).
- ✅ Daily Stand-up: **15 minutes**, three questions (yesterday / today / impediments).
- ✅ Scrum has **3 roles, 5 events, 3 artifacts**.
- ✅ Scrum is built on **3 pillars**: Transparency, Inspection, Adaptation.
- ✅ XP inventor: **Kent Beck**; Scrum originators: **Ken Schwaber & Jeff Sutherland**.
- ✅ TDD cycle: **Red → Green → Refactor**.
- ✅ Pair programming: **Driver + Navigator** at one workstation.
- ✅ Continuous Integration: Integrate & test **multiple times per day**.
- ✅ DSDM priority technique: **MoSCoW** (Must / Should / Could / Won't).
- ✅ Kanban core idea: **Visualize + Limit WIP + Manage Flow**.
- ✅ Sustainable pace = **~40 hours/week**; overtime is *anti-Agile*.
- ✅ FDD unit of work = **Feature** (small, client-valued).
- ✅ Velocity = **story points completed per sprint**.
- ✅ "Working software" is the *primary* measure of progress (Principle 7).
- ✅ Crystal (Cockburn), ASD (Highsmith), Lean (Poppendieck) are *other* Agile techniques.
- ✅ SAFe = Scaled Agile Framework for *enterprise-level* scaling.
- ✅ Always answer with **definition → explanation → example** pattern to score full marks in KTU.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Agile Project Management. Case Studies: An Insulin Pump Control System. Mentcare - a Patient Information System for Mental Health Care
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Agile Project Management. Case Studies: An Insulin Pump Control System. Mentcare - a Patient Information System for Mental Health Care

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Agile Project Management (APM) — Formal Definition

**Agile Project Management (APM)** is an iterative and incremental software project management methodology that organizes work into short, time-boxed cycles called *iterations* or *sprints*, typically lasting **1 to 4 weeks**, with continuous stakeholder feedback, adaptive planning, and evolutionary delivery of working software.

It is formally grounded in the **Agile Manifesto (2001)**, which declares four foundational values and twelve guiding principles that prioritize individuals, working software, customer collaboration, and responsiveness to change over rigid plans, processes, documentation, and contract negotiation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 NEP-aligned Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, Agile Project Management is the **counter-paradigm to the Plan-Driven (Waterfall) approach** studied in the classical Software Engineering lifecycle. It is mandatory to know *when* to choose Agile vs. Plan-Driven based on system criticality, requirements stability, and team size.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant vs. The Caterer"

| Style | Real-World Analogy | Project Style |
|---|---|---|
| **Plan-Driven (Waterfall)** | A **caterer** who takes a full order months in advance, locks the menu, and delivers everything on the day of the event. If the host changes the menu, it is expensive and slow. | Predictive; requirements fixed upfront. |
| **Agile** | A **restaurant chef** who cooks in small batches — one dish at a time — taking feedback from the customer after each plate and adapting the next dish accordingly. | Adaptive; requirements evolve. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** In the restaurant analogy, the *customer* (Product Owner) tastes the *dish* (increment) after every sprint, and the *chef* (Scrum Team) refines the *menu* (Product Backlog) continuously. This is exactly how APM delivers value.

### 1.3 The Agile Manifesto — The Four Foundational Values

The **Agile Manifesto** (Beck et al., 2001) is the philosophical bedrock of all agile methods:

1. **Individuals and interactions** *over* processes and tools
2. **Working software** *over* comprehensive documentation
3. **Customer collaboration** *over* contract negotiation
4. **Responding to change** *over* following a plan

> *Note: The items on the right have value, but the items on the left are valued more.*

### 1.4 The Twelve Principles of Agile (Condensed for KTU)

1. Early and continuous delivery of valuable software.
2. Welcome changing requirements, even late in development.
3. Deliver working software frequently (weeks, not months).
4. Business people and developers must work together daily.
5. Build projects around motivated individuals; give them the environment and support they need.
6. Face-to-face conversation is the most effective method of communication.
7. Working software is the primary measure of progress.
8. Agile processes promote sustainable development — maintain a constant pace indefinitely.
9. Continuous attention to technical excellence and good design.
10. Simplicity — the art of maximizing the amount of work not done — is essential.
11. The best architectures, requirements, and designs emerge from self-organizing teams.
12. The team regularly reflects on how to become more effective, then tunes its behavior.

### 1.5 The Three Pillars of Scrum (Empirical Process Control)

Scrum — the most widely used Agile framework — is built on three pillars:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of Scrum:**
> 1. **Transparency** — significant aspects of the process must be visible to those who perform and receive the work.
> 2. **Inspection** — team members must frequently inspect artifacts and progress toward the sprint goal.
> 3. **Adaptation** — if any inspected artifact deviates unacceptably, the process or material must be adjusted immediately.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Plan-Driven vs. Agile Development — A Comparative Matrix

The KTU examiner frequently tests the *boundary* conditions — when to use which approach. The table below is the single most important KTU high-yield summary for this module.

| **Parameter** | **Plan-Driven Development** | **Agile Development** |
|---|---|---|
| **Requirements** | Fixed, well-understood, stable | Evolving, emergent, high uncertainty |
| **Delivery Style** | Single delivery at project end | Frequent incremental deliveries (sprints) |
| **Customer Involvement** | Mainly at start and end | Continuous, embedded in the team |
| **Documentation** | Heavy, comprehensive | Lightweight, just-enough |
| **Change Management** | Formal change-control board | Welcome changes at any sprint boundary |
| **Team Structure** | Hierarchical, specialized roles | Self-organizing, cross-functional |
| **Feedback Loop** | Long (months/years) | Short (1–4 weeks per sprint) |
| **Risk Profile** | High late-stage discovery risk | Risk spread across iterations |
| **Typical Domain Fit** | Safety-critical, regulated, hardware-coupled | Web apps, startups, internal tools, UX-heavy |
| **Example Method** | Waterfall, V-Model, Spiral (classical) | Scrum, XP, Kanban, DSDM |

### 2.2 The Scrum Framework — The "Engine" of Agile

Scrum defines **3 Roles, 5 Events, and 3 Artifacts**. Memorizing this triad is a **3-mark direct question** almost every KTU cycle.

#### 2.2.1 Scrum Roles

| Role | Responsibility | Accountability |
|---|---|---|
| **Product Owner (PO)** | Manages the Product Backlog; prioritizes items by business value; single voice of the customer. | Maximizing product value. |
| **Scrum Master (SM)** | Facilitator; removes impediments; enforces Scrum rules; shields the team. | Process integrity and team effectiveness. |
| **Development Team** | Cross-functional (3–9 members); self-organizing; delivers the increment. | Creating a usable increment each sprint. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The Scrum Master is *not* a project manager in the traditional sense. They have **no authority over the team** — they are a *servant-leader*. Writing "Scrum Master manages the team" will cost marks.

#### 2.2.2 Scrum Events (Ceremonies)

| Event | Duration | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Sprint** | Fixed, ≤ 1 month (usually 2 weeks) | Container for all other events; produces a "Done" increment. |
| **Sprint Planning** | ≤ 8 hours (for 1-month sprint) | Team selects Product Backlog Items (PBIs) for the sprint. |
| **Daily Scrum (Stand-up)** | 15 minutes | Synchronize; each member answers: *What did I do? What will I do? Any impediments?* |
| **Sprint Review** | ≤ 4 hours (for 1-month sprint) | Demonstrate the increment to stakeholders; gather feedback. |
| **Sprint Retrospective** | ≤ 3 hours (for 1-month sprint) | Team inspects *itself*; identifies process improvements for next sprint. |

#### 2.2.3 Scrum Artifacts

| Artifact | Description | Commitment |
|---|---|---|
| **Product Backlog** | Ordered list of *everything* that might be needed in the product. | Product Goal |
| **Sprint Backlog** | Set of PBIs selected for the sprint + a plan to deliver them. | Sprint Goal |
| **Increment** | The sum of all PBIs completed during a sprint + value of all previous sprints. | Definition of Done (DoD) |

### 2.3 Extreme Programming (XP) — Engineering Practices

XP is the **engineering-discipline twin** of Scrum. Scrum focuses on *project management*; XP focuses on *coding practices*.

| **XP Practice** | **Engineering Purpose** |
|---|---|
| **Pair Programming** | Two developers, one keyboard — continuous code review. |
| **Test-Driven Development (TDD)** | Write failing test → write code → refactor. |
| **Continuous Integration (CI)** | Integrate and test code multiple times per day. |
| **Refactoring** | Improve internal structure without changing external behavior. |
| **Collective Code Ownership** | Anyone can fix any code; no silos. |
| **On-site Customer** | Real user available full-time for questions. |
| **Small Releases** | Ship the smallest useful feature quickly. |
| **Simple Design** | Implement only what is needed *today*; no speculative features. |
| **Planning Game** | Customer writes user stories; developers estimate. |
| **Sustainable Pace** | No overtime; "40-hour week." |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Heuristic Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although Agile is qualitative, KTU expects numerical fluency on the following **velocity and estimation metrics**. These are tested almost every exam cycle.

| **Metric** | **Formula / Heuristic** | **Unit** | **Engineering Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Velocity** | $V = \dfrac{\sum \text{Story Points Completed}}{\text{Sprint Length}}$ | Story Points / Sprint | Team's actual delivery rate. |
| **Burndown** | $B(t) = B_0 - V \cdot t$ | Story Points (Y) vs. Time (X) | Remaining work over the sprint. |
| **Release Forecast** | $N_{\text{sprints}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{Total Backlog Size}}{V} \right\rceil$ | Sprints (integer) | Number of sprints to deliver backlog. |
| **Sprint Goal Completion %** | $C_{\%} = \dfrac{\text{Story Points Delivered}}{\text{Committed Story Points}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) | Sprint success indicator. |
| **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $\text{DRE} = \dfrac{E}{E + D} \times 100$ | Percent (%) | E = defects found before release; D = defects found after release. |
| **Story Point Estimate (Fibonacci)** | $SP \in \{1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, 40, 100\}$ | Story Points | Planning Poker scale; reflects *uncertainty*. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Markdown Safeguard:** All vertical bars in formulas use `\vert` or `\mid`, **not** the raw `|` character, to prevent table-breaking.

### 2.5 Why Agile Matters in Real Engineering

Agile is not just a process — it is a **risk-reduction and value-maximization strategy**:

- **Risk spreading:** Failure is detected in days/weeks, not months/years.
- **Customer lock-in:** Stakeholders are co-creators, reducing the "we-didn't-ask-for-this" rejection risk.
- **Quality compounding:** XP's TDD + CI yields DRE values often exceeding **95%**.
- **Predictability:** Once velocity stabilizes (usually by Sprint 3–4), release dates become mathematically forecastable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Applications & Case Study Derivations

### 3.1 How to Select an Agile or Plan-Driven Approach — The Decision Logic

This is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question**. The following step-by-step decision process must be written out explicitly in the exam.

**Step 1 — Identify System Criticality.**
- If the system is **safety-critical** (e.g., insulin pump, avionics, nuclear plant), Plan-Driven dominates because **regulatory certification** (FDA, IEC 62304, DO-178C) demands exhaustive upfront documentation and traceability.

**Step 2 — Identify Requirements Volatility.**
- If requirements are **stable and known** (e.g., a payroll system with a fixed tax law), Plan-Driven is efficient.
- If requirements are **emergent** (e.g., a startup's MVP), Agile is superior.

**Step 3 — Identify Team Size and Expertise.**
- Small (≤ 10) and co-located → Agile.
- Large (> 50) and distributed → Plan-Driven or hybrid (e.g., SAFe).

**Step 4 — Identify Customer Availability.**
- A **dedicated Product Owner** → Agile.
- An **absent customer** → Plan-Driven.

**Step 5 — Apply the Decision Rule.**

$$
\text{Approach} = \begin{cases}
\text{Plan-Driven} & \text{if (Criticality = High) } \lor \text{ (Requirements = Stable) } \lor \text{ (Team > 50)} \\
\text{Agile} & \text{if (Criticality = Low/Med) } \land \text{ (Requirements = Volatile) } \land \text{ (Team} \leq 10)
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Most real systems fall in between, and the **correct KTU answer** is often: *"Use Agile for early exploration and Plan-Driven for the final, certified, safety-critical hardener."*

### 3.2 Case Study 1 — The Insulin Pump Control System (Somerville, Chapter 3)

#### 3.2.1 System Description

An **insulin pump** is a **safety-critical, embedded, real-time, regulated medical device** worn by diabetes patients. It continuously measures blood glucose (via a sensor) and infuses insulin doses through a subcutaneous pump. The system is governed by **IEC 62304** and **FDA Class II/III** medical-device software regulations.

Key stakeholders:
- **Patient** (primary user).
- **Diabetologist / Endocrinologist** (prescriber).
- **Medical-device regulator** (FDA, CE-Mark).
- **Hospital IT** (data integration).

#### 3.2.2 Why Plan-Driven is *Required*

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Regulatory Compliance** | The FDA mandates design history files, traceability matrices, risk analyses (per ISO 14971), and V&V evidence. Agile's light documentation is legally insufficient. |
| **Safety-Criticality** | A software failure can kill the patient (hypoglycemia, hyperglycemia, diabetic ketoacidosis). All requirements must be exhaustively specified. |
| **Hardware Coupling** | The pump is a cyber-physical system with sensors, actuators, battery, and wireless telemetry — all requiring fixed hardware specifications. |
| **Verification Cost** | The system must undergo formal verification, hazard analysis, and clinical trials before release. |

#### 3.2.3 Why Agile Elements Are *Useful* in Early Stages

Even in a Plan-Driven project, the early **requirements exploration** phase benefits from Agile:
- **User stories** from patients reveal usability problems (e.g., "As a patient, I want the pump to alarm 15 minutes before insulin depletion").
- **Rapid prototyping** of the user interface improves patient acceptance.
- **Spike solutions** (time-boxed experiments) help engineers evaluate glucose-prediction algorithms.

#### 3.2.4 Recommended Hybrid Process for the Insulin Pump

$$
\text{Process} = \text{Plan-Driven Core} \; + \; \text{Agile Front-End Exploration} \; + \; \text{Formal V\&V Back-End}
$$

The recommended workflow:

1. **Phase 1 — Agile Discovery (2–3 sprints):** Elicit user stories from patients/doctors. Build UI prototypes. Hold a backlog-grooming session.
2. **Phase 2 — Plan-Driven Engineering:** Freeze the SRS (Software Requirements Specification). Design the safety kernel, dose-calculation algorithm, and alarm subsystem. Apply IEC 62304 Class B/C classification.
3. **Phase 3 — Formal V&V:** Conduct unit tests, integration tests, hazard analysis, and clinical simulation.
4. **Phase 4 — Regulatory Submission & Release.**

#### 3.2.5 KTU-Mark-Worthy Point — "Plan-Driven with Agile V&V Feedback Loops"

Even within the V-Model, the test teams can use **short feedback loops** to incrementally verify subsystems — this is the "Agile inside a Plan-Driven envelope" pattern.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Writing "Agile should be used for the insulin pump because it delivers faster" will **lose full marks** in KTU. The examiner expects a *defended* hybrid answer citing IEC 62304 / FDA.

### 3.3 Case Study 2 — Mentcare: A Patient Information System for Mental Health Care (Somerville, Chapter 3)

#### 3.3.1 System Description

**Mentcare** is a **Patient Information System (PIS)** designed to manage records for patients undergoing mental-health treatment in the UK NHS (or similar public-health setting). It supports:
- Patient registration and demographic data.
- Consultation notes (psychiatrists, nurses, therapists).
- Medication prescriptions and history.
- Care plans and risk assessments.
- Appointment scheduling.
- Integration with GP (General Practitioner) systems.

#### 3.3.2 Why Agile is *Appropriate* (and Plan-Driven is *Not*)

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Evolving Requirements** | Mental-health practice evolves rapidly (new therapies, new drug protocols, regulatory changes from CQC, GDPR/HIPAA, etc.). The system must adapt. |
| **Strong User Co-Design** | Psychiatrists, nurses, and patients are the actual users; their continuous input is essential. |
| **No Embedded/Hardware Constraint** | The system is a typical enterprise web application — no sensor integration, no safety-critical loop. |
| **Large UI Surface** | Many screens, complex workflows (e.g., risk-assessment forms, care-plan templates) — iterative UI refinement is valuable. |
| **Frequent Regulatory Change** | New data-protection laws (e.g., GDPR Article 9 on health data) and clinical guidelines (NICE) demand rapid updates. |

#### 3.3.3 Recommended Agile Process for Mentcare

1. **Sprint 0 — Inception:** Set up the team, define the Product Goal ("improve clinician workflow"), establish the Definition of Done (DoD) including security tests and accessibility compliance).
2. **Backlog Creation:** Write user stories, e.g.:
   - *"As a psychiatrist, I can record a patient's current mental-state examination in under 2 minutes."*
   - *"As a data-protection officer, I can generate a GDPR Article 15 (Right of Access) report in one click."*
3. **Sprint Cycles (2 weeks each):** Develop, test, and demo the increment to clinical staff.
4. **Continuous Compliance:** Embed security (OWASP), encryption-at-rest, audit-logging, and penetration tests into every sprint's DoD.
5. **Release:** Deploy to a pilot clinic, gather usage data, and adapt.

#### 3.3.4 Why Mentcare is *Not* Pure Plan-Driven

A Plan-Driven approach would lock requirements to a 2015-vintage specification. By 2018, GDPR would have invalidated the entire design. **Agile's ability to absorb late changes is operationally essential.**

#### 3.3.5 Key Engineering Challenges Specific to Mentcare

- **Privacy-by-Design:** Mental-health data is *specially protected* under GDPR Article 9 and HIPAA §164.508. Every sprint must include threat-modelling.
- **Interoperability:** HL7 FHIR, SNOMED-CT, and ICD-10 codes must be supported.
- **Auditability:** Every record access must be logged with user, timestamp, and purpose.
- **Offline-Resilience:** Community mental-health nurses may work in areas with poor connectivity.

### 3.4 Side-by-Side Comparison of the Two Case Studies

| **Dimension** | **Insulin Pump** | **Mentcare** |
|---|---|---|
| **Domain** | Embedded medical device | Enterprise health IT |
| **Safety Class** | Class C (IEC 62304) — death possible | Non-safety-critical |
| **Regulator** | FDA, MHRA, CE-Mark | CQC, ICO (GDPR) |
| **Hardware** | Yes — sensors, pump motor, battery | No — pure software |
| **Data Sensitivity** | Medium (clinical + biometric) | Very High (mental-health stigma) |
| **Customer Type** | Patient + doctor + regulator | Clinician + patient + DPO |
| **Volatility** | Low (biology is stable) | High (laws, therapies, UX) |
| **Recommended Process** | Plan-Driven + Agile exploration | Pure Agile / Scrum |
| **Documentation Need** | Exhaustive (design history file) | Lightweight, just-enough |
| **Testing Style** | Formal V&V, hazard analysis | TDD, BDD, security testing |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Velocity and Release Forecast

**Problem:** A Scrum team has completed **3 sprints** with the following delivered story points:
- Sprint 1: 28 SP
- Sprint 2: 34 SP
- Sprint 3: 30 SP

The remaining Product Backlog is **250 story points**. How many sprints are needed to complete the backlog?

**Step 1 — Compute Average Velocity.**

$$
V_{\text{avg}} = \frac{28 + 34 + 30}{3} = \frac{92}{3} \approx 30.67 \text{ SP/sprint}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Sprints to Completion.**

$$
N_{\text{sprints}} = \left\lceil \frac{250}{30.67} \right\rceil = \lceil 8.15 \rceil = 9 \text{ sprints}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Calendar Duration.**
If each sprint = 2 weeks:

$$
T_{\text{release}} = 9 \times 2 = 18 \text{ weeks} \approx 4.5 \text{ months}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation.**
The forecast is probabilistic — actual delivery will vary ± 1 sprint based on team stability, scope creep, and defect-rework overhead.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Agile Scrum Workflow (End-to-End Sprint Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Product Vision]) --> stepB[Product Backlog Prioritized by PO]
    stepB --> stepC{Sprint Planning: Select PBIs}
    stepC --> stepD[Sprint Backlog]
    stepD --> stepE[Sprint: 1 to 4 weeks]
    stepE --> stepF[Daily Scrum 15 min]
    stepF --> stepF
    stepE --> stepG[Development and Testing CI CD]
    stepG --> stepH[Increment Ready]
    stepH --> stepI{Sprint Review Demo}
    stepI --> stepJ[Stakeholder Feedback]
    stepJ --> stepK{Sprint Retrospective}
    stepK --> stepL[Process Improvements]
    stepL --> stepB
    stepI --> stepM([Done Increment Shipped to Production])
```

### 4.2 Decision Logic: Agile vs. Plan-Driven

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qStart([New Software Project]) --> q1{Safety Critical?}
    q1 -- Yes --> pd1[Use Plan Driven]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Requirements Stable?}
    q2 -- Yes --> pd2[Use Plan Driven]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Team Size and Co Located?}
    q3 -- Less than 10 Co Located --> q4{Continuous Customer Available?}
    q4 -- Yes --> agilePath[Use Agile Scrum or XP]
    q4 -- No --> hybridPath[Use Hybrid Approach]
    q3 -- More than 50 Distributed --> pd3[Use Plan Driven or SAFe]
```

### 4.3 Hybrid Process Block Architecture for the Insulin Pump

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[Phase 1 Agile Discovery: User Stories and UI Prototype] --> blockB[Phase 2 Plan Driven Engineering: SRS Freeze and Safety Kernel]
    blockB --> blockC[Phase 3 Formal V and V: Hazard Analysis and IEC 62304 Compliance]
    blockC --> blockD[Phase 4 Regulatory Submission: FDA and CE Mark]
    blockD --> blockE([Released Insulin Pump Device])
```

### 4.4 Mentcare Agile Sprint Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ms0([Sprint 0 Inception and Backlog]) --> ms1[Sprint N: Clinician Workflow Features]
    ms1 --> ms2[Continuous Compliance: GDPR Article 9 and OWASP Tests]
    ms2 --> ms3[Sprint Review with Psychiatrists and Nurses]
    ms3 --> ms4{Stakeholder Accepts Increment?}
    ms4 -- Yes --> ms5[Deploy to Pilot Clinic]
    ms4 -- No --> ms6[Refine Backlog]
    ms6 --> ms1
    ms5 --> ms7([Production Release and Continuous Monitoring])
```

### 4.5 Scrum Role-Responsibility Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph s1Roles [Scrum Team]
        rA[Product Owner: Maximizes Product Value]
        rB[Scrum Master: Servant Leader and Impediment Remover]
        rC[Development Team: Cross Functional 3 to 9 Members]
    end
    rA --> a1[Product Backlog]
    rB --> a2[Sprint Events]
    rC --> a3[Increment]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**  
*Define Agile Project Management. List any three principles of the Agile Manifesto.*

**Model Answer:**

Agile Project Management is an iterative and incremental approach to software project management that delivers software in short, time-boxed iterations (typically 1–4 weeks), embracing changing requirements and continuous customer feedback. It is governed by the Agile Manifesto (2001), which provides four values and twelve principles.

Three principles (out of twelve):
1. *Welcome changing requirements, even late in development. Agile processes harness change for the customer's competitive advantage.*
2. *Deliver working software frequently, from a couple of weeks to a couple of months, with a preference to the shorter timescale.*
3. *Business people and developers must work together daily throughout the project.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Mentioning the year (2001) and authors (Beck et al.) earns an extra half-mark in valuation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**  
*Differentiate between a Product Owner and a Scrum Master. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Product Owner | Scrum Master |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Role** | Voice of the customer; maximizes product value. | Servant-leader; facilitates Scrum process. |
| **Key Responsibility** | Manages and prioritizes the Product Backlog. | Removes impediments; enforces Scrum rules. |
| **Authority** | Decides *what* to build. | Decides *how* the team works. |
| **Interaction with Team** | Defines acceptance criteria for user stories. | Coaches the team on self-management. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Writing "Scrum Master is the project manager" or "Product Owner manages the team" is **incorrect** and will cost 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, PEEET524]**  
*Agile methods are often considered universal solutions for software development, yet some systems are not suitable for Agile approaches.*

**(a)** Explain the four values of the Agile Manifesto in detail. **(7 Marks)**  
**(b)** With reference to the **Insulin Pump Control System** case study, justify why a **plan-driven** approach is preferred over Agile, while identifying any Agile elements that may still be incorporated. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — The Four Values (7 Marks)**

The **Agile Manifesto**, authored by 17 software practitioners in 2001, declares four values that prioritize one set of concerns over another (though both have value):

**Value 1 — Individuals and Interactions over Processes and Tools.**  
Software is built by *people*. The Manifesto recognizes that motivated, skilled individuals communicating effectively will outperform rigid process compliance and heavyweight tooling. In practice, this means daily face-to-face communication, pair programming, and self-organizing teams are prioritized over formal sign-offs and tool-driven workflows.

**Value 2 — Working Software over Comprehensive Documentation.**  
The primary measure of progress is *running, tested code* delivered to the user, not reams of design documents. Documentation is still produced — but only *just-enough* to support development and maintenance.

**Value 3 — Customer Collaboration over Contract Negotiation.**  
Customers are treated as *collaborative partners*, not adversaries. Frequent demos, embedded product owners, and rapid feedback replace the "we'll-sue-you-for-requirement-omissions" contract model.

**Value 4 — Responding to Change over Following a Plan.**  
Agile teams welcome late-stage requirement changes because delivering value to the customer *now* is more important than slavishly following a pre-fixed plan. The plan is revisited every sprint.

> **Mnemonic for the exam:** "**I-C-W, W-D-C-R**" → *Individuals, Collaboration, Working software, Welcoming change* over *process, contracts, docs, rigidity*.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Insulin Pump Case Study (7 Marks)**

The **Insulin Pump Control System** is a **safety-critical, embedded, regulated medical device**. A plan-driven approach is preferred for the following reasons:

| Reason | Detailed Justification |
|---|---|
| **Regulatory Compliance (FDA / IEC 62304)** | Medical-device software must produce a *Design History File* with full traceability from requirements to tests. Agile's lightweight documentation is legally insufficient for Class B/C devices. |
| **Safety-Criticality** | A dosing error can cause **hypoglycemia, hyperglycemia, or death**. All failure modes (e.g., insulin overdose, sensor disconnection) must be exhaustively enumerated and mitigated — a Plan-Driven hazard analysis (per ISO 14971) is mandatory. |
| **Hardware-Coupled Engineering** | The pump integrates sensors, motors, and battery systems with fixed electrical/mechanical specifications. Hardware constraints freeze the requirements early, favoring Plan-Driven development. |
| **Verification & Validation** | The system must undergo formal V&V, unit/integration/system testing, and clinical trials. These activities are most efficiently orchestrated under a V-Model. |
| **Long Lifecycle & Maintainability** | Devices ship for 10+ years; backward-compatibility and serviceability require a stable, well-documented architecture. |

**Agile elements that can be incorporated:**
- **Front-end Agile Discovery:** Use 2–3 sprints of user-story elicitation with patients and endocrinologists to refine the SRS.
- **UI Prototyping in Sprints:** Use rapid iterations to design the patient-facing display.
- **Spike Solutions for Algorithms:** Time-boxed experiments to evaluate glucose-prediction models.
- **Retrospectives on the V&V Process:** Even formal teams benefit from end-of-phase retrospectives.

**Conclusion:** A **hybrid process** — Agile for the *front-end* requirements exploration and UI design, and Plan-Driven for the *core engineering, V&V, and regulatory submission* — is the correct professional answer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Do **not** write "Agile is faster, so use Agile." The correct answer is to *defend* a plan-driven core with agile *spikes*. Mention **IEC 62304, ISO 14971, FDA Class C, hazard analysis** — these are the high-value keywords the examiner scans for.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, PEEET524]**  
*The Mentcare Patient Information System is a large-scale healthcare IT system for mental health record management.*

**(a)** Discuss why an **Agile approach** is more suitable than a plan-driven approach for the Mentcare system. List the user stories that may form the initial product backlog. **(7 Marks)**  
**(b)** Explain the Scrum framework in detail — roles, events, and artifacts — and compute the **expected release date** for Mentcare given a velocity of **35 story points/sprint** and a remaining backlog of **400 story points**, with 2-week sprints. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Agile Suitability for Mentcare (7 Marks)**

**Why Agile is more suitable than Plan-Driven for Mentcare:**

1. **Evolving Clinical Practice:** Mental-health treatment protocols, NICE guidelines, and ICD-10/ICD-11 codes update frequently. A frozen SRS would be obsolete within 18–24 months.
2. **Strong User Co-Design Need:** Psychiatrists, mental-health nurses, and patients are the actual users. Their continuous involvement is essential to capture workflow nuances (e.g., risk-assessment forms, suicide-watch protocols).
3. **No Safety-Critical Hardware:** The system is a standard enterprise web application with no embedded control loop — Agile's lack of formal V&V is not a regulatory blocker.
4. **Frequent Regulatory & Legal Change:** GDPR Article 9 (special-category data), HIPAA §164.508, and the UK's Data Protection Act 2018 all require ongoing compliance updates — Agile's iterative cycle absorbs these naturally.
5. **Large UI Surface:** The system has many complex screens (consultation notes, care plans, prescription forms) that benefit from iterative UI/UX refinement via user feedback.
6. **Privacy-by-Design Iteration:** Threat-modelling and security testing can be embedded in every sprint's Definition of Done.

**Sample Initial Product Backlog (User Stories):**

| # | As a... | I want to... | So that... | Story Points |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Psychiatrist | record a patient's mental-state examination in < 2 minutes | I can complete a consultation efficiently. | 5 |
| 2 | Nurse | view a patient's medication history | I can administer drugs safely. | 8 |
| 3 | Patient | access my own care plan via a portal | I can review my treatment. | 13 |
| 4 | Data-Protection Officer | generate a GDPR Article 15 (Right of Access) report in 1 click | I can comply with subject-access requests. | 8 |
| 5 | Administrator | schedule recurring therapy appointments | the clinic runs smoothly. | 5 |
| 6 | Clinician | flag a patient as "high risk" with an auditable alert | emergency staff are warned. | 13 |
| 7 | Auditor | view a tamper-evident log of all record accesses | I can investigate data breaches. | 8 |
| 8 | Psychiatrist | e-prescribe a controlled drug with two-factor authentication | I comply with controlled-drug regulations. | 13 |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Scrum Framework and Release Forecast (7 Marks)**

**The Scrum Framework** has three pillars — *Transparency, Inspection, Adaptation* — and is structured around **3 Roles, 5 Events, 3 Artifacts**.

**3 Roles:**
- **Product Owner:** Manages the Product Backlog; prioritizes PBIs by business value; single voice of the customer.
- **Scrum Master:** Facilitator; removes impediments; enforces Scrum rules; servant-leader.
- **Development Team:** Cross-functional, 3–9 members; self-organizing; delivers a "Done" increment.

**5 Events:**
- **Sprint** (≤ 1 month) — the container event.
- **Sprint Planning** (≤ 8 hours) — selects PBIs for the sprint.
- **Daily Scrum** (15 minutes) — synchronization meeting.
- **Sprint Review** (≤ 4 hours) — demo to stakeholders.
- **Sprint Retrospective** (≤ 3 hours) — process improvement.

**3 Artifacts:**
- **Product Backlog** — ordered list of all desired features.
- **Sprint Backlog** — PBIs selected for the current sprint + the plan.
- **Increment** — the sum of all completed PBIs in the sprint plus all prior sprints.

**Numerical Computation — Release Forecast:**

Given:
- Velocity $V = 35$ story points / sprint
- Remaining backlog $B = 400$ story points
- Sprint length $T_{\text{sprint}} = 2$ weeks

**Step 1 — Compute Number of Sprints.**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{B}{V} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{400}{35} \right\rceil = \lceil 11.43 \rceil = 12 \text{ sprints}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Calendar Duration.**

$$
T_{\text{release}} = N \times T_{\text{sprint}} = 12 \times 2 = 24 \text{ weeks} = 6 \text{ months}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Caveats.**
- This assumes **velocity remains constant**; in practice, velocity stabilizes only after Sprint 3–4.
- **Scope creep** (new regulatory requirements, e.g., a new GDPR ruling) will increase $B$ and push the release date.
- **Defect-rework overhead** typically consumes 15–20% of velocity.
- A safer forecast is $T_{\text{safe}} = 6 \text{ months} + 20\% \text{ buffer} = 7.2 \text{ months}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Calculation Part):** Forgetting the **ceiling function** ($\lceil \cdot \rceil$) is a common error. Writing "11.43 sprints" is wrong — you cannot run a *fraction* of a sprint in standard Scrum. The final answer must be **12 sprints**, not 11.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1 / Topic: Agile Project Management + Case Studies**

- [ ] **Agile Manifesto Year:** 2001; authored by 17 practitioners including Kent Beck, Martin Fowler, Robert C. Martin.
- [ ] **Four Values** (in order): *Individuals & Interactions, Working Software, Customer Collaboration, Responding to Change* — all "over" the right-hand items.
- [ ] **Scrum is the most popular Agile framework** — know the 3-5-3 structure (3 roles, 5 events, 3 artifacts).
- [ ] **Scrum Master ≠ Project Manager** — servant-leader, no authority over team.
- [ ] **Product Owner ≠ Project Sponsor** — PO is embedded in the team, makes daily trade-off decisions.
- [ ] **Sprint duration:** typically **2 weeks** (can be 1–4 weeks). Never exceeds **1 calendar month**.
- [ ] **Daily Scrum is exactly 15 minutes** — same time, same place, every day.
- [ ] **Velocity stabilizes by Sprint 3–4** — never trust Sprint 1's velocity for forecasting.
- [ ] **Story Points use the Fibonacci sequence** (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, 40, 100) — the gap reflects *uncertainty*, not arithmetic progression.
- [ ] **Definition of Done (DoD)** is a checklist the team must satisfy before a PBI is "Done" (e.g., code reviewed, unit tested, integrated, documented).
- [ ] **Burndown Chart** plots *remaining* work on the Y-axis vs. *time* on the X-axis.
- [ ] **Burnup Chart** plots *completed* work — preferred for tracking scope changes.
- [ ] **Insulin Pump = Plan-Driven core** — because of FDA, IEC 62304, ISO 14971, and safety-criticality.
- [ ] **Insulin Pump can use Agile *spikes*** for early requirements exploration and UI prototyping.
- [ ] **Mentcare = Pure Agile** — because requirements are volatile, users are co-designers, and no safety-critical hardware exists.
- [ ] **Mentcare's biggest risks** are *data-privacy* (GDPR Article 9) and *interoperability* (HL7 FHIR, SNOMED-CT).
- [ ] **Hybrid Process** is the *professionally correct* answer for safety-critical medical devices — never recommend "pure Agile" for the insulin pump in the exam.
- [ ] **Agile is NOT faster** — it is *more responsive to change* and *lower-risk in late discovery*.
- [ ] **The "Iron Triangle"** of project management (scope, time, cost) is *fixed two, flex one* in Agile — scope is flexed every sprint, not the other dimensions.
- [ ] **Velocity Formula:** $V = \dfrac{\sum \text{SP Done}}{\text{Sprint Count}}$.
- [ ] **Release Forecast Formula:** $N = \lceil B / V \rceil$ — always apply the ceiling function.
- [ ] **DRE Formula:** $\text{DRE} = \dfrac{E}{E + D} \times 100$ — top metric for testing effectiveness in Agile.
- [ ] **Remember the KTU Examiner's favourite "Why not Agile for the insulin pump?" question** — the answer must cite *regulatory compliance, safety-criticality, hardware coupling, and exhaustive V&V* as the four pillars.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Agile Project Management + Case Studies**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functional and Non-Functional Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Functional and Non-Functional Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Software Requirements

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Software Engineering (PEEET524), **Software Requirements** are the fundamental descriptions of *what* the system must do and *how well* it must do it. They form the contractual foundation between stakeholders, developers, and end-users. The IEEE Standard 830 / IEEE 29148 classifies requirements into two principal categories: **Functional Requirements (FR)** and **Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> A **Functional Requirement** specifies a particular behavior, function, or feature of the system — i.e., the *specific actions* a system must be capable of performing. A **Non-Functional Requirement** (also called a *quality attribute* or *constraint*) specifies the criteria by which the operation of a system is judged, rather than its specific behaviors.

## 1.2 Functional Requirements (FR) — The "What"

**Functional Requirements** describe the *core services* the system should provide, how the system reacts to particular inputs, and how the system should behave in specific situations. They are **behavioral** in nature.

**Examples:**
- The ATM shall dispense cash when a valid PIN and sufficient balance are entered.
- The login module shall authenticate users against a stored database.
- The e-commerce portal shall generate an invoice in PDF format upon order confirmation.

## 1.3 Non-Functional Requirements (NFR) — The "How Well"

**Non-Functional Requirements** are constraints on the *quality attributes* of the system. They describe *how well* the system performs its functions. They apply to the system as a whole, rather than to individual features.

**Examples:**
- The ATM must dispense cash within **3 seconds** of PIN validation.
- The login module must handle **10,000 concurrent users**.
- The e-commerce portal must be available **99.99%** of the time (uptime).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:**
> A common student mistake is treating **NFRs as "less important"** because they are not features. In reality, NFRs often *decide the architectural style* of the system. For example, "system must respond in <200ms" forces the choice between a monolithic vs. microservices architecture.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant"

Imagine you are designing a **Restaurant** system:

| Aspect | Functional Requirement | Non-Functional Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | "What the system does" | "How well the system does it" |
| **Restaurant Analogy** | The menu items served (Biryani, Dosa, Coffee) | Service speed, hygiene, ambience, seating capacity |
| **Testable?** | Yes (you can verify biryani was served) | Yes (you can measure service time, check ISO ratings) |
| **Failure Impact** | A dish is missing from the menu | Food arrives cold after 2 hours |

The **dishes** (functional) are useless if the **service** (non-functional) is terrible. Similarly, software features are useless if the system is slow, insecure, or unreliable.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:**
> **FR = "Functions and Features"** → Verbs (calculate, validate, send, display)
> **NFR = "Qualities and Constraints"** → Adjectives (fast, secure, reliable, portable)

## 1.5 Why This Distinction Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. The ability to *correctly classify* a requirement as functional or non-functional directly maps to:
- **CO2**: *Identify and analyze software requirements for a given problem.*
- **Bloom's Level**: *Understand / Analyze*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Taxonomy of Functional Requirements

Functional Requirements can be sub-classified as follows:

1. **Business Requirements**: High-level objectives of the organization (e.g., "Increase online sales by 20%").
2. **User Requirements**: User-level goals the system must support (e.g., "Customer should be able to track orders").
3. **System Requirements**: Detailed functional descriptions of system services (e.g., "The order-tracking module shall poll the shipping API every 30 seconds").
4. **Functional Specifications**: Precise mathematical/algorithmic statements (e.g., "Tax = (Subtotal × 0.18) + Shipping Fee").

### 2.1.1 Common Functional Categories
- **Data Processing**: Calculations, transformations, validations.
- **Interface/Interaction**: User inputs, screen flows, navigation logic.
- **Data Storage/Retrieval**: Database operations, queries, archival.
- **Communication**: API calls, messaging, notifications.
- **Security Functionalities**: Authentication, authorization, audit logs.

## 2.2 Detailed Taxonomy of Non-Functional Requirements

The industry-standard classification is the **FURPS+ Model** (Grady, Hewlett-Packard):

| Category | Acronym Expansion | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **F**unctional | (Already covered above — note that FURPS+ originally used 'F' ambiguously) | — |
| **U**sability | Ease of use, learnability, aesthetics | "System shall be operable by a 10th-grade literate user" |
| **R**eliability | Availability, MTBF, fault tolerance, recoverability | "System availability ≥ 99.95% with MTBF ≥ 720 hours" |
| **P**erformance | Throughput, response time, capacity, scalability | "Page load time ≤ 2 seconds for 95th percentile" |
| **S**upportability | Maintainability, testability, configurability, installability | "Code coverage ≥ 80% for unit tests" |
| **+** Design Constraints | Physical, environmental, regulatory | "Must comply with GDPR" |
| **+** Implementation | Language, tools, standards | "Backend must be developed in Java 17" |
| **+** Interface | External system constraints | "Must integrate with SAP ERP via RESTful API" |
| **+** Physical | Hardware, network topology | "Must run on 8GB RAM, 256GB SSD" |

## 2.3 Other Important NFR Categories for KTU 2024

- **Security (often its own category)**: Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Authorization, Non-repudiation (the *CIAAN* triad).
- **Maintainability**: Modularity, reusability, analyzability, modifiability, testability.
- **Portability**: Adaptability, installability, conformance, replaceability.
- **Compatibility**: Co-existence, interoperability with legacy systems.
- **Localization (L10n) / Internationalization (i18n)**: Multi-language, multi-currency support.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Concept Matrix

| Attribute | Type | Description | Sample Metric / Unit | Testable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Functional | FR | Specific behavior of system | Pass/Fail test case | Yes |
| Performance | NFR | Response/throughput under load | Latency (ms), RPS | Yes |
| Availability | NFR | Uptime percentage | % (e.g., 99.9%) | Yes |
| Reliability | NFR | Failure-free operation | MTBF (hours), MTTR (min) | Yes |
| Security | NFR | Resistance to attack | OWASP compliance | Yes |
| Usability | NFR | Ease of use | SUS Score (0–100) | Yes |
| Maintainability | NFR | Ease of modification | Cyclomatic Complexity | Yes |
| Portability | NFR | Platform independence | Number of supported OS | Yes |

## 2.5 Key Trade-offs: FR vs. NFR

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:**
> Functional and Non-Functional Requirements are **NOT independent** — they exhibit **trade-offs**:
>
> - Adding a *functional* feature (e.g., a new payment gateway) may **degrade** the *performance NFR* (slower checkout).
> - A *reliability* NFR (e.g., 99.999% availability) increases *cost* and *complexity*.
> - The **"Iron Triangle"** of project management: **Scope (FR) ↔ Cost ↔ Time** is influenced heavily by NFRs.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

This classification is foundational in:
- **DevOps/SRE**: NFRs become **SLOs** (Service Level Objectives).
- **Architecture**: NFRs drive **architectural patterns** (e.g., high availability → load balancers + redundancy).
- **Testing**: FRs are tested via *functional tests*; NFRs are tested via *non-functional tests* (load, stress, security, usability).
- **Contract Law**: SLAs (Service Level Agreements) are legally binding NFRs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The "SMART" Framework for Specifying Requirements

A KTU-favored method to specify *both* FR and NFR is using the **SMART** criteria. Let us derive this step by step.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of the SMART Framework

**Step 1 — Identify the need.**
A stakeholder states a vague desire: "The system should be fast."

**Step 2 — Apply the 'S' (Specific).**
Eliminate ambiguity. Specify the subject and the action.

$$\text{Requirement: The search API shall return query results.}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 'M' (Measurable).**
Attach a quantitative metric.

$$\text{Requirement: The search API shall return query results within } \leq 200 \text{ ms.}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the 'A' (Attainable).**
Verify feasibility with the engineering team given hardware constraints. If the database is on a separate server, 200 ms is *attainable*; if it is on a cold standby, it is not.

**Step 5 — Apply the 'R' (Relevant).**
Ensure the metric supports a business goal. The 200 ms aligns with the business goal of reducing bounce rate.

**Step 6 — Apply the 'T' (Time-bound).**
Define the measurement window and conditions.

$$\text{Requirement: The search API shall return query results in } \leq 200 \text{ ms, measured at the 95th percentile (p95) over any rolling 24-hour window.}$$

### 3.1.2 Final SMART Spec

The above derivation yields a **complete, testable NFR specification**. This 6-step derivation is frequently asked in KTU ESE questions (Module 2, 14-mark level).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Functional Requirement Specification

Let us derive a **comprehensive specification template** step by step.

### 3.2.1 The Volere Template (Step-by-Step Construction)

The Volere Requirements Specification Template is widely used. Its construction follows these logical steps:

**Step 1 — Assign a unique identifier (FR-NNN).**
Each functional requirement is given a serial number for traceability.

**Step 2 — Define the event/Trigger.**
Specify the *precondition* that initiates the function.

$$\text{Precondition: User is logged in AND cart contains } \geq 1 \text{ item.}$$

**Step 3 — Define the input.**
What data does the function consume?

$$\text{Input: } \langle \text{user\_id}, \text{item\_ids[]}, \text{shipping\_address}, \text{payment\_method} \rangle$$

**Step 4 — Define the processing logic.**
The transformation or business rule applied.

$$\text{Logic: } \text{Total} = \sum(\text{item\_price} \times \text{quantity}) + \text{ShippingFee} - \text{Discount}$$

$$\text{Tax} = \text{Total} \times \text{0.18}$$

$$\text{FinalAmount} = \text{Total} + \text{Tax}$$

**Step 5 — Define the output.**
The result produced.

$$\text{Output: } \langle \text{order\_id}, \text{invoice\_pdf}, \text{confirmation\_email\_status} \rangle$$

**Step 6 — Define the postcondition.**
The state of the system after execution.

$$\text{Postcondition: Order is persisted in DB with status='PAID'; inventory is decremented.}$$

**Step 7 — Define side effects.**
External interactions triggered.

$$\text{Side Effects: Payment gateway charged; email sent; SMS notification dispatched.}$$

This 7-step derivation is the **gold standard** for FR specification in KTU 2024 examinations.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Non-Functional Requirement Metrics

NFRs must be **quantified** to be testable. Below is a derivation-style table of common NFR metrics:

### 3.3.1 Availability Derivation

**Availability (A)** is derived from the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) and Mean Time To Repair (MTTR):

$$A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$$

**Derivation Walkthrough:**

**Step 1:** Define MTTF (reliability metric).
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{\text{Total Operational Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$$

**Step 2:** Define MTTR (recoverability metric).
$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$$

**Step 3:** Combine into Availability.

**Step 4: Numerical Example.**
