Given: System fails **2 times per year**, each recovery takes **1 hour**.
Total operation time = 365 × 24 = 8760 hours.
Downtime = 2 × 1 = 2 hours.
MTTF = (8760 - 2) / 2 = 4379 hours.
MTTR = 2 / 2 = 1 hour.

$$A = \frac{4379}{4379 + 1} \times 100\% = 99.977\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> This corresponds to the **"Three Nines"** of availability (99.9%). Achieving **"Four Nines"** (99.99%) is significantly harder and 10× more expensive.

### 3.3.2 Performance — Throughput and Latency

**Throughput (T)**: The number of transactions per unit time.

$$T = \frac{N_{\text{transactions}}}{t_{\text{interval}}} \quad \text{(e.g., 5000 TPS)}$$

**Latency (L)**: The time to complete a single transaction.

$$L = t_{\text{response}} - t_{\text{request}} \quad \text{(e.g., 150 ms)}$$

**Little's Law** (a fundamental KTU concept linking the two):

$$L = \lambda \times W$$

Where $\lambda$ is the arrival rate (throughput) and $W$ is the average waiting time (latency).

**Step-by-step numerical verification:**
If a server handles $\lambda = 100$ requests/sec and each request spends $W = 0.05$ sec in the system:

$$L = 100 \times 0.05 = 5 \text{ concurrent requests}$$

This is the *minimum number of concurrent threads* the server must handle to avoid queue overflow — a **critical Sizing NFR**.

## 3.4 Classification Algorithm (Decision Tree)

A KTU 2024 favorite: classifying a statement as FR or NFR. The decision tree is:

```
Is the statement about a specific behavior/function?
   ├── YES → Functional Requirement (FR)
   └── NO  → Is it about a quality/constraint/quality metric?
              ├── YES → Non-Functional Requirement (NFR)
              └── NO  → Ambiguous — needs clarification
```

> [!TIP]
> **Heuristic Rule**: If the sentence starts with a **verb** (calculate, send, validate) → likely **FR**. If the sentence starts with an **adjective** (fast, secure, available) or **constraint** (must, shall be) → likely **NFR**.

## 3.5 Requirements Elicitation Workflow (Exhaustive)

The complete, end-to-end process for deriving requirements from stakeholders:

**Step 1: Stakeholder Identification** — List all direct and indirect users.

**Step 2: Goal Modeling** — Use *i\* framework* or *KAOS* to derive goals from goals.

**Step 3: Scenario Development** — Write concrete use cases from goals.

**Step 4: Requirement Extraction** — From scenarios, extract atomic FR and NFR.

**Step 5: Negotiation & Prioritization** — Use **MoSCoW** (Must, Should, Could, Won't) to resolve conflicts.

**Step 6: Specification** — Document using Volere / IEEE 29148 template.

**Step 7: Validation** — Review with stakeholders; check for correctness, completeness, consistency, traceability.

**Step 8: Management** — Store in a Requirements Management Tool (e.g., Jama, DOORS, JIRA).

This 8-step workflow is the **complete Requirements Engineering process** referenced in Module 2 of the KTU syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Master Classification of Requirements

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Software Requirements] --> B[Functional Requirements]
    A --> C[Non-Functional Requirements]
    B --> B1[Business Requirements]
    B --> B2[User Requirements]
    B --> B3[System Requirements]
    B --> B4[Functional Specifications]
    C --> C1[Usability]
    C --> C2[Reliability]
    C --> C3[Performance]
    C --> C4[Supportability]
    C --> C5[Security]
    C --> C6[Portability]
    C --> C7[Design Constraints]
    A --> D[Domain Requirements]
    A --> E[Inverse Requirements]
    A --> F[Design and Implementation Constraints]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The root node `Software Requirements` branches into the two primary KTU categories. The sub-branches represent the FURPS+ decomposition and supplementary classifications. This diagram is the **canonical answer diagram** for any "Classify software requirements" question in the KTU 2024 ESE.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: FURPS+ Detailed Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph LR
    FURPS[FURPS Plus Model] --> F[Functional]
    FURPS --> U[Usability]
    FURPS --> R[Reliability]
    FURPS --> P[Performance]
    FURPS --> S[Supportability]
    FURPS --> P2[Plus Constraints]
    P2 --> PC1[Design Constraints]
    P2 --> PC2[Implementation Constraints]
    P2 --> PC3[Interface Constraints]
    P2 --> PC4[Physical Constraints]
    U --> U1[Learnability]
    U --> U2[Efficiency of Use]
    U --> U3[Memorability]
    U --> U4[Error Tolerance]
    R --> R1[Availability]
    R --> R2[MTBF MTTR]
    R --> R3[Fault Tolerance]
    R --> R4[Recoverability]
    P --> P3[Throughput]
    P --> P4[Response Time]
    P --> P5[Scalability]
    P --> P6[Capacity]
    S --> S1[Maintainability]
    S --> S2[Testability]
    S --> S3[Configurability]
    S --> S4[Installability]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Requirements Engineering Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Stakeholder Identification] --> S2[Step 2 Goal Modeling]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Scenario Development]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Requirement Extraction]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Negotiation and Prioritization]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Specification]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Validation]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Requirements Management]
    S8 --> S9{Approved by Stakeholders?}
    S9 -->|Yes| DONE[Baseline Requirements]
    S9 -->|No| S5
    S1 -.Feedback Loop.-> S5
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: The Requirements Trade-off Triangle

```mermaid
graph TD
    F[Functional Features] <--> P[Performance NFR]
    F <--> C[Cost]
    P <--> C
    F -->|More Features Degrades| P
    P -->|Higher Performance Costs More| C
    C -->|More Budget Enables More| F
    P -->|Tuning May Add| F
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Visual Takeaway:** This triangle visualizes the inherent trade-offs. A 14-mark question may ask you to *analyze the trade-offs* when adding a new functional feature to a system with strict performance NFRs.

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: SMART Specification Construction (State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Vague
    Vague --> Specific: Apply S Specific
    Specific --> Measurable: Apply M Measurable
    Measurable --> Attainable: Apply A Attainable
    Attainable --> Relevant: Apply R Relevant
    Relevant --> TimeBound: Apply T Time Bound
    TimeBound --> Testable: Final Verification
    Testable --> [*]
```

## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Requirements in SDLC

```mermaid
graph TD
    REQ[Requirements Phase] --> DES[Design Phase]
    DES --> IMP[Implementation Phase]
    IMP --> TEST[Testing Phase]
    TEST --> DEP[Deployment Phase]
    DEP --> MNT[Maintenance Phase]
    REQ -->|NFRs drive| ARCH[Architectural Decisions]
    ARCH --> DES
    TEST -->|Validates| REQ
    MNT -->|Change Requests Feed Back| REQ
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Insight:** Notice the *feedback loops* — testing validates requirements, and maintenance generates change requests that update requirements. This is the **closed-loop traceability** demanded by KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Functional Requirements with two examples. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Functional Requirements** are statements that describe the *specific behaviors, functions, or features* a system must provide. They define what the system does in response to inputs and under specific conditions.
>
> **Examples:**
> 1. *The system shall allow users to reset their password via email verification.*
> 2. *The system shall calculate the total bill amount as the sum of all item prices plus applicable taxes.*

### Question 2: Differentiate between Functional and Non-Functional Requirements. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Functional Requirement | Non-Functional Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Specifies *what* the system does | Specifies *how well* the system performs |
| **Also called** | Behavioral requirements | Quality attributes / Constraints |
| **Nature** | Verifiable through test cases | Verifiable through metrics / measurements |
| **Example** | "Login system shall authenticate users" | "Login shall complete within 2 seconds for 1000 concurrent users" |
| **Priority** | Directly tied to business value | Often tied to user experience & SLA |
| **Phase influence** | Drives feature development | Drives architectural decisions |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently **swap the example** of FR and NFR. Remember: a *behavior* (e.g., "system shall encrypt data") is **functional**; a *quality measure* (e.g., "encryption shall use AES-256 with <5ms overhead") is **non-functional**. Mixing them costs you **1.5 marks** out of 3.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: Explain the FURPS+ model for classifying non-functional requirements. List and briefly describe any six quality attributes with examples. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### (a) The FURPS+ Model — Definition and Origin (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**FURPS+** is an acronym introduced by **Robert Grady** at Hewlett-Packard. It is a widely adopted model for classifying software quality attributes (i.e., **Non-Functional Requirements**). The acronym stands for:

- **F** — **Functional** (functional features, in the original sense of capabilities)
- **U** — **Usability**
- **R** — **Reliability**
- **P** — **Performance**
- **S** — **Supportability**
- **+** — **Plus constraints** (Design, Implementation, Interface, Physical)

The "+" extends the original five categories to include auxiliary constraints that influence the architectural and implementation decisions of the system. FURPS+ is typically applied during **Requirements Engineering** and **Software Architecture** phases of the SDLC, providing a structured way to ensure *completeness* of the NFR specification.

> **Why is FURPS+ important in KTU 2024?**
> It provides a **checklist** to ensure no NFR category is missed during elicitation, addressing the CO2 outcome: *"Identify and analyze software requirements for a given problem."*

#### (b) Six Quality Attributes with Examples (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

| # | Attribute | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Usability** | The ease with which users can learn and operate the system. | "A new user shall complete the checkout process within 3 minutes without training." |
| 2 | **Reliability** | The ability of the system to perform its intended function under stated conditions for a specified period. | "The system shall have an MTBF of at least 1000 hours and recover from a crash within 60 seconds." |
| 3 | **Performance** | The responsiveness and throughput of the system under a workload. | "The search API shall return results within 200 ms for 95% of queries, handling 5000 requests per second." |
| 4 | **Supportability** | The ease with which the system can be maintained, tested, and extended. | "All modules shall have unit test coverage of at least 80% and conform to coding standards (MISRA-C)." |
| 5 | **Security** | The system's ability to protect data and resist unauthorized access. | "All passwords shall be stored using bcrypt hashing with a minimum cost factor of 12." |
| 6 | **Portability** | The ease with which the system can be transferred from one environment to another. | "The mobile app shall run natively on Android 11+ and iOS 15+ without code modification." |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often write *one-word answers* like "Usability means easy to use." This is **incomplete** and loses **2–3 marks**. The examiner expects: **(i) Definition, (ii) Sub-attribute, (iii) Quantitative example with a metric.** Always quantify!

---

### Question B: What are Functional Requirements? Explain the structure of a well-written functional requirement specification using a suitable template. Illustrate with an example. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### (a) Functional Requirements — Definition and Characteristics (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Functional Requirements (FR)** are statements that describe the *specific services, functions, or features* a system must perform. They define:

1. **Inputs** the system accepts
2. **Operations** the system performs on those inputs
3. **Outputs** the system produces
4. **Behavior** in response to normal, abnormal, and edge-case inputs
5. **State changes** (e.g., database updates, session management)

**Characteristics of a good FR specification:**

- **Complete**: Covers all functional cases
- **Correct**: Accurately reflects stakeholder needs
- **Unambiguous**: Has only one interpretation
- **Verifiable**: Can be tested via test cases
- **Traceable**: Has a unique ID and links to design/code
- **Modifiable**: Easy to update without breaking references

#### (b) Volere-Style Specification Template with Example (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Template Structure:**

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| Requirement ID | Unique identifier (e.g., FR-007) |
| Title | Short descriptive name |
| Description | Narrative explanation |
| Rationale | Why this requirement exists |
| Source | Stakeholder / document origin |
| Trigger / Precondition | Condition that initiates the function |
| Input | Data required |
| Processing Logic | Business rules / calculations |
| Output | Result produced |
| Postcondition | System state after execution |
| Side Effects | External interactions |
| Assumptions | Pre-existing conditions |
| Priority | MoSCoW rating |

**Example Specification — "Place Order":**

- **FR-012: Place Order**
- **Description**: The system shall allow a registered customer to place an order for items in their shopping cart.
- **Trigger / Precondition**: User is authenticated and the cart contains at least one item.
- **Input**: $\langle \text{user\_id}, \text{cart\_items}, \text{shipping\_address}, \text{payment\_method} \rangle$
- **Processing Logic**:
$$\text{Subtotal} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{price}_i \times \text{qty}_i)$$
$$\text{Tax} = \text{Subtotal} \times 0.18$$
$$\text{Total} = \text{Subtotal} + \text{Tax} + \text{ShippingFee} - \text{Discount}$$
- **Output**: $\langle \text{order\_id}, \text{invoice\_pdf\_url}, \text{estimated\_delivery\_date} \rangle$
- **Postcondition**: Order is persisted in the `orders` table with status `PAID`; inventory is decremented.
- **Side Effects**: Payment gateway is charged via secure API; confirmation email and SMS are sent.
- **Priority**: **Must Have** (MoSCoW).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:**
> A 14-mark question on FR specification **requires a concrete example with calculations**. Students who write only theory lose **5–7 marks**. Always include: (i) a numbered ID, (ii) clear inputs/outputs, (iii) at least one mathematical formula, and (iv) a postcondition.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2**

- **Functional Requirements (FR)** describe *what* the system does — they are **behavioral, verifiable through test cases**, and start with verbs (e.g., calculate, send, validate).
- **Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)** describe *how well* the system performs — they are **quality attributes / constraints**, often starting with adjectives or "shall be" (e.g., fast, secure, available).
- The **FURPS+** model is the **gold-standard classification** for NFRs — remember: **F**unctional, **U**sability, **R**eliability, **P**erformance, **S**upportability, **+** Design/Implementation/Interface/Physical constraints.
- NFRs are **quantified** via metrics: **availability (%)**, **MTBF/MTTR (hours)**, **latency (ms)**, **throughput (RPS)**, **SUS score (0–100)**, **code coverage (%)**.
- **SMART** framework converts vague requirements into testable ones: **Specific, Measurable, Attainable, Relevant, Time-bound**.
- **Volere Template** is a comprehensive FR specification structure with 12+ fields including preconditions, postconditions, and side effects.
- The **Availability formula** is $A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$.
- **Little's Law** $L = \lambda \times W$ links concurrency, throughput, and latency.
- **Trade-offs** always exist between FR (more features) and NFR (performance/cost). Architecture decisions are heavily driven by NFRs.
- **MoSCoW prioritization** is the standard for resolving requirement conflicts: **M**ust, **S**hould, **C**ould, **W**on't (this time).
- **KTU-favorite exam question patterns**:
  1. "Classify the following as FR or NFR" (3 marks) — *table format expected*.
  2. "Explain FURPS+ with examples" (14 marks) — *6 attributes with quantified examples mandatory*.
  3. "Write a detailed FR specification for [feature]" (14 marks) — *Volere template + math required*.
  4. "Differentiate between FR and NFR" (3 or 14 marks) — *always include a comparison table*.
- **Common exam blunders to AVOID**:
  - ❌ Writing "Usability = easy to use" (too vague — quantify!).
  - ❌ Confusing reliability with availability (reliability = failure frequency; availability = uptime %).
  - ❌ Omitting metrics in NFR examples (always quantify: %, ms, RPS, etc.).
  - ❌ Forgetting postconditions in FR specification.
  - ❌ Treating NFRs as "optional" — they are **architecturally decisive**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** "**FURPS+** is a **FUR-PS** + plus" → think of a **fur coat** that is **usable, reliable, performs well, supportable** + extra constraints. Wear it well in the exam! 🧥

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirements Engineering Processes. Requirements Elicitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Requirements Engineering Processes. Requirements Elicitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Requirements Engineering (RE)?

**Requirements Engineering (RE)** is the systematic and disciplined approach to the *description, specification, analysis, elicitation, validation, documentation, and management* of software system requirements. It is the **bridge** between the informal, often fuzzy real-world needs of stakeholders and the precise, formal specifications that drive software design.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, RE encompasses **four core processes** that run iteratively (and often concurrently) throughout the software development life cycle:

| # | Core RE Process | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Requirements Elicitation** | Discovering & gathering requirements from stakeholders |
| 2 | **Requirements Analysis & Negotiation** | Refining, modelling, prioritizing & resolving conflicts |
| 3 | **Requirements Specification** | Documenting requirements formally (SRS) |
| 4 | **Requirements Validation & Management** | Checking correctness + handling changes (traceability) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition:** *Requirements Engineering is the branch of Software Engineering concerned with the real-world goals, functions, and constraints of software-intensive systems — often described as the "what" phase that must be completed rigorously before any "how" (design) begins.*

## 1.2 Requirements Elicitation — The Formal Definition

**Requirements Elicitation** (also called *Requirements Discovery* or *Requirements Acquisition*) is the **first and most critical process** within Requirements Engineering. It is the activity of **proactively discovering, uncovering, and collecting** the requirements of a system from all relevant stakeholders, documents, existing systems, and the application domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The word **"Elicit"** literally means *"to draw out"*. Unlike simple "gathering" or "taking orders," elicitation implies that many requirements are **hidden, unspoken, contradictory, or unknown even to the stakeholders themselves**. The analyst's job is to *draw them out* using structured techniques.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🏠

> **Building a Custom Dream House** 🏡
> 
> Imagine you are an architect asked to design a house for a family. **Requirements Elicitation** is the process you go through *before* drawing a single blueprint:
> 
> 1. You don't just ask *"What do you want?"* (closed question → poor results).
> 2. You conduct a long **interview** with the family: *"Tell me about your daily routine"*.
> 3. You **observe** how they currently use their old house.
> 4. You give them a **questionnaire** about preferences.
> 5. You build a small **prototype** (3D model) so they can react: *"Oh, I didn't realize the kitchen would feel this small — let's change it."*
> 6. You study **similar houses** (domain analysis) and discuss with masons/carpenters (domain experts).
> 7. You bring the whole family to a **workshop** to resolve conflicts (Mom wants a big kitchen, Dad wants a study room).
> 
> That entire multi-technique discovery process = **Requirements Elicitation** in Software Engineering. The blueprint you eventually draw = **SRS Document**.
> 
> *Without elicitation, you'd hand over a beautiful house with no bathrooms — a literal disaster.*

## 1.4 Why Elicitation is the Hardest Part of RE

> [!WARNING]
> **Industry Statistics (Standish Group CHAOS Report):**
> - **~44%** of all software project failures are attributed to **poor or incomplete requirements**.
> - Of those, the **majority** originate from inadequate elicitation — not from bad coding or design.
> - Common phrase: *"If we build the wrong system perfectly, we have still failed."*

Key reasons elicitation is hard:
- **Stakeholders often don't know what they really want** (tacit knowledge).
- **Different stakeholders have conflicting goals** (e.g., user wants simplicity, manager wants control, regulator wants compliance).
- **Domain experts use jargon** that developers may misinterpret.
- **Political & organizational constraints** affect what people will admit they need.

## 1.5 Sources of Requirements

A requirement can originate from **any** of the following five sources:

1. **Stakeholders** — Users, customers, managers, regulators, sponsors (most important).
2. **Documents** — Existing system manuals, business plans, standards, legacy SRS, RFPs.
3. **Application Domain** — Domain knowledge, regulatory rules, scientific principles (e.g., tax calculation rules in accounting software).
4. **Existing / Competing Systems** — Reverse-engineering features from competitor products or legacy systems.
5. **Operating Environment** — Hardware constraints, OS, network bandwidth, physical environment (e.g., medical device used in sterile operating rooms).

## 1.6 The Stakeholder Landscape

A **Stakeholder** is *anyone who is affected by, has an influence on, or has an interest in* the system. KTU expects students to identify and categorize stakeholders.

| Stakeholder Type | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Direct Users (End Users)** | Daily operators of the system |
| **Customers / Clients** | Who pay for the system |
| **Managers / Sponsors** | Who authorize the budget |
| **Domain Experts** | Subject matter specialists (e.g., doctors for medical software) |
| **Regulators / Legal** | Govt. compliance, audit teams |
| **Maintenance Teams** | Who will support the system post-deployment |
| **External Systems** | APIs, databases, hardware dependencies |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Requirements Elicitation Process — Step-by-Step Logic

Elicitation is **not a single activity** — it is a structured process with **six logical phases**:

### Step 1: Identify Stakeholders (Stakeholder Identification)
- Identify **all** parties who will be affected by, will benefit from, or will constrain the system.
- Use techniques like **stakeholder analysis grids** (power vs. interest matrix).

### Step 2: Understand the Business Context (Domain Understanding)
- Study the **business processes**, workflows, organizational goals, and domain rules.
- Required to ask the *right* questions later.

### Step 3: Select Elicitation Technique(s)
- Choose **one or more** techniques (interview, observation, prototype, etc.) based on:
  - Project size & complexity
  - Stakeholder availability
  - Domain familiarity
  - Budget & timeline

### Step 4: Execute Elicitation Activities
- Conduct interviews, distribute questionnaires, run workshops, observe workflows, build prototypes.
- Document everything — including **non-functional** hints (e.g., "system feels slow").

### Step 5: Discover Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional + Constraints)
- Capture **functional requirements** (what the system does).
- Capture **non-functional requirements** (quality attributes: performance, security, usability).
- Capture **constraints** (technology, regulatory, budget).

### Step 6: Resolve Conflicts & Prioritize (Pre-Negotiation Step)
- Conflicts discovered during elicitation are noted and **flagged** for the negotiation phase.
- Initial prioritization using techniques like **MoSCoW** (Must / Should / Could / Won't).

## 2.2 The Seven Major Elicitation Techniques (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU board questions almost always test these seven techniques. Memorize the name, type, advantages, and disadvantages of each.

### Technique 1: 🗣️ Interviews (Most Common)

**Definition:** A structured or semi-structured conversation between the requirements analyst and one or more stakeholders.

| Interview Type | Description | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Closed / Structured** | Pre-defined set of fixed questions, same order for all interviewees | When stakeholders are many and you need quantitative comparison |
| **Open / Unstructured** | Free-flowing conversation, no fixed agenda | When exploring unfamiliar domains, discovering tacit knowledge |
| **Semi-Structured** | Mix of fixed questions + open-ended probes | Most common in practice |

**Advantages:** Deep insights, builds rapport, allows probing, flexible.
**Disadvantages:** Time-consuming, depends on analyst's skill, geographically constrained, only as good as the interviewee's recall.

### Technique 2: 📋 Questionnaires / Surveys

**Definition:** A written list of questions (open or closed) distributed to a large group of stakeholders for self-completion.

**Advantages:** Reaches large audiences cheaply, quantitative data, anonymous responses reduce bias.
**Disadvantages:** Low return rates, no opportunity to probe deeper, requires literacy, inflexible.

### Technique 3: 👀 Observation (Ethnography / Social Analysis)

**Definition:** The analyst **passively or actively observes** stakeholders performing their real tasks in their natural environment (workshop, factory floor, hospital).

> [!NOTE]
> **Ethnography** is a *deeper* form of observation rooted in anthropology — the analyst immerses themselves in the user's environment for extended periods to understand cultural and social practices.

**Advantages:** Reveals **actual** practices (not idealized), uncovers tacit knowledge, excellent for safety-critical systems.
**Disadvantages:** Time-intensive, may cause "Hawthorne effect" (people behave differently when observed), observer bias.

### Technique 4: 👥 Workshops / Focus Groups

**Definition:** A facilitated, structured group meeting (typically 5–10 stakeholders + 1–2 analysts) where requirements are elicited collaboratively.

**Advantages:** Builds consensus, surfaces conflicts early, faster than 1-on-1 interviews, cross-pollination of ideas.
**Disadvantages:** Dominant personalities can hijack the discussion, groupthink, scheduling difficulties.

### Technique 5: 💡 Brainstorming

**Definition:** An unstructured, idea-generation session where participants freely suggest requirements without criticism.

**Advantages:** Generates many ideas quickly, encourages creativity, engages team.
**Disadvantages:** Produces many impractical ideas, no formal output, requires skilled facilitator.

### Technique 6: 🛠️ Prototyping

**Definition:** Building a **quick, throw-away working model** (low-fidelity paper mock-up, high-fidelity interactive UI) of the system so stakeholders can *react* to it and refine requirements.

| Prototype Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Throwaway / Rapid** | Built to learn, then discarded |
| **Evolutionary** | Grows incrementally into the final system |
| **Horizontal** | Wide but shallow (UI only) |
| **Vertical** | Deep slice (one feature fully implemented) |

**Advantages:** *"A picture is worth a thousand words"* — stakeholders react better to something tangible; clarifies ambiguous requirements.
**Disadvantages:** Time-consuming to build, may set false expectations ("Is this the final system?"), stakeholder focus on UI may hide missing features.

### Technique 7: 📜 Document / Domain / Existing System Analysis

**Definition:** Studying **existing documents** (legacy SRS, manuals, regulations, RFPs), **competing systems**, and **domain knowledge sources** to extract requirements.

**Advantages:** Cheap, no stakeholder time needed, captures regulatory constraints.
**Disadvantages:** Documents may be outdated or incomplete, may miss true user needs.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

| Technique | Type | Stakeholder Involvement | Best For | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Interviews** | Interactive | High (1-on-1) | Deep insights, tacit knowledge | Time-consuming |
| **Questionnaires** | Passive | Low (one-shot) | Large user base, quantitative data | No probing |
| **Observation** | Passive | None during | Actual workflows, safety-critical | Hawthorne effect |
| **Workshops** | Interactive | High (group) | Consensus, conflict resolution | Groupthink |
| **Brainstorming** | Interactive | High (group) | Ideation, creativity | Many impractical ideas |
| **Prototyping** | Interactive | High | Ambiguous UI requirements | Time, false expectations |
| **Document Analysis** | Passive | None | Regulatory, legacy systems | May be outdated |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Industry

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Best Practice:** Mature organizations (Google, Microsoft, IBM, TCS, Infosys) **never rely on a single elicitation technique**. They use a **hybrid approach** combining:
> - **Interviews** with key stakeholders (depth)
> - **Questionnaires** with the broader user base (breadth)
> - **Workshops** for cross-functional alignment
> - **Prototyping** for ambiguous UI/UX requirements
> - **Document analysis** for regulatory/legal constraints
> - **Observation** in safety-critical domains (medical, aviation, nuclear)

## 2.5 Challenges in Requirements Elicitation

1. **Stakeholders may not know what they want** (especially for novel systems).
2. **Stakeholders may have conflicting requirements** (negotiation needed).
3. **Stakeholders may use domain-specific jargon** misunderstood by developers.
4. **Changing requirements** during elicitation (volatility).
5. **Political / organizational pressures** suppress honest statements.
6. **Geographic distribution** of stakeholders (remote teams).
7. **Tacit knowledge** — requirements embedded in habits, not articulated.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Decision Matrix

> Since Requirements Elicitation is a **process-oriented conceptual topic** (not a numerical one), the "derivations" in this section take the form of **step-by-step procedural analysis** and **analytical decision matrices** — exactly the depth KTU examiners expect.

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough 1 — Choosing the Right Elicitation Technique

**Problem Scenario:** Your team is building a **Hospital Management System (HMS)** for a 200-bed multi-specialty hospital. Stakeholders include doctors, nurses, admin staff, patients, insurance agencies, and the medical director. Budget is ₹80 lakh, timeline is 9 months.

**Step-by-Step Decision Analysis:**

**Step 1 — Stakeholder Analysis (Classify by Impact & Availability)**
The analyst first creates a stakeholder map:
- **High Power + High Interest** → Medical Director, Chief Surgeon (engage closely).
- **High Power + Low Interest** → Hospital CEO (keep informed).
- **Low Power + High Interest** → Nurses, patients (inform adequately).
- **Low Power + Low Interest** → Insurance agencies (minimal effort).

**Step 2 — Domain Analysis**
The hospital domain has **strict regulatory requirements** (NABH accreditation, ABDM guidelines, HL7/FHIR standards). This is non-negotiable.

**Step 3 — Technique Selection Matrix**

| Stakeholder Group | Selected Technique | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Doctors (15 nos.) | **Semi-Structured Interviews** | Doctors are busy; need deep but focused discussion |
| Nurses (80 nos.) | **Questionnaires + Observation** | Large group; observe actual ward workflows |
| Patients (200/day) | **Questionnaires + Workshop (sample)** | Need quantitative + qualitative insights |
| Medical Director | **Workshop (Executive)** | High-level consensus needed |
| Insurance Agencies | **Document Analysis** | Standardized claim formats — no interviews needed |
| Domain Rules (HL7, NABH) | **Document Analysis** | Regulatory standards are documented |

**Step 4 — Execution Order**
1. Start with **document analysis** to understand regulatory baseline.
2. Run **observation** in wards & OPDs to understand actual workflows.
3. Conduct **interviews** with doctors and director.
4. Distribute **questionnaires** to nurses and patients.
5. Run **workshops** to resolve conflicts (e.g., doctors want voice notes; nurses want structured forms).
6. Build **prototype** of the patient registration module → circulate for feedback.

**Step 5 — Output Artifacts**
- Stakeholder map
- Interview transcripts / notes
- Completed questionnaires (statistical summary)
- Observation logs
- Workshop minutes
- Prototype screenshots with feedback
- **Initial list of functional + non-functional + constraint requirements** → ready for Analysis phase.

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough 2 — The Interview Process (Step-by-Step)

KTU frequently asks: *"Explain the interview process for requirements elicitation"*. Here is the **complete step-by-step analytical flow**:

**Step 1 — Decide who to interview**
Identify stakeholders based on their domain expertise and system impact.

**Step 2 — Decide interview type**
Closed (structured), open (unstructured), or semi-structured — based on the goal.

**Step 3 — Prepare questions**
- Begin with **easy, non-threatening** questions (background, current role).
- Progress to **domain-specific** questions.
- End with **open-ended reflection** questions (*"What would make your job easier?"*).

**Step 4 — Prepare the interviewee**
Brief them on the system goal, duration, and confidentiality.

**Step 5 — Conduct the interview**
- Start with rapport-building.
- Use the **5 Whys** technique to uncover root causes.
- Avoid leading questions.
- Take notes (ideally audio-recorded with consent).

**Step 6 — Analyze & document responses**
Convert raw interview notes into structured requirements.

**Step 7 — Validate with interviewee**
Confirm accuracy of captured requirements in a follow-up.

**Step 8 — Resolve conflicts**
If two interviewees contradict each other, flag for negotiation.

## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough 3 — The 5 Whys Technique (Elicitation Helper)

Used to drill down from a surface statement to the **root requirement**.

```
Surface Statement: "We need the system to be fast."
        ↓ Why?
"Because patients wait too long at registration."
        ↓ Why?
"Because our staff enters data manually into 3 different forms."
        ↓ Why?
"Because there is no centralized database."
        ↓ Why?
"Because our legacy systems don't talk to each other."
        ↓ Why?
"Because each department procured its own software over 10 years."
        ↓
ROOT REQUIREMENT: "The system must provide a unified, single-point-of-entry 
                   patient registration with auto-population across modules."
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **5 Whys** is particularly useful during interviews and observation to uncover **latent** (hidden) requirements that the stakeholder themselves may not realize are the actual need.

## 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough 4 — Stakeholder Power-Interest Matrix (Decision Aid)

This is a common KTU question: *"How do you identify and prioritize stakeholders for elicitation?"*

|  | **Low Interest** | **High Interest** |
|---|---|---|
| **High Power** | Keep Informed (e.g., Hospital CEO) | Manage Closely (e.g., Medical Director) |
| **Low Power** | Monitor (e.g., External auditors) | Keep Satisfied (e.g., End-users, Nurses) |

- **Manage Closely**: Conduct detailed interviews, frequent workshops.
- **Keep Satisfied**: Inform via newsletters, conduct surveys.
- **Keep Informed**: Periodic status reports, formal communication.
- **Monitor**: Minimal engagement, automatic notifications.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What (High-Yield for KTU)

| Situation | Recommended Primary Technique | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Large, geographically dispersed user base | **Questionnaires** | Cost-effective, scalable |
| Safety-critical system (medical, aviation) | **Observation / Ethnography** | Real workflows, not stated ones |
| Highly ambiguous UI requirements | **Prototyping** | Visual feedback beats verbal description |
| Need to understand executive vision | **Interviews (Open)** | Tacit strategic knowledge |
| Cross-departmental conflicts | **Workshops** | Surfaces and resolves conflicts |
| Regulatory-heavy domain | **Document Analysis** | Captures legal constraints |
| Brainstorming new features | **Brainstorming** | Divergent thinking |
| Legacy system replacement | **Document Analysis + Observation** | Combines old rules with actual use |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU-Style Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Requirements Elicitation. List any four elicitation techniques.**

#### 📝 Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (2 Marks):**
> **Requirements Elicitation** (also known as *Requirements Discovery*) is the process of **proactively identifying, gathering, and uncovering** the requirements of a software system from various sources such as stakeholders, documents, domain knowledge, existing systems, and the operating environment. It is the *first step* of the Requirements Engineering process and is considered the most difficult because requirements are often hidden, conflicting, or unstated.

**Four Techniques (½ Mark each):**
1. **Interviews** — One-on-one conversations with stakeholders.
2. **Questionnaires / Surveys** — Written questions distributed to a large audience.
3. **Observation / Ethnography** — Watching users work in their natural environment.
4. **Workshops / Focus Groups** — Facilitated group sessions.
5. **Prototyping** — Building throwaway models to gather feedback.
6. **Brainstorming** — Free idea-generation sessions.
7. **Document / Domain Analysis** — Studying existing artifacts and domain rules.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between Closed (Structured) Interviews and Open (Unstructured) Interviews.**

#### 📝 Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Aspect | **Closed (Structured) Interview** | **Open (Unstructured) Interview** |
|---|---|---|
| **Question Type** | Fixed, pre-defined questions | Free-flowing, no fixed questions |
| **Order** | Same for all interviewees | Varies per interviewee |
| **Depth of Insight** | Surface-level, quantitative | Deep, qualitative, tacit knowledge |
| **Analyst Skill Needed** | Low–Moderate | High (probing ability critical) |
| **Time Required** | Shorter per interview | Longer per interview |
| **Best Used When** | Many stakeholders, comparison needed | Exploring unknown domains, strategic inputs |
| **Flexibility** | Rigid | Highly flexible |

> **Conclusion:** Structured interviews are efficient for breadth; unstructured interviews are powerful for depth. Most practical projects use a **semi-structured** combination.

---

## 5.2 KTU-Style Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> *Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B questions carry 14 marks with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Two alternative full-length options are provided below — exactly as in the board paper.*

---

### 🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a) Explain the Requirements Engineering (RE) process in detail. Describe the four major sub-processes of RE.** *(7 Marks)*

**(b) With a suitable real-world example, explain any five Requirements Elicitation Techniques in detail.** *(7 Marks)*

---

#### 📝 Model Answer to Q.A (a) — RE Process (7 Marks):

**1. Definition of RE (1 Mark):**
Requirements Engineering is a systematic, disciplined approach to describing, analyzing, eliciting, validating, documenting, and managing software system requirements. It transforms real-world stakeholder needs into precise specifications suitable for design.

**2. The Four Major Sub-Processes (6 Marks):**

**2.1 Requirements Elicitation (Discovery)**
- The process of identifying, gathering, and uncovering requirements from stakeholders, documents, existing systems, and the domain.
- Involves stakeholder identification, context understanding, technique selection, and execution.
- Output: An *initial set* of functional, non-functional, and constraint requirements.

**2.2 Requirements Analysis & Negotiation**
- Requirements are refined, modelled (using UML, DFDs), classified, and prioritized.
- **Conflicts** between stakeholders (e.g., cost vs. features) are identified and resolved through negotiation.
- Techniques: MoSCoW prioritization, conflict resolution matrices, cost-value analysis.
- Output: A *prioritized, consistent* set of requirements.

**2.3 Requirements Specification (Documentation)**
- The agreed requirements are documented formally as the **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** — typically following IEEE 830/29148 standards.
- Includes functional, non-functional, interface, and constraint specifications.
- May use natural language, structured specifications, or formal specification languages (Z, VDM).
- Output: The **SRS Document** (single source of truth).

**2.4 Requirements Validation & Management**
- **Validation** ensures requirements are *correct, complete, consistent, feasible, testable, and unambiguous*.
- Techniques: Reviews, walkthroughs, inspections, prototyping, acceptance test design.
- **Management** handles changes, traceability (forward & backward), and version control throughout the project.
- Output: Validated SRS + change management process.

> These four processes are **iterative**, not strictly linear — feedback loops exist between each.

---

#### 📝 Model Answer to Q.A (b) — Five Elicitation Techniques (7 Marks):

**Real-World Context:** We are building a **Library Management System** for a university with 5,000 students, 200 staff, and 50 librarians.

**Technique 1 — Interviews (1.5 Marks)**
We conduct semi-structured interviews with the Chief Librarian (open questions about long-term vision) and with department heads (closed questions about specific reports). This captures both strategic and tactical needs.

**Technique 2 — Questionnaires (1.5 Marks)**
A 15-question Google Form is emailed to all 5,000 students: *"How often do you use the library? What features do you want?"*. 800 responses received → quantitative data on most-requested features.

**Technique 3 — Observation (1.5 Marks)**
The analyst spends 3 days in the library watching students and staff: how they search for books, where bottlenecks occur, how overdue fines are handled. Reveals *actual* behavior vs. *stated* behavior (e.g., students say they use the OPAC; observation shows most ask the librarian).

**Technique 4 — Workshops (1 Mark)**
A 4-hour workshop with 8 stakeholders (chief librarian, 2 staff, 3 department reps, IT head) to resolve conflicts: *"Should digital resources be downloadable?"* → consensus on DRM-protected streaming.

**Technique 5 — Prototyping (1.5 Marks)**
A low-fidelity paper prototype of the book-search screen is shown to students → they immediately request a *recently-returned* filter that nobody had mentioned earlier. High-fidelity HTML prototype is then built for the circulation desk module.

> **Conclusion:** No single technique is sufficient. A **hybrid approach** combining interviews, questionnaires, observation, workshops, and prototyping yields the most complete requirements set.

---

### 🔷 QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]`

**(a) What is Requirements Elicitation? Discuss its importance and major challenges.** *(7 Marks)*

**(b) Compare and contrast Observation (Ethnography) and Prototyping as elicitation techniques. Under what circumstances would you prefer one over the other?** *(7 Marks)*

---

#### 📝 Model Answer to Q.B (a) — Importance & Challenges (7 Marks):

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Requirements Elicitation is the systematic process of discovering and gathering software requirements from stakeholders, documents, existing systems, and the application domain. It is the *first* and most critical step in Requirements Engineering.

**Importance (3 Marks):**

1. **Foundation of the Project:** All subsequent phases (design, coding, testing) depend on accurate elicitation. Garbage in = garbage out.
2. **Reduces Rework:** Studies show that a requirement defect fixed post-deployment costs **50–200× more** than one fixed during elicitation.
3. **Ensures Stakeholder Alignment:** Proper elicitation surfaces hidden expectations, builds shared understanding, and reduces future conflicts.
4. **Captures Non-Functional Requirements:** User interviews and observation often reveal quality attributes (performance, usability) that pure documentation misses.
5. **Prevents Project Failure:** The Standish Group reports ~44% of project failures trace back to poor requirements — most originating at the elicitation stage.

**Major Challenges (3 Marks):**

1. **Tacit Knowledge:** Stakeholders often *do not know* what they want (especially for novel systems); many requirements are unspoken.
2. **Conflicting Requirements:** Different stakeholders have competing goals (e.g., security team wants strict access; users want convenience).
3. **Communication Barriers:** Domain jargon, geographic distribution, and cultural differences hinder clear understanding.
4. **Changing Requirements:** Stakeholders' needs evolve during elicitation — "scope creep" begins here.
5. **Organizational & Political Constraints:** People may withhold information due to office politics, fear of job loss, or budget sensitivity.
6. **Analyst Skill:** Poorly skilled analysts ask leading questions, miss non-verbal cues, and fail to probe deeper.

> Effective elicitation requires a **skilled analyst**, a **structured process**, and a **mix of techniques**.

---

#### 📝 Model Answer to Q.B (b) — Observation vs. Prototyping (7 Marks):

| Parameter | **Observation / Ethnography** | **Prototyping** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Analyst passively watches users perform real tasks in their natural environment. | Building a working model of the system for users to interact with and react to. |
| **User Involvement** | Minimal during observation; users just work normally | High — users actively interact, critique, and refine the prototype |
| **Type of Knowledge Captured** | Actual current practices, tacit knowledge, workflow bottlenecks | Future desired behavior, UI preferences, feature clarity |
| **Time & Cost** | High time, low cost | High time, moderate cost |
| **Hawthorne Effect** | Yes — users may behave differently when observed | No — but may set false expectations ("Is this final?") |
| **Best For** | Safety-critical, complex legacy workflows, undefined domains | Ambiguous UI/UX, novel systems, customer-facing apps |
| **Output** | Workflow diagrams, task analysis, observation logs | Working (throwaway) model + feedback transcripts |
| **Strength** | Captures "what people actually do" vs. "what they say they do" | Forces concrete commitment to features and resolves ambiguity |

**When to Prefer Observation (2 Marks):**
Choose **Observation** when:
- The system is **safety-critical** (medical, aviation, nuclear) and you cannot rely on stated requirements alone.
- You are replacing a **legacy system** and must understand existing workflows in detail.
- Stakeholders are too busy for interviews but allow passive observation.

**When to Prefer Prototyping (2 Marks):**
Choose **Prototyping** when:
- Requirements are **ambiguous**, especially in the UI/UX layer.
- The system is **customer-facing** (e-commerce, banking app) where user reactions drive design.
- Stakeholders **cannot articulate** what they want but can react to a tangible artifact.

> **Conclusion:** Observation tells you **"what is"**; Prototyping tells you **"what could be"**. Both are often used **together** — observe first to understand current state, then prototype to design the future state.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> 🚨 **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Conflating Elicitation with Specification:** Elicitation = *discovering*; Specification = *documenting*. Do not mix the two. Many students write the SRS format inside an "elicitation" answer — instant mark loss.
> 
> 2. **Listing techniques without explaining:** Writing only *"interviews, questionnaires, observation, prototyping"* without describing **what they are, when used, advantages, disadvantages** earns **zero** marks for that technique.
> 
> 3. **Missing the "Hybrid Approach":** A common 1–2 mark follow-up question asks *"Which is the best technique?"* — the correct answer is **"No single technique is best; a hybrid approach is recommended"** with justification.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring Stakeholder Identification:** Many answers jump straight into techniques without first identifying **who** to elicit from. Always start with stakeholder analysis.
> 
> 5. **Confusing Requirements Elicitation with Requirements Engineering:** Elicitation is a **sub-process** of RE, not RE itself. Don't write the entire RE process when the question asks only about elicitation.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting Challenges/Problems of Elicitation:** A "Discuss" question without the difficulties section is incomplete — you lose 2–3 marks.
> 
> 7. **No diagrams in long answers:** KTU examiners award up to **1 extra mark** for a relevant, well-labeled diagram in a 14-mark question. Always include a flowchart or block diagram.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Final-Day Reading)**

- ✅ **Requirements Engineering (RE)** = Elicitation + Analysis + Specification + Validation/Management.
- ✅ **Requirements Elicitation** = *first step* of RE; goal = *discover* requirements from all sources.
- ✅ **Five Sources of Requirements:** Stakeholders, Documents, Application Domain, Existing Systems, Operating Environment.
- ✅ **Seven Elicitation Techniques (KTU-Standard):** Interviews, Questionnaires, Observation/Ethnography, Workshops, Brainstorming, Prototyping, Document/Domain Analysis.
- ✅ **Interview Types:** Closed (structured) vs. Open (unstructured) vs. Semi-structured.
- ✅ **Stakeholder = anyone affected by, influencing, or interested in** the system.
- ✅ **Power-Interest Grid:** Manage Closely | Keep Satisfied | Keep Informed | Monitor.
- ✅ **Ethnography** = deep, long-term observation rooted in anthropology; reveals tacit knowledge.
- ✅ **Prototyping Types:** Throwaway vs. Evolutionary; Horizontal vs. Vertical.
- ✅ **The 5 Whys** = root-cause elicitation helper used inside interviews/observation.
- ✅ **Hybrid Approach** is the industry best practice — no single technique is sufficient.
- ✅ **Common Challenges:** Tacit knowledge, conflicts, jargon, politics, changing requirements, geographic dispersion, analyst skill gap.
- ✅ **Output of Elicitation:** Initial set of *Functional + Non-Functional + Constraint* requirements → fed to Analysis phase.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for Techniques:** **I**nterviews, **Q**uestionnaires, **O**bservation, **W**orkshops, **B**rainstorming, **P**rototyping, **D**ocument Analysis → *"I Q O W B P D"* or think *"I Quit Our Wild Brain Projects Diligently"*.
- ✅ **Always include a diagram** in long answers (KTU marks).
- ✅ **Always mention challenges** in "Discuss/Explain" type questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirements Validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Requirements Validation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Requirements Validation** is the process of ensuring that the software requirements defined for a system actually satisfy the real-world needs of the stakeholders, customers, and end-users. It is the *dynamic* counterpart to requirements *verification* and is performed *after* requirements have been elicited, analyzed, negotiated, and specified.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance (aligned with Sommerville's framework), Requirements Validation is a quality-assurance gate that asks the pivotal question: **"Are we building the RIGHT system?"**, in contrast to verification which asks: **"Are we building the system RIGHT?"**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *Requirements Validation is the process of checking that the defined requirements are actually the requirements of the system, i.e., it checks that the specification defines the system that the customer really wants. It is concerned with finding problems with the requirements.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are commissioning an architect to build your dream house.

- **Elicitation** = You tell the architect "I want 3 bedrooms, a kitchen, and a garden."
- **Specification** = The architect draws a blueprint (the SRS document).
- **Validation** = The architect shows the blueprint to you and says, *"Is this really the house you want? Does it satisfy your family's lifestyle, budget, and future needs?"* If you say "Wait, I forgot to mention my elderly mother needs a ground-floor bedroom!", that is a *validation defect* caught early.

If validation is skipped, construction (coding) begins on a flawed blueprint. By the time the house is half-built and you say "This is wrong", reworking is **10x–100x more expensive** than fixing it on paper.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Validation:**
> Validation ensures the **right product**. It is a *value-ensuring* activity focused on *fitness for purpose in the real-world operational environment*.

### 1.3 Validation vs. Verification (Board-Favorite Distinction)

| Aspect | **Validation** | **Verification** |
|---|---|---|
| Question Asked | *Are we building the **right** system?* | *Are we building the system **right**?* |
| Focus | Real-world user needs | Specifications, design, code |
| Timing | After requirements specification | Throughout the lifecycle |
| Method | Reviews, prototyping, user demos | Inspections, walkthroughs, testing |
| Defect Type | Wrong/missing requirements | Defects in artifacts |
| Analogy | "Did we build the right house?" | "Did we build the house according to the blueprint?" |

### 1.4 When is Requirements Validation Performed?

According to the KTU syllabus flow for Module 2, validation occurs:
- **After** Requirements Elicitation
- **After** Requirements Analysis & Negotiation
- **After** Requirements Specification (SRS drafting)
- **Before** Requirements Management (change control) and Design phases

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A very common 3-mark question is *"Distinguish between requirements validation and verification."* Always present this as a **comparison table** and end with the one-liner: *"Validation is dynamic; Verification is static."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Core Properties a Validated Requirements Specification Must Satisfy

The KTU 2024 syllabus (mapped to Sommerville's Chapter 4) requires students to know **six** validation properties. These are the *checklist* the validator mentally (or formally) ticks off:

1. **Correctness** – The requirements truly represent the system the customer wants. No omissions, no over-specifications.
2. **Unambiguity** – Every requirement has **exactly one interpretation**. No vague adjectives like "fast", "user-friendly", "efficient" without measurable clauses.
3. **Completeness** – All possible user requirements, including responses to exceptional/edge cases, are included.
4. **Consistency** – No requirement conflicts with another. No contradictory statements (e.g., "system shall allow guest login" *and* "system shall reject anonymous users" in the same context).
5. **Verifiability / Testability** – Every requirement can be objectively verified. *"The system shall be fast"* is **NOT** testable. *"The login response time shall be ≤ 2 seconds at 95th percentile"* **IS** testable.
6. **Traceability** – Each requirement has a unique identifier (e.g., `REQ-LOGIN-004`) so it can be traced forward to design/code and backward to stakeholder source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 6-Point Validation Checklist (High-Yield for 14-mark questions):**
> Memorize as **C-U-C-C-V-T** → **C**orrectness, **U**nambiguity, **C**ompleteness, **C**onsistency, **V**erifiability, **T**raceability.

### 2.2 Why Validate? The Cost-of-Defect Argument

Boehm's classical curve (often referenced in KTU 2024's *Software Project Management* course) shows the relative cost of fixing a defect at different lifecycle stages:

| Stage Defect Introduced | Relative Cost to Fix |
|---|---|
| Requirements | **1×** |
| Design | **5–10×** |
| Coding | **10–50×** |
| Integration Testing | **50–200×** |
| Post-deployment | **200–1000×** |

This proves validation is the **single highest-ROI** activity in software engineering.

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Metrics Sheet (Requirements Quality Metrics)

There is no single physics-style equation, but KTU examiners expect these **metric formulas** for SRS quality assessment:

| Metric | Formula | Purpose / Target Value |
|---|---|---|
| Defect Density (D) | $D = \dfrac{N_{defects}}{N_{requirements}}$ | Measures quality of SRS; lower is better |
| Completeness Ratio (C) | $C = \dfrac{N_{implemented\_reqs}}{N_{total\_requirements}} \times 100\%$ | Target: $\geq 95\%$ before sign-off |
| Ambiguity Index (AI) | $AI = \dfrac{N_{ambiguous\_terms}}{N_{total\_sentences}}$ | Target: $\leq 0.05$ |
| Traceability Coverage (TC) | $TC = \dfrac{N_{traced\_reqs}}{N_{total\_reqs}} \times 100\%$ | Target: $100\%$ (KTU strict requirement) |
| Review Effort (RE) | $RE = N_{reviewers} \times T_{review\_hours}$ | Used for project estimation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition:** For any KTU numerical problem, a requirements specification is *accepted* only if all six checklist properties (C-U-C-C-V-T) are satisfied. If any one fails, the SRS is **rejected** and returned for revision.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Requirements Validation is used in:
- **Safety-critical systems** (aerospace, medical devices) where undetected requirements errors cause loss of life.
- **Banking/FinTech** where missing regulatory requirements (e.g., RBI, GDPR) lead to massive fines.
- **IoT/Embedded systems** where unstated environmental requirements (temperature, humidity) cause field failures.
- **AI/ML systems** where bias and ethical requirements must be validated upfront, not patched later.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Requirements Validation Process (7-Step Derivation)

Validation is **not a single action** — it is a sequenced process. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to enumerate and explain these steps. Here is the exhaustive derivation from the elicitation-output to the validated SRS:

**Step 1 — Preliminary Requirements Drafted**
After elicitation and analysis, the requirements engineer has a draft set of requirements $R = \{r_1, r_2, r_3, \dots, r_n\}$ where each $r_i$ is a candidate requirement statement.

**Step 2 — Apply the C-U-C-C-V-T Checklist Filter**
For each $r_i$, we evaluate the predicate:
$$V(r_i) = Correct(r_i) \land Unambiguous(r_i) \land Complete(r_i) \land Consistent(r_i) \land Verifiable(r_i) \land Traceable(r_i)$$

If $V(r_i) = 1$ (true), the requirement passes. If $V(r_i) = 0$, it is flagged as a defect and routed to Step 3.

**Step 3 — Defect Classification**
Detected defects are categorized using an analytical matrix:

| Defect Class | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Omission** | Required function missed entirely | "Forgot to specify password reset feature" |
| **Ambiguity** | Multiple interpretations possible | "System shall be fast" |
| **Contradiction** | Conflicts with another requirement | "All users are admins" vs. "Admins only" |
| **Infeasibility** | Technically impossible | "AI must predict stock prices with 100% accuracy" |
| **Inconsistency with standards** | Violates regulatory norms | GDPR data-retention rule not followed |

**Step 4 — Conduct Formal Validation Techniques** (see Section 4 for techniques)
Each technique produces a *validation report* $\mathcal{V}_R$.

**Step 5 — Negotiate & Refine**
The requirements engineer meets stakeholders and resolves the defects. Updated requirement set becomes $R'$.

**Step 6 — Re-validate**
The predicate $V(r_i)$ is re-evaluated for all requirements in $R'$. Iterate until convergence:
$$R^{(k+1)} = R^{(k)} \setminus \{\text{Defective Requirements}\} \cup \{\text{Corrected Requirements}\}$$
$$V(r_i^{(k+1)}) = 1 \quad \forall i \in [1, n]$$

**Step 7 — Sign-off and Baseline**
The SRS is **baselined** and placed under formal change-control management.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Validation Techniques

The KTU 2024 Module 2 specifically expects students to enumerate and compare these six techniques. The full analytical matrix:

| Technique | Participants | Type | Strength | Weakness | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Reviews** | Author + Reviewers | Informal/Formal | Cheap, early defect detection | May miss deep defects | Low |
| **Inspections** | Trained team (Fagan) | Highly Formal | Highest defect detection rate | Time-consuming, requires training | Medium-High |
| **Walkthroughs** | Author leads | Informal | Knowledge transfer, easy to organize | Author-bias possible | Low |
| **Prototyping** | Developer + User | Empirical | Validates *fitness for use*; resolves ambiguity | May oversimplify | Medium |
| **Test-Case Generation** | QA Team | Analytical | Forces unambiguous & testable requirements | Time-intensive | Medium |
| **Requirements Animation/Simulation** | Model-based tools | Model-driven | Catches dynamic behavior defects | Requires formal models | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Heuristic (Fagan's Inspection):**
> If a 14-mark question asks *"Explain requirements validation techniques"*, **Fagan Inspections** must be detailed because the 6-stage Fagan process is the *gold-standard* in KTU's reference materials.
>
> Fagan's 6 stages: **Planning → Overview → Individual Preparation → Inspection Meeting → Rework → Follow-up**

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Defect Density Calculation)

**Problem:** During validation of a 240-requirement SRS, the inspection team found 18 requirements with defects (5 omissions, 7 ambiguities, 4 contradictions, 2 infeasibilities). Calculate the Defect Density $D$ and the Validation Yield $Y$.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Given:
- Total requirements: $N_{total} = 240$
- Total defects: $N_{defects} = 5 + 7 + 4 + 2 = 18$

**Compute Defect Density:**
$$D = \dfrac{N_{defects}}{N_{requirements}} = \dfrac{18}{240} = 0.075 \text{ defects per requirement}$$

**Compute Validation Yield (Proportion of passing requirements):**
$$Y = \left(1 - D\right) \times 100\% = \left(1 - 0.075\right) \times 100\% = 92.5\%$$

**Interpretation:** Since $Y = 92.5\% < 95\%$ (the KTU target threshold), the SRS is **rejected** for revision. After rework, re-validation is required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold Rule (Memorize for Board Exams):** Any SRS with Validation Yield $< 95\%$ is deemed *not validated* and must undergo another inspection cycle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Requirements Validation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Draft Requirements Elicited] --> B[Apply CUC CV T Checklist]
    B --> C{All Six Properties Satisfied?}
    C -->|No| D[Identify Defects: Omission Ambiguity Contradiction Infeasibility]
    D --> E[Conduct Validation Techniques: Reviews Inspections Walkthroughs Prototyping Test Case Gen]
    E --> F[Generate Validation Report]
    F --> G[Negotiate with Stakeholders]
    G --> H[Refine Requirements to R Prime]
    H --> B
    C -->|Yes| I[Baseline the SRS]
    I --> J[Enter Change Control Management]
    J --> K[Hand-off to Design Phase]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Validation Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_ZONE
        docA[Draft SRS Document]
    end
    subgraph TECHNIQUE_ZONE
        tech1[Reviews]
        tech2[Fagan Inspections]
        tech3[Walkthroughs]
        tech4[Prototyping]
        tech5[Test Case Generation]
        tech6[Model Based Simulation]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT_ZONE
        repA[Validation Report]
        metA[Defect Density Metric]
        signA[Sign off Decision]
    end
    docA --> tech1
    docA --> tech2
    docA --> tech3
    docA --> tech4
    docA --> tech5
    docA --> tech6
    tech1 --> repA
    tech2 --> repA
    tech3 --> repA
    tech4 --> repA
    tech5 --> repA
    tech6 --> repA
    repA --> metA
    metA --> signA
    signA -->|Pass| END1[Baselined SRS]
    signA -->|Fail| END2[Return to Negotiation]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Fagan's 6-Stage Inspection Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Stage 1 Planning] --> s2[Stage 2 Overview]
    s2 --> s3[Stage 3 Individual Preparation]
    s3 --> s4[Stage 4 Inspection Meeting]
    s4 --> s5[Stage 5 Rework by Author]
    s5 --> s6[Stage 6 Follow up Verification]
    s6 --> q{All Defects Closed?}
    q -->|Yes| endA[SRS Validated]
    q -->|No| s1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** Arrows denote control/data flow. Every box represents a *phase* or *artifact*, never a physical object. All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `s1`, `tech1`) per the Mermaid compilation safeguard.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define Requirements Validation. Why is it considered a "value-ensuring" activity?*

**Model Answer (Memorize Structure):**
Requirements Validation is the process of checking that the defined requirements in the SRS are the actual requirements of the system — i.e., it confirms that the specification correctly reflects the customer's real-world needs. It is a *value-ensuring* activity because it ensures the *right product* is being built (fitness for purpose), as opposed to *verification* which is *value-preserving* (building the product right). It is performed after requirements specification and is essential to avoid costly rework, as defects fixed post-deployment are 200–1000× more expensive than those caught at the requirements stage.

*(Expected length: 8–10 lines. Marks awarded for: definition 1M, value-ensuring explanation 1M, real-world/cost justification 1M.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List the six properties that a validated requirements specification must satisfy. Briefly explain any two.*

**Model Answer:**
The six properties are: **Correctness, Unambiguity, Completeness, Consistency, Verifiability, and Traceability** (CUC-CVT).
- *Correctness:* The requirement accurately reflects a real stakeholder need and is not an over- or under-specification.
- *Verifiability:* The requirement can be objectively tested. E.g., "login must complete in ≤ 2 s" is verifiable, while "login must be fast" is not.

*(Any two explained with 2 lines each earns full marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**
*(a)* Explain the requirements validation process in detail with a neat diagram. (7 Marks)
*(b)* Discuss Fagan's Inspection method as a formal requirements validation technique. List its six stages. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Requirements Validation Process (7 M):**
The validation process is a 7-step iterative pipeline. It begins with the draft requirements $R$ obtained from elicitation. Each requirement $r_i \in R$ is filtered through the **CUC-CVT checklist** (Correctness, Unambiguity, Completeness, Consistency, Verifiability, Traceability). Requirements failing any property are flagged as defects and classified into one of four categories: **Omission, Ambiguity, Contradiction, Infeasibility** (or Inconsistency with standards).

The validation techniques (reviews, inspections, walkthroughs, prototyping, test-case generation, and model-based simulation) are then applied to generate a *validation report* $\mathcal{V}_R$. The engineer negotiates with stakeholders to resolve defects, producing a refined set $R'$. The cycle repeats until:
$$V(r_i) = 1 \quad \forall i \in [1, n]$$
After convergence, the SRS is **baselined** and handed over to the design phase under formal change-control management. *(Include the Section 4.1 diagram for full marks.)*

**(b) Fagan's Inspection Method (7 M):**
Fagan's Inspection is a **highly formal**, structured, and systematic requirements validation technique developed by Michael Fagan at IBM in 1976. It is considered the *gold standard* for defect detection in SRS documents.

Key features:
- Uses a **trained inspection team** (not the author).
- Employs defined **roles**: Moderator, Reader, Recorder, Author, Inspector.
- Follows strict entry/exit criteria for each stage.
- Produces a formal **inspection log** of defects.

**Six Stages:**
1. **Planning** – Moderator forms the team, distributes the SRS, and schedules the meeting.
2. **Overview** – Author presents the requirements so inspectors gain context.
3. **Individual Preparation** – Each inspector studies the SRS independently and notes potential defects.
4. **Inspection Meeting** – The team collectively reviews the defects found; the Recorder logs them. **No solutions are discussed** (critical rule).
5. **Rework** – The Author fixes the logged defects.
6. **Follow-up** – Moderator verifies that all defects have been addressed before sign-off.

Fagan reported that inspections can detect **60–90% of defects** in requirements documents at a fraction of the cost of testing.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**
*(a)* Compare and contrast Requirements Validation and Requirements Verification in 7 distinct points. (7 Marks)
*(b)* Calculate the Defect Density and Validation Yield of an SRS containing 320 requirements, of which 22 were found defective during validation (8 omissions, 6 ambiguities, 5 contradictions, 3 infeasibilities). State whether the SRS is accepted. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Validation vs. Verification (7 M):** *(Present as a 7-row table)*

| # | Aspect | Validation | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Question | Are we building the *right* system? | Are we building the system *right*? |
| 2 | Focus | Real-world user needs | Specifications, design, code |
| 3 | Phase | After requirements specification | Throughout development lifecycle |
| 4 | Nature | Dynamic (involves execution/use) | Static (involves review/analysis) |
| 5 | Methods | Reviews, prototyping, user demos | Inspections, walkthroughs, testing |
| 6 | Defect Type | Wrong/missing requirements | Defects in artifacts |
| 7 | Goal | Value-ensuring (fitness for purpose) | Value-preserving (conformance to spec) |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 M):**

Given:
- $N_{total} = 320$ requirements
- $N_{defects} = 8 + 6 + 5 + 3 = 22$

**Step 1 — Defect Density:**
$$D = \dfrac{N_{defects}}{N_{requirements}} = \dfrac{22}{320} = 0.06875 \text{ defects per requirement}$$

**Step 2 — Validation Yield:**
$$Y = (1 - D) \times 100\% = (1 - 0.06875) \times 100\% = 93.125\%$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**
Since $Y = 93.125\% < 95\%$ (the KTU acceptance threshold), the SRS is **REJECTED** and must undergo another round of inspection, defect removal, and re-validation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Writing "validation and verification are the same" → **ZERO marks** for the comparison question. Always emphasize the *"right system"* vs. *"system right"* distinction first.
> - **Pitfall 2:** In numerical problems, students often forget to express yield as a **percentage** and forget to apply the **95% threshold** rule. The decision statement (accept/reject) carries 1 of the 7 marks.
> - **Pitfall 3:** In Fagan's Inspection, students list only 5 stages, missing **"Overview"**. Always remember the mnemonic: **P-O-P-I-R-F** → Planning, Overview, Preparation, Inspection meeting, Rework, Follow-up.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Using vague requirements (e.g., *"the system should be fast"*) in your own answers — examiners instantly deduct 0.5–1 mark for ambiguity when the question itself is about avoiding ambiguity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before exam):**

- [x] **Definition:** Validation = "Building the *right* system." Verification = "Building the system *right*."
- [x] **Six Validation Properties:** **C-U-C-C-V-T** → Correctness, Unambiguity, Completeness, Consistency, Verifiability, Traceability.
- [x] **Four Defect Classes:** Omission, Ambiguity, Contradiction, Infeasibility (plus Inconsistency with standards).
- [x] **Six Validation Techniques:** Reviews, Fagan Inspections, Walkthroughs, Prototyping, Test-Case Generation, Model-Based Simulation.
- [x] **Fagan's 6 Stages:** **P-O-P-I-R-F** → Planning, Overview, Preparation, Inspection Meeting, Rework, Follow-up.
- [x] **Key Formulae:**
  - Defect Density: $D = N_{defects} / N_{requirements}$
  - Validation Yield: $Y = (1 - D) \times 100\%$
  - Acceptance Threshold: $Y \geq 95\%$
- [x] **Cost Argument:** Requirement-stage defects are **1×** to fix; post-deployment defects are **200–1000×** to fix.
- [x] **When Performed:** After specification, before design, under change-control management.
- [x] **Who Performs:** Requirements Engineer, Reviewers, Inspectors, Stakeholders, QA Team.
- [x] **Golden Rule:** Never accept an SRS with vague, untestable, or untraceable requirements.
- [x] **Mermaid/Visual:** Always include the 7-step process flowchart in any 7-mark explanation question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Requirements Change
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Requirements Change

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Requirements Change** is the formal, controlled process of identifying, evaluating, approving, implementing, and tracking modifications to the originally baselined software requirements specification (SRS) throughout the software development lifecycle, driven by evolving stakeholder needs, environmental factors, or defect discoveries.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, **Requirements Change** is treated as a subset of **Software Configuration Management (SCM)** and falls under the **Requirements Management** process area. It is a structured, governance-driven activity, NOT an ad-hoc modification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The official module descriptor lists "Requirements Change" as a direct sub-topic of *Module 2: Requirements Engineering Process*, which means examiners **expect a process-oriented answer** — not just a list of reasons why requirements change. Marks are awarded for the *Change Management Process (CMP)* and the *Impact Analysis* workflow.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The "Home Renovation" Metaphor

Imagine you commissioned an architect to build your dream house.

- **Initial Plan (SRS):** 3 bedrooms, 1 kitchen, 1 hall.
- **Month 3 (Construction Ongoing):** Your parents move in → you need a **4th bedroom**.
- **Month 5:** Tile prices rise → you want to **change flooring material** to save cost.
- **Month 7:** A new government rule requires a **rainwater harvesting pit**.

**Every change above is a "Requirements Change."**

Now, observe what happens if the contractor just *randomly* makes these changes:
- The extra bedroom might clash with the drainage pipe already laid.
- The flooring change might invalidate the warranty of the water heater installed.
- The rainwater pit might be impossible because the septic tank is already in that spot.

This is **WHY** software teams need a *formal Requirements Change Management Process* — to:
1. **Capture** every change request officially.
2. **Analyze** its ripple effect on design, code, tests, schedule, and cost.
3. **Approve/Reject** based on impact.
4. **Implement & Verify** the change safely.

Without this process, the "house" (software) collapses — just like a real construction project if changes are unmanaged.

---

## 1.3 The "Why" Behind Requirements Change (KTU Triggers)

| # | Trigger | KTU-Standard Term | Example |
|---|---------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | New business rule from client | **Business-driven change** | "Tax calculation must include GST now." |
| 2 | Found defect in existing req | **Corrective change** | "Login page says 'Submit' but should say 'Login'." |
| 3 | Technology upgrade | **Adaptive change** | "Migrate from MySQL 5.7 to PostgreSQL 16." |
| 4 | Performance/Security issue | **Enhancement change** | "Add 2FA authentication." |
| 5 | Regulatory / Legal compliance | **Mandatory change** | "GDPR Right-to-be-Forgotten implementation." |
| 6 | User feedback after UAT | **Evolutionary change** | "Add Dark Mode toggle." |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric to Remember:** Industry studies (cited in *Sommerville* and *Pressman*) indicate that **~25% to 35%** of initial requirements **change** during a typical project. In large-scale ERP/custom systems, this can rise up to **50%**. The keyword for exams is **"Requirements Volatility."**

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology Lockdown (Examiner Traps)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Traps to Avoid:**
> 1. Do NOT confuse **Requirements Change** with **Requirements Elicitation** — elicitation is about *discovering* requirements, change is about *modifying* an already baselined SRS.
> 2. Do NOT confuse **Change Request (CR)** with **Defect Report (Bug)** — a CR modifies scope, a Bug means something was built wrong against an existing requirement.
> 3. **Baseline** = the *frozen, officially-approved* version of SRS. Any change request after baseline is governed by the **Change Control Board (CCB)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
