# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Requirements Change Management Process (RCMP) — The Core 7-Stage Pipeline

This is the **single most important process** for KTU 14-mark answers on this topic. Examiners will award 7–8 marks purely on the accuracy and completeness of this pipeline.

### Stage-by-Stage Breakdown

**Stage 1: Problem / Change Recognition (Trigger)**
- A stakeholder identifies a need for change.
- Output: A *Request for Change (RFC)* or *Change Request (CR)* document is raised.
- Actor: Any stakeholder — client, developer, tester, BA.

**Stage 2: Change Request Submission & Logging**
- The CR is formally logged in a **Requirements Management Tool** (e.g., Jama, IBM DOORS, JIRA).
- Each CR is given a **unique CR-ID**, priority, severity, and submitter identity.
- Output: A *Logged CR Ticket* in the repository.

**Stage 3: Impact Analysis (CRITICAL STAGE)**
- This is the technical heart of the change process.
- Analysts evaluate the *ripple effect* across:
  - Other requirements (upstream/downstream dependencies)
  - System design & architecture
  - Existing source code modules
  - Test cases and test plans
  - User documentation
  - Schedule and budget
- Output: An *Impact Analysis Report (IAR)* with **Cost**, **Effort**, **Risk**, and **Schedule** estimates.

**Stage 4: Change Control Board (CCB) Review & Decision**
- The **CCB** (also called Change Control Board, Configuration Control Board) — a cross-functional committee — reviews the IAR.
- Members typically: Project Manager, BA Lead, Architect, QA Lead, Client Representative.
- Decisions: **Approve / Reject / Defer / Approve with Modifications**.

**Stage 5: Change Implementation**
- Approved CR is assigned to developers/designers.
- Affected modules are re-coded, re-tested, and re-documented.
- Version control commits reference the CR-ID for traceability.

**Stage 6: Verification & Re-Validation**
- QA team executes **regression testing** to ensure existing functionality is not broken.
- New test cases for the changed feature are added.
- Output: A *Change Verification Report*.

**Stage 7: Baseline Update & Notification**
- The SRS is updated, re-versioned (e.g., v1.0 → v1.1), and re-baselined.
- All stakeholders are notified of the new baseline.
- The change history is archived in the **Project Knowledge Base**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following metrics, formulas, and ratios are the ones examiners explicitly test in numerical or short-answer form.**

### Table 2.1 — Core Requirements Change Metrics & Formulas

| # | Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Unit / Value | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Requirements Volatility (RV)** | $RV = \dfrac{N_c}{N_t} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Measures % of requirements that changed. **Industry benchmark: 25%–35%.** |
| 2 | **Boehm's Cost-of-Change Factor** | $C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{k \cdot t}$ | Cost (₹ / $) | Exponential cost growth as development phase advances. |
| 3 | **Change Impact Effort (CIE)** | $CIE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (M_i \times E_i)$ | Person-hours | Total effort to implement a CR across *n* affected modules. |
| 4 | **Requirement Stability Index (RSI)** | $RSI = \dfrac{N_s}{N_t} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Where $N_s$ = unchanged requirements. **Higher is better.** |
| 5 | **Ripple Effect Ratio (RER)** | $RER = \dfrac{N_{affected}}{N_{changed}}$ | Ratio (≥ 1) | One change affecting multiple modules. **RER > 3 = high risk.** |
| 6 | **Defect Injection Rate per CR** | $DIR = \dfrac{D_{new}}{N_{CR}}$ | Defects per CR | Quality of the change process. |
| 7 | **Mean Time To Change (MTTC)** | $MTTC = \dfrac{\sum T_{cycle}}{N_{CR}}$ | Days / Hours | Average time from CR submission to baseline update. |
| 8 | **Change Approval Rate (CAR)** | $CAR = \dfrac{N_{approved}}{N_{submitted}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Health of the change funnel. |

**Where:**
- $N_c$ = Number of changed requirements
- $N_t$ = Total number of requirements (original)
- $N_s$ = Number of stable (unchanged) requirements
- $C_0$ = Cost of change at Requirements Phase (baseline cost)
- $k$ = Domain-specific growth constant (typically 0.1 to 0.4 for IT projects)
- $t$ = Time (in phase units, e.g., 0 = Requirements, 1 = Design, 2 = Coding, 3 = Testing, 4 = Maintenance)
- $M_i$ = Effort per affected module *i*
- $E_i$ = Exposure factor (1.0 = low, 2.0 = medium, 3.0 = high coupling)
- $N_{affected}$ = Total downstream requirements/components affected
- $N_{changed}$ = Number of originally changed requirements
- $D_{new}$ = New defects introduced by the change
- $N_{CR}$ = Total change requests processed
- $T_{cycle}$ = Cycle time of a single CR

### Table 2.2 — Phase-Wise Relative Cost Multipliers (Boehm's Curve)

| Development Phase | Relative Cost Multiplier | Exam Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Requirements** | **1×** | Cheapest to fix here. |
| **Design** | **3× to 6×** | Cost escalates. |
| **Coding** | **10× to 25×** | Most common exam figure. |
| **System Testing** | **50× to 100×** | A bug found here is 50–100× costlier. |
| **Post-Deployment / Maintenance** | **100× to 1000×** | The "1-10-100 Rule" in extreme. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** When asked "Why manage requirements change?" — the *best* answer links directly to **Boehm's Curve**. A defect / change discovered at the requirements stage costs ~₹10,000, but the *same* change discovered post-deployment costs ~₹10,00,000. This is the "**cost escalation argument**" for change management.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility in the Real World

In industries like **aerospace (DO-178C standard)** and **medical devices (IEC 62304)**, requirements change is so tightly regulated that:
- Every CR must have a **traceability link** to its parent requirement.
- Every change must be **bi-directionally traceable** (requirement → code → test).
- The CCB meetings are **audited** and **logged** for regulatory compliance.

In agile contexts (Scrum), the equivalent of a CR is a **Product Backlog Item (PBI) refinement**, but the *spirit* of impact analysis and stakeholder approval remains the same — managed through the **Product Owner** and **Sprint Planning**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Boehm's Cost-of-Change Exponential Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Directive:** When a 14-mark question asks you to "explain the cost of change with a mathematical model," this is the derivation examiners want.

### Given Assumption
Industry empirical data shows that the cost of fixing a defect / implementing a change grows **exponentially** with project phase progression.

### Step 1 — Define the Baseline
Let the cost of change at the **Requirements Phase** (the earliest phase) be $C_0$. By convention, $t = 0$ at the Requirements Phase, and $t$ increases as we move toward Maintenance.

$$C(0) = C_0$$

### Step 2 — State the Exponential Growth Postulate

> The cost function is assumed to be a continuous exponential in the phase index $t$:

$$C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{k \cdot t}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Domain-Calibrated Constant

For a typical software project, empirical calibration gives $k \approx 0.25$ (this means each phase is ~28% costlier than the previous one in continuous model terms).

$$C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{0.25 \cdot t}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Cost Ratio Between Two Phases

Let us find the ratio of cost at the **Testing Phase ($t = 3$)** vs the **Requirements Phase ($t = 0$)**:

$$\frac{C(3)}{C(0)} = \frac{C_0 \cdot e^{0.25 \times 3}}{C_0 \cdot e^{0.25 \times 0}}$$

$$\frac{C(3)}{C(0)} = \frac{e^{0.75}}{e^{0}} = e^{0.75}$$

$$\frac{C(3)}{C(0)} \approx 2.117$$

### Step 5 — Interpret in Discrete Phase Terms

While the continuous model gives ~2.1×, the **discrete** Boehm curve uses **1×, 5×, 10×, 50×, 100×, 1000×** jumps because of compounded effects (rework, retest, redeploy, customer churn). The continuous and discrete models are reconcilable because real projects have non-linear *rework cascades* that amplify the pure exponential.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The exponential model is a *first-order approximation*. The true cost is **super-exponential** in practice, but $C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{k \cdot t}$ is the academically accepted KTU answer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Requirements Volatility & Stability Index

### Given
A project has the following requirements data after 3 months of development:

- Total originally baselined requirements: $N_t = 200$
- Number of requirements changed: $N_c = 50$
- Number of requirements deleted: $N_d = 10$
- Number of requirements added: $N_a = 15$

### Step 1 — Compute Requirements Volatility (RV)

$$RV = \frac{N_c}{N_t} \times 100 = \frac{50}{200} \times 100 = 25\%$$

### Step 2 — Compute Requirement Stability Index (RSI)

A requirement is "stable" if it has NOT been changed. So:

$$N_s = N_t - N_c = 200 - 50 = 150$$

$$RSI = \frac{N_s}{N_t} \times 100 = \frac{150}{200} \times 100 = 75\%$$

### Step 3 — Adjusted Volatility (Including Add/Delete)

Some KTU papers use a *net* change formula:

$$RV_{adj} = \frac{N_c + N_a + N_d}{N_t} \times 100$$

$$RV_{adj} = \frac{50 + 15 + 10}{200} \times 100 = 37.5\%$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

- A **Volatility of 25%** is within the **acceptable industry range** (25–35%).
- A **Stability Index of 75%** is **good** — anything below 60% is a *red flag* signalling poor elicitation.
- A *net volatility of 37.5%* indicates significant scope churn, which warrants **a formal CCB review of the elicitation process**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Change Impact Effort (CIE) — Numerical Worked Example

### Problem Statement
A CR has been approved. The impact analysis identifies 4 affected modules with the following data:

| Module | Estimated Effort (person-hours) | Coupling Factor $E_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| User Authentication | 8 | 1.5 |
| Database Schema | 12 | 2.0 |
| Reporting Engine | 6 | 1.0 |
| API Gateway | 10 | 1.8 |

### Step 1 — Recall the Formula

$$CIE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (M_i \times E_i)$$

### Step 2 — Compute Each Term

$$\text{Auth Module: } 8 \times 1.5 = 12 \text{ person-hours}$$

$$\text{DB Module: } 12 \times 2.0 = 24 \text{ person-hours}$$

$$\text{Reporting: } 6 \times 1.0 = 6 \text{ person-hours}$$

$$\text{API Gateway: } 10 \times 1.8 = 18 \text{ person-hours}$$

### Step 3 — Sum Up

$$CIE = 12 + 24 + 6 + 18 = 60 \text{ person-hours}$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

This CR will require **60 person-hours** of effort. At 8 hours/day, this is **7.5 person-days**. If the project has a 2-week sprint (10 working days), this single CR consumes **~37.5% of one developer's sprint capacity**. The CCB should weigh this against the business value before approval.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Change Classification vs. Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is the highest-yield visual for a 7-mark sub-question.** Examiners love to ask: *"Classify the following changes and explain the process for each."*

| Change Class | Typical Examples | Process Path | Authority Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cosmetic** (UI text, label change) | "Rename 'Cancel' to 'Close'" | CR → BA review → Dev (same-day) | BA Lead only |
| **Functional** (new feature) | "Add export-to-PDF button" | Full RCMP — CCB approval required | CCB |
| **Non-Functional** (performance, security) | "Reduce page load to < 2 sec" | Full RCMP + Architect review | CCB + Architect |
| **Emergency** (production outage) | "Fix broken payment gateway" | Hotfix path: Fast-track CCB (verbal OK) | PM + Tech Lead |
| **Regulatory** (compliance) | "Add GDPR consent banner" | Mandatory — no rejection possible | CCB (rubber-stamp) |
| **Defect-driven** (bug fix) | "Login button doesn't work in Safari" | Bug triage → RCA → linked to CR if scope changes | QA Lead + Dev |

---

## 3.5 The "Anti-Pattern" of Unmanaged Change (Conceptual Derivation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why unmanaged change destroys projects — a stepwise logical build-up:**

1. **Step 1:** Unmanaged CRs are accepted verbally → no central log.
2. **Step 2:** Developers implement them in parallel → code conflicts.
3. **Step 3:** Test cases are not updated → regression test suite becomes stale.
4. **Step 4:** Schedule slippage → no traceability between effort spent and original scope.
5. **Step 5:** Customer sees inconsistent behaviour → trust loss → contract disputes.
6. **Step 6:** Project enters **"Change Chaos"** — the **Death Spiral** of uncontrolled scope creep.

The formal RCMP **prevents** this spiral by enforcing *single-point-of-truth* and *gated approval*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Requirements Change Management Process (RCMP) — End-to-End Flow

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid block represents the **7-stage formal process** defined in Section 2.1. The arrows show the workflow direction. The diamond at Stage 4 represents the **CCB decision gate**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Stage 1: Identify Change Need"] --> B["Stage 2: Log Change Request in Repository"]
    B --> C["Stage 3: Conduct Impact Analysis Report"]
    C --> D["Stage 4: CCB Reviews CR"]
    D --> E{"Decision Gate"}
    E -- Approved --> F["Stage 5: Implement Change in Code and Design"]
    E -- Rejected --> G["Archive CR with Reason Logged"]
    E -- Deferred --> H["Add to Future Backlog"]
    F --> I["Stage 6: Regression and Verification Testing"]
    I --> J["Stage 7: Update Baseline and Notify Stakeholders"]
    J --> K["Knowledge Base Updated"]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    K --> A
```

**How to read this diagram in an exam answer:**
- Start at the **top (A)** with "A stakeholder identifies a change need."
- Move **downward (A → B → C → D)** through logging, impact analysis, and CCB review.
- Hit the **diamond gate (E)** — this is where Approve/Reject/Defer happens.
- If approved, follow the **right branch** to F → I → J → K.
- If rejected or deferred, follow the **left/center branches** to G/H and then to the knowledge base.
- The **loop back to A** from K shows that this is a **continuous, lifecycle-spanning** process — not a one-time activity.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Ripple Effect of a Single Change Request

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This diagram visualizes the **Ripple Effect Ratio (RER)**. A single CR can cascade through the entire project artifact stack. The colored boxes show *affected* artifacts; the gray boxes show *unaffected* ones.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ["Original Requirement R-101"]:::changed
    REQ -->|triggers| CR["Change Request CR-205"]:::changed
    CR -->|impacts| DSGN["Design Document DD-12"]:::affected
    CR -->|impacts| CODE["Source Code Module M-07"]:::affected
    CR -->|impacts| TCMD["Test Case Document TC-44"]:::affected
    CR -->|impacts| USR["User Manual UM-03"]:::affected
    CR -->|impacts| SCHED["Project Schedule"]:::affected
    REQ2["Requirement R-205 - Dependency"]:::affected
    REQ2 --> DSGN
    REQ3["Requirement R-310 - Indirect"]:::unaffected
    classDef changed fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#900,color:#fff
    classDef affected fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#AA8,color:#000
    classDef unaffected fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#888,color:#444
```

**Reading guide for the exam:**
- The **red box (R-101)** is the *changed* requirement.
- The **yellow boxes** are the *directly and indirectly affected* downstream artifacts.
- The **gray box (R-310)** is an *unaffected* requirement — the impact analysis correctly identified it as safe.
- The **Ripple Effect Ratio (RER)** for this CR = 5 affected artifacts / 1 changed = **5.0** — which classifies it as a **high-risk change** (RER > 3).

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Change Management System

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram shows the **architectural components** of a typical Requirements Change Management Tool (e.g., IBM DOORS, Jama Connect, or an in-house JIRA-based system). This is the alternative if the topic demands a system view rather than a process view.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INTF["External Interface Layer"]
        UI1["Stakeholder Web Portal"]
        UI2["Email Notification Service"]
        UI3["API Endpoint for Tool Integration"]
    end

    subgraph LOGIC["Business Logic Layer"]
        CR_ENG["Change Request Engine"]
        IA_ENG["Impact Analysis Engine"]
        CCB_WF["CCB Workflow Manager"]
        VER_ENG["Verification and Regression Engine"]
        VER_LOG["Version Control Adapter"]
    end

    subgraph DATA["Data and Storage Layer"]
        CR_DB["Change Request Database"]
        TRACE_DB["Traceability Matrix Store"]
        BASE_REPO["Baseline Repository"]
        KB["Knowledge Base Archive"]
    end

    UI1 --> CR_ENG
    UI3 --> CR_ENG
    CR_ENG --> CR_DB
    CR_ENG --> IA_ENG
    IA_ENG --> TRACE_DB
    IA_ENG --> CCB_WF
    CCB_WF --> CR_DB
    CCB_WF --> UI2
    CCB_WF --> VER_ENG
    VER_ENG --> VER_LOG
    VER_LOG --> BASE_REPO
    VER_ENG --> KB
    BASE_REPO --> UI1
```

**Architectural interpretation:**
- **External Interface Layer (top):** The touchpoints — stakeholder UI, notifications, and API integrations with dev tools.
- **Business Logic Layer (middle):** The five core engines that *execute* the RCMP — CR Engine logs, Impact Analysis Engine analyzes, CCB Workflow manages approvals, Verification Engine tests, and Version Control Adapter updates baselines.
- **Data and Storage Layer (bottom):** The persistence backbone — CR DB, Traceability Matrix (for bi-directional tracing), Baseline Repository (for versioned SRS), and Knowledge Base (for historical learning).

This 3-layer architecture is **industry-standard** (e.g., IEEE 830 + ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 aligned).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part A questions are short-answer, requiring a **definition + 2–3 supporting lines**. No diagrams mandatory, but a small example earns bonus marks.

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 3 Marks
**Define Requirements Change. List any FOUR reasons why requirements change in a software project.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Requirements Change** is a formal, controlled modification to the baselined Software Requirements Specification (SRS) initiated after the requirements baseline has been approved, governed by a Change Management Process involving impact analysis and Change Control Board (CCB) approval.

**Four reasons (any four of the following):**
1. **Business-driven changes** — new market conditions, competitor features, or revised business strategy.
2. **User feedback** — usability issues discovered during UAT or pilot deployment.
3. **Regulatory / Legal compliance** — new government mandates (e.g., DPDP Act 2023 in India, GDPR).
4. **Technology obsolescence** — deprecation of frameworks, libraries, or platforms.
5. **Defect correction** — errors, ambiguities, or inconsistencies found in the original SRS.
6. **Environmental changes** — hardware platform shifts, OS upgrades, third-party API changes.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — 3 Marks
**What is a Change Control Board (CCB)? State its composition.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Change Control Board (CCB)** — also known as the Configuration Control Board — is a formally constituted cross-functional committee responsible for reviewing, evaluating, approving, rejecting, or deferring all submitted Change Requests (CRs) against a documented **Impact Analysis Report**.

**Typical Composition:**
1. **Project Manager** (Chair)
2. **Business Analyst Lead**
3. **Solution Architect**
4. **Quality Assurance Lead**
5. **Client / Customer Representative**
6. **Configuration Manager** (Secretary — maintains the CR log)

> In agile setups, the CCB role is **distributed** across the Product Owner, Scrum Master, and Development Team during Sprint Planning and Backlog Refinement.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Choice Format:** You must answer **either Option A or Option B**. Both are independent 14-mark questions, typically split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### 📘 Option A — 14 Marks
**Question A:** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain in detail the Requirements Change Management Process (RCMP) with a neat diagram. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Discuss Boehm's Cost-of-Change curve. A project has $C_0 = ₹50{,}000$ as the cost of a change at the Requirements Phase. Using the continuous model $C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{0.25t}$, compute the cost at the Design, Coding, and Testing phases. Comment on the implications. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Option A (a) [7 Marks]

**The Requirements Change Management Process (RCMP)** is a structured, seven-stage workflow:

1. **Change Identification & Trigger** — A stakeholder (client, developer, tester, end-user) identifies the need for a change. Output: a verbal or written *Request for Change (RFC)*.

2. **Change Request (CR) Logging** — The CR is formally recorded in a Requirements Management Tool (e.g., IBM DOORS, JIRA, Jama). Each CR receives a **unique ID**, submitter metadata, severity, and priority. This creates a **single source of truth**.

3. **Impact Analysis** — The Business Analyst + Architect evaluate the change for:
   - **Requirements impact:** Upstream/downstream dependencies.
   - **Design impact:** Affected architectural components.
   - **Code impact:** Modules to be modified.
   - **Test impact:** New/regression test cases.
   - **Schedule & cost impact:** Effort, timeline, budget delta.
   - **Risk impact:** Probability of new defects.

   Output: A formal **Impact Analysis Report (IAR)**.

4. **CCB Review & Decision Gate** — The Change Control Board convenes. Based on the IAR, it makes one of four decisions: **Approve / Reject / Defer / Approve with Conditions**.

5. **Change Implementation** — Approved CRs are scheduled into a sprint or iteration. Developers modify code, designers update design docs, and the CR-ID is referenced in all commits for traceability.

6. **Verification & Validation** — QA performs **regression testing** to ensure no existing functionality is broken, plus new tests for the changed feature. The CR is not considered "done" until the test suite passes.

7. **Baseline Update & Closure** — The SRS is re-versioned (e.g., v1.0 → v1.1), re-baselined, and all stakeholders are notified. The CR is closed in the log, and the change history is archived.

**Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**
A flowchart with the 7 stages as boxes, connected by arrows in a downward sequence, with a **diamond decision gate** after the CCB review branching into Approve → Implement → Verify → Baseline, and Reject/Defer → Archive.

> **Mark Distribution (Examiner's Key):**
> - 7 stages listed: 3.5 marks
> - Explanation of Impact Analysis depth: 1.5 marks
> - Diagram: 1.5 marks
> - Terminology (CCB, CR-ID, Baseline): 0.5 marks

---

#### Model Answer — Option A (b) [7 Marks]

**Boehm's Cost-of-Change Curve** is a foundational concept in Software Engineering Economics. Barry Boehm's empirical observation was that the **relative cost of fixing a defect or implementing a change grows exponentially** as the project advances through phases. This is because the "ripple effect" of a change cascades through more and more artifacts (design, code, test, documentation, deployed systems) at later stages.

**Given:**
- $C_0 = ₹50{,}000$
- $k = 0.25$
- $C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{0.25t}$

**Calculations:**

**At Requirements Phase ($t = 0$):**

$$C(0) = 50000 \cdot e^{0} = 50000 \cdot 1 = ₹50{,}000$$

**At Design Phase ($t = 1$):**

$$C(1) = 50000 \cdot e^{0.25} = 50000 \cdot 1.2840 = ₹64{,}200$$

**At Coding Phase ($t = 2$):**

$$C(2) = 50000 \cdot e^{0.50} = 50000 \cdot 1.6487 = ₹82{,}435$$

**At Testing Phase ($t = 3$):**

$$C(3) = 50000 \cdot e^{0.75} = 50000 \cdot 2.1170 = ₹1{,}05{,}850$$

**Implications:**
1. The cost of a change at the **Testing Phase is ~2.12× the cost at the Requirements Phase**.
2. The earlier a change is identified, the cheaper it is. This **justifies the investment in thorough requirements engineering upfront**.
3. The continuous model gives conservative numbers; the **discrete Boehm curve** (1×, 10×, 50×, 100×) is more aggressive because it factors in real-world rework cascades.
4. The model reinforces the need for **formal change management** — uncontrolled late-stage changes can blow project budgets.

> **Mark Distribution:**
> - Concept explanation: 2 marks
> - Formula and substitution: 2 marks
> - Numerical calculations (3 phases): 2 marks
> - Implications: 1 mark

---

### 📗 Option B — 14 Marks
**Question B:** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) Differentiate between Requirements Elicitation and Requirements Change Management. Why is impact analysis considered the most critical step in change management? (7 Marks)**

**(b) The following data is collected from a software project:
Total requirements baselined = 400
Requirements changed during development = 80
Requirements deleted = 15
Requirements added = 25
Number of downstream artifacts affected by the changes = 320
Total CRs submitted = 60
CRs approved = 45
Average cycle time per CR = 8 days
Compute Requirements Volatility, Requirement Stability Index, Ripple Effect Ratio, Change Approval Rate, and Mean Time to Change. Comment on the project's change health. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Option B (a) [7 Marks]

**Differences between Requirements Elicitation and Requirements Change Management:**

| Aspect | Requirements Elicitation | Requirements Change Management |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Discover and document *initial* requirements from stakeholders. | Manage *modifications* to baselined requirements. |
| **Phase** | Pre-baseline (early project). | Post-baseline (throughout lifecycle). |
| **Trigger** | Initial stakeholder interviews, workshops, observation. | Change Request (CR) submitted by any stakeholder. |
| **Process** | Elicitation → Analysis → Specification → Validation. | RCMP — Log → Impact Analysis → CCB → Implement → Verify. |
| **Artifacts Produced** | Initial SRS, use cases, user stories. | Updated SRS versions, change logs, IAR. |
| **Stakeholders** | Primarily end-users, clients, domain experts. | CCB, BA, developers, testers, client. |
| **Risk** | Missing requirements (incompleteness). | Scope creep, regression defects. |
| **Tools** | Interview guides, focus groups, prototyping. | DOORS, JIRA, Jama, version control systems. |

**Why Impact Analysis is the MOST CRITICAL step:**

1. **Reveals Hidden Costs:** A seemingly small change (e.g., renaming a field) can cascade through 20+ downstream artifacts. Impact analysis surfaces this *before* approval.

2. **Informs CCB Decision:** Without an IAR, the CCB cannot make an *informed* approve/reject decision. It would be guessing.

3. **Prevents Scope Creep:** Quantifies the cost/effort delta so the CCB can compare it against the business value.

4. **Risk Identification:** Spots ripple effects that could destabilize the architecture.

5. **Baseline Protection:** Ensures the integrity of the baselined SRS is preserved by understanding exactly what will change.

6. **Schedule Realism:** Prevents the "just one small change" trap that derails project timelines.

7. **Audit & Compliance:** The IAR becomes a legal/audit document proving the change was evaluated.

> Without impact analysis, change management degenerates into **arbitrary scope modifications** — defeating the entire purpose of having a formal process.

---

#### Model Answer — Option B (b) [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- $N_t = 400$
- $N_c = 80$
- $N_d = 15$
- $N_a = 25$
- $N_{affected} = 320$
- $N_{CR} = 60$
- $N_{approved} = 45$
- $T_{avg} = 8$ days

**Step 1 — Requirements Volatility (RV):**

$$RV = \frac{N_c}{N_t} \times 100 = \frac{80}{400} \times 100 = 20\%$$

**Step 2 — Requirement Stability Index (RSI):**

$$N_s = N_t - N_c = 400 - 80 = 320$$

$$RSI = \frac{320}{400} \times 100 = 80\%$$

**Step 3 — Ripple Effect Ratio (RER):**

$$RER = \frac{N_{affected}}{N_c} = \frac{320}{80} = 4.0$$

**Step 4 — Change Approval Rate (CAR):**

$$CAR = \frac{45}{60} \times 100 = 75\%$$

**Step 5 — Mean Time to Change (MTTC):**

$$MTTC = 8 \text{ days (already given as average)}$$

**Project Change Health Commentary:**

| Metric | Value | Industry Benchmark | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| RV | 20% | 25–35% | **Good** — better than average. |
| RSI | 80% | ≥ 70% | **Healthy** — high stability. |
| RER | 4.0 | < 3 ideal | **High Risk** — one change ripples to 4 artifacts on average. |
| CAR | 75% | 60–80% | **Healthy** — CCB is not rubber-stamping. |
| MTTC | 8 days | < 7 ideal | **Acceptable** — could improve. |

**Overall Verdict:** The project has a **mature change management process** (good RV, RSI, CAR) but suffers from **high architectural coupling** (RER = 4.0). The recommendation is to **invest in module decoupling** (e.g., microservices, interface contracts) to reduce the ripple effect of future changes.

> **Mark Distribution:**
> - 5 correct formulas with values: 4 marks
> - Interpretation table with benchmarks: 2 marks
> - Final recommendation: 1 mark

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on "Requirements Change" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Change with Elicitation (–2 marks):** Writing about interviews and workshops when the question is about *change* management. Always anchor your answer in the **post-baseline** context.
>
> 2. **Skipping the CCB (–1.5 marks):** Forgetting to mention the **Change Control Board** is the single most common deduction. CCB is *not* optional — it is the gatekeeper.
>
> 3. **No Diagram in 14-mark answers (–1 to –1.5 marks):** KTU examiners explicitly reward a **flowchart** of the RCMP. A textual list without a diagram will lose at least 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Confusing CR with Bug (–1 mark):** A *Change Request* is a *scope modification*. A *Bug Report* is a *defect against existing scope*. They follow different workflows.
>
> 5. **Skipping the numerical computation in part (b) (–2 to –3 marks):** When a numerical sub-part is given, you **must show every substitution step**. Writing only the final answer = 0 marks for the computation step.
>
> 6. **Not stating units / benchmarks (–0.5 to –1 mark):** A volatility of "20%" without saying "which is below the industry average of 25–35%" is incomplete. **Always contextualize your number.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Requirements Change (Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

- **Definition:** Formal, controlled modification to the baselined SRS, governed by the RCMP.
- **Why it matters:** Cost of change grows **exponentially** with phase progression (Boehm's Law).
- **RCMP 7 Stages:** Identify → Log → Impact Analysis → CCB Review → Implement → Verify → Baseline Update.
- **CCB composition:** PM, BA Lead, Architect, QA Lead, Client Rep, Config Manager.
- **Boehm's Cost Curve:** $C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{k \cdot t}$ with $k \approx 0.25$ in the continuous model.
- **Phase multipliers (discrete):** 1× (Req) → 5–6× (Design) → 10× (Coding) → 50–100× (Test) → 1000× (Maintenance).
- **Volatility formula:** $RV = (N_c / N_t) \times 100$. Industry norm: 25–35%.
- **Stability Index formula:** $RSI = (N_s / N_t) \times 100$. Higher is better; ≥ 70% is healthy.
- **Ripple Effect Ratio:** $RER = N_{affected} / N_{changed}$. RER > 3 = high-risk change.
- **Change Approval Rate:** $CAR = (N_{approved} / N_{submitted}) \times 100$.
- **Mean Time to Change:** $MTTC = \sum T_{cycle} / N_{CR}$. < 7 days is ideal.
- **Change Impact Effort:** $CIE = \sum (M_i \times E_i)$.
- **Triggers for change:** Business, user feedback, regulatory, technology, defect, environment.
- **Change classes:** Cosmetic, Functional, Non-Functional, Emergency, Regulatory, Defect-driven.
- **Tools:** IBM DOORS, Jama Connect, JIRA, Polarion, Azure DevOps.
- **Anti-pattern:** Unmanaged change → scope creep → schedule slippage → "Change Chaos Death Spiral."
- **Key exam line:** "The earlier a change is caught, the cheaper it is to implement — Boehm's Cost-of-Change principle justifies the investment in formal Requirements Engineering upfront."
- **Distinguish in exam:** Requirements Change ≠ Requirements Elicitation ≠ Bug Fix.
- **Traceability:** Every CR must be bi-directionally traceable (req → code → test).
- **Audit:** CCB decisions and IARs are auditable documents in regulated industries (aerospace, medical, finance).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Traceability Matrix. Developing Use Cases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Traceability Matrix. Developing Use Cases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Traceability Matrix (TM)

### Formal Definition
A **Traceability Matrix** is a tabular document (typically a 2D matrix) used in Software Engineering to establish and maintain a verifiable link between **requirements** and the corresponding **design elements, source code modules, test cases, and associated business needs** throughout the entire Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC). It acts as a bidirectional audit trail that confirms every requirement is implemented, tested, and traceable to a specific stakeholder need.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Traceability Matrix is treated as a *core deliverable* under the Software Requirements phase (Module 2). It is mandatory for IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148 compliant SRS documentation and is often a high-weightage question in Part B (14-mark) exam questions.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are a civil contractor building a 50-floor apartment. The customer's master list says: "Provide 200 windows of type-A, 150 doors of type-B, install 10 elevators." If 3 months later, the client asks *"Did you install 200 windows of type-A on floors 10-20?"*, can you answer in 2 seconds?

- **Without a Traceability Matrix:** You have to run around the site, count windows, check invoices, dig through emails — chaotic, error-prone, and slow.
- **With a Traceability Matrix:** You glance at one single spreadsheet. It says: `REQ-W-001 → 200 Windows → Design-D-015 → Contractor Invoice #4487 → Installed on Floors 10-20`. Done.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Traceability Matrix is the single source of truth (SSoT)** for tracking *what was promised, what was designed, what was built, and what was tested*. If a row is empty in any column, that requirement is at risk.

### The Three Cardinal Types of Traceability

| Type | Direction | Question Answered | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Forward Traceability** | Requirements → Design, Code, Test | *Did we build what was asked?* | Confirms completeness of implementation |
| **Backward Traceability** | Design, Code, Test → Requirements | *Why was this built?* | Eliminates gold-plating and unjustified code |
| **Bi-Directional Traceability** | Both directions simultaneously | *Is the system fully covered and free of orphans?* | **Industry-preferred standard** (CMMI Level 3+) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bi-Directional Traceability** is the gold standard. A requirement with no forward trace is **un-implemented**; a code module with no backward trace is a **dangling/orphan** (gold-plated, unauthorized) element.

---

## 1.2 Developing Use Cases

### Formal Definition
A **Use Case** is a technique in Requirements Engineering (popularized by Ivar Jacobson via the Unified Modeling Language) that captures a piece of **functional behavior** of a system from an external actor's perspective. It describes *who* can do *what* with the system, under *what preconditions*, to deliver *what post-condition value*, while describing the *main success scenario* and *alternative (exceptional) flows*.

A **Use Case Diagram** is a UML behavioral diagram that visually depicts a set of **actors**, **use cases**, and the **associations** (links) between them, placed within a **system boundary**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a **Use Case** as a *single, well-defined conversation* between a user and an ATM machine:

- The user **inserts a card** (trigger).
- The system **asks for PIN** (response).
- The user **enters the PIN** (input).
- The system **validates and dispenses cash** (success scenario).
- OR the system **rejects the card** (alternative flow).

That entire dialogue, written down in a structured template, is one **Use Case**. A *Use Case Diagram* is a bird's-eye-view map showing every distinct conversation (Use Case) and every character (Actor) the system interacts with.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Lock-in:** A *Use Case* is NOT a function or a method. It is a **goal-oriented, complete interaction** initiated by an actor to obtain a measurable result. Each step in a use case is a *user-system dialogue*, not an internal subroutine.

### Key Vocab Anchor Table

| Term | Definition | Example (ATM System) |
|---|---|---|
| **Actor** | An external entity (human or another system) that interacts with the system to achieve a goal. | `Customer`, `Bank Server` |
| **Primary Actor** | The actor whose goal the use case fulfils. | `Customer` (wants cash) |
| **Supporting Actor** | An external system the use case needs to complete its goal. | `Bank Server` (verifies balance) |
| **Precondition** | State that must be true *before* the use case begins. | *Customer is authenticated, has a valid card* |
| **Postcondition** | State that must be true *after* the use case successfully completes. | *Cash is dispensed; account is debited* |
| **Main Flow** | The most common, "happy path" sequence of events. | Card → PIN → Amount → Cash |
| **Alternative Flow** | Variations or exceptional branches. | *Invalid PIN, insufficient balance, network failure* |
| **System Boundary** | A rectangle that visually separates the system from external actors. | The ATM box in the diagram |
| **`<<include>>`** | A mandatory sub-use case that is always invoked. | `Validate PIN` is included in every transaction |
| **`<<extend>>`** | An optional sub-use case that adds behavior under conditions. | `Print Receipt` extends `Withdraw Cash` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common KTU pitfall: confusing `<<include>>` with `<<extend>>`. Remember: **Include = Mandatory decomposition (no choice)**, **Extend = Optional insertion (under a guard condition)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Traceability Matrix (Row × Column Structure)

A Traceability Matrix is conceptually a **sparse 2D grid** where rows represent *artifacts* (requirements, modules, tests) and columns represent their *linked artifacts*. Each cell contains an **identifier** (e.g., `REQ-12`, `TC-45`) that establishes the relationship.

### Standard Cell Content Logic

Let the matrix be denoted mathematically as $M$, where:

$$
M_{ij} = \begin{cases} \text{identifier string} & \text{if artifact } i \text{ is linked to artifact } j \\ \phi \; (\text{blank cell}) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The **traceability density** for requirement $R_i$ is given by:

$$
TD(R_i) = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}(M_{ij} \neq \phi)}{n}
$$

where $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ is the indicator function and $n$ is the total number of related artifact columns (e.g., design, code, test). A requirement with $TD = 1.0$ is **fully traced**; a requirement with $TD = 0$ is **untraced (at risk)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coverage** is the *complementary* metric. Project-level coverage is calculated as:

$$
\text{Coverage} = \frac{\text{Number of fully traced requirements}}{\text{Total number of requirements}} \times 100\%
$$

A KTU-acceptable project must demonstrate **100% forward and backward traceability** by the System Testing phase.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: Building a Traceability Matrix

The construction follows a strict **5-stage cascade**. Skipping a stage breaks the chain.

1. **Requirement Enumeration:** Extract every numbered functional and non-functional requirement from the validated SRS document. Assign a unique ID (e.g., `FR-01`, `NFR-PERF-03`).
2. **Design Mapping:** For each requirement, identify the architectural component, module, or DFD process that fulfills it. Mark the cell in the *Design* column.
3. **Code Linking:** Trace each design element to specific source files, classes, or functions. Mark the *Code* column.
4. **Test Case Association:** Link each requirement to one or more test case IDs (Unit, Integration, System, Acceptance).
5. **Validation & Gap Analysis:** Iterate over every cell. Any blank means an orphan (gold-plating) or a missing trace (gap). Both must be resolved.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Questions often present a partial matrix and ask students to "fill in the missing cells" or "identify the orphan requirement." Memorize the cascade: **Requirement → Design → Code → Test**.

---

## 2.3 Anatomy of a Use Case Description Template

A **Use Case Description** is a structured textual document (NOT the diagram). The IEEE/industry-standard template includes the following slots:

| Slot | Purpose | Mandatory? |
|---|---|---|
| **Use Case ID** | Unique identifier (e.g., `UC-04`) | Yes |
| **Use Case Name** | Verb-noun phrase (e.g., *"Withdraw Cash"*) | Yes |
| **Actor(s)** | Primary + Supporting actors | Yes |
| **Description** | One-line purpose statement | Yes |
| **Precondition(s)** | State required before the use case starts | Yes |
| **Postcondition(s)** | State guaranteed after success | Yes |
| **Main Flow** | Numbered, sequenced list of actor-system steps | Yes |
| **Alternative Flows** | Numbered list of exceptions, each linked to a main-flow step | Recommended |
| **Trigger** | The event that initiates the use case | Recommended |
| **Assumptions** | External conditions assumed to be true | Optional |

### Key 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why a precondition?** It scopes the responsibility — the system need not handle states it cannot legally be in.
- **Why a postcondition?** It enables **state-based testing** and **test oracle** design (you can verify the system's final state automatically).
- **Why a verb-noun name?** Use cases are *goals*, not implementations. *"Withdraw Cash"* is a goal; *"CheckBalanceAndDispense"* is not.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trap:** A use case name should be **goal-oriented**, not implementation-oriented. *"Process Transaction"* is weak; *"Withdraw Cash"* is correct.

---

## 2.4 Use Case Diagram: The Four Graphical Elements

A use case diagram contains only **4 primitive elements** plus **3 relationships**:

### Elements (Nodes)
1. **Actor** — Stick figure (human) or `<<system>>` rectangle (external system).
2. **Use Case** — Ellipse containing a verb-noun phrase.
3. **System Boundary** — Rectangle enclosing all use cases.
4. **Association** — Solid line connecting an actor to a use case (no arrows unless communicating direction matters).

### Relationships (Edges)
1. **Association** (Actor ↔ Use Case): Solid line. No arrowhead by default.
2. **Generalization** (Actor ↔ Actor OR Use Case ↔ Use Case): Solid line with a **hollow triangle arrowhead**. Indicates inheritance/specialization.
3. **`<<include>>`**: Dashed arrow with the label `<<include>>` from the *base* use case to the *mandatory* sub-use case.
4. **`<<extend>>`**: Dashed arrow with the label `<<extend>>` from the *optional extension* use case to the *base* use case. Always carries a **condition (guard)**.

> [!WARNING]
> The arrow direction is **strictly opposite** for `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>`:
> - `<<include>>`: **A → B** means *"A always invokes B"*.
> - `<<extend>>`: **A → B** means *"A may optionally add behavior to B"*.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Condensed)

| Construct | Cardinal Rule | KTU Expected Answer |
|---|---|---|
| **Traceability Matrix row** | One requirement = one row | Each row = full lifecycle trace |
| **Forward Trace** | Req → Design → Code → Test | Answers "Did we build it?" |
| **Backward Trace** | Test → Code → Design → Req | Answers "Why was it built?" |
| **Use Case name** | Verb + Object (goal-oriented) | "Withdraw Cash", not "Process" |
| **Primary Actor** | Initiates the use case to achieve a goal | Listed on the left in template |
| **Precondition** | System state *before* the use case | "Customer is logged in" |
| **Postcondition** | System state *after* success | "Cash dispensed, account debited" |
| **`<<include>>`** | Mandatory reuse, always runs | "Validate PIN" inside every transaction |
| **`<<extend>>`** | Optional behavior, under a guard | "Print Receipt" if user presses [Y] |
| **System Boundary** | Rectangle containing all use cases | Defines scope of the system under design |
| **Actor generalization** | Hollow-triangle arrow | "Customer" and "Admin" inherit from "User" |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Constructing a Traceability Matrix (Worked Example)

**Scenario:** A small library management system has 4 functional requirements, 3 design modules, and 4 test cases.

### Step 1 — Enumerate Requirements
From the SRS, extract:
- `FR-01`: User can search for a book by title.
- `FR-02`: User can issue a book (borrow).
- `FR-03`: System computes a fine on late return.
- `FR-04`: Admin can add a new book to the catalog.

### Step 2 — Map Design Elements
- `DES-A`: Search Module
- `DES-B`: Issue/Return Module
- `DES-C`: Fine Calculation Engine
- `DES-D`: Catalog Admin Module

### Step 3 — Map Code (Source Files)
- `CODE-A1`: `search.controller.js`
- `CODE-B1`: `issue.service.ts`
- `CODE-C1`: `fine.calculator.py`
- `CODE-D1`: `admin.routes.js`

### Step 4 — Map Test Cases
- `TC-01`: Search by exact title.
- `TC-02`: Search by partial title.
- `TC-03`: Issue a book successfully.
- `TC-04`: Calculate fine for 5-day-late return.

### Step 5 — Assemble the Traceability Matrix

| Req ID | Requirement Description | Design Module | Code File | Test Case(s) | Trace Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `FR-01` | Search book by title | `DES-A` | `CODE-A1` | `TC-01`, `TC-02` | Fully Traced |
| `FR-02` | Issue a book | `DES-B` | `CODE-B1` | `TC-03` | Fully Traced |
| `FR-03` | Compute fine on late return | `DES-C` | `CODE-C1` | `TC-04` | Fully Traced |
| `FR-04` | Admin adds new book | `DES-D` | `CODE-D1` | *(none)* | **GAP — No Test Case** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Insight:** The matrix immediately surfaces that `FR-04` has no test case. A KTU examiner would award full marks to a student who:
> (a) identifies the gap,
> (b) names it as a **"Test Coverage Gap"**,
> (c) suggests adding a new test case `TC-05: Admin adds a new book successfully`.

### Step 6 — Compute Coverage

$$
\text{Coverage} = \frac{\text{Requirements with all 3 forward links (Design, Code, Test)}}{\text{Total Requirements}}
$$

$$
\text{Coverage} = \frac{3}{4} \times 100\% = 75\%
$$

This falls short of the **100% target** mandated by KTU/CMMI quality benchmarks.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough: Developing a Use Case (Step-by-Step)

**System:** A simple ATM Machine.

**Goal:** Develop the use case *“Withdraw Cash”*.

### Step 1 — Identify the Actor(s)
- **Primary Actor:** `Customer` (the human who wants cash).
- **Supporting Actor:** `BankServer` (external system that validates the account).

### Step 2 — State the Goal
The customer wants to obtain physical cash from their account.

### Step 3 — Identify the Trigger
The customer inserts a valid ATM card into the card reader.

### Step 4 — Define Preconditions
- The ATM is online and has cash.
- The customer has a valid, unexpired card.
- The customer has an active account with sufficient balance.

### Step 5 — Define Postconditions
- **Success Postcondition:** Cash is dispensed; the account is debited by the withdrawal amount; the card is returned.
- **Failure Postcondition:** Card is returned; no money is dispensed; account balance is unchanged.

### Step 6 — Write the Main Flow

| Step | Actor | Action | System Response |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Customer | Inserts ATM card | Reads card, prompts for PIN |
| 2 | Customer | Enters PIN | Validates PIN via `<<include>>` UC *Validate PIN* |
| 3 | Customer | Selects "Withdraw Cash" | Displays account type menu |
| 4 | Customer | Selects account type and enters amount | Sends request to `BankServer` |
| 5 | `BankServer` | Confirms balance ≥ amount | System prepares cash |
| 6 | System | Dispenses cash | Prints receipt prompt |
| 7 | Customer | Takes cash and card | Ejects card, transaction complete |

### Step 7 — Write Alternative Flows

| AF# | Triggered At | Condition | System Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| `AF-1` | Step 2 | Invalid PIN (3 times) | Retains card, ends use case |
| `AF-2` | Step 4 | Amount > balance | Displays "Insufficient Funds", cancels |
| `AF-3` | Step 4 | Amount > daily limit | Displays "Limit Exceeded", cancels |
| `AF-4` | Step 5 | `BankServer` offline | Displays "Network Error", returns card |
| `AF-5` | Step 7 | Cash not collected in 30s | Retracts cash, cancels transaction |

### Step 8 — Draw the Use Case Diagram (Symbolic Representation)

The diagram will contain:
- Two actors: `Customer` (stick figure) and `BankServer` (`<<system>>` rectangle).
- The ellipse *Withdraw Cash*.
- A dashed arrow with label `<<include>>` from *Withdraw Cash* to *Validate PIN*.
- A dashed arrow with label `<<extend>>` from *Print Receipt* to *Withdraw Cash*, with the condition `[User selects "Yes"]`.
- A system boundary rectangle enclosing *Withdraw Cash*, *Validate PIN*, and *Print Receipt*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 14-Mark Standard:** A full-use-case answer should include the **diagram + the template** (steps 1–7). Skipping the diagram costs 4–5 marks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the "Use Case Granularity" Heuristic

To decide whether to split or merge use cases, KTU examiners expect the **EBP (Elementary Business Process)** rule:

$$
\text{Use Case} \equiv \text{EBP} \iff \text{The task delivers value to a primary actor in a single transaction}.
$$

Formally, a candidate use case $U$ is valid if:

$$
\forall \, a \in \text{Actors}(U), \quad \text{Value}(U, a) > 0 \;\text{and}\; \text{TransactionBoundary}(U) = \text{Atomic}
$$

- *Value > 0*: The actor's goal is achieved.
- *Atomic*: The whole task is committed or rolled back as a single unit (no half-states visible to the actor).

**Example Application:**
- "Withdraw Cash" → atomic (cash given, account debited, OR nothing happens) → **Valid Use Case**.
- "Browse Catalog, then Add to Cart, then Checkout" → three separate atomic transactions → **Three Use Cases**, not one.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Use Case Development Lifecycle (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This diagram shows the **5-stage pipeline** for converting raw stakeholder needs into a validated use case diagram + description. Each stage feeds the next; feedback loops exist between Stages 3 and 4 for iterative refinement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Stage 1: Identify Actors and System Boundary] --> A2[Stage 2: Enumerate Candidate Use Cases from Actor Goals]
    A2 --> A3[Stage 3: Write Use Case Descriptions Template Driven]
    A3 --> A4[Stage 4: Construct Use Case Diagram with include and extend]
    A4 --> A5[Stage 5: Peer Review and Validate against SRS]
    A5 --> A6[Approved Use Case Model and SRS Annexure]
    A3 -.Reuse Detection.-> A3
    A4 -.Refactor.-> A2

    classDef process fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B1F4A
    classDef deliverable fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853,stroke-width:2px,color:#0F5132
    classDef loop fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#7C5E00

    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5 process
    class A6 deliverable
    class A3,A2 loop
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Stages 1–5 are linear; the dashed loops indicate *reuse detection* (finding common sub-flows for `<<include>>`) and *refactoring* (splitting or merging use cases after diagram review).

---

## 4.2 Traceability Matrix Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The diagram below maps the **artifact cascade** underlying a Traceability Matrix. Each artifact in the cascade has a one-to-many relationship with the artifact in the next stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Requirements SRS] --> D[Design Modules and DFDs]
    D --> C[Source Code Files and Classes]
    C --> T[Test Cases and Test Scripts]
    R -.Backward Trace.-> T
    D -.Backward Trace.-> R
    C -.Backward Trace.-> D
    T -.Backward Trace.-> C

    TM[Traceability Matrix Table] -.Aggregates.-> R
    TM -.Aggregates.-> D
    TM -.Aggregates.-> C
    TM -.Aggregates.-> T

    RP[Reports: Coverage, Gap, Orphan, Impact] -.Derived From.-> TM

    classDef stage fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B1F4A
    classDef matrix fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:2px,color:#7A1F1A
    classDef report fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853,stroke-width:2px,color:#0F5132

    class R,D,C,T stage
    class TM matrix
    class RP report
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The solid arrows (→) represent the **forward trace** (the build pipeline). The dashed arrows (-.->) represent the **backward trace** (the audit pipeline). The Traceability Matrix sits at the center, ingesting all four artifact types and emitting QA/QC reports.

---

## 4.3 Sample Use Case Diagram (ATM System) — Block-Level Functional Architecture

This is a *visual mock-up* of a Use Case Diagram for an ATM. It shows actors, use cases, system boundary, and `<<include>>` / `<<extend>>` relationships in a node-link form (Mermaid's `flowchart` engine is used to render UML-style links without graphical stick figures).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Customer((Customer - Primary Actor))
    BankServer([BankServer - Supporting System])
    Admin((Bank Admin))

    subgraph SYS_BOUNDARY [ATM System Boundary]
        UC01([Withdraw Cash])
        UC02([Check Balance])
        UC03([Deposit Cash])
        UC04([Validate PIN])
        UC05([Print Receipt])
        UC06([Add New User])
    end

    Customer --- UC01
    Customer --- UC02
    Customer --- UC03
    BankServer --- UC04
    Admin --- UC06

    UC01 -.->|include| UC04
    UC01 -.->|extend under condition Yes| UC05
```

> [!NOTE]

> - The rectangle `ATM System Boundary` separates internal behavior from external actors.
> - `Withdraw Cash` and `Check Balance` both *must* `<<include>>` *Validate PIN* — this is a **mandatory sub-flow**.
> - `Print Receipt` is *optionally* `<<extend>>`-ed onto `Withdraw Cash`, typically under the guard *"if the user presses the receipt button"*.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse the *Use Case Diagram* with the *Use Case Description*. The diagram is worth ~4 marks; the description is worth ~10 marks. Missing either loses half the question.
> 2. **Do not** write use case names in implementation language (e.g., *"call validate()"*). Always use **goal-oriented verb-noun** phrasing.
> 3. **Do not** mix `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` arrow directions. A reversed arrow is an instant 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Do not** present a Traceability Matrix as a plain list. It must be a **table with at least 4 columns** (Req, Design, Code, Test) and **all cells populated or explicitly marked as gaps**.
> 5. **Always** identify the *gap* (missing test) AND the *orphan* (extra design with no requirement). Examiners award bonus marks for proactive gap detection.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a Traceability Matrix. List any two advantages of using it in software projects. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Traceability Matrix** is a tabular document that establishes and tracks bidirectional links between *requirements* and the corresponding *design, code, and test artifacts* throughout the software lifecycle.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Completeness Verification:** It ensures that every stakeholder requirement is mapped to at least one design element, code module, and test case — exposing gaps early.
2. **Impact Analysis:** When a requirement changes, the matrix instantly shows which design documents, source files, and test cases are affected, reducing rework risk.

> *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 per advantage)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` relationships in a Use Case Diagram. Give one example of each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `<<include>>` | `<<extend>>` |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Mandatory reuse of a common sub-flow | Optional insertion of extra behavior |
| **Arrow** | From base use case to included use case | From extension to base use case |
| **Condition / Guard** | None — always executes | A guard condition is mandatory |
| **Example** | *"Withdraw Cash"* `<<include>>` *"Validate PIN"* | *"Print Receipt"* `<<extend>>` *"Withdraw Cash"` if user selects "Yes" |

> *(3 marks: 1 for the table, 1 for the example of include, 1 for the example of extend)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Traceability Matrix `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a Traceability Matrix. With a neat diagram, describe the **forward and backward traceability** process. Differentiate between the two with one example each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A point-of-sale (POS) system has the following requirements:
- `FR-01`: Cashier scans a product barcode.
- `FR-02`: System displays the product price.
- `FR-03`: System computes the total bill.
- `FR-04`: System supports a UPI payment option.
- `FR-05`: System sends an e-receipt via SMS.

The design has modules `M1: Barcode Scanner Interface`, `M2: Pricing Engine`, `M3: Bill Calculator`, `M4: Payment Gateway`, `M5: SMS Notifier`. Construct a **complete Traceability Matrix** linking each requirement to the design module and at least one test case. Identify any **coverage gap** or **orphan design element**.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Concept + Diagram (7 marks):**

**Concept (3 marks):** A Traceability Matrix is a tabular tool that links *requirements* to *design, code, and test artifacts* and back, ensuring each requirement is implemented, tested, and traceable to a stakeholder need. It supports *forward traceability* (Requirement → Design → Code → Test) which answers *"Did we build what was asked?"* and *backward traceability* (Test → Code → Design → Requirement) which answers *"Why was this built?"*.

**Diagram (2 marks):** The diagram from **Section 4.2** of these notes (block-level flow) may be redrawn showing the four artifact stages connected by solid forward arrows and dashed backward arrows, converging into the central Traceability Matrix table.

**Differentiation Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | Forward Traceability | Backward Traceability |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Requirement → Implementation | Implementation → Requirement |
| Question | "Did we build it?" | "Why was this built?" |
| Detects | Un-implemented requirements | Orphan/gold-plated code |
| Example | `FR-02: Display Price` → `M2: Pricing Engine` → `pricing.py` → `TC-P-05` | `pricing.py` → `M2` → `FR-02` (justifies existence) |

---

**(b) Constructed Traceability Matrix (7 marks):**

| Req ID | Requirement | Design Module | Code File (Assumed) | Test Case(s) | Trace Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `FR-01` | Scan barcode | `M1` | `scanner.cpp` | `TC-01: Scan valid barcode` | Fully Traced |
| `FR-02` | Display price | `M2` | `pricing.py` | `TC-02: Display price after scan` | Fully Traced |
| `FR-03` | Compute total bill | `M3` | `billcalc.js` | `TC-03: Total = sum of items` | Fully Traced |
| `FR-04` | UPI payment | `M4` | `upi_gateway.java` | `TC-04: UPI success`, `TC-05: UPI failure` | Fully Traced |
| `FR-05` | Send SMS e-receipt | `M5` | `sms_notifier.py` | *(none provided)* | **GAP — Missing Test Case** |

**Coverage Calculation (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Coverage} = \frac{4}{5} \times 100\% = 80\%
$$

**Gap/Orphan Analysis (1 mark):**
- **Gap identified:** `FR-05` has no test case. **Remediation:** Add `TC-06: Verify SMS sent on successful UPI payment`.
- **Orphan check:** Every design module (`M1`–`M5`) is mapped to at least one requirement — **no orphan module exists**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Developing Use Cases `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **steps to develop a use case** with a suitable example. List the elements of a **Use Case Diagram** with a neat sketch.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a **Library Management System**, develop the use case *"Issue a Book"* using a complete Use Case Description template. The primary actor is `Student`; supporting actor is `LibraryServer`. State all preconditions, postconditions, main flow (at least 5 steps), and at least 3 alternative flows. Also draw the Use Case Diagram.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Steps + Elements (7 marks):**

**Steps to Develop a Use Case (4 marks):**

1. **Identify the system boundary** and the **primary actor(s)**.
2. **Enumerate candidate use cases** from the goals of each primary actor.
3. **Write the Use Case Description** using the template (Name, Actors, Pre/Post, Main Flow, Alternative Flows).
4. **Construct the Use Case Diagram** showing actors, use cases, system boundary, and `<<include>>` / `<<extend>>` relationships.
5. **Review and refactor** for granularity, reuse, and naming consistency.

**Elements of a Use Case Diagram (3 marks):**
- **Actor** (stick figure for human, rectangle with `<<actor>>` for external system).
- **Use Case** (ellipse with verb-noun label).
- **System Boundary** (rectangle enclosing all use cases).
- **Association** (solid line between actor and use case).
- **`<<include>>` relationship** (dashed arrow toward mandatory sub-use case).
- **`<<extend>>` relationship** (dashed arrow toward base use case, with guard condition).
- **Generalization** (solid line with hollow-triangle arrowhead).

---

**(b) Library Management System — Use Case *"Issue a Book"* (7 marks):**

**Use Case ID:** `UC-03`
**Use Case Name:** *Issue a Book*
**Primary Actor:** `Student`
**Supporting Actor:** `LibraryServer`
**Trigger:** The student presents a book and a valid library card at the circulation desk.

**Preconditions:**
- The student must be a registered library member with an active, non-blocked account.
- The book must be available in the catalog and physically present on the shelf.
- The student must not have any overdue books or unpaid fines exceeding ₹500.

**Postconditions (Success):**
- The book is officially issued to the student.
- The `LibraryServer` updates the book status to *"Issued"* and records the due date (14 days from issue).
- A transaction receipt is generated.

**Postcondition (Failure):**
- No record is changed. The book remains in *"Available"* status. The student is informed of the reason.

**Main Flow (5 marks — 1 per step):**

| Step | Actor | Action | System Response |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Student` | Submits book and library card | System reads card via `<<include>>` UC *Verify Membership* |
| 2 | System | Sends member ID to `LibraryServer` | `LibraryServer` returns eligibility status |
| 3 | `Student` | Confirms request to issue | System requests ISBN of the book |
| 4 | System | Sends ISBN to `LibraryServer` | `LibraryServer` returns book status *"Available"* |
| 5 | System | Records issue in DB, sets due date | `<<extend>>` UC *Print Receipt* is triggered |

**Alternative Flows:**

| AF# | Triggered At | Condition | System Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| `AF-1` | Step 2 | Member has overdue books | Displays "Account Blocked", ends UC |
| `AF-2` | Step 2 | Fines exceed ₹500 | Displays "Clear dues first", ends UC |
| `AF-3` | Step 4 | Book status is *"Issued"* or *"Reserved"* | Displays "Book Unavailable", ends UC |
| `AF-4` | Step 4 | `LibraryServer` offline | Displays "Network Error", ends UC |

**Use Case Diagram (2 marks):** The diagram should show:
- `Student` (stick figure) connected to *Issue a Book* (ellipse).
- `LibraryServer` (`<<system>>` rectangle) connected to *Issue a Book*.
- A dashed arrow from *Issue a Book* to *Verify Membership* labeled `<<include>>`.
- A dashed arrow from *Print Receipt* to *Issue a Book* labeled `<<extend>>`.
- A system boundary rectangle enclosing *Issue a Book*, *Verify Membership*, and *Print Receipt*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Traceability Matrix** = bidirectional mapping of **Requirements ↔ Design ↔ Code ↔ Test**.
- **Forward trace** = Req → Implementation. **Backward trace** = Implementation → Req.
- A **Gap** = a requirement with no implementation link. An **Orphan** = an implementation with no requirement link.
- Coverage = (Fully Traced Reqs) / (Total Reqs) × 100%. **Target = 100%**.
- A **Use Case** = a complete, goal-oriented actor-system interaction that delivers value in a single transaction.
- A **Use Case Diagram** shows **actors, use cases, system boundary, and three relationships**: association, `<<include>>`, `<<extend>>`.
- **`<<include>>`** = mandatory sub-flow; arrow points from base → included.
- **`<<extend>>`** = optional insertion with a guard; arrow points from extension → base.
- **Use case name** must be a **verb-noun goal phrase** (e.g., *"Withdraw Cash"*), NOT an implementation term.
- **Preconditions** describe the *required state before* the use case starts.
- **Postconditions** describe the *guaranteed state after* successful completion — they enable state-based testing.
- The **Use Case Description template** is worth more marks than the diagram; always write it out in full.
- A good use case is **atomic** (committed or rolled back as one unit) and **actor-driven** (the primary actor's goal is achieved).
- For the **KTU exam**: every 14-mark question on this topic demands **both a diagram AND a textual description** — missing either costs 4–5 marks.
- Remember the **EBP rule** (Elementary Business Process) when judging whether to split or merge use cases.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Requirements Specification Template
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Software Requirements Specification Template

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** is a formal, structured document that completely describes the **external behaviour**, **functional capabilities**, **performance constraints**, **design constraints**, and **external interfaces** of a software system intended for development. It serves as the **contract** between the *stakeholders* (customers, users, business analysts) and the *development team* (designers, coders, testers).

As per the widely accepted **IEEE 29148:2018** (which superseded **IEEE 830-1998**) standard — both of which are the canonical references in KTU Board Examinations — the SRS is the **single authoritative source** of requirements against which the validity of the final system is measured.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> The KTU Board specifically expects students to know the **IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148 standard template structure**, the **difference between functional and non-functional requirements**, and the **characteristics of a good SRS** (correct, unambiguous, complete, consistent, ranked for importance, verifiable, modifiable, traceable).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are commissioning an **architect to build your dream house**. Before a single brick is laid, you hand them a written document that specifies:

- How many rooms you need (3 bedrooms, 2 bathrooms)
- The maximum budget (₹80 Lakhs)
- The materials to be used (red brick, vitrified tiles)
- The orientation of the house (east-facing)
- The timeline (12 months)

That document is your **"House Requirements Specification."** The architect works *only* against that document. If the builder later says, *"You never told me the kitchen needed a chimney!"* — the blame falls on a **poorly written requirements document**.

The **SRS does exactly this for software** — it is the *blueprint-precursor* that locks down **WHAT** the system must do (and equally important, **WHAT** it must *not* do) *before* a single line of code is written.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — "Requirements" vs. "Design":**
> The SRS specifies **WHAT** the system should do, not **HOW** it should do it. For example, "The system shall authenticate a user in under 2 seconds" is a **requirement**. "The system shall use OAuth 2.0 with JWT tokens" is a **design decision** — and **does not belong** in a pure SRS.

## 1.3 Why is the SRS the Most Critical Document in Software Engineering?

| # | Reason | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cost Amplification (Boehm's Law)** | The cost of fixing a defect discovered during the **requirements phase is 1×**, but if the same defect is discovered during **maintenance, it can be 100× to 1000×**. |
| 2 | **Legal & Contractual Validity** | The SRS is the legally binding deliverable in fixed-price contracts (per IEEE 29148). |
| 3 | **Baseline for Validation & Verification (V&V)** | All test cases are derived *traceably* from SRS requirements. |
| 4 | **Knowledge Preservation** | Prevents loss of institutional memory when developers leave mid-project. |
| 5 | **Scope Control** | Prevents **"Scope Creep"** — the silent killer of projects. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love asking: *"List the characteristics of a good SRS."* Memorize the eight properties — **Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Consistent, Ranked, Verifiable, Modifiable, Traceable** — using the mnemonic **"CUC-RVMT"**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148 SRS Template — Section-by-Section Breakdown

The standard SRS template is divided into **four major parts**, with **Section 3 (Specific Requirements)** being the *core* and the most heavily weighted in KTU exams.

### **Part 1 — Introduction (Section 1 of SRS)**

This section sets the **context** for the entire document. It is read first by every stakeholder and must be comprehensible to non-technical readers.

1.1 **Purpose**
- Identifies the *intent* of the SRS document.
- Specifies the *intended audience* (e.g., developers, testers, project managers, client).
- Example phrasing: *"This SRS describes the functional and non-functional requirements for the Online Library Management System (OLMS), version 1.0, developed for ABC University. The intended readers are the project sponsor, the design team, and the QA team."*

1.2 **Document Conventions**
- Declares *typographical* or *stylistic* rules used throughout the document.
- Example: *"All requirements are numbered as [REQ-001], [REQ-002]…; the word 'shall' indicates a mandatory requirement, while 'should' indicates a desirable one."*

1.3 **Intended Audience and Reading Suggestions**
- Identifies *who* will read the document and in *what order*.
- Different roles may read different sections (e.g., clients read Sections 1 & 2; developers read all sections).

1.4 **Scope of the Project**
- States *what* the product will do, and equally important, *what it will NOT do*.
- Example: *"The OLMS will support book search, issue, return, and fine calculation. It will NOT support inter-library loan or digital e-book lending."*
- This is the **single most important sub-section for scope control**.

1.5 **References**
- Lists all external documents (e.g., IEEE 830:1998, project charter, related standards).

### **Part 2 — Overall Description (Section 2 of SRS)**

This section provides a **high-level view** of the product and its environment.

2.1 **Product Perspective**
- Describes whether the product is **standalone**, part of a **larger system**, or a **successor** to an existing system.
- Example: *"OLMS is a new, standalone web-based system that will replace the existing desktop application 'LibPro 2.0'."*

2.2 **Product Functions**
- A *summary* of the major functions (not the detailed requirements — those come in Section 3).
- Example: *"The OLMS will provide: user authentication, catalog search, book issue/return, fine management, and report generation."*

2.3 **User Classes and Characteristics**
- Identifies *distinct user categories* (e.g., *Student*, *Librarian*, *Administrator*) and their technical proficiency.

2.4 **Operating Environment**
- Specifies the *hardware*, *OS*, *network*, and *DBMS* constraints.
- Example: *"Windows Server 2019, Apache Tomcat 10, MySQL 8.0, minimum 8 GB RAM."*

2.5 **Design and Implementation Constraints**
- Lists *limits* imposed on the design (e.g., *"Must be compatible with all major browsers: Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari"*; *"Must comply with GDPR data privacy regulations"*).

2.6 **Assumptions and Dependencies**
- States *assumptions* made (e.g., *"Users have a stable internet connection"*) and *dependencies* on external factors (e.g., *"Depends on the university's LDAP server for authentication"*).

### **Part 3 — Specific Requirements (Section 3 of SRS) — THE CORE**

This is the **largest and most detailed** section. It must be written with *mathematical precision* and zero ambiguity.

3.1 **External Interface Requirements**
- **3.1.1 User Interfaces** — UI standards, layout constraints, error message formats.
- **3.1.2 Hardware Interfaces** — Printer, barcode scanner, RFID reader requirements.
- **3.1.3 Software Interfaces** — APIs, libraries, third-party services.
- **3.1.4 Communication Interfaces** — Protocols (HTTP/HTTPS, SMTP, FTP), data formats (JSON, XML).

3.2 **Functional Requirements (FRs)**
- Describe *what the system does* in response to inputs.
- Each FR is **uniquely numbered** and uses the word **"shall"**.
- Example: *"[REQ-F-007] The system shall allow a student to search the catalog by Author Name, Title, ISBN, or Subject."*

3.3 **Non-Functional Requirements (NFRs) / Quality Attributes**
- Describe *how well* the system performs its functions. NFRs are **measurable**.
- Categories: **Performance, Security, Reliability, Usability, Maintainability, Portability, Scalability**.

3.4 **Design Constraints**
- Hard *limitations* on the design (e.g., *"Must use PostgreSQL as the database"*).

3.5 **Logical Database Requirements**
- High-level data model, ER diagram references, retention policies.

### **Part 4 — Appendices (Section 4 of SRS)**

Contains **supporting information**: data flow diagrams, ER diagrams, sample data models, definitions of acronyms, change history log.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Cognitive Level — Apply & Analyze:**
> In ESE (End Semester Examination), students are often asked to *write a partial SRS* for a given mini-scenario (e.g., *"Write the Scope, Functional Requirements, and Non-Functional Requirements for an Online Examination System"*). Knowing the **template structure** cold is the only way to score full 14 marks.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Requirements Engineering Metrics

Although SRS writing is largely qualitative, the *analysis* of an SRS uses several **quantitative metrics** that frequently appear in KTU numerical/model questions.

| # | Metric | Formula / Definition | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Number of Requirements (N)** | Count of uniquely numbered REQs in the SRS. | Count | Measures SRS **size**. |
| 2 | **Requirement Stability Index (RSI)** | $\text{RSI} = \dfrac{N_{\text{unchanged}}}{N_{\text{total baseline}}} \times 100$ | **%** | Measures requirements **volatility**. |
| 3 | **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Score (1–1000) | FMEA-style **prioritization**. |
| 4 | **Requirement Defect Density (RDD)** | $\text{RDD} = \dfrac{\text{Defects found in SRS}}{N}$ | Defects per REQ | Measures SRS **quality**. |
| 5 | **Volatility (V)** | $V = \dfrac{\Delta N_{\text{requirements}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Ratio (0–1) | Measures **scope creep** rate. |
| 6 | **Traceability Coverage (TC)** | $\text{TC} = \dfrac{N_{\text{traced REQs}}}{N_{\text{total REQs}}} \times 100$ | **%** | Measures **backward + forward** traceability. |
| 7 | **Ambiguity Index (AI)** | $\text{AI} = 1 - \dfrac{N_{\text{unambiguous}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Ratio (0–1) | Lower is **better**; uses NLP heuristics. |
| 8 | **Eisner Formalism Defect Rate** | $\text{EDR} = \dfrac{D_{\text{formal}}}{N}$ | Defects per REQ | Measures **formal-spec** accuracy. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for the Table Above:**
> The **RPN (Risk Priority Number)** formula is the most tested metric. KTU examiners use a 1–10 scale for each of **Severity (S)**, **Occurrence (O)**, and **Detection (D)** — so the **range is 1 to 1000**. Any requirement with RPN > **200** is considered **high risk** and must be addressed first.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Avionics & Medical Devices (DO-178C, IEC 62304):** SRS is mandated as a regulatory document. A missing NFR (e.g., *"Failure rate < 10⁻⁹"*) can cause FDA / DGCA rejection.
- **Banking & FinTech (PCI-DSS):** Security NFRs in the SRS are auditable compliance items.
- **IT Services (CMMI Level 3+):** Process maturity assessments score organizations on the **traceability** of their SRS.
- **Defense (MIL-STD-498):** The SRS follows a strict DoD-styled template; deviations require formal waivers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Building an SRS from Scratch (Step-by-Step Workflow)

The KTU Board frequently asks for a **detailed, sequential explanation of how an SRS is constructed**. Below is the **complete, unbroken derivation workflow** that is worth **14 marks** when written cleanly in the exam hall.

### **Step 1 — Project Inception & Feasibility Analysis**

Before the SRS is written, the **System Concept** document is approved.

$$\text{System Concept} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Feasibility Study (TELOS)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Project Charter Approved}$$

Where **TELOS** stands for **T**echnical, **E**conomic, **L**egal, **O**perational, **S**chedule feasibility.

### **Step 2 — Requirements Elicitation**

The analyst gathers raw requirements from stakeholders using **elicitation techniques**.

$$
\text{Elicitation Techniques} = \{\text{Interviews},\ \text{Surveys},\ \text{Observation},\ \text{Workshops (JAD)},\ \text{Prototyping},\ \text{Document Analysis},\ \text{Focus Groups}\}
$$

Each technique has a specific **yield rate**. The **average completeness** achievable is:

$$
C_{\text{completeness}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - c_i)
$$

where $c_i$ is the fractional completeness contributed by technique $i$, and $n$ is the total number of techniques used.

> **Example Interpretation:** If Interview contributes $c_1 = 0.55$ and Workshop contributes $c_2 = 0.40$, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{completeness}} &= 1 - (1 - 0.55)(1 - 0.40) \\
&= 1 - (0.45)(0.60) \\
&= 1 - 0.27 \\
&= 0.73
\end{aligned}
$$

So using **both** techniques yields **73%** completeness, whereas using *either alone* gives only 55% or 40%.

### **Step 3 — Requirements Analysis & Negotiation**

Conflicting requirements are **negotiated** using **prioritization schemes** (MoSCoW, AHP, etc.).
