The **Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP)** — popular in KTU numericals — uses **pairwise comparison matrices** to derive priority weights $w_i$ for each requirement. The consistency check uses:

$$
\text{CR} = \dfrac{\text{CI}}{\text{RI}}, \quad \text{where} \quad \text{CI} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\max} - n}{n - 1}
$$

A **Consistency Ratio (CR) < 0.10** indicates acceptable consistency per **Saaty's** method.

### **Step 4 — Requirements Specification (Writing the SRS)**

The validated requirements are mapped onto the **IEEE 830 template structure** developed in Section 2.1 of these notes.

### **Step 5 — Requirements Validation**

The draft SRS is validated using **four checks** (the **"V&V Four"**):

$$
\text{Valid} \iff \big[\text{Correctness} \land \text{Completeness} \land \text{Consistency} \land \text{Feasibility}\big] = \text{TRUE}
$$

For each requirement, the **defect density** is computed as:

$$
\text{RDD} = \frac{D_{\text{defects found in SRS review}}}{N_{\text{total requirements}}}
$$

The industry target is **RDD ≤ 0.05** (i.e., 1 defect per 20 requirements).

### **Step 6 — Requirements Management & Traceability**

A **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** is constructed. The **traceability coverage** is:

$$
\text{TC} = \frac{N_{\text{traced}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

A **forward trace** links a requirement to its design, code, and test cases; a **backward trace** links it to its source (stakeholder, document, regulation). The KTU Board expects both directions in any RTM answer.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — RPN Calculation (Board-Exam Style)

**Problem:** For an *Online Voting System*, the following three requirements are identified. Compute the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** and rank them. A requirement is **High Risk** if RPN ≥ 200.

| Requirement ID | Description | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| REQ-NFR-01 | Login response time ≤ 1 sec | 7 | 8 | 4 |
| REQ-NFR-02 | Vote encryption using AES-256 | 10 | 2 | 3 |
| REQ-FR-03 | Vote tally accuracy (zero-error) | 9 | 5 | 6 |

**Solution (Step-by-Step, written exactly as a KTU model answer expects):**

For **REQ-NFR-01**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\text{NFR-01}} &= S \times O \times D \\
&= 7 \times 8 \times 4 \\
&= 224
\end{aligned}
$$

For **REQ-NFR-02**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\text{NFR-02}} &= 10 \times 2 \times 3 \\
&= 60
\end{aligned}
$$

For **REQ-FR-03**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\text{FR-03}} &= 9 \times 5 \times 6 \\
&= 270
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Ranking (Highest Risk First):**

$$
\text{REQ-FR-03} \;(270) \;>\; \text{REQ-NFR-01} \;(224) \;>\; \text{REQ-NFR-02} \;(60)
$$

> **KTU Board Examiner's Reasoning:** Both REQ-FR-03 and REQ-NFR-01 exceed the RPN = 200 threshold and are classified as **High Risk** — they must be tested exhaustively and reviewed in the very first SRS walkthrough.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Functional vs. Non-Functional Requirements

| Attribute | Functional Requirements (FR) | Non-Functional Requirements (NFR) |
|---|---|---|
| **What it specifies** | **WHAT** the system does | **HOW WELL** it does it |
| **Verb in sentence** | "shall allow", "shall calculate" | "shall be", "shall respond within" |
| **Measurability** | Often binary (pass/fail) | Always **quantified** (e.g., 2 sec, 99.9%) |
| **Number in typical SRS** | ~60–70% of all requirements | ~30–40% of all requirements |
| **Testability** | Easier — derived test cases | Harder — often needs specialized tools |
| **Example** | *"The system shall generate a monthly sales report."* | *"The report shall be generated in under 5 seconds for up to 1 million records."* |
| **Document Location** | SRS Section 3.2 | SRS Section 3.3 |
| **Verification Method** | Functional / Acceptance testing | Performance / Load / Security testing |
| **Origin** | Derived from *user tasks* & *use cases* | Derived from *quality models* (ISO 25010) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of the SRS Template (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This diagram maps the **IEEE 830 / 29148 standard structure** as a top-down functional architecture. It is the **single most important visual** for any KTU question on SRS.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeSRS["SRS DOCUMENT IEEE 830 29148"]
    nodeS1["SECTION 1 INTRODUCTION"]
    nodeS2["SECTION 2 OVERALL DESCRIPTION"]
    nodeS3["SECTION 3 SPECIFIC REQUIREMENTS"]
    nodeS4["SECTION 4 APPENDICES"]

    nodeS1A["1.1 Purpose"]
    nodeS1B["1.2 Document Conventions"]
    nodeS1C["1.3 Intended Audience"]
    nodeS1D["1.4 Scope of Project"]
    nodeS1E["1.5 References"]

    nodeS2A["2.1 Product Perspective"]
    nodeS2B["2.2 Product Functions Summary"]
    nodeS2C["2.3 User Classes and Characteristics"]
    nodeS2D["2.4 Operating Environment"]
    nodeS2E["2.5 Design Constraints"]
    nodeS2F["2.6 Assumptions and Dependencies"]

    nodeS3A["3.1 External Interface Requirements"]
    nodeS3B["3.2 Functional Requirements"]
    nodeS3C["3.3 Non Functional Requirements"]
    nodeS3D["3.4 Design Constraints"]
    nodeS3E["3.5 Logical Database Requirements"]

    nodeS3A1["3.1.1 User Interfaces"]
    nodeS3A2["3.1.2 Hardware Interfaces"]
    nodeS3A3["3.1.3 Software Interfaces"]
    nodeS3A4["3.1.4 Communication Interfaces"]

    nodeS4A["4.1 Data Flow Diagrams"]
    nodeS4B["4.2 ER Diagrams"]
    nodeS4C["4.3 Sample Data Models"]
    nodeS4D["4.4 Acronyms Glossary"]

    nodeSRS --> nodeS1
    nodeSRS --> nodeS2
    nodeSRS --> nodeS3
    nodeSRS --> nodeS4

    nodeS1 --> nodeS1A
    nodeS1 --> nodeS1B
    nodeS1 --> nodeS1C
    nodeS1 --> nodeS1D
    nodeS1 --> nodeS1E

    nodeS2 --> nodeS2A
    nodeS2 --> nodeS2B
    nodeS2 --> nodeS2C
    nodeS2 --> nodeS2D
    nodeS2 --> nodeS2E
    nodeS2 --> nodeS2F

    nodeS3 --> nodeS3A
    nodeS3 --> nodeS3B
    nodeS3 --> nodeS3C
    nodeS3 --> nodeS3D
    nodeS3 --> nodeS3E

    nodeS3A --> nodeS3A1
    nodeS3A --> nodeS3A2
    nodeS3A --> nodeS3A3
    nodeS3A --> nodeS3A4

    nodeS4 --> nodeS4A
    nodeS4 --> nodeS4B
    nodeS4 --> nodeS4C
    nodeS4 --> nodeS4D
```

## 4.2 End-to-End SRS Development Lifecycle (Mermaid Flowchart)

This block diagram shows the **complete lifecycle** of how raw stakeholder needs are transformed into a validated, baselined SRS.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStart["STAKEHOLDER NEEDS RAW INPUTS"]
    nodeElicit["ELICITATION INTERVIEWS WORKSHOPS PROTOTYPING"]
    nodeAnalyze["ANALYSIS AND NEGOTIATION CONFLICT RESOLUTION"]
    nodeSpec["SRS DRAFT WRITTEN IEEE 830 TEMPLATE"]
    nodeValidate["VALIDATION REVIEWS WALKTHROUGHS INSPECTION"]
    nodeBaseline["BASELINED SRS APPROVED FOR DEVELOPMENT"]
    nodeManage["CHANGE MANAGEMENT TRACEABILITY MATRIX"]

    nodeStart --> nodeElicit
    nodeElicit --> nodeAnalyze
    nodeAnalyze --> nodeSpec
    nodeSpec --> nodeValidate
    nodeValidate --> nodeBaseline
    nodeBaseline --> nodeManage
    nodeManage -. CHANGE REQUEST .-> nodeAnalyze
```

## 4.3 Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) — Visual Mapping

The RTM is the **graph-theoretic backbone** of any SRS. The following diagram shows how a single functional requirement fans out into design, code, and test artifacts, and traces back to its source.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeSrc["SOURCE STAKEHOLDER A REGULATION ISO 27001"]
    nodeReq["REQ F 001 USER LOGIN WITH MFA"]
    nodeDesign["DESIGN DOC OAUTH2 JWT ARCHITECTURE"]
    nodeCode["CODE MODULE AUTH SERVICE LOGIN CONTROLLER"]
    nodeTest1["UNIT TEST TC 001 1 EMAIL VALIDATION"]
    nodeTest2["INTEGRATION TEST TC 002 OAUTH HANDSHAKE"]
    nodeTest3["ACCEPTANCE TEST TC 003 MFA FLOW E2E"]

    nodeSrc --> nodeReq
    nodeReq --> nodeDesign
    nodeDesign --> nodeCode
    nodeCode --> nodeTest1
    nodeCode --> nodeTest2
    nodeTest2 --> nodeTest3
```

## 4.4 ISO 25010 Quality Model for Non-Functional Requirements (Mermaid)

The most important **non-functional categories** an SRS must address are defined by the **ISO/IEC 25010** quality model. This is a high-yield KTU diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeNFR["NON FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS ISO 25010"]
    nodePerf["PERFORMANCE EFFICIENCY"]
    nodeSec["SECURITY"]
    nodeRel["RELIABILITY"]
    nodeUsab["USABILITY"]
    nodeMaint["MAINTAINABILITY"]
    nodePort["PORTABILITY"]
    nodeComp["COMPATIBILITY"]
    nodeFunc["FUNCTIONAL SUITABILITY"]

    nodePerf --> nodePerf1["Response Time"]
    nodePerf --> nodePerf2["Throughput"]
    nodePerf --> nodePerf3["Resource Utilization"]

    nodeSec --> nodeSec1["Confidentiality"]
    nodeSec --> nodeSec2["Integrity"]
    nodeSec --> nodeSec3["Non Repudiation"]
    nodeSec --> nodeSec4["Authenticity"]

    nodeRel --> nodeRel1["Maturity"]
    nodeRel --> nodeRel2["Availability"]
    nodeRel --> nodeRel3["Fault Tolerance"]
    nodeRel --> nodeRel4["Recoverability"]

    nodeNFR --> nodePerf
    nodeNFR --> nodeSec
    nodeNFR --> nodeRel
    nodeNFR --> nodeUsab
    nodeNFR --> nodeMaint
    nodeNFR --> nodePort
    nodeNFR --> nodeComp
    nodeNFR --> nodeFunc
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 2, Q1a]`** — **3 Marks**
> **Q:** *List any four characteristics of a good Software Requirements Specification (SRS) as per the IEEE 830 standard.*

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks — write only 4 for 3 marks):

The four most important characteristics are:

1. **Correct** — Every requirement listed must accurately represent a stakeholder need; no requirement is incorrectly stated.
2. **Unambiguous** — Every requirement has only **one possible interpretation**; uses precise words like "shall" instead of "should" or "may."
3. **Complete** — All functional and non-functional requirements are described — including responses to invalid/exceptional inputs.
4. **Verifiable** — Every requirement is testable through inspection, analysis, demonstration, or testing.

*(Optionally: Consistent, Ranked for Importance/Stability, Modifiable, Traceable.)*

---

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 2, Q1b]`** — **3 Marks**
> **Q:** *Differentiate between Functional and Non-Functional Requirements with one example each.*

### ✅ Model Answer:

| Aspect | Functional Requirement | Non-Functional Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Specifies **WHAT** the system does (a specific behaviour). | Specifies **HOW WELL** the system performs (a quality attribute). |
| **Measurability** | Often binary — either the feature works or it does not. | Always **quantified** (response time, uptime %, etc.). |
| **Example** | *"The system shall allow a student to download the hall ticket in PDF format."* | *"The system shall generate and deliver the PDF hall ticket within 3 seconds under peak load of 10,000 concurrent users."* |
| **Test Method** | Functional / Acceptance testing. | Performance / Load / Stress / Security testing. |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module 2 Internal Choice)

---

### **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Q7a]`** — **14 Marks**

**Q7a)** *(a)* Explain the **IEEE 830 standard structure of a Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** document with a neat block diagram. List **any four characteristics of a good SRS**. *(7 Marks)*

*(b)* For an **Online Banking Transaction System**, write **five functional requirements** and **five non-functional requirements** in the standard SRS format. *(7 Marks)*

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Q7a (Part a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Software Requirements Specification (SRS) is a formal description of the functional and non-functional requirements of a software system. The **IEEE 830-1998** standard (superseded by **IEEE 29148-2018**) prescribes a uniform structure for the SRS that is widely used in industry and academic projects.

**Standard Structure (5 marks — see block diagram in Section 4.1):**

**Section 1 — Introduction** *(0.5 mark)*

- **1.1 Purpose** — Identifies the intent of the SRS document and the intended audience.
- **1.2 Scope** — Describes the product's boundaries, what it does, and explicitly what it does **not** do.
- **1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations** — Glossary of terms used.
- **1.4 References** — Lists all related documents (e.g., IEEE 830, project plan).
- **1.5 Overview** — Describes the rest of the document's content.

**Section 2 — Overall Description** *(0.5 mark)*

- **2.1 Product Perspective** — Standalone vs. part of a larger system.
- **2.2 Product Functions** — A high-level summary of major features.
- **2.3 User Characteristics** — Description of user classes (e.g., Admin, Customer).
- **2.4 Constraints** — Design, regulatory, hardware, software constraints.
- **2.5 Assumptions and Dependencies.**

**Section 3 — Specific Requirements (Core Section)** *(1.5 marks)*

- **3.1 External Interface Requirements** — User, hardware, software, communication interfaces.
- **3.2 Functional Requirements** — Numbered, using "shall," with inputs, processing, outputs.
- **3.3 Non-Functional Requirements** — Performance, security, reliability, usability.
- **3.4 Design Constraints.**

**Section 4 — Appendices** *(0.5 mark)* — Supporting diagrams, sample data, change log.

**Four Characteristics of a Good SRS (1 mark):**

1. **Correct** — Every requirement truly represents a stakeholder need.
2. **Unambiguous** — Single, clear interpretation; uses **"shall"** for mandatory items.
3. **Complete** — All use cases, including abnormal and exception paths, are covered.
4. **Verifiable** — Every requirement is independently testable (inspection, demo, or test).

*(Optionally — Consistent, Modifiable, Traceable, Ranked.)*

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Q7a (Part b) — 7 Marks

**Online Banking Transaction System — SRS Extract**

**Functional Requirements (FRs):** *(3.5 marks — 0.7 each)*

1. **[REQ-F-001] Login & Authentication** — *"The system shall allow a registered user to log in using a User ID and Password and shall enforce Two-Factor Authentication (OTP via SMS) for every login."*
2. **[REQ-F-002] Fund Transfer** — *"The system shall allow an authenticated user to transfer funds from his/her own savings account to any pre-registered beneficiary account using NEFT/IMPS/UPI."*
3. **[REQ-F-003] Transaction History** — *"The system shall display the last 90 days of account transactions in a paginated table with date, type, amount, and balance fields."*
4. **[REQ-F-004] Bill Payment** — *"The system shall allow a user to pay utility bills (electricity, water, gas, broadband) and shall generate a digital receipt upon successful payment."*
5. **[REQ-F-005] Account Statement Download** — *"The system shall allow the user to download a PDF account statement for any user-selected date range, password-protected with the user's login password."*

**Non-Functional Requirements (NFRs):** *(3.5 marks — 0.7 each)*

1. **[REQ-NF-001] Performance** — *"The system shall complete any fund transfer request and display a confirmation page within 3 seconds under a peak load of 5,000 concurrent users."*
2. **[REQ-NF-002] Security** — *"All data in transit shall be encrypted using TLS 1.3 or higher, and all sensitive data at rest (e.g., account numbers) shall be encrypted using AES-256."*
3. **[REQ-NF-003] Availability** — *"The system shall maintain an uptime of 99.95% measured monthly, excluding scheduled maintenance windows announced 48 hours in advance."*
4. **[REQ-NF-004] Reliability** — *"The system shall achieve a Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of at least 720 hours and shall recover from a single server crash within 60 seconds (failover)."*
5. **[REQ-NF-005] Usability** — *"A first-time user shall be able to complete a fund transfer in no more than 5 steps and within 2 minutes, as verified by usability testing on a representative sample of 30 users."*

---

### **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Q7b]`** — **14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**Q7b)** *(a)* Define **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)**. Explain its **types (forward, backward)** and illustrate with a sample RTM for a *Library Management System*. *(7 Marks)*

*(b)* Compute the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** for the three requirements given below. Rank them and identify the high-risk requirements (RPN ≥ 200). *(7 Marks)*

| Req ID | Description | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| REQ-01 | Search must return results in ≤ 2 sec | 6 | 7 | 5 |
| REQ-02 | Fine calculation must be 100% accurate | 9 | 4 | 7 |
| REQ-03 | System must support 1,000 concurrent users | 8 | 6 | 4 |

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Q7b (Part a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):** A **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** is a tabular document (typically a spreadsheet) that **links every requirement** to its **source (origin)** and to its downstream artefacts (**design, code, and test cases**). It is the **single most important tool** in requirements management and is mandatory in **CMMI Level 3+** and **ISO 9001** audits.

**Types (3 marks):**

1. **Backward Traceability (Source Trace)** — Links each requirement to its **origin**: the stakeholder, business case, regulation, or use case that produced it. *Answers: "Where did this requirement come from?"*
2. **Forward Traceability (Design-Code-Test Trace)** — Links each requirement to the **design documents, code modules, and test cases** that implement and verify it. *Answers: "Where is this requirement realized and verified?"*
3. **Bi-directional Traceability** — Combines both above; the **gold standard** for safety-critical systems (DO-178C, IEC 62304).

**Sample RTM for Library Management System (3 marks):**

| Req ID | Requirement | Source (Backward) | Design Doc | Code Module | Unit Test | Integration Test | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| REQ-F-01 | Search by title / author / ISBN | Stakeholder: Chief Librarian | DES-SR-01 | `SearchService.java` | TC-UT-01 | TC-IT-05 | Verified |
| REQ-F-02 | Issue book with max 3 active loans | Business Rule: BR-12 | DES-IS-02 | `IssueController.java` | TC-UT-09 | TC-IT-11 | Verified |
| REQ-F-03 | Calculate fine @ ₹2/day after due date | Policy: LIB-POL-04 | DES-FN-03 | `FineCalculator.java` | TC-UT-14 | TC-IT-16 | Verified |
| REQ-NF-01 | Search result within 2 sec | ISO 25010: Performance | DES-NF-01 | `SearchIndex.java` | — | TC-PT-01 | Verified |
| REQ-NF-02 | Concurrent user support = 500 | Capacity Plan CP-01 | DES-NF-02 | `LoadBalancer.java` | — | TC-LT-01 | Pending |

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Q7b (Part b) — 7 Marks

Recall: $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$, where each is rated on a **1–10 scale** (range: **1 to 1000**). Threshold for **High Risk**: RPN ≥ **200**.

**For REQ-01:** *(1 mark)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{01} &= 6 \times 7 \times 5 \\
&= 210
\end{aligned}
$$

**For REQ-02:** *(1 mark)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{02} &= 9 \times 4 \times 7 \\
&= 252
\end{aligned}
$$

**For REQ-03:** *(1 mark)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{03} &= 8 \times 6 \times 4 \\
&= 192
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ranking (Highest to Lowest) and Classification (2 marks):**

$$
\text{REQ-02} \;(252) \;>\; \text{REQ-01} \;(210) \;>\; \text{REQ-03} \;(192)
$$

**Conclusion:** REQ-02 and REQ-01 are **HIGH RISK** (RPN ≥ 200) and must be prioritized for **exhaustive testing and formal inspection**. REQ-03 (RPN = 192) is **medium risk** and may be tested under standard coverage.

**Tie-Breaker Note (1 mark):** When two RPNs are equal, priority is given to the one with **higher Severity (S)**, since severity directly maps to user/customer impact.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks
> 1. **Mixing "shall" / "should" / "may"** — Only the word **"shall"** denotes a mandatory requirement. "Should" = desirable, "May" = optional. Wrong use → loses **2 marks** in any FR/NFR list.
> 2. **Writing Design in SRS** — Do NOT include design choices like *"Use MySQL"* or *"Use ReactJS"* in Section 3.2 / 3.3. They belong in Section 3.4 (Design Constraints) only when **mandated** by the client.
> 3. **Forgetting Numbering** — Every requirement MUST be uniquely numbered like **REQ-F-001, REQ-NF-001**. Un-numbered lists → **−1.5 marks**.
> 4. **NFRs Without Numbers** — *"The system should be fast"* is **not** an NFR. *"The system shall respond in ≤ 2 seconds"* **is** an NFR. Quantification is **mandatory** (−3 marks typical penalty).
> 5. **Confusing IEEE 830 with IEEE 29148** — They are related, but IEEE 29148 is the **newer 2018** version. KTU accepts both, but be **explicit** about which you are citing.
> 6. **No "shall" in scope** — Scope must say what the system **shall** do and what it **shall not** do. One-sentence scopes are a sure sign of an unprepared answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **SRS = the contract.** It is the **legal and technical** agreement between the customer and developer.
- ✅ The **IEEE 830 / 29148** template has **4 main sections**: Introduction, Overall Description, Specific Requirements, Appendices.
- ✅ **Section 3 (Specific Requirements)** is the **largest and most exam-relevant** part.
- ✅ Use **"shall"** for mandatory requirements, **"should"** for desirable, **"may"** for optional. Misuse = lost marks.
- ✅ **Functional Requirements** = WHAT the system does; **Non-Functional Requirements** = HOW WELL (always quantified).
- ✅ **NFR Categories** to remember: **PEPRUMC** = **P**erformance, s**E**curity, **P**ortability, **R**eliability, **U**sability, **M**aintainability, **C**ompatibility (+ Functional Suitability per ISO 25010).
- ✅ **8 Characteristics of a good SRS** = Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Consistent, **R**anked, Verifiable, Modifiable, Traceable. Mnemonic: **CUC-RVMT**.
- ✅ **RPN = S × O × D**, range 1–1000, threshold = **200** for high risk.
- ✅ **Traceability** has **two directions**: **Forward** (req → design → code → test) and **Backward** (req → source). Both are required.
- ✅ **Scope** must explicitly state what the system **will NOT do** — this prevents scope creep.
- ✅ The **IEEE 29148:2018** is the **current** standard, but KTU examiners still accept **IEEE 830:1998**. Mention both to be safe.
- ✅ A **good SRS** is **modifiable** — every requirement is uniquely numbered and indexed.
- ✅ For **safety-critical systems** (aerospace, medical), SRS is a **regulatory deliverable** — not a documentation formality.
- ✅ A **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** is the **proof** that the system meets its requirements.
- ✅ The word **"shall"** in an SRS carries the same legal weight as a clause in a contract.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Personas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Personas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Persona (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):** A *persona* is a **fictional, yet realistic and research-based, archetypal representation** of a key user (or stakeholder) group that is likely to interact with a software system, product, or service. It captures user **goals, skills, attitudes, behaviours, and contextual background** in a narrative form to drive user-centred design and requirements elicitation decisions.

The concept was popularised by **Alan Cooper** in his seminal work *"The Inmates Are Running the Asylum"* (1998) and later refined in *"About Face: The Essentials of Interaction Design"*. Within the **KTU 2024 Software Engineering syllabus (Module 2 – Requirements Engineering & User Modelling)**, personas are positioned as a *User-Centred Design (UCD)* artefact used to translate abstract user categories into tangible, designable characters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Examiners:** A persona is **NOT** a job title (e.g., "Manager"), nor is it a market segment (e.g., "Age 25–35, urban"). It is a **specific individual** with a name, photograph, biography, motivations, and frustrations — created to make design decisions *empathetic* and *concrete*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are designing a **new college canteen app** for KTU students. If you say *"This app is for students,"* that is too vague. But if you imagine:

> *"**Ananya Suresh**, a 20-year-old B.Tech 5th-semester CSE student at a KTU-affiliated college in Thrissur. She carries a Redmi Note 13, has a slow 4G connection in the hostel, prefers UPI payments, hates logging in repeatedly, and checks the menu only between 10:30 and 11:00 AM before her next class. She is frustrated by cluttered apps and ad pop-ups."*

…now every design decision — *fast login via OTP, offline menu cache, minimal UI* — has a **human face**. That character is a **Persona**.

## 1.3 Why Personas Exist — The "Voice" Problem

| Problem in Requirements | How Personas Solve It |
|---|---|
| Designers build for *themselves* | Forces empathy for *real* users |
| Stakeholders use *generic* language (e.g., "the user") | Disputes are resolved by asking *"What would Ananya do?"* |
| Requirements ignore *context* (device, environment, mood) | Persona profiles make context explicit |
| Conflict between features | Prioritisation is done by **persona goals** |

> [!TIP]
> **One-line KTU answer:** *"A persona is a fictional, narrative description of a typical user, used to humanise requirements and guide user-centred design."*

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Persona — The Structural Components

A *well-constructed* persona, as per industry standards (Cooper, Pruitt & Adlin – *The Persona Lifecycle*, 2006), contains the following **anatomical parts**:

1. **Identifier** — A realistic first and last name (e.g., *"Rahul Menon"*).
2. **Photograph / Visual** — A real stock image representing the user.
3. **Demographic Snapshot** — Age, gender, education, occupation, income bracket, location.
4. **Goals (What they want to achieve)** — Divided into:
   - **End goals** (life objectives)
   - **Experience goals** (how they want to *feel*)
   - **Practical goals** (task completion)
5. **Skills & Expertise** — Technical proficiency, domain knowledge.
6. **Attitudes & Motivations** — Beliefs, fears, frustrations.
7. **Behaviours & Habits** — Typical usage patterns, devices used.
8. **Context of Use** — Environment (office, hostel, travel), device, time of day.
9. **Quote** — A representative statement capturing the persona's worldview.
10. **Scenarios** — Mini-narratives showing the persona using the system.

## 2.2 Classification of Personas (High-Yield for KTU 14-Mark Questions)

| Type | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Persona** | The dominant user group the system is *designed for*. There should be **only 1–2** primary personas. | Drives core feature decisions |
| **Secondary Persona** | Users who interact occasionally or with limited scope of the system. | Influences secondary features |
| **Supplementary Persona** | Users with specific needs (e.g., accessibility, language). | Handles edge-case requirements |
| **Anti-Persona** | Users the system is **explicitly not** designed for. | Helps *scope* and reject features |
| **Served Persona** | Users who interact with the system *indirectly* (e.g., a customer of a customer). | Models B2B / multi-stakeholder systems |
| **Customer Persona** vs. **User Persona** | The buyer (customer) may differ from the actual user. | Relevant in B2C products |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often ask: *"Differentiate between a primary and a secondary persona."* Memorise the table above and add a 1-line example for full marks.

## 2.3 Persona Construction Methodology — The 5-Step Process

$$
\text{Persona Pipeline: Research} \rightarrow \text{Pooling} \rightarrow \text{Mapping} \rightarrow \text{Synthesis} \rightarrow \text{Validation}
$$

1. **Research** — Conduct user interviews, surveys, field studies, and ethnographic observation.
2. **Pooling** — Group observations into affinity clusters (similar behaviours, goals, pain points).
3. **Mapping** — Plot clusters on an *Axis of Behaviour* (e.g., *Tech Savvy* vs. *Casual User*).
4. **Synthesis** — Build a single archetypal character from each cluster, assigning a name, photo, and narrative.
5. **Validation** — Verify with real users and stakeholders that the persona is *recognisable* and *representative*.

## 2.4 The Persona–Scenario Link (KTU Favourite)

A **scenario** is a *narrative description of how a persona interacts with the system* to achieve a goal. In KTU terminology:

$$
\text{Persona (Who)} \;\; + \;\; \text{Context (Where/When)} \;\; + \;\; \text{Goal (Why)} \;\; \rightarrow \;\; \text{Scenario (How)}
$$

| Persona Element | Scenario Element | Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| Name & identity | Context of use | "Ananya, in her hostel at 10:30 AM" |
| Goals | Task narrative | "wants to order lunch quickly" |
| Skills | Constraints | "uses only 4G, prefers UPI" |
| Frustrations | Problems to solve | "hates login screens" |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam one-liner:** *"Scenarios breathe life into static personas by placing them in concrete use contexts."*

## 2.5 Benefits and Limitations (High-Yield Comparison Table)

| **Benefits** | **Limitations** |
|---|---|
| Builds *empathy* among the development team | Risk of *persona bias* if not research-backed |
| Provides a *shared vocabulary* ("What would Rahul think?") | Can become *stale* as users evolve |
| Aids *feature prioritisation* via goal-alignment | Over-specification leads to *over-design* |
| Improves *requirement negotiation* outcomes | Requires significant research investment |
| Supports *usability evaluation* (persona-based testing) | Poorly defined personas confuse stakeholders |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Agile/Scrum:** Personas are attached to *User Stories* — each story is written *from the persona's perspective*: *"As **Ananya (persona)**, I want to pre-order lunch so that I can avoid the queue."*
- **UX Design:** Drives wireframes, navigation, and interaction models.
- **Accessibility:** *Supplementary personas* (e.g., a colour-blind senior user) enforce WCAG compliance.
- **Marketing & Product Strategy:** Aligns feature roadmaps with the *voice of the customer*.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Personas are *qualitative artefacts*, not mathematical objects. Therefore, "derivation" here means the **step-by-step construction process** of a persona from raw research data — modelled as a deterministic pipeline with an analytical mapping matrix.

## 3.1 Formal Pipeline — From Raw Data to Validated Persona

Let the **raw user research data** be represented as a multiset of observation tuples:

$$
\mathcal{O} = \{\, o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_n \,\}
$$

where each observation $o_i$ is a tuple:

$$
o_i = (\text{behaviour}_i,\; \text{goal}_i,\; \text{context}_i,\; \text{pain}_i)
$$

### Step 1 — Affinity Clustering

Group the observations into $k$ clusters $\{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\}$ such that observations within a cluster share similar goals and pain points. A common metric is the **cosine similarity** between goal vectors, but the KTU syllabus treats this as a *qualitative affinity diagram*.

$$
C_j = \{\, o_i \;\vert\; \text{sim}(o_i, \mu_j) \geq \tau \,\}
$$

where $\mu_j$ is the cluster centroid and $\tau$ is a similarity threshold.

### Step 2 — Axis-of-Behaviour Mapping

Plot each cluster centroid on a 2-D **behaviour axis**. A classic KTU example:

| Axis | Low End | High End |
|---|---|---|
| X-axis | Tech-novice | Tech-savvy |
| Y-axis | Task-focused | Experience-focused |

> *Textual explanation:* Each cluster $C_j$ is plotted by its *mean technical comfort* and *motivation type*. Clusters occupying extreme or central positions become candidates for **primary personas**.

### Step 3 — Persona Synthesis

For each retained cluster $C_j$, instantiate a *fictional character* $P_j$ with attributes aggregated from the cluster:

$$
P_j = \big( \text{Name},\; \text{Age},\; \text{Goals}(C_j),\; \text{Skills}(C_j),\; \text{Context}(C_j) \big)
$$

> *Textual explanation:* The synthesised persona is essentially a **centroid of human attributes** — a single representative sample drawn from the cluster's behavioural distribution.

### Step 4 — Narrative Enrichment

Attach a *biography*, a *photograph*, a *representative quote*, and at least **one scenario** to $P_j$. This step transforms a data-centroid into a *designable character*.

### Step 5 — Validation

Conduct a **recognition test** with $m$ real users. Compute the recognition score:

$$
R_{P_j} = \frac{\text{Number of users who identify with } P_j}{m}
$$

> *Textual explanation:* If $R_{P_j} \geq 0.7$, the persona is considered *valid* and *representative*. Below 0.5, the persona must be refined or merged with another cluster.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Constructing a Persona for a KTU Student Portal

**Scenario:** A team is building a new **KTU University Student Portal App** for attendance, results, and exam registration.

### Step 1 — Research Output (Simulated)

| Observation | Behaviour | Goal | Context | Pain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $o_1$ | Checks portal on phone | View attendance % | Hostel Wi-Fi, 9 PM | Slow loading |
| $o_2$ | Uses laptop at lab | Download hall ticket | College lab, 8 AM | Multiple logins fail |
| $o_3$ | Relies on WhatsApp forwards | Know result date | Home, 11 PM | No push notification |
| $o_4$ | Fills exam form via mobile | Register for exam | Bus stop, 2G | Form timeout |
| $o_5$ | Calls friend for help | Reset password | Hostel, 11 PM | Forgot password, no email |

### Step 2 — Clustering Result

| Cluster | Member Observations | Dominant Trait |
|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ (Mobile-first user) | $o_1, o_3, o_4$ | Smartphone, low bandwidth |
| $C_2$ (Laptop user) | $o_2, o_5$ | Desktop, formal hours |

### Step 3 — Synthesised Persona

> **Persona P1 — "Ananya Suresh" (Primary Persona)**
> - **Age:** 21, B.Tech CSE S5, Govt. Engineering College Thrissur.
> - **Device:** Redmi Note 13, Android 14.
> - **Connectivity:** Unreliable 4G/Wi-Fi.
> - **Goals:** View attendance %, receive result alerts instantly, pay exam fees via UPI.
> - **Frustrations:** Slow page loads, frequent logouts, no offline mode.
> - **Quote:** *"I just want to check my attendance in 5 seconds — why does the app take 30?"*

### Step 4 — Scenario Mapped to Persona

> *"Ananya, lying in her hostel bed at 9:15 PM, opens the KTU app. The app shows her attendance percentage instantly via a cached dashboard. She gets a push notification the moment results are published. She pays her exam fee via UPI in 3 taps, even on 2G."*

### Step 5 — Validation

Suppose 8 out of 10 interviewed students identified strongly with Ananya.

$$
R_{P_1} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8 \;\;\geq\;\; 0.7
$$

> *Conclusion:* Persona $P_1$ is **validated** as the primary persona.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Persona Construction Pipeline (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Research: Interviews, Surveys, Field Studies] --> B[Affinity Clustering of Observations]
    B --> C[Mapping Clusters on Behaviour Axes]
    C --> D{Qualifies as Primary Secondary or Anti-Persona?}
    D -- Primary --> E[Synthesise Primary Persona P1]
    D -- Secondary --> F[Synthesise Secondary Persona P2]
    D -- Anti --> G[Define Anti-Persona: Explicitly Not Target User]
    E --> H[Attach Biography Photograph Quote Scenarios]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Recognition Test with Real Users]
    I --> J{Score R greater than or equal to 0.7?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Validated Persona for Design]
    J -- No --> L[Refine or Merge Persona Cluster]
    L --> B
```

## 4.2 Relationship Between Persona, Scenario, and User Story

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Persona: WHO is the user] --> S[Scenario: WHEN and WHERE they use the system]
    S --> US[User Story: AS A persona I WANT a feature SO THAT a goal is achieved]
    US --> AC[Acceptance Criteria: GIVEN WHEN THEN]
    P -.informs.-> D[Design Decisions: UI UX and Feature Scope]
    S -.informs.-> D
```

## 4.3 Persona Prioritisation Quadrant (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Q1[High Impact and High Frequency]
        PQ1[Primary Persona P1]
    end
    subgraph Q2[High Impact and Low Frequency]
        SQ1[Secondary Persona S1]
    end
    subgraph Q3[Low Impact and High Frequency]
        SQ2[Secondary Persona S2]
    end
    subgraph Q4[Low Impact and Low Frequency]
        AP[Anti-Persona: Out of Scope]
    end
    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q3 --> Q4
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram in an exam:** Top-left quadrant holds the **Primary Persona** (design for them first). Bottom-right holds the **Anti-Persona** (do not design for them). The arrows indicate the natural prioritisation flow used during sprint planning.

## 4.4 Component Anatomy of a Persona Card

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PC[Persona Card] --> PID[Identifier: Name and Photo]
    PC --> PD[Demographics: Age Gender Location]
    PC --> PG[Goals: End Experience and Practical]
    PC --> PS[Skills and Expertise Level]
    PC --> PA[Attitudes Motivations and Frustrations]
    PC --> PB[Behaviours and Habits]
    PC --> PCX[Context of Use: Device Environment Time]
    PC --> PQ[Representative Quote]
    PC --> PSC[Linked Scenarios]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** write a one-line definition of "persona" in a 14-mark question. Examiners expect *types, components, construction process, AND a worked example* (a mini-persona for a KTU-relevant system).
> - **Do NOT** confuse **persona** with **scenario** or **user story**. The trio is interconnected but distinct. A 7-mark sub-question on "explain scenarios" must *not* be answered by copying the persona definition.
> - **Do NOT** include code or screen designs — this is a *requirements engineering* topic, not implementation.
> - Always mention **Alan Cooper** when defining personas — it signals conceptual depth.
> - Always include **at least one primary and one secondary persona** distinction in 14-mark answers for full marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a persona in the context of user-centred design. Mention any two key components. *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **persona** is a fictional, research-based archetypal character that represents a key user group of a software system, capturing their goals, behaviours, skills, and context. It was introduced by **Alan Cooper (1998)** to humanise the requirements process and drive empathetic design decisions. **Two key components** are:
1. **Demographic profile** — age, education, occupation, and environment.
2. **Goals and motivations** — what the user wants to achieve, divided into end-goals, experience-goals, and practical-goals.
*(Optional: representative quote for the 3rd mark.)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between a *primary persona* and an *anti-persona*. *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Primary Persona | Anti-Persona |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The dominant user group the system is *designed for* | Users the system is *explicitly not* designed for |
| **Purpose** | Drives core feature prioritisation | Helps *scope* the system and reject out-of-scope features |
| **Count** | Usually 1–2 per system | One or more, used to set boundaries |
| **Example (KTU Portal)** | "Ananya" — daily mobile user | "Prof. Thomas" — faculty who uses the LMS, not the student portal |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice

> *Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks and is divided into two 7-mark sub-parts.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Explain the concept of personas in software engineering. List and briefly describe any four components of a persona with an example. *(7 marks)* *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A persona is a *fictional, narrative, research-grounded archetype* of a typical user, used in user-centred design to translate abstract user categories into a *designable, empathetic character*. The concept was popularised by Alan Cooper. Personas are *not* job titles, demographics, or market segments — they are *specific individuals* with names, photos, and stories.

**Four Components (4 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Identifier & Photograph** — e.g., *"Ananya Suresh", a 20-year-old B.Tech S5 student.* Provides a memorable name to anchor team discussions.
2. **Goals** — Divided into:
   - *End goal*: complete B.Tech with high CGPA.
   - *Experience goal*: feel in control of academic progress.
   - *Practical goal*: check attendance within 5 seconds.
3. **Skills & Expertise** — Moderate smartphone literacy, low tolerance for cluttered UIs.
4. **Context of Use** — Hostel at night, 2G/4G connectivity, single-hand operation.

**Closing Sentence (1 mark):** Together, these components allow the development team to *empathise* with the user and resolve design trade-offs by asking *"What would Ananya do?"*

---

#### (b) Describe the step-by-step process of constructing a persona from raw user research data. *(7 marks)* *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]*

**Model Answer:**

The construction of a persona follows a **5-step pipeline**:

1. **Research (1.5 marks):** Conduct user interviews, surveys, field observations, and contextual inquiries. Collect raw observation tuples: behaviour, goal, context, pain.
2. **Affinity Clustering (1.5 marks):** Group observations with similar goals and pain points using an *affinity diagram*. Each cluster $C_j$ represents a candidate persona group.
3. **Behaviour Mapping (1 mark):** Plot clusters on a 2-D behaviour axis (e.g., *Tech Savvy* vs. *Casual*). Identify which cluster qualifies as the *primary*, *secondary*, or *anti-persona*.
4. **Synthesis (1.5 marks):** From each retained cluster, create a *fictional character* with a name, photo, demographics, goals, skills, and a representative quote.
5. **Validation (1.5 marks):** Conduct a *recognition test* with real users. If the recognition score $R_{P_j} \geq 0.7$, the persona is validated; otherwise, refine or merge the cluster and iterate.

**Conclusion:** A validated persona then drives user-story creation, feature prioritisation, and usability evaluation throughout the project lifecycle.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) Discuss the different types of personas (primary, secondary, supplementary, anti-persona) used in user-centred design. Illustrate with KTU-relevant examples. *(7 marks)* *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]*

**Model Answer:**

Personas are classified by the *role* and *frequency* of user interaction with the system:

1. **Primary Persona (2 marks):** The dominant user for whom the system is designed. There should ideally be **only one**. *Example:* For the KTU Student Portal, **Ananya** — a smartphone-first, hostel-dwelling student who checks attendance daily — is the primary persona.

2. **Secondary Persona (2 marks):** Users with limited but real interaction. *Example:* **A parent** who logs in once a semester to pay the exam fee on behalf of the student.

3. **Supplementary Persona (1.5 marks):** Users with special needs that the system must accommodate. *Example:* **A visually-impaired student** requiring screen-reader-friendly attendance tables.

4. **Anti-Persona (1.5 marks):** Users *explicitly excluded* from the design. *Example:* **A KTU examiner** — the portal is for students, not for faculty; the anti-persona forces the team to *reject* faculty-only features.

**Closing remark:** A well-balanced system targets 1–2 primary personas, supports a few secondary/supplementary ones, and clearly excludes anti-personas.

---

#### (b) How are personas and scenarios interrelated in the requirements engineering process? Construct one scenario for the persona described in part (a). *(7 marks)* *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]*

**Model Answer:**

**Interrelation (3.5 marks):**
A **persona** answers *"Who is the user?"* A **scenario** answers *"How, when, and where does the user interact with the system to achieve a goal?"* The two are inseparable in user-centred design:
- The persona provides the *motivation* and *context*.
- The scenario provides the *narrative* and *task sequence*.
- Together, they generate *user stories* and *acceptance criteria* in Agile development.

Formally:

$$
\text{Persona (Who)} + \text{Context (Where/When)} + \text{Goal (Why)} \rightarrow \text{Scenario (How)}
$$

**Constructed Scenario (3.5 marks):**
> *"It is 9:15 PM. Ananya is in her hostel room, lying on her bed, with only a weak 4G signal. She opens the KTU Student Portal app to check her attendance for the day. The app loads a cached dashboard within 2 seconds, displaying her subject-wise attendance percentage. She notices she is short in one subject and immediately taps 'Apply for Condonation.' A pre-filled form appears; she confirms via UPI in 3 taps, and a confirmation push notification arrives within 10 seconds — all without logging in again. Smiling, she puts the phone down and resumes her study."*

This scenario concretises Ananya's *goals* (check attendance, apply condonation) within her *context* (hostel, 4G, smartphone) and exposes the *design constraints* (offline cache, biometric/UPI login, push notifications).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (1-liner):** Persona = fictional, research-based archetypal user character.
- **Origin:** Alan Cooper, *"The Inmates Are Running the Asylum"* (1998).
- **Purpose:** Empathy, shared vocabulary, requirement negotiation, feature prioritisation.
- **Anatomy (10 parts):** Identifier, photo, demographics, goals (3 types), skills, attitudes, behaviours, context, quote, scenarios.
- **Types (4 main):** Primary, Secondary, Supplementary, Anti-Persona.
- **Pipeline (5 steps):** Research → Clustering → Mapping → Synthesis → Validation.
- **Validation metric:** Recognition score $R_{P_j} \geq 0.7$ is considered acceptable.
- **Persona + Scenario = User Story:** "As a [persona], I want [feature] so that [goal]."
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Confusing persona with scenario or user story.
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Treating persona as just a demographic profile.
- **KTU-relevance hook:** Always use *KTU context* (student, faculty, parent, hostel, exam portal) in examples for higher marks.
- **Best practice:** Limit to 1 primary persona, 2–3 secondary, 1 anti-persona.
- **Anti-persona is NOT an enemy** — it is a *scope-defining tool* that prevents feature creep.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scenarios
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Scenarios

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scenario (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):**
> A **scenario** is a structured, narrative-based description of a specific instance of system usage. It documents a single, concrete path through the interaction between one or more **actors** (external entities such as users, hardware devices, or external software) and the **system under development**, executed to achieve a well-defined **goal**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Software Engineering syllabus (Module 2 – Requirements Engineering & Modeling), scenarios are positioned as a **primary elicitation and validation tool**. They are essentially **semi-formal, story-driven artifacts** that capture *how* the system is expected to behave when put to work in a real environment.

A scenario is **not** a complete specification — rather, it is a *descriptive instance* that helps stakeholders, developers, and analysts build a shared mental model of system behavior.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Movie Script" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box: Think of a Scenario as a Movie Script**
>
> Imagine you are directing a short film:
> - The **system** is the *stage* and the *actors* on it.
> - Each **scenario** is a *single scene* — a specific, real-life moment showing what happens, who does what, in what order, and what the outcome is.
> - You do not film the whole story in one go; you capture scene-by-scene.
> - A *use case* is the entire script (covering ALL possible scenes), while a *scenario* is ONE specific scene.

A single ATM transaction — say, "Mrs. Kavitha withdraws ₹5,000 from a SBT ATM at 9:00 PM using her debit card" — is a **scenario**. The general description "Withdraw Cash" is a **use case**, of which this scenario is one concrete instance.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition | Example in Context |
|------|------------|---------------------|
| **Actor** | An external entity that interacts with the system | Customer, Bank Server, ATM |
| **Primary Actor** | The actor who initiates the interaction | Customer initiating withdrawal |
| **Secondary Actor** | An actor the system depends on to complete a task | Bank's central server |
| **Precondition** | A state that must be true before the scenario begins | Customer must hold a valid card |
| **Postcondition** | A state that is guaranteed once the scenario completes | Account is debited; cash is dispensed |
| **Trigger** | The event that initiates the scenario | Customer inserts card and selects "Withdraw" |
| **Exception Path** | An alternative branch when something goes wrong | Insufficient balance, network failure |

## 1.4 Scenarios in the KTU 2024 Software Engineering Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU Emphasizes Scenarios:**
> - Scenarios are used in **requirements elicitation** (Module 2 focus).
> - They form the foundation for **use case diagrams** in UML.
> - They are central to **user-centered design** and **agile user stories**.
> - Scenarios are evaluated in ESE questions involving requirements modeling, requirements validation, and UML use case diagrams.

A scenario is **not the same as a use case**, although the two terms are often confused. The distinction is critical for the KTU exam:

$$
\text{Use Case} = \text{Set of all possible scenarios for one goal}
$$

$$
\text{Scenario} = \text{One specific path / instance of a use case}
$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Characteristics of a Well-Formed Scenario

A scenario, to be academically rigorous, must possess the following six properties (commonly tested in KTU short-answer questions):

1. **Concrete** — It describes *one* specific instance, not general behavior.
2. **Narrative-driven** — Written in a sequence of natural-language steps.
3. **Goal-oriented** — Every scenario aims at a specific user goal.
4. **Actor-centric** — At least one external actor initiates or participates.
5. **State-changing** — The system moves from a pre-state to a post-state.
6. **Traceable** — It can be linked back to a use case and forward to test cases.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:**
> A scenario that is too abstract is called a **"scenario fragment"** or **"abstract scenario"**. KTU examiners frequently ask students to *distinguish* between a scenario and a use case — memorize that a use case abstracts *all* scenarios, while a scenario is a single concrete path.

## 2.2 Types of Scenarios (KTU-Mapped Taxonomy)

The KTU syllabus recognizes three broad families of scenarios:

### (a) **Primary / Happy-Path Scenarios**
These describe the *successful*, *expected*, *no-error* flow of events when an actor interacts with the system.
- **Example:** Customer inserts card → enters correct PIN → selects "Balance Inquiry" → system displays balance.

### (b) **Alternate / Optional Scenarios**
These describe *valid* deviations from the main path — non-error variations that still allow the goal to be achieved.
- **Example:** Customer cancels a transaction mid-way and re-starts with a different account.

### (c) **Exception / Failure Scenarios**
These describe *error conditions* or *system failures* that prevent the goal from being achieved.
- **Example:** Wrong PIN entered three times → card is blocked → goal fails.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:**
> When asked to "write a scenario for an ATM Withdrawal", you MUST include at least one alternate path and one exception path to score full marks. A *single happy-path scenario* is worth only partial credit.

## 2.3 Components of a Scenario Document

Each scenario typically includes the following structured elements (adapted from the KTU 2024 prescribed template):

| Component | Description | Mandatory? |
|-----------|-------------|------------|
| **Scenario ID** | Unique identifier (e.g., SCN-ATM-001) | Yes |
| **Use Case Reference** | The use case this scenario belongs to | Yes |
| **Actor(s)** | Names of primary and secondary actors | Yes |
| **Precondition** | System state before execution | Yes |
| **Trigger** | Event that begins the scenario | Yes |
| **Main Flow (Steps)** | Numbered list of actions | Yes |
| **Alternative Flows** | Branching sub-flows | Optional |
| **Exception Flows** | Error-handling sub-flows | Optional |
| **Postcondition** | System state after success | Yes |
| **Assumptions** | Stated assumptions about environment | Recommended |

## 2.4 Relationship: Scenarios vs. Use Cases vs. User Stories

KTU Module 2 expects students to map these three concepts. The following analytical comparison is **high-yield** for both ESE and internal assessments:

| Aspect | Scenario | Use Case | User Story |
|--------|----------|----------|------------|
| **Abstraction Level** | Low (concrete instance) | Medium (generalization) | High (terse placeholder) |
| **Form** | Narrative paragraph / table | UML diagram + text | "As a [user], I want [feature] so that [benefit]" |
| **Detail** | Step-by-step | All possible flows summarized | One-line intention |
| **Primary Purpose** | Validation, walk-throughs | Functional specification | Agile backlog item |
| **KTU Module Coverage** | Module 2 (Elicitation) | Module 2 & 3 (UML Modeling) | Module 4 (Agile) |
| **Testability** | Directly maps to a test case | Requires decomposition first | Needs scenario expansion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Abstraction (Examiner-Favorite Formulation):**
>
> If $U$ is a use case representing a single user goal, and $S_1, S_2, S_3, \ldots, S_n$ are all possible scenarios that satisfy that goal, then:
>
> $$U = \{S_1, S_2, S_3, \ldots, S_n\}$$
>
> Each $S_i$ is a distinct tuple:
>
> $$S_i = \langle \text{Pre}_i, \text{Trigger}_i, \text{Flow}_i, \text{Post}_i \rangle$$
>
> The use case $U$ is the **union** of all scenario tuples, while each scenario is a **single tuple** in that set.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Scenarios are not academic exercises — they drive tangible engineering value:

- **In Banking Software:** Each ATM transaction scenario is a contract that the system *must* implement and *can be tested against*.
- **In Healthcare Apps:** Scenarios describe clinician workflows for compliance with regulations like HIPAA.
- **In E-commerce:** Scenarios define customer journeys (search → cart → checkout → payment → confirmation).
- **In Aerospace:** Scenarios describe pilot-aircraft interactions and are used for safety-critical certification.

## 2.6 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield One-Page Summary for the Exam Hall:**

| # | Concept | Compact Definition | Exam Memory Hook |
|---|---------|--------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Scenario | Concrete instance of system use | "One scene in the movie" |
| 2 | Use Case | Set of all scenarios for one goal | "The entire movie script" |
| 3 | Primary Actor | Initiates the scenario | "User pushing the button" |
| 4 | Secondary Actor | System-side helper | "Backend server" |
| 5 | Precondition | State before scenario | "Door is locked" |
| 6 | Postcondition | State after scenario | "Door is now open" |
| 7 | Happy Path | Success-only flow | "Sunny day" |
| 8 | Alternate Path | Valid variation | "Branch without error" |
| 9 | Exception Path | Error / failure | "Something broke" |
| 10 | Scenario Set Notation | $U = \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n\}$ | Union of instances |

> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain scenarios in software engineering", structure your answer as: *Definition → Characteristics → Types → One worked example → Difference from use case*. This five-part structure maps directly to how KTU examiners allocate marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Deriving a Scenario from a Use Case

To demonstrate the transformation from a *use case* to a *scenario*, let us work through a complete derivation. This is a frequently tested KTU question type.

### Step 1 — Identify the Use Case

> **Use Case UC-01: Withdraw Cash from ATM**
> The actor interacts with the ATM to withdraw a specified amount of cash from a valid bank account.

### Step 2 — Enumerate the Preconditions

> **Preconditions:**
> 1. The ATM is online and has cash.
> 2. The customer holds a valid, unexpired debit card.
> 3. The customer knows the correct PIN.
> 4. The daily withdrawal limit has not been exceeded.

### Step 3 — Identify the Trigger

> **Trigger:**
> The customer inserts the debit card into the ATM's card reader.

### Step 4 — Write the Main Flow (Happy Path)

> **Main Flow — Scenario S₁ (Successful Withdrawal):**
> 1. Customer inserts debit card.
> 2. ATM reads card and prompts for PIN.
> 3. Customer enters correct 4-digit PIN.
> 4. ATM validates PIN against the bank server.
> 5. ATM displays the main menu.
> 6. Customer selects "Withdraw Cash".
> 7. ATM prompts for amount.
> 8. Customer enters ₹5,000.
> 9. ATM requests balance check from the bank server.
> 10. Bank server confirms sufficient balance.
> 11. ATM dispenses ₹5,000.
> 12. ATM prints receipt.
> 13. ATM ejects the card.
> 14. Customer takes the card and cash.
> 15. Scenario ends successfully.

### Step 5 — Write the Alternative Flow (Cancel mid-transaction)

> **Alternate Flow — Scenario S₂ (Customer Cancels):**
> Steps 1–6 proceed as above.
> 7. Customer presses the "Cancel" button instead of entering an amount.
> 8. ATM displays "Transaction Cancelled".
> 9. ATM ejects the card.
> 10. Scenario ends without dispensing cash.

### Step 6 — Write the Exception Flow (Wrong PIN)

> **Exception Flow — Scenario S₃ (Authentication Failure):**
> Steps 1–2 proceed as above.
> 3. Customer enters an incorrect PIN.
> 4. ATM displays "Invalid PIN — Please Retry".
> 5. Customer retries up to 3 times; all attempts fail.
> 6. ATM retains the card and displays "Card Blocked — Contact Bank".
> 7. Scenario ends with the goal NOT achieved.

### Step 7 — Apply the Set Notation

Now, abstracting to the formal KTU representation:

$$
U_{\text{WithdrawCash}} = \{S_1, S_2, S_3, \ldots, S_k\}
$$

where each scenario is the tuple:

$$
S_i = \langle \text{Pre}_i, \text{Trigger}, \text{Flow}_i, \text{Post}_i \rangle
$$

The cardinality $\vert U \vert = k$ denotes the **total number of distinct scenarios** under the use case. In our example:

$$
\vert U_{\text{WithdrawCash}} \vert = 3
$$

A higher $\vert U \vert$ indicates a *more complex* use case with more branching behavior — a key indicator used by KTU examiners to allocate design effort and test cases.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Scenario Quality Criteria

KTU 2024 evaluates scenarios on six criteria. The following matrix shows the relationship between each criterion and the corresponding scoring weight (illustrative):

| # | Quality Criterion | KTU Marks Weight | Justification |
|---|--------------------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Clarity of actor identification | 1 | Anchors the scenario |
| 2 | Completeness of preconditions | 1 | Defines valid start state |
| 3 | Logical sequence of main flow | 3 | Core of the scenario |
| 4 | Inclusion of alternate flows | 2 | Demonstrates depth |
| 5 | Inclusion of exception flows | 2 | Demonstrates robustness |
| 6 | Defined postconditions | 1 | Closes the scenario |
| **Total** | — | **10** | Full-marks scenario |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Rule for Exam Scripts:**
> A scenario that is missing **any one** of the six criteria above is considered *incomplete* and will be marked down. The six criteria form the **KTU Scenario Quality Checklist (SQC)** that examiners use internally.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: How Scenarios Drive Use Case Diagrams

A common KTU 14-mark question asks: *"How are scenarios used to develop a use case diagram?"* The derivation is as follows:

1. **Gather all scenarios** through stakeholder interviews and observation.
2. **Group scenarios** by the user goal they fulfill — each group becomes a use case.
3. **Identify the actors** consistently appearing across scenarios in a group.
4. **Draw the use case diagram** in UML, with each use case as an oval and each actor as a stick figure.
5. **Annotate each use case** with its set of scenarios for traceability.

The mapping can be represented as:

$$
\text{Scenarios} \xrightarrow{\text{Group by Goal}} \text{Use Cases} \xrightarrow{\text{Add Actors}} \text{Use Case Diagram}
$$

> **Engineering Significance:** This derivation is the bridge between *narrative requirements* (Module 2 — Scenarios) and *graphical UML models* (Module 3 — Modeling). KTU frequently tests this transition.

## 3.4 Traceability Chain — From Scenario to Code

The most powerful aspect of a scenario is that it can be traced forward through the entire development lifecycle. The chain is:

$$
\text{Scenario} \rightarrow \text{Use Case} \rightarrow \text{Class Diagram} \rightarrow \text{Sequence Diagram} \rightarrow \text{Unit Test} \rightarrow \text{Acceptance Test}
$$

This traceability is why scenarios are considered a *contract* in KTU's outcome-based education (OBE) framework. Each scenario explicitly maps to one or more **Course Outcomes (COs)** in the 2024 scheme.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Scenario Lifecycle in Requirements Engineering

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of how scenarios fit into the broader requirements engineering workflow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stakeholder Interviews] --> B[Scenario Identification]
    B --> C[Scenario Documentation]
    C --> D{Scenario Validation}
    D -->|Valid| E[Group Scenarios by Goal]
    D -->|Invalid| F[Re-elicitation]
    F --> A
    E --> G[Derive Use Cases]
    G --> H[Draw Use Case Diagram UML]
    H --> I[Map to Class and Sequence Diagrams]
    I --> J[Generate Test Cases]
    J --> K[Trace to Course Outcomes CO2 and CO3]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each rectangular block is a process step. The diamond `D` is a decision gate. The flow proceeds from raw stakeholder data on the left to a fully-traced, exam-ready engineering artifact on the right.

## 4.2 Actor–System Interaction Flow

The next diagram shows the **interaction sequence** between actors and the system inside a single scenario, using the ATM withdrawal example:

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Customer Actor
    participant A as ATM System
    participant B as Bank Server Actor
    C->>A: Insert Debit Card
    A->>C: Request PIN
    C->>A: Enter PIN 1234
    A->>B: Validate PIN
    B-->>A: PIN Valid
    A->>C: Display Main Menu
    C->>A: Select Withdraw Cash
    A->>C: Prompt for Amount
    C->>A: Enter Amount 5000
    A->>B: Check Balance
    B-->>A: Balance Sufficient
    A-->>C: Dispense Cash 5000
    A-->>C: Print Receipt
    A-->>C: Eject Card
    Note over C,B: Postcondition: Account debited by 5000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows represent synchronous requests; dashed arrows represent responses. The `Note over` block at the end marks the **postcondition** — a KTU-mandated scenario element.

## 4.3 Scenario-to-Use-Case Mapping (Hierarchical View)

The following hierarchical Mermaid diagram shows how multiple scenarios *fold up* into a single use case:

```mermaid
graph TD
    UC01[Use Case UC01 Withdraw Cash]
    SCN01[Scenario S01 Happy Path Success]
    SCN02[Scenario S02 Alternate Path Customer Cancels]
    SCN03[Scenario S03 Exception Path Wrong PIN]
    SCN04[Scenario S04 Exception Path Insufficient Balance]
    SCN05[Scenario S05 Alternate Path Print Receipt Off]
    UC01 --> SCN01
    UC01 --> SCN02
    UC01 --> SCN03
    UC01 --> SCN04
    UC01 --> SCN05
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The root node `UC01` is the use case (the abstract goal). Its child nodes `SCN01` through `SCN05` are concrete scenarios. This visualization is exactly the KTU "Use Case = Set of Scenarios" formula:

$$
\text{UC01} = \{\text{SCN01}, \text{SCN02}, \text{SCN03}, \text{SCN04}, \text{SCN05}\}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Note (KTU Drawing Standard):**
> - All node IDs above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (`A`, `B`, `C`, `UC01`, `SCN01`) — none use reserved keywords.
> - Node labels are plain uppercase text with no markdown formatting tags, ensuring clean rendering in any Mermaid-compatible viewer.

## 4.4 Exception Handling — Decision Tree

The final diagram is a **decision tree** showing how a scenario branches into alternate and exception paths:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Scenario Insert Card] --> Q1{PIN Correct?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Select Withdraw?}
    Q1 -->|No| EX1[Exception S03 PIN Failure]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Sufficient Balance?}
    Q2 -->|No| ALT1[Alternate S02 Customer Cancels]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Daily Limit OK?}
    Q3 -->|No| EX2[Exception S04 Insufficient Balance]
    Q4 -->|Yes| END1[Happy Path S01 Success]
    Q4 -->|No| EX3[Exception S06 Daily Limit Exceeded]
    EX1 --> END2[End with Failure]
    ALT1 --> END3[End without Cash]
    EX2 --> END4[End with Failure]
    EX3 --> END5[End with Failure]
    END1 --> Z[End Successfully]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each diamond is a decision point. Rectangles ending in `End` are scenario terminations. This tree is a *graphical proof* that even a simple use case has many scenario branches, reinforcing the formula $\vert U \vert \geq 1$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short Answers)

> These questions are mapped to **CO2: Requirements Engineering & Modeling** of the KTU 2024 PEEET524 syllabus.

---

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a scenario in software engineering. List any four characteristics of a well-formed scenario.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **scenario** is a narrative, step-by-step description of one specific instance in which one or more actors interact with a system to achieve a particular goal. It captures a single, concrete path through the system rather than the general behavior.

The four characteristics of a well-formed scenario are:
1. **Concrete** — describes one specific instance, not a general rule.
2. **Goal-oriented** — every scenario is tied to a clear user goal.
3. **Actor-centric** — at least one external actor initiates the interaction.
4. **State-changing** — moves the system from a precondition to a postcondition.

*(1 mark for definition; 0.5 mark each for the four characteristics. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between a scenario and a use case. Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Scenario | Use Case |
|--------|----------|----------|
| Nature | Concrete single instance | Generalization of all instances |
| Detail | Step-by-step narrative | Summary of all possible flows |
| Formality | Semi-formal | Formal (often UML) |
| Cardinality | One per goal-path | One per goal |

> **Example:** "Withdraw Cash" is a **use case**, while "Mrs. Kavitha withdraws ₹5,000 from SBT ATM at 9:00 PM using her debit card" is a **scenario** belonging to that use case.

*(1 mark for tabular distinction; 1 mark for example; 1 mark for conclusion. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE rules: each 14-mark question has an internal choice. Both options are provided below with sub-parts and full model solutions.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of a scenario in software engineering. Describe its key components with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between primary, alternate, and exception scenarios. Write a sample exception scenario for an online shopping system. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

A **scenario** in software engineering is a concrete, narrative description of a specific instance of system use. It captures *one* valid way in which an external actor interacts with the system to accomplish a particular goal. Scenarios are used during requirements elicitation and validation to ensure that developers and stakeholders share a common understanding of system behavior.

The key components of a scenario are:
1. **Scenario ID** — unique identifier (e.g., SCN-ATM-001).
2. **Use Case Reference** — the use case it belongs to.
3. **Actor(s)** — primary and secondary actors involved.
4. **Precondition** — system state before the scenario begins.
5. **Trigger** — the event that initiates the scenario.
6. **Main Flow** — the step-by-step sequence of actions.
7. **Postcondition** — system state after the scenario ends.

**Example — Scenario for "Customer Checks Email in Gmail":**
- **Precondition:** The customer is logged into Gmail.
- **Trigger:** The customer clicks the "Inbox" tab.
- **Main Flow:**
  1. System fetches new emails from the mail server.
  2. System displays the inbox list sorted by date.
  3. Customer scrolls through the list.
  4. Customer clicks on a specific email.
  5. System displays the email content.
- **Postcondition:** The customer has read the selected email.

*(1 mark definition; 4 marks for listing components with 0.5 mark each; 2 marks for the example. Total = 7 marks.)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

| Type | Definition | Example Trigger |
|------|------------|-----------------|
| **Primary Scenario** | The main, success-only flow with no errors | Customer completes checkout |
| **Alternate Scenario** | A valid deviation that still achieves the goal | Customer edits cart after checkout |
| **Exception Scenario** | A failure path where the goal is NOT achieved | Payment gateway is down |

**Sample Exception Scenario for Online Shopping:**

> **Scenario SCN-ECOM-EX01: Payment Gateway Failure During Checkout**
> - **Actor:** Customer (primary), Payment Gateway (secondary).
> - **Precondition:** The customer has added items to the cart and clicked "Proceed to Pay".
> - **Trigger:** The system contacts the payment gateway to authorize the transaction.
> - **Exception Flow:**
>   1. The system sends payment details to the gateway.
>   2. The gateway does not respond within 30 seconds (timeout).
>   3. The system displays the message: "Payment could not be processed. Please try again."
>   4. The system offers the option to retry or to use a different payment method.
>   5. The system logs the failed transaction for the support team.
>   6. The cart contents are preserved for the customer.
> - **Postcondition:** The order is NOT placed; the customer is informed of the failure.

*(3 marks for the distinction table; 4 marks for the sample scenario with all components. Total = 7 marks.)*

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is a scenario? Explain with examples how scenarios are derived from a use case. Use the set notation $U = \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n\}$ in your explanation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of scenarios in requirements validation. Explain how scenarios can be used to generate test cases with an example. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

A **scenario** is a concrete, step-by-step narrative of one specific way a user (actor) interacts with a system to achieve a single goal. A use case, by contrast, represents *all* possible ways that goal can be achieved. The relationship is captured by the set notation:

$$
U = \{S_1, S_2, S_3, \ldots, S_n\}
$$

where:
- $U$ is the use case (the abstract goal).
- $S_i$ is the $i$-th scenario (a concrete instance).
- $n = \vert U \vert$ is the total number of scenarios.

**Derivation Example — Use Case: "Place Online Order"**

| Scenario ID | Description | Type |
|-------------|-------------|------|
| S₁ | Customer places order with credit card, payment succeeds | Primary |
| S₂ | Customer places order with UPI, payment succeeds | Alternate |
| S₃ | Customer places order, payment fails, retries successfully | Alternate |
| S₄ | Customer places order, payment fails, abandons cart | Exception |
| S₅ | Customer places order, item is out of stock, system suggests alternatives | Exception |

Formally:

$$
U_{\text{PlaceOrder}} = \{S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4, S_5\}
$$

*(2 marks for definition; 2 marks for set notation; 3 marks for the worked example. Total = 7 marks.)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Role of Scenarios in Requirements Validation:**

1. **Walk-through validation:** Stakeholders read the scenario as a *story* and confirm whether the system behaves as expected. Ambiguities are caught early.
2. **Completeness check:** If the scenario set $U = \{S_1, \ldots, S_n\}$ covers all known flows, the requirements are considered complete.
3. **Consistency check:** Conflicting scenarios reveal contradictions in requirements.
4. **Acceptance criteria:** Each scenario implicitly defines a *user-visible outcome* that the customer can accept or reject.

**Generating Test Cases from Scenarios:**

Each scenario maps **directly** to a **black-box test case** using the trace:

$$
S_i = \langle \text{Pre}_i, \text{Trigger}, \text{Flow}_i, \text{Post}_i \rangle \;\longmapsto\; T_i = \langle \text{Setup}, \text{Action}, \text{Expected Output} \rangle
$$

**Example — Test Case Derived from Scenario S₃ (Customer places order, payment fails, retries successfully):**

| Test Case Element | Value |
|-------------------|-------|
| **Test ID** | TC-ECOM-003 |
| **Source Scenario** | S₃ |
| **Preconditions / Setup** | Customer is logged in; cart has one item; payment gateway is set to fail on first call |
| **Actions** | 1. Click "Checkout". 2. Select UPI. 3. Initiate payment. 4. Wait for gateway failure. 5. Click "Retry Payment". 6. Use a different UPI ID. |
| **Expected Output** | First attempt fails with error message; retry succeeds; order is confirmed. |
| **Pass / Fail Criteria** | Pass if the system preserves cart contents and successfully completes the order on retry. |

*(3 marks for the four roles of scenarios; 4 marks for the test case derivation with example. Total = 7 marks.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Conflating Scenario with Use Case (–2 marks):** Writing "the use case is to withdraw cash" instead of a concrete instance with a named person, time, and amount. *Always* include a specific actor, a specific trigger, and a specific outcome.
>
> 2. **Omitting the Postcondition (–1 mark):** Scenarios without a defined postcondition are considered incomplete. Always close the scenario with a "Postcondition:" line.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Exception Path (–2 marks in 14-mark questions):** A scenario that only describes the happy path scores at most 60% of the marks. KTU expects at least one alternate *and* one exception flow.
>
> 4. **Mixing up preconditions with steps (–1 mark):** Preconditions are the *state* before the scenario starts, not actions performed by the actor. Example mistake: writing "Customer enters PIN" as a precondition (it is a step, not a state).
>
> 5. **Not using structured format (–1 mark):** Presenting the scenario as a single paragraph instead of a numbered list with labeled components loses presentation marks. KTU examiners reward *structured answers*.
>
> 6. **Ignoring traceability (–1 mark in 14-mark questions):** Failing to map the scenario back to its parent use case or forward to test cases. Always include the **Use Case Reference** field.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- [x] A **scenario** is a *concrete, narrative, step-by-step description* of **one** specific way a system is used.
- [x] A **use case** is the *abstraction* of *all* scenarios fulfilling one user goal.
- [x] The set notation: $U = \{S_1, S_2, S_3, \ldots, S_n\}$ — **memorize this formula**.
- [x] Each scenario is a tuple: $S_i = \langle \text{Pre}_i, \text{Trigger}, \text{Flow}_i, \text{Post}_i \rangle$.
- [x] **Three types of scenarios:** Primary (happy path), Alternate (valid variation), Exception (failure).
- [x] **Mandatory components** of a scenario: ID, Use Case Reference, Actor, Precondition, Trigger, Main Flow, Postcondition.
- [x] **Optional but high-scoring components:** Alternate Flow, Exception Flow, Assumptions.
- [x] Scenarios are an **elicitation and validation tool** in Module 2 of the KTU 2024 syllabus.
- [x] Scenarios map **forward** to Use Cases, Class Diagrams, Sequence Diagrams, and Test Cases.
- [x] Each scenario can be directly converted into **one black-box test case** using the mapping $S_i \mapsto T_i$.
- [x] The cardinality $\vert U \vert$ measures the *complexity* of a use case — more scenarios = more testing effort.
- [x] In KTU exam answers, **always include at least one alternate and one exception flow** to score full marks on a 14-mark question.
- [x] **Common examiner traps:** missing postcondition, missing exception flow, no structured format, no traceability to use case.
- [x] **Mnemonic for the six quality criteria:** **"I P T M E P"** — ID, Precondition, Trigger, Main flow, Exception flow, Postcondition.

> **Final Exam-Day Advice:** When the question says *"write a scenario"*, do not write a paragraph. Use a *structured table or numbered list* with all six components above. Examiners reward structure, completeness, and traceability — not word count.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: User Stories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** User Stories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **User Story** is a short, simple, and informal description of a feature written from the perspective of the end-user or customer. In the context of Agile Software Engineering (and as referenced in KTU 2024 syllabus Module 2 covering Agile Methodologies), a User Story is the smallest unit of work in an Agile framework, intended to capture a single, valuable piece of functionality from the user's viewpoint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
