2. **Preliminary (Architectural) Design (1 Mark)** — System is decomposed into modules; control hierarchy, data structures, and algorithms are chosen.
3. **Information Hiding & Functional Independence (1 Mark)** — Modules hide design decisions; cohesion/coupling are optimized.
4. **Detailed Design (1 Mark)** — Internal logic of each module is specified using PDL, flowcharts, or decision tables.
5. **Design Review and Walkthrough (1 Mark)** — Static review to catch errors before coding.

The phases are **iterative** — issues found in review send the process back to refinement. **(1 Mark)**

**Diagram (1 Mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SRS] --> B[Requirements Refinement] --> C[Architectural Design]
    C --> D[Information Hiding]
    C --> E[Interface Design]
    C --> F[Data Class Design]
    D --> G[Detailed Design]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Design Review] --> I[SDD]
```

#### ✅ Model Answer (b)

Coupling refers to the **degree of interdependence between modules**. Lower coupling is desirable. **(1 Mark)**

| Rank | Type | Description | Example |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 (Best) | **Data Coupling** | Only simple parameters passed | `add(a, b)` |
| 2 | **Stamp Coupling** | Full data structure passed; only part used | Passing entire `student` object when only `id` is needed |
| 3 | **Control Coupling** | Flag/switch controls internal logic of callee | `printReport(reportType, isUrgent)` |
| 4 | **External Coupling** | Modules share an external protocol/format | Modules sharing an XML schema |
| 5 | **Common Coupling** | Modules share global data | Global variable `MAX_USERS` |
| 6 (Worst) | **Content Coupling** | One module directly accesses another's internals | Module A modifies Module B's local variable |

*(1 Mark for each correctly described type — sample 5 types fully + 2 mentioned briefly.)*

**Best Practice:** Always aim for **Data Coupling** or at most **Stamp Coupling**. **(1 Mark)**

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **seven types of cohesion** with examples. Why is functional cohesion considered the best? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **Modularity**? Derive the relationship between **module development cost** and **module integration cost**. Show that an optimal number of modules exists. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Answer (a)

Cohesion measures the **internal strength** of a module — how closely the responsibilities within it are related. **(1 Mark)**

The seven types in ascending order:

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Coincidental** | Elements grouped arbitrarily, no logical relationship | A `Utility` class with random math + I/O + sorting methods |
| **Logical** | Similar logical functions, one chosen at runtime | An `IO` module handling both read AND write (chosen by flag) |
| **Temporal** | Elements executed at the same time (initialisation) | A `startup()` module that opens DB + loads config + starts logger |
| **Procedural** | Elements follow a defined order | An `editDocument()` that → open → modify → save (each part a separate function but in same module) |
| **Communicational** | Elements operate on the same input/output data | A `processCustomerOrder(customer, cart)` uses both `customer` and `cart` |
| **Sequential** | Output of one part is input to the next | A `validateInput() → cleanData() → storeInDB()` chain |
| **Functional** | All elements contribute to ONE well-defined task | A `computeSquareRoot(x)` module |

*(5 Marks for description with examples)*

**Why Functional is best (1 Mark):**
- Highest reusability — does one thing only.
- Easiest to test, maintain, and understand.
- Minimizes the ripple effect of changes.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Good design always pushes towards the right side of this hierarchy.

#### ✅ Model Answer (b)

**Modularity (1 Mark):**
> Modularity is the property of a system decomposed into a set of cohesive and loosely-coupled modules. It allows independent development, testing, and maintenance.

**Cost Relationship (3 Marks derivation):**

Total cost of a software system with $n$ modules:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total}(n) = C_{d}(n) + C_{i}(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $C_{d}(n)$ = total module development cost (decreases per module as $n$ grows).
- $C_{i}(n)$ = total module integration cost (increases as $n$ grows).

For $C_{d}(n)$: As $n$ rises, each module is simpler — cost per module decreases.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{d}(n) \approx k \cdot n^{\alpha}, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1
\end{aligned}
$$

For $C_{i}(n)$: Number of inter-module interfaces is $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{i}(n) \approx \beta \cdot \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Cost (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total}(n) = k \cdot n^{\alpha} + \beta \cdot \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Optimal n (1 Mark):** Differentiating w.r.t. $n$ and setting $\dfrac{dC_{total}}{dn} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k \cdot \alpha \cdot n^{\alpha - 1} + \beta \cdot \frac{2n - 1}{2} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields a unique positive root $n^*$, representing the **optimal number of modules**. For $n < n^*$, modularity reduces cost. For $n > n^*$, integration cost dominates. **(1 Mark)**

**Practical takeaway:** Always seek a balance — not too few (monolithic) and not too many (over-engineered).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Cohesion ↔ Coupling order** — Many write them in arbitrary order. *Always state "from worst to best" or "from best to worst" explicitly.*
> 2. **Forgetting examples** — A definition without an example earns only partial marks. KTU board examiners always look for the *applied* understanding.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram** — In Q.(a) of Part B, a flow/block diagram is **mandatory** and carries 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Writing design ≠ coding** — Design is *what* and *how*, NOT the actual code. Do not write Java/C snippets in Part B unless the question explicitly asks.
> 5. **Mixing analysis vs. design** — DFD is *analysis*; module structure is *design*. Students often confuse the two.
> 6. **Omitting the iterative nature** — The design process is NOT a strict waterfall; mention *refinement and feedback loops*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Software Design = Transformation of Analysis Model → Design Model.**
- ✅ Four pillars: **Data Design, Architectural Design, Interface Design, Component Design.**
- ✅ **Generic Design Process** (Pressman): Requirements Refinement → Preliminary Design → Information Hiding → Detailed Design → Design Review.
- ✅ **Seven Fundamental Concepts:** Abstraction, Architecture, Patterns, Modularity, Information Hiding, Functional Independence, Refinement/Refactoring.
- ✅ **Cohesion Order (Worst → Best):** Coincidental < Logical < Temporal < Procedural < Communicational < Sequential < **Functional**.
- ✅ **Coupling Order (Best → Worst):** Data < Stamp < Control < External < Common < **Content**.
- ✅ **Goal:** High Cohesion + Low Coupling.
- ✅ **Modularity Trade-off:** $\dfrac{dC_{total}}{dn} = 0$ gives optimal $n$.
- ✅ **Design Quality Attributes:** Functionality, Reliability, Usability, Maintainability, Portability, Efficiency.
- ✅ **Tools:** UML (class, sequence, component, deployment diagrams), PDL, Decision Tables.
- ✅ **Design Patterns:** Reusable proven solutions — MVC, Singleton, Observer, Factory.
- ✅ **SDD** (Software Design Description) is the final deliverable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Software Design?

**Software Design** is the technical kernel of software engineering. It is the activity within software engineering that transforms *what* the system must do (requirements) into *how* it will be realized (architecture, components, interfaces, and data structures). According to KTU's outcome-based framework, design sits at the heart of the development phase and establishes a blueprint for coding, testing, and maintenance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):**
> *Software Design is a problem-solving and planning activity that transforms user requirements into a software system representation — a model that describes the data structure, software architecture, interface representation, and procedural (algorithmic) detail at successively refined levels of abstraction.*

### Conceptual Analogy — The Blueprint Analogy

Imagine an architect commissioned to design a new KTU campus building.

- The **requirements** (number of classrooms, labs, capacity, fire exits, aesthetic) are gathered first — these are the *what*.
- The architect then creates **floor plans, elevation drawings, structural diagrams, electrical schematics** — these are the *design*.
- Only after the design is approved do the **masons, electricians, and plumbers** start construction — this is the *coding phase*.

In the same way, software design transforms "what the customer wants" into "how the developers will build it." Without a design, construction becomes chaotic, error-prone, and nearly impossible to verify or change later.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Design is taught as the bridge between *Analysis* (Module 1) and *Implementation/Coding*. Every design decision should be traceable back to a requirement, and every requirement should be represented in the design.

---

## 1.2 The Design Process — A Phased View

The design process is **iterative and evolutionary**, not a single linear pass. KTU models it across two levels of abstraction:

1. **Preliminary (High-Level) Design** — Identifies the software as a system with major components and defines their interrelationships.
2. **Detailed (Low-Level) Design** — Describes the internal structure, behavior, and procedural logic of each component.

### Categories of Design Activities

| Design Layer | Purpose | Key Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Architectural Design** | Identifies subsystems and their interactions | Software architecture document |
| **Data Design** | Translates analysis-class model into data structures and persistent storage | Class-Responsibility-Collaborator (CRC) cards, ER mapping |
| **Interface Design** | Describes how software communicates internally and externally | Interface specifications, UI prototypes |
| **Procedural (Component) Design** | Defines algorithmic detail of each module | Pseudocode, structured charts, flow logic |

---

## 1.3 Design Quality — Why It Matters

A good design is judged on **quality**, not just correctness. KTU emphasizes the following attributes for an excellent design:

- **Correctness** — The design must satisfy all requirements.
- **Understandability** — Designers and coders should read it effortlessly.
- **Efficiency** — Runtime and memory costs must be acceptable.
- **Maintainability** — Easy to evolve as requirements change.
- **Reliability** — Robust under unanticipated conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Henry Petroski's Principle (Quoted in KTU Reference):**
> *Design is a controlled attempt to express the irrational human wish for the rational.* A design must therefore balance conflicting goals — simplicity vs. feature richness, performance vs. portability.

---

## 1.4 Key Design Principles (Top-Level View)

| # | Principle | One-Line Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Abstraction** | Hiding complexity by exposing only essentials |
| 2 | **Refinement** | Top-down stepwise elaboration |
| 3 | **Modularity** | Divide software into well-named, well-bounded modules |
| 4 | **Software Architecture** | Overall structural framework |
| 5 | **Control Hierarchy (Program Structure)** | Tree/owner-of relationships among modules |
| 6 | **Structural Partitioning** | Separate branches of the hierarchy (horizontal vs vertical) |
| 7 | **Data Structure** | Logical/physical organization of information |
| 8 | **Software Procedure** | Precise specification of event-driven processing |
| 9 | **Information Hiding** | Each module hides critical decisions from others |

These will be expanded in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 Abstraction

**Definition:** Abstraction allows the designer to focus on a problem at some level of generalization without worrying about irrelevant low-level details.

In procedural abstraction, a sequence of instructions is given a name; in data abstraction, a data structure is named along with the operations that apply to it.

### Types of Abstraction (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Level | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Abstraction** | Named collection of data that describes a data object | `Student { name, roll, marks }` |
| **Procedural Abstraction** | Named sequence of instructions with a specific function | `sortArray(arr)` |
| **Control Abstraction** | Implies a program control mechanism without specifying internal details | `while`, `for`, scheduler |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 module specifies that abstraction and refinement are complementary concepts — abstraction proceeds *from the inside outward*, refinement proceeds *from the outside inward*.

---

## 2.2 Refinement

**Refinement** is the process of elaboration. The designer opens an abstraction to reveal progressively more detail as the design proceeds. KTU emphasizes **stepwise refinement** — each refinement adds more algorithmic detail.

```
Initial abstraction    ->    "Process a customer order"
First refinement       ->    "Validate order, compute total, bill customer"
Second refinement      ->    "Read order_id, check inventory, apply discount rule,
                                compute tax, generate invoice, debit wallet, email receipt"
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heuristic for Refinement:** At every step, the refinement must remain at one level of abstraction — do not mix high-level and low-level statements in the same description.

---

## 2.3 Modularity

**Definition:** Modularity is the property of a software system that has been decomposed into a set of cohesive and loosely coupled modules.

### Why Modularity?

The classical argument rests on **Cost vs. Module Count** (illustrative for KTU):

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{module development}} \propto f\left(\frac{1}{M}\right)
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{integration}} \propto g(M)
$$

$$
\text{Total Cost}(M) = f\left(\frac{1}{M}\right) + g(M)
$$

Where $M$ = number of modules. The total cost has a minimum at an **optimal** $M^*$ — beyond which integration cost dominates; before which per-module complexity dominates.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "Five Attributes of a Good Module"**
> 1. Single, well-defined purpose
> 2. Identifiable interface
> 3. Self-contained behavior
> 4. Predictable behavior
> 5. Information-hiding implementation

---

## 2.4 Software Architecture

**Definition:** The structure of a software system — its components, the externally visible properties of those components, and the relationships among them.

KTU highlights two architectural views:

- **Pattern-based view** — Compositions of recurring, well-named structures (e.g., MVC, Pipe-and-Filter, Layered).
- **Module-based view** — Grouping modules into meaningful subsystems (e.g., UI, Business, Data).

---

## 2.5 Control Hierarchy (Program Structure)

A tree-like representation in which the **root is the main module**, **leaves do the actual work**, and **intermediate nodes distribute control**.

Two related concepts:

- **Depth** — The longest path from the root to a leaf.
- **Width** — The maximum number of nodes at any level.
- **Fan-out** — Number of immediate subordinates of a module.
- **Fan-in** — Number of immediate superiors of a module.

### Heuristic Limits (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Metric | Recommended Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Fan-out (per module)** | $\leq 7 \pm 2$ (Miller's Law) |
| **Depth** | Moderate, avoid $> 10$ levels |
| **Width** | Should grow gradually, not exponentially |

---

## 2.6 Structural Partitioning

The control hierarchy is partitioned into:
- **Horizontal Partitioning** — Each branch of the hierarchy performs a different portion of the overall function. (More fault-tolerant, easier to test independently.)
- **Vertical Partitioning** — Top performs input/control, middle performs processing, bottom performs output. (Encourages reuse but tightly couples layers.)

---

## 2.7 Data Structure

The representation of the logical relationship among individual data elements. Choice of data structure is **as important as procedural design** — wrong data structure leads to inefficient algorithms.

---

## 2.8 Software Procedure

The precise specification of processing events that drive the software — the mechanics of each module. KTU terms this **event-driven processing**, emphasizing that the procedure must specify:

- The order of module invocation.
- The conditions under which each is invoked.
- The data exchanged.
- The iteration structure.

---

## 2.9 Information Hiding (Parnas, 1972)

**Definition:** Each module in a system should hide *design decisions* (data structures, algorithms, representation choices) from the rest of the system, exposing only an interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Information Hiding?**
> - Limits the global effects of local design changes.
> - Reduces propagation of errors.
> - Encourages independent development and testing.

---

## 2.10 Functional Independence — The Heart of Design

**Functional independence** is the single most important quality indicator in module-level design. It is achieved through two complementary attributes:

### (a) Cohesion — The Strength Within a Module

Cohesion measures **how strongly the elements inside a module belong together**. KTU orders seven types from **worst to best**:

| # | Type | Description | Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Coincidental** | Arbitrary grouping; elements unrelated | Worst |
| 2 | **Logical** | Similar activities, selected at run-time | Low |
| 3 | **Temporal** | Activated at the same time | Low |
| 4 | **Procedural** | Follow a prescribed sequence | Medium |
| 5 | **Communicational** | Operate on same data | Medium |
| 6 | **Sequential** | Output of one is input to the next | High |
| 7 | **Functional** | All elements contribute to a single, well-defined function | **Best** |

### (b) Coupling — The Strength Between Modules

Coupling measures **the degree of interdependence between modules**. KTU orders types from **best to worst**:

| # | Type | Description | Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Data** | Pass simple parameters | Best |
| 2 | **Stamp** | Pass entire data structure but use only part | Low |
| 3 | **Control** | Pass control flag influencing logic | Medium |
| 4 | **External** | Share external data format/protocol | High |
| 5 | **Common (Global)** | Share global variables | Very High |
| 6 | **Content** | One module directly modifies another's internals | Worst |

### KTU Quality Formula (Conceptual)

$$
\text{Design Quality} \propto \frac{\text{High Cohesion}}{\text{Low Coupling}}
$$

The aim: **maximize cohesion within modules, minimize coupling between modules.**

---

## 2.11 Design Heuristics (KTU — High-Weight Topic)

Heuristic = experience-based rule, not a theorem. KTU's prescribed heuristics:

1. Evaluate the **first-cut design** for quality.
2. Reduce **fan-out** if depth is high.
3. Keep **scope of effect and span of control** consistent for each module.
   - *Scope of effect* = all modules affected by a decision.
   - *Span of control* = subordinate modules of a manager.
4. Don't overload a single module.
5. **Fan-in** should be encouraged (good for reuse).
6. Avoid "modules that look alike" — they likely are coincidentally cohesive.
7. Provide uniform access (all modules accessed uniformly when possible).
8. Size modules within **heuristic limits** (suggested 50–100 source instructions).

> [!TIP]
> **Scope of Effect vs Span of Control Rule:**
> *The span of control of a module should be a subset of its scope of effect.* That is, a manager module should directly control the modules that its decision impacts.

---

## 2.12 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference

| Symbol / Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $M$ | Number of modules |
| $D$ | Depth of program structure |
| $W$ | Width of program structure |
| $FO_i$ | Fan-out of module $i$ |
| $FI_i$ | Fan-in of module $i$ |
| $S_i$ | Span of control of module $i$ |
| $E_i$ | Scope of effect of module $i$ |
| $C(M)$ | Total cost as a function of $M$ |

| Constraint (Heuristic) | Recommended Form |
|---|---|
| Miller's fan-out | $FO_i \leq 7 \pm 2$ |
| Cohesion optimum | Functional |
| Coupling optimum | Data |
| Modularity rule | $E_i \supseteq S_i$ |

> **Note on LaTeX safety:** No vertical-pipe `|` symbol is used inside table cells. All "OR" / "subset" relations are written using $\supseteq$, $\subseteq$, or plain English.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations, Refinement Walkthroughs & Design Matrices

> **Why this section?**
> KTU Part-B questions regularly test whether you can *demonstrate* design concepts by hand. This section walks through **classic worked examples** (refinement, modular decomposition, coupling/cohesion classification, scope-of-effect resolution). Every step is written out — no skipped logic.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Stepwise Refinement of a "Library Book Issue" Function

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> *"Design a refinement ladder for a module `issueBook(studentID, bookID)` that issues a book from a library, validating the student's eligibility and the book's availability."*

### Step 1 — Top-Level Abstraction

$$
\text{issueBook(studentID, bookID)} \; : \; \text{Issue a book to a student}
$$

### Step 2 — First-Level Refinement (One level of abstraction only)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{issueBook(studentID, bookID)} \Rightarrow & \\
& \text{Step 1: validateStudent(studentID)} \\
& \text{Step 2: validateBook(bookID)} \\
& \text{Step 3: createLoanRecord(studentID, bookID)} \\
& \text{Step 4: updateInventory(bookID)} \\
& \text{Step 5: notifyIssue(studentID, bookID)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Each step is named at a single abstraction level — no implementation detail (e.g., "open DB connection") yet.

### Step 3 — Second-Level Refinement (drill down only `validateStudent`)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{validateStudent(studentID)} \Rightarrow & \\
& \text{a: readStudentRecord(studentID)} \\
& \text{b: if exists, checkOutstandingFines()} \\
& \text{c: if exists, checkActiveLoanLimit()} \\
& \text{d: return eligibilityFlag}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Third-Level Refinement (drill `checkOutstandingFines`)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{checkOutstandingFines()} \Rightarrow & \\
& \text{i: read fineTable[studentID]} \\
& \text{ii: if sum(fines) } > \text{ threshold, return FALSE} \\
& \text{iii: else return TRUE}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Stopping:** Refinement stops when the algorithm can be expressed using the chosen programming language primitives. At that level, the design is *executable*.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Designing a Module Structure (Control Hierarchy)

**Problem Statement:**
> *Design a control hierarchy for a student registration system that handles: (a) student registration, (b) course catalog management, (c) examination enrollment, (d) grade processing, and (e) transcript generation.*

### Step 1 — Identify Main Module

Root: `StudentRegistrationSystem`

### Step 2 — Identify Direct Subordinates (First-Level Decomposition)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{StudentRegistrationSystem} \Rightarrow & \\
& \text{RegistrationModule} \\
& \text{CourseCatalogModule} \\
& \text{ExaminationModule} \\
& \text{GradeProcessingModule} \\
& \text{TranscriptModule}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Fan-Out Check

Fan-out of root = **5**. Acceptable (within Miller's $7 \pm 2$).

### Step 4 — Depth and Width Decision

| Sub-Module | Direct Subordinates | Fan-out | Depth (sub-tree) |
|---|---|---|---|
| RegistrationModule | addStudent, editStudent, deleteStudent, searchStudent | 4 | 2 |
| CourseCatalogModule | addCourse, editCourse, viewCourses | 3 | 2 |
| ExaminationModule | enrollExam, deregisterExam, listExams | 3 | 2 |
| GradeProcessingModule | enterMarks, calculateGPA, publishGrade | 3 | 2 |
| TranscriptModule | generateTranscript, printTranscript, emailTranscript | 3 | 2 |

### Step 5 — Conclusion

The hierarchy has **Depth = 2** and **Width = 5**, with all fan-outs $\leq 5$. This satisfies the KTU heuristic for a *well-balanced* program structure.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Classifying Cohesion (Part-B Frequent Question)

**KTU-style Question:**
> *"For each of the following module descriptions, identify the type of cohesion."*

### Case (a)
*"`calculatePay()` computes the monthly salary, tax deduction, and prints a payslip."*

**Step 1 — Examine internal elements.**
- Salary calculation
- Tax deduction
- Print payslip

**Step 2 — Identify the cohesion pattern.**
Tax deduction uses salary result → **Sequential cohesion** is technically applicable; but multiple unrelated logical activities (computing and printing) appear. However, the dominant relationship is: *one element's output is the next's input* (salary $\to$ tax). 

$$
\boxed{\text{Sequential Cohesion}}
$$

### Case (b)
*"`errorHandler()` prints to screen, writes to log file, and sends an email, all when an error occurs."*

**Step 1 — Activation context.**
All three sub-functions run **at the same time** in response to a single event.

$$
\boxed{\text{Temporal Cohesion}}
$$

### Case (c)
*"`inputValidator()` validates student name OR age OR email OR phone based on a flag."*

**Step 1 — Element selection.**
Multiple unrelated activities selected at run-time via flag.

$$
\boxed{\text{Logical Cohesion}}
$$

### Case (d)
*"`sin()` computes $\sin(x)$."*

**Step 1 — Single, well-defined purpose.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Functional Cohesion (BEST)}}
$$

### Case (e)
*"`miscUtilities()` formats dates, sends email, computes discount, and validates input."*

**Step 1 — Elements unrelated.**
Arbitrary grouping.

$$
\boxed{\text{Coincidental Cohesion (WORST)}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Classifying Coupling

**KTU-style Question:**
> *"Module A calls Module B with parameters `(int studentCount, char[] fullStudentRecord, int flag)`. The flag controls B's processing flow. Inside B, B reads/writes the global array `GlobalLog[]`. Determine the worst form of coupling present."*

### Step 1 — Analyze the Parameter Pass

- `int studentCount` — primitive → Data coupling.
- `char[] fullStudentRecord` — passes entire record, but A may use only `studentCount` → Stamp coupling.
- `int flag` — flag controls B's logic → **Control coupling**.

### Step 2 — Analyze Global Access

- `GlobalLog[]` shared globally → **Common coupling**.

### Step 3 — Combine (Take Worst)

Coupling types present: Data, Stamp, Control, Common.
The **worst present** coupling type is:

$$
\boxed{\text{Common (Global) Coupling}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:**
> Students often answer "Control coupling" because of the flag — but the presence of a higher-severity coupling (Common) raises the rating. Always check **all** interaction channels before finalizing your answer.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Scope of Effect vs Span of Control

**Scenario:**
> *Module M1 controls modules M2 and M3. A decision variable `d` in M1 affects M2 and also affects M5, which is NOT a direct subordinate of M1.*

### Step 1 — Identify Span of Control

$$
S_{M1} = \{M2, M3\}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify Scope of Effect

$$
E_{M1} = \{M2, M5\}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare

$$
M5 \in E_{M1} \quad \text{but} \quad M5 \notin S_{M1}
$$

Hence $S_{M1} \not\supseteq E_{M1}$ — the design **violates** the heuristic.

### Step 4 — Fix

Restructure so that M1 controls M5 *indirectly* through M3, or relocate the decision variable `d` into a module whose scope covers both M2 and M5.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 6 — Modularity Cost Curve (Quantitative Intuition)

KTU often presents the modularity-cost relationship conceptually. The classic statement:

$$
\text{Total Cost}(M) = \underbrace{C_m\left(\frac{1}{M}\right)}_{\text{module dev cost}} + \underbrace{C_i(M)}_{\text{integration cost}}
$$

Where $M$ is the number of modules. Empirical heuristic shows:
- As $M \uparrow$, $C_m$ decreases (each module is simpler).
- As $M \uparrow$, $C_i$ increases (more interfaces to wire).

The **optimal number of modules** $M^*$ satisfies:

$$
\frac{d\,\text{Total Cost}(M)}{dM} = 0
$$

The curve is U-shaped, with minimum near $M^* \approx 7 \pm 2$ for typical software (small/medium projects).

> **Note:** No numerical derivation is normally required by KTU — only the qualitative shape and meaning are tested. The formula above is presented for completeness of the conceptual model.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> **Diagram Compilation Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `n1`, `n2`, `abstr`, `refine`).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) used as node names.
> - All node labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown bold/italics inside labels.

---

## 4.1 Diagram — The Two-Level Software Design Process

This diagram maps the **Preliminary Design → Detailed Design** pipeline used in the KTU 2024 Software Engineering module.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1[REQUIREMENTS ANALYSIS OUTPUT] --> n2[PRELIMINARY DESIGN]
    n2 --> n3[ARCHITECTURAL DESIGN]
    n2 --> n4[DATA DESIGN]
    n2 --> n5[INTERFACE DESIGN]
    n3 --> n6[DETAILED DESIGN]
    n4 --> n6
    n5 --> n6
    n6 --> n7[COMPONENT LEVEL DESIGN]
    n7 --> n8[PROCEDURAL DESCRIPTION]
    n8 --> n9[FINAL DESIGN MODEL DOCUMENT]
    n9 --> n10[CODING AND UNIT TESTING]
```

**Reading Guide:**
- `PRELIMINARY DESIGN` is the boundary between analysis and design — produces the *blueprint*.
- `DETAILED DESIGN` is component-by-component elaboration.
- Output flows into coding/test phases, completing the engineering pipeline.

---

## 4.2 Diagram — Control Hierarchy of a Sample Student System

This is a tree-like structure showing **fan-out, depth, and width** for a representative academic information system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[ROOT STUDENT INFORMATION SYSTEM]
    rootA --> mod1A[REGISTRATION MODULE]
    rootA --> mod2A[COURSE CATALOG MODULE]
    rootA --> mod3A[EXAM MODULE]
    rootA --> mod4A[GRADE MODULE]
    rootA --> mod5A[TRANSCRIPT MODULE]
    mod1A --> leaf1A[ADD STUDENT]
    mod1A --> leaf2A[EDIT STUDENT]
    mod1A --> leaf3A[SEARCH STUDENT]
    mod2A --> leaf4A[ADD COURSE]
    mod2A --> leaf5A[VIEW COURSE]
    mod3A --> leaf6A[ENROLL EXAM]
    mod3A --> leaf7A[LIST EXAMS]
    mod4A --> leaf8A[ENTER MARKS]
    mod4A --> leaf9A[CALC GPA]
    mod5A --> leaf10A[GENERATE TRANSCRIPT]
    leaf10A --> leaf11A[EMAIL TRANSCRIPT]
    leaf10A --> leaf12A[PRINT TRANSCRIPT]
```

**Reading Guide:**
- Root fan-out = 5 (acceptable).
- Maximum depth = 3.
- Leaf module `GENERATE TRANSCRIPT` has fan-out = 2.
- `EMAIL TRANSCRIPT` and `PRINT TRANSCRIPT` both have **fan-in = 1**.

---

## 4.3 Diagram — Information Hiding Across Modules

This block-level functional architecture illustrates how internal decisions are encapsulated.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    caller[CALLING MODULE] --> iface[PUBLIC INTERFACE]
    iface --> hidden[HIDDEN INTERNAL MODULE]
    hidden --> secret1[DATA STRUCTURE DECISION]
    hidden --> secret2[ALGORITHM CHOICE]
    hidden --> secret3[REPRESENTATION CHOICE]
    caller -. cannot see .-> secret1
    caller -. cannot see .-> secret2
    caller -. cannot see .-> secret3
```

**Reading Guide:**
- Solid arrows = legitimate, allowed interaction.
- Dotted arrows with `cannot see` label = forbidden channels (enforced by encapsulation).

---

## 4.4 Diagram — Cohesion Spectrum (Worst → Best)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    c1[COINCIDENTAL] --> c2[LOGICAL] --> c3[TEMPORAL] --> c4[PROCEDURAL] --> c5[COMMUNICATIONAL] --> c6[SEQUENTIAL] --> c7[FUNCTIONAL]
```

**Reading Guide:** Each step upward represents a tighter internal relationship within a module. The KTU goal is always to push designs rightward.

---

## 4.5 Diagram — Coupling Spectrum (Best → Worst)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    k1[DATA] --> k2[STAMP] --> k3[CONTROL] --> k4[EXTERNAL] --> k5[COMMON] --> k6[CONTENT]
```

**Reading Guide:** Each step rightward represents a tighter inter-module binding. The KTU goal is always to keep designs leftward.

---

## 4.6 Diagram — The Design Concept Inter-Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    designC[DESIGN CONCEPTS] --> p1[ABSTRACTION]
    designC --> p2[REFINEMENT]
    designC --> p3[MODULARITY]
    designC --> p4[ARCHITECTURE]
    designC --> p5[INFORMATION HIDING]
    p3 --> fi[FUNCTIONAL INDEPENDENCE]
    p5 --> fi
    fi --> coH[COHESION WITHIN MODULE]
    fi --> coK[COUPLING BETWEEN MODULES]
    coH --> qual[HIGH QUALITY DESIGN]
    coK --> qual
```

**Reading Guide:** Functional Independence emerges as the intersection of Modularity and Information Hiding, and is operationalized through Cohesion + Coupling measurements. Together they yield a high-quality design.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> **All questions below are modeled on the actual KTU 2024 Scheme assessment pattern: 3-mark Part A items (definitions, identification) and 14-mark Part B items (internal-choice sub-questions of 7 marks each). Model answers follow KTU's expected valuation key style.**

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Define software design. State any two design principles.

**Model Answer:**
Software design is the engineering activity that transforms user requirements into a structured representation — describing the data, architecture, interfaces, and procedural detail of the software — at successively refined levels of abstraction.

**Two design principles:**
1. **Abstraction** — Exposing essential features while hiding unnecessary details.
2. **Modularity** — Decomposing the software into well-named, well-bounded, and functionally independent modules.

*(Examiner awards 1 mark for definition + 1 mark per principle = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Differentiate between cohesion and coupling. Which is preferred and why?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Cohesion | Coupling |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Within a module | Between modules |
| **Goal** | High (Functional) | Low (Data) |
| **Effect** | Measures internal strength | Measures inter-dependency |
| **Ideal** | Functional cohesion | Data coupling |

**Preferred:** High cohesion *and* low coupling. A functionally cohesive module whose only contact with the rest of the system is through primitive data parameters represents the cleanest design.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition each, 1 for the ideal-pair justification.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice — KTU ESE Pattern)

> **KTU Pattern:** Every Part-B question offers an internal choice between Q(A) and Q(B). Each choice has two sub-parts, typically of 7 marks each.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 MARKS

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of *abstraction* and *refinement* in software design. Illustrate with an example. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Explain the seven types of cohesion in increasing order of strength with one-line examples each. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer — (a) Abstraction & Refinement

**Step 1 — Define Abstraction.**
Abstraction is the process of defining a software entity (procedure or data) by its essential characteristics without including background details. It lets the designer focus on the problem at a chosen level of generalization.

**Step 2 — Define Refinement.**
Refinement is the top-down elaboration of an abstraction. Each step opens an abstraction to expose more detail while staying at one level of abstraction.

**Step 3 — Worked Example (Open a Door problem).**

*Level 1 (Most abstract):* `openDoor()`

*Level 2 (Refined once):*
- `reachHandle`
- `graspHandle`
- `twistHandle`
- `pushDoor`

*Level 3 (Refined further — `graspHandle` only):*
- `moveHandTowardHandle`
- `openFingers`
- `closeFingersAroundHandle`
- `applyPressure`

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**
Abstraction lets us reason at one level; refinement lets us descend systematically until the design is implementable in code.

*(7 marks: 2 for definitions, 3 for the example, 2 for concluding design rationale.)*

#### Model Answer — (b) Seven Types of Cohesion

| # | Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Coincidental** | Arbitrary grouping of unrelated functions | `utilityFunctions()` that does printing, math, and logging |
| 2 | **Logical** | Similar activities selected at run-time | `processInput(type)` selecting int/float/string |
| 3 | **Temporal** | Activated at the same time | `systemStartup()` that initializes all subsystems |
| 4 | **Procedural** | Must follow a prescribed order | `editDocument()` that reads, formats, saves in sequence |
| 5 | **Communicational** | Operate on the same input data | `generateReport(record)` printing + emailing |
| 6 | **Sequential** | Output of one is input to next | `readSalary → computeTax` |
| 7 | **Functional** | All elements contribute to a single function | `sin(x)` |

**Conclusion:** Designer's goal is to push designs toward **functional cohesion**.

*(7 marks: 1 per row with brief explanation = 7 marks.)*

---

### QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
> **(a)** Explain the six types of module coupling from best to worst with examples. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** What is functional independence? Apply the scope-of-effect vs span-of-control heuristic to a 4-module scenario and recommend a fix if violated. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer — (a) Six Types of Coupling

| # | Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Data** | Pass primitive parameters | `add(a, b)` |
| 2 | **Stamp** | Pass entire record, use only part | `process(entireStudent)` using only name |
| 3 | **Control** | Pass flag to influence internal flow | `process(flag=true)` switching logic |
| 4 | **External** | Both share an external data format/protocol | Both read same file format |
| 5 | **Common (Global)** | Share global variables | Reading/writing `globalCounter` |
| 6 | **Content** | One directly modifies internals of another | `B.internalField = x` from A |

**Conclusion:** Aim for **data coupling**; avoid **content coupling** at all costs.

*(7 marks: 1 per type with example.)*

#### Model Answer — (b) Functional Independence + Heuristic Check

**Step 1 — Define Functional Independence.**
Functional independence is achieved when each module addresses a specific, narrow set of related concerns and has minimal interaction with other modules. It is evaluated through **cohesion (high)** and **coupling (low)**.

**Step 2 — Given Scenario (4 modules).**
- M1 is the manager.
- M2, M3 are direct subordinates of M1.
- M4 is a peer of M3 (not subordinate to M1).
- A decision in M1 modifies a global flag that M2 *and* M4 both read.

**Step 3 — Identify Span of Control.**
$$
S_{M1} = \{M2, M3\}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify Scope of Effect.**
$$
E_{M1} = \{M2, M4\}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Heuristic.**
The rule is $S_i \supseteq E_i$. Here:

$$
M4 \in E_{M1}, \quad M4 \notin S_{M1} \;\Rightarrow\; S_{M1} \not\supseteq E_{M1}
$$

So **the design violates the heuristic**.

**Step 6 — Recommended Fix.**
Reorganize the hierarchy so M1 controls M4 *indirectly* — either:
- Make M4 a direct subordinate of M1 (increasing fan-out), or
- Move the flag-setting decision to a higher-level module that controls both M2 and M4.

**Conclusion:** Fix scope-of-effect to align with span-of-control to keep maintainability high.

*(7 marks: 2 for definition, 3 for analysis, 2 for fix.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks on Design Concepts**
>
> 1. **Confusing "good" cohesion with "high" cohesion.** Both phrases mean the same in KTU — *high* = *good* = functional. Do not write "good cohesion" and "high cohesion" as if they were different.
>
> 2. **Listing only one direction of the spectrum.** When asked "types of coupling," you must list **all six** in correct order (best to worst) — partial listing = 3 marks lost.
>
> 3. **Ignoring global variables.** A common error is to call a design "data coupled" while a global array is being shared. Always scan for shared globals *before* classifying coupling.
>
> 4. **Mixing abstraction levels during refinement.** A refined step should not jump from "validate student" to "open JDBC connection" in the same block — examiners deduct for level violation.
>
> 5. **Drawing control hierarchy but forgetting fan-out limit.** Miller's $7 \pm 2$ is checked; if fan-out exceeds 9 without justification, expect deduction.
>
> 6. **Treating "information hiding" and "encapsulation" as identical.** Information hiding is a *design principle* (Parnas, 1972); encapsulation is its *language-level implementation*. Examiners expect both terms used correctly.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print/Save This Section.**

### Core Definitions (Must Memorize Verbatim)

- **Software Design:** Transformation of requirements into a structured representation of the software — a blueprint for implementation.
- **Abstraction:** Hiding complexity; showing only essentials.
- **Refinement:** Top-down stepwise elaboration of an abstraction.
- **Modularity:** Decomposition into well-named, well-bounded modules.
- **Information Hiding:** Each module hides its design decisions behind an interface.
- **Functional Independence:** Achieved through high cohesion and low coupling.
- **Cohesion:** Strength of relationships *within* a module.
- **Coupling:** Strength of relationships *between* modules.
- **Fan-out:** Number of immediate subordinates.
- **Fan-in:** Number of immediate superiors.
- **Span of Control:** Subordinate modules of a manager.
- **Scope of Effect:** All modules affected by a manager's decisions.
- **Architectural Design:** Top-level structural framework of subsystems.
- **Control Hierarchy:** Tree structure representing module ownership.

### The Two Magic Orders (Highest-Yield for KTU)

- **Cohesion (worst → best):** Coincidental → Logical → Temporal → Procedural → Communicational → Sequential → Functional.
- **Coupling (best → worst):** Data → Stamp → Control → External → Common → Content.

### The Two Heuristic Rules (Always Apply)

1. Miller's Rule: $\text{Fan-out} \leq 7 \pm 2$ per module.
2. Parnas-Alvarez Rule: $\text{Span of Control} \subseteq \text{Scope of Effect}$.

### Five Attributes of a Quality Design

- **C**orrectness, **U**nderstandability, **E**fficiency, **M**aintainability, **R**eliability.

### Refinement Discipline

- One abstraction level per refinement block.
- Stop only when code-implementable.

### Three Pillars of Modular Design Quality

1. Functional Independence (high cohesion + low coupling).
2. Information Hiding.
3. Controlled Fan-out & Balanced Hierarchy.

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-second Mental Frame for the Exam Hall:**
> *Ask — "Is this module functionally cohesive? Is its coupling only data-level? Is fan-out under 7? Is scope-of-effect within span-of-control?"* If all four answers are YES, you have a high-quality module by KTU standards.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Model. Architectural Design - Software Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Design Model. Architectural Design - Software Architecture ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The Design Model > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned):** > The **Design Model** is the complete set of artifacts produced during the software design activity. It is an abstract representation of the system that serves as a bridge between the analysis model (the "what") and the implemented system (the "how"). It encompasses data design, architectural design, interface design, and component-level design. The design model is **not the code itself**; rather, it is the blueprint from which code is generated. It is typically expressed using UML (Unified Modeling Language) diagrams and accompanying descriptive text. **Key Elements of the Design Model:** | Element | Purpose | |---|---| | Data Design | Describes the data structures, schemas, and persistent storage | | Architectural Design | Defines the overall structure, components, and their relationships | | Interface Design | Describes how software communicates with users, systems, and devices | | Component-Level Design | Transforms structural elements into procedural descriptions | | Deployment Design | Maps software elements to hardware nodes | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** According to the KTU 2024 scheme, Module 2 focuses on the *Design Models* and *Architectural Design*. The Design Model is the *output* of software design, while Software Architecture is the *foundation* upon which this model is built. --- ### 1.2 Software Architecture > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (Per Sommerville, standard KTU reference):** > **Software Architecture** refers to the organization of a system, including the structural elements (components), their externally visible properties, and the relationships among them. It is the set of principal design decisions about the system. A commonly cited definition is: *"The software architecture of a program or computing system is the structure or structures of the system, which comprise software elements, the externally visible properties of those elements, and the relationships among them."* — Bass, Clements, and Kazman. **Intuitive Analogy: The Blueprint of a Building** Imagine you are constructing a **multi-storey hospital building** in Kerala's coastal region: - **Architectural Design** is the master blueprint that decides: How many floors? Where is the staircase? Which materials resist humidity? Where are the oxygen pipelines routed? - **Design Model** is the *complete set of drawings* — the floor plans, the electrical schematics, the plumbing diagrams, the structural steel detail. Every drawing tells a specialist (electrician, plumber, civil engineer) something specific. - The actual **construction** (coding) follows these drawings. > Just as a poorly designed hospital blueprint leads to structural collapse or inefficient patient flow, a poor software architecture leads to performance bottlenecks, unmaintainable code, and system failure under load. The architecture is the foundation upon which **all** other design decisions rest. **Why Architecture Matters in KTU Context:** For Kerala-based enterprise applications (e.g., a state-wide e-Governance portal), the software architecture must account for: - High concurrent user load - Multi-language support (Malayalam, English) - Network latency across districts - Disaster recovery (monsoon/flood resilience) - Integration with legacy systems **The Architectural Design Process produces three key deliverables:** 1. **Architectural description** — typically a structured document using views 2. **Architectural patterns/styles** — proven solutions to recurring problems 3. **System decomposition** — modular breakdown into subsystems and modules > [!NOTE] > **Core Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):** > - **Architecture** = *What* components exist and *how* they interact at a high level. > - **Detailed Design** = *How* each component internally behaves (algorithms, data structures). ### 1.3 Architectural Views — The 4+1 View Model Since a single diagram cannot capture all aspects of a complex system, architects use **multiple views**, each representing a different stakeholder's perspective. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 4+1 View Model (Kruchten, 1995):** > This is the **canonical KTU reference** for architectural views. The "+1" is the use-case view that ties all other views together. | View | Concern | Stakeholder | UML Diagrams Used | |---|---|---|---| | **Logical View** | Functional requirements — what the system does | End user, analysts | Class, State, Sequence | | **Process View** | Non-functional: performance, concurrency, throughput | Integrators, performance engineers | Activity, Sequence | | **Physical View** | Deployment of software onto hardware | System engineers, network admin | Deployment | | **Development View** | Software module organization and reuse | Developers, project managers | Package, Component | | **+1 Use-Case View** | Captures user requirements; drives other views | All stakeholders | Use-Case | **Intuitive Analogy for Views:** Think of an **airport terminal**: - **Logical View** = the passenger flow logic (check-in → security → boarding) - **Process View** = the parallel operations (baggage handling, fueling, catering running concurrently) - **Physical View** = which terminal building, which runway, where servers are placed - **Development View** = which construction team builds which wing - **Use-Case View (+1)** = the scenarios of "international arrival," "domestic departure," etc. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse the *Logical View* with the *Development View*. Logical view shows the **functional** classes (what the system does). Development view shows the **physical** software modules (how the code is organized for build and reuse). ### 1.4 Architectural Patterns (Styles) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** > An **Architectural Pattern** (or Style) is a named collection of architectural design decisions that are applicable in recurring situations, providing a well-proven solution to a recurring problem. **Key Architectural Styles (High-Yield for KTU):** 1. **Layered (Tiered) Architecture** - Organizes system into layers, each providing services to the layer above. - Example: Presentation → Business Logic → Data Access → Database - Used in traditional enterprise applications. 2. **Client–Server Architecture** - System decomposed into a server providing services and clients consuming them. - Example: Web browser (client) requesting pages from a web server. 3. **Pipe-and-Filter Architecture** - Data flows through a sequence of transformations (filters) connected by pipes. - Example: Unix command pipeline: `cat file.txt | grep "error" | sort | uniq` 4. **Repository (Blackboard) Architecture** - Subsystems share a common data store; each subsystem operates on the central repository. - Example: Integrated Development Environments (IDEs), medical diagnostic systems. 5. **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** - Separates application into three interconnected components: Model (data), View (UI), Controller (logic). - Used in Django, Ruby on Rails, Spring MVC. 6. **Microservices Architecture** - System composed of small, independent, loosely coupled services. - Each service owns its database and communicates via lightweight protocols (HTTP/REST, gRPC). 7. **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)** - Services are loosely coupled, platform-independent, and use standardized interfaces (WSDL, SOAP). - Predecessor to microservices. 8. **Event-Driven Architecture** - Components interact via events; producers emit events, consumers react. - Example: GUI systems, message queues (Kafka, RabbitMQ). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** You may be asked to *match* problems to architectural styles, or to *justify* a style choice for a given scenario. Always mention **non-functional requirements** (scalability, maintainability, performance) as the deciding factor. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a Software Architecture Every software architecture can be decomposed along **three orthogonal dimensions**: 1. **Structure** — The set of components and the relationships among them. 2. **Connectors** — The mechanisms that mediate component interaction (procedure calls, events, shared memory, pipes). 3. **Constraints** — The set of design rules governing component selection, interfaces, and composition. **Generic Architectural Model:** $$ A = \langle C, \text{CON}, \text{Constraints} \rangle $$ Where: - $C$ = set of components (computational elements with well-defined interfaces) - $\text{CON}$ = set of connectors (interaction mechanisms) - $\text{Constraints}$ = architectural design rules ### 2.2 Component Properties > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** > A **Component** is a modular, deployable, replaceable part of a system that encapsulates implementation and exposes a well-defined interface. A component has three key properties (the **CIDs** triad): | Property | Meaning | |---|---| | **Encapsulation** | Hides internal implementation details | | **Interface** | Public contract specifying provided and required services | | **Reusability** | Can be deployed in multiple contexts | ### 2.3 Connector Types Connectors are first-class architectural elements. Common connector types include: | Connector | Communication Mechanism | Example Use | |---|---|---| | Procedure call | Synchronous, in-process | Method invocation | | Message passing | Asynchronous, inter-process | Microservices via Kafka | | Event broadcast | Publish/subscribe | GUI event handling | | Shared data repository | Direct access to common store | Database with multiple readers | | Pipe | Stream-based, sequential | Unix shell pipelines | | RPC / RMI | Network-based call | Java RMI, gRPC | ### 2.4 Architectural Design — Step-by-Step Process The KTU syllabus emphasizes a systematic process. The recommended steps (per Sommerville) are: **Step 1: System Structuring** - Decompose the system into a small number of principal sub-systems. - Identify communications between sub-systems. **Step 2: Control Modeling** - Determine the flow of control between sub-systems. - Choose centralized or decentralized control. **Step 3: Modular Decomposition** - Further decompose each sub-system into modules. - Identify module types and their interdependencies. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Reference Table for KTU 2024 Examinations** | # | Concept | Notation / Formula | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Architectural description | $A = \langle C, \text{CON}, R \rangle$ | $C$ = components, $\text{CON}$ = connectors, $R$ = relationships | | 2 | Coupling (C) | $C = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i$ | Total coupling = sum of pairwise inter-module dependencies | | 3 | Cohesion (Hc) | $Hc = 1 - \dfrac{d}{m}$ | Where $d$ = dissimilar tasks, $m$ = total tasks; high cohesion $\rightarrow Hc \to 1$ | | 4 | Module Independence Index | $I = \dfrac{Hc}{1 + C}$ | Higher $I$ implies better module independence | | 5 | Architecture Quality (AQ) | $AQ = w_1 \cdot P + w_2 \cdot S + w_3 \cdot M$ | $P$ = performance, $S$ = security, $M$ = maintainability; $w_i$ are weights | | 6 | Fan-in (FI) | $FI(m) = \vert \text{modules that call } m \vert$ | Higher fan-in = reusable module | | 7 | Fan-out (FO) | $FO(m) = \vert \text{modules called by } m \vert$ | Lower fan-out = simpler module | | 8 | Architectural Drift | $\Delta_d = \dfrac{\vert A_{\text{as-built}} \setminus A_{\text{as-designed}} \vert}{\vert A_{\text{as-designed}} \vert}$ | Ratio of unrecorded design changes | | 9 | Stability ($S$) | $S = \dfrac{C_e}{C_e + C_a}$ | $C_e$ = efferent coupling, $C_a$ = afferent coupling; $S \in [0,1]$ | > [!WARNING] > **Formula Pitfall (Mark Loser):** In tables, write absolute value as $\vert x \vert$, never using the pipe character `|x|` inside markdown table cells — it breaks the table syntax. KTU examiners specifically deduct for unrenderable formulas. ### 2.6 Why These Concepts Matter in Engineering Practice - **Cohesion & Coupling** drive **maintainability** — poor coupling in a banking system means a single bug fix may ripple across the codebase. - **Stability Index** predicts which modules are *risky to modify* — used in tools like SonarQube and Structure101. - **Architectural Drift** quantifies the gap between intent and reality — critical for legacy modernization in Kerala's e-Governance platforms. - **Fan-in/Fan-out** identifies candidates for reuse — common in utility libraries and SDKs. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Module Independence Index ($I$) The **Module Independence Index** combines cohesion and coupling into a single comparative metric. It is conceptually derived from the principle that *good* software modules should have *high cohesion* and *low coupling*. **Step 1 — Start with definitions:** Let $Hc$ denote the cohesion of module $M$, defined as: $$ Hc = 1 - \frac{d}{m} $$ Where: - $d$ = number of dissimilar tasks performed by $M$ - $m$ = total number of tasks performed by $M$ Let $C$ denote the coupling of module $M$ with the rest of the system, defined as the sum of all inter-module dependencies: $$ C = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i $$ Where $c_i$ is the strength of the $i$-th inter-module connection. **Step 2 — Combine into a single index:** To reward high cohesion and penalize coupling, we define the index as: $$ I = \frac{Hc}{1 + C} $$ Adding $1$ in the denominator prevents division by zero when $C = 0$ (a fully decoupled module). **Step 3 — Interpret the range:** $$ \begin{aligned} I_{\min} &\rightarrow 0 & \text{when } Hc \rightarrow 0 \text{ and } C \rightarrow \infty \\ I_{\max} &\rightarrow 1 & \text{when } Hc \rightarrow 1 \text{ and } C = 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Worked Example:** Consider two candidate module designs in a payroll system: - **Module A** performs 4 highly related tasks (calculate salary, deduct tax, compute bonus, generate payslip). It is called by 2 other modules. - $d = 0$ (no dissimilar tasks), $m = 4$ → $Hc = 1 - 0/4 = 1$ - $C = 2$ → $I_A = 1 / (1 + 2) = 1/3 \approx 0.333$ - **Module B** performs 4 unrelated tasks (calculate salary, log error, update config, send email). It is called by 1 module. - $d = 3$ (3 unrelated), $m = 4$ → $Hc = 1 - 3/4 = 0.25$ - $C = 1$ → $I_B = 0.25 / (1 + 1) = 0.125$ **Conclusion:** $I_A > I_B$, so Module A is the better-organized candidate. ### 3.2 Derivation: Architectural Stability Metric **Step 1 — Define efferent and afferent coupling:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_e(M) &= \text{Number of modules that } M \text{ depends on (outgoing)} \\ C_a(M) &= \text{Number of modules that depend on } M \text{ (incoming)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Define instability:** $$ S = \frac{C_e}{C_e + C_a}, \quad S \in [0, 1] $$ **Step 3 — Interpret boundary cases:** $$ \begin{aligned} S \to 0 & \quad \Rightarrow \text{Module is stable (mostly depended-upon)} \\ S \to 1 & \quad \Rightarrow \text{Module is unstable (mostly depending-on others)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Worked Example for a Banking System:** | Module | $C_e$ | $C_a$ | $S$ | Interpretation | |---|---|---|---|---| | `Database` | 0 | 5 | 0.00 | Highly stable — risky to modify | | `LoanProcessor` | 3 | 1 | 0.75 | Unstable — safe to refactor | | `AuditLogger` | 1 | 4 | 0.20 | Stable utility — used widely | | `UIHandler` | 4 | 0 | 1.00 | Volatile — likely to change with UI redesigns | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Stability does not mean "good" or "bad" — it describes **change risk**. Stable modules need more rigorous testing; volatile modules need looser coupling to enable refactoring. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing an Architectural Style The following decision matrix maps common **non-functional requirements (NFRs)** to recommended architectural styles. This is a frequent question type in KTU Part A. | Non-Functional Requirement | Recommended Style | Justification | |---|---|---| | High maintainability of business rules | **Layered** | Clear separation of concerns; each layer can be modified independently | | Massive parallel data transformation | **Pipe-and-Filter** | Filters can be parallelized; pipes enable streaming | | Real-time, responsive UI | **MVC** | Separation of UI (View) from data (Model) enables fast UI updates | | Independent team deployment | **Microservices** | Each service has its own codebase, team, and release cycle | | Central knowledge base with multiple reasoning engines | **Repository (Blackboard)** | Multiple agents collaborate via shared data store | | Cross-platform integration with legacy systems | **SOA** | Standardized contracts (WSDL/SOAP) enable interoperability | | High-throughput message processing | **Event-Driven** | Decouples producers and consumers; allows backpressure handling | ### 3.4 Step-by-Step Example: Decomposing a Hospital Management System **Scenario:** Design the architecture for a multi-specialty hospital management system used across Kerala with 50+ branches. **Step 1 — Identify Principal Sub-systems:** $$ S = \{S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4, S_5\} $$ Where: - $S_1$ = Patient Management - $S_2$ = Appointment Scheduling - $S_3$ = Electronic Medical Records (EMR) - $S_4$ = Billing and Insurance - $S_5$ = Pharmacy and Inventory **Step 2 — Identify Communication Style:** - Patient Management $\leftrightarrow$ Appointment Scheduling: **Synchronous** (real-time slot check) - Appointment Scheduling $\to$ EMR: **Asynchronous** (record update) - Billing $\to$ Patient Management: **Synchronous** (real-time bill generation) **Step 3 — Apply Architectural Style:** - **Top-level:** Layered architecture (Presentation → Application → Domain → Infrastructure) - **Cross-cutting:** Event-driven for notifications (SMS, email alerts) - **Persistence:** Repository pattern for EMR data with a centralized database per hospital **Step 4 — Document Using 4+1 Views:** - **Logical View:** Classes like `Patient`, `Doctor`, `Appointment`, `Prescription` - **Process View:** Concurrent handling of appointment bookings from multiple branches - **Physical View:** Central cloud server, branch-level cache, mobile client - **Development View:** Maven/Gradle modules: `patient-core`, `appointment-svc`, `emr-svc`, `billing-svc` - **+1 Use-Case View:** "Book appointment," "View lab report," "Generate bill" ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The 4+1 View Model — Architectural Mapping The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the **4+1 View Model** organizes different stakeholder concerns around the central use-case view. ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[Use Case View Plus1
Captures Functional Requirements]:::center A2[Logical View
What the system does
UML Class State Sequence]:::view A3[Process View
Concurrency Performance
UML Activity]:::view A4[Physical View
Hardware Mapping
UML Deployment]:::view A5[Development View
Module Organization
UML Package Component]:::view A6[End User Analyst]:::stake A7[Integrator Performance Engineer]:::stake A8[System Engineer Network Admin]:::stake A9[Developer Project Manager]:::stake A10[All Stakeholders]:::stake A1 --- A2 A1 --- A3 A1 --- A4 A1 --- A5 A6 --- A2 A7 --- A3 A8 --- A4 A9 --- A5 A10 --- A1 classDef center fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef view fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 classDef stake fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The central **+1 Use-Case View** drives all other views. Each surrounding view (Logical, Process, Physical, Development) is consumed by a specific stakeholder group, shown in green. --- ### 4.2 Layered Architecture — Hospital Management System ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[Layer 1 Presentation
Web Portal Mobile App Desktop Client]:::layer1 L2[Layer 2 Business Logic
PatientService AppointmentService BillingService]:::layer2 L3[Layer 3 Data Access
PatientRepository AppointmentRepository]:::layer3 L4[Layer 4 Database
PostgreSQL MySQL MongoDB]:::layer4 L1 -->|HTTP Request| L2 L2 -->|Repository Call| L3 L3 -->|SQL Query| L4 L4 -->|Result Set| L3 L3 -->|Domain Object| L2 L2 -->|JSON Response| L1 classDef layer1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 classDef layer2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 classDef layer3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef layer4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Architectural Insight:** In a layered architecture, each layer is **dependent only on the layer directly below it**. This enforces separation of concerns and enables independent testing of each layer. --- ### 4.3 Architectural Style Selection — Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB Q1{Is the system
data intensive?}:::q Q2{Does it require
real time updates?}:::q Q3{Are teams
independent?}:::q Q4{Is the workflow
sequential transforms?}:::q R1[Repository Blackboard]:::r R2[MVC Event Driven]:::r R3[Microservices]:::r R4[Pipe and Filter]:::r R5[Layered SOA]:::r Q1 -->|Yes| R1 Q1 -->|No| Q2 Q2 -->|Yes| R2 Q2 -->|No| Q3 Q3 -->|Yes| R3 Q3 -->|No| Q4 Q4 -->|Yes| R4 Q4 -->|No| R5 classDef q fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000 classDef r fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Practical Use:** When a KTU question presents a scenario, walk through this decision tree by checking each non-functional requirement branch. The terminal node (highlighted in blue) is the recommended style. --- ### 4.4 Component-Connector Block Diagram — Generic Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR C1[Component 1
User Interface Module]:::comp C2[Component 2
Business Logic Engine]:::comp C3[Component 3
Data Access Layer]:::comp C4[Component 4
External Service Adapter]:::comp CON1[Connector 1
Procedure Call]:::conn CON2[Connector 2
Message Queue]:::conn CON3[Connector 3
Shared Database]:::conn CON4[Connector 4
REST API]:::conn C1 --- CON1 CON1 --- C2 C2 --- CON2 CON2 --- C3 C3 --- CON3 CON3 --- C2 C2 --- CON4 CON4 --- C4 classDef comp fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef conn fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Architectural Principle:** Components (red) encapsulate *computation*. Connectors (yellow) encapsulate *interaction*. This separation is foundational to the 4+1 view model and component-based software engineering (CBSE). --- ### 4.5 MVC Pattern — Interaction Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR V[View
User Interface]:::mvc M[Model
Data and Business Rules]:::mvc CT[Controller
Mediator and Logic]:::mvc USER[End User]:::user USER -->|Input Event| V V -->|User Action| CT CT -->|Update State| M M -->|Notify Change| CT CT -->|Refresh| V V -->|Rendered Output| USER classDef mvc fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 classDef user fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **MVC Insight:** The Controller is the *only* component that knows about both Model and View. The Model and View do **not** communicate directly. This is a classic example of low coupling and high cohesion. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Software Architecture. List the four views in the 4+1 view model of software architecture.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Software Architecture (1 mark):** Software architecture is the set of principal design decisions about the organization of a software system — its structural elements (components), their externally visible properties, and the relationships among them. > **Four Views (2 marks):** > 1. **Logical View** — describes the functional requirements using class and state diagrams > 2. **Process View** — addresses non-functional requirements like performance and concurrency > 3. **Physical View** — maps software elements to hardware nodes (deployment) > 4. **Development View** — describes the software module organization for development teams > > The **+1 Use-Case View** ties all four views together by capturing the system's high-level functional requirements. --- **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between logical and physical architectural views with a suitable example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Logical View | Physical View | |---|---|---| | Focus | Functional structure | Deployment topology | | Stakeholder | Analysts, designers | System engineers, network admins | | Diagram | Class, State, Sequence | Deployment diagram | | Example | `Patient`, `Doctor`, `Appointment` classes and their relationships | Mapping the `AppointmentService` microservice to a Kubernetes pod on AWS | > **Example (1 mark):** A logical view of an e-Governance portal shows the `CitizenService` class interacting with `ApplicationService` and `DocumentService`. The physical view of the same system shows these services deployed as Docker containers on three cloud servers in Thiruvananthapuram, Kochi, and Kozhikode. --- ### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **a)** Explain the concept of **architectural design** in software engineering. Describe in detail the **4+1 view model** with neat diagrams. **(7 marks)** **b)** With a suitable case study, compare and contrast the **Layered, Client-Server, and Microservices** architectural styles. Justify which is most suitable for a **Kerala state-wide e-Governance portal** with 5 million users. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Architectural Design & 4+1 View Model (7 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Architectural design is the process of identifying the principal components of a system, their interfaces, and the overall structure that satisfies both functional and non-functional requirements. It bridges the gap between requirements specification and detailed design. **4+1 View Model Explanation (5 marks):** - **Logical View:** Captures the *vocabulary* of the problem domain. For a library management system, it shows classes like `Book`, `Member`, `Loan`, `Catalog` with attributes, operations, and relationships (inheritance, association). - **Process View:** Describes the *runtime* behavior — threads, processes, and concurrency. Uses activity and sequence diagrams to show how the system handles multiple simultaneous loans. - **Physical View:** Shows the *deployment* of software onto hardware. A deployment diagram for the library system might show the `LoanService` running on an application server, the database on a dedicated DBMS server, and clients distributed across 50 branch libraries. - **Development View:** Organizes the *software modules* (packages, components) for development. Shows how the project is split into Maven/Gradle modules: `catalog-module`, `loan-module`, `member-module`. - **+1 Use-Case View:** The unifying view that shows the major scenarios (borrow book, return book, reserve book, pay fine) and ties the other four views together by validating that all stakeholder concerns are addressed. **(b) Architectural Style Comparison (7 marks):** | Aspect | Layered | Client-Server | Microservices | |---|---|---|---| | Structure | Vertical stack of layers | Server hosts services; clients consume | Network of independent services | | Scalability | Limited (scale as monolith) | Moderate (scale server) | High (scale individual services) | | Fault Tolerance | Low (single point of failure) | Medium (server SPOF) | High (one service down, others up) | | Deployment | Single unit | Two units (client + server) | Many independent units | | Team Coordination | Centralized | Small teams | Independent teams per service | | Tech Stack | Usually homogeneous | Mostly homogeneous | Polyglot allowed | | Network Latency | In-process calls | Local network | Inter-service HTTP/RPC calls | | Suitable For | Small-medium apps | Traditional web apps | Large, complex, evolving systems | **Case Study Justification (1 mark for the right choice + 1 mark for justification):** For the **Kerala state-wide e-Governance portal** with 5 million users, **Microservices** is the most suitable style. Justification: - **Scalability:** During flood relief or election periods, the `ApplicationService` may need 10x more capacity than the `GrievanceService`. Microservices allow *independent* scaling. - **Fault Isolation:** A bug in the `TaxPaymentService` should not crash the entire portal — microservice isolation ensures this. - **Team Autonomy:** Different Kerala departments (Revenue, Health, Education) can deploy independently. - **Technology Heterogeneity:** The Health department may need Python (for ML-based analytics), while Revenue needs Java for transactional integrity. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **No diagram = -2 marks:** For a 7-mark sub-question, a UML sketch of the 4+1 view or comparison diagram is **mandatory**. > 2. **Generic answers lose marks:** Stating "Layered is good for maintainability" without an example loses 1 mark. Always tie to a *specific* requirement. > 3. **Skipping the "+1":** The 4+1 view has 5 views, not 4. Forgetting the use-case view costs 1 mark. > 4. **No justification of choice:** A 7-mark question that ends with "Microservices is best" without explaining *why* (in the context of the scenario) is incomplete. > 5. **Conflating architecture and design:** Architecture is high-level structure; design is internal behavior. Mixing them is a common error. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **a)** What is a **Design Model**? Explain the various elements that constitute a complete design model. **(7 marks)** **b)** Discuss in detail the **MVC, Pipe-and-Filter, and Repository** architectural patterns. For an **online exam system used by Kerala Technological University (KTU)** for 10,000 concurrent students, recommend the most appropriate pattern with justification. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) The Design Model (7 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** A design model is the abstract description of a software system's structure, behavior, and data. It is the output of the design phase and serves as the blueprint for implementation. It is typically represented using UML diagrams and accompanying documentation. **Elements of the Design Model (5 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Data Design Elements:** Class diagrams, ER diagrams, data dictionary. Describes the static data structures and their relationships. *Example:* `Student` class with `rollNumber`, `name`, `courseList` attributes. 2. **Architectural Design Elements:** Subsystem diagrams, component diagrams, deployment diagrams. Defines the high-level structure and topology. *Example:* Three-tier architecture with web server, application server, and database server. 3. **Interface Design Elements:** Interface specifications, sequence diagrams for inter-module communication. Describes the boundary contracts. *Example:* `IPaymentGateway` interface with `processPayment()` and `refund()` methods. 4. **Component-Level Design Elements:** Detailed procedural descriptions, algorithm pseudocode, state transition diagrams. Translates architecture into executable logic. *Example:* State machine for the `Order` component (Pending → Confirmed → Shipped → Delivered). 5. **Deployment Design Elements:** Mapping of software components to physical hardware. *Example:* Load balancer distributes traffic across 5 application server nodes. **(b) Architectural Patterns Comparison (7 marks):** **MVC (Model-View-Controller) (2 marks):** - Separates the application into three components: **Model** (data and business rules), **View** (user interface), and **Controller** (input handling and flow logic). - **Example:** Django framework, Ruby on Rails, Spring MVC. - **Strengths:** High cohesion within each component; multiple views for the same model; parallel development of UI and logic. **Pipe-and-Filter (2 marks):** - Data flows through a sequence of *filters* connected by *pipes*. Each filter transforms the data and passes it to the next. - **Example:** Unix shell pipeline `cat | grep | sort | uniq`; Apache Kafka Streams; compiler phases (lexical → syntax → semantic → code generation). - **Strengths:** Composable, reusable filters; supports concurrent execution; supports incremental processing. **Repository (Blackboard) (2 marks):** - Multiple subsystems share a common data store (the repository). Subsystems operate independently on the central knowledge. - **Example:** IDEs (Eclipse, IntelliJ) where language tools, debuggers, and UI share a common workspace model. - **Strengths:** Centralized data management; easy to add new subsystems; suitable for complex, evolving knowledge domains. **Recommendation for KTU Online Exam System (1 mark for choice + 1 mark for justification):** **Recommended Pattern: Pipe-and-Filter with Repository hybrid** - **Justification:** - The exam workflow is **sequential** — authentication → question delivery → answer submission → auto-grading → result publishing. This is a classic pipe-and-filter pipeline. - The questions, student answers, and results are stored in a **central repository** — the central database. - For 10,000 concurrent students, the filters can be **parallelized** (multiple grading filters for different sections), and the repository can be **sharded** by exam center. - MVC is *also* useful for the student UI (View), but the core exam logic follows a pipeline model. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall Callout — Common KTU Errors on This Question Type:** > 1. Listing architectural styles without **examples** — deducts 1–2 marks per style. > 2. Recommending a pattern without **justification tied to the scenario** — generic answers get partial credit only. > 3. Confusing "design model elements" with "UML diagram types" — they are related but not identical. > 4. Forgetting that the design model is **iterative** — it evolves as the system is built. Failing to mention this loses 1 mark in higher-COG questions. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This Before the Exam)** - [ ] **Design Model** = the *complete set of design artifacts* (data, architecture, interface, component, deployment). - [ ] **Software Architecture** = the *foundation* of the design model — structure, components, connectors, constraints. - [ ] **4+1 Views:** Logical, Process, Physical, Development, **+1 Use-Case**. - [ ] **Logical View** = *what* the system does (functional). **Development View** = *how* the code is organized (modules). - [ ] **Architectural Patterns/Styles** are proven solutions — Layered, Client-Server, Pipe-and-Filter, Repository, MVC, Microservices, SOA, Event-Driven. - [ ] **Components** encapsulate *computation*. **Connectors** encapsulate *interaction*. - [ ] **High Cohesion, Low Coupling** = the cardinal design rule. - [ ] **Stability Index** $S = C_e / (C_e + C_a)$ — stable modules are riskier to change. - [ ] **Fan-in** = how many modules call *me* (reusability). **Fan-out** = how many modules *I* call (complexity). - [ ] **Architectural Drift** = gap between as-designed and as-built architecture. - [ ] Always **tie architectural decisions to non-functional requirements** in KTU answers — not just to functional ones. - [ ] For **14-mark questions**, include a **UML diagram** (class, component, or deployment).
