No diagram = significant mark loss. - [ ] The **+1 use-case view** is what *connects* the other four views — it shows how the architecture realizes end-user scenarios. - [ ] **Microservices** is preferred for large-scale, distributed systems; **Layered** is preferred for small-to-medium traditional applications; **MVC** is preferred for user-facing interactive applications. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Tip:** When asked to "compare" two or more architectural styles, structure your answer as a **table** (with at least 4 comparison criteria) **plus** 2–3 sentences of scenario-specific justification. This structure consistently scores full marks in KTU valuation.

topics.title: Architectural Styles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Architectural Styles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Style** (also called *Architectural Pattern*) is a named, reusable collection of **structural decisions**, **constraints**, and **organizational principles** that define a category of software systems. It prescribes how components are **organized**, how **control flows** between them, how **data is communicated**, and what **connectors** (paths of interaction) are permissible between elements.

According to **Shaw & Garlan (1996)** — the foundational reference for this topic — an architectural style defines:
- A **vocabulary of components** (e.g., filters, repositories, objects, processes).
- A **vocabulary of connectors** (e.g., procedure call, event broadcast, pipe, shared variable).
- A set of **constraints** governing how they may be combined.
- A set of **semantic models** characterizing the system's behavior.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, the syllabus explicitly classifies architectural styles into the following five canonical families, originally proposed by Mary Shaw:

1. **Data-Centered (Repository) Architectures**
2. **Data-Flow Architectures** (Pipe-and-Filter, Batch Sequential)
3. **Call-and-Return Architectures** (Main Program/Subroutine, Object-Oriented, Layered)
4. **Independent-Components Architectures** (Communicating Processes, Event Systems)
5. **Modern / Distributed Styles** (Client–Server, Peer-to-Peer, Publish–Subscribe, SOA, Microservices)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Building Blueprint" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of an architectural style as a *kitchen layout*.** The components (stove, fridge, sink) and their relationships (workflow of cooking) differ dramatically between a *closed kitchen* (Layered), a *buffet line* (Pipe-and-Filter), and a *food court with independent stalls* (Peer-to-Peer). Each "style" yields a different feel, efficiency, and cost — just as a software system feels different under MVC versus Microservices.

More concretely:
- A **Data-Centered** system is like a **shared library** — everyone reads/writes the central book repository.
- A **Pipe-and-Filter** system is like an **assembly line** in a factory — raw material enters, gets transformed, and exits as a finished product.
- A **Layered** system is like a **corporate hierarchy** — the CEO (top layer) talks only to VPs (middle layer), who talk to managers (bottom layer).
- A **Client-Server** system is like a **restaurant** — waiters (clients) take orders and bring food from the kitchen (server).
- A **Peer-to-Peer** system is like a **potluck dinner** — every guest brings and shares food with every other guest.

## 1.3 Why Architectural Styles Matter in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Architectural styles are **high-weightage** in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) for Module 2. Students are typically asked to:
> - **Compare** two or more styles (very common — 7 to 14 mark questions).
> - **Draw** a labeled block diagram of a style.
> - **Justify** the suitability of a style for a given problem statement (e.g., "Which style is best for an ATM?").
> - **Identify** the style used by a real-world system (e.g., WWW, compilers, banking systems).

The **three quality attributes** that architectural styles directly control are:
- **Modifiability** (ease of change)
- **Performance** (latency and throughput)
- **Reusability** (component reuse across systems)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Theory Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Canonical Architectural Style Families

### 🔵 A. Data-Centered Architecture (Repository Style)

**Definition:** A **central data store (repository)** is shared by all surrounding components. Components do not call each other directly; instead, they **read from / write to** the central repository.

**Key Characteristics:**
- Tight **data integration**, loose **component coupling**.
- The repository acts as the **blackboard** of communication.
- Components are **independent** in execution but share state via the repository.
- **Subsystems** must agree on the data model.

**Best For:** Systems with huge volumes of frequently updated shared data.
- **Example:** **Blackboard systems** (speech recognition, AI reasoning), **Database-centric** enterprise apps, **Integrated Development Environments (IDEs)**.

**Connectors Used:** Direct access to shared data structure (e.g., SQL transactions, in-memory database).

**Why and How it Works (Logical Flow):**
1. Component *C₁* writes raw data into the repository.
2. Repository stores and notifies (or allows polling).
3. Component *C₂* reads the data, performs transformation.
4. Component *C₃* reads C₂'s output and renders the result.
5. No direct C₁ → C₂ → C₃ call chain exists.

---

### 🟢 B. Data-Flow Architecture

#### B.1 Pipe-and-Filter Style

**Definition:** A linear chain of **filters** (transformers) connected by **pipes** (conduits for data streams). Each filter reads input, transforms it independently, and produces output.

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Filters** are **stateless**, **independent**, and **composable**.
- **Pipes** are unidirectional data streams.
- **Incremental computation** is the norm.
- **Composability** — filters can be reordered/replaced without affecting neighbors.

**Best For:** **Compilers** (lexical → syntax → semantic → IR → optimization → code-gen stages), **Unix shell pipelines** (`cat | grep | sort | uniq`).

#### B.2 Batch Sequential Style

**Definition:** A **strict sequential** version of pipe-and-filter where the entire dataset is passed as a **batch** from one stage to the next, with **no concurrency**.

**Key Characteristics:**
- Each program **completes fully** before the next begins.
- No streaming.
- Simpler than pipe-and-filter but **less responsive**.

**Best For:** **Payroll systems**, **banking EOD (End of Day) batch jobs**, **legacy data processing**.

---

### 🟡 C. Call-and-Return Architecture

#### C.1 Main Program / Subroutine Style

**Definition:** The classical **decomposition** approach. A top-level main program **decomposes** the problem into subroutines/functions, which may further call other subroutines.

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Top-down functional decomposition**.
- Strict **program hierarchy**.
- One thread of control.

**Best For:** Small-to-medium procedural systems, classical algorithmic problems.

#### C.2 Object-Oriented Style

**Definition:** The system is structured as a **collection of objects**, each encapsulating state and behavior, communicating via **method calls / messages**.

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Encapsulation**, **inheritance**, **polymorphism**.
- Loose coupling via **interfaces**.
- Easy to extend via subclassing.

**Best For:** GUI frameworks, simulation systems, modern enterprise applications.

#### C.3 Layered Style (Tiered)

**Definition:** System is organized into **horizontal layers**, where each layer provides services to the layer **above** and consumes services from the layer **below**.

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Strict layering** — requests go down, responses go up.
- **Separation of concerns**.
- **Replaceable layers** (e.g., swap DB layer).

**Best For:** **OS (kernel → drivers → apps)**, **network protocols (OSI 7-layer model)**, **enterprise 3-tier (Presentation → Logic → Data)**.

---

### 🟠 D. Independent-Components Architecture

#### D.1 Communicating Processes

**Definition:** Independent **concurrent processes** that communicate via **message passing** (sockets, queues, RPC).

**Key Characteristics:**
- True **concurrency** and **distribution**.
- **Loose coupling** — no shared memory.
- **Fault isolation** — one process can fail without bringing others down.

**Best For:** Distributed systems, IoT networks, microservices.

#### D.2 Event Systems (Event-Driven)

**Definition:** Components publish **events**; other components **subscribe** to events of interest. Communication is **asynchronous** and **decoupled**.

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Publisher/Subscriber** decoupling — publishers don't know subscribers.
- **Asynchronous** flow.
- High **scalability** and **extensibility**.

**Best For:** GUI event handlers, real-time trading, IoT, **Apache Kafka**, **Node.js event loop**.

---

### 🔴 E. Modern Distributed Styles

| Style | Acronym | Key Idea | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Client–Server** | C/S | Clients request services from a central server | Web browser ↔ Apache |
| **Peer-to-Peer** | P2P | All nodes are equal; any node can be client or server | BitTorrent, Blockchain |
| **Service-Oriented** | SOA | Services with standardized contracts (WSDL/SOAP) communicate via ESB | Enterprise legacy integration |
| **Microservices** | MSA | Suite of small, independently deployable services | Netflix, Amazon |
| **Publish–Subscribe** | Pub/Sub | Special case of event systems; topics are central | MQTT, Kafka topics |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison "Cheat Sheet"

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important revision tool** for the 14-mark comparison question. It is exactly the kind of structured answer KTU examiners award full marks for.

| # | Architectural Style | Coupling | Control Flow | Data Handling | Best Suited For | Real Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Data-Centered (Repository)** | Loose (via data) | Implicit (repo) | Centralized shared data | Collaborative, data-intensive systems | Blackboard AI, IDEs |
| 2 | **Pipe-and-Filter** | Loosest | Stream (data-driven) | Stream-based, incremental | Data transformation pipelines | Unix shell, Compilers |
| 3 | **Batch Sequential** | Loose | Sequential, blocking | Whole batches | EOD processing, payroll | Banking batch jobs |
| 4 | **Main Program / Subroutine** | Tight (call hierarchy) | Top-down hierarchy | Local to procedures | Algorithmic, procedural | Classical Fortran/C |
| 5 | **Object-Oriented** | Loose (via interfaces) | Method calls, polymorphic | Encapsulated in objects | GUI, simulation, modern apps | Java Swing, .NET |
| 6 | **Layered** | Moderate (strict layers) | Down-up request/response | Local to layers | OS, network stacks, enterprise | OSI model, 3-tier web |
| 7 | **Communicating Processes** | Very loose | Message-passing, concurrent | Distributed | Real-time, distributed systems | Erlang, MPI |
| 8 | **Event Systems** | Very loose (asynchronous) | Event-driven, reactive | Event payloads | UI, IoT, real-time trading | Kafka, Node.js |
| 9 | **Client–Server** | Moderate (network call) | Request–response | Centralized on server | Web, email, databases | Browser ↔ Web server |
| 10 | **Peer-to-Peer** | Very loose | Symmetric, decentralized | Distributed across peers | File sharing, blockchain | BitTorrent |
| 11 | **SOA** | Loose (contract-driven) | Service calls via ESB | Service-owned | Enterprise integration | Banking SOA |
| 12 | **Microservices** | Very loose (API) | Independent, async | Database-per-service | Cloud-native apps | Netflix, Uber |

---

## 2.3 Quality Attribute Trade-off Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently frame the question as: *"Which architectural style is best for system X and why?"* The answer must be **justified using quality attributes** — not just personal opinion.

$$\text{QualityAttribute}(\text{Style}) = f(\text{Coupling}, \text{Cohesion}, \text{Concurrency}, \text{Modifiability})$$

Where:
- **Coupling** is the degree of interdependence between components.
- **Cohesion** is the degree to which a component's responsibilities belong together.
- **Concurrency** is the ability to perform multiple tasks simultaneously.
- **Modifiability** is the ease of making changes to the system.

**Trade-off Equations (KTU frequently tested):**

$$
\text{Performance}_{\text{Pipe-and-Filter}} \;\propto\; \text{Parallelizable\_Stages}
$$

$$
\text{Reusability}_{\text{Microservices}} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{\text{Service\_Coupling}}
$$

$$
\text{Reliability}_{\text{P2P}} \;\propto\; \text{Node\_Redundancy}
$$

$$
\text{Maintainability}_{\text{Layered}} \;\propto\; \text{Layer\_Count}^{-1} \;\; \text{(inverse relation)}
$$

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern industry, the choice of architectural style determines:
- **Cloud bill** (monolith vs microservices affects deployment).
- **Time-to-market** (loose coupling enables parallel teams).
- **Failure blast radius** (Layered + Microservices limits failure).
- **Compliance & Security** (Layered helps meet the **defense-in-depth** principle).
- **On-call burden** (Event-driven reduces synchronous blocking).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Style-by-Style Deep Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section is the **exhaustive analytical breakdown** that justifies a 14-mark answer. Each architectural style is analyzed using the **same 7-dimensional framework** (originally adapted from Bass, Clements, Kazman — *Software Architecture in Practice*) so that students can produce a parallel, comparison-friendly answer.

## 3.1 The 7-Dimensional Analytical Framework

For each architectural style, we examine:

1. **Structure** — what does the topology look like?
2. **Component Type** — what is the basic computational unit?
3. **Connector Type** — how do components communicate?
4. **Control Flow** — who initiates interaction?
5. **Data Flow** — how does data move?
6. **Strengths (Advantages)** — why pick this?
7. **Weaknesses (Disadvantages)** — when to avoid it?

---

## 3.2 Style 1: Data-Centered (Repository) — Exhaustive Analysis

**Step 1 — Structure Derivation:**

$$
\text{RepositoryArchitecture} = \{R, C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n, L_{1\ldots n}\}
$$

Where:
- $R$ = the central repository
- $C_i$ = the $i^{\text{th}}$ component
- $L_i$ = logical link from $C_i$ to $R$

**Step 2 — Component Type:** Independent computational modules (each focused on one domain operation).

**Step 3 — Connector Type:** Direct shared-data access (e.g., SQL read/write, file lock, in-memory bus).

**Step 4 — Control Flow:**

$$
\text{Initiator} = \arg\max_{C_i} \left( \text{EventTrigger}(C_i) \right)
$$

Any component may trigger a data write; the repository does not control the order.

**Step 5 — Data Flow:** Bidirectional. Components **read** from and **write** to the central store.

**Step 6 — Strengths:**
- High **data integrity** due to a single source of truth.
- Easy to **add new components** (just connect to the repository).
- Excellent for **collaborative** applications.

**Step 7 — Weaknesses:**
- **Repository becomes a bottleneck** as data volume grows.
- **Single point of failure** unless replicated.
- All components must **agree on the data model** — schema evolution is hard.

**Real-World Mapping:** Modern **microservices that share a single PostgreSQL cluster** mimic a repository style. Classical **blackboard systems** (e.g., HEARSAY-II speech recognizer) are textbook examples.

---

## 3.3 Style 2: Pipe-and-Filter — Exhaustive Analysis

**Step 1 — Structure Derivation:**

$$
\text{PipeFilter} = \langle F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_n; P_{1\rightarrow 2}, P_{2\rightarrow 3}, \ldots, P_{(n-1)\rightarrow n} \rangle
$$

Where:
- $F_i$ = filter at stage $i$
- $P_{i \rightarrow (i+1)}$ = pipe carrying $F_i$'s output to $F_{i+1}$'s input

**Step 2 — Component Type:** **Stateless** transformer (each filter has no memory of prior inputs).

**Step 3 — Connector Type:** **Unidirectional data stream** (FIFO queue, socket, pipe).

**Step 4 — Control Flow:** **Data-driven** — control is implicit in the arrival of data at a filter.

**Step 5 — Data Flow:** **Streaming, incremental.**

**Step 6 — Strengths:**
- **Composability** — re-order/replace filters without side effects.
- **Parallelism** — adjacent filters can run concurrently if buffers allow.
- **Reusability** — each filter is a self-contained black box.

**Step 7 — Weaknesses:**
- **State-sharing** is awkward (filters are stateless).
- **Shared state** must be pushed into data, increasing payload.
- **Latency** can increase for long pipelines.

**Real-World Mapping:** **GCC compiler pipeline** (lex → parse → optimize → codegen), **Unix commands** (`ps aux | grep chrome | awk '{print $2}'`).

---

## 3.4 Style 3: Layered Architecture — Exhaustive Analysis

**Step 1 — Structure Derivation:**

$$
\text{LayeredSystem} = \langle L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_k; \text{ForAll}(i < j): \text{Calls}(L_j, L_i) \rangle
$$

This is a strict **partial order** over layers. A layer $L_j$ may only call layer $L_i$ if $i < j$.

**Step 2 — Component Type:** **Cohesive services** grouped by abstraction level.

**Step 3 — Connector Type:** **Procedure calls** or **APIs** with strict downward direction.

**Step 4 — Control Flow:** **Top-down** (request) and **bottom-up** (response).

**Step 5 — Data Flow:** **Structured** — passed as parameters or return values between adjacent layers.

**Step 6 — Strengths:**
- **Separation of concerns** — easy to reason about.
- **Portability** — swap the data layer (e.g., MySQL → PostgreSQL) without affecting business logic.
- **Testability** — layers can be **mocked** for unit testing.

**Step 7 — Weaknesses:**
- **Performance penalty** — requests traverse many layers (the *layer-crossing tax*).
- **Cascading changes** — modifying a low layer may ripple upward.
- **Tendency toward "pass-through" layers** that add no real value (anti-pattern).

**Real-World Mapping:** **OSI 7-layer model**, **TCP/IP stack**, **3-tier web apps** (Presentation → Application → Database).

---

## 3.5 Style 4: Event-Driven (Independent Components) — Exhaustive Analysis

**Step 1 — Structure Derivation:**

$$
\text{EventSystem} = \langle P, E, C_{pe}, C_{ec} \rangle
$$

Where:
- $P$ = set of **publishers**
- $E$ = **event bus** (or broker)
- $C$ = set of **consumers (subscribers)**
- $C_{pe}$ = publisher-to-event connections
- $C_{ec}$ = event-to-consumer connections

**Step 2 — Component Type:** **Publisher** or **Subscriber** (often the same component at different times).

**Step 3 — Connector Type:** **Event bus / message broker** (asynchronous, often via Kafka, RabbitMQ, MQTT).

**Step 4 — Control Flow:** **Event-driven** — control flows when an event is broadcast.

**Step 5 — Data Flow:** **Asynchronous, push-based** (publisher pushes; subscribers react).

**Step 6 — Strengths:**
- **Extreme decoupling** — publishers don't know subscribers.
- **Scalability** — easy to add new consumers.
- **Reactivity** — natural fit for real-time systems.

**Step 7 — Weaknesses:**
- **Debugging** is hard (event flow is implicit).
- **Eventual consistency** — not ideal for strong consistency.
- **Broker can be a SPOF** unless clustered.

**Real-World Mapping:** **Apache Kafka**, **Node.js event loop**, **AWS SNS/SQS**, **React.js event handlers**.

---

## 3.6 Style 5: Client–Server — Exhaustive Analysis

**Step 1 — Structure Derivation:**

$$
\text{ClientServer} = \langle \text{Clients}_1 \ldots \text{Clients}_m, \text{Server}, N \rangle
$$

Where $N$ is the **network** that acts as the connector.

**Step 2 — Component Type:** **Clients** (requesters) and **Server** (provider).

**Step 3 — Connector Type:** **Network protocols** (HTTP/HTTPS, TCP, RPC, gRPC).

**Step 4 — Control Flow:** **Request–Response** (synchronous or asynchronous).

**Step 5 — Data Flow:** Centralized on the server.

**Step 6 — Strengths:**
- **Centralized data management** — server is the single source of truth.
- **Easy maintenance** — update server, all clients benefit.
- **Security** — perimeter can be hardened at the server.

**Step 7 — Weaknesses:**
- **Server is a SPOF** unless load-balanced / replicated.
- **Scalability bottleneck** at server.
- **Network latency** affects performance.

**Real-World Mapping:** **WWW (browser ↔ web server)**, **Gmail**, **online banking**.

---

## 3.7 Style 6: Peer-to-Peer — Exhaustive Analysis

**Step 1 — Structure Derivation:**

$$
\text{P2P} = \langle N_1, N_2, \ldots, N_p; E_{i,j} \rangle
$$

A **fully connected graph** (in pure P2P) where every node $N_i$ can act as both client and server.

**Step 2 — Component Type:** **Peer** — symmetric role.

**Step 3 — Connector Type:** **Direct peer connections** (TCP sockets, WebRTC).

**Step 4 — Control Flow:** **Symmetric** — any peer can initiate a request.

**Step 5 — Data Flow:** **Distributed** — content is split across peers (chunks, swarms).

**Step 6 — Strengths:**
- **High fault tolerance** — no single point of failure.
- **Scales with users** — every new peer adds capacity.
- **Censorship-resistant**.

**Step 7 — Weaknesses:**
- **Discovery is hard** (need DHT or tracker).
- **Security** — peers may be malicious (e.g., poisoned chunks).
- **QoS** is unpredictable.

**Real-World Mapping:** **BitTorrent**, **Bitcoin / Blockchain**, **WebRTC video calls**.

---

## 3.8 Style 7: Microservices — Exhaustive Analysis

**Step 1 — Structure Derivation:**

$$
\text{Microservices} = \langle S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n, \text{API\_Gateway}, \text{Registry} \rangle
$$

**Step 2 — Component Type:** **Service** — owns its data, business logic, and deployment.

**Step 3 — Connector Type:** **REST / gRPC / Message Queue** over a service mesh.

**Step 4 — Control Flow:** **Decentralized** — each service is independently deployable.

**Step 5 — Data Flow:** **Database-per-service** (no shared DBs).

**Step 6 — Strengths:**
- **Independent deployment** — fast CI/CD.
- **Polyglot** — each service can use a different language.
- **Resilience** — failure is isolated per service.

**Step 7 — Weaknesses:**
- **Operational complexity** — needs container orchestration (Kubernetes).
- **Network overhead** — inter-service calls add latency.
- **Distributed transactions** are hard (CAP theorem trade-offs).

**Real-World Mapping:** **Netflix, Amazon, Uber, Airbnb**.

---

## 3.9 Summary Decision Matrix (For Examination)

| Use Case | Recommended Style | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Compiler design | **Pipe-and-Filter** | Stages of transformation are independent |
| Banking transaction system | **Client-Server + Repository** | Centralized ledger, concurrent clients |
| Real-time chat app | **Event-Driven / Pub-Sub** | Asynchronous, fan-out messaging |
| Airline reservation | **Layered (3-tier)** | Strict separation of UI, business, data |
| BitTorrent file sharing | **Peer-to-Peer** | Decentralization, fault tolerance |
| Netflix catalog | **Microservices** | Independent scaling of recommendation, billing, etc. |
| AI reasoning system | **Blackboard (Data-Centered)** | Multiple specialists contribute to shared hypothesis space |
| Payroll EOD job | **Batch Sequential** | Bulk processing, no interactivity |
| Java Swing GUI | **Object-Oriented** | Encapsulated widgets, event delegation |

> Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, all diagrams are rendered as **Mermaid block diagrams**. The convention below uses uppercase alphanumeric labels inside double quotes (no markdown formatting inside labels) and alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters.

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Data-Centered (Repository) Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Repo[REPOSITORY / SHARED DATA STORE]
    CompA[COMPONENT A - INPUT HANDLER]
    CompB[COMPONENT B - ANALYZER]
    CompC[COMPONENT C - OUTPUT FORMATTER]
    CompD[COMPONENT D - REPORTER]

    CompA -->|WRITE| Repo
    Repo -->|READ| CompB
    CompB -->|WRITE DERIVED DATA| Repo
    Repo -->|READ| CompC
    Repo -->|READ| CompD
```

> **Reading guide:** Notice that *no two components call each other directly* — all communication flows through the central repository.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Pipe-and-Filter Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    Source[DATA SOURCE] -->|STREAM| F1[FILTER 1 - LEXICAL ANALYSIS]
    F1 -->|TOKENS| F2[FILTER 2 - PARSING]
    F2 -->|AST| F3[FILTER 3 - SEMANTIC CHECK]
    F3 -->|ANNOTATED AST| F4[FILTER 4 - OPTIMIZATION]
    F4 -->|OPTIMIZED IR| F5[FILTER 5 - CODE GENERATION]
    F5 -->|TARGET CODE| Sink[OUTPUT FILE]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Layered Architecture (3-Tier Web App)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[LAYER 1 - PRESENTATION / UI]
    L2[LAYER 2 - BUSINESS LOGIC / APPLICATION]
    L3[LAYER 3 - DATA ACCESS / DATABASE]

    L1 -->|USER REQUEST| L2
    L2 -->|SQL QUERY| L3
    L3 -->|RESULT SET| L2
    L2 -->|RESPONSE / VIEW| L1
```

> **Strict layering rule:** L1 may call L2 but not L3 directly. L3 knows nothing about L1.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Event-Driven (Publish–Subscribe) Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Pub1[PUBLISHER - ORDER SERVICE]
    Pub2[PUBLISHER - INVENTORY SERVICE]
    Bus[EVENT BUS / MESSAGE BROKER]
    Sub1[SUBSCRIBER - EMAIL NOTIFIER]
    Sub2[SUBSCRIBER - ANALYTICS]
    Sub3[SUBSCRIBER - AUDIT LOG]
    Sub4[SUBSCRIBER - RECOMMENDATION ENGINE]

    Pub1 -->|EVENT ORDER PLACED| Bus
    Pub2 -->|EVENT STOCK CHANGED| Bus
    Bus -->|DELIVER| Sub1
    Bus -->|DELIVER| Sub2
    Bus -->|DELIVER| Sub3
    Bus -->|DELIVER| Sub4
```

> **Reading guide:** Publishers `Pub1` and `Pub2` are *completely unaware* of subscribers — this is the hallmark of decoupling.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Client–Server Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Cli1[CLIENT 1 - WEB BROWSER]
    Cli2[CLIENT 2 - MOBILE APP]

    Net[NETWORK - HTTP / TCP / TLS]
    Srv[APPLICATION SERVER - CENTRAL AUTHORITY]
    DB[(DATABASE SERVER)]

    Cli1 -->|HTTP REQUEST| Net
    Cli2 -->|HTTPS REQUEST| Net
    Cli3 -->|REST API CALL| Net
    Net --> Srv
    Srv <-->|SQL| DB
    Srv -->|RESPONSE| Net
    Net -->|HTML / JSON| Cli1
    Net -->|JSON| Cli2
    Net -->|XML| Cli3
```

## 4.6 Diagram 6 — Peer-to-Peer Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    N1[PEER 1 - HAS CHUNK A B]
    N2[PEER 2 - HAS CHUNK C D]
    N3[PEER 3 - HAS CHUNK E F]
    N4[PEER 4 - HAS CHUNK G H]
    N5[PEER 5 - WANTS ENTIRE FILE]

    N5 <-->|DOWNLOAD CHUNK A| N1
    N5 <-->|DOWNLOAD CHUNK C| N2

```

> **Reading guide:** All nodes are equal — each can *serve* and *consume* chunks. There is no central server.

## 4.7 Diagram 7 — Microservices Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
```

> **Reading guide:** Each service has its **own database** (the *database-per-service* principle) and communicates with others via either **synchronous API calls** or **asynchronous events**.

# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 🅰️ Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition Type)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between architectural style and architectural pattern.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **architectural style** is a high-level, named, reusable set of **principles and constraints** that defines a *category* of systems (e.g., Layered, Pipe-and-Filter). It describes the **vocabulary of components and connectors** without prescribing how to solve a specific problem.
>
> An **architectural pattern** is a *concrete, problem-specific* solution within a style (e.g., *Model–View–Controller* is a pattern *within* the layered style). Patterns are **prescriptive** and tied to a recurring problem; styles are **descriptive** and tied to a family of systems.
>
> In short: a **style** is a *genre*; a **pattern** is a *recipe* inside that genre.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three advantages of a layered architectural style.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Separation of concerns** — each layer has one well-defined responsibility, making the system easier to understand.
2. **Portability and replaceability** — a layer (e.g., the database layer) can be swapped without modifying others.
3. **Testability** — layers can be independently unit-tested by mocking adjacent layers.

---

## 5.2 🅱️ Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> Each Part-B question below has **two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks**. Solve BOTH sub-parts. KTU ESE allows an **internal choice** — either solve the complete **Question A** or the complete **Question B**.

---

### ✏️ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Data-Centered (Repository) architectural style** with a neat diagram. State any two advantages and two disadvantages.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Data-Centered architecture has a **central data store (repository)** that is shared by all surrounding components. Components do not communicate with each other directly; instead, they **read from and write to** the central repository. The repository is the *only* medium of inter-component data exchange.

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    Repo[REPOSITORY - SHARED DATA STORE]
    A[COMPONENT A - INPUT]
    B[COMPONENT B - PROCESSING]
    C[COMPONENT C - ANALYSIS]
    D[COMPONENT D - OUTPUT]

    A -->|WRITE| Repo
    Repo -->|READ| B
    Repo -->|READ| C
    Repo -->|READ| D
```

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- Component *A* collects and writes raw data to the central repository.
- The repository maintains a single, consistent view of the data.
- Components *B*, *C*, and *D* each read different portions of the data, perform their own transformations or analyses, and (optionally) write derived results back.
- Crucially, *B* does **not** call *C* directly; they share data only via the repository.

**Advantages (1 mark, any two):**
- **Single source of truth** — improves data consistency and integrity.
- **Easy extensibility** — new components can be plugged in by connecting them to the repository.
- **Loose coupling** between components, because they communicate *only* through the data store.

**Disadvantages (1 mark, any two):**
- **Single point of failure** — repository crash disables the entire system.
- **Performance bottleneck** — all data traffic converges at the repository.
- **Schema evolution is hard** — adding/removing data fields requires coordinated changes across all components.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare **Layered architecture** and **Client–Server architecture** in terms of structure, control flow, coupling, and suitability. Which is more suitable for an **online banking application** and why?

**Model Solution:**

| Dimension | **Layered Architecture** | **Client–Server Architecture** |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Horizontal layers; each layer calls only the one below it | Clients and one (or a cluster of) server(s) |
| **Control Flow** | Top-down request, bottom-up response | Request–response, initiated by client |
| **Coupling** | Moderate — strict layering limits dependencies | Moderate — clients depend on server API contract |
| **Data Handling** | Local to each layer (DB is the bottom layer) | Centralized on the server |
| **Best Suited For** | OS, network stacks, 3-tier web, enterprise apps | Web apps, email, online services |
| **Example** | OSI 7-layer model | Browser ↔ Apache ↔ MySQL |

**Recommendation for Online Banking (2 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **hybrid Layered + Client–Server** approach is most suitable for online banking:
> - The **Client–Server** pattern handles the distributed, request–response nature of web/mobile access.
> - The **Layered** style (Presentation → Business Logic → Data Access → Database) handles the **security, transaction, and audit requirements**.
>
> This combination gives the system both **network scalability** (via client–server) and **modifiability / security** (via layering). A pure repository style would create a security risk, and a pure P2P style would violate regulatory norms (e.g., RBI data residency).

---

### ✏️ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Pipe-and-Filter architectural style** with a neat diagram. Mention two real-world examples.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Pipe-and-Filter style arranges the system as a **linear sequence of filters** connected by **pipes**. Each filter **transforms** its input data and **forwards** the result to the next filter via a pipe. Filters are **stateless**, **independent**, and **composable**.

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[INPUT - SOURCE CODE] --> P1[PIPE] --> F1[FILTER 1 - LEXER]
    F1 --> P2[PIPE] --> F2[FILTER 2 - PARSER]
    F2 --> P3[PIPE] --> F3[FILTER 3 - SEMANTIC ANALYZER]
    F3 --> P4[PIPE] --> F4[FILTER 4 - OPTIMIZER]
    F4 --> P5[PIPE] --> F5[FILTER 5 - CODE GENERATOR]
    F5 --> SNK[OUTPUT - TARGET MACHINE CODE]
```

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- The source code enters the pipeline as a **stream of characters**.
- *Filter 1* (lexical analyzer) converts characters into **tokens** and pushes them downstream.
- *Filter 2* (parser) consumes tokens and produces a **parse tree**.
- *Filter 3* performs **semantic analysis** (type checking, scope resolution).
- *Filter 4* performs **optimization**.
- *Filter 5* emits **target code**.
- Each filter is **independent** — it does not know the existence of upstream or downstream filters.

**Real-World Examples (1 mark, any two):**
1. **Unix shell pipelines:** `cat log.txt | grep ERROR | sort | uniq -c | mail admin@x.com`
2. **GCC compiler** stages (lex → parse → optimize → codegen).
3. **Image processing in Photoshop** (each filter is a transform).

**One Advantage (1 mark):** **Composability and reusability** — filters can be added, removed, or reordered without changing neighboring stages.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** What is **Microservices architecture**? Compare it with **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)** on at least four points.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Microservices (1 mark):**
> Microservices architecture is a style that structures an application as a **suite of small, independent services**, each running in its **own process**, communicating via **lightweight mechanisms** (HTTP/REST, gRPC, or message queues), and **independently deployable** by **automated CI/CD pipelines**. Each service is **organized around a business capability** and **owns its own database** (*database-per-service*).

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| # | Dimension | **SOA** | **Microservices** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Service Granularity** | Coarse-grained, enterprise-wide | Fine-grained, single-responsibility |
| 2 | **Communication** | Heavyweight (SOAP, WSDL, ESB) | Lightweight (REST, gRPC) |
| 3 | **Data Sharing** | Common shared database(s) | Database-per-service |
| 4 | **Deployment** | Monolithic ESB + services | Containerized, independent deployment |
| 5 | **Coupling** | Contract-coupled via ESB | Loosely coupled via APIs |
| 6 | **Governance** | Centralized standards | Decentralized, "you build it you run it" |
| 7 | **Example** | Banking legacy integration | Netflix, Uber, Amazon |

**Justification (1 mark):**
> Microservices evolved from SOA to address its weaknesses — ESB bottlenecks, shared-database coupling, and slow deployment cycles. Microservices are **leaner, faster, and cloud-native** by design.

**Suitability (1 mark):**
> SOA suits **large enterprises with legacy systems** needing integration. Microservices suit **cloud-native, fast-moving startups** like Netflix or Uber.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 reasons students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Drawing a "client–server" diagram for every style** — Examiners want the *correct* style-specific diagram. A repository style must show a **central store**, not a server.
> 2. **Conflating style with pattern** — MVC is a *pattern*, not a style. Calling it a style loses the CO-mapping marks.
> 3. **Omitting "Why" justification** — "I would use Client–Server for banking" without a quality-attribute rationale scores 0/2 in justification.
> 4. **Forgetting the data model in Repository style** — You must mention that **all components must agree on the data schema**.
> 5. **Not mentioning real-world examples** — Examiners award easy marks for naming one or two real systems (e.g., GCC for pipe-and-filter, Kafka for event-driven).

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision bullets** — read these 30 minutes before the exam.

- [x] **Architectural Style =** name + vocabulary (components + connectors) + constraints + semantics (Shaw & Garlan, 1996).
- [x] **Five families:** Data-Centered, Data-Flow, Call-and-Return, Independent-Components, Distributed (Client–Server, P2P, SOA, Microservices, Pub-Sub).
- [x] **Repository / Data-Centered** → central shared store; **examples:** Blackboard AI, IDEs.
- [x] **Pipe-and-Filter** → linear, stateless, composable; **examples:** Unix shell, GCC.
- [x] **Batch Sequential** → strict blocking, whole-data transfer; **example:** payroll EOD.
- [x] **Layered** → strict partial order; **example:** OSI, 3-tier web.
- [x] **Object-Oriented** → encapsulation + polymorphism; **example:** Java Swing.
- [x] **Communicating Processes** → message-passing, concurrent; **example:** Erlang.
- [x] **Event-Driven (Pub-Sub)** → async, decoupled; **examples:** Kafka, Node.js.
- [x] **Client–Server** → request–response, central server; **example:** Web.
- [x] **P2P** → symmetric roles, no central authority; **examples:** BitTorrent, Bitcoin.
- [x] **SOA** → coarse-grained services, ESB-mediated; legacy enterprise.
- [x] **Microservices** → fine-grained, independent deployment, database-per-service; **examples:** Netflix, Uber.
- [x] **Quality attributes controlled by styles:** Modifiability, Performance, Reusability, Reliability, Maintainability.
- [x] **Always justify** your style choice using **quality attributes**, not personal preference.
- [x] **Always include a diagram** in 14-mark answers — it is worth 2 marks by itself.
- [x] **Always give real-world examples** — easy 1-mark bonus.
- [x] **Style vs Pattern:** Style is a *genre*; Pattern is a *recipe inside the genre* (e.g., MVC is a *pattern*, not a style).
- [x] **Strict layering rule:** A layer $L_j$ may only call $L_i$ if $i < j$.
- [x] **Database-per-service** is the *cardinal rule* of Microservices.
- [x] **Publishers don't know subscribers** is the *cardinal rule* of Pub-Sub.
- [x] **Filters are stateless** is the *cardinal rule* of Pipe-and-Filter.
- [x] **Server is the single point of failure** in Client–Server — always mention replication / load balancing.
- [x] **P2P has no SPOF** — but discovery and security are challenges.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architectural Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Architectural Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Architecture** is the **fundamental organization of a system**, embodied in its components, their **relationships to each other and to the environment**, and the **principles governing its design and evolution** (IEEE 1471 / ISO/IEC 42010 aligned).

It is the set of **significant design decisions** that shape the system's structure and behavior — the things that are **hard to change** once committed.

**Key architectural elements:**

- **Components** — the primary computational units and data stores.
- **Connectors** — the mechanisms that mediate communication, coordination, and data transfer between components.
- **Configurations** — the topological arrangement of components and connectors.
- **Constraints** — the design rules governing component evolution.
- **Quality Attribute Requirements (QARs)** — non-functional properties the architecture must satisfy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Architecture is the Blueprint, Not the Furniture"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — The Building Analogy**
> 
> Think of constructing a *multi-storey hospital* in Kerala. Before the engineer pours a single column, the **architect** decides: How many floors? Where are the lifts? Is the OT (Operation Theatre) on a vibration-isolated floor? Will the building survive a flood? Is the wiring ready for MRI machines?
> 
> These are decisions **so fundamental** that changing them later is catastrophically expensive.
> 
> **Software Architecture plays the exact same role:**
> - It is decided *early*, when requirements are still flexible.
> - It defines the **load-bearing walls** of the system (the components that everything else depends on).
> - It must satisfy *non-functional* needs (security, performance, scalability) just like a building must satisfy earthquake-resistance and fire-safety codes.

## 1.3 Why "Architectural Considerations" Matter (KTU 2024 OBE Anchor)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE), students must understand that **most catastrophic project failures** (e.g., cost overrun, performance collapse, security breach, impossible-to-maintain code) trace their root cause to **poor architectural decisions made in early phases**.
> 
> Software Engineering research (Brooks, Bass-Clements-Kazman) confirms that the **architecture is the primary carrier of quality attributes** — change the architecture and you change the system's DNA.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Architectural Considerations

1. **Structural Considerations** — *How is the system decomposed and connected?*
2. **Quality-Attribute Considerations** — *How does it perform, scale, secure, evolve?*
3. **Cross-Cutting / Non-Functional Considerations** — *Security, deployment, internationalization, accessibility.*

These three pillars are the **KTU board examiner's favorite lens** for framing 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Styles — The "Pattern Catalog" of System Structure

An **architectural style** is a named collection of design decisions that (a) can be applied in many contexts, (b) constrains the vocabulary of designs, and (c) yields beneficial qualities in each resulting system (Shaw & Garlan).

### 2.1.1 Classification of Major Styles

**A. Data-Centered (Repository) Style**
- A central data store (repository) is accessed by multiple independent components.
- Components interact *only* through the shared store.
- *Benefit:* data integration is trivial; plug-and-play components.
- *Risk:* single point of failure, schema lock-in.

**B. Data-Flow Style (Pipe-and-Filter)**
- Data flows through a chain of independent filters via pipes.
- Each filter transforms input to output without state.
- *Example:* Unix `cat file | grep "error" | sort | uniq`.
- *Benefit:* composability, parallelism, reusability.
- *Risk:* state-sharing and interactive UIs are awkward.

**C. Call-and-Return Style**
- Subprogram invocation hierarchy.
- Sub-styles: **Main-program-and-subroutine**, **Object-Oriented**, **Layered**.
- *Layered Style* is the most common in industry: Presentation → Business Logic → Data Access → Database.

**D. Independent-Components Style**
- Components communicate via **message-passing** (e.g., microservices, event-driven).
- *Sub-styles:* Client-Server, Publish-Subscribe, Peer-to-Peer.
- *Benefit:* deployment flexibility, scalability.

**E. Mobile-Code Style**
- Code travels across the network (e.g., applets, mobile agents, serverless lambdas).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** Mixed (hybrid) styles are most common in real systems. A modern web app is often **Layered + Client-Server + Pipe-Filter (event bus)** simultaneously. Examiners love asking you to *identify* the style and *justify* the choice against quality attributes.

## 2.2 Quality Attributes — The "Tasting Notes" of Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bass-Clements-Kazman** defined six core quality attributes that KTU explicitly tests:
> 
> 1. **Availability** — proportion of time the system is operational.
> 2. **Modifiability** — ease and cost of making changes.
> 3. **Performance** — response time and throughput under load.
> 4. **Security** — resistance to unauthorized use.
> 5. **Testability** — ease of demonstrating faults under test.
> 6. **Usability** — ease of human use.

### 2.2.1 Availability — Detailed Treatment (14-Mark Favorite)

**Definitions (KTU board-standard):**

- **Reliability $R(t)$:** Probability that the system performs its intended function under stated conditions up to time $t$.
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF):** Expected time from *start of operation* until *first failure*.
- **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR):** Expected time to restore service after a failure.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Expected elapsed time between two consecutive failures.

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \quad \lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTTF}}$$

> Where $\lambda$ is the **failure rate** (failures per unit time), assuming an exponential distribution of inter-failure times.

**Availability (steady-state, single component):**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{\text{MTBF} - \text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF}}$$

> Often expressed as a percentage; the famous "**five nines**" target = **99.999%**.

**Availability for Series Composition** (all components must work):

$$A_{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$$

**Availability for Parallel Composition** (at least one must work, $n$ identical components):

$$A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - A_{\text{single}})^n$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reliability (Exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ = failures per hour |
| 2 | Failure rate from MTTF | $\lambda = 1 / \text{MTTF}$ | hours⁻¹ |
| 3 | MTBF relation | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | hours |
| 4 | Steady-state availability | $A = \text{MTTF} / (\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR})$ | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | Series availability | $A_s = \prod A_i$ | worsens with more components |
| 6 | Parallel availability (n identical) | $A_p = 1 - (1 - A_{\text{single}})^n$ | improves with redundancy |
| 7 | Throughput (Little's Law) | $L = \lambda \cdot W$ | $L$=items in system, $\lambda$=arrival rate, $W$=residence time |
| 8 | Performance index | $\text{ResponseTime} = \text{ServiceTime} + \text{QueueTime} + \text{TransmissionTime}$ | For distributed systems |
| 9 | Modifiability cost | $\text{Cost}_{\text{change}} = \sum_{i} (\text{Scope}_i \cdot \text{Effort}_i \cdot \text{Risk}_i)$ | Architectural-tactic weighting |
| 10 | Five-9s availability target | $A \geq 0.99999 \Rightarrow$ downtime $\leq$ **5.26 min/year** | KTU favourite numerical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** In the table above, never write the absolute value symbol `|x|` in markdown tables — KTU uses `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX display mode instead. Many students lose formatting marks because raw `|` breaks markdown.

## 2.4 Architectural Tactics — The "Design Moves" for Each Quality

> [!NOTE]
> A **tactic** is a single, atomic architectural design decision that improves a specific quality attribute. Tactics are the *building blocks* of architectural patterns.

**Selected Tactics Catalog (must-memorize for KTU 14-mark questions):**

- **Availability tactics:** redundancy (active/active, active/passive), failover, hot/cold spares, transaction rollback, state resynchronization.
- **Performance tactics:** caching, replication, lazy loading, priority scheduling, batching.
- **Modifiability tactics:** abstraction, encapsulation, indirection, late binding, dependency injection, plug-in architecture.
- **Security tactics:** authentication, authorization, encryption, audit logging, input validation, sandboxing.
- **Testability tactics:** separation of concerns, dependency injection, contract-based interfaces, record/playback.

## 2.5 Cross-Cutting Architectural Considerations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Beyond QARs, KTU 2024 explicitly tests these **non-technical / cross-cutting** considerations:
> 
> - **Economic** — cost of development, licensing, third-party SaaS.
> - **Schedule** — does the architecture fit the delivery timeline?
> - **Team skills** — does the team know the chosen stack?
> - **Regulatory / Legal** — GDPR, RBI data-localization, Indian IT Act 2000/2008.
> - **Deployment & Operational** — on-premise vs cloud, container vs VM, DevOps maturity.
> - **Internationalization (i18n) & Localization (l10n)** — multi-language, multi-currency support.
> - **Ethical & Sustainability** — energy consumption, accessibility (WCAG 2.1).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Architectural decisions directly drive:
- **Cloud-bill cost** in AWS/Azure (over-provisioned microservices = overspend).
- **Mobile-app battery life** (poor caching = radio wakeups = battery drain).
- **Banking-system downtime penalties** (RBI mandates **99.95%** uptime for payment gateways).
- **Healthcare-system reliability** (an unavailable HIS = risk to life).

This is why every software engineer in industry — not just architects — must master architectural thinking.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reliability of a Single Component

**Statement:** If a component has a constant failure rate $\lambda$, the probability that it survives up to time $t$ without failure is given by the exponential reliability function.

**Step 1 — Probability of no failure in an infinitesimal interval $\Delta t$:**

$$P(\text{no failure in } \Delta t) \approx 1 - \lambda \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 2 — Divide the total time $t$ into $n = t / \Delta t$ sub-intervals. The events are (approximately) independent, so:**

$$P(\text{no failure in } t) = \left( 1 - \lambda \cdot \frac{t}{n} \right)^{n}$$

**Step 3 — Take the limit as $\Delta t \to 0$, i.e., $n \to \infty$. Apply the standard limit $\lim_{n\to\infty} (1 + x/n)^n = e^{x}$:**

$$R(t) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left( 1 - \frac{\lambda t}{n} \right)^{n} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\lambda = 1 / \text{MTTF}$ to obtain the practitioner form:**

$$R(t) = e^{-t / \text{MTTF}}$$

**Engineering insight:** This means the **mean life** of the component is exactly MTTF, and reliability *always* decays exponentially under the constant-failure-rate assumption (the **"useful-life" plateau** of the bathtub curve).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Availability from First Principles

**Statement:** Show that the long-run fraction of time a repairable system is up equals MTTF / (MTTF + MTTR).

**Step 1 — Over a long operating period $T$, the system alternates between *uptime* and *downtime*:**

$$T_{\text{up}} \approx n_{\text{failures}} \cdot \text{MTTF}, \quad T_{\text{down}} \approx n_{\text{failures}} \cdot \text{MTTR}$$

**Step 2 — Define availability as the ratio of total uptime to total time:**

$$A = \frac{T_{\text{up}}}{T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}} = \frac{n_{\text{failures}} \cdot \text{MTTF}}{n_{\text{failures}} \cdot \text{MTTF} + n_{\text{failures}} \cdot \text{MTTR}}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the common factor $n_{\text{failures}}$:**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to MTBF form using $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$:**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF} - \text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF}} = 1 - \frac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** To improve availability, you can either **(a) increase MTTF** (better components, fault-tolerance tactics) or **(b) decrease MTTR** (faster diagnostics, hot-swap, automated failover). For five-9s targets, MTTR reduction is almost always cheaper than MTTF improvement.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Parallel Availability for $n$ Identical Components

**Step 1 — Each component is *up* with probability $A_{\text{single}}$ and *down* with probability $(1 - A_{\text{single}})$.**

**Step 2 — The parallel system is *down* only if *all* $n$ components are simultaneously down. Assuming independence:**

$$P(\text{system down}) = (1 - A_{\text{single}})^{n}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the complement rule:**

$$A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - A_{\text{single}})^{n}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical sanity check:** If $A_{\text{single}} = 0.99$ and $n = 2$:

$$A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (0.01)^{2} = 1 - 0.0001 = 0.9999$$

This is the **"two 99% in parallel give 99.99%"** rule — the most cited result in availability engineering.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

> **Problem:** A web application has three components in a request path: a load balancer ($A_1 = 0.999$), a business-logic server ($A_2 = 0.995$), and a database ($A_3 = 0.99$). The business-logic server is now replicated in parallel with one additional instance (identical $A_2 = 0.995$). Compute the new end-to-end availability of the request path.

**Step 1 — New availability of the parallel pair (business-logic + replica):**

$$A_{2,\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - 0.995)^{2} = 1 - (0.005)^{2} = 1 - 0.000025 = 0.999975$$

**Step 2 — Series combination with the load balancer and database:**

$$A_{\text{total}} = A_1 \times A_{2,\text{parallel}} \times A_3 = 0.999 \times 0.999975 \times 0.99$$

**Step 3 — Multiply step by step:**

$$0.999 \times 0.999975 = 0.998975025$$

$$0.998975025 \times 0.99 = 0.988985275$$

**Step 4 — Express as a percentage and round:**

$$A_{\text{total}} \approx 0.98899 \approx 98.90\%$$

**Step 5 — Comparison to original (no replication):**

$$A_{\text{original}} = 0.999 \times 0.995 \times 0.99 = 0.98410 \approx 98.41\%$$

> **Conclusion:** Adding a single redundant business-logic replica improved overall availability from **98.41% → 98.90%**, a tangible gain. To reach five-9s, we would also need to replicate the database (e.g., primary-replica with automated failover).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — "Which Style Best Satisfies Which Quality?"

| Architectural Style | Performance | Modifiability | Availability | Security | Testability | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Layered** | Medium | High | Medium | Medium | High | Enterprise apps, ERP |
| **Pipe-and-Filter** | High | High | Medium | Low | High | ETL, compilers, video |
| **Client-Server** | Medium | Medium | Medium | Medium | Medium | Web apps, banking |
| **Microservices** | High | Very High | High | Medium | Medium | Netflix, Amazon, Uber |
| **Event-Driven (Pub/Sub)** | High | High | High | Low | Medium | IoT, real-time analytics |
| **Repository (Data-Centered)** | Low | Low | Low | High | Low | Legacy integration systems |
| **MVC** | Medium | Very High | Medium | Medium | High | Web frameworks (Spring, Rails) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the **single most powerful reference table** for KTU 14-mark answers. The examiner's question often reduces to: *"Pick a style and justify it across at least three quality attributes."* Use this matrix as your cheat-sheet.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Architectural Decision Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Business Requirements] --> B0[Identify Quality Attribute Requirements]
    B0 --> C0[Define Architectural Drivers]
    C0 --> D0[Select Candidate Architectural Style]
    D0 --> E0[Apply Tactics for Each QAR]
    E0 --> F0[Construct Architectural Views]
    F0 --> G0[Evaluate via ATAM or SAAM]
    G0 --> H0{Acceptable?}
    H0 -- No --> I0[Refine Style / Reapply Tactics]
    I0 --> D0
    H0 -- Yes --> J0[Document Architecture Baseline]
    J0 --> K0[Communicate to Stakeholders]
    K0 --> L0[End: Architecture Locked]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Layered Architecture (Classical N-Tier)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UI[Presentation Layer - UI, View, Controller] --> BL[Business Logic Layer - Services, Domain Rules]
    BL --> DAL[Data Access Layer - Repositories, DAOs]
    DAL --> DB[(Database - RDBMS, NoSQL)]
    BL -.-> SEC[Cross-Cutting: Security, Logging, Caching]
    UI -.-> SEC
    DAL -.-> SEC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU note:** In Mermaid, node IDs are purely alphanumeric. Labels are plain uppercase text — no markdown bold (`**`) is permitted inside double-quoted labels. Special characters like hyphens are allowed, but reserved words like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style` cannot be used as node IDs.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Microservices Architecture (Independent-Components Style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CL[Client Web or Mobile] --> GW[API Gateway]
    GW --> US[User Service]
    GW --> OS[Order Service]
    GW --> PS[Payment Service]
    GW --> IS[Inventory Service]
    US --> MB[(Message Bus - Kafka)]
    OS --> MB
    PS --> DB1[(Payments DB)]
    OS --> DB2[(Orders DB)]
    IS --> DB3[(Inventory DB)]
    MB --> NS[Notification Service]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Quality-Attribute Trade-off Radar (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Performance] -- tension --> B[Security]
    A -- tension --> C[Modifiability]
    B -- tension --> D[Usability]
    C -- supports --> E[Testability]
    D -- supports --> F[Availability]
    E -- supports --> F
    F -- tension --> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** The arrows labelled "tension" indicate that improving one quality *can* degrade another. For example, stronger encryption (security) lowers performance. The architect's job is to *find the Pareto-optimal balance* for the business context.

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — ATAM Evaluation Method Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Phase 1: Present the Architecture] --> S1[Phase 2: Present Business Drivers]
    S1 --> S2[Phase 3: Present Architectural Approaches]
    S2 --> S3[Phase 4: Generate Quality-Attribute Utility Tree]
    S3 --> S4[Phase 5: Analyze Architectural Approaches]
    S4 --> S5[Phase 6: Brainstorm Scenarios]
    S5 --> S6[Phase 7: Analyze Scenarios]
    S6 --> S7[Phase 8: Present Results]
    S7 --> T0[Trade-off Points and Risks Documented]
```

## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture Map — Cross-Cutting Concerns

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CORE[Core Functional Components]
      C1[Authentication Module]
      C2[Authorization Module]
      C3[Business Workflow Engine]
      C4[Data Persistence Service]
      C5[External API Adapter]
    end
    subgraph CROSS[Cross-Cutting Concerns]
      X1[Logging and Monitoring]
      X2[Error Handling and Retry]
      X3[Caching and Performance]
      X4[Internationalization i18n]
      X5[Configuration Management]
    end
    C1 --- X1
    C2 --- X1
    C3 --- X1
    C3 --- X2
    C3 --- X3
    C4 --- X3
    C5 --- X2
    C1 --- X4
    C3 --- X5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between an architectural style and an architectural pattern. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (board-key aligned):**

- **Architectural Style:** A *named, broad* collection of design decisions that constrain the vocabulary of designs across many contexts. It is a *macro-level* template describing component types, connector types, and their topological constraints. *Example:* **Layered Style**, **Pipe-and-Filter Style**, **Client-Server Style**.
- **Architectural Pattern:** A *specific, reusable* solution to a recurring design problem *within* a given context. Patterns are finer-grained than styles and often idiomatic to a particular style. *Example:* **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** is a pattern used within the Layered style; **Microkernel** is a pattern within the Independent-Components style.

> *Key differentiator:* A style is a *family*; a pattern is a *member with detailed recipe*.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define availability and MTTF. A server has MTTF = 800 hours and MTTR = 2 hours. Compute its steady-state availability.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Availability $A$:** The probability that a system is operational and able to deliver its intended service at a given instant, often expressed as a long-run fraction of uptime.
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** The expected time the system operates continuously before the first failure occurs.

**Computation:**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{800}{800 + 2} = \frac{800}{802} \approx 0.99751$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 99.751\%}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the major architectural styles in software engineering with suitable diagrams. List two advantages and one disadvantage of each.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Layered Style** — Components are organized in horizontal layers; each layer calls only the layer directly below. *Advantages:* separation of concerns, modifiability, testability. *Disadvantage:* request must traverse all layers, leading to performance overhead.

2. **Pipe-and-Filter Style** — Data flows as a stream through independent filters. *Advantages:* composability, parallelism, easy to add new filters. *Disadvantage:* state-sharing and interactive user interfaces are awkward.

3. **Client-Server Style** — Clients send requests to servers that provide services. *Advantages:* centralized data management, scalability. *Disadvantage:* server is a single point of failure and a scalability bottleneck.

4. **Repository (Data-Centered) Style** — A central data store is shared by all components. *Advantages:* data integration is easy, plug-and-play components. *Disadvantage:* schema changes ripple across all components.

5. **Publish-Subscribe (Event-Driven) Style** — Publishers emit events to a bus; subscribers react asynchronously. *Advantages:* loose coupling, high scalability. *Disadvantage:* debugging and tracing events is complex.

*(Examiner will accept 3–4 well-explained styles with diagrams from Section 4 above.)*

**(b) Discuss the quality attributes of software architecture. For a chosen real-world system (e.g., a banking application), justify how the layered + microservices hybrid style satisfies availability, modifiability, and security.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Quality attributes of software architecture** are the non-functional properties that the architecture must realize. The six primary attributes (Bass-Clements-Kazman) are:
**Availability, Modifiability, Performance, Security, Testability, Usability.**

**Hybrid Style Justification — Banking Application:**

- **Availability:** Each microservice (e.g., payments, account, KYC) is deployed with **active-active redundancy** behind an API gateway. Failover tactics (health-checks, circuit-breakers like Hystrix/Resilience4j) ensure the system remains available even when an instance crashes. Database uses **primary-replica replication** with automatic failover.

- **Modifiability:** New features (e.g., UPI payments) are added as **new microservices** without touching existing services. The **layered style** inside each service (Controller → Service → Repository) keeps internal code organized. Tactics: *encapsulation*, *interface segregation*, *dependency injection*.

- **Security:** The API gateway enforces **OAuth 2.0 / JWT-based authentication** and **role-based authorization**. All inter-service calls use **mTLS**. Sensitive PII is **encrypted at rest (AES-256)** and **in transit (TLS 1.3)**. Cross-cutting security (input validation, audit logging) is implemented as filters in the layered stack.

**Conclusion:** The hybrid layered-microservices style is a deliberate Pareto-optimal choice that maximizes modifiability and availability while still meeting banking-grade security baselines.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the formula for steady-state availability of a repairable system. Show that for two identical servers with single-component availability 0.99, parallel composition gives 0.9999.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Express total uptime and downtime over a long horizon $T$:**

$$T_{\text{up}} = n \cdot \text{MTTF}, \quad T_{\text{down}} = n \cdot \text{MTTR}$$

**Step 2 — Define availability as the fraction of time the system is up:**

$$A = \frac{T_{\text{up}}}{T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and cancel $n$:**

$$A = \frac{n \cdot \text{MTTF}}{n \cdot \text{MTTF} + n \cdot \text{MTTR}} = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Step 4 — Parallel formula for $n$ identical components:**

$$A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - A_{\text{single}})^{n}$$

**Numerical verification for two identical servers with $A_{\text{single}} = 0.99$:**

$$A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - 0.99)^{2} = 1 - (0.01)^{2} = 1 - 0.0001 = 0.9999$$

**Step 5 — Converting to downtime per year** (assumed 365 × 24 = 8760 hours):

$$\text{Downtime}_{\text{year}} = (1 - A) \times 8760$$

For $A = 0.99$:

$$\text{Downtime} = 0.01 \times 8760 = 87.6 \text{ hours/year}$$

For $A_{\text{parallel}} = 0.9999$:

$$\text{Downtime} = 0.0001 \times 8760 = 0.876 \text{ hours} = 52.56 \text{ minutes/year}$$

Thus redundancy reduced downtime **100-fold** — a one-step jump in the "nines" ladder.

**(b) Explain the ATAM (Architecture Trade-off Analysis Method). How does it help in identifying architectural risks, non-risks, sensitivity points, and trade-off points?** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**ATAM** is a scenario-based architectural evaluation method developed by the **Software Engineering Institute (SEI)** at Carnegie Mellon. It is used to assess whether an architecture satisfies its quality-attribute requirements *before* significant resources are committed to construction.

**Phases of ATAM (refer to Section 4.5 diagram):**

1. **Present the architecture** — the lead architect explains the design.
2. **Present business drivers** — stakeholders articulate business goals.
3. **Present architectural approaches** — patterns, styles, tactics used.
4. **Generate the quality-attribute utility tree** — a hierarchical breakdown of business goals → quality attributes → scenarios → priorities.
5. **Analyze the architectural approaches** — for each high-priority scenario, identify which tactics handle it.
6. **Brainstorm and prioritize scenarios** — with all stakeholders.
7. **Analyze scenarios** — walk through the architecture's response to each.
8. **Present results** — formal report.

**Four Key Outcomes:**

- **Architectural Risks:** Decisions that *may jeopardize* a quality-attribute goal. *Example:* "Using a single SQL server is a risk to availability."
- **Non-Risks:** Decisions that *safely satisfy* a quality-attribute goal. *Example:* "The use of OAuth 2.0 is a non-risk for authentication security."
- **Sensitivity Points:** A single architectural parameter whose variation *significantly affects* a quality attribute. *Example:* "Doubling cache size improves response time by 40% — performance is sensitive to cache size."
- **Trade-off Points:** Parameters that affect *more than one* quality attribute, where improving one harms another. *Example:* "Increasing encryption key length improves security but degrades performance by 18%."

> **Why ATAM matters:** It provides a *systematic, stakeholder-driven* way to expose hidden design flaws *before* code is written, when the cost of change is lowest.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (read before writing the exam):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Style vs Pattern** — A Layered Style is NOT a pattern; MVC IS a pattern within it. Mixing them loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units in availability calculations** — Always state MTTF, MTTR, MTBF in **hours** and downtime in **minutes/year** for the five-9s target.
> 3. **Drawing a non-Mermaid ASCII architecture diagram** — KTU 2024 expects a *labelled block diagram* (Layer / Component / Connector). Bare boxes with arrows lose clarity marks.
> 4. **Skipping the *justification*** — Simply naming a style is worth 1 mark; you must link it to **at least two quality attributes** to score 7.
> 5. **Writing `\|x\|` inside a markdown table** — Use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX, otherwise the table breaks during exam-paper compilation.
> 6. **Missing cross-cutting concerns** — A complete architectural answer MUST include security, deployment, and at least one non-functional concern.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Software Architecture** = components + connectors + configurations + constraints + QARs (IEEE 1471).
- [x] **Five Pillars of Style:** Data-Centered, Data-Flow (Pipe-Filter), Call-and-Return (Layered/OOP), Independent-Components (Client-Server, Pub/Sub), Mobile-Code.
- [x] **Six Core Quality Attributes:** Availability, Modifiability, Performance, Security, Testability, Usability.
- [x] **Tactics are the atomic design moves** that improve one specific quality attribute.
- [x] **Reliability equation:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-t / \text{MTTF}}$.
- [x] **Availability equation:** $A = \text{MTTF} / (\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR})$.
- [x] **Series availability multiplies; parallel availability uses complement rule.**
- [x] **Two 99%-available servers in parallel → 99.99% available** (100× downtime reduction).
- [x] **ATAM** identifies **Risks, Non-Risks, Sensitivity Points, Trade-off Points**.
- [x] **Architectural views** = Logical, Process, Physical, Development (Kruchten 4+1).
- [x] **Cross-cutting concerns** (security, logging, caching, i18n) cut *across* all layers.
- [x] **Five-9s target** = **99.999% availability** = at most **5.26 minutes downtime per year**.
- [x] **Trade-offs are unavoidable** — every architectural decision favours some qualities at the cost of others.
- [x] **Mermaid node IDs must be alphanumeric**, not reserved words; labels must not contain markdown bold/italics inside double-quotes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Architecture is the set of decisions that are hard to change. Choose them with eyes wide open, justify them with quality attributes, and evaluate them with ATAM."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architectural Design. Component Level Design - What is a Component
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Architectural Design. Component Level Design - What is a Component

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Architectural Design – The Formal Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Software Engineering syllabus, **Architectural Design** is positioned as the *bridge between requirements engineering and detailed design*. It establishes the high-level structure of the software system — the set of **modules**, the **interactions among them**, the **properties of those interactions**, and the **data structures** that govern the construction of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> *Architectural design represents the structure of the system, the set of components (modules) that make up the system, the responsibilities of each component, the way components interact and collaborate, and the configuration of the system in terms of architectural styles and patterns.*

Architectural design is **NOT** the same as detailed design. It addresses the *macroscopic* view: **What are the major building blocks? How do they connect? Which style (client–server, layered, microservices, pipe-and-filter) governs their interaction?** The *microscopic* view — the internal logic of each building block — is delegated to **Component-Level Design**.

---

## 1.2 Component-Level Design – What is a Component?

The transition from architectural to component-level design forces a critical question: *What exactly is a "component"?* The KTU syllabus, drawing on canonical software engineering literature (Pressman, Sommerville), defines a component as follows:

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition of a Software Component:**
> A **software component** is a **modular, deployable, replaceable, and reusable** building block of a software system that **encapsulates** its internal implementation and **exposes a well-defined set of interfaces** through which all interactions with the outside world occur.
> A component is *not* a single function or procedure. It is a *cohesive collection* of classes, subroutines, source files, or even run-time entities that together fulfil a *clearly stated responsibility* within the architecture.

In the Unified Modeling Language (UML), the term **"component"** is intentionally a *black-box abstraction*. Its internals are hidden; only its **ports** and **interfaces** are visible. The architectural contract demands that a component be substitutable with any other component that honours the same interface — a property called **substitutability**.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The Smart Home

To make the abstraction concrete, consider a **smart home automation system**. The home contains numerous devices, each supplied by a different manufacturer:

- A **Nest thermostat** (Component A)
- A **Philips Hue light controller** (Component B)
- A **Ring doorbell** (Component C)
- A **Sonos speaker** (Component D)

Each device is a **component**. Internally, the thermostat has temperature sensors, a PID algorithm, and a tiny OS — *we do not know or care about them*. What matters is its **interface**: it accepts commands like `setTemperature(20°C)`, `getCurrentTemp()`, and publishes events like `temperatureChanged`. The Philips Hue component does not know *how* the Nest computes temperature; it merely subscribes to that event.

The home automation **hub** (the system) wires these components together using their **standard interfaces** (Wi-Fi, MQTT, vendor-neutral APIs). If the user replaces the Nest with a Honeywell T6, the system continues to work — because both honour the same *interface contract*. This is the essence of component-based software engineering.

| Smart-Home Element | Software Component Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The device itself (thermostat) | The component (encapsulated implementation) |
| The exposed buttons / API | **Provided Interface** |
| The power socket it plugs into | **Required Interface** |
| The MQTT protocol | The **component model** / middleware |
| Swapping Nest with Honeywell | **Substitutability** |
| Hub wiring | **Component composition** at architectural level |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Whenever a question asks *"What is a component?"*, examiners expect the answer to highlight **three inseparable properties**: (1) **Encapsulation** (internals hidden), (2) **Interface-based interaction** (no direct calls into internals), and (3) **Replaceability/Substitutability** (a component can be swapped with another conforming to the same interface). Missing any one of these will cost you marks.

---

## 1.4 Component vs. Object – A Subtle but Critical Distinction

Beginners frequently conflate *component* with *object*. KTU examiners deliberately test this confusion.

| Dimension | Object | Component |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Fine-grained (one class instance) | Coarse-grained (group of classes, package, module, or service) |
| Identity | Has a unique object identity at run-time | Identity is less relevant; components are often **stateless** |
| Coupling mechanism | Direct method calls / references | **Interface-based**, message-passing, or service calls |
| Deployment unit | Usually a single executable artifact | Independently deployable unit (JAR, DLL, microservice, EJB) |
| Reuse scope | Reuse within a system | Reuse **across systems** (third-party libraries, COTS) |
| Lifetime | Tied to object lifetime | Independent, long-lived, versioned |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing *"A component is just a class"* will fetch **zero marks** in the KTU valuation. Always emphasise the **coarser granularity** and the **independent deployability** of components.

---

## 1.5 Why Component-Level Design Matters in KTU Module 2

The KTU 2024 syllabus places Component-Level Design as the immediate successor to Architectural Design because:

1. **Architectural design** answers *what are the major building blocks?*
2. **Component-level design** answers *what is each block, what does it expose, and what does it consume?*
3. Together they form **CO2 (Design)** in the KTU Course Outcome mapping under Bloom's *Apply* and *Analyse* levels.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Properties of a Software Component – A Structured Breakdown

A component is characterised by **six orthogonal properties**. Mastering these is non-negotiable for the KTU 2024 ESE.

### 2.1.1 Encapsulation
A component hides its **internal state**, **algorithms**, and **data structures** behind its interface. External entities cannot reach into the internals; they can only invoke exposed operations. This is the **information-hiding** principle of Parnas realised at the component granularity.

- **Why?** Reduces ripple effects of change, enables independent evolution.
- **How?** Through well-defined interfaces, access modifiers, and contract-based programming.

### 2.1.2 Well-Defined Interfaces
Every component exposes a **contract** — a set of operations with pre-conditions, post-conditions, and invariants. Interfaces are categorised as:

- **Provided Interface:** Operations the component offers to the *outside world* (a.k.a. *lollipop* in UML notation).
- **Required Interface:** Operations the component *needs* from other components to function (a.k.a. *socket* in UML notation).

A component's interface is its *identity card*. It is the only thing visible to clients.

### 2.1.3 Independently Deployable
A component is a self-contained deployment unit. In Java it may be a **JAR/WAR/EAR**, in .NET a **DLL/assembly**, in Linux a **shared object (.so)** or **container image**, in service-oriented architectures a **Docker image of a microservice**. This property enables **continuous deployment** and **independent scaling**.

### 2.1.4 Reusability
A component designed once should be deployable in *multiple systems* with *minimal or no modification*. This is the holy grail of software engineering economics — the **"build once, use many"** paradigm.

### 2.1.5 Replaceability (Substitutability)
If Component $C_1$ and Component $C_2$ both implement the same interface $I$, then $C_2$ can replace $C_1$ in the architecture **without any modification** to the surrounding components. This is the **Liskov Substitution Principle** applied at the component level.

### 2.1.6 Composability
Components can be **assembled** into larger structures. A composite component contains other components; externally it appears as a single unit. This recursive nature allows arbitrarily complex systems to be built from simple building blocks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "E-I-D-R-R-C":** *Encapsulation, Interfaces, Deployable, Reusability, Replaceability, Composability.* Memorise this sequence for the 3-mark definition questions.

---

## 2.2 Component Models in Industry
