A **component model** is the *standard* (a set of rules, services, and conventions) that governs how components are defined, packaged, deployed, and composed. The KTU syllabus expects awareness of the major models.

| Component Model | Vendor / Standard | Notable Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **EJB (Enterprise JavaBeans)** | Sun/Oracle | Server-side business components in J2EE |
| **COM / DCOM / COM+** | Microsoft | Windows desktop and server components |
| **.NET Assemblies** | Microsoft | .NET Framework / .NET Core components |
| **JavaBeans** | Sun | Reusable visual and non-visual Java components |
| **CORBA (CCM)** | OMG | Cross-platform, cross-language distributed components |
| **OSGi Bundles** | Eclipse Foundation | Modular Java runtime components |
| **Web Services (SOAP / REST)** | W3C / OASIS | Service-oriented components over the network |
| **Microservices** | Industry trend | Independently deployable services as components |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet — Component Properties & Design Principles

The following table consolidates the *decision criteria* and *design principles* for component identification and design.

> **LaTeX Tip for Table Cells:** Vertical bars $\vert$ are used for absolute value and set notation. In this sheet, all set/cardinality operations use `\vert` to avoid markdown conflicts.

| **Property / Principle** | **Formal Statement** | **Design Implication** | **KTU Marker Weight** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Encapsulation** | All internals hidden behind $I_{provided}$ | Use private/protected modifiers; publish only the interface | ★★★★ |
| **Cohesion** | All operations of a component serve a single, focused purpose | Aim for *functional* or *information* cohesion inside the component | ★★★★★ |
| **Loose Coupling** | $\text{Coupling}(C_i, C_j) \approx 0$ for $i \neq j$ | Use interfaces, not concrete classes; prefer message-passing | ★★★★★ |
| **Provided Interface** | $I_{provided} = \{op_k \mid op_k \text{ is offered to clients}\}$ | Models the *lollipop* in UML component diagrams | ★★★★ |
| **Required Interface** | $I_{required} = \{op_k \mid op_k \text{ must be supplied by other components}\}$ | Models the *socket* in UML component diagrams | ★★★★ |
| **Substitutability** | $\forall C_1, C_2 : (C_1 \to I \land C_2 \to I) \Rightarrow (C_1 \text{ replaceable by } C_2)$ | Honour interface contracts; do not add hidden pre-conditions | ★★★ |
| **Reusability Index** | $R = \dfrac{N_{reuse}}{N_{total}}$ where $N_{reuse}$ = # systems reusing the component | Higher $R$ ⇒ better ROI on component development | ★★ |
| **Independent Deployment** | Component $C$ is deployable without rebuilding $C'$ for $C' \neq C$ | Use semantic versioning, containerisation | ★★★ |
| **Composability** | $C_{composite} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} C_i$ with shared interface $I_{composite}$ | Enables hierarchical architectures | ★★★ |
| **Statelessness (preferred)** | Component has no mutable shared state between calls | Improves scalability, concurrency safety | ★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The principles above are the foundation of **modern microservices architecture** at companies like Netflix, Amazon, and Uber. Each microservice is a *component* with a REST/gRPC interface, independent deployment, and high cohesion. Loose coupling is enforced via service contracts (OpenAPI specifications).

---

## 2.4 Component vs. Module vs. Subsystem — Clearing the Confusion

| Term | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Module** | A logical grouping of related code (file/package/namespace) | `package com.ktu.erp.payments` |
| **Component** | A *deployable* unit with well-defined interfaces | `payments-service.jar`, `payments-service` Docker image |
| **Subsystem** | A *system within a system*; an encapsulation of related components that delivers a major capability | The "Payment Subsystem" containing `CardValidator`, `BankGateway`, `ReceiptGenerator` components |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *Module = code-level, Component = deployment-level, Subsystem = architecture-level.* Subsystems are composed of components; components are composed of modules.

---

## 2.5 The Three Views of a Component

A KTU-mandated concept: every component can be described in **three orthogonal views**.

1. **Black-box view** — The component is a *sealed box*; only inputs and outputs are visible. This is the **client's** view.
2. **White-box view** — The component is *transparent*; internals (classes, sub-components, algorithms) are visible. This is the **developer's** view.
3. **Grey-box view** — A *partial* view revealing only architectural internals (e.g., sub-components) but hiding algorithms. This is the **integrator's** view.

Examiners often test whether students can articulate which view is appropriate at which stage of design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown — Designing & Identifying Components

## 3.1 The KTU-Standard Component Design Process

The KTU syllabus aligns with Pressman's six-step component design process. Each step is now explained exhaustively, with the **"why"** behind it.

### Step 1 — Identify All Candidate Components

**Goal:** Enumerate a *raw list* of components from the requirements specification, use cases, and architectural model.

**How to identify candidate components (in order of priority):**
1. **Domain analysis:** Extract nouns from the problem statement → these are *candidate classes* → cluster into *candidate components*.
2. **Use-case analysis:** For each use case, identify the *controllers*, *entities*, and *boundary* objects (Jacobson's approach) → group into components.
3. **Architectural styles:** If the architecture is layered, each layer is a candidate component. If it is MVC, the Model, View, and Controller are candidate components.
4. **GRASP patterns:** Apply *Information Expert*, *Creator*, and *Controller* patterns to identify responsibilities → cluster into components.

> [!NOTE]
> **Transition to Step 2 (Explicit):**
> Once the raw list is ready, the next step refines the list by examining the *data structures* and *inter-component interfaces* that each candidate must expose.

---

### Step 2 — Examine Data Structures and Applicable Interfaces

**Why this step?** A component's interface is dictated by the *data it owns* and the *operations* needed on that data.

For each candidate component $C_i$, perform the following analytical pass:

$$
I_{provided}(C_i) = \{op_k \mid op_k \text{ is needed to fulfil } C_i\text{'s responsibility} \}
$$

$$
I_{required}(C_i) = \{op_k \mid op_k \text{ is supplied by some other component } C_j\}
$$

The set of all interfaces is then:

$$
I_{total} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \left( I_{provided}(C_i) \cup I_{required}(C_i) \right)
$$

**Example derivation** — A simple **Library Management System**:

| Component $C_i$ | $I_{provided}(C_i)$ | $I_{required}(C_i)$ |
|---|---|---|
| `BookCatalog` | `searchByTitle`, `searchByAuthor`, `addBook`, `removeBook` | `PersistenceService` |
| `MemberManager` | `registerMember`, `validateMember`, `getMemberDetails` | `PersistenceService`, `NotificationService` |
| `LoanProcessor` | `issueLoan`, `returnLoan`, `calculateFine` | `BookCatalog`, `MemberManager`, `FineCalculator` |
| `NotificationService` | `sendEmail`, `sendSMS` | None (leaf component) |
| `FineCalculator` | `computeFine` | None (leaf component) |
| `PersistenceService` | `save`, `retrieve`, `update`, `delete` | `DatabaseConnector` |

---

### Step 3 — Elaborate All Attributes, Operations, and Data Types

For **every** operation in $I_{provided}$, define the complete signature:

$$
op : \underbrace{(\tau_1, \tau_2, \ldots, \tau_m)}_{\text{input parameters}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\tau_{ret}}_{\text{return type}}
$$

where each $\tau_k$ is a well-defined data type (primitive, domain object, or collection).

> **Example:** `searchByTitle : String \rightarrow List<Book>` — takes a string, returns a list.

**Pre-conditions and post-conditions** must be specified. For `searchByTitle`:
- *Pre-condition:* `title != null`.
- *Post-condition:* `Result.size() \geq 0`; each element of `Result` has `title.contains(query)`.

---

### Step 4 — Define Interfaces Using Stepwise Refinement

Apply **stepwise refinement** (Dijkstra/Wirth) to the interfaces:

$$
I_{provided}^{(0)} \xrightarrow{\text{refine}} I_{provided}^{(1)} \xrightarrow{\text{verify}} I_{provided}^{*}
$$

where $I_{provided}^{(0)}$ is the initial set, and $I_{provided}^{*}$ is the final, fully elaborated interface.

At each refinement step, ensure:
- The interface is **minimal** (no redundant operations).
- The interface is **complete** (all client needs can be satisfied).
- The interface is **primitive** (no operation can be expressed as a combination of others).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Stepwise refinement of interfaces is a **direct mapping** to the CO2 outcome at Bloom's *Apply* level. A student who skips this step loses marks for "lack of analytical depth."

---

### Step 5 — Define Component-Level Control Mechanisms

Identify *how* components invoke one another. Three classical patterns:

1. **Procedure-call style:** Component A directly calls methods of Component B (tightest coupling; acceptable within a process).
2. **Event-based style:** Component A publishes events; Component B subscribes. Loose coupling, asynchronous.
3. **Message-passing / Service-call style:** Components exchange messages (e.g., REST, gRPC, AMQP). Used in distributed/microservices architectures.

Mathematically, the **call graph** of the system is a directed graph:

$$
G = (V, E) \quad \text{where} \quad V = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n\}, \quad E = \{(C_i, C_j) \mid C_i \text{ calls } C_j\}
$$

The **cyclomatic complexity** of the call graph gives a rough measure of inter-component complexity:

$$
M(G) = E - V + P
$$

where $P$ is the number of connected components in $G$ (typically $P = 1$ for a single application). Lower $M$ ⇒ simpler architecture.

---

### Step 6 — Develop a Component Qualification Test Plan

Each component must have a **test plan** validating:
- **Interface conformance:** Does the component implement the contract?
- **Functional correctness:** Does it produce correct outputs for valid inputs?
- **Boundary behaviour:** What happens for null, empty, or invalid inputs?
- **Performance:** Does it meet its SLA (latency, throughput)?
- **Robustness:** Does it handle exceptions gracefully?

> [!TIP]
> **A useful formula for component test coverage:**
> $$C_{\text{coverage}} = \frac{N_{\text{paths tested}}}{N_{\text{paths in component}}}$$
> Aim for $C_{\text{coverage}} \rightarrow 1.0$ for critical components.

---

## 3.2 Component Identification Matrix — A KTU-Valued Analytical Tool

When identifying components from a use case, apply the following decision matrix. For each candidate, score it on a scale of 1–5 (5 = best).

| Candidate Component | Cohesion | Reusability | Loose Coupling Potential | Deployment Independence | Total Score |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ | 5 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 18 |
| $C_2$ | 3 | 2 | 4 | 3 | 12 |
| $C_3$ | 4 | 5 | 3 | 5 | 17 |
| Threshold for inclusion | $\geq 3$ | $\geq 3$ | $\geq 3$ | $\geq 3$ | $\geq 13$ |

Candidates scoring below the threshold are either **merged with another component** or **refactored**. This quantitative lens is a KTU-recognised analytical technique.

---

## 3.3 Summary of the Analytical Process

$$
\text{Requirements} \xrightarrow{\text{identify}} \text{Candidate Set} \xrightarrow{\text{interface analysis}} \text{Interface Set} \xrightarrow{\text{refine}} \text{Refined Interfaces} \xrightarrow{\text{test plan}} \text{Qualified Components}
$$

Every step in this chain is **traceable** to a KTU Course Outcome. Mastering this pipeline is the single highest-leverage activity for scoring in Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UML Component Diagram (Ball-and-Socket Notation)

The diagram below illustrates a **Library Management System** in UML 2.0 component notation, with components, provided interfaces (lollipops), required interfaces (sockets), and inter-component dependencies.

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef componentBox fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef interfaceNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:1px,color:#BF360C
    classDef externalNode fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:1px,color:#311B92

    subgraph LMS [Library Management System]
        direction TB
        BookCatalog["BookCatalog COMPONENT"]:::componentBox
        MemberManager["MemberManager COMPONENT"]:::componentBox
        LoanProcessor["LoanProcessor COMPONENT"]:::componentBox
        NotificationSvc["NotificationService COMPONENT"]:::componentBox
        FineCalc["FineCalculator COMPONENT"]:::componentBox
        PersistenceSvc["PersistenceService COMPONENT"]:::componentBox
    end

    DbConnector["DatabaseConnector EXTERNAL"]:::externalNode
    SmtpGateway["SMTP / SMS GATEWAY EXTERNAL"]:::externalNode

    ProvidedSearchByTitle(("searchByTitle")):::interfaceNode
    ProvidedSearchByAuthor(("searchByAuthor")):::interfaceNode
    ProvidedAddBook(("addBook")):::interfaceNode

    RequiredPersistence(("persistenceAPI")):::interfaceNode
    RequiredMemberMgr(("memberAPI")):::interfaceNode
    RequiredNotification(("notifyAPI")):::interfaceNode
    RequiredFineCalc(("fineAPI")):::interfaceNode
    RequiredCatalog(("catalogAPI")):::interfaceNode

    BookCatalog --- ProvidedSearchByTitle
    BookCatalog --- ProvidedSearchByAuthor
    BookCatalog --- ProvidedAddBook
    BookCatalog --- RequiredPersistence

    MemberManager --- RequiredPersistence
    MemberManager --- RequiredNotification

    LoanProcessor --- RequiredCatalog
    LoanProcessor --- RequiredMemberMgr
    LoanProcessor --- RequiredFineCalc
    LoanProcessor --- RequiredPersistence

    NotificationSvc --- RequiredPersistence

    PersistenceSvc --- DbConnector
    NotificationSvc --- SmtpGateway
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Each rounded box is a *component*. Each lollipop (circle on a stick) is a *provided interface*. Each socket (semi-circular arc) is a *required interface*. A *required* interface of one component is *provided* by another — and the diagram draws the dependency edge.

---

## 4.2 Component Design Process — Top-Level Flow

The following block diagram captures the *six-step* component design process mandated by the KTU syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Requirements Spec and Use Cases]):::startNode
    S1[Step 1 Identify Candidate Components]:::processNode
    S2[Step 2 Examine Data Structures and Interfaces]:::processNode
    S3[Step 3 Elaborate Attributes Operations and Data Types]:::processNode
    S4[Step 4 Define Interfaces via Stepwise Refinement]:::processNode
    S5[Step 5 Define Component Level Control Mechanisms]:::processNode
    S6[Step 6 Develop Component Qualification Test Plan]:::processNode
    Decision{All Components Designed and Tested}:::decisionNode
    End([Qualified Component Library Ready for Construction]):::endNode
    Reiterate[Refine and Re iterate Design]:::processNode

    Start --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> Decision
    Decision -- No --> Reiterate
    Reiterate --> S2
    Decision -- Yes --> End

    classDef startNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
    classDef processNode fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef decisionNode fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#BF360C
    classDef endNode fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#880E4F
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** The decision diamond enforces *iterative refinement*. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly values the ability to *iterate* over the design until a stable set of components emerges. Do **not** present a linear, one-shot design — examiners deduct marks for ignoring iteration.

---

## 4.3 Architecture vs. Component-Level Design — Scope Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef archBox fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#311B92
    classDef compBox fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
    classDef detailBox fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#BF360C

    Requirements[Requirements Specification]:::archBox
    ArchStyle[Architectural Style and Patterns]:::archBox
    ArchStruct[System Structure and Modules]:::archBox
    CompIdentify[Component Identification]:::compBox
    CompInterface[Interface Design Provided and Required]:::compBox
    CompControl[Control and Communication Mechanisms]:::compBox
    ClassDesign[Class Level Design and Algorithms]:::detailBox
    CodeImpl[Code Implementation]:::detailBox

    Requirements --> ArchStyle --> ArchStruct
    ArchStruct --> CompIdentify --> CompInterface --> CompControl
    CompControl --> ClassDesign --> CodeImpl
```

> **Legend:** *Purple* boxes = Architectural Design scope. *Green* boxes = Component-Level Design scope. *Orange* boxes = Detailed (Class) Design scope.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. Define a software component. List any FOUR of its essential characteristics.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 2]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **software component** is a modular, deployable, and replaceable building block of a software system that encapsulates its internal implementation and exposes a well-defined set of interfaces through which all external interactions occur. A component is a coarse-grained, independently deployable unit that can be combined with other components to form larger systems.

**Four essential characteristics:**

1. **Encapsulation** — Internal implementation details (data, algorithms, state) are hidden from external entities; only the interface is visible.
2. **Well-Defined Interfaces** — Every component provides a *provided interface* (operations it offers) and declares a *required interface* (operations it needs from other components).
3. **Independent Deployability** — A component can be deployed, upgraded, or rolled back without affecting other components.
4. **Reusability** — A well-designed component can be reused across multiple systems or applications with minimal or no modification.

*(Any four of: encapsulation, interface-based interaction, independent deployment, reusability, replaceability/substitutability, composability, statelessness, versioned identity.)*

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Note:** Award 1 mark for the definition and 0.5 marks each for four characteristics. Students frequently lose marks by writing *object-oriented* properties (inheritance, polymorphism) instead of *component-level* properties.

---

### **Q2. Distinguish between *provided interface* and *required interface* of a component. Illustrate with a UML-style diagram.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 2]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Provided Interface | Required Interface |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Set of operations that the component **offers** to external clients | Set of operations that the component **needs** from other components to fulfil its own responsibility |
| **UML Notation** | *Lollipop* (a small circle on a stick) attached to the component | *Socket* (a semi-circular arc) attached to the component |
| **Direction of Dependency** | Outgoing from the component | Incoming to the component |
| **Role** | Service that the component exposes to the system | Dependency that the component declares on other components |
| **Example** | `BookCatalog` provides `searchByTitle()` | `BookCatalog` requires `PersistenceService.save()` |

**UML-style illustration (text representation):**

```
+----------------+                                                  +---------------------+
|  BookCatalog   | --o searchByTitle()                              |  PersistenceService |
|  COMPONENT     | --o searchByAuthor()                             |  COMPONENT           |
|                | --o addBook()                                    |                      |
|                | ( )--> persistenceAPI     -------------------->  | --o save()           |
+----------------+                                                  | --o retrieve()       |
                                                                    +---------------------+
```

The required interface `persistenceAPI` of `BookCatalog` is satisfied by the provided interface (`save`, `retrieve`) of `PersistenceService`. The dependency arrow points *from* the client (requiring) *to* the supplier (providing).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks, with a *Module-Internal Choice* (i.e., the alternative question must belong to the same module).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) Explain the six essential characteristics of a software component in detail. (7 marks)**
**(b) Discuss the component-level design process as prescribed by Pressman. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

The six essential characteristics of a software component are:

**1. Encapsulation (1 mark):**
A component hides its internal state, data structures, and implementation algorithms behind a well-defined interface. External entities can interact with the component only through the exposed interface. This realises the *information-hiding* principle of Parnas and minimises the ripple effect of changes.

**2. Well-Defined Interfaces (1.5 marks):**
Every component has two interface sets:
- *Provided Interface:* operations the component offers to clients (UML lollipop).
- *Required Interface:* operations the component must obtain from other components (UML socket).
The contract includes operation signatures, pre-conditions, post-conditions, and quality-of-service attributes. A component's interface is the only part of the component visible to its clients.

**3. Independent Deployability (1 mark):**
A component is a self-contained deployment artifact (JAR, DLL, container image, microservice). It can be deployed, upgraded, versioned, and rolled back independently of other components. This property enables continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD) practices.

**4. Reusability (1 mark):**
A well-designed component can be deployed in multiple systems or contexts with little or no modification. Reusability reduces development cost, time, and risk. Examples include standard library components (e.g., Java's `ArrayList`), third-party UI components, and COTS software.

**5. Replaceability / Substitutability (1 mark):**
If two components $C_1$ and $C_2$ both implement the same interface $I$, then $C_2$ can replace $C_1$ in any architectural configuration without modifying the surrounding components. This property is the component-level manifestation of the **Liskov Substitution Principle**.

**6. Composability (1.5 marks):**
Components can be assembled to form larger components (composite components). The composite exposes a single, aggregated interface, hiding the internals. This recursive nature permits the construction of arbitrarily complex systems from simple building blocks.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

The **Component-Level Design Process** (per Pressman, applied in KTU Module 2) consists of **six steps**:

**Step 1 — Identify all candidate components (1 mark):**
Use domain analysis, use-case analysis (Controller/Entity/Boundary), and architectural styles to enumerate candidate components.

**Step 2 — Examine data structures and applicable interfaces (1.5 marks):**
For each candidate, identify the data it owns and the operations needed on that data. Define the $I_{provided}$ and $I_{required}$ sets explicitly.

**Step 3 — Elaborate all attributes, operations, and data types (1 mark):**
Define complete operation signatures with parameter types, return types, pre-conditions, and post-conditions.

**Step 4 — Define interfaces using stepwise refinement (1 mark):**
Iteratively refine interfaces until they are *minimal* (no redundancy), *complete* (cover all client needs), and *primitive* (no operation expressible as a combination of others).

**Step 5 — Describe component-level control mechanisms (1.5 marks):**
Specify how components invoke each other: procedure-call, event-based, or message-passing. Construct the call graph $G = (V, E)$ and analyse its complexity using:
$$M(G) = E - V + P$$

**Step 6 — Develop a component qualification test plan (1 mark):**
Define test cases for interface conformance, functional correctness, boundary behaviour, performance, and robustness. Compute the test coverage:
$$C_{\text{coverage}} = \frac{N_{\text{paths tested}}}{N_{\text{paths in component}}}$$

The process is **iterative** — results from later steps often necessitate revisiting earlier steps. The outcome is a *qualified component library* ready for construction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) With a neat UML component diagram, explain the concept of *provided* and *required* interfaces. Show at least four components. (7 marks)**
**(b) Differentiate between a *module*, a *component*, and a *subsystem*. Give a real-world example for each. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

**UML Component Diagram** (reproduced here, with annotations):

```
+----------------+     ( )--> paymentAPI  <--------------- +------------------+
| OrderProcessor |                                          |  PaymentGateway   |
| COMPONENT      | --o placeOrder()                          |  COMPONENT        |
|                | --o cancelOrder()                        | --o processPayment|
|                | ( )--> inventoryAPI <-----------+        | --o refundPayment |
+----------------+                                 |        +------------------+
                                                  |
+----------------+                                 |        +------------------+
| InventoryMgr   | --o getStock()                 |        | NotificationSvc  |
| COMPONENT      | --o reserveItems()             +-------> | COMPONENT        |
|                | ( )--> persistenceAPI  <----------------+ | --o sendEmail    |
+----------------+                                          | --o sendSMS      |
                                                             +------------------+
                                                  |
+----------------+                                  |
| PersistenceSvc | --o save()  --o retrieve()  <----+
| COMPONENT      |
+----------------+
```

**Explanation (annotations to the diagram):**

- **Lollipops (`--o`) = Provided Interfaces:** Each component *exposes* operations to its clients. For example, `InventoryMgr` provides `getStock()` and `reserveItems()`.
- **Sockets (`( )-->`) = Required Interfaces:** Each component *declares* dependencies on operations supplied by others. For example, `OrderProcessor` requires `paymentAPI` (satisfied by `PaymentGateway`) and `inventoryAPI` (satisfied by `InventoryMgr`).
- **Dependency arrows:** They always flow *from* the requiring component *to* the providing component. The direction is the *direction of dependency*, not the direction of data flow.
- **Substitutability:** Any other component providing the same `paymentAPI` (e.g., a `StripeGateway` or `PayPalGateway`) can replace `PaymentGateway` *without modifying* `OrderProcessor`.
- **Composability:** The `OrderProcessor` itself could be packaged as a component inside a larger `ECommerceSystem` composite component.

*(2 marks for diagram with at least 4 components, 3 marks for explaining provided/required interfaces, 2 marks for highlighting substitutability and dependency direction.)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

| Term | Definition | Real-World Example | Distinguishing Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Module** | A *logical grouping* of related code artifacts (classes, functions, files) within a program. A module is a *code-level* concept. | A Java `package com.ktu.erp.payments` containing `CardValidator.java`, `Receipt.java`, etc. | Code-level, not independently deployable |
| **Component** | A *deployable, versioned unit* with well-defined interfaces that encapsulates its implementation. A component is a *deployment-level* concept. | The `payments-service.jar` or `payments-service` Docker image containing all classes of the payments module and exposing a REST API | Independently deployable, interface-based |
| **Subsystem** | An *encapsulated, architecturally significant* collection of components (and possibly modules) that delivers a major capability of the system. A subsystem is an *architecture-level* concept. | The "Payment Subsystem" of an e-commerce platform, comprising `CardValidator`, `BankGateway`, `ReceiptGenerator` components and the `PaymentService` API | Architecture-level, comprises multiple components |

**Key distinctions (2 marks):**
- **Module vs Component:** A module is *code*; a component is *deployment*. A component may *contain* multiple modules.
- **Component vs Subsystem:** A subsystem is a *larger architectural unit* containing components; a component is a *single* deployable unit.
- **Module vs Subsystem:** A module is a thin code-organisation concept; a subsystem is a thick architectural concept with its own responsibilities, interfaces, and behaviour.

**Hierarchy (1 mark):** Subsystems contain components; components contain modules.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **1. Conflating "Component" with "Object":** A component is *coarse-grained* and *deployable*; an object is *fine-grained* and *runtime-instance*. Writing "a component is a class" will fetch **zero marks**. Always mention **independent deployability** and **interface-based interaction**.
>
> **2. Confusing Module, Component, Subsystem:** Examiners specifically test this triad. Memorise the table in Section 5.2 Q.B(b). Mixing the three will lose 2–3 marks.
>
> **3. Skipping "Why" in the design process:** When describing the 6-step component design process, do not merely list the steps. **Explain the rationale** for each step (e.g., "Step 4 refines interfaces to achieve minimality, completeness, and primitiveness").
>
> **4. Forgetting UML Notation:** Whenever you draw a component diagram, you MUST use the **lollipop (provided) and socket (required)** notation. A plain box without interfaces is incomplete and loses 2 marks.
>
> **5. Linear, non-iterative design:** The 6-step process is **iterative**. Presenting it as a strict waterfall pipeline loses a mark for "missing iterative refinement."
>
> **6. Missing Pre-conditions / Post-conditions:** When specifying interfaces, always include **pre-conditions** (what must be true before the call) and **post-conditions** (what is guaranteed after). Omitting them costs marks under Bloom's *Analyse* level.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Architectural Design** establishes the *macroscopic* structure of the system — components, their responsibilities, and the architectural style governing their interaction.
- **Component-Level Design** elaborates each component — its interfaces, internal structure, control mechanisms, and test plan.
- A **software component** is **modular, deployable, replaceable, and reusable**, with **encapsulated** internals and **well-defined interfaces**.
- Six essential properties: **E**ncapsulation, **I**nterfaces, **D**eployable, **R**eusability, **R**eplaceability, **C**omposability (mnemonic: **E-I-D-R-R-C**).
- **Provided interface** = *lollipop* (what the component offers). **Required interface** = *socket* (what the component needs).
- Components differ from **objects** in granularity, deployment, and coupling mechanism.
- Components differ from **modules** in being *deployable* units.
- Components differ from **subsystems** in being *atomic* in the architecture, while subsystems are *composite*.
- The **six-step component design process** (Pressman): Identify candidates → Examine data/interface → Elaborate signatures → Refine interfaces → Define control mechanisms → Develop test plan.
- **Iterative refinement** is non-negotiable in component design.
- The process is *traceable* to KTU **CO2** at Bloom's **Apply / Analyse** levels.
- Always include **pre-conditions and post-conditions** when defining component interfaces.
- A component's *identity* is its **interface**, not its internal implementation.
- **Substitutability** is the Liskov Substitution Principle applied at the component granularity.
- For 14-mark answers, always include a **UML component diagram** with at least 4 components, lollipops, sockets, and explicit dependency arrows.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Designing Class-Based Components
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Designing Class-Based Components

# Designing Class-Based Components

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> A **class-based component** is a modular, reusable software unit in object-oriented design (OOD) that encapsulates a cohesive set of **attributes** (data) and **operations** (methods/services) behind a well-defined **interface**, conforming to the principles of **high cohesion** and **low coupling**. The class-based design is typically captured using the **UML Class Diagram**, which serves as the bridge between the analysis model (conceptual) and the implementation model (physical).

A *design class* differs from a *conceptual class* (analysis model):

| Aspect | Conceptual Class (Analysis) | Design Class (Design Phase) |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Captures **domain vocabulary** | Specifies **software solution** |
| Detail Level | Conceptual (real-world) | Includes **interfaces, visibility, types** |
| Source | Requirements, use-cases | Refined from analysis + system architecture |
| Notation | Simple name + attributes | Full UML signature with **stereotypes** |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Architect's Blueprint":**
>
> Imagine you are an architect designing a residential township.
> - A **Class** is the *blueprint* of a house (e.g., `Villa`).
> - An **Object** is an *actual house* constructed from that blueprint (e.g., `Villa #A-12, Sector 21`).
> - **Attributes** are the room dimensions (`length`, `width`).
> - **Operations** are the activities performed (`openDoor()`, `turnOnLights()`).
> - **Visibility** is like deciding whether the plumbing diagram is *public* (visible to all) or *private* (hidden inside the walls).
>
> Just as a good architect hides internal wiring but exposes the switches to the user, a well-designed class **hides internal state** (encapsulation) and **exposes a clean interface** (public methods).

### 1.3 Key Metrics to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Standard Metrics for Class-Based Design:**
> - **LCOM (Lack of Cohesion of Methods)** — Measures intra-class cohesion. Good design: **LCOM < 100** (ideally low).
> - **CBO (Coupling Between Objects)** — Number of classes a class is coupled to. Good design: **CBO < 5** for high modularity.
> - **RFC (Response For a Class)** — Number of methods that can be invoked in response to a message. Lower is better for testability.
> - **DIT (Depth of Inheritance Tree)** — Indicates depth of inheritance. **Sweet spot: 3–5** (deep trees reduce reusability).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a UML Design Class

A design class in UML is represented as a **three-compartment rectangle**:

1. **Class Name Compartment** — Optionally with **stereotypes** like `<<interface>>`, `<<abstract>>`, `<<entity>>`, `<<control>>`.
2. **Attributes Compartment** — Lists fields with visibility, name, type, and multiplicity.
3. **Operations Compartment** — Lists methods with visibility, signature, and return type.

**Visibility Notation (UML Standard):**

| Symbol | Visibility | C++ / Java Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| `+` | **Public** | `public` |
| `-` | **Private** | `private` |
| `#` | **Protected** | `protected` |
| `~` | **Package** | default (Java) / `friend` |

## 2.2 Identifying Classes — Heuristic Approach

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Class Identification (Beck/Fowler Heuristics):**
>
> 1. **Reuse** — Identify existing classes from prior projects, libraries, frameworks.
> 2. **Domain Analysis** — Use **noun-phrase analysis** on the requirements/use-case narrative. Nouns → candidate classes.
> 3. **Behavioral Analysis** — Use **CRC Cards** (Class-Responsibility-Collaborator) — proposed by *Wilkinson & Beck* — to discover responsibilities.

**Noun-Phrase Filtering Rules:**

| Filter Rule | Example |
|---|---|
| Eliminate redundant synonyms | `Student` vs `Scholar` → keep one |
| Eliminate vague/abstract nouns | `System`, `Process` → usually not classes |
| Eliminate roles outside system scope | `User` (only if system maintains user data) |
| Keep tangible entities + required services | `Order`, `Payment`, `Invoice` |

## 2.3 Class Relationships (UML Standard)

### 2.3.1 Association
A general structural relationship where two classes are connected (e.g., `Student` *enrolls in* `Course`).

### 2.3.2 Multiplicity
Specifies how many instances participate:
- `1` — exactly one
- `*` or `0..*` — zero or many
- `1..*` — at least one

### 2.3.3 Aggregation vs Composition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aggregation (whole–part, weak ownership):** The child **can exist independently** of the parent. UML symbol: empty diamond.
> **Composition (whole–part, strong ownership):** The child **cannot exist** without the parent; lifecycle is tied. UML symbol: filled diamond.

### 2.3.4 Generalization (Inheritance)
An "is-a" relationship. The subclass inherits attributes and operations from the superclass.

### 2.3.5 Dependency
A "uses" relationship — a change in one class affects the other. Weakest coupling form.

### 2.3.6 Realization
A class implements an interface (`<<interface>>` stereotype).

## 2.4 GRASP Principles (General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns)

> [!NOTE]
> **GRASP** is a knowledge-based set of nine patterns proposed by *Craig Larman* for assigning responsibilities to classes. KTU frequently tests the **first five**.

| Pattern | Responsibility | Core Question |
|---|---|---|
| **Information Expert** | Assign responsibility to the class with the *information* needed to fulfill it | Who has the data? |
| **Creator** | Assign creation responsibility to the class that *aggregates* or *contains* the new instance | Who creates A? |
| **High Cohesion** | Keep responsibilities focused on a single purpose | How focused is the class? |
| **Low Coupling** | Minimize inter-class dependencies | How dependent is the class? |
| **Controller** | Assign system-event handling to a non-UI class | Who handles system events? |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Metric | Formula / Notation | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lack of Cohesion (simplified) | $LCOM = \dfrac{1}{n(n-1)} \cdot \sum_{i,j} \vert m_i - m_j \vert$ where $n$ = methods, $m_i$ = methods accessing attribute $i$ | Measures **intra-class** cohesion |
| 2 | Coupling Between Objects | $CBO(c) = \sum_{r \in C, r \neq c} \mathbb{1}[c \leftrightarrow r]$ | Measures **inter-class** coupling |
| 3 | Response For a Class | $RFC(c) = \vert RS(c) \vert$ where $RS$ = set of methods invoked by $c$ | Predicts **testing complexity** |
| 4 | Depth of Inheritance | $DIT(c) = \max(\text{depth from root})$ | Indicates **reuse hierarchy depth** |
| 5 | Number of Children | $NOC(c) = \vert \text{direct subclasses of } c \vert$ | Measures **inheritance fan-out** |
| 6 | Weighted Methods per Class | $WMC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i$ where $c_i$ = complexity of method $i$ | Indicates **class size & complexity** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE NOTE:** All absolute-value notations are rendered using `\vert` to maintain KTU-premium markdown table integrity. Exam scripts may use plain `|x|`, but in tables, always prefer `\vert`.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Class Identification Workflow

### Stage 1 — Extract Noun-Phrases from Use-Case

**Use-Case Excerpt (Library Management System):**
> *"A **Student** requests a **Book** from the **Library**. The **Librarian** verifies the **Membership** and issues the **Loan**. The system generates a **Receipt**."*

**Identified Noun-Phrases (raw):**
`Student`, `Book`, `Library`, `Librarian`, `Membership`, `Loan`, `Receipt`, `System`

### Stage 2 — Apply Filtering Rules

| Noun-Phrase | Decision | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| `Student` | ✅ **Class** | Tangible entity, system stores data |
| `Book` | ✅ **Class** | Core domain entity |
| `Library` | ❌ **Boundary/Context** | Out of system scope; acts as context |
| `Librarian` | ✅ **Actor (Class)** | External user, may be modelled |
| `Membership` | ✅ **Class** | Persisted entity |
| `Loan` | ✅ **Class** | Persisted transaction record |
| `Receipt` | ❌ **Artifact** | Output document, not a class |
| `System` | ❌ **Abstract** | Vague, eliminate |

### Stage 3 — Derive Attributes & Operations

For each retained class, derive **attributes** (data) and **operations** (services) using the **Information Expert** GRASP pattern.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — LCOM Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Given a class `Invoice` with 3 attributes $\{a_1, a_2, a_3\}$ and 3 methods $\{m_1, m_2, m_3\}$ where:
> - $m_1$ accesses $\{a_1, a_2\}$
> - $m_2$ accesses $\{a_2, a_3\}$
> - $m_3$ accesses $\{a_1, a_3\}$
>
> Compute the **simplified Lack of Cohesion of Methods (LCOM)** and interpret the result.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Build the Attribute Access Matrix $P$:**
The matrix $P$ is of dimension $n \times k$ where $n$ = number of methods, $k$ = number of attributes.

$$
P = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 1 \\
1 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Rows represent methods $\{m_1, m_2, m_3\}$ and columns represent attributes $\{a_1, a_2, a_3\}$. A value of $1$ indicates the method accesses the attribute.

**Step 2 — Compute the Pairwise Difference Sum:**

For each pair of methods $(m_i, m_j)$ with $i < j$, compute $\vert m_i - m_j \vert$ as the count of attributes accessed by only one of the two methods.

Pair $(m_1, m_2)$: Access sets $\{a_1, a_2\}$ vs $\{a_2, a_3\}$

$$
\vert m_1 - m_2 \vert = \text{count}(a_1, a_3) = 2
$$

Pair $(m_1, m_3)$: Access sets $\{a_1, a_2\}$ vs $\{a_1, a_3\}$

$$
\vert m_1 - m_3 \vert = \text{count}(a_2, a_3) = 2
$$

Pair $(m_2, m_3)$: Access sets $\{a_2, a_3\}$ vs $\{a_1, a_3\}$

$$
\vert m_2 - m_3 \vert = \text{count}(a_2, a_1) = 2
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the Pairwise Differences:**

$$
S = \sum_{i<j} \vert m_i - m_j \vert = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Maximum Possible $P_{max}$:**

$$
P_{max} = k(k-1) = 3 \times 2 = 6
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Final LCOM:**

$$
LCOM = \begin{cases}
0, & \text{if } P_{\max} = 0 \\
\dfrac{S}{P_{\max}} \cdot 100, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
LCOM = \frac{6}{6} \cdot 100 = 100
$$

### Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **An LCOM of 100** indicates **zero cohesion**. The class `Invoice` should be **refactored** into smaller, more cohesive classes (e.g., `InvoiceHeader`, `InvoiceLineItem`, `InvoicePayment`). A healthier design target is **LCOM ≤ 50**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Applying GRASP Information Expert

**Use-Case Snippet:**
> *"Compute the total price of all items in an Order."*

**Step 1 — Identify the responsibility:**
`calculateTotal()` is the responsibility.

**Step 2 — Apply Information Expert rule:**
Ask: *Which class holds the information (the list of line items and their prices) needed to fulfill this responsibility?*

**Answer:** `Order` (it aggregates `OrderLineItem` objects).

**Step 3 — Assign responsibility:**
The `Order` class is the **Information Expert** → it owns the `calculateTotal()` operation.

**Step 4 — Apply Low Coupling check:**
Does placing `calculateTotal()` in `Order` create extra dependencies? No — it only uses its own attributes. ✅ Coupling remains low.

**Step 5 — Apply High Cohesion check:**
Is `calculateTotal()` related to other responsibilities of `Order` (addItem, removeItem)? Yes — all deal with order management. ✅ Cohesion is high.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The 14-mark Part-B questions often ask students to *justify* responsibility assignments using GRASP. You **must** name the pattern AND show both the "Who has the information?" and the "Does it maintain cohesion/coupling?" checks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UML Class Diagram — Library Management System

The diagram below illustrates a fully designed class-based component view, including attributes, operations, visibility, and key relationships (association, aggregation, inheritance).

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Person {
        +String name
        +String address
        +String phone
        +getDetails() String
    }

    class Member {
        -int memberID
        -Date membershipDate
        +borrowBook(Book b) boolean
        +returnBook(Book b) boolean
    }

    class Librarian {
        -int employeeID
        +addBook(Book b) void
        +removeBook(Book b) void
    }

    class Book {
        -String ISBN
        -String title
        -String author
        -boolean isAvailable
        +checkOut() boolean
        +returnBook() void
    }

    class Loan {
        -int loanID
        -Date issueDate
        -Date dueDate
        +calculateFine() double
    }

    class Catalog {
        -List~Book~ books
        +searchByTitle(String t) List~Book~
        +searchByAuthor(String a) List~Book~
    }

    Person <|-- Member : Inheritance
    Person <|-- Librarian : Inheritance
    Member "1" --> "0..*" Loan : borrows
    Librarian "1" --> "0..*" Loan : processes
    Catalog "1" o-- "0..*" Book : aggregates
    Loan "0..*" --> "1" Book : references
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - `<|--` denotes **generalization** (inheritance).
> - `-->` denotes **association** (with multiplicity).
> - `o-->` denotes **aggregation** (weak ownership — `Catalog` contains `Book`s, but a `Book` can exist independently).
> - Notice the **Information Expert** pattern: `Loan.calculateFine()` is in `Loan` because `Loan` holds `issueDate` and `dueDate` — the data needed.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Class Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Requirements Document] --> B[Use-Case Narrative]
    B --> C[Noun-Phrase Extraction]
    C --> D{Filter by Heuristics}
    D --> E[Candidate Class List]
    E --> F[CRC Card Session]
    F --> G[Identify Responsibilities]
    G --> H[Apply GRASP Patterns]
    H --> I{Information Expert}
    I --> J[Assign Operations]
    J --> K[Define Class Relationships]
    K --> L[Design Class Diagram UML]
    L --> M{Validate Cohesion and Coupling}
    M -->|Pass| N[Final Class-Based Component Design]
    M -->|Fail| F
    N --> O[Forward to Implementation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Rationale:** The flow demonstrates the iterative nature of class design. CRC (Class-Responsibility-Collaborator) sessions feed directly into GRASP responsibility assignment. Validation via LCOM/CBO metrics triggers a feedback loop to CRC refinement — this is the **engineering quality gate**.

---

## 4.3 Relationship Hierarchy (Composition vs Aggregation vs Association)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Strongest: Composition] -->|filled diamond| B[Lifecycle-bound: Whole owns Part]
    B --> C[Aggregation]
    C -->|empty diamond| D[Weak ownership: Whole references Part]
    D --> E[Association]
    E -->|solid line| F[General structural link]
    F --> G[Dependency]
    G -->|dashed arrow| H[Weakest: transient usage]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**

### Q1. Differentiate between **conceptual class** and **design class** in object-oriented analysis and design. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
A *conceptual class* is identified during the **analysis phase** to model real-world domain entities and their attributes, focusing purely on the problem vocabulary without considering software solutions. Example: in a library system, `Book` is a conceptual class. A *design class*, on the other hand, is developed during the **design phase** and includes software-specific details such as *visibility modifiers*, *data types*, *method signatures*, *interfaces*, and *stereotypes* (e.g., `<<entity>>`, `<<boundary>>`). Design classes explicitly capture solutions like `BookRepository`, `BookController`, etc. The transition from conceptual to design class is a *refinement* step, often informed by **GRASP** principles and architectural decisions.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Q2. What is the **Information Expert** pattern in GRASP? Illustrate with a one-line example. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
The **Information Expert** pattern assigns a responsibility to the class that has the **information necessary to fulfill it**. *Example:* Assigning `calculateTotal()` to the `Order` class — not to `Customer` — because `Order` holds the list of `LineItem` objects and their prices.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2, ESE Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Consider a University Course Registration System. From the following use-case narrative, identify the design classes using the noun-phrase heuristic, draw the corresponding UML class diagram showing attributes, operations, visibility, and at least three distinct relationships. Justify class responsibilities using GRASP patterns. (14 Marks)**

> *"A **Student** logs into the system and selects **Courses** for enrolment. The system verifies that the **Prerequisites** for each selected course have been completed. If prerequisites are satisfied and seats are available, the system creates an **Enrollment** record. The **Registrar** approves or rejects the enrollment. Each **Course** is offered by a **Department** and assigned to a **Faculty** member. A **Grade** is recorded for each student per course at the end of the semester."*

#### Sub-Part (a) — Class Identification + UML Class Diagram (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Noun-Phrase Extraction:**

| Noun-Phrase | Decision | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| `Student` | ✅ Class | Persisted entity |
| `Course` | ✅ Class | Persisted entity |
| `Prerequisite` | ✅ Class (attribute association) | Persisted rule |
| `Enrollment` | ✅ Class | Transactional record |
| `Registrar` | ✅ Class (boundary/actor role) | System user |
| `Department` | ✅ Class | Organizational entity |
| `Faculty` | ✅ Class | Persisted entity |
| `Grade` | ✅ Class | Persisted entity |

**Step 2 — Attribute & Operation Assignment:**

| Class | Attributes | Operations |
|---|---|---|
| `Student` | `-studentID`, `-name`, `-email`, `-program` | `+enroll(Course)`, `+viewGrades()` |
| `Course` | `-courseID`, `-title`, `-credits`, `-capacity` | `+checkAvailability()`, `+getPrerequisites()` |
| `Enrollment` | `-enrollmentID`, `-date`, `-status` | `+approve()`, `+reject()`, `+verifyPrerequisites()` |
| `Grade` | `-gradeID`, `-letter`, `-gpa` | `+recordGrade()`, `+computeGPA()` |
| `Faculty` | `-facultyID`, `-name`, `-deptID` | `+assignCourse()`, `+submitGrades()` |
| `Department` | `-deptID`, `-deptName` | `+listCourses()` |

**Step 3 — UML Class Diagram (Answer Sketch):**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Student {
        -int studentID
        -String name
        -String email
        -String program
        +enroll(Course c) boolean
        +viewGrades() List~Grade~
    }

    class Course {
        -int courseID
        -String title
        -int credits
        -int capacity
        +checkAvailability() boolean
        +getPrerequisites() List~Course~
    }

    class Enrollment {
        -int enrollmentID
        -Date enrollmentDate
        -String status
        +approve() void
        +reject() void
        +verifyPrerequisites() boolean
    }

    class Grade {
        -int gradeID
        -String letter
        -double gpa
        +recordGrade(String grade) void
    }

    class Faculty {
        -int facultyID
        -String name
        -String department
        +assignCourse(Course c) void
        +submitGrades(List~Grade~ g) void
    }

    class Department {
        -int deptID
        -String deptName
        +listCourses() List~Course~
    }

    Student "1" --> "0..*" Enrollment : initiates
    Enrollment "0..*" --> "1" Course : for
    Course "0..*" --> "0..*" Course : has prerequisite
    Course "0..*" --> "1" Department : offeredBy
    Faculty "1" --> "0..*" Course : teaches
    Enrollment "0..*" --> "1" Grade : resultsIn
    Student "1" --> "0..*" Grade : receives
```

#### Sub-Part (b) — GRASP Justification (7 Marks)

| Responsibility | GRASP Pattern Applied | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| `Enrollment.verifyPrerequisites()` | **Information Expert** | `Enrollment` has access to `Course` and `Student` records (via references), so it can verify prerequisites without coupling `Student` to `Course` logic. |
| `Enrollment.approve()` / `reject()` | **Controller** | The `Enrollment` class acts as a controller for the enrollment approval use-case, decoupling UI from business logic. |
| `Course` creation by `Department` | **Creator** | `Department` *aggregates* `Course`s, so per GRASP Creator, `Department` should instantiate `Course` objects. |
| `Grade` ownership by `Enrollment` | **Information Expert + High Cohesion** | `Grade` logically belongs to an `Enrollment`; placing `recordGrade()` here keeps the class focused. |
| Class independence | **Low Coupling** | `Student` does not directly access `Course`; communication happens through `Enrollment`, minimizing inter-class dependencies. |

---

### Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)

**Explain in detail the **GRASP principles** used in class-based component design. For each pattern, describe its intent, apply it to a real-world scenario of an **Online Shopping Cart System**, and justify how it improves **cohesion** and **coupling**. (14 Marks)**

#### Sub-Part (a) — GRASP Patterns 1 to 5 (7 Marks)

| # | GRASP Pattern | Intent | Shopping Cart Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Information Expert** | Assign responsibility to the class with the needed information. | `Cart.calculateTotal()` is placed in `Cart` because `Cart` holds the list of `CartItem` objects with prices. |
| 2 | **Creator** | Assign instantiation to the class that aggregates/contains. | `Cart` creates `CartItem` because it aggregates them (aggregation relationship). |
| 3 | **High Cohesion** | Keep responsibilities focused. | `Cart` is responsible only for cart-related operations (add, remove, total). It does NOT handle payment logic. |
| 4 | **Low Coupling** | Minimize dependencies. | `Cart` does not directly call `PaymentGateway`; instead, `OrderProcessor` mediates. |
| 5 | **Controller** | Handle system events in a non-UI class. | `CheckoutController` handles the "Place Order" system event, separating UI from logic. |

#### Sub-Part (b) — GRASP Patterns 6 to 9 + Justification (7 Marks)

| # | GRASP Pattern | Intent | Shopping Cart Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 6 | **Polymorphism** | Use polymorphic operations instead of conditional logic. | Different `PaymentStrategy` types (`CreditCard`, `UPI`, `Wallet`) override `pay()`. |
| 7 | **Pure Fabrication** | Invent a class to achieve low coupling/high cohesion (e.g., `DatabaseAccessor`). | `OrderRepository` is invented to persist orders without polluting the domain model. |
| 8 | **Indirection** | Reduce coupling by introducing a mediator. | `OrderProcessor` mediates between `Cart` and `PaymentGateway`. |
| 9 | **Protected Variations** | Protect unstable elements behind a stable interface. | `PaymentGateway` interface shields the system from changes in payment provider APIs. |

**Justification Summary:**

- **Cohesion Improvement:** `Cart` and `PaymentGateway` are highly cohesive — each has a single, well-defined purpose.
- **Coupling Improvement:** By introducing `OrderProcessor` (Indirection) and `PaymentGateway` interface (Protected Variations), the dependency graph becomes a *star* rather than a *mesh*, dramatically reducing CBO.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting visibility symbols** — Many students draw boxes without `+`, `-`, `#` and lose **2 marks** in the UML portion.
> 2. **Confusing aggregation and composition** — Using a filled diamond for *aggregation* or vice-versa is a **1-mark deduction** and shows weak conceptual clarity.
> 3. **Naming GRASP patterns without application** — Stating "Information Expert" without saying *which* class holds the *which* information loses marks. Always answer: *"Who has the data? Who owns the responsibility?"*
> 4. **Mixing analysis and design diagrams** — Including analysis stereotypes (e.g., `<<actor>>`) inside a design class diagram is a conceptual mismatch.
> 5. **Omitting multiplicities** — Multiplicities like `1`, `*`, `0..*` are mandatory in design diagrams; missing them costs **1 mark** per relationship.
> 6. **No justification of cohesion/coupling** — Merely drawing the diagram is *not enough*; KTU explicitly tests whether the design *satisfies* quality metrics.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Designing Class-Based Components"**

- 🔹 A **design class** encapsulates attributes + operations with explicit visibility, types, and stereotypes — refined from conceptual (analysis) classes.
- 🔹 UML Class Diagram has **three compartments**: Name, Attributes, Operations.
- 🔹 **Visibility notations**: `+` public, `-` private, `#` protected, `~` package.
- 🔹 **Stereotypes**: `<<interface>>`, `<<abstract>>`, `<<entity>>`, `<<boundary>>`, `<<control>>`.
- 🔹 **Relationships** (from weakest to strongest): Dependency → Association → Aggregation → Composition → Inheritance (Generalization).
- 🔹 **Aggregation** = empty diamond (weak ownership); **Composition** = filled diamond (lifecycle-bound).
- 🔹 **GRASP** = 9 patterns; KTU focuses on the first 5: *Information Expert, Creator, High Cohesion, Low Coupling, Controller*.
- 🔹 **Information Expert Rule**: *Assign responsibility to the class that has the information needed to fulfill it.*
- 🔹 **Creator Rule**: *Assign creation responsibility to the class that aggregates or closely uses the new instance.*
- 🔹 **Cohesion Metric (LCOM)**: lower is better; refactor classes with **LCOM > 100**.
- 🔹 **Coupling Metric (CBO)**: target **CBO < 5** for high modularity.
- 🔹 **Noun-phrase heuristic** is the primary technique for class identification; filter out vague/redundant/out-of-scope nouns.
- 🔹 **CRC Cards** (Class-Responsibility-Collaborator) are used during brainstorming to assign responsibilities before formal diagramming.
- 🔹 Always show **multiplicities** on both ends of every association in a design class diagram.
- 🔹 Always **justify** GRASP-based responsibility assignments by stating both the *who has the data* and the *cohesion/coupling check*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conducting Component Level Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Conducting Component Level Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Component-Level Design (CLD)** is the **software engineering action** that occurs **after** architectural design and **before** construction (coding). It defines the **internal data structures**, **processing logic**, and **interface behaviors** of every individual software component (sometimes called a *module* or *class*) identified during architectural design.

In the words of **Roger S. Pressman** (the standard reference for KTU PEEET524), component-level design establishes:

- The **algorithm logic** that implements every component's responsibility.
- The **data structures** manipulated by each component.
- The **interface details** (signatures, parameters, return types, exception handling) of each component.
- The **inter-component collaboration** required to satisfy architectural and non-functional requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of *PEEET524* explicitly requires students to explain the *component-level design process*, apply *design guidelines*, classify **cohesion** and **coupling**, and illustrate *component-level design for web* and *mobile* applications. These are **HIGH-YIELD** topics for both ESE and internal assessments.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you are the **architect of a large hospital building**.

- **Architectural Design** (Module 1) decides *where* the ICU, the Out-Patient (OP) block, the operation theatre, and the reception desk will be located and how they connect through corridors.
- **Component-Level Design** (this topic) is when each department's head writes the **SOP (Standard Operating Procedure)**: *What exactly does the receptionist do? What forms are filled? What data is passed to the OP? What phone lines connect the OP to the ICU in an emergency?*

In short:
- **Architectural Design** = "Where the rooms are and which corridor links them."
- **Component-Level Design** = "What each room does internally and how the staff inside communicates with the next room."

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** If architectural design is the **"BLUEPRINT"**, component-level design is the **"RULEBOOK"** that tells each component (room/office) exactly how to behave.

## 1.3 Where CLD Fits in the Software Process

The position of Component-Level Design in the Pressman waterfall model is:

$$\text{System/Architectural Design} \;\longrightarrow\; \textbf{Component-Level Design} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Coding/Construction} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Testing}$$

The **outputs** of CLD are typically:

- **Algorithmic representations** (pseudocode, flowcharts, decision tables).
- **Interface specifications** for every module/class.
- **Data structure definitions**.
- **Restructured/refactored classes** that satisfy cohesion and coupling heuristics.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics Used in CLD

The following are the four **primary quality indices** evaluated during component-level design (all marked **bold** as per protocol):

- **Cohesion (C)**: Internal strength of a single component.
- **Coupling (K)**: Inter-dependency strength between two components.
- **Complexity (X)**: Cyclomatic / structural complexity of a component's logic.
- **Reusability (R)**: Degree to which a component can be deployed in multiple contexts.

> [!CAUTION]
> The **goal** of CLD is to **maximize Cohesion** and **Reusability**, while **minimizing Coupling** and **Complexity**. This single line is worth 2–3 marks in KTU exams when phrased as "state the design goal."

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stepwise Component-Level Design Process

Pressman describes CLD as an iterative **four-stage process**. The logic must be reproduced in the same order for full marks in descriptive KTU questions.

### Step 1 — Identify All Design Classes
- Revisit the **analysis class diagram** and the **architectural components** identified earlier.
- For each architectural element (a subsystem, a layer, a package), **decompose** it into a set of design classes.
- A design class refines an analysis class by adding:
  - **Type information** (attributes with data types).
  - **Method signatures** (visibility, return type, parameter list).
  - **Persistence & state-management** details.
  - **Exception handling** specifications.

### Step 2 — Specify Data Structures and Algorithms
- Choose appropriate **abstract data types (ADT)** for attributes:
  - Stacks, queues, lists, hash tables, trees, graphs.
- Express algorithmic logic in:
  - **Structured English / Pseudocode**, **Decision Tables**, **Flowcharts**, or **PDL (Program Design Language)**.
- Goal: every operation must be **unambiguous** before coding.

### Step 3 — Specify Interfaces and Message Protocols
- For every public method, list:
  - **Method name**, **return type**, **parameters (with type)**, **pre-conditions**, **post-conditions**, **exceptions thrown**.
- This becomes the **Application Programming Interface (API) contract**.

### Step 4 — Refine Component Behavior Using Design Heuristics
- Apply **cohesion and coupling** guidelines (see Section 2.3 and 2.4 below).
- Reorganize classes/modules until every component is:
  - **Highly cohesive**, **loosely coupled**, **reusable**, and **understandable**.

## 2.2 Standard Component-Level Design Guidelines (Pressman)

These are the **must-know guidelines** that KTU expects you to enumerate in exam answers:

1. **Components** (classes/modules) should be **logically cohesive** — all operations inside a class should belong together.
2. **Components should be small and focused** — typically, a class should not exceed a few hundred lines.
3. **Naming conventions** must be consistent, meaningful, and domain-driven.
4. **Interface design** must be **simple** — avoid long parameter lists (more than 3 parameters is suspicious).
5. **Dependencies between components** should be **explicit** and **minimized**.
6. **Inheritance** should be used **sparingly**; prefer **composition** ("has-a") over **inheritance** ("is-a") when no true generalization exists.
7. **Inheritance should not violate encapsulation** — never expose parent-class internals to subclasses unnecessarily.
8. **Component communication** should pass only the **data required** and nothing more.
9. **Components should depend on abstractions, not on concrete implementations** (the **Dependency Inversion** guideline).
10. **Every component should be testable in isolation** (a direct consequence of low coupling).

## 2.3 COHESION — The "Inside Strength" of a Component

**Cohesion** measures how strongly the responsibilities inside a single component are related to one another.

> [!NOTE]
> **High cohesion is GOOD.** A highly cohesive component does *one* well-defined task.

The **seven classical types of cohesion** (in **ascending order** of strength — the order is itself an exam question):

| # | Type | Strength | Meaning |
|---|------|----------|---------|
| 1 | **Coincidental (Worst)** | ❌❌❌❌❌ | Random grouping of unrelated tasks. |
| 2 | **Logical** | ❌❌❌ | Performs a set of related but *different* tasks chosen by a flag. |
| 3 | **Temporal** | ❌❌ | All tasks execute at the same time (e.g., *initialization*). |
| 4 | **Procedural** | ❌ | Tasks follow a fixed *order* but are not strongly related. |
| 5 | **Communicational / Informational** | ⭕ | Tasks operate on the *same* input data. |
| 6 | **Sequential** | ⭕⭕ | Output of one task is the *input* of the next. |
| 7 | **Functional (Best)** | ✅ | Every element contributes to a *single, well-defined* function. |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** *"Cats Love To Play, Cats Sometimes Fall"*
> **C**oincidental → **L**ogical → **T**emporal → **P**rocedural → **C**ommunicational → **S**equential → **F**unctional.

## 2.4 COUPLING — The "Outside Stickiness" Between Components

**Coupling** measures the strength of *interconnection* between two modules. **Low coupling is GOOD.**

| # | Type | Strength | Meaning |
|---|------|----------|---------|
| 1 | **Content (Worst)** | ❌❌❌❌❌ | One module directly modifies the data inside another. |
| 2 | **Common / Global** | ❌❌❌ | Both modules share global data. |
| 3 | **External** | ❌❌ | Both modules share an externally-imposed data format. |
| 4 | **Control** | ❌ | One module passes a *control flag* that changes another's logic. |
| 5 | **Stamp / Data-Structured** | ⭕ | Modules share a composite data structure but use only part of it. |
| 6 | **Data (Best)** | ✅ | Modules share *only simple data* (parameters), not structure. |
| — | *(No coupling)* | ✨ | Modules have no inter-connection at all. |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** *"Can Cats Eat Cold Soup Daily"*
> **C**ontent → **C**ommon → **E**xternal → **C**ontrol → **S**tamp → **D**ata.

## 2.5 Cohesion–Coupling Mathematical Scoring (High-Yield Formula Sheet)

For numerical/diagram-based KTU questions, the following formulas apply:

$$
\textbf{Cohesion Score} \;=\; \frac{\text{Number of internally related responsibilities}}{\text{Total number of responsibilities in the component}}
$$

$$
\textbf{Coupling Score} \;=\; \frac{\text{Number of inter-module data exchanges}}{\text{Number of modules in the system}}
$$

$$
\textbf{Relative Modularity} \;=\; \frac{1}{\text{Coupling} \times \text{Cohesion}} \quad \text{(a heuristic proxy for system quality)}
$$

$$
\textbf{Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe)} \;=\; X(G) \;=\; e \;-\; n \;+\; 2p
$$

Where:
- $e$ = number of edges (control-flow arrows) in the program graph $G$.
- $n$ = number of nodes (statements) in $G$.
- $p$ = number of connected components (typically $p = 1$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **McCabe complexity** is **not** a direct measure of cohesion or coupling, but it is the *third* standard metric examined during component-level design. The **accepted threshold** for any single component is $X(G) \le 10$. Anything above is considered *untestable* and must be refactored.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The cohesion–coupling principle is the **backbone of**:

- **Microservices architecture** (low coupling ⇒ independent deployability).
- **REST API design** (data coupling ⇒ JSON-based message passing).
- **Object-Oriented Programming** (encapsulation ⇒ high cohesion inside classes).
- **FPGA/Embedded firmware** in core engineering — loosely coupled driver modules allow hardware-software teams to work in parallel.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Cohesion Classification

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
"Classify the cohesion of the following component:
*A `ReportGenerator` module contains methods to (i) query the database, (ii) format the data into PDF, (iii) send the PDF via email, and (iv) log the email status to a file.*"

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the *single, well-defined* function the component performs.
The component performs **four logically different tasks**:
1. Database I/O
2. PDF formatting
3. Email dispatch
4. Logging

**Step 2:** Check if all tasks operate on the *same input data*.
- Database query uses SQL parameters.
- PDF formatting uses the *output* of the database query.
- Email dispatch uses the *output* of PDF formatting.
- Logging uses the *output* of email dispatch.

**Step 3:** Each task's output becomes the *next task's input*. This is the textbook definition of **Sequential Cohesion**.

**Step 4:** Verify it is **not Functional Cohesion**.
A *functional* component would do **only** one thing — e.g., *just* generate the PDF. Email and logging would be separate components.

### Final Classification
$$\boxed{\textbf{Cohesion Type} \;=\; \text{Sequential Cohesion} \;(\text{2nd Best})}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Improvement suggestion (worth 1 mark):** Decompose into four components — `DBReader`, `PDFFormatter`, `EmailSender`, `Logger` — and orchestrate them from a single `ReportFacade`. Each new class will then have **Functional Cohesion**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Coupling Classification

**Problem Statement:**
"Module A passes a **complete customer object** to Module B. Module B uses only the `customerID` and `email` fields. What type of coupling exists between A and B?"

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the *type of data* passed.
The argument is a **structured composite object** (a record/class), not a primitive value.

**Step 2:** Identify how much of that structure Module B actually uses.
Module B uses only **2 out of (say) 8 fields** of the customer object.

**Step 3:** Match against the coupling classification table.
- **Data Coupling** ⇒ *primitive* data only. ❌
- **Stamp Coupling** ⇒ *composite data structure*, only a *portion* is used. ✅

### Final Classification
$$\boxed{\textbf{Coupling Type} \;=\; \text{Stamp Coupling} \;(\text{2nd Best})}$$

> [!CAUTION]
> A *subtle trap* in KTU questions: if the *entire* customer object is needed by B, then it is **Control** or **External** coupling depending on whether a flag is also passed. Always read the question carefully for *"uses only a part of"* — that phrase is the **stamp-coupling trigger word**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity (Full Derivation)

**Problem Statement:**
"Given the following pseudocode for component `checkDiscount(price, isStudent)`:

```
1. IF price > 1000
2.     IF isStudent == TRUE
3.         discount = 20%
4.     ELSE
5.         discount = 10%
6. ELSE
7.     discount = 0%
```
Compute the cyclomatic complexity $X(G)$."

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Build the **control-flow graph (CFG)** with one node per statement.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \text{number of nodes} \\
  &= 7 \quad (\text{statements 1 through 7})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Count the **edges** (control-flow paths).

The edges are:
1. $1 \to 2$ (price > 1000 is TRUE)
2. $1 \to 6$ (price > 1000 is FALSE)
3. $2 \to 3$ (isStudent == TRUE)
4. $2 \to 5$ (isStudent == FALSE)
5. $3 \to \text{exit}$
6. $5 \to \text{exit}$
7. $6 \to \text{exit}$ (after discount = 0%)
8. $7 \to \text{exit}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
e &= 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply McCabe's formula. The graph is a *single connected component*, so $p = 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(G) &= e - n + 2p \\
     &= 8 - 7 + 2(1) \\
     &= 1 + 2 \\
     &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpret the result.

$$
X(G) = 3 \le 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Component is ACCEPTABLE in complexity.}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative McCabe formula using predicates:** $X(G) = \pi + 1$, where $\pi$ is the number of *predicate (decision) nodes*.
> Here, predicate nodes are at lines 1 and 2, so $X(G) = 2 + 1 = 3$. ✅ The result **matches**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Cohesion/Coupling Should I Choose?"

The following matrix is the **decision-maker's cheat sheet** for KTU 14-mark design questions:

| Scenario | Recommended Cohesion | Recommended Coupling | Reasoning |
|----------|---------------------|----------------------|-----------|
| **Utility class** (e.g., Math helpers) | Functional | Data | Single, pure functions; primitives passed. |
| **Authentication module** with login + signup + reset | Sequential | Stamp → Refactor to Data | Output of one method feeds the next; isolate methods. |
| **Hardware driver** (read sensor, log, retry) | Communicational | External → Refactor to Data | Operates on same sensor data; reduce shared format. |
| **Initialization module** (`boot()` doing 10 unrelated tasks) | Temporal | Common | All tasks at startup; refactor to facade. |
| **Big-ball-of-mud legacy code** | Coincidental | Content | Worst case — total refactor required. |

---

## 3.5 Component-Level Design for Web-Based Applications

Web applications demand a **layered, loosely-coupled** component structure. KTU specifically requires familiarity with the following design considerations:

1. **Separation of Concerns** — Divide the application into:
   - **Presentation Layer** (HTML, CSS, JS, React, Angular).
   - **Business Logic Layer** (Java/Spring, Python/Django, NodeJS).
   - **Data Layer** (SQL, NoSQL, ORM).
2. **MVC (Model-View-Controller)** architectural pattern, where each component has *one* responsibility.
3. **RESTful Interface Design** — Use HTTP verbs (`GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`) and JSON payloads to ensure **data coupling** between client and server.
4. **Asynchronous Messaging** — Use message queues (Kafka, RabbitMQ) to make services *loosely coupled*.
5. **Session Management** — Cookies/JWT must be passed as *parameter data* (data coupling), never as shared globals (common coupling).

## 3.6 Component-Level Design for Mobile Applications

Mobile CLD adds **resource-constrained** considerations:

1. **Battery & Memory Budgets** — Components must be lightweight; complex operations should be **offloaded to the cloud** (loose coupling via API).
2. **Offline-First Design** — Local caches (SQLite, Room) decouple UI from network availability.
3. **MVVM Pattern** — Model-View-ViewModel ensures the *View* is decoupled from business logic.
4. **Push Notification Components** — Should be *reusable* across multiple apps (Firebase Cloud Messaging, APNs).
5. **Platform-Specific Reuse** — Use *interface abstractions* (Dependency Inversion) so the same business logic runs on Android and iOS.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the CLD Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Architectural Design Outputs]) --> stepA1[Identify All Design Classes]
    stepA1 --> stepA2[Specify Data Structures and Algorithms per Class]
    stepA2 --> stepA3[Define Interface Contracts: Signatures, Exceptions, Pre/Post Conditions]
    stepA3 --> stepA4[Apply Cohesion and Coupling Heuristics]
    stepA4 --> stepA5[Compute Cyclomatic Complexity per Method]
    stepA5 --> checkA{All metrics within thresholds?}
    checkA -- No --> stepA4
    checkA -- Yes --> endA([Coding and Unit Testing Begin])
    classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef process fill:#E0F0FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:1px
    classDef terminal fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1px
    class stepA1,stepA2,stepA3,stepA4,stepA5 process
    class checkA decision
    class startA,endA terminal
```

## 4.2 Cohesion Spectrum — Strength Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    coincidental[Coincidental: Worst, random grouping] --> logical[Logical: Related tasks chosen by a flag]
    logical --> temporal[Temporal: Executed together in time]
    temporal --> procedural[Procedural: Fixed execution order]
    procedural --> communicational[Communicational: Share same input data]
    communicational --> sequential[Sequential: Output feeds next input]
    sequential --> functional[Functional: Best, single well-defined task]
    classDef weakest fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    classDef strongest fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    classDef mid fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B
    class coincidental weakest
    class logical,temporal,procedural mid
    class communicational,sequential,functional strongest
```

## 4.3 Coupling Spectrum — Strength Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    content[Content: Worst, direct internal modification] --> common[Common: Shared global data]
    common --> external[External: Shared external data format]
    external --> control[Control: Control flag changes logic]
```
