```mermaid
    control --> stamp[Stamp: Composite data, only part used]
    stamp --> data[Data: Best, primitives only]
    data --> nocoupling[No Coupling: Independent]
    classDef weakest fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    classDef strongest fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    classDef mid fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B88600
    class content,common external
    class external,control,stamp mid
    class data,nocoupling strongest
```

> [!NOTE]
> *Note: The `external` node appears in both red and yellow groups for visual emphasis on its mid-range placement.*

## 4.4 Component-Level Design Refinement Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Initial Component Set from Architecture] --> node2[Apply Cohesion Check]
    node2 --> node3{Is Cohesion Functional or Sequential?}
    node3 -- No --> node4[Decompose Component further]
    node4 --> node2
    node3 -- Yes --> node5[Apply Coupling Check]
    node5 --> node6{Is Coupling Data or No Coupling?}
    node6 -- No --> node7[Refactor Interfaces: use primitives or message queues]
    node7 --> node5
    node6 -- Yes --> node8[Compute McCabe Complexity]
    node8 --> node9{Is X G less or equal 10?}
    node9 -- No --> node10[Break Method into smaller helper methods]
    node10 --> node8
    node9 -- Yes --> node11[Final, Quality-Assured Component]
    classDef check fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    classDef refine fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513
    classDef finalOK fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    class node3,node6,node9 check
    class node4,node7,node10 refine
    class node11 finalOK
```

## 4.5 Web Application CLD — Layered Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph presentationLayer[Presentation Layer]
        uiA[HTML and CSS]
        uiB[JavaScript Framework React or Angular]
    end
    subgraph businessLayer[Business Logic Layer]
        serviceA[Authentication Service]
        serviceB[Order Processing Service]
    end
    subgraph dataLayer[Data Access Layer]
        dbA[ORM Hibernate or Sequelize]
        dbB[Database MySQL or MongoDB]
    end
    uiA --> uiB
    uiB --> serviceA
    uiB --> serviceB
    serviceA --> dbA
    serviceB --> dbA
    dbA --> dbB
    classDef layer1 fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC
    classDef layer2 fill:#FFF0E6,stroke:#FF8C00
    classDef layer3 fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#228B22
    class uiA,uiB layer1
    class serviceA,serviceB layer2
    class dbA,dbB layer3
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define component-level design. State its goal.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
Component-level design is the software-engineering activity that defines the **internal data structures, processing logic, and interface behaviors** of every software component identified during architectural design.

The **goal** is to transform each architectural element into a **highly cohesive, loosely coupled, reusable, and easily testable** design class/module, with **Cyclomatic Complexity ≤ 10**, before construction (coding) begins.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between cohesion and coupling.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Cohesion | Coupling |
|--------|----------|----------|
| **Definition** | Measure of *internal* relatedness of responsibilities within a *single* component. | Measure of *inter-dependency* between *two* components. |
| **Goal** | Should be **HIGH** (Functional is best). | Should be **LOW** (Data coupling is best). |
| **Scope** | Inside one module. | Between two modules. |
| **KTU Formula** | Ratio of related tasks to total tasks. | Ratio of inter-module data exchanges to module count. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often *list* the 7 types of cohesion/coupling but **forget to rank them in order of strength** — this loses 1–2 marks. Always end the list with "in ascending order of strength."
> 2. In **cohesion/coupling classification questions**, students confuse *Sequential* with *Communicational*. **Memory check:** Sequential = *output of one is input of the next*; Communicational = *operate on the same input data* simultaneously.
> 3. In **McCabe** questions, students forget to add the `$+2p$` term or assume $p=1$ without stating it.
> 4. **Don't write code for non-CS theory** — present the answer as a *structured table* or *numbered list* with class types and justifications.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

#### (a) Explain the component-level design process in detail. (7 marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

**Model Answer:**

The component-level design process, as defined by **Roger S. Pressman**, is a four-stage iterative procedure:

**Step 1 — Identify All Design Classes**
- Begin with the analysis class diagram and the architectural components.
- For each architectural element (subsystem, layer, package), decompose it into a set of design classes.
- Refine each analysis class with **type information**, **method signatures**, **persistence rules**, and **exception specifications**.

**Step 2 — Specify Data Structures and Algorithms**
- Choose appropriate **abstract data types** (stacks, queues, hash tables, trees) for class attributes.
- Express algorithmic logic using **pseudocode**, **decision tables**, **flowcharts**, or **PDL**.
- Ensure every operation is **unambiguous** before coding begins.

**Step 3 — Specify Interfaces and Message Protocols**
- For every public method, document:
  - **Method name**, **return type**, **parameters (with type)**.
  - **Pre-conditions** (what must be true before calling).
  - **Post-conditions** (what must be true after calling).
  - **Exceptions thrown**.
- This forms the **API contract**.

**Step 4 — Refine Component Behavior Using Design Heuristics**
- Apply **cohesion** and **coupling** guidelines (Sections 2.3 & 2.4).
- Reorganize until every component is **highly cohesive, loosely coupled, reusable, and testable**.
- Compute **McCabe complexity** for each method; refactor any method with $X(G) > 10$.

---

#### (b) Classify and rank the **seven types of cohesion** with one-line examples. (7 marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]`

**Model Answer:**

| Rank | Cohesion Type | Example |
|------|---------------|---------|
| 1 (Worst) | **Coincidental** | A `Utility` class containing `printReport()`, `sendEmail()`, `sortArray()` — random grouping. |
| 2 | **Logical** | A `processInput()` module that handles keyboard, file, and network input chosen by a `source` flag. |
| 3 | **Temporal** | A `systemStartup()` module that opens DB, loads config, and starts logger all at boot time. |
| 4 | **Procedural** | A `processOrder()` module that validates input, then calculates tax, then prints receipt — in fixed order. |
| 5 | **Communicational / Informational** | A `updateCustomer()` module that updates name, address, and phone — all operating on the same `customer` record. |
| 6 | **Sequential** | A `ReportGenerator` module: query DB → format PDF → email PDF → log status. |
| 7 (Best) | **Functional** | A `calculateSquareRoot(x)` module that does *only* square-root computation. |

The list is in **ascending order of strength** — from worst (Coincidental) to best (Functional).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

#### (a) Classify and rank the **six types of coupling** with one-line examples. (7 marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Model Answer:**

| Rank | Coupling Type | Example |
|------|---------------|---------|
| 1 (Worst) | **Content** | Module A directly modifies the local variable of Module B via a pointer. |
| 2 | **Common / Global** | Two modules read/write the same global variable `g_totalUsers`. |
| 3 | **External** | Two modules must agree on a shared file format (e.g., a CSV schema imposed externally). |
| 4 | **Control** | Module A passes a `flag=true` to Module B that changes B's internal `if-else` branch. |
| 5 | **Stamp / Data-Structured** | Module A passes a full `Customer` object to B, but B uses only `customerID`. |
| 6 (Best) | **Data** | Module A passes two primitive `int` and `String` values to Module B. |
| — | **(No Coupling)** | Two modules never reference each other. |

The list is in **ascending order of strength** — from worst (Content) to best (Data / No coupling).

---

#### (b) A module contains 8 statements and 11 edges with one connected component. Compute the **McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity** and state whether the module needs refactoring. (7 marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $n = 8$ (number of nodes/statements)
- $e = 11$ (number of edges)
- $p = 1$ (number of connected components)

**Step 1:** Apply McCabe's formula.

$$
X(G) \;=\; e \;-\; n \;+\; 2p
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(G) &= 11 - 8 + 2(1) \\
     &= 3 + 2 \\
     &= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Interpret the result.

$$
X(G) = 5 \le 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Module is ACCEPTABLE, NO refactoring required.}
$$

**Step 4 (Bonus — Alternative method):**
Count the predicate (decision) nodes $\pi = 4$. Then:

$$
X(G) = \pi + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5 \quad \checkmark
$$

The result **matches**, confirming the calculation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Conducting Component-Level Design**

- ✅ **Definition:** CLD refines architectural components into design classes with internal data structures, algorithms, and interface contracts.
- ✅ **Four-Step Process (Pressman):**
  1. Identify all design classes.
  2. Specify data structures & algorithms.
  3. Specify interfaces & message protocols.
  4. Refine behavior using cohesion/coupling heuristics.
- ✅ **Design Goal:** **Maximize Cohesion & Reusability**; **Minimize Coupling & Complexity.**
- ✅ **Cohesion Order (Worst → Best):** Coincidental → Logical → Temporal → Procedural → Communicational → Sequential → Functional.
- ✅ **Coupling Order (Worst → Best):** Content → Common → External → Control → Stamp → Data → (No coupling).
- ✅ **McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity:** $X(G) = e - n + 2p$. **Acceptable threshold: ≤ 10.**
- ✅ **Alt. McCabe formula:** $X(G) = \pi + 1$ (number of predicate nodes + 1).
- ✅ **Web CLD pillars:** Separation of Concerns, MVC, RESTful APIs, Asynchronous Messaging, Stateless Sessions.
- ✅ **Mobile CLD pillars:** Battery/Memory budget, Offline-first caching, MVVM, Push notifications, Platform abstraction.
- ✅ **Design guidelines:** Composition over inheritance; depend on abstractions, not concretions; long parameter lists are a code smell.
- ✅ **Always state** the *order* (ascending/descending) when listing cohesion or coupling types — it is a *frequently missed* mark.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Component Level Design for Web Apps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Component Level Design for Web Apps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Component Level Design for Web Apps — Core Foundations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic falls under **Module 2 — Design Engineering**, specifically the section on **Component-Level Design** applied to the **Web Engineering domain**. It is a high-yield area for Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Component-Level Design for Web Apps** is the process of transforming the **Web App architectural model** and the **Web App navigation/semantic/interface design** into a detailed, implementation-ready structural blueprint. It defines the *internal structure*, *behavior*, *collaboration*, and *content distribution* of each individual **Web App component** (e.g., JSP files, ASP/ASPX pages, servlets, PHP scripts, content objects, database connectors).

According to **Pressman's Web Engineering framework**, a **Web App component** is defined as:

> A *modular, deployable, replaceable, and cohesive* unit of web content (static + dynamic) that exposes one or more **web services / endpoints**, encapsulates a specific business function or content presentation, and participates in the overall application through a **well-defined interface contract**.

The output of component-level design is the **Component Specification Document (CSD)**, which acts as the bridge between **architectural design** and **construction (coding)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building Floor Plan" 

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine you are an architect. The **architectural design** of a building tells you *how many floors* exist, *where the elevators and staircases* are, and *how water/electricity pipes flow between floors*. That's the high-level plan.
>
> Now, **Component-Level Design** is the moment you sit at a desk and decide: *"In Floor 3, Apartment 3B, the kitchen sink connects to the ½-inch PVC pipe, the cabinet door opens left, and the electrical socket is a 5-amp type on the north wall."* You are zooming in on every plug, screw, and wire — *the small, functional units*.
>
> In a **Web App**, architecture says "we use MVC with a presentation server and a database." Component design says "The `LoginServlet` class has these methods: `validateUser()`, `setSession()`, `redirectToHome()`; it depends on `UserDAO` and `SessionManager`."

## 1.3 Why is Component-Level Design Critical?

| Engineering Driver | What Goes Wrong Without It? | What Goes Right With It? |
|---|---|---|
| **Maintainability** | A single change to a banner breaks the checkout page. | Changes are isolated to one component. |
| **Reusability** | Every developer rewrites the login logic. | A `Login` component is reused across the app. |
| **Testability** | You cannot unit-test because everything is tangled. | Each component has a clean interface for testing. |
| **Scalability** | One slow component drags down the whole server. | Bottlenecks are localized and optimizable. |
| **Parallelism** | Two developers clash while editing the same file. | Components can be developed in parallel. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Cognitive Mapping:** This topic primarily targets **CO2: Design Engineering** with Bloom's Level *Apply / Analyze*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis — Mechanics, Principles & Design Matrix

## 2.1 The Three Sub-Phases of Web Component Design

The component-level design process is divided into three logical sub-phases. The order matters: you cannot refine a component that hasn't been identified, and you cannot refactor a design that hasn't been refined.

### Sub-Phase 1: Component Identification

You walk through the **class-based** and **content-based** elements extracted during architectural design and decide which classes/elements will be realized as **reusable components**, which will be **deployed as content objects**, and which will be **merged** for efficiency.

**Inputs:**
- Architectural design (subsystem decomposition)
- Use cases, sequence diagrams
- Class diagrams from analysis model

**Outputs:**
- An enumerated list of Web components
- A mapping table: `Requirement → Component`

### Sub-Phase 2: Component Description

For every identified component, you produce a complete **algorithmic + structural specification**. This includes:
- Processing narrative (what does this component do?)
- Interface description (inputs, outputs, exceptions)
- Data structures / persistent storage
- Algorithms (pseudocode, decision logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **What is a "Component Interface"?**
> The interface is the **contract** between the component and the outside world. It is *not* the implementation. For a Web App, an interface could be: a REST endpoint, a JSP page contract, or a method signature like `getUserById(int id) : User`.

### Sub-Phase 3: Component Refinement (Quality Tuning)

You revisit every component and apply **refactoring** to improve cohesion, reduce coupling, and align with Web-specific quality attributes. This is *quality-driven refinement* — not a complete redesign.

## 2.2 Component-Level Design Principles (The 7 Cardinal Rules)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Block — Memorize this list. It is a guaranteed Part A question.**

1. **Naming Conventions** — Component names should reflect their function (e.g., `OrderValidator`, not `HelperClass1`).
2. **Naming Conventions for Operations** — Method names use verb-noun form (`calculateTax()`, not `doStuff()`).
3. **Interface Specification Stability** — Once published, interfaces should be backward-compatible.
4. **Cohesion Maximization** — Each component should do **one thing** and do it well.
5. **Coupling Minimization** — Components should depend on **abstractions**, not concrete classes.
6. **Information Hiding (Encapsulation)** — Internal data and logic are private; only the interface is public.
7. **Web-Specific Concerns** — Address state management, session handling, security, and caching at this level.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Design Cheat Matrix

Because Web Component Design is not a numeric domain, the "formula sheet" here is a **Design Decision Matrix** — a contract you can apply to any component problem in the exam.

| # | Design Attribute | KTU-Ready Statement | Practical Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cohesion** | Aim for **functional cohesion** (best) → communicational → sequential → logical → coincidental (worst). | Easier to maintain, test, reuse. |
| 2 | **Coupling** | Aim for **data coupling** (best) → stamp → control → external → common → content (worst). | Lower ripple-effect of changes. |
| 3 | **Component Granularity** | Prefer **fine-grained** for reusability, **coarse-grained** for performance over the network. | Trade-off decision. |
| 4 | **Content vs. Logic Split** | Separate **static content** (HTML templates) from **dynamic logic** (servlets/JS). | Enables CDN caching. |
| 5 | **State Management** | Push state to the **client** for scalability, to the **server** for security. | Affects architecture. |
| 6 | **Error Handling** | Each component must have a defined **exception propagation policy**. | Prevents cascading failures. |
| 7 | **Security Boundary** | Each component defines a **trust boundary** and authentication requirement. | Prevents unauthorized access. |

> [!TIP]
> **The 6 Types of Cohesion (in descending order of quality):**
> 1. **Functional** — All elements contribute to one single, well-defined task.
> 2. **Sequential** — Output of one part is input to the next.
> 3. **Communicational** — Parts operate on the same data.
> 4. **Procedural** — Parts follow a specific order.
> 5. **Logical** — Parts are grouped because they are logically similar.
> 6. **Coincidental** — Random grouping (worst, avoid!).

> [!TIP]
> **The 6 Types of Coupling (in ascending order of "badness"):**
> 1. **Data** — Components share only data parameters. (Best)
> 2. **Stamp** — Share a composite data structure but use only part of it.
> 3. **Control** — One passes a control flag to another.
> 4. **External** — Both depend on an external format/protocol.
> 5. **Common** — Share a global data area.
> 6. **Content** — One directly modifies another's internal data. (Worst)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-Commerce:** Separating `CartManager`, `PaymentGateway`, `InventoryChecker` components prevents the entire site from crashing if a payment API fails.
- **Banking Web Apps:** A `TransactionAuditor` component is isolated, ensuring SOX compliance.
- **Streaming Services:** `RecommendationEngine` is decoupled from `VideoPlayer` to allow independent ML model upgrades.

## 2.5 Refactoring — The Quality Tuning Step

**Refactoring** is the process of *restructuring* an existing component's internal design *without changing its external behavior*. The objective is to improve **non-functional attributes** (readability, extensibility, performance).

| Refactoring Type | What It Does | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Extract Method/Class** | Pulls a chunk of code into a named method/class. | A method is doing too many things. |
| **Encapsulate Field** | Make fields private; expose via getters/setters. | Public fields are being misused. |
| **Replace Magic Numbers** | Replace literals with named constants. | Hard-coded values reduce clarity. |
| **Introduce Parameter Object** | Group related parameters into a class. | Method signatures are too long. |
| **Move Method/Field** | Relocate to the most appropriate class. | A method accesses another class's data more than its own. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Refactoring is *not* debugging. It does not change functionality. If the behavior changes, you are not refactoring — you are re-engineering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — From Architecture to Component

This section performs a **complete end-to-end derivation** of how a Web App's component-level design is constructed, using a running example so you can see every analytical step.

## 3.1 The Working Example: "Online Bookstore — Search & Order"

Consider the `SearchAndOrder` subsystem of an online bookstore. After architectural design, we have a high-level structure. We will now drive it down to component level.

### Step 1 — Analyze the Architectural Sub-systems

The architect has identified these sub-systems:
- **Presentation Layer** (JSP pages)
- **Business Logic Layer** (servlets + JavaBeans)
- **Data Access Layer** (DAOs)
- **Database** (MySQL)

We are designing the **Business Logic + Data Access** intersection for the *Search & Order* use case.

### Step 2 — Walk Through the Use Case and Extract Candidate Components

> [!NOTE]
> **Method:** Use the **use-case realization** technique. For every actor interaction, identify the *responsibilities* and map each to a component candidate.

**Use Case Text:** *A user enters a book title, sees results, clicks "Buy," enters shipping info, and receives an order confirmation.*

| Interaction Step | Responsibility | Component Candidate |
|---|---|---|
| User submits search query | Parse & validate query | `SearchQueryParser` |
| System retrieves matches | Query DB for books | `BookCatalogDAO` |
| System displays results | Format & render results | `SearchResultsRenderer` |
| User clicks "Buy" | Create new order session | `OrderInitializer` |
| User submits shipping info | Validate shipping data | `ShippingValidator` |
| System processes payment | Call payment gateway | `PaymentProcessor` |
| System saves order | Persist order to DB | `OrderDAO` |
| System sends confirmation | Email + display | `OrderConfirmationNotifier` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Rule:** Each candidate component is checked against the **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)**. If it has more than one reason to change, split it.

### Step 3 — Describe Each Component in Detail

For the `PaymentProcessor` component, the description is:

**Processing Narrative:**
> Receives an `Order` object and a `PaymentInfo` object from the calling servlet. Validates the payment info. Connects to an external payment gateway (e.g., Stripe). On success, returns a `PaymentReceipt`. On failure, throws a `PaymentDeclinedException`.

**Interface Description:**

$$
\text{paymentProcessor.process}(\text{Order } o, \text{ PaymentInfo } p) \rightarrow \text{PaymentReceipt}
$$

**Exceptions Defined:**
- `PaymentDeclinedException` — Gateway rejected the card.
- `PaymentGatewayUnavailableException` — Gateway down.

**Algorithms (Pseudocode):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{FUNCTION process}(o, p) \\
&\quad \text{IF NOT validate}(p) \text{ THEN} \\
&\quad\quad \text{THROW PaymentDeclinedException} \\
&\quad \text{amount} \leftarrow \text{o.getTotal}() \\
&\quad \text{response} \leftarrow \text{gateway.charge}(p, \text{amount}) \\
&\quad \text{IF response.status} = \text{success} \text{ THEN} \\
&\quad\quad \text{RETURN new PaymentReceipt(response.id, o.id, amount)} \\
&\quad \text{ELSE} \\
&\quad\quad \text{THROW PaymentDeclinedException} \\
&\text{END FUNCTION}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic Explanation:**
- Line 1 declares the function.
- Lines 2–4 perform input validation (defensive programming).
- Line 5 reads the order total (assuming `o` is the input order).
- Line 6 invokes the external gateway — note that the gateway is a **dependency** (handled later by injection).
- Lines 7–9 handle the success path — return an immutable `PaymentReceipt`.
- Lines 10–11 handle the failure path — propagate the exception to the caller.

### Step 4 — Refine for Quality (Refactoring Pass)

We scan all components for **cohesion/coupling** issues and apply refactorings.

| Component | Issue Found | Refactoring Applied | New State |
|---|---|---|---|
| `SearchQueryParser` | Has a hardcoded max-words limit of **5**. | **Replace Magic Number** → introduce `MAX_QUERY_WORDS = 10` constant. | More maintainable. |
| `OrderDAO` | Mixes SQL for orders, items, and addresses. | **Extract Class** → `OrderItemDAO`, `AddressDAO`. | Higher cohesion, better testability. |
| `PaymentProcessor` | Directly instantiates `StripeGateway`. | **Inject Dependency** → receive gateway via constructor. | Decoupled; can mock for tests. |

### Step 5 — Build the Component Specification Document (CSD)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Artifact:** The **CSD** is the deliverable of component-level design. It contains for each component:
> 1. Component name and purpose
> 2. Interface (signatures)
> 3. Data structures used
> 4. Algorithms (pseudocode)
> 5. Exception handling policy
> 6. Dependencies on other components
> 7. Refactoring history / quality notes

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Quality Attributes × Component Decisions

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Table: Quality Attributes vs. Component Decisions} \\
&\begin{array}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Quality Attribute} & \textbf{Design Decision} & \textbf{Trade-off} \\
\hline
\text{Performance} & \text{Use caching, coarse-grained components} & \text{Reusability} \downarrow \\
\hline
\text{Security} & \text{Isolate auth in dedicated component} & \text{Performance} \downarrow \\
\hline
\text{Maintainability} & \text{Apply SRP, high cohesion} & \text{Component count} \uparrow \\
\hline
\text{Reusability} & \text{Fine-grained, generic components} & \text{Network overhead} \uparrow \\
\hline
\text{Testability} & \text{Dependency injection, pure interfaces} & \text{Initial setup complexity} \uparrow \\
\hline
\end{array}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Matrix:** Every quality gain has a cost. The art of component-level design is finding the *equilibrium point* where the project constraints are satisfied without one attribute starving another.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Web App Component Architecture — Layered Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **high-level interactions** of components in a typical 3-tier Web App. This replaces a physical drawing and shows the *collaborative flow*.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef pres fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef biz fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef ext fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000

    A[Client Browser]:::pres
    B[Login JSP Page]:::pres
    C[Search Servlet]:::biz
    D[AuthService Component]:::biz
    E[CatalogDAO Component]:::biz
    F[OrderService Component]:::biz
    G[PaymentProcessor Component]:::biz
    H[Database Server]:::data
    I[External Payment Gateway]:::ext
    J[Email Notifier Component]:::biz

    A -->|HTTP GET| B
    B -->|submit credentials| C
    C -->|validate user| D
    D -->|return session token| C
    C -->|search query| E
    E -->|SQL query| H
    H -->|rows| E
    E -->|result set| C
    C -->|place order| F
    F -->|charge card| G
    G -->|API call| I
    I -->|response| G
    G -->|receipt| F
    F -->|persist order| H
    F -->|send confirmation| J
    J -->|SMTP| A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Blue nodes** are presentation components (JSP / HTML).
> - **Green nodes** are business-logic components (Servlets, Services).
> - **Red node** is the data tier (DB).
> - **Yellow node** is an external dependency.
> - Arrows denote *control flow* and *data flow*.

## 4.2 Component Refinement Cycle — IDEAL Process Model

The following diagram illustrates the **iterative refinement loop** that brings a component from initial description to high-quality production-ready state.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial Component Description] --> B[Apply SRP Check]
    B --> C{Component Has Multiple Responsibilities?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Extract Class / Method]
    D --> E[Update Component Description]
    E --> C
    C -- No --> F[Check Coupling Level]
    F --> G{Coupling Type Acceptable?}
    G -- No --> H[Apply Dependency Injection]
    H --> I[Update Component Description]
    I --> F
    G -- Yes --> J[Check Cohesion Level]
    J --> K{Cohesion Functional or Higher?}
    K -- No --> L[Reorganize Methods by Purpose]
    L --> M[Update Component Description]
    M --> J
    K -- Yes --> N[Finalize Component Specification]
    N --> O[Add to CSD]
```

> [!TIP]
> **For Examiners:** This loop is what distinguishes a *junior developer* from a *senior engineer*. Senior engineers **iterate** until cohesion and coupling are at optimal levels. This diagram can be used as a sketch in your 14-mark answer to score full marks.

## 4.3 CSD Document Structure (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component Specification Document CSD] --> B[Section 1 Component Identification]
    A --> C[Section 2 Component Description]
    A --> D[Section 3 Interface Specification]
    A --> E[Section 4 Data Structures and Algorithms]
    A --> F[Section 5 Exception Handling Policy]
    A --> G[Section 6 Dependencies]
    A --> H[Section 7 Refactoring and Quality Notes]

    classDef root fill:#e7f1ff,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sec fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000

    class A root
    class B,C,D,E,F,G,H sec
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When a 14-mark question asks you to "perform component-level design for X," your answer should explicitly produce this CSD structure. It signals to the examiner that you know the formal output of the design phase.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define component-level design. List any four Web App component-level design principles.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Component-level design transforms the Web App architectural model and the navigation/semantic design into a detailed structural blueprint of individual components, defining their internal structure, behavior, collaboration, and content distribution.

**Four Principles (½ mark each = 2 marks):**
1. **Naming Conventions** — Component names should reflect their function.
2. **Interface Specification Stability** — Once defined, interfaces should remain stable.
3. **Cohesion** — Each component should perform one well-defined function.
4. **Coupling** — Minimize inter-component dependencies.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between cohesion and coupling in the context of Web App components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Cohesion | Coupling |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Measure of how strongly the internal elements of a component belong together. | Measure of interdependence between two or more components. |
| **Goal** | Should be **high** (functional cohesion is best). | Should be **low** (data coupling is best). |
| **Focus** | Internal to a single component. | Between two components. |
| **Effect** | High cohesion = easier to maintain. | Low coupling = fewer ripple effects. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions in Module 2 typically come with an **internal choice**. Students answer **either** OR. You are given **two completely independent alternatives** below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Component Identification & Description*

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the three sub-phases of Web App component-level design with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the use case *"A registered student logs into an online learning portal, views available courses, enrolls in a course, and downloads the course material."* Identify at least **six candidate components** and produce a **CSD-style description** for the `CourseEnrollment` component. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Component Identification (2 marks):**
The analyst examines the architectural model, class diagrams, and use-case realizations to identify candidate components. Each candidate is checked against the Single Responsibility Principle.

**2. Component Description (2 marks):**
For each identified component, a detailed description is produced: processing narrative, interface signature, data structures, algorithms, and exception policies.

**3. Component Refinement (2 marks):**
The descriptions are refactored iteratively to improve cohesion, reduce coupling, and apply Web-specific optimizations (caching, security boundaries, state handling).

**4. Diagram (1 mark):** Use the *Component Refinement Cycle* (Section 4.2) or the *CSD Block Diagram* (Section 4.3) to illustrate the flow.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 Marks

**Six Identified Components (3 marks):**

| # | Component | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `LoginAuthenticator` | Validate student credentials. |
| 2 | `CourseCatalogDAO` | Retrieve list of available courses. |
| 3 | `EnrollmentValidator` | Check eligibility (prerequisites, fees). |
| 4 | `CourseEnrollment` | Persist enrollment to DB. |
| 5 | `MaterialDownloadService` | Serve PDF/video resources. |
| 6 | `EnrollmentNotifier` | Send confirmation email. |

**CSD Entry for `CourseEnrollment` (4 marks):**

**Processing Narrative:**
> Receives a `Student` object and a `Course` object from the calling servlet. Validates pre-requisites and current enrollment capacity. Persists the enrollment record to the `enrollments` table. Returns an `EnrollmentConfirmation` containing enrollment ID and timestamp. Throws `EnrollmentFullException` or `PrerequisiteNotMetException` on failure.

**Interface:**
$$
\text{enrollmentService.enroll}(\text{Student } s, \text{ Course } c) \rightarrow \text{EnrollmentConfirmation}
$$

**Pseudocode:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{FUNCTION enroll}(s, c) \\
&\quad \text{IF NOT validator.check}(s, c) \text{ THEN} \\
&\quad\quad \text{THROW PrerequisiteNotMetException} \\
&\quad \text{IF catalog.isFull}(c) \text{ THEN} \\
&\quad\quad \text{THROW EnrollmentFullException} \\
&\quad \text{enrId} \leftarrow \text{dao.persist}(s, c) \\
&\quad \text{RETURN new EnrollmentConfirmation}(enrId, \text{now}()) \\
&\text{END FUNCTION}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Dependencies:** `EnrollmentValidator`, `CourseCatalogDAO`, `EnrollmentDAO`, `EnrollmentNotifier`.

**Refactoring Note:** Initially, the validator and persistence logic were merged. After refactoring, validation is delegated to `EnrollmentValidator` for better cohesion.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Cohesion, Coupling & Refactoring*

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **six types of cohesion** and the **six types of coupling** with examples relevant to a Web App. Indicate which is the most and least desirable in each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a Web App's `OrderProcessingServlet`, the following issues are observed: it directly accesses the `cart` session attribute, embeds SQL queries, and hardcodes a tax rate of **18%**. Apply **three different refactoring techniques** to improve its design. Show the refactored structure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 Marks

**Cohesion Types (3.5 marks):**

| Type | Description | Web App Example | Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Functional** | All elements contribute to one task. | `PasswordHasher` only hashes. | Best |
| **Sequential** | Output of one part is input to next. | `RequestParser → Validator → Executor` chain. | Good |
| **Communicational** | Parts operate on same data. | `ReportFormatter` and `ReportSender` both work on a `Report` object. | Good |
| **Procedural** | Parts follow a specific procedure. | `InstallWizard` steps. | Moderate |
| **Logical** | Grouped by logical similarity. | `Utility` class with `formatDate()`, `formatCurrency()`. | Poor |
| **Coincidental** | Random grouping. | `MiscHelpers` with unrelated functions. | Worst |

**Coupling Types (3.5 marks):**

| Type | Description | Web App Example | Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data** | Share only parameters. | `addItem(itemId, qty)` | Best |
| **Stamp** | Share a composite, use only part. | `submitOrder(Order o)` — but `o` carries unused `customerNotes`. | Acceptable |
| **Control** | Pass a control flag. | `renderPage(page, isAdmin)`. | Avoid |
| **External** | Both depend on external protocol. | Both components use a specific XML schema. | Avoid |
| **Common** | Share global data. | `$_SESSION['user']` accessed by many. | Poor |
| **Content** | One modifies another's internals. | Servlet A rewrites Servlet B's private field. | Worst |

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 Marks

**Issue 1 → Refactoring: Encapsulate Field / Move to Model (2 marks)**

The servlet should **not** access `session.getAttribute("cart")` directly. Instead, the cart is fetched via a `CartService` component with a clean method:
$$
\text{cartService.getCurrentCart}(\text{HttpSession } s) \rightarrow \text{Cart}
$$
This shifts the responsibility to a dedicated component.

**Issue 2 → Refactoring: Extract Class — `OrderDAO` (2.5 marks)**

SQL queries are removed from the servlet and moved to a `OrderDAO` component. The servlet now calls:
$$
\text{orderDAO.save}(\text{Order } o)
$$
The DAO encapsulates the JDBC/SQL logic, achieving **separation of concerns**.

**Issue 3 → Refactoring: Replace Magic Number with Symbolic Constant (2.5 marks)**

The hardcoded `0.18` is replaced with a named constant:
$$
\text{TAX\_RATE} = 0.18
$$
This constant is stored in a `TaxConfig` class or a properties file, making future rate changes a single-point edit.

**Refactored Servlet Pseudocode (Bonus ½ mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{FUNCTION doPost}(req, res) \\
&\quad \text{cart} \leftarrow \text{cartService.getCurrentCart}(req.session) \\
&\quad \text{order} \leftarrow \text{orderFactory.create}(cart) \\
&\quad \text{order.tax} \leftarrow \text{order.subtotal} \times \text{TAX\_RATE} \\
&\quad \text{receipt} \leftarrow \text{orderDAO.save}(order) \\
&\quad \text{res.sendRedirect}(\text{"/confirmation?id=" + receipt.id}) \\
&\text{END FUNCTION}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing component-level design with architectural design.** Component design happens *after* architecture, *not before*.
> 2. **Listing all 6 types of cohesion and coupling in the wrong order.** Always sort from *best* to *worst*.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention refactoring.** Many answers stop at identification; KTU's marking scheme allocates marks specifically for the *refinement* step.
> 4. **Not providing a diagram.** A 14-mark answer without any diagram typically loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Writing code without explaining the *why*.** Always link your refactoring to a *quality attribute* (e.g., "this increases cohesion" or "this reduces coupling").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this as your last-minute revision sheet the night before the exam.**

- [ ] **Definition Ready:** Component-level design is the *post-architectural* phase that defines internal structure, behavior, and interfaces of each Web App component.
- [ ] **Three Sub-Phases Memorized:** Identification → Description → Refinement.
- [ ] **Seven Design Principles:** Naming, naming operations, interface stability, cohesion, coupling, info hiding, Web-specific concerns.
- [ ] **Six Cohesion Types (Best → Worst):** Functional, Sequential, Communicational, Procedural, Logical, Coincidental.
- [ ] **Six Coupling Types (Best → Worst):** Data, Stamp, Control, External, Common, Content.
- [ ] **CSD Sections:** Identification, Description, Interface, Data Structures/Algorithms, Exceptions, Dependencies, Refactoring Notes.
- [ ] **Refactoring ≠ Debugging:** Refactoring changes *structure*, not *behavior*.
- [ ] **SRP (Single Responsibility Principle):** One component = one reason to change.
- [ ] **Web-Specific Concerns:** Caching, state management (client vs server), security boundaries, content separation.
- [ ] **Component Interface vs. Implementation:** The interface is the *contract*; implementation is *hidden* via information hiding.
- [ ] **Naming Conventions:** Components = nouns or noun-phrases; operations = verb-noun form.
- [ ] **Refactoring Techniques to Remember:** Extract Class/Method, Encapsulate Field, Replace Magic Number, Move Method, Introduce Parameter Object, Dependency Injection.
- [ ] **Common Web Components to Identify in Exam:** Servlets, JSPs, DAOs, Service classes, Validators, Notifiers, Authenticators, Controllers.
- [ ] **Always draw a diagram** in 14-mark answers; it shows the examiner you understand the architecture-component relationship.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Identify → Describe → Refine."* This three-word formula is the heart of component-level design. If your answer to a 14-mark question follows this sequence with a diagram, you are guaranteed high marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation and Testing. Object-Oriented Design Using the UML
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Implementation and Testing. Object-Oriented Design Using the UML

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Implementation Phase

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implementation** in software engineering is the phase where the *carefully engineered design* is translated into a working, executable software system through coding, integration, and unit testing. It is the **construction** stage of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC).

**KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Sommerville / Pressman style):**
> "Implementation is the software engineering life cycle phase in which software design is realized as a program code. It includes coding, code reviews, unit testing, integration testing, and the construction of an executable system."

### Conceptual Analogy 🏗️
> Think of software implementation like **constructing a building from a blueprint**. The *blueprint* is your design document. Implementation is when the **mason (developer) lays bricks, fits windows, and plumbs the pipes (writes code)** following the architectural drawings. Before declaring the building habitable, you must *inspect each room* (unit test) and then *inspect the connected water and electrical systems together* (integration testing).

### Key Implementation Activities
- **Coding** the source modules in the chosen programming language.
- **Code Reviews** (formal Fagan inspections or walkthroughs).
- **Unit Testing** individual modules.
- **Integration** of modules into a coherent system.
- **Documentation** (inline, technical, user manuals).

> [!NOTE]
> **Implementation ≠ Programming**. Implementation is a *broader process* that includes programming plus testing, documentation, and integration.

---

## 1.2 Software Testing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Testing** is the process of executing a program with the intent of finding defects. As per KTU syllabus (Myers definition): *"Testing is the process of executing a program with the intent of finding errors."*

**Two Foundational Goals of Testing:**
1. **Verification (Are we building the product right?)** — Checks if the software conforms to its specifications. (Static — reviews, inspections, walkthroughs).
2. **Validation (Are we building the right product?)** — Checks if the software meets the user's real-world needs. (Dynamic — actual execution).

### Conceptual Analogy 🔍
> Imagine you are a **quality inspector at a car manufacturing plant**. Verification is checking if the car *matches the engineering drawing* (e.g., 4 wheels, 200 HP engine, red color). Validation is taking the car for a *test drive on a highway* to ensure it actually performs well for a *real driver*. Both are essential — a car can match the drawing but still feel awful to drive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Testing Principle:** *Exhaustive testing is impossible* (except for trivial programs). Therefore, testing must be **risk-based** and **prioritized** using techniques like equivalence partitioning and boundary value analysis.

---

## 1.3 Object-Oriented Design Using UML

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Object-Oriented Design (OOD)** is a design methodology that organizes software as a collection of **cooperating objects**, each encapsulating state (attributes) and behavior (methods), identified from real-world problem domain entities.

> **UML (Unified Modeling Language)** is a *standardized, general-purpose, visual modeling language* in the field of software engineering. It was developed by **Grady Booch, James Rumbaugh, and Ivar Jacobson** at Rational Software (the "Three Amigos") and is now maintained by the **Object Management Group (OMG)**. UML provides **14 diagram types** (in UML 2.5) to specify, visualize, construct, and document software artifacts.

### Conceptual Analogy 🎭
> Think of UML as the **architect's multi-view drawing** of a theatre production:
> - **Class diagram** = the *cast list* and their roles.
> - **Use case diagram** = the *scenes* the audience will witness (external view).
> - **Sequence diagram** = the *script* showing who says what, in what order, with time flowing top to bottom.
> - **State diagram** = the *emotional states* an actor can be in (e.g., happy → angry → asleep).
> - **Activity diagram** = the *choreography* (workflow of actions).

> [!NOTE]
> UML is **not a programming language** — it is a *visual blueprint* language. Tools like **StarUML, Rational Rose, Visual Paradigm, Lucidchart, PlantUML** can render UML.

---

## 1.4 Quick Summary of Key Terminology

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Verification** | Does the product meet specifications? | Static, review-based |
| **Validation** | Does the product meet user needs? | Dynamic, execution-based |
| **Unit Testing** | Tests individual functions/classes | Done by developer |
| **UML** | Unified Modeling Language | 14 standard diagrams |
| **OOD** | Object-Oriented Design | Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism |
| **Defect** | A flaw in software | Discovered during testing |
| **Failure** | Manifestation of a defect | Caused by execution |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Implementation Phase — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Structured Implementation Steps
1. **Reuse Patterns First:** Identify reusable components (design patterns, libraries, legacy code, COTS — Commercial Off-The-Shelf).
2. **Coding Standards:** Apply organizational/national standards (e.g., MISRA C for safety-critical, Google's style guides, KTU recommends consistent indentation, naming conventions).
3. **Code Review:** Peer reviews using **Fagan Inspections** (Planning → Overview → Individual Prep → Inspection Meeting → Rework → Follow-up).
4. **Unit Testing:** Test each module *in isolation* using stubs and drivers.
5. **Integration:** Combine modules and test their interfaces.
6. **CM (Configuration Management):** Track versions, baselines, and changes.

### 2.1.2 Integration Strategies
| Strategy | Order of Integration | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Big-Bang** | All at once | Simple | Hard to isolate defects |
| **Top-Down** | Main → Sub-modules | Early skeleton runnable; uses stubs | Lower-level modules tested late |
| **Bottom-Up** | Atomic → Higher | No stubs needed; early test of core logic | Driver code required; no system skeleton until end |
| **Sandwich (Hybrid)** | Middle layer tested with both top-down & bottom-up | Best of both | Complex coordination |

---

## 2.2 Software Testing — Comprehensive Analysis

### 2.2.1 Levels of Testing (V-Model Hierarchy)

```
Requirements  ─────── Acceptance Testing
   (System)            (User-acceptance)
   Design  ────────── System Testing
                      (End-to-end)
   Architecture  ───── Integration Testing
                      (Module interfaces)
   Detailed Design  ── Unit Testing
                      (Individual modules)
   Coding
```

> [!NOTE]
> **The V-Model** is the canonical KTU exam figure for testing levels. It maps each development phase to a corresponding test phase, emphasizing that test planning occurs *in parallel* with development.

### 2.2.2 Testing Types

**A. Functional Testing** — *What the system does.*
- Unit Testing
- Integration Testing
- System Testing
- User Acceptance Testing (UAT) — includes **Alpha** (in-house) & **Beta** (customer site).

**B. Non-Functional Testing** — *How well the system behaves.*
- Performance Testing (Load, Stress, Spike, Soak)
- Security Testing
- Usability Testing
- Compatibility Testing
- Reliability Testing
- Recovery Testing

**C. Structural (White-Box) Testing** — *Based on internal code structure.*
- Statement Coverage
- Branch (Decision) Coverage
- Condition Coverage
- Path Coverage
- Cyclomatic Complexity-driven testing

**D. Regression Testing** — *Re-run old tests after a change* to ensure no new defects. Uses **test suites** stored in a **regression test repository**.

### 2.2.3 Test Case Design Techniques

#### Black-Box Techniques (Specification-Based)
1. **Equivalence Partitioning (EP):** Divide input domain into classes where any value in a class is treated equivalently. Example: Age input field for a 18-60 range → valid (18-60), invalid (<18), invalid (>60). Reduces redundant tests.
2. **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA):** Test values *at, just below, and just above* the boundaries. Example: 17, 18, 19, 59, 60, 61.
3. **Decision Table Testing:** For systems with logical combinations of inputs, build a table of conditions and actions.
4. **State Transition Testing:** Test sequences of events causing state changes (used with state diagrams).
5. **Use Case Testing:** Derive test cases from use case scenarios.

#### White-Box Techniques (Structure-Based)
1. **Statement Coverage:** Every executable statement executed at least once.
2. **Branch Coverage:** Every branch (true/false) of each decision executed.
3. **Cyclomatic Complexity** (McCabe, 1976) — *the single most important metric in white-box testing.*

### 2.2.4 Why Cyclomatic Complexity Matters?
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cyclomatic Complexity** measures the number of **independent linear paths** through a program's source code. It defines the **minimum number of test cases** required to cover every path at least once.

---

## 2.3 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET 📐

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Meaning | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cyclomatic Complexity (V(G)) — Flow Graph Method** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components (usually 1) | Independent paths count |
| 2 | **Cyclomatic Complexity — Decision Points Method** | $V(G) = \pi + 1$ | $\pi$ = number of predicate (decision) nodes | Quick rule for code |
| 3 | **Cyclomatic Complexity — Regions Method** | $V(G) = R$ | $R$ = number of enclosed regions in flow graph + 1 (outer) | Visual method |
| 4 | **Statement Coverage** | $S_c = \dfrac{S_e}{S_t} \times 100$ | $S_e$ = executed statements, $S_t$ = total statements | Percentage |
| 5 | **Branch (Decision) Coverage** | $B_c = \dfrac{B_e}{B_t} \times 100$ | $B_e$ = executed branches, $B_t$ = total branches | Percentage |
| 6 | **Defect Density** | $DD = \dfrac{N_d}{S}$ | $N_d$ = defects, $S$ = size in KLOC or Function Points | defects/KLOC |
| 7 | **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** | $MTTF = \dfrac{T_{total}}{N_f}$ | $T_{total}$ = total operating time, $N_f$ = failures | Hours |
| 8 | **Test Effectiveness** | $TE = \dfrac{N_{td}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | $N_{td}$ = defects detected by tests, $N_{total}$ = total defects | Percentage |
| 9 | **Code Coverage (general)** | $CC = \dfrac{L_{executed}}{L_{total}} \times 100$ | Lines covered / total lines | Percentage |
| 10 | **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair | Decimal / Percentage |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In exam tables, never use the `|` symbol inside table cells for absolute value. Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ (e.g., write $\vert V(G) \vert$ not $\vert V(G) \vert$ in raw `|`). This is a markdown table-breaking trap.

---

## 2.4 Object-Oriented Design with UML — Deep Analysis

### 2.4.1 The 14 UML 2.5 Diagrams — Classified

| Category | Diagram | Purpose | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Structure (Static)** | Class Diagram | Classes, attributes, methods, relationships | ⭐⭐⭐ HIGH |
| | Object Diagram | Snapshot of instances at a moment | ⭐ Low |
| | Component Diagram | Software modules & dependencies | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| | Deployment Diagram | Physical hardware nodes & artifacts | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| | Package Diagram | Logical grouping of model elements | ⭐ Low |
| | Composite Structure | Internal structure of a class | ⭐ Low |
| **Behavior (Dynamic)** | Use Case Diagram | Functional requirements (actor-system) | ⭐⭐⭐ HIGH |
| | Activity Diagram | Workflow / business process flow | ⭐⭐⭐ HIGH |
| | State Machine Diagram | Object lifecycle states | ⭐⭐⭐ HIGH |
| **Interaction (a subset of Behavior)** | Sequence Diagram | Time-ordered message exchange | ⭐⭐⭐ HIGH |
| | Communication Diagram | Object interactions with numbered messages | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| | Timing Diagram | State changes over time (real-time systems) | ⭐ Low |
| | Interaction Overview | Combines activity & sequence | ⭐ Low |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU exams, focus on **Class, Use Case, Sequence, Activity, and State Machine** diagrams. These 5 are the most frequently tested.

### 2.4.2 UML Class Diagram — The Heart of OOD

**Visibility Modifiers (in front of attribute/operation):**
| Symbol | Visibility | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `+` | Public | Accessible from anywhere |
| `-` | Private | Accessible only within the class |
| `#` | Protected | Accessible within class & subclasses |
| `~` | Package | Accessible within same package |

**Relationships in Class Diagrams (HEAVILY TESTED):**

| Relationship | Symbol | Strength | Lifetime | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Association** | ──── | Weak "uses-a" | Independent | Student ──── Course |
| **Directed Association** | ────▶ | One-way reference | Independent | Order ──▶ Customer |
| **Aggregation** | ◇─── | Weak "has-a" (whole–part, parts survive) | Part can exist without whole | Department ◇── Employee |
| **Composition** | ◆─── | Strong "owns-a" (parts die with whole) | Part cannot exist without whole | House ◆── Room |
| **Generalization (Inheritance)** | ────▷ | "is-a" | Subclass inherits parent | Dog ───▷ Animal |
| **Realization (Implementation)** | ─ ─ ─▷ | Class implements interface | Contractual | ArrayList - - -▷ List |
| **Dependency** | - - - ▶ | "uses-temporarily" (e.g., parameter) | Weaker than association | Client - - -▶ Service |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aggregation vs Composition** is the single most confused topic in KTU UML exams. Memorize: *Aggregation = empty diamond (hollow ◇), Composition = filled diamond (◆)*. The "death test": if the whole is destroyed, does the part die too? **Yes → Composition, No → Aggregation.**

### 2.4.3 Multiplicity Notation

| Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `1` | Exactly one |
| `0..1` | Optional (zero or one) |
| `*` or `0..*` | Zero or many |
| `1..*` | One or many |
| `n..m` | Range from n to m |

**Example:** A `Library (1) ── (*) Book` means one library contains zero or many books.

| Concept | Industry Application |
|---|---|
| **V-Model** | Safety-critical systems (aerospace DO-178C, automotive ISO 26262) |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity** | Code quality gate in CI/CD pipelines (SonarQube rejects $V(G) > 10$) |
| **UML Class Diagrams** | Auto-generation of code (forward engineering) and reverse engineering of legacy code |
| **UML Sequence Diagrams** | Documenting microservices API calls and message flows |
| **Boundary Value Analysis** | Testing IoT sensor range limits (e.g., temperature alarms 0°C – 100°C) |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Cyclomatic Complexity — Exhaustive Derivation (3 Methods)

> [!NOTE]
> The following problem is a **classic KTU 14-mark** style question. We solve it using all three methods and verify consistency — this is the exact approach that fetches full marks.

### **Problem Statement**
Given the following C code segment, compute the **Cyclomatic Complexity** using all three methods, and determine the **minimum number of test cases** required for path coverage.

```c
int grade(int marks) {
    int g;
    if (marks >= 90)         // Decision 1
        g = 1;
    else if (marks >= 75)    // Decision 2
        g = 2;
    else if (marks >= 50)    // Decision 3
        g = 3;
    else
        g = 4;
    while (g < 4) {          // Decision 4
        g = g + 1;
    }
    return g;
}
```

---

### **STEP 1: Construct the Flow Graph**

A **flow graph** is a directed graph where:
- **Nodes** represent sequential statements (or a basic block of statements).
- **Edges** represent control flow between nodes.
- **Predicate nodes** (decisions) are the diamonds.

Mapping the code:

```
Node 1: Entry
Node 2: marks >= 90 ?         (D1)
Node 3: g = 1
Node 4: marks >= 75 ?         (D2)
Node 5: g = 2
Node 6: marks >= 50 ?         (D3)
Node 7: g = 3
Node 8: g = 4
Node 9: g < 4 ?               (D4 - while)
Node 10: g = g + 1
Node 11: Exit (return g)
```

**Edges:**
1 → 2
2 → 3 (T)
2 → 4 (F)
3 → 4
4 → 5 (T)
4 → 6 (F)
5 → 6
6 → 7 (T)
6 → 8 (F)
7 → 8
8 → 9
9 → 10 (T)
9 → 11 (F)
10 → 9

So: **N (Nodes) = 11, E (Edges) = 14, P (Connected Components) = 1**

---

### **STEP 2: Apply Method 1 — Flow Graph Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= E - N + 2P \\
&= 14 - 11 + 2(1) \\
&= 14 - 11 + 2 \\
&= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Minimum test cases required = V(G) = 5**

---

### **STEP 3: Apply Method 2 — Predicate (Decision Points) Method**

Count the number of predicate (decision) nodes in the code:
- `if (marks >= 90)` → 1
- `else if (marks >= 75)` → 1
- `else if (marks >= 50)` → 1
- `while (g < 4)` → 1

Total predicates $\pi = 4$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= \pi + 1 \\
&= 4 + 1 \\
&= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **STEP 4: Apply Method 3 — Regions Method**

Count enclosed regions in the flow graph:
- Region 1: between D1, D2 (T branch)
- Region 2: between D2, D3 (T branch)
- Region 3: between D3, else (T branch)
- Region 4: inside the while loop
- Region 5: the outer region (around everything)

Total regions $R = 5$

$$
V(G) = R = 5
$$

---

### **STEP 5: Verification — All 3 Methods Agree** ✅

$$
V(G)_{\text{Method 1}} = V(G)_{\text{Method 2}} = V(G)_{\text{Method 3}} = 5
$$

**Conclusion:** The program has **5 independent paths**, so a minimum of **5 test cases** is required for complete path coverage.

**The 5 independent paths (a typical KTU sub-part):**
1. $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 6 \to 8 \to 9 \to 11$ (marks ≥ 90, skip loop)
2. $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 5 \to 6 \to 8 \to 9 \to 11$
3. $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 6 \to 7 \to 8 \to 9 \to 11$
4. $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 6 \to 8 \to 9 \to 10 \to 9 \to 11$ (enters loop once)
5. $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 6 \to 8 \to 9 \to 10 \to 9 \to 10 \to 9 \to 11$ (enters loop twice)

---

## 3.2 Statement Coverage vs Branch Coverage — Analytical Matrix

Given a small piece of code with 2 decision points (A and B):

```c
if (A)        // Decision 1
    x = 1;
if (B)        // Decision 2
    y = 1;
```

| Test # | A | B | Statement Coverage | Branch Coverage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | T | T | 100% (4/4) | 100% (4/4) |
| 2 | F | T | 100% (4/4) | 100% (4/4) |
| 3 | T | F | 100% (4/4) | 100% (4/4) |
| 4 | F | F | 100% (4/4) | 100% (4/4) |

**Insight:** For this trivial code, 4 tests give 100% both. But consider a *3-condition* decision:

```c
if (A && (B || C)) { ... }
```

- **Statement coverage** can be 100% with 2 tests (e.g., A=T, B=T, C=T and A=F).
- **Branch coverage** requires all 4 combinations of (B || C) outcomes.
- **Condition coverage** requires 8 tests (every boolean variable both T and F).

> [!NOTE]
> **100% Branch coverage ⇒ 100% Statement coverage**, but **NOT vice versa.** This is a high-frequency KTU 2-mark or 3-mark question.

---

## 3.3 OO Design Step-by-Step (Unified Process Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU expects students to know the **iterative OO design steps** — this maps directly to a 7-mark sub-question.

**Step 1: Identify Classes from Problem Statement (using noun extraction).**
- E.g., "A university has faculties, and faculties have departments. Each department offers courses. Students enroll in courses." → Classes: `University, Faculty, Department, Course, Student`.

**Step 2: Identify Attributes (using adjectives / state info).**
- `Student: name, rollNo, email, cgpa`
- `Course: courseId, title, credits`

**Step 3: Identify Operations/Methods (using verbs).**
- `Student: enroll(course), drop(course), viewGrades()`
- `Course: addStudent(student), removeStudent(student)`

**Step 4: Identify Relationships.**
- `University 1 ── * Faculty` (composition — faculties can't exist without university? Or aggregation? Use judgment.)
- `Faculty 1 ── * Department` (composition)
- `Department 1 ── * Course` (aggregation — courses can be inter-departmental)
- `Student * ── * Course` (associative — many-to-many via an `Enrollment` class)

**Step 5: Identify Multiplicity & Roles.**

**Step 6: Construct Class Diagram (see Section 4).**

**Step 7: Refine using GRASP / SOLID principles** (advanced — Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion).

---

## 3.4 Test Case Design — Equivalence Partitioning (EP) + BVA Worked Example

**Problem:** A function `discount(age)` gives 0% discount if age < 18, 10% if 18 ≤ age ≤ 60, 20% if age > 60.

**Step 1: Identify Equivalence Partitions**
| Partition | Class | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Invalid | age < 0 | -5, -100 |
| Valid 1 | 0 ≤ age < 18 | 5, 10, 17 |
| Valid 2 | 18 ≤ age ≤ 60 | 18, 30, 45, 60 |
| Valid 3 | age > 60 | 61, 75, 100 |

**Step 2: Apply BVA at Boundaries 0, 17, 18, 60, 61**

| Test # | Age | Expected Discount | Boundary Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | -1 | Error | On-boundary (invalid) |
| 2 | 0 | 0% | On-boundary (valid 1) |
| 3 | 17 | 0% | On-boundary (valid 1) |
| 4 | 18 | 10% | On-boundary (valid 2) |
| 5 | 60 | 10% | On-boundary (valid 2) |
| 6 | 61 | 20% | On-boundary (valid 3) |
| 7 | 100 | 20% | On-boundary (valid 3) |

**Note:** Without BVA, EP would test only ONE value per partition (e.g., 10, 30, 70) — only 4 tests. BVA adds 3 more boundary tests, making 7 total. This is the *risk-based* approach.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Testing Strategy Selection

| Test Level | Performed By | Granularity | Defects Found |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit** | Developer | Function/Class | Logic errors, typos, edge cases |
| **Integration** | Developer/Tester | Module interfaces | Mismatched signatures, data flow errors |
| **System** | Independent Test Team | Whole application | Missing requirements, performance issues |
| **Acceptance (UAT)** | End-user / Customer | Business scenarios | Wrong features, poor usability |
| **Alpha** | Internal testers (in-house) | Pre-release | Combined system + acceptance |
| **Beta** | External users (limited release) | Real environment | Platform-specific bugs |

## 4.1 The V-Model of Testing (Functional Architecture Flow)

This is the **most important diagram for KTU 7-mark questions on testing levels**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DEV["DEVELOPMENT PHASE (Left Arm of V)"]
        REQ["Requirements<br/>Analysis"]
        SYS["System / High-Level<br/>Design"]
        ARC["Architecture<br/>Design"]
        MOD["Module / Detailed<br/>Design"]
        COD["Coding /<br/>Implementation"]
    end

    subgraph TEST["TESTING PHASE (Right Arm of V)"]
        AT["Acceptance<br/>Testing"]
        ST["System<br/>Testing"]

    end

    REQ -.-> AT
    SYS -.-> ST
    ARC -.-> IT
    MOD -.-> UT
    COD ==> UT

    AT ==> REQ
    ST ==> SYS
    IT ==> ARC

    classDef devNode fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2,color:#000
    classDef testNode fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00,color:#000

```

**How to read this:** Each development activity on the left has a *correspondence* (dotted arrow) to a testing activity on the right. The "V" shape emphasizes that **test planning for a level begins in parallel with the corresponding development phase** — not after coding is finished.

## 4.2 Integration Testing Strategies — Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Module A"]:::unit
    B["Module B"]:::unit
```

## 4.3 UML Class Diagram — Worked Example (Vehicle Hierarchy)

This diagram represents a **classic OO design** mapping exactly to KTU exam questions.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Vehicle {
        -String make
        -String model
        -int year
        +startEngine() void
        +stopEngine() void
        +accelerate() void
    }

    class Car {
        -int numDoors
        +honk() void
    }

    class Truck {
        -double payloadCapacity
        +loadCargo() void
    }

    class Engine {
        -int horsepower
        -String fuelType
        +start() void
        +stop() void
    }

    class Driver {
        -String licenseNo
        -String name
        +drive(Vehicle) void
    }

    Vehicle <|-- Car : Generalization
    Vehicle <|-- Truck : Generalization
    Car *-- Engine : Composition
    Truck *-- Engine : Composition
    Driver --> Vehicle : Association
    Driver ..> Engine : Dependency
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `<|--` = **Generalization** (Car **is-a** Vehicle).
- `*--` = **Composition** (Car **owns** Engine; engine cannot exist without car).
- `-->` = **Association** (Driver **uses** a Vehicle).
- `..>` = **Dependency** (Driver occasionally **depends on** Engine for some operation).

---

## 4.4 UML Use Case Diagram — Online Shopping System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Customer((Customer)):::actor
    Admin((Admin)):::actor
    PaymentGateway((Payment<br/>Gateway)):::actor

    UC1([Browse Products])
    UC2([Add to Cart])
    UC3([Checkout])
    UC4([Make Payment])
    UC5([Manage Inventory])
    UC6([View Reports])
    UC7([Register / Login]):::auth
    UC8([Track Order])

    Customer --- UC1
    Customer --- UC2
    Customer --- UC3
    Customer --- UC4
    Customer --- UC7
    Customer --- UC8

    Admin --- UC5
    Admin --- UC6
    Admin --- UC7

    PaymentGateway --- UC4

    UC3 .> UC4 : <<include>>
    UC2 .> UC1 : <<include>>
    UC5 .> UC6 : <<extend>>

    classDef actor fill:#ffe082,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef auth fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    class Customer,Admin,PaymentGateway actor
    class UC7 auth
```

**Notation explained:**
- Stick figure = **Actor** (external role).
- Oval/ellipse = **Use Case** (functionality).
- `<<include>>` (dashed arrow) = mandatory sub-use-case.
- `<<extend>>` (dashed arrow, reversed direction) = optional extension.

## 4.5 UML Sequence Diagram — ATM Withdrawal

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor User
    participant ATM as ATM Machine
    participant Bank as Bank Server
    participant DB as Database

    User->>ATM: Insert Card
    activate ATM
    ATM->>User: Request PIN
    User->>ATM: Enter PIN (1234)
    ATM->>Bank: Validate PIN
    activate Bank
    Bank->>DB: Check Account
    activate DB
    DB-->>Bank: Account Valid
    deactivate DB
    Bank-->>ATM: Authentication OK
    deactivate Bank

    User->>ATM: Request Withdrawal (5000)
    ATM->>Bank: Process Transaction
    activate Bank
    Bank->>DB: Debit Account
    activate DB
    DB-->>Bank: Debit Successful
    deactivate DB
    Bank-->>ATM: Dispense Cash
    deactivate Bank
    ATM->>User: Dispense Cash + Receipt
    deactivate ATM
```

**Reading:** Time flows **top to bottom**. The numbered labels (`autonumber`) help examiners track the message sequence. `activate`/`deactivate` show the *lifeline* of an object.

## 4.6 UML State Machine Diagram — Login States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Locked
    Locked --> Active : Correct PIN
    Active --> Locked : Lock Timeout
    Active --> Authenticated : Correct Password
    Active --> Error : Wrong Password 3 times
    Authenticated --> [*] : Logout
    Error --> Locked : Wait 30s
    Authenticated --> Active : Session Timeout
```

**Reading:** Each rounded rectangle is a **state**. Arrows are **transitions** labeled with their **trigger event**. Filled circle = start; bullseye = end.
