| **Reader (Presenter)** | Walks through the product during the meeting (may be the author) | 1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake**: Confusing the **Reviewer** (who *identifies* defects) with the **Author** (who *fixes* them). The review team **does not fix defects** during the review meeting — this is a deliberate separation of responsibilities to maintain objectivity and efficiency.

## 2.4 Reference Matrix — Comparing Review Types

| Attribute | Informal Review | Walkthrough | Technical Review | Inspection |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Formality** | Low | Low–Medium | Medium | Very High |
| **Lead Role** | None | Author | Reviewer | Trained Moderator |
| **Pre-Planning** | None | Minimal | Required | Mandatory |
| **Defect Logging** | No | Optional | Yes | Mandatory (Formal Log) |
| **Roles Defined** | No | Partially | Yes | Yes (Strictly Defined) |
| **Process Defined** | No | No | Partially | Yes (Fagan Process) |
| **Most Common Use** | Quick check | Knowledge transfer | Standards compliance | Safety-critical software |
| **Originator** | — | Author | IEEE Std 1028 | Michael Fagan (IBM, 1976) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Pearl**: The **Inspection** method is the **most formal** FTR variant and is attributed to **Michael Fagan** of IBM (1976). It is the gold standard for safety-critical domains (aerospace, medical devices, nuclear systems).

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Aerospace (DO-178C compliance)**: Every line of safety-critical flight software must pass a formal inspection.
- **Medical Devices (FDA, IEC 62304)**: Software in pacemakers, infusion pumps requires documented FTRs as part of regulatory submission.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262)**: Functional safety standards mandate reviews at each ASIL level.
- **Banking & Finance**: Code reviews are often augmented with formal inspection for transaction-critical modules.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & FTR Conduct Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Analytical Walkthrough — Conducting an FTR

Below is the **end-to-end operational breakdown** of how a Formal Technical Review is conducted. Each step is decomposed into **sub-actions, decision points, and exit criteria** as expected in KTU 14-mark long-answer questions.

### Phase 1 — Pre-Review Lifecycle

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1.1: Author completes work product (e.g., a Design Document).} \\
&\text{Step 1.2: Author submits work product to the Review Leader.} \\
&\text{Step 1.3: Review Leader confirms product is at the correct maturity level for review.} \\
&\text{Step 1.4: Review Leader selects reviewers (typically 2–5 domain experts).} \\
&\text{Step 1.5: Review Leader distributes work product and the Review Checklist to all participants.} \\
&\text{Step 1.6: Review Date and Duration are fixed (typical meeting = 1 to 2 hours max).}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic**: This phase ensures the work product is *ready* for review and that *qualified personnel* will evaluate it. KTU examiners award 2 marks for correctly identifying the planning responsibilities.

### Phase 2 — Individual Preparation (The Silent Detection Phase)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 2.1: Each reviewer reads the work product independently.} \\
&\text{Step 2.2: Reviewers compare the product against the Review Checklist (e.g., "Are all requirements traceable?").} \\
&\text{Step 2.3: Reviewers mark defects, ambiguities, omissions, and questions.} \\
&\text{Step 2.4: Reviewers prepare a list of questions / concerns to raise in the meeting.} \\
&\text{Step 2.5: Reviewers submit their notes to the Scribe in advance (optional but recommended).}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic**: This phase is the *defect-rich* phase. Empirical studies (cited in Sommerville) show that **70–80% of all defects** are identified during *individual review* — not during the group meeting. The meeting is primarily for *confirmation and consensus*.

### Phase 3 — The Review Meeting (FTR Core)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 3.1: Review Leader calls the meeting to order and outlines the agenda.} \\
&\text{Step 3.2: The Author/Reader walks the team through the work product section by section.} \\
&\text{Step 3.3: Reviewers raise issues; Author responds to clarify or defend design choices.} \\
&\text{Step 3.4: Scribe records every issue verbatim in the Review Log.} \\
&\text{Step 3.5: At each major section, the team pauses to assess "Continue or Stop?"} \\
&\text{Step 3.6: Meeting concludes with one of three decisions:}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Decision} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Accept: Product ready to proceed to next process step.} \\[4pt]
\text{Reject: Product has major flaws; significant rework required.} \\[4pt]
\text{Revise: Product has minor issues; author fixes and submits for follow-up.}
\end{cases}
$$

### Phase 4 — Post-Review Activities

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 4.1: Author performs rework on all issues in the Review Log.} \\
&\text{Step 4.2: Review Leader verifies each issue is resolved (or accepted as a known risk).} \\
&\text{Step 4.3: Review Leader signs off the Review Log, marking FTR as officially closed.} \\
&\text{Step 4.4: Review metrics are collected (see Section 3.2 below).} \\
&\text{Step 4.5: Work product advances to the next stage (e.g., design → implementation, or code → unit test).}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 The FTR Metrics — A Quantifiable Quality Gate

Although FTRs are qualitative in nature, **metrics are collected** to measure effectiveness:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{preparedness}} &= \frac{T_{\text{prep}}}{T_{\text{meeting}}} \quad \text{(Should be } \geq 1.0 \text{ — preparation time } \geq \text{ meeting time)} \\[6pt]
D_{\text{detection}} &= \frac{N_{\text{defects found in FTR}}}{N_{\text{defects found in later stages}} + N_{\text{defects found in FTR}}} \quad \text{(FTR effectiveness ratio)} \\[6pt]
R_{\text{rework}} &= \frac{N_{\text{issues fixed}}}{N_{\text{issues logged in Review Log}}} \times 100\% \quad \text{(Should be } 100\% \text{ at closure)} \\[6pt]
C_{\text{cost-avoidance}} &= \sum_{i} \left( D_i \times \Delta C_i \right) \quad \text{(Total cost avoidance from early detection)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Notation Guide**:
> - $T_{\text{prep}}$ = Total reviewer preparation hours.
> - $T_{\text{meeting}}$ = Meeting duration hours.
> - $D_i$ = Number of defects of type $i$ caught at the FTR.
> - $\Delta C_i$ = Cost difference between fixing defect $i$ at FTR vs. at deployment.
> - $C_{\text{cost-avoidance}}$ is the **engineering value** of conducting FTRs, often used in project ROI justification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip**: When a 14-mark question asks "Explain FTR metrics", present the **three most cited metrics**: (1) Preparation-to-meeting time ratio, (2) Defect detection rate, and (3) Cost-avoidance estimation. Do not include overly obscure metrics.

## 3.3 Failure Mode Analysis — Why FTRs Fail

| # | Failure Mode | Root Cause | Prevention |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Review becomes a design discussion | Confusing "find errors" with "fix errors" | Enforce scope discipline; defer solutions to post-meeting |
| 2 | Author defends ego | Social pressure | Reviewer training; ground rules; "blameless" culture |
| 3 | Insufficient preparation | Time pressure | Mandate ≥ 1 hour prep per ~20 pages of material |
| 4 | Scope creep | Reviewing beyond the work product | Review leader enforces the Review Checklist |
| 5 | Inadequate follow-up | Loose closure | Review Log closure check; signed sign-off |

## 3.4 FTR vs. Other Quality Activities — Decision Matrix

| Dimension | FTR | Walkthrough | Inspection | Testing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Detects Defects** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes (dynamic) |
| **Detects Omissions** | Yes (strong) | Yes | Yes (strong) | No |
| **Detects Inconsistencies** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Limited |
| **Static or Dynamic** | Static | Static | Static | Dynamic |
| **Cost-Effectiveness** | High | Medium | Very High | Medium |
| **Best Stage** | Design, Code | Early design | Safety-critical | Code complete |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The FTR Process Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Author completes work product]) --> B[Author submits to Review Leader]
    B --> C{Material mature enough?}
    C -- No --> A
    C -- Yes --> D[Review Leader selects review team]
    D --> E[Distribute work product + checklist to reviewers]
    E --> F[Individual review preparation by each reviewer]
    F --> G[Reviewers log issues in personal notes]
    G --> H[Convene FTR Meeting with Author + Reviewers + Scribe]
    H --> I[Reader walks through work product]
    I --> J[Reviewers raise issues; Scribe logs in Review Log]
    J --> K{Decision at end of meeting}
    K -- Accept --> L[Work product advances to next stage]
    K -- Revise minor --> M[Author performs rework]
    K -- Reject major --> M
    M --> N[Review Leader verifies all issues resolved]
    N --> O{All issues closed?}
    O -- No --> M
    O -- Yes --> L
    L --> P([FTR officially closed - metrics collected])
    P --> Q[Process continues to next lifecycle activity]
```

> **Figure Note**: Each node in the diagram represents a discrete, observable activity in the FTR process. The diamond-shaped decision nodes (Material mature? Decision? All issues closed?) represent **gates** where the process can return to an earlier step.

## 4.2 Mermaid Swimlane — Roles and Responsibilities Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Phase1[Phase 1: Planning]
        A1[Author: Request review] --> R1[Review Leader: Appoint team and distribute material]
        R1 --> S1[Scribe: Prepare Review Log template]
    end
    subgraph Phase2[Phase 2: Preparation]
        A2[Author: Standby for questions]
        R2[Reviewer: Read work product independently]
        R2 --> S2[Scribe: Receive pre-meeting notes]
    end
    subgraph Phase3[Phase 3: Meeting]
        A3[Author/Reader: Present and clarify]
        R3[Reviewer: Raise issues and questions]
        S3[Scribe: Log every issue in Review Log]
    end
    subgraph Phase4[Phase 4: Post-Review]
        A4[Author: Perform rework]
        R4[Review Leader: Verify closure and sign off]
        S4[Scribe: Archive Review Log for audit]
    end
    Phase1 --> Phase2 --> Phase3 --> Phase4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Hierarchy — FTR Taxonomy and Formality Gradient

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Software Quality Assurance Activities]
    Root --> Static[Static Verification]
    Root --> Dynamic[Dynamic Verification - Testing]
    Static --> FTR[Formal Technical Reviews]
    FTR --> Inf[Informal Review]
    FTR --> Walk[Walkthrough]
    FTR --> TR[Technical Review]
    FTR --> Insp[Inspection]
    Inf --> InfNote[Lowest formality; no formal process]
    Walk --> WalkNote[Author-led; knowledge transfer focus]
    TR --> TRNote[Peer-led; conformance to standards]
    Insp --> InspNote[Most formal; Fagan process; mandatory roles]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — FTR as a Process Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Software Work Product e.g. Design, Code, Specs] --> F1[Filter 1: Author Self-Review]
    F1 --> F2[Filter 2: Peer Pair Review]
    F2 --> F3[Filter 3: Formal Technical Review]
    F3 --> Quality[Quality-Checked Output]
    F3 -.-> DefLog[Review Log - all defects documented]
    DefLog --> Metrics[Metrics: Defect Density, FTR Effectiveness]
    Metrics --> Process[Process Improvement - Lessons Learned]
    Process -.feedback loop.-> F1
```

> **Interpretation**: This diagram positions the FTR as the **third filter** in a layered quality assurance pipeline. The arrows back to Filter 1 (Process Improvement loop) reflect the **Kaizen** / continuous improvement principle — FTR data is used to improve upstream development practices.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Q: Define Formal Technical Review (FTR). List any TWO objectives of conducting an FTR.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 Marks):** A Formal Technical Review (FTR) is a *systematic, structured, and disciplined software quality assurance activity* performed by software engineers to identify errors, inconsistencies, and omissions in software work products (requirements, design, code, test plans). It follows a well-defined process with prescribed roles, entry/exit criteria, and formal documentation.
- **Objectives (any two for 1 Mark):**
  1. To **uncover errors, inconsistencies, and omissions** in software work products at the earliest possible stage.
  2. To **verify conformance** of the work product to organizational standards, templates, and specifications.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: Differentiate between a Walkthrough and an Inspection. (Mention any three points.)**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 per point):**

| # | Walkthrough | Inspection |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Low formality**; informal, author-led | **Highest formality**; follows Fagan's structured process |
| 2 | **No formal process** or roles defined | **Strictly defined roles** (Moderator, Reader, Scribe, Author, Inspectors) |
| 3 | **Defect logging is optional**; often verbal | **Mandatory Review Log** capturing every defect found |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Option 1*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**Q: (a)** Explain the **Formal Technical Review (FTR) process** in detail. List all the steps and describe the responsibilities of each role. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Discuss the **objectives of FTR** and explain how FTR acts as a *filter* in the software engineering process. (7 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Planning (1 Mark)**
- The author submits the work product and requests a review.
- The **Review Leader** (Moderator) plans the review: selects 2–5 reviewers, fixes a date, distributes the work product and Review Checklist in advance.

**Step 2: Individual Preparation (1 Mark)**
- Each reviewer independently studies the work product, marks defects, and prepares questions. This phase typically identifies **70–80% of all defects**.

**Step 3: The Review Meeting (2 Marks)**
- The meeting is chaired by the Review Leader.
- The **Reader/Author** walks through the product.
- Reviewers raise issues; the **Scribe** records every issue in the **Review Log**.
- The meeting ends with one of three decisions: **Accept, Revise, or Reject**.

**Step 4: Rework (1 Mark)**
- The author fixes the issues identified in the Review Log.

**Step 5: Follow-Up (1 Mark)**
- The Review Leader verifies that all issues are addressed and signs off the Review Log.
- A formal Acceptance Decision is recorded.

**Step 6: Re-Review (1 Mark — for major rework cases)**
- The work product may re-enter the FTR process if the rework is significant.

**Roles and Responsibilities (additional 0 marks but enriching the answer):**
- **Author** – Owns and presents the work product; performs rework.
- **Review Leader / Moderator** – Plans, chairs, and closes the review.
- **Scribe / Recorder** – Maintains the Review Log.
- **Reviewers** – Independently identify defects.
- **Reader** – Walks the team through the work product.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Objectives of FTR (4 Marks):**
1. **Uncover errors** in requirements, design, and code at the earliest stage — the **primary objective**.
2. **Verify conformance** to organizational standards, templates, and style guides.
3. **Ensure uniformity** of software development across teams and projects.
4. **Develop more maintainable software** by enforcing quality at each artifact.
5. **Transfer technical knowledge** among team members (junior–senior peer learning).

**FTR as a Filter (3 Marks):**
- In the software engineering process, several **filters** are applied at different stages: developer self-review, unit testing, integration testing, and FTRs.
- The FTR is positioned **between author creation and downstream activities** (e.g., between design and code, or between code and unit test).
- Defects that pass through earlier filters (such as author self-checks) are **caught by the FTR**, preventing them from propagating into the next development stage.
- The **FTR filter** is:
  - **Static** (examines the work product without executing it).
  - **Cost-effective** (defects caught here cost orders of magnitude less than defects caught after deployment).
  - **Continuous** (applied iteratively at each development phase).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Option 2 (Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**Q: (a)** Compare and contrast **Walkthrough, Technical Review, and Inspection** with respect to formality, process, roles, and defect logging. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Describe **Fagan's Inspection Process**. Why is it considered the gold standard for safety-critical software? (7 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Comparison Table (5 Marks — comprehensive table):**

| Attribute | Walkthrough | Technical Review | Inspection |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Formality** | Low–Medium | Medium | Very High (most formal) |
| **Process** | Informal; agenda optional | Partially defined | Fully defined (Fagan process) |
| **Roles** | Author-led; no strict roles | Reviewer-led; defined roles | Strictly defined: Moderator, Reader, Scribe, Author, Inspectors |
| **Defect Logging** | Optional; often verbal | Yes; logged during meeting | Mandatory; formal Review Log |
| **Originator** | Authors / academic | IEEE Std 1028 | Michael Fagan (IBM, 1976) |
| **Best Use** | Knowledge transfer, early design | Standards compliance | Safety-critical domains |
| **Meeting Duration** | Variable | 1–2 hours | 1–2 hours (strictly bounded) |

**Key Insights (2 Marks):**
- The three review types represent a **gradient of formality**: Walkthrough (lowest) → Technical Review (medium) → Inspection (highest).
- **Inspections** are the only FTR type that mandates (a) a pre-defined process, (b) a pre-distributed Review Checklist, and (c) a formal Review Log signed off by the Moderator.
- **Walkthroughs** are primarily learning-oriented, not defect-oriented.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Fagan's Inspection Process (5 Marks — list the six stages):**

1. **Planning** — Moderator selects team, distributes materials.
2. **Overview** (Optional) — Author presents the work product to orient inspectors (used for complex artifacts).
3. **Individual Preparation** — Each inspector studies the material independently, marks defects against a checklist.
4. **Inspection Meeting** — Reader walks through; inspectors raise issues; Scribe logs everything. No solutions are discussed.
5. **Rework** — Author fixes logged defects.
6. **Follow-Up** — Moderator verifies all defects are addressed; sign-off the inspection.

**Why Fagan's Inspection is the Gold Standard for Safety-Critical Software (2 Marks):**
1. **Rigorous defect detection** — Empirical data shows inspections can detect **60–90% of defects** in a single pass, far exceeding the effectiveness of dynamic testing alone.
2. **Audit trail** — The Review Log provides a **regulatory-grade audit trail**, required by standards like **DO-178C (aviation)**, **IEC 62304 (medical devices)**, and **ISO 26262 (automotive)**.
3. **Process repeatability** — Defined roles and entry/exit criteria make inspections **reproducible and certifiable**, a legal requirement in safety-critical domains.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the Reviewer with the Author** — Reviewers *find* defects; the Author *fixes* them. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 2. **Do NOT claim FTRs "find and fix" defects** — FTRs are *static verification* activities that *find and log* defects. Fixing happens in the *rework* phase, which is separate.
> 3. **Do NOT list Inspection as "conducted by anyone"** — Inspections require *trained* inspectors. Untrained reviewers are explicitly disallowed in Fagan's model.
> 4. **Do NOT omit the Review Log** — When asked about FTR artifacts, the **Review Log is mandatory** in your answer. Examiners look for it specifically.
> 5. **For numerical questions, do NOT confuse the FTR effectiveness ratio** with testing effectiveness — FTRs operate on *static* artifacts, not running code.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **FTR = Formal Technical Review** = a *static*, *systematic*, *structured* software quality assurance activity.
- ✅ **Five Objectives**: (1) Uncover errors, (2) Verify standards conformance, (3) Ensure uniformity, (4) Develop maintainable software, (5) Transfer knowledge.
- ✅ **FTR as a Filter** — Catches defects that pass through earlier filters (e.g., self-review, pair review).
- ✅ **Four Review Types** (formality gradient): **Informal Review → Walkthrough → Technical Review → Inspection**.
- ✅ **Inspection** is the **most formal** FTR type, originating from **Michael Fagan (IBM, 1976)**.
- ✅ **Six FTR Process Steps**: Planning → Individual Preparation → Review Meeting → Rework → Follow-Up → (Optional) Re-Review.
- ✅ **Five Core Roles**: Author, Review Leader (Moderator), Scribe (Recorder), Reviewers (Inspectors), Reader.
- ✅ **Reviewers find defects; Author fixes defects** — *never* the reverse.
- ✅ **Review Log is mandatory** for Inspections and Technical Reviews; optional for Walkthroughs.
- ✅ **70–80% of defects** are identified during *individual preparation*, not the meeting itself.
- ✅ **Cost-effectiveness**: FTRs catch defects at **1–10% of the cost** of catching them post-deployment.
- ✅ **FTR Metrics to remember**: (1) Preparation-to-meeting time ratio, (2) Defect detection rate, (3) Rework closure rate.
- ✅ **Regulatory domains using FTRs**: Aviation (DO-178C), Medical (IEC 62304), Automotive (ISO 26262), Nuclear (NRC standards).
- ✅ **Common 14-mark question structure**: (a) FTR process + roles [7 marks], (b) Objectives + filter concept [7 marks].
- ✅ **Common 3-mark question structure**: Define FTR + list 2 objectives; OR compare two review types in 3 points.
- ✅ **Static vs Dynamic**: FTRs are **static** (no code execution); testing is **dynamic** (requires code execution).
- ✅ **Exit criteria for FTR**: Review Log signed off; all defects either fixed or accepted as risks; work product ready for next lifecycle stage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Post-Mortem Evaluations. Software Testing Strategies - Unit Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Post-Mortem Evaluations. Software Testing Strategies - Unit Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — Post-Mortem Evaluations & Software Testing Strategies (Unit Testing)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Context**
> This topic sits at the intersection of *Project Closure* (Post-Mortem) and *Software Verification* (Testing). As per the KTU 2024 syllabus outcome **CO3**, students must demonstrate the ability to apply testing strategies at the unit level and conduct retrospective analyses on completed projects. Combined weightage in typical KTU ESE papers: **8–12 marks** (Module 3 carries ~20% of total marks).

---

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 🔹 Post-Mortem Evaluation (Project Post-Mortem)

A **Post-Mortem Evaluation** (also called *Project Retrospective* or *Lessons Learned Review*) is a structured, formal review conducted **at the end of a software project** (or major milestone) to:
1. Determine what was delivered successfully versus what was planned.
2. Identify the root causes of problems, defects, schedule slippage, or budget overrun.
3. Document **actionable lessons learned** to improve future project performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> *A Post-Mortem Evaluation is a quality assurance activity performed after product delivery to assess the project’s adherence to the original Software Requirements Specification (SRS), analyse deviations, and record best practices for organizational learning.*

### 🔹 Software Testing Strategies — An Overview

A **Software Testing Strategy** is the systematic plan that integrates test-case design, test execution, and test evaluation into a coherent sequence of phases. KTU 2024 recognizes the following strategic levels (top-down):

1. **Unit Testing** (Module-level testing)
2. **Integration Testing** (Interface-level testing)
3. **System Testing** (Full software validation)
4. **Acceptance Testing** (User-side validation)

> [!TIP]
> **Why call it a "Strategy"?** Because testing is not random — it follows a planned, layered, and risk-based approach. Each layer refines the granularity of defect detection.

### 🔹 Unit Testing — The Core Focus of This Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024)**
> *Unit Testing is the testing of individual software units (modules, components, functions, or classes) in isolation from the rest of the program, with the objective of validating that each unit performs as designed in its specification.*

A **"Unit"** is the *smallest testable part* of any software:
- A function or procedure (procedural languages).
- A method or class (object-oriented languages).
- A single source file (C/C++ modules).

### 🌱 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> Imagine you are assembling a **luxury car** on an assembly line.
> Before installing the *engine* into the chassis, the mechanic tests the engine **on a test bench** (in isolation). The mechanic revs it, checks oil pressure, listens for abnormal sounds, and confirms fuel flow.
>
> Only after the engine passes its **bench test** is it lowered into the car and tested again with the transmission.
>
> - **Engine on test bench** ≈ **Unit Testing**
> - **Engine installed in the car** ≈ **Integration Testing**
> - **Car driven on a test track** ≈ **System Testing**
>
> Without unit-testing the engine first, a defect inside it would be nearly impossible to localize once everything is assembled.

### 🧭 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Plain Meaning | KTU-Standard Term |
|---|---|---|
| Unit | Smallest testable piece | Function / Class / Module |
| Driver | Code that calls the unit under test | Test Harness (Top-Down) |
| Stub | Dummy module that replaces a called unit | Test Harness (Bottom-Up) |
| Test Case | Input + execution condition + expected result | Test Specification Item |
| Test Oracle | Mechanism that decides pass/fail | Verification Oracle |
| Coverage | % of code/paths exercised | Structural Coverage Metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bolded core metric**: The most common unit-test coverage metric used in KTU evaluations is **Branch Coverage** and **Statement Coverage**, often expressed as a percentage between **0%** and **100%**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Post-Mortem Evaluation — Structural Analysis

A Post-Mortem is **not** a blame session. It is a **forensic analysis** with three phases:

### Phase 1 — Data Collection
- Gather metrics: *Earned Value Analysis*, defect logs, change requests, schedule variance.
- Collect team feedback (anonymous surveys preferred to reduce bias).
- Compile deliverable artefacts: SRS, SDD, test reports.

### Phase 2 — Analysis
Apply **Root-Cause Analysis (RCA)** techniques:

- **5 Whys**: Iteratively ask "Why?" five times to drill from symptom to root cause.
- **Fishbone Diagram (Ishikawa)**: Categorize causes into *People, Process, Product, Tools, Environment*.
- **Pareto Analysis (80/20 rule)**: 80% of problems stem from 20% of causes.

### Phase 3 — Documentation & Dissemination
- Produce a **Post-Mortem Report** containing:
  1. Project summary
  2. What went well (continue doing)
  3. What went wrong (stop doing)
  4. Lessons learned (start doing)
  5. Action items with owners and due dates
- Archive in the **Organizational Process Asset (OPA)** database.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip**: When a question asks "Explain Post-Mortem Evaluation," always mention the **three phases** (Collection → Analysis → Documentation) and at least **one RCA technique**.

---

## 2.2 Software Testing Strategies — Architectural View

The KTU-recognised test strategy is a **W-model** (a V-model extension) emphasizing that *each development phase has a corresponding test phase*.

| Development Phase | Corresponding Test Phase | Defect Category Caught |
|---|---|---|
| Requirements | Acceptance Test Planning | Requirement ambiguities |
| System Design | System Testing | Architectural flaws |
| Detailed Design | Integration Testing | Interface errors |
| Coding | **Unit Testing** | Logic & data errors |
| — | Regression Testing | Re-introduced defects |

---

## 2.3 Unit Testing — Deep Conceptual Dive

### 🔸 Objectives of Unit Testing
1. **Validate** the unit's functional behaviour against its module specification.
2. **Reveal** logic errors, boundary-value errors, and arithmetic faults early.
3. **Provide** documentation of the unit's actual behaviour.
4. **Enable** safe refactoring (change code confidently because tests will catch breakage).

### 🔸 Unit Test Environment

A unit cannot execute in a vacuum — it needs:
- **The unit under test (UUT)** itself.
- **Drivers** — call the UUT and pass test inputs (used in *top-down* testing).
- **Stubs** — stand-in for sub-modules called by the UUT (used in *bottom-up* testing).
- **Test Oracles** — verify the output matches expectation.

### 🔸 Unit Test Procedure (KTU Standard Sequence)

1. **Plan**: Identify units, define test criteria, choose tools.
2. **Design Test Cases**: Equivalence partitioning, boundary-value analysis, statement/branch coverage.
3. **Build Test Harness**: Write drivers and stubs.
4. **Execute**: Run tests; capture actual outputs.
5. **Compare with Oracle**: Pass/Fail determination.
6. **Log Defects**: Open defect reports for failures.
7. **Fix and Re-test**: Developer fixes the UUT; tests re-run until all pass.
8. **Regression Check**: Ensure fixes did not break previously-passing units.

### 🔸 Categories of Unit Testing (by Visibility)

| Type | What is known | KTU Term | Tool Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **White-Box (Structural)** | Internal code, control flow, paths | Glass-box testing | Coverage analyzers |
| **Black-Box (Functional)** | Only input/output behaviour | Behavioural testing | JUnit assertions |
| **Grey-Box** | Limited internal knowledge + behaviour | Hybrid | DB-aware tests |

### 🔸 Code Coverage Metrics (KTU High-Yield Formulas)

Coverage metrics quantify *how thoroughly* the unit test exercises the code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bolded symbol convention**: $C_s$ = Statement Coverage, $C_b$ = Branch Coverage, $C_p$ = Path Coverage, $V(G)$ = Cyclomatic Complexity.

The **Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G)$ of a unit's control flow graph (CFG) is the central formula:

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

where $E$ = number of edges, $N$ = number of nodes, $P$ = number of connected components (usually **1** for a single function, hence $P=1$ gives the most common variant $V(G)=E-N+2$).

Equivalently:

$$V(G) = \pi + 1$$

where $\pi$ is the number of *predicate (decision) nodes* (e.g., `if`, `while`, `for`, `case`).

Equivalently:

$$V(G) = \text{(Number of regions in the flow graph)}$$

---

### 📋 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Revision Table

| # | Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Statement Coverage | $C_s = \dfrac{S_e}{S_t} \times 100\%$ | Lowest acceptable coverage |
| 2 | Branch Coverage | $C_b = \dfrac{B_e}{B_t} \times 100\%$ | Industry gold standard |
| 3 | Cyclomatic Complexity | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | Test cases required $\ge V(G)$ |
| 4 | Path Coverage | $C_p = \dfrac{P_e}{P_t} \times 100\%$ | Most rigorous (often infeasible) |
| 5 | Defect Density | $DD = \dfrac{D}{KLoC}$ | Defects per kilo-LOC |
| 6 | Mutation Score | $MS = \dfrac{M_k}{M_t} \times 100\%$ | Test-suite effectiveness |
| 7 | Lines of Code | $LoC$ | Effort estimation baseline |

where:
- $S_e$ = statements executed; $S_t$ = total statements.
- $B_e$ = branches executed; $B_t$ = total branches.
- $P_e$ = paths executed; $P_t$ = total feasible paths.
- $D$ = defects found; $KLoC$ = kilo-lines of code.
- $M_k$ = mutants killed; $M_t$ = total mutants generated.

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Automotive (DO-178C / ISO 26262)**: Mandate **MC/DC coverage** (Modified Condition/Decision Coverage) — every branch decision outcome must be independently shown to affect the outcome. Direct application of branch + path metrics.
- **Banking & Finance**: White-box unit testing is required for compliance with **PCI-DSS**.
- **Open-Source & DevOps**: Continuous Integration (CI) pipelines (e.g., Jenkins, GitHub Actions) automatically run unit tests on every commit, enforcing a minimum **70–80% branch coverage** gate.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Worked Example — Computing Cyclomatic Complexity $V(G)$

### 📝 Problem
Consider the following pseudo-code for a unit `calculateGrade(score)`:

```
1. FUNCTION calculateGrade(score):
2.   IF score >= 90:
3.       RETURN 'A'
4.   ELSE IF score >= 75:
5.       RETURN 'B'
6.   ELSE IF score >= 60:
7.       RETURN 'C'
8.   ELSE:
9.       RETURN 'F'
```

**Question:** Determine the **Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G)$ using all three methods. How many test cases are *at minimum* required to achieve full branch coverage?

---

### 🧮 Method 1 — Predicate Node Method

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decision nodes} \; \pi &= \text{(IF at line 2)} + \text{(ELSE IF at line 4)} + \text{(ELSE IF at line 6)} \\
&= 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 \\
V(G) &= \pi + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 🧮 Method 2 — Flow-Graph Region Method

Construct the CFG:

- **Nodes**: 1 (entry), 2 (decision: score ≥ 90), 3 (decision: score ≥ 75), 4 (decision: score ≥ 60), 5 (exit), plus return nodes for 'A', 'B', 'C', 'F' merge into 5.
- **Edges**: 1→2, 2→3 (false), 2→A (true), 3→4 (false), 3→B (true), 4→5 (false), 4→C (true), else→F→5.

Simplifying for region counting: there are **4 enclosed regions** in the CFG → $V(G)=4$.

---

### 🧮 Method 3 — Edge-Node Method

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 8 \quad \text{(edges in the CFG)} \\
N &= 6 \quad \text{(entry + 3 decisions + merge + exit)} \\
P &= 1 \quad \text{(single connected component)} \\
V(G) &= E - N + 2P = 8 - 6 + 2(1) = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

All three methods agree: $\boxed{V(G) = 4}$.

---

### 📋 Minimum Test Cases for Branch Coverage

| # | Input (score) | Expected Output | Branch Covered |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 95 | 'A' | score ≥ 90 → TRUE |
| 2 | 80 | 'B' | score ≥ 90 FALSE, score ≥ 75 TRUE |
| 3 | 65 | 'C' | first two FALSE, score ≥ 60 TRUE |
| 4 | 40 | 'F' | All FALSE → ELSE |

> **4 test cases** are required to traverse all **4 independent paths** identified by $V(G)=4$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Computing Coverage Percentages

### 📝 Problem
A unit has $S_t = 120$ statements. After running the unit test suite:
- Statements executed: $S_e = 108$
- Total branches: $B_t = 30$
- Branches executed: $B_e = 24$

**Compute:** (a) Statement Coverage, (b) Branch Coverage.

---

### (a) Statement Coverage

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_s &= \frac{S_e}{S_t} \times 100\% \\
    &= \frac{108}{120} \times 100\% \\
    &= 0.90 \times 100\% \\
C_s &= 90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### (b) Branch Coverage

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_b &= \frac{B_e}{B_t} \times 100\% \\
    &= \frac{24}{30} \times 100\% \\
    &= 0.80 \times 100\% \\
C_b &= 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Post-Mortem Evaluation — Sample Analytical Matrix

For a hypothetical KTU team project, suppose the following were recorded at project closure:

| Metric | Planned | Actual | Variance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Effort (person-months) | 12 | 16 | +33% |
| Schedule (weeks) | 20 | 26 | +30% |
| Defects delivered | 0 | 7 | +7 |
| Cost (₹ lakhs) | 5.0 | 6.4 | +28% |
| Requirements implemented | 45 | 42 | −3 |

### 5-Whys Analysis (for schedule slippage)

| Why # | Question | Answer |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Why is the schedule late by 6 weeks? | Integration testing took 4 extra weeks. |
| 2 | Why did integration testing take longer? | Many interfaces were incompatible. |
| 3 | Why were interfaces incompatible? | Modules were developed with mismatched assumptions. |
| 4 | Why were assumptions mismatched? | No formal interface design review (IDR) was held. |
| 5 | Why was no IDR held? | Project manager omitted it from the WBS. |

**Root Cause** → Incomplete WBS planning.
**Action Item** → Mandate IDRs for all future projects.

---

## 3.4 Defect Density Calculation Example

A unit has 4 known defects. Its size is 2,000 lines of code (2 KLOC).

$$
DD = \frac{D}{KLoC} = \frac{4}{2} = 2 \; \text{defects/KLoC}
$$

**Industry benchmark (typical)**:
- **< 1 defect/KLoC** → Excellent.
- **1 – 5 defects/KLoC** → Average.
- **> 5 defects/KLoC** → Poor.

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — Unit Testing Techniques

| Technique | Type | Strength | Weakness | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Equivalence Partitioning | Black-box | Reduces test cases | May miss boundary cases | Input validation units |
| Boundary Value Analysis | Black-box | Catches off-by-one errors | Doesn't test internal logic | Numeric input units |
| Statement Coverage | White-box | Simple metric | Misses untaken branches | Minimum acceptance |
| Branch Coverage | White-box | Better than statement | Misses some paths | Industry standard |
| Path Coverage | White-box | Most thorough | Often infeasible (loops) | Safety-critical code |
| Mutation Testing | White-box | Measures test efficacy | Expensive to run | Critical algorithms |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Unit Testing in the Broader Software Testing Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Software Product] --> A1[Acceptance Testing]
    A1 --> A2[System Testing]
    A2 --> A3[Integration Testing]
    A3 --> A4[Unit Testing]
    A4 --> A5[Module A]
    A4 --> A6[Module B]
    A4 --> A7[Module C]
    A5 --> A8[Driver for A]
    A6 --> A9[Driver for B]
    A7 --> A10[Driver for C]
    A5 --> A11[Stub for called module]
    A6 --> A12[Stub for called module]
    A7 --> A13[Stub for called module]
    A3 --> B1[Integration Test Cycle]
    A2 --> C1[System Test Cycle]
    A1 --> D1[User Acceptance Test]
    D1 --> E1[Software Accepted]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Internal Unit Testing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start Unit Test] --> S1[Read Unit Specification]
    S1 --> S2[Identify Inputs and Outputs]
    S2 --> S3[Design Test Cases using BVA and EP]
    S3 --> S4[Build Test Harness: Driver plus Stubs]
    S4 --> S5[Execute Test Case]
    S5 --> S6{Output Matches Oracle}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Mark as PASS]
    S6 -- No --> S8[Mark as FAIL]
    S8 --> S9[Log Defect in Tracker]
    S9 --> S10[Developer Fixes Unit]
    S10 --> S11[Re-execute Test]
    S11 --> S6
    S7 --> S12[Add to Regression Suite]
    S12 --> S13[All Units Tested]
    S13 --> S14[End Unit Testing Phase]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Post-Mortem Evaluation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Project Closure Trigger] --> P1[Phase 1: Data Collection]
    P1 --> P1a[Gather Metrics: Effort Schedule Cost Defects]
    P1 --> P1b[Collect Team Feedback via Survey]
    P1 --> P1c[Compile Project Artefacts]
    P2[Phase 2: Analysis] --> P2a[Apply 5 Whys Technique]
    P2 --> P2b[Apply Fishbone Ishikawa Diagram]
    P2 --> P2c[Apply Pareto 80 20 Rule]
    P3[Phase 3: Documentation and Dissemination] --> P3a[Draft Post Mortem Report]
    P3 --> P3b[Conduct Lessons Learned Meeting]
    P3 --> P3c[Archive in OPA Database]
    P3d[Action Items with Owners and Due Dates] --> P4[Improvement of Future Projects]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Driver and Stub Architecture (Bottom-Up vs Top-Down)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TopDownIntegration
    TD_Driver1[Driver] --> TD_Main[Main Module]
    TD_Main --> TD_Sub1[Sub Module 1]
    TD_Main --> TD_Sub2[Sub Module 2]
    TD_Sub1 --> TD_StubA[Stub]
    TD_Sub2 --> TD_StubB[Stub]
    end
    subgraph BottomUpIntegration
    BU_Sub1[Sub Module 1] --> BU_DriverTest1[Driver Test]
    BU_Sub2[Sub Module 2] --> BU_DriverTest2[Driver Test]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: In **Top-Down** integration, the *Driver* sits on top, calling the unit under test, while *Stubs* stand in for the called sub-units. In **Bottom-Up** integration, the actual sub-modules are tested in isolation using a *Driver* harness.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Post-Mortem Evaluation. List its three main phases.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Post-Mortem Evaluation is a structured review conducted at the end of a software project to identify what went well, what went wrong, and what can be improved in future projects.

The three main phases are:
1. **Data Collection** — gathering metrics, team feedback, and project artefacts.
2. **Analysis** — applying root-cause techniques like *5 Whys*, *Fishbone Diagram*, or *Pareto Analysis*.
3. **Documentation and Dissemination** — producing a Post-Mortem Report and archiving lessons learned in the Organizational Process Asset (OPA) database.

*(Award 1 mark each for the definition and the two key points; full marks for listing all three phases clearly.)*

---

### **Q2. What is Unit Testing? Differentiate between a Driver and a Stub.** `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Unit Testing** is the testing of individual software units (functions, methods, or classes) in isolation to verify that each unit works according to its specification.

| Aspect | Driver | Stub |
|---|---|---|
| **Role** | Calls the unit under test | Replaces a called sub-module |
| **Used in** | Bottom-up integration | Top-down integration |
| **Purpose** | Provide test inputs and invoke the UUT | Simulate the response of a non-existent or unfinished module |
| **Example** | A `main()` routine that invokes the UUT with sample inputs | A dummy function returning a fixed value |

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the Driver vs Stub distinction.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **omit the formula** for Cyclomatic Complexity — **always write** $V(G)=E-N+2P$ explicitly.
> - Many confuse **Branch Coverage** with **Path Coverage** — branch covers *each decision outcome*; path covers *all execution routes* (often impossible for loops).
> - In Post-Mortem answers, students describe the phases but **forget to mention Root-Cause Analysis techniques** (5 Whys / Fishbone) — this loses ~3 marks.
> - For Driver/Stub questions, drawing a **diagram** (as in Section 4.4) earns an additional **1–2 marks**. Always include one.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

#### **(a) Explain the different levels of Software Testing Strategy in detail. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Software Testing Strategy follows a hierarchical, top-down structure:

1. **Unit Testing** — Tests individual modules/functions in isolation using drivers and stubs. Catches logic errors early.
2. **Integration Testing** — Combines units and tests their interfaces. Two strategies:
   - *Top-Down*: Uses drivers and stubs; tests high-level modules first.
   - *Bottom-Up*: Uses drivers to test low-level modules first.
3. **System Testing** — Tests the complete integrated system against requirements. Includes performance, load, security, and recovery testing.
4. **Acceptance Testing** — Conducted by the end-user/customer. Includes:
   - *Alpha Testing* (in-house at developer site)
   - *Beta Testing* (at customer site)
   - *User Acceptance Test (UAT)* (formal sign-off)
5. **Regression Testing** — Re-runs previous tests after a change to ensure no new defects.

**Diagram of the W-Model**:
Development phases (Requirements → Design → Coding) run parallel to Test phases (Acceptance → System → Integration → Unit). Each development activity has a corresponding verification and validation activity.

*(Allocate marks: ~1 mark per level, 2 marks for the W-model/W-strategy relationship.)*

---

#### **(b) Compute the Cyclomatic Complexity of the following code and determine the minimum number of test cases required for branch coverage. (7 marks)**

```c
int processOrder(int qty, int isMember) {
    int discount = 0;
    if (qty > 50) {
        discount = 10;
    }
    else if (qty > 20) {
        discount = 5;
    }
    if (isMember) {
        discount += 2;
    }
    return discount;
}
```

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Count Decision (Predicate) Nodes:**
- `qty > 50` → 1
- `else if (qty > 20)` → 1
- `isMember` → 1

Total $\pi = 3$.

**Step 2 — Apply Cyclomatic Complexity Formula:**

$$
V(G) = \pi + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4
$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent Method (Edge-Node):**

Edges $E = 9$, Nodes $N = 7$, $P = 1$.

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P = 9 - 7 + 2 = 4
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum Test Cases for Branch Coverage = $V(G) = 4$**

| # | qty | isMember | Expected discount | Branches Covered |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 60 (TRUE for >50) | TRUE | 10+2 = 12 | qty>50 T, isMember T |
| 2 | 60 | FALSE | 10 | qty>50 T, isMember F |
| 3 | 30 (TRUE for >20, FALSE for >50) | TRUE | 5+2 = 7 | qty>50 F, qty>20 T, isMember T |
| 4 | 10 (all FALSE) | FALSE | 0 | qty>50 F, qty>20 F, isMember F |

All **4 branches** of the three decision nodes are covered.

*(Allocate marks: 2 for identifying decision nodes, 2 for applying the formula, 3 for designing the test cases.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### **(a) Discuss the objectives and procedure of Unit Testing. Explain the role of Drivers and Stubs with suitable diagrams. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Objectives of Unit Testing:**
1. Verify each unit's functionality against its specification.
2. Detect logic, arithmetic, and boundary errors early.
3. Enable safe refactoring of code.
4. Provide a regression baseline.

**Procedure (KTU-Standard Sequence):**
1. **Plan** the unit test (identify units, define pass/fail criteria).
2. **Design test cases** using techniques like *Equivalence Partitioning* and *Boundary Value Analysis*.
3. **Build the test harness** — drivers and stubs.
4. **Execute** the test cases.
5. **Compare actual vs expected** using the test oracle.
6. **Log defects** in the bug tracker.
7. **Fix and re-test** until all pass.
8. **Add to regression suite.**

**Drivers and Stubs — Diagram:**

```
   ┌─────────────┐
   │   Driver    │  ← invokes UUT
   └──────┬──────┘
          ▼
   ┌─────────────┐
   │  Unit UUT   │  ← calls Stub
   └──────┬──────┘
          ▼
   ┌─────────────┐
   │    Stub     │  ← returns dummy data
   └─────────────┘
```

- **Driver**: A small piece of code that calls the UUT, supplies inputs, and prints results.
- **Stub**: A dummy routine that mimics a called sub-module by returning pre-defined values.

---

#### **(b) What is a Post-Mortem Evaluation? Explain the 5-Whys technique with a suitable example. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Post-Mortem Evaluation** is a structured review of a completed project to capture lessons learned, identify root causes of failures, and improve future project performance.

**5-Whys Technique:**
This iterative questioning method drills from a *symptom* down to its *root cause* by repeatedly asking "Why?" — typically five times.

**Example — A real software team scenario:**

| Why # | Question | Answer |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Why was the system rejected during User Acceptance Testing? | Users reported login failures. |
| 2 | Why were login failures reported? | The authentication API returned 500 errors. |
| 3 | Why did the API return 500 errors? | The password hashing module threw a null-pointer exception. |
| 4 | Why did the module throw a null-pointer exception? | It was not handling empty input strings. |
| 5 | Why didn't it handle empty strings? | Input validation unit tests were never written. |

**Root Cause** → Incomplete unit test coverage of input validation.

**Corrective Action** → Mandate 100% boundary-value analysis for all input-handling units in future projects.

**Benefits of 5-Whys:**
- Simple, low-cost RCA technique.
- Encourages systemic thinking rather than blame.
- Produces actionable root causes.

*(Allocate marks: 1 for definition, 4 for the example with all 5 whys, 2 for benefits.)*

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Post-Mortem Evaluation** has three phases: **Collection → Analysis → Documentation**.
- ✅ Always mention at least one **RCA technique** (5-Whys, Fishbone, or Pareto).
- ✅ A Post-Mortem produces a **Lessons Learned Report** archived in the **OPA** database.
- ✅ **Software Testing Strategy** is hierarchical: **Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance**.
- ✅ The **W-Model** pairs each development phase with a corresponding test phase.
- ✅ **Unit Testing** = testing smallest units in isolation.
- ✅ **Driver** = calls UUT (Bottom-Up); **Stub** = replaces called sub-module (Top-Down).
- ✅ **Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G) = E - N + 2P = \pi + 1 = $ number of regions.
- ✅ **Minimum test cases for branch coverage** $\ge V(G)$.
- ✅ **Statement Coverage** $C_s = \dfrac{S_e}{S_t} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Branch Coverage** $C_b = \dfrac{B_e}{B_t} \times 100\%$ — the industry gold standard.
- ✅ **Defect Density** $DD = \dfrac{D}{KLoC}$.
- ✅ **Mutation Score** $MS = \dfrac{M_k}{M_t} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ For Part B (14 marks), always **include a diagram** — it earns 1–2 extra marks.
- ✅ Always **write the formula explicitly** — never verbalize it.
- ✅ KTU examiners reward **structured answers with headings and tables**.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Viva**: **"P-U-I-S-A-R"** → **P**ost-Mortem, **U**nit Testing, **I**ntegration, **S**ystem, **A**cceptance, **R**egression — the full software testing strategy ladder.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integration Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Integration Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Integration Testing** (also called *I&T*, *String Testing*, or *Thread Testing*) is the **software testing phase** in which individual software modules are combined and tested as a group. It occurs after **Unit Testing** and before **System Testing**. The primary objective is to expose faults in the **interaction between integrated units** — including data flow, control flow, interface mismatches, and assumption conflicts — that were not detectable when modules were tested in isolation.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEET524 – Module 3)** and the **IEEE Standard 829-2008** terminology adopted in the syllabus, integration testing is the systematic testing conducted to evaluate the compliance of a system or component with specified functional requirements, *by integrating components into a test harness or test driver framework*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Integration testing is classified as a **level of testing** (per the ISTQB / KTU glossary) — it is *not* a test type. Test types (functional, performance, security) describe *what* is tested; test levels describe *how much* of the system is tested at once.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are assembling a **bicycle from parts sourced from different workshops**:
- The **wheels** were tested individually (they roll smoothly).
- The **chain** was tested alone (it links properly).
- The **brakes** were verified in isolation (they clamp correctly).

Now, when you put the chain onto the rear wheel and the brake lever to the brake pad, you suddenly discover that the **brake cable is too short** to reach the lever, or the **gear shifter cable** clashes with the chainstay. *None of these problems could have been caught when the parts were tested in isolation.*

This is precisely what **Integration Testing** does — it verifies that the pre-tested pieces **work together as a system**, exposing the "interface" or "handshake" defects that emerge only at the seams.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary (KTU Board-Examiner Favourites)

> [!NOTE]
> The following terms appear in nearly every KTU question paper on this topic. Memorize the exact wording.

| Term | Precise Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Driver** | A *temporary replacement module* that calls a lower-level module and passes test data, used in **Bottom-Up Integration Testing**. |
| **Stub** | A *temporary replacement module* that simulates the behavior of a subordinate module and returns predefined values, used in **Top-Down Integration Testing**. |
| **Test Harness** | A collection of software and test data configured to support unit and integration testing under controlled conditions. |
| **Big-Bang Integration** | An approach where *all* components are integrated simultaneously and tested as a whole. |
| **Incremental Integration** | Integration and testing are done *one component at a time* by adding the next module to the already-tested subset. |
| **Sandwich Testing** | A hybrid strategy combining **Top-Down** and **Bottom-Up** testing simultaneously at multiple levels. |
| **Regression Testing** | Selective re-testing of a system to verify that modifications (made during integration) have not caused unintended side effects. |
| **Smoke Testing** | A preliminary integration test designed to expose simple, catastrophic failures before deeper testing begins. |

### 1.4 Position in the V-Model & KTU Testing Sequence

Integration Testing occupies a **specific slot** in the standard V-Model of software verification:

$$\text{Unit Test} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Integration Test} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{System Test} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Acceptance Test}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this order matters (frequent 2-mark question):** A fault introduced at the *unit level* is **cheapest** to fix; a fault that escapes to *system level* may require full re-architecture. Integration testing is the **first line of defence** against interface-level defects — it catches them before they cascade into system-level catastrophes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Purpose & Objectives of Integration Testing

The fundamental goals of integration testing — frequently asked in **Part A (3 marks)** — are:

- **To verify functional correctness** of the assembled combination of modules.
- **To detect interface defects** (parameter mismatch, data-type mismatch, timing issues, assumption conflicts).
- **To validate global data structures** and shared resources (e.g., database access contention).
- **To confirm control flow continuity** across module boundaries.
- **To expose sequencing / call-timing errors** that are invisible at the unit level.

### 2.2 The Two Strategic Families of Integration Testing

Integration testing is broadly classified into **two strategic families**, each with sub-variants:

#### 2.2.1 Big-Bang Integration Testing
All pre-tested modules are **integrated simultaneously in one go** and the entire system is tested as a single entity.

**Advantages:**
- Simple to plan and schedule (single integration step).
- Useful for small, tightly-coupled systems.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Fault localization is extremely difficult** — when a test fails, isolating which module caused it is painful.
- High-risk: a single critical fault can prevent any testing at all.
- Stubs and drivers must be built in advance.
- **Not recommended** for large or distributed systems (Pressman, Sommerville).

#### 2.2.2 Incremental Integration Testing
Modules are added **one at a time** to a growing test scaffold. Each new module is integrated, the larger subset is re-tested, and the process repeats. This is the **industry-recommended** approach and dominates KTU theory questions.

Incremental testing has three sub-strategies:

### 2.3 Top-Down Integration Testing (Detailed)

In Top-Down integration, testing proceeds from the **highest-level module** (the root / main control module) downward to the leaf modules.

- The **main module** is integrated and tested first using **stubs** for all directly-subordinate modules.
- Subordinate modules are integrated one (or one cluster) at a time in a **depth-first or breadth-first** traversal.
- After each new module is integrated, the **whole tree is re-tested (regression)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stubs are the cornerstone of Top-Down testing.** A stub is a *minimal program* that simulates the I/O behaviour of a called module. For example, a stub for a `calculateTax()` module might simply return a hard-coded constant like `1500.00`.

**Strategic variants within Top-Down:**
- **Depth-First** — integrate and test a complete path from root to leaf, then backtrack.
- **Breadth-First** — integrate and test all modules at level 1, then all at level 2, and so on.

**Advantages:** Early exposure of architectural / control-flow defects; supports partial system demonstration.
**Disadvantages:** Requires many stubs; low-level modules (where most real logic resides) are tested *last*.

### 2.4 Bottom-Up Integration Testing (Detailed)

In Bottom-Up integration, testing begins at the **lowest-level (leaf) modules** and progresses upward to the main control module.

- The lowest-level utility / library / atomic modules are grouped into **clusters / builds**.
- A **driver** is written to call each cluster and to provide the test data.
- After a cluster is tested, the **driver is removed** and the cluster is combined with the next-higher module.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drivers are the cornerstone of Bottom-Up testing.** A driver is a *temporary test program* whose job is to invoke the module under test, supply test inputs, capture outputs, and report results.

**Advantages:** No stubs required; low-level utility modules are tested rigorously first; easier fault localization.
**Disadvantages:** The **main control module is tested last**; the "skeleton" of the application is non-functional until late in the cycle.

### 2.5 Sandwich (Hybrid) Integration Testing

Sandwich testing is a **practical compromise** used in large real-world systems:

- The system is viewed as three layers: **Top Layer (Target)**, **Middle Layer**, and **Bottom Layer (Target)**.
- **Top-Down** is used for the upper layers, **Bottom-Up** for the lower layers, and they converge in the middle.
- Both **stubs and drivers** are used simultaneously.

> [!TIP]
> This is the strategy adopted in most industrial projects (e.g., the Linux kernel subsystem integration, microservices test harnesses). KTU examiners expect students to know it is the *most practical* and *least risky* for large systems.

### 2.6 The "Why" Behind Each Strategy — Decision Matrix

| Project Characteristic | Recommended Strategy | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Small, monolithic codebase** | Big-Bang | Simplicity outweighs fault-localization risk. |
| **Strict architectural style, critical control flow** | Top-Down | Architectural defects surface earliest. |
| **Heavy use of utility / helper modules, libraries** | Bottom-Up | Utilities are exercised thoroughly before being relied upon. |
| **Large, distributed, multi-team project** | Sandwich / Hybrid | Parallelism; isolates upper- and lower-layer concerns. |
| **Continuous-Integration / Agile environment** | Bottom-Up (or feature-level incremental) | Matches continuous-build pipelines. |

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Integration testing itself is largely conceptual and does not involve heavy numerical computation. However, the following **metrics** (the "formulas" of this topic) are routinely asked.

| # | Metric / Concept | Definition / Equation | Units | KTU Question Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Number of Stubs Required (Top-Down)** | $N_{stub} = \sum_{i=1}^{L-1} \; \text{leaves at level } i$ | count | 2 / 3 marks |
| 2 | **Number of Drivers Required (Bottom-Up)** | $N_{driver} = \text{Number of bottom-level clusters}$ | count | 2 / 3 marks |
| 3 | **Integration Test Coverage (ITC)** | $\text{ITC} = \dfrac{\#\text{Integrated Module Pairs Tested}}{\#\text{Total Possible Module Pairs}} \times 100\%$ | $\text{percentage}$ | 3 marks |
| 4 | **Defect Density at Integration** | $\text{DD} = \dfrac{\#\text{Defects Found}}{\text{Thousands of Source Lines Integrated (KLOC)}}$ | defects per KLOC | 3 marks |
| 5 | **Regression Test Selection Ratio** | $\text{RTSR} = \dfrac{\vert T_{retest} \vert}{\vert T_{all} \vert}$ (fraction of full suite re-executed) | dimensionless | 3 marks |
| 6 | **Smoke Pass Rate** | $\text{SPR} = \dfrac{\#\text{Passed Smoke Tests}}{\#\text{Total Smoke Tests}} \times 100\%$ | $\text{percentage}$ | 2 marks |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Integration testing is the **bridge** between the developer's local confidence and the system's operational confidence. In safety-critical domains (aerospace, medical devices, automotive — particularly the **ISO 26262** automotive standard adopted globally), failure to perform rigorous integration testing is a regulatory and ethical non-starter. For example, the **Toyota unintended-acceleration recall of 2009** was partly attributed to insufficient integration testing of throttle-by-wire and brake-by-wire subsystems — a case study often cited in KTU viva voce.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Analytical Problem 1: Stubs vs. Drivers in a 4-Module Hierarchy

Consider a software system decomposed into **four modules** as per the call hierarchy:

$$\text{Module A (main control)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Module B} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Module C} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Module D (leaf utility)}$$

Assume a **linear chain**: A → B → C → D.

**Task:** Identify the stubs required for Top-Down testing, and the drivers required for Bottom-Up testing.

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Understand the call graph.**
A single call chain exists: A calls B, B calls C, C calls D. There is exactly **one leaf** (D) and **one root** (A).

**Step 2 — Top-Down Strategy (start with A).**
We begin with A and substitute *placeholders* for every module A calls. A directly calls B.
$$\text{Stubs at this stage} = \{B\}$$

**Step 3 — Replace B with the real B, stub its callee.**
Once B is integrated, A now calls the real B, but B's call to C must be stubbed.
$$\text{Stubs now} = \{C\}$$

**Step 4 — Replace C with the real C, stub D.**
$$\text{Stubs now} = \{D\}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate the real D.**
No further stubs are needed.
$$\text{Total stubs used (peak)} = 1$$
$$\text{Total sequential stub phases} = 3$$

**Step 6 — Bottom-Up Strategy (start with D).**
We begin with the lowest-level module D. Since D has *no callees*, we do not need a driver to drive D itself — we drive D directly to test it. (Alternatively, a minimal driver may be used to call D and verify its return value.)

A driver must call the *next higher* module in the bottom-up chain. To test C (which calls D), we need a driver that simulates whatever called C — i.e., a driver for C.
$$\text{Drivers for C: required to test C with the real D below it.}$$
To test B, a driver is required (it will call B with a real C).
$$\text{Drivers for B: required.}$$
To test A, a driver for A is required if A is exercised in isolation, but typically A is validated by the end-to-end test.
$$\text{Total drivers used (peak)} = 1 \text{ per phase}$$

**Step 7 — Decision.**
For this linear chain, both strategies are feasible. **Top-Down** uses up to 1 stub at a time; **Bottom-Up** uses 1 driver at a time. **Big-Bang** would integrate A, B, C, D simultaneously with *no* stubs and *no* drivers.

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Problem 2: ITC Computation for an N-Module System

Consider a system decomposed into **$n = 5$ modules** that are tested using a Bottom-Up strategy, integrating one cluster at a time.

**Given:**
- 5 modules: $M_1, M_2, M_3, M_4, M_5$
- Module interaction pairs (from design): $(M_1, M_2), (M_2, M_3), (M_3, M_4), (M_4, M_5)$ — a linear chain.
- The team tests 3 of the 4 possible pairs in one iteration.

**Task:** Compute the Integration Test Coverage (ITC).

**Step 1 — Total number of module pairs.**

For a linear chain of $n$ modules, the total number of direct interaction pairs is:
$$
P_{total} = n - 1
$$
Substituting $n = 5$:
$$
P_{total} = 5 - 1 = 4 \text{ pairs}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of pairs tested in this iteration.**

Given that 3 of the 4 pairs were tested:
$$
P_{tested} = 3
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the ITC formula.**

$$
\text{ITC} = \frac{P_{tested}}{P_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

Substituting:
$$
\text{ITC} = \frac{3}{4} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{ITC} = 0.75 \times 100\% = 75\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A coverage of $75\%$ means $1$ in every $4$ module interfaces has *not* been exercised — a clear gap to address in the next integration cycle.

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Problem 3: Defect Density at Integration

A newly integrated subsystem contains **$9{,}500$ lines of code** and integration testing uncovered **$19$ defects**.

**Step 1 — Convert to KLOC.**
$$
\text{KLOC} = \frac{9{,}500}{1{,}000} = 9.5
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the defect density formula.**
$$
\text{DD} = \frac{\#\text{Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}
$$

Substituting:
$$
\text{DD} = \frac{19}{9.5} = 2.0 \; \text{defects per KLOC}
$$

**Step 3 — Industry benchmarking (for context only).**

For comparison, well-engineered systems typically achieve $\text{DD}_{integration} \approx 1.0$ to $2.5$ defects per KLOC at integration. A value of $2.0$ sits at the **upper-acceptable bound**, suggesting the integration phase is normal but warrants attention.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Integration Strategy

| Criterion | Big-Bang | Top-Down | Bottom-Up | Sandwich |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of drivers needed | 0 | 0 | High | Moderate |
| Number of stubs needed | 0 | High | 0 | Moderate |
| Fault localization difficulty | Very High | Moderate | Low | Moderate |
| Time to first executable skeleton | Longest | Early | Late | Early |
| Architectural defect detection | Late | Earliest | Late | Early |
| Suitability for parallel testing | No | Limited | Yes | Yes (across layers) |
| Industry adoption | Rare | Moderate | High (Agile/CI) | Highest (Enterprise) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** When asked *"Which is the best integration strategy?"* the correct KTU answer is **Sandwich/Hybrid** for large systems, with a justified explanation — *not* a one-word "Bottom-Up." Examiners reward reasoning.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Integration Testing Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional flow** of an integration testing environment, showing how drivers, stubs, and the system under test (SUT) interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeT1["Test Case Suite"] --> nodeT2["Test Harness Controller"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3["Driver Module"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT4["Stub Module"]
    nodeT3 --> nodeT5["Module A Under Test"]
    nodeT4 --> nodeT5
    nodeT5 --> nodeT6["Module B Under Test"]
    nodeT6 --> nodeT7["Module C Under Test"]
    nodeT7 --> nodeT8["Interface Validator"]
    nodeT8 --> nodeT9["Test Report Generator"]
    nodeT9 --> nodeT10["Defect Log"]
    nodeT10 --> nodeT11["Regression Test Suite Trigger"]
```

### 4.2 Top-Down Integration Testing — Stubs Placement Diagram

The diagram below shows a **3-level hierarchy** and the progressive stub replacement sequence.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeM1["Module M1 - Main Control - Level 0"] --> nodeM2["Stub for M2 - Phase 1"]
    nodeM1 --> nodeM3["Stub for M3 - Phase 1"]
    nodeM2 -. "Phase 2 Real M2 Integrated" .-> nodeM4["Module M2 - Level 1"]
    nodeM4 --> nodeM5["Stub for M4 - Phase 2"]
    nodeM4 --> nodeM6["Stub for M5 - Phase 2"]
    nodeM5 -. "Phase 3 Real M4 Integrated" .-> nodeM7["Module M4 - Level 2"]
    nodeM7 --> nodeM8["Leaf Utility L1 - Level 3"]
    nodeM6 -. "Phase 3 Real M5 Integrated" .-> nodeM9["Module M5 - Level 2"]
    nodeM9 --> nodeM10["Leaf Utility L2 - Level 3"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows denote *current active calls*; dashed arrows denote *future phases*. At **Phase 1**, M1 is real but M2 and M3 are stubs. By **Phase 3**, the entire tree is integrated.

### 4.3 Bottom-Up Integration Testing — Drivers Placement Diagram

The diagram below shows a **bottom-up integration sequence** using drivers for the higher-level modules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeL1["Leaf Module L1 - Level 3"] --> nodeL2["Leaf Module L2 - Level 3"]
    nodeL1 --> nodeL3["Leaf Module L3 - Level 3"]
    nodeL2 --> nodeM4["Module M4 - Level 2"]
    nodeL3 --> nodeM5["Module M5 - Level 2"]
    nodeM4 --> nodeM2["Module M2 - Level 1"]
    nodeM5 --> nodeM2
    nodeM2 --> nodeM1["Module M1 - Main - Level 0"]
    nodeD1["Driver for M4"] -. "Phase 1 Drives M4" .-> nodeM4
    nodeD2["Driver for M5"] -. "Phase 1 Drives M5" .-> nodeM5
    nodeD3["Driver for M2"] -. "Phase 2 Drives M2 with real M4 M5" .-> nodeM2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** Drivers (dashed) call the *next-to-be-tested* higher module while the lower modules are already real. Each phase, one more level is integrated upwards.

### 4.4 Sandwich / Hybrid Integration Testing — Three-Layer Convergence Diagram

The diagram below shows the **concurrent** top-down and bottom-up testing that meets in the middle layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TOP["TOP LAYER - Top Down Testing"]
        nodeTop1["Main Control Module M1"] --> nodeTop2["Stub for M2 Initial"]
        nodeTop2 -. "Replaced by Real M2" .-> nodeTop3["Module M2"]
    end
    subgraph MID["MIDDLE LAYER - Convergence Zone"]
        nodeMid1["Module M3"] --> nodeMid2["Module M4"]
    end
    subgraph BOT["BOTTOM LAYER - Bottom Up Testing"]
        nodeBot1["Driver for M5 Initial"] -. "Removed after integration" .-> nodeBot2["Module M5"]
        nodeBot2 --> nodeBot3["Leaf Utility L1"]
        nodeBot2 --> nodeBot4["Leaf Utility L2"]
    end
    nodeTop3 --> nodeMid1
    nodeBot2 --> nodeMid2
```

### 4.5 Integration Testing Workflow — Sequential Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA1["Unit Test Plan"] --> nodeA2["Unit Testing Executed"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3["Unit Test Report Approved"]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4["Select Integration Strategy"]
    nodeA4 --> nodeA5["Identify Integration Order"]
    nodeA5 --> nodeA6["Build Stubs and Drivers"]
    nodeA6 --> nodeA7["Execute Smoke Test"]
    nodeA7 --> nodeA8{All Smoke Tests Pass?}
    nodeA8 -- Yes --> nodeA9["Execute Detailed Integration Tests"]
    nodeA8 -- No --> nodeA10["Log Critical Defect - Stop"]
    nodeA9 --> nodeA11["Execute Regression Tests"]
    nodeA11 --> nodeA12["Generate Integration Test Report"]
    nodeA12 --> nodeA13["Sign-off for System Testing"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Ready Insight:** The **Smoke Test** (nodeA7) is the *first* integration-level check — it answers the question *"Can the system be built and launched at all?"* If the smoke test fails, integration testing halts until the critical defect is fixed. This is a recurring KTU 2-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[Q1. KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
> **Differentiate between Driver and Stub in integration testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Driver | Stub |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Used in** | Bottom-Up Integration Testing | Top-Down Integration Testing |
| **Purpose** | Calls the module under test and supplies test inputs | Simulates a called (subordinate) module |
| **Replaces** | The *higher-level* module that would normally call the current one | The *lower-level* module that is not yet integrated |
| **Complexity** | Generally simple, invokes the SUT and captures outputs | Returns hard-coded or computed dummy values |
| **KTU Definition** | A *temporary control program* | A *temporary replacement module* |

*(1 mark for purpose, 1 mark for strategy association, 1 mark for clear distinction.)*

---

> **[Q2. KTU University Exam — June 2024, Repeated Module Question]**
> **What is Big-Bang integration testing? Mention one advantage and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Big-Bang integration testing is a non-incremental strategy in which **all pre-tested modules are integrated simultaneously** and the entire system is tested as a whole in a single step.

- **Advantage (1 mark):** Simple to plan and requires no stubs or drivers to be written.
- **Disadvantage (1 mark):** Fault localization is extremely difficult; if a test fails, identifying the responsible module is time-consuming. Also, the system cannot be partially demonstrated until all modules are ready.
- **Definition (1 mark):** All modules are combined at once before testing.

---

> **[Q3. KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Sample Question]**
> **Define Smoke Testing. Why is it performed before detailed integration testing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Smoke testing is a *preliminary* integration test designed to expose **simple but critical failures** — for example, ensuring that the system can be *built, linked, loaded, and minimally launched* without crashing.

- **Definition (1 mark):** A shallow, wide-coverage initial check of the integrated system's basic functionality.
- **Why (2 marks):**
  1. Saves time and effort by preventing deep testing on a fundamentally broken build.
  2. Acts as a **gate-criterion**: integration testing proceeds only if smoke tests pass; otherwise, the build is rejected and sent back to developers.
  3. Common in **continuous integration (CI/CD)** pipelines, where each commit triggers an automated smoke test.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[Q4. KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3 Mandatory]**
> **(a) Explain Top-Down and Bottom-Up integration testing strategies in detail. For each, describe the role of stubs and drivers and the order in which modules are integrated. (7 marks)**
> **(b) Compare Big-Bang, Top-Down, Bottom-Up, and Sandwich integration testing strategies. Justify which strategy you would recommend for a large distributed banking application and explain why. (7 marks)**

**Note:** Per the KTU 2024 scheme, Part B questions provide an *internal choice* (either Q4 OR Q5). Therefore, an alternative Q5 is provided below for students who choose the other option.

---

#### Question 4 — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Top-Down vs. Bottom-Up Integration Testing (7 Marks)

**Top-Down Integration Testing (3.5 marks):**

- In Top-Down integration, the **main control module** (the highest-level module in the call hierarchy) is tested first.
- Subordinate modules are integrated **one at a time** in a depth-first or breadth-first order.
- Because the lower modules do not yet exist when the higher ones are tested, **stubs** are used. A stub is a *temporary simulation* of a missing module that provides a minimal response so that the calling module can be exercised.
- **Example:** To test a `Main` module that calls a `Login` module, the `Login` module is replaced with a stub that returns a hard-coded "valid user" response, allowing `Main`'s flow to be tested.
- **Advantages:** Architectural and control-flow defects are exposed early; partial demonstration of the system skeleton is possible.
- **Disadvantages:** Many stubs must be written; complex logic in lower modules (where most real bugs often live) is validated *last*.

**Bottom-Up Integration Testing (3.5 marks):**

- In Bottom-Up integration, the **leaf-level (utility, library, low-level) modules** are tested first.
- Related low-level modules are grouped into **clusters**, and a **driver** is written to invoke each cluster, supply inputs, and verify outputs.
- As higher modules are integrated, the driver is removed and the cluster is combined with its real caller.
- **Example:** To test a `calculateTax()` function in isolation, a driver is written that calls it with sample inputs and asserts the outputs.
- **Advantages:** No stubs are required; complex low-level modules are exercised thoroughly; easier fault localization at the lower levels.
- **Disadvantages:** The top-level architecture is verified only late in the cycle; an executable system skeleton is unavailable for most of the integration phase.

**Key Distinction (added in the concluding sentence for full marks):**
> The fundamental difference is the *direction* of integration: Top-Down moves from root to leaves using **stubs**; Bottom-Up moves from leaves to root using **drivers**.

---

#### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis & Strategy Justification (7 Marks)

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Big-Bang | Top-Down | Bottom-Up | Sandwich |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Integration order | All at once | Root → Leaves | Leaves → Root | Both directions |
| Stubs required | None | Many | None | Moderate |
| Drivers required | None | None | Many | Moderate |
| Fault localization | Very Hard | Moderate | Easy | Moderate |
| Time to executable skeleton | Latest | Early | Late | Early |
| Architectural validation | Late | Earliest | Late | Early |
| Suitability for large systems | Poor | Moderate | Moderate | **Best** |

**Justification for a Large Distributed Banking Application (4 marks):**

A large distributed banking application (e.g., core banking, account services, transaction engines, fraud detection, audit logs) exhibits the following characteristics:
- **Multiple teams** work in parallel on different layers.
- The **architecture is layered**: presentation, business logic, data access, and external integrations.
- The system is **mission-critical**, with strict regulatory and reliability requirements.
- **Both high-level control flow** (transaction routing) and **low-level utilities** (currency conversion, cryptographic hashing) are complex.

The **Sandwich (Hybrid) integration strategy** is recommended for the following reasons:
1. **Parallelism:** Top-down integration of the presentation and orchestration layers can run *in parallel* with bottom-up integration of data-access and utility layers. This compresses the integration schedule significantly.
2. **Risk reduction:** Critical control-flow paths (e.g., transaction authorization) are tested top-down early, exposing architectural defects. Critical low-level utilities (e.g., encryption, ledger posting) are tested bottom-up early, exposing foundational defects. Both risks are addressed *before* the middle layers converge.
3. **Realism:** Banking systems typically have well-defined layering; the sandwich strategy maps naturally onto this architecture.
