| Configuration Management | Declarative system state | Ansible, Puppet, Chef, SaltStack | Idempotent, consistent servers |
| Infrastructure as Code | Provision infra via scripts | Terraform, CloudFormation, Pulumi | Reproducible environments |
| Containerization | App packaged with dependencies | Docker, Podman, containerd | Environment parity |
| Container Orchestration | Manages container fleets | Kubernetes, Docker Swarm, Nomad | Self-healing, auto-scaling |
| Monitoring & Logging | Observability of running systems | Prometheus, Grafana, ELK, Splunk | Proactive incident response |
| Release Strategy | Pattern for safe rollouts | Blue-Green, Canary, Rolling | Zero-downtime deployments |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Recall Rule:** If a question asks "list types of DevOps automation," the examiner expects **at minimum**: Build, Test, Deployment, Infrastructure, Configuration, and Monitoring automation. These six categories appear in nearly every past-paper question.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking:** ICICI and HDFC deploy thousands of microservices via automated pipelines, reducing release cycles from **months to minutes**.
- **E-commerce:** Amazon reportedly deploys **production code every second** — only feasible through end-to-end DevOps automation.
- **Telecommunications:** Telecom operators automate 5G network function deployments using IaC + GitOps.
- **Healthcare:** HIPAA-compliant pipelines enforce audit trails automatically during every patient-data release.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Automation Maturity Matrix

## 3.1 The DevOps Automation Pipeline — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

This section walks through the **canonical pipeline** as it would be drawn on a KTU exam answer sheet. Each step's *why* and *how* is unpacked.

### Step 1 — Source Commit Trigger
A developer executes `git push origin main` on their local machine.

- **Why:** A push event is the universal hook for the pipeline to begin.
- **How:** A **webhook** is configured on the Git server. When the push lands, Git POSTs an event payload to the CI server.

### Step 2 — Source Checkout
The CI server clones the repository into a fresh, ephemeral build agent (often a container).

- **Why:** A clean checkout prevents "leaked state" from prior builds.
- **How:** `git clone <repo> --depth=1` minimizes fetch time.

### Step 3 — Dependency Resolution
Build tools fetch external libraries.

- **Why:** Application correctness depends on correct versions of every dependency.
- **How:** Package managers (`npm install`, `mvn package`, `pip install -r requirements.txt`) consult a lockfile (`package-lock.json`, `pom.xml`, `requirements.txt`) to guarantee deterministic versions.

### Step 4 — Compilation / Image Build
Source is compiled (Java → bytecode) or interpreted source is baked into a container image.

- **Why:** Produces the deployable artifact.
- **How:** For compiled languages, `javac` or `mvn compile`. For containerized apps, `docker build -t app:v1 .` produces a layered image.

### Step 5 — Unit Test Execution
Thousands of unit tests run in parallel across multiple build agents.

- **Why:** Catches logic defects at the cheapest possible moment.
- **How:** Test runner (`pytest`, `JUnit`, `Mocha`) reports pass/fail counts and coverage.

### Step 6 — Static Analysis & Security Scan
The code is examined for style violations, code smells, known vulnerabilities.

- **Why:** Defects invisible to the test suite (SQL injection, hardcoded secrets) are detected.
- **How:** **SAST** tools (SonarQube, Checkmarx) and **SCA** tools (Snyk, Dependabot).

### Step 7 — Artifact Publication
The validated artifact is pushed to a binary repository with an immutable version tag.

- **Why:** Downstream stages (staging, production) must consume the *exact* artifact that passed testing — never a rebuild.
- **How:** `mvn deploy` to Nexus / `docker push` to a private registry.

### Step 8 — Staging Deployment
The artifact is deployed to a staging environment that mirrors production.

- **Why:** Provides a high-fidelity validation surface.
- **How:** Orchestrator (Kubernetes `apply -f deployment.yaml`) rolls out the new pods.

### Step 9 — Integration & Acceptance Tests
End-to-end tests, performance tests, and smoke tests validate the staging deployment.

- **Why:** Confirms the artifact behaves correctly in a realistic environment.
- **How:** Selenium drives a browser; JMeter hammers APIs.

### Step 10 — Production Deployment (CD Strategy Selection)
A release strategy is selected and executed.

- **Why:** Different strategies balance risk vs. speed.
- **How (Blue-Green):** Switch router from "blue" to "green" pool.
- **How (Canary):** Route 5% of traffic to new version; promote if metrics remain healthy.
- **How (Rolling):** Replace pods incrementally (`maxUnavailable=25%`).

### Step 11 — Continuous Monitoring
Production telemetry flows into the observability stack.

- **Why:** A deployment is *not* the end of the pipeline — it is the start of feedback.
- **How:** Prometheus scrapes metrics; Grafana visualizes; Alertmanager triggers PagerDuty.

### Step 12 — Feedback to Developers
If SLOs degrade, an automated rollback is triggered, and the team is notified.

- **Why:** Closes the loop, feeding insights into the next commit.
- **How:** ArgoCD's "auto-sync" with health checks; rollback hooks.

## 3.2 The DevOps Automation Maturity Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Table — Stages of DevOps Maturity**

| **Maturity Level** | **Build** | **Test** | **Deploy** | **Infra** | **Feedback** | **Typical Org** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Level 0 — Manual | Manual | Manual | Manual | Manual | Log reviews | Siloed teams |
| Level 1 — Scripted | Cron scripts | Partial | Scripted | Manual | Email alerts | Small startups |
| Level 2 — Automated CI | CI server | Unit + integration | Semi-auto | Partially IaC | Dashboards | Mid-size firms |
| Level 3 — Continuous Delivery | CI + packaging | Full regression | One-click | Mostly IaC | APM + tracing | Mature SaaS |
| Level 4 — Continuous Deployment | CI + CD | Automated + canary | Fully auto | Fully GitOps | Auto-rollback | Netflix, Amazon |
| Level 5 — Autonomous (AIOps) | Self-healing | AI-generated | Self-optimizing | Self-provisioning | Predictive | Future state |

## 3.3 Mathematical Viewpoint — The Automation Equation

Although DevOps is qualitative, KTU examiners occasionally test a *simplified* analytical model for the **Deployment Frequency** of a pipeline.

Let $D_f$ = deployments per day, $T_{pipeline}$ = average pipeline runtime (hours), $T_{manual}$ = manual overhead removed (hours), and $N_{changes}$ = number of merged code changes per day.

$$
D_f = \frac{N_{changes} \times 24}{T_{pipeline} + T_{manual}}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- As $T_{manual} \rightarrow 0$ (full automation), $D_f$ approaches its theoretical maximum.
- As $T_{pipeline}$ shrinks (faster CI agents), $D_f$ grows linearly.

**Lead Time for Change (LT)** — the time from commit to production — is analogously:

$$
LT = T_{commit} + T_{CI} + T_{test} + T_{deploy} + T_{feedback}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If a question asks "how does automation reduce lead time," point to the equation above and explain that automation drives each term toward zero.

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example

**Question:** A team manually deploys 2 times per week. Each manual deployment takes 6 hours, of which 4 hours are toil. If the team automates the toil away, and the automated pipeline runs in 1 hour, with 10 commits per day, compute the new deployment frequency.

**Solution Walkthrough:**

$$
T_{pipeline}^{old} = 6 \text{ hours}, \quad T_{manual}^{old} = 4 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
T_{pipeline}^{new} = 1 \text{ hour}, \quad T_{manual}^{new} = 0 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
D_f^{old} = \frac{N_{changes} \times 24}{T_{pipeline} + T_{manual}} = \frac{14 \times 24}{6 + 4} = \frac{336}{10} = 33.6 \text{ per week}
$$

$$
D_f^{new} = \frac{14 \times 24}{1 + 0} = \frac{336}{1} = 336 \text{ per week}
$$

**Speedup factor:**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{D_f^{new}}{D_f^{old}} = \frac{336}{33.6} = 10 \times
$$

**Verdict:** Automation yields a **10× increase in deployment frequency** — a number that aligns with industry case studies (DORA reports).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The End-to-End DevOps Automation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    devA[Developer Commits Code] --> vcsB[Version Control System Git]
    vcsB --> ciC[CI Server Triggered Webhook]
    ciC --> buildD[Build and Compile Stage]
    buildD --> depE[Dependency Resolution Lockfile]
    depE --> unitF[Unit Test Execution]
    unitF --> saG[Static Analysis and SAST Scan]
    saG --> pkgH[Artifact Packaging Docker Image]
    pkgH --> regI[Artifact Registry Nexus or Artifactory]
    regI --> stgJ[Deploy to Staging Environment]
    stgJ --> intK[Integration and Acceptance Tests]
    intK --> canL{Health Check Pass}
    canL -->|Yes| prodM[Blue Green or Canary Deploy to Production]
    canL -->|No| rollN[Automatic Rollback to Last Stable]
    prodM --> obsO[Observability Stack Prometheus Grafana]
    obsO --> alertP{Alert Threshold Breached}
    alertP -->|Yes| notifQ[PagerDuty and Team Notification]
    alertP -->|No| feedR[Feedback to Developers Loop]
    notifQ --> feedR
    feedR --> devA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram (exam tip):** The flow forms a **closed loop** — the defining characteristic of DevOps automation. The `Health Check` and `Alert Threshold` diamonds are **decision gates** that gate production promotion. Any KTU diagram question will almost certainly include decision diamonds; ensure you mark them clearly.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — DevOps Automation Maturity Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[L0 Manual Silos] --> L1[L1 Scripted Builds]
    L1 --> L2[L2 Automated CI]
    L2 --> L3[L3 Continuous Delivery]
    L3 --> L4[L4 Continuous Deployment]
    L4 --> L5[L5 Autonomous AIOps]

    L0 -.- note0[No tooling, manual handoffs]
    L1 -.- note1[Cron scripts, ad-hoc automation]
    L2 -.- note2[CI server, automated tests, partial IaC]
    L3 -.- note3[One click deploy, full regression]
    L4 -.- note4[Auto release with canary and rollback]
    L5 -.- note5[Self healing, AI generated tests]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Toolchain Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph layer1[Layer 1 Source and Plan]
        t1A[Git] --> t1B[Jira]
    end

    subgraph layer2[Layer 2 Build and Test]
        t2A[Jenkins] --> t2B[SonarQube]
        t2B --> t2C[Selenium]
    end

    subgraph layer3[Layer 3 Package and Store]
        t3A[Docker] --> t3B[Nexus]
        t3B --> t3C[Artifactory]
    end

    subgraph layer4[Layer 4 Provision and Configure]
        t4A[Terraform] --> t4B[Ansible]
        t4B --> t4C[Kubernetes]
    end

    subgraph layer5[Layer 5 Release and Observe]
        t5A[ArgoCD] --> t5B[Prometheus]
        t5B --> t5C[Grafana]
    end

    layer1 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer3
    layer3 --> layer4
    layer4 --> layer5
    layer5 -.feedback.-> layer1
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Drawing Shortcut:** If pressed for time, draw **three boxes** — `Build → Test → Deploy` — and label the arrows with the corresponding tool category. The examiner awards full marks for capturing the *flow*, not the tool names.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define DevOps Automation. List any four categories of automation in the DevOps lifecycle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** DevOps Automation is the use of technology and standardized toolchains to automate the entire software delivery process — from code commit to production monitoring — eliminating manual intervention, reducing errors, and accelerating release velocity.

**Four Categories (2 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Build Automation** — automated compilation and packaging of source code (e.g., Maven, Gradle).
2. **Test Automation** — scripted execution of unit, integration, and regression tests (e.g., Selenium, JUnit).
3. **Deployment Automation** — automated promotion of artifacts across environments (e.g., Ansible, ArgoCD).
4. **Infrastructure Automation (IaC)** — provisioning infrastructure through code (e.g., Terraform, CloudFormation).

*(Acceptable alternatives: Configuration Automation, Monitoring Automation, Release Automation.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Continuous Integration, Continuous Delivery, and Continuous Deployment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Continuous Integration (CI)** | **Continuous Delivery (CD)** | **Continuous Deployment (CD)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Trigger** | Code commit | Successful CI build | Successful CI/CD validation |
| **Automation Scope** | Build + automated tests | Build + test + package + stage | Build + test + package + **auto-promote to prod** |
| **Human Step** | None | Manual approval for production | **None** — fully automated |
| **Goal** | Detect integration issues early | Keep artifact always deployable | Release on every green build |

**Key distinction (1 mark):** Continuous Delivery stops short of production; Continuous Deployment goes all the way automatically.

---

## 📕 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q.A. (a)** Explain the **DevOps Automation Lifecycle** in detail with a neat diagram. Describe the role of **Continuous Integration** in the lifecycle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The DevOps Automation Lifecycle is a closed-loop, end-to-end process that takes source code from a developer's workstation through build, test, package, deploy, and monitor stages — with feedback continuously flowing back to development.

**Lifecycle Stages (4 marks):**

1. **Plan & Develop** — Requirements and code are created collaboratively (Agile, Scrum). Code is versioned in Git.
2. **Build (CI)** — On every commit, the CI server compiles the code and resolves dependencies. Tools: Jenkins, GitLab CI.
3. **Test** — Unit, integration, security, and performance tests run automatically. Tools: JUnit, Selenium, SonarQube.
4. **Package & Stage (CD)** — A versioned artifact (Docker image) is pushed to a registry and deployed to a staging environment.
5. **Deploy to Production** — Using Blue-Green, Canary, or Rolling strategies orchestrated by Kubernetes/ArgoCD.
6. **Monitor & Feedback** — Prometheus/Grafana collect metrics; alerts feed back to the development team, restarting the cycle.

**Role of Continuous Integration (2 marks):**
- **Early defect detection:** Each commit is built and tested, surfacing integration errors within minutes.
- **Reduced merge conflicts:** Frequent small merges replace large "integration weekends."
- **Build confidence:** Every commit produces a verified, deployable artifact.
- **Foundation for CD:** CI is the prerequisite for any continuous delivery or deployment practice.

**Diagram (must draw — see Section 4.1 for reference).**

---

**Q.A. (b)** Discuss **Infrastructure as Code (IaC)** and **Configuration Management Automation**. Compare **Terraform** and **Ansible** in terms of purpose and approach. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Infrastructure as Code — IaC (1.5 marks):**
IaC is the practice of defining and provisioning computing infrastructure (servers, networks, load balancers) through **machine-readable definition files** rather than manual setup. Benefits:
- **Reproducibility:** Identical environments for dev, test, prod.
- **Versioning:** Infrastructure changes tracked in Git.
- **Speed:** Provision 100 servers in minutes.

**Configuration Management Automation (1.5 marks):**
Once servers exist, configuration management tools install software, manage services, and enforce desired state across fleets. They are typically **imperative or declarative** scripts (Ansible playbooks, Chef recipes, Puppet manifests).

**Terraform vs Ansible — Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| **Criterion** | **Terraform** | **Ansible** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary Purpose** | Provision infrastructure (servers, VPCs, DNS) | Configure and manage software on existing servers |
| **Approach** | Declarative — describe desired end-state | Procedural + declarative — execute ordered tasks |
| **State Management** | Maintains a state file; idempotent | Agentless; idempotent modules |
| **Best For** | Cloud resource provisioning (AWS, Azure, GCP) | Application deployment, OS configuration |
| **Tool Category** | Infrastructure provisioning | Configuration management |
| **Typical Workflow** | `terraform plan` → `terraform apply` | `ansible-playbook site.yml` |

**Conclusion (½ mark):** Terraform and Ansible are **complementary** — Terraform creates the infrastructure, Ansible configures it.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q.B. (a)** What is a **CI/CD pipeline**? Explain each stage with the tools typically used. Discuss the **benefits of CI/CD** in modern software engineering. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **CI/CD pipeline** is an automated, sequential workflow that moves code from a developer's commit through build, test, packaging, and deployment stages, enforcing quality gates at each transition.

**Stage-by-Stage Breakdown (4 marks):**

1. **Source Stage** — Code hosted in a Git repository (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket). A webhook fires on every commit.
2. **Build Stage** — CI server (Jenkins, GitHub Actions) compiles code, resolves dependencies via `npm`, `mvn`, `pip`.
3. **Test Stage** — Unit tests (JUnit, pytest), integration tests, code-coverage analysis (JaCoCo, Istanbul), static analysis (SonarQube).
4. **Package Stage** — Artifact (JAR, WAR, Docker image) is versioned and pushed to Nexus / Artifactory.
5. **Release Stage** — Tagged artifact is prepared for deployment; release notes auto-generated.
6. **Deploy Stage** — Ansible, Kubernetes, or ArgoCD deploy to staging then production.
7. **Monitor Stage** — Telemetry via Prometheus, ELK, Datadog.

**Benefits of CI/CD (2 marks):**
- **Faster time-to-market** — releases in hours, not months.
- **Higher quality** — automated tests catch defects early.
- **Lower risk** — small, frequent, reversible changes.
- **Better collaboration** — shared visibility via dashboards.
- **Continuous feedback** — production metrics inform the next iteration.

---

**Q.B. (b)** Explain the **Blue-Green Deployment** and **Canary Release** strategies. State two advantages and one limitation of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Blue-Green Deployment (3 marks):**
- **Concept:** Two identical production environments exist — **Blue** (current live version) and **Green** (new version).
- **Process:** The new version is deployed to Green. After validation, the **load balancer / router** switches traffic from Blue to Green atomically. Blue becomes the standby for the next release.
- **Advantages:**
  1. **Zero-downtime deployments** — switch is instantaneous.
  2. **Instant rollback** — flip the router back to Blue.
- **Limitation:**
  1. **Doubles infrastructure cost** — both environments run simultaneously.

**Canary Release (3 marks):**
- **Concept:** The new version is rolled out to a **small subset of users** (e.g., 5%) while the majority continue on the old version.
- **Process:** Traffic is gradually shifted (5% → 25% → 50% → 100%) based on real-time health metrics (error rate, latency).
- **Advantages:**
  1. **Real-user validation** — actual production traffic tests the new version.
  2. **Blast-radius limited** — only canary users are impacted if the version fails.
- **Limitation:**
  1. **Slower rollout** — gradual traffic shifting takes hours to days.

**Comparison (1 mark):** Blue-Green prioritizes **speed and atomicity**; Canary prioritizes **risk mitigation and observability**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in DevOps Automation Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating CI with CD** — saying "CI automatically deploys to production." It does **not**. CI builds + tests; CD delivers. Examiners deduct 1-2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Listing tools without explaining stages** — a tool dump earns partial credit. Always pair a tool with **the stage it serves** (e.g., "Jenkins — Build stage").
> 3. **Missing the feedback loop in diagrams** — DevOps is a *closed* loop. Drawing a linear pipeline and omitting the "Monitor → Feedback → Plan" return arrow costs full marks on diagram questions.
> 4. **Ignoring IaC as a distinct category** — many students fold "Infrastructure Automation" under "Deployment." It is its **own** category; examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 5. **No diagram when a question says "with a neat diagram"** — a 7-mark question with an explicit diagram requirement awards **at least 2 marks** to the visual; omitting it forfeits those marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the rollback path** — every deployment strategy answer must mention what happens **on failure**. Examiners reward risk-aware answers.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DevOps Automation**
>
> - ✔ **Definition:** Automation across the software delivery lifecycle — from commit to monitor.
> - ✔ **Six Automation Categories:** Build, Test, Deployment, Configuration, Infrastructure, Monitoring.
> - ✔ **CI** = build + test on commit. **CD (Delivery)** = package + stage, manual prod gate. **CD (Deployment)** = auto to prod.
> - ✔ **CI/CD Pipeline Stages:** Source → Build → Test → Package → Release → Deploy → Monitor.
> - ✔ **IaC Tools:** Terraform (provision), Ansible (configure). They are **complementary**, not competing.
> - ✔ **Release Strategies:** Blue-Green (atomic, zero-downtime, costly) vs. Canary (gradual, low-risk, slow).
> - ✔ **Maturity Ladder:** Manual → Scripted → CI → Continuous Delivery → Continuous Deployment → Autonomous (AIOps).
> - ✔ **Feedback Loop:** A defining feature — monitoring feeds back into planning. **Never draw an open-ended pipeline.**
> - ✔ **DORA Metrics:** Deployment Frequency, Lead Time for Change, Change Failure Rate, Mean Time to Recovery — all improve with automation.
> - ✔ **Equation to recall:** $D_f = \frac{N_{changes} \times 24}{T_{pipeline} + T_{manual}}$ — shows the multiplicative effect of removing manual toil.
> - ✔ **Tool-Stage Mapping (memorize):**
>   - *Build:* Jenkins, GitLab CI
>   - *Test:* Selenium, JUnit, SonarQube
>   - *Package:* Docker, Nexus
>   - *Provision:* Terraform
>   - *Configure:* Ansible
>   - *Release:* ArgoCD, Kubernetes
>   - *Monitor:* Prometheus, Grafana, ELK
> - ✔ **Always** mention **rollback** when discussing deployment strategies.
> - ✔ **Always** include a **closed feedback loop** in any DevOps diagram.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CI/CD/CD. Software Evolution - Evolution Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** CI/CD/CD. Software Evolution - Evolution Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Continuous Integration (CI)

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuous Integration (CI)** is a software engineering practice in which members of a team integrate their work frequently — usually each person integrates at least daily — leading to multiple integrations per day. Each integration is verified by an **automated build** and **automated test** to detect integration errors as quickly as possible. *(Pressman & Maxim, Software Engineering in Practice)*

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy — The Newspaper Printing Press
Imagine a city newspaper. In the *old days*, reporters wrote stories all week, and the editor assembled them on Friday night, discovering at the last moment that two reporters covered the same event differently. In the *modern CI press*, every reporter submits their paragraph **as soon as it is written**, and a small test-print is run every hour. Errors are caught at paragraph level, not at the final newspaper level. **CI is this small test-print that runs every time any developer commits code.**

### Key CI Metrics (Industry-Standard)
- **Build Frequency:** Multiple times per day (target).
- **Build Duration:** **< 10 minutes** for the feedback loop.
- **Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR):** **< 1 hour**.
- **Change Lead Time:** Time from commit to production-ready.

---

## 1.2 Continuous Delivery (CD) and Continuous Deployment (CD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuous Delivery (CD)** is the capability to release changes of all types — new features, configuration changes, bug fixes, experiments — to production **on demand, safely, and quickly** in a sustainable way. The release to production is a **manual business decision**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuous Deployment (CD)** goes one step further: every change that passes all stages of the production pipeline is **released automatically to end users**, with no human intervention. There is no "release day" — every successful commit is a release.

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen
Think of a high-end restaurant (Continuous Delivery) where the **head chef (product owner)** decides which dishes go out and when. The kitchen (pipeline) is always ready to serve — sauces, plating, and quality checks are pre-done. Now imagine the same restaurant with a **conveyor belt to customers** (Continuous Deployment) — every dish that passes quality automatically rolls out to the next table. The food is identical, but the **release trigger** differs: human approval vs. automatic.

### The Distinguishing Table

| Aspect | Continuous Delivery | Continuous Deployment |
|---|---|---|
| Release trigger | **Manual approval** | **Automatic** |
| Pipeline is ready? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Last gate | Human decision | Automated tests only |
| Rollback strategy | Mandatory | Mandatory |

---

## 1.3 Software Evolution

> [!NOTE]
> **Software Evolution** is the process of developing and maintaining software systems, encompassing all the activities and transitions that occur from the inception of a system through to its eventual replacement or retirement. *(Sommerville, Software Engineering, 10th ed.)*

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy — The Family Car
A car bought in 2020 is not the same car in 2025. Tires are replaced, the battery upgraded, the infotainment system patched, and sometimes the engine is overhauled — yet the **chassis number (identity)** remains. The car **evolves**. Software is the same: its identity persists, but its internals continuously change to meet new requirements, environments, and user expectations.

### Key Definitions to Memorize
- **Software Maintenance:** Modification of a software product after delivery to correct faults, improve performance, or adapt to a changed environment.
- **Lehman's Laws of Software Evolution:** A set of empirically derived laws governing how large-scale software systems evolve over time.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a CI/CD/CD Pipeline (Stage-by-Stage)

The CI/CD/CD pipeline is a **staged, automated, and observable workflow**. Each stage has a specific purpose and must pass a defined quality gate before the next stage begins.

### Stage 1 — Source / Commit Stage
- **Why:** Trigger the pipeline on every code change.
- **How:** Developers push code to a shared Git repository (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket). A **webhook** notifies the CI server.
- **Quality Gate:** Branch policy check, code review approval (PR/MR).

### Stage 2 — Build Stage
- **Why:** Compile the code and produce deployable artifacts (`.jar`, `.war`, Docker image).
- **How:** Tools such as **Maven, Gradle, npm, Webpack, Docker**.
- **Quality Gate:** Build success, **zero compilation errors**, artifact signed.

### Stage 3 — Test Stage (The Heart of CI)
- **Why:** Verify functional and non-functional correctness *before* deployment.
- **How:** Multi-layer test pyramid:
  1. **Unit Tests** — fast, isolated, developer-written (JUnit, pytest).
  2. **Integration Tests** — verify inter-module contracts.
  3. **System / E2E Tests** — Selenium, Cypress, Postman/Newman.
  4. **Non-functional Tests** — performance (JMeter), security (OWASP ZAP).
- **Quality Gate:** Code coverage ≥ **80%**, all critical tests pass.

### Stage 4 — Staging / Pre-Production Stage
- **Why:** Mirror production environment for final validation.
- **How:** Infrastructure-as-Code (Terraform, Ansible) provisions an identical cluster.
- **Quality Gate:** Smoke tests, UAT (User Acceptance Testing) sign-off.

### Stage 5 — Release / Deploy Stage
- **Why:** Push the validated artifact to the target environment.
- **How:** Blue-Green, Canary, or Rolling deployment strategies.
- **Quality Gate:** Health checks, smoke tests on live traffic.

### Stage 6 — Monitor & Feedback
- **Why:** Detect post-deployment anomalies and feed learning back to the dev cycle.
- **How:** Tools — **Prometheus, Grafana, ELK Stack, Splunk, New Relic, Sentry**.
- **Output:** Mean Time To Detect (MTTD), Mean Time To Recover (MTTR).

---

## 2.2 Software Evolution Processes — The Four Maintenance Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEEE 1219 Standard** classifies software maintenance into four categories. This is a **high-yield KTU topic** — definitions are frequently asked in Part A.

| Type | Definition | Example | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Corrective Maintenance** | Reactive modification to **fix defects** discovered after delivery. | Fixing a null-pointer exception in production. | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Adaptive Maintenance** | Modification to keep software usable in a **changed or changing environment**. | Migrating from MySQL 5.7 → 8.0, updating API for new OS version. | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Perfective Maintenance** | Modification to **improve performance, maintainability, or other attributes** based on user requests. | Adding a new feature, optimizing a slow query. | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Preventive Maintenance** | Modification to **detect and correct latent faults** before they become effective faults; improve future maintainability. | Refactoring legacy code, updating deprecated libraries. | ⭐⭐ |

### Maintenance Effort Distribution (Industry Benchmark — Lientz & Swanson Study)
| Type | Typical Effort Share |
|---|---|
| Perfective | **~ 50%** |
| Adaptive | **~ 25%** |
| Corrective | **~ 20%** |
| Preventive | **~ 5%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *Adaptive* and *Perfective* maintenance. The **decision keyword** is:
> - "Environment changed" → **Adaptive**.
> - "User asked for a new feature or improvement" → **Perfective**.

---

## 2.3 Lehman's Laws of Software Evolution (MUST-KNOW for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **M.M. Lehman** formulated these laws after decades of empirical study on large-scale, real-world systems (IBM OS/360, etc.). They apply specifically to **E-type systems** — systems that operate in the real world and must be continually adapted.

### The Key Laws (Exam-Relevant)

| Law | Statement | Intuitive Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Law of Continuing Change (1974)** | A program used in a real-world environment must change, or it becomes progressively less useful. | Software decays in value if frozen. |
| **Law of Increasing Complexity (1974)** | As a program evolves, its complexity increases unless work is actively done to reduce it. | Entropy of software. |
| **Law of Declining Quality (1996)** | The quality of a program will appear to decline unless rigorously maintained and adapted. | Erosion of structure. |
| **Law of Conservation of Organizational Stability (1996)** | The **rate of change** in a system over its lifetime is roughly invariant. | Pace of evolution is constant. |
| **Law of Conservation of Familiarity (1996)** | The **amount of incremental change** in each release is roughly constant. | Avoid disruptive "big bang" releases. |
| **Law of Continuing Growth (1996)** | Functional content of a system must increase to maintain user satisfaction. | Features must be added. |
| **Law of Declining Quality (Reinforced)** | System quality erodes unless explicitly addressed. | Justifies **preventive maintenance**. |
| **Feedback Law (1996)** | Significant system changes require **organizational reinforcement** to maintain performance. | People + process + tech. |

### KTU Cheat Sheet — Evolution Process Categories

| Process | Description | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Re-engineering** | Examining and altering an existing system to **restructure** it in a new form, with or without functional changes. | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Reverse Engineering** | Analyzing a system to identify its **components and interrelationships**, then creating representations of it at a higher level of abstraction. | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Forward Engineering** | The traditional development process applied to evolve legacy systems. | ⭐⭐ |
| **Migration** | Transferring a system from **one platform/environment to another**. | ⭐⭐ |
| **Retirement** | Phasing out a system when it is no longer cost-effective to maintain. | ⭐ |

### KTU High-Yield Definitions Table

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Software Re-engineering** | "The examination and alteration of a system to reconstitute it in a new form and to implement it in a new way." — *Chikofsky & Cross* |
| **Reverse Engineering** | "The process of analyzing a subject system to (1) identify the system's components and their interrelationships and (2) create representations of the system in another form or at a higher level of abstraction." — *Chikofsky & Cross* |
| **Forward Engineering** | "The traditional software engineering paradigm applied to evolve legacy systems by applying modern best practices." — *Sommerville* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Chikofsky & Cross (1990)** is the **canonical reference** for these terms. Memorize the exact wording of *Re-engineering* — KTU has asked it verbatim.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of CI/CD/CD + Evolution

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Banking & FinTech** | Hundreds of micro-deployments per day, ensuring PCI-DSS compliance and zero-downtime releases. |
| **Healthcare** | HIPAA-compliant evolution of Electronic Health Record systems via CI/CD. |
| **IoT / Embedded** | Over-The-Air (OTA) evolution of firmware in millions of devices. |
| **Aerospace** | DO-178C-compliant evolution pipelines with formal verification gates. |
| **E-commerce** | Canary deployments during Black Friday to roll back faulty changes within seconds. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the CI Feedback Loop (Process Walk-through)

> Although software engineering is not a purely mathematical discipline, the **workflow derivation** below formalizes the *cause → effect → feedback* chain of a CI cycle. KTU examiners value this structured reasoning.

### Step 1 — Trigger Generation
A developer completes a unit of work and pushes a commit to a feature branch. Let:
- $C_i$ = the $i$-th commit
- $T_{commit}$ = timestamp of the push
- $W_i$ = the set of working files changed in $C_i$

The push event $E$ can be expressed as:
$$
E = \{C_i, T_{commit}, W_i\}
$$

### Step 2 — Pipeline Invocation
A **webhook** subscribes to the repository. On detecting $E$, the CI server spawns a build job $J$:
$$
J = f(E)
$$
where $f$ is the orchestration function (e.g., GitHub Actions, Jenkins, GitLab CI).

### Step 3 — Build & Static Analysis
The artifact is compiled and statically analyzed. The outcome $O_{build}$ is binary:
$$
O_{build} = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if compile succeeds AND static-analysis passes} \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 4 — Automated Test Execution
A test suite $\mathcal{T} = \{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n\}$ is executed. Let $p_k$ be the pass indicator of test $t_k$. The aggregate pass rate is:
$$
P_{suite} = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{n} p_k}{n}
$$
The quality gate threshold is fixed at:
$$
P_{suite} \ge \tau \quad \text{where} \quad \tau = 0.95 \quad (95\%)
$$
If $P_{suite} < \tau$, the pipeline **fails** and feedback is sent to the developer.

### Step 5 — Artifact Publication
On success, a versioned, immutable artifact is published:
$$
A = \langle \text{name}, \text{version}, \text{SHA-256}(A), \text{timestamp} \rangle
$$
where $\text{SHA-256}(A)$ is the cryptographic hash ensuring **artifact immutability**.

### Step 6 — Deployment Gate (CD Decision)
- For **Continuous Delivery**, let $D_{manual}$ be the deployment decision:
$$
D_{manual} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Deploy} & \text{if Approver signs off} \\
\text{Hold} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$
- For **Continuous Deployment**, $D_{manual}$ is replaced by an automated policy function $g$:
$$
D_{auto} = g(O_{build}, P_{suite}, \text{SLOs}) \in \{0, 1\}
$$

### Step 7 — Monitoring Feedback
Post-deployment, telemetry $\mathcal{M}$ is collected. An anomaly is flagged if:
$$
\mathcal{M}_{anomaly} = \{m \in \mathcal{M} \mid m \text{ violates SLO}\}
$$
Anomalies feed back into the next CI cycle as a **new trigger** $E'$.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Evolution Strategy Decision

A KTU-favorite question type is: *"Given a legacy system in state X, which evolution strategy is appropriate?"* The matrix below provides the derivation logic.

| Current System State | Symptom | Recommended Strategy | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| High business value, **poor code quality**, no documentation | Frequent defects, hard to maintain | **Re-engineering** | Restructure without changing external behavior. |
| Legacy in old language (COBOL), full functionality known | Need to migrate to new platform | **Reverse → Re-engineer → Forward** | Understand first, restructure, then rewrite in new tech. |
| Working system, but **environment change** required (e.g., new OS) | System still functions in old env | **Migration / Porting** | A form of *Adaptive Maintenance*. |
| Module X is a **performance bottleneck** | Users complain of slow response | **Re-engineering of X** (targeted) | Local restructure, keep rest untouched. |
| System is **end-of-life**, no business value left | Replacing with SaaS | **Retirement** | Planned decommission. |

### The Decision Equation (Heuristic)
Let:
- $V_b$ = business value (0–10)
- $Q_s$ = software quality (0–10)
- $C_m$ = cost of full rewrite
- $C_r$ = cost of re-engineering

Then:
$$
\text{Strategy} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Retire} & \text{if } V_b < 3 \\
\text{Re-write (Forward Engineering)} & \text{if } V_b \ge 7 \text{ AND } Q_s < 3 \text{ AND } C_m < 2 \cdot C_r \\
\text{Re-engineer} & \text{if } V_b \ge 5 \text{ AND } Q_s < 5 \\
\text{Incremental Maintenance} & \text{if } Q_s \ge 5
\end{cases}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)
> A legacy payroll system has a business value of **8/10**, a code quality index of **2/10**, an estimated re-write cost of ₹50 lakhs, and a re-engineering cost of ₹30 lakhs. Apply the decision equation.

**Step 1:** Evaluate $V_b = 8 \ge 7$ — true.  
**Step 2:** Evaluate $Q_s = 2 < 3$ — true.  
**Step 3:** Evaluate $C_m = 50 < 2 \cdot C_r = 2 \cdot 30 = 60$ — true.  
**Step 4:** All three conditions are satisfied → **Forward Engineering (Full Re-write)** is recommended.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Re-engineering Process (Stage Sequence)

The standard re-engineering sequence, as defined by Sommerville, is:

$$
\text{Source Code} \xrightarrow{\text{Reverse Eng.}} \text{Model} \xrightarrow{\text{Re-structure}} \text{Refined Model} \xrightarrow{\text{Forward Eng.}} \text{New Code}
$$

Mathematically expressed as a transformation chain:
$$
S_{old} \xrightarrow{\;R\;} M_{abs} \xrightarrow{\;T\;} M_{ref} \xrightarrow{\;F\;} S_{new}
$$
Where:
- $R$ = Reverse Engineering operator.
- $T$ = Re-structuring / Transformation operator.
- $F$ = Forward Engineering operator.
- The **behavioral equivalence** is preserved: $\text{Behavior}(S_{old}) \approx \text{Behavior}(S_{new})$.

> [!NOTE]
> This transformation chain is a **favourite KTU diagram question** — students are asked to draw or describe it in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CI/CD/CD Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Developer Commit"] --> B["Source Stage - Git Push"]
    B --> C{"Branch Policy OK?"}
    C -- No --> Z1["Reject - Notify Developer"]
    C -- Yes --> D["Build Stage - Compile and Package"]
    D --> E{"Build Success?"}
    E -- No --> Z2["Fail - Rollback Artifacts"]
    E -- Yes --> F["Test Stage - Unit, Integration, E2E"]
    F --> G{"All Tests Pass?"}
    G -- No --> Z3["Fail - Send Report"]
    G -- Yes --> H["Stage to Staging Environment"]
    H --> I["UAT and Smoke Tests"]
    I --> J{"UAT Approved?"}
    J -- No --> Z4["Hold - Reject Release"]
    J -- Yes --> K{"Deployment Mode?"}
    K -- "Continuous Delivery" --> L["Manual Approval Gate"]
    K -- "Continuous Deployment" --> M["Automatic Policy Check"]
    L --> N["Blue-Green / Canary Deploy"]
    M --> N
    N --> O["Production Environment"]
    O --> P["Monitor - Prometheus, Grafana"]
    P --> Q{"SLO Violated?"}
    Q -- Yes --> R["Auto Rollback or Alert"]
    Q -- No --> S["Feedback to Next Commit"]
```

---

## 4.2 Software Re-engineering Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Existing System - Legacy Code"] --> B["Source Code Translation"]
    B --> C["Reverse Engineering"]
    C --> D["Abstract Model - UML, DFD"]
    D --> E{"Re-engineering Decision"}
    E -- "Improve Structure" --> F["Program Restructuring"]
    E -- "Improve Data" --> G["Data Restructuring"]
    E -- "Translate Language" --> H["Source Code Translation"]
    F --> I["Refactored System"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["Forward Engineering"]
    J --> K["New Improved System"]
    K --> L["Continuous Maintenance Loop"]
```

---

## 4.3 Lehman Evolution Process (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Real-World Environment"] --> B["E-Type System"]
    B --> C["Continuous Change Activity"]
    C --> D{"Quality Decline Detected?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Preventive Maintenance"]
    D -- No --> F["Functional Enhancement"]
    E --> G["Refactored System"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Increased Complexity"]
    H --> I{"Active Reduction?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["Re-engineering Triggered"]
    I -- No --> K["System Degrades"]
    J --> G
    G --> A
```

---

## 4.4 Maintenance Type Decision Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Defect Reported in Production"] --> B{"Cause Category?"}
    B -- "Bug in Code" --> C1["Corrective Maintenance"]
    B -- "Environment Changed" --> C2["Adaptive Maintenance"]
    B -- "User Requests Improvement" --> C3["Perfective Maintenance"]
    B -- "Latent Fault Anticipated" --> C4["Preventive Maintenance"]
    C1 --> D["Fix and Patch"]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E["New Version Released"]
    E --> F["Monitor Feedback"]
    F --> A
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Continuous Delivery and Continuous Deployment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Continuous Delivery** is a software engineering approach in which the team ensures that every change to the system is releasable, but the **actual release to production is a manual business decision**. (1½ marks)
- **Continuous Deployment** extends this by **automatically deploying every change that passes all automated tests** to production, with **no human intervention** in the release trigger. (1½ marks)
- **Key differentiator:** The **release trigger** — manual approval in Delivery vs. automatic policy in Deployment.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is Corrective Maintenance? Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Corrective Maintenance** is the reactive modification of a software product **after delivery** to correct discovered faults, errors, or defects that affect the system's performance or correctness. (2 marks)
- **Example:** Fixing a NullPointerException that causes the login page to crash on Android 14 devices. (1 mark)

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **stages of a typical CI/CD pipeline** with a neat diagram. Discuss the **role of automated testing** in this pipeline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **four types of software maintenance** as per the IEEE 1219 standard. For each type, give **one real-world example**. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define CI/CD pipeline (1 mark):**
A CI/CD pipeline is an automated workflow that takes source code from a developer's commit through build, test, and deployment stages to a production environment with minimal manual intervention.

**Step 2 — List and explain the 6 stages (4 marks):**
1. **Source Stage:** Developer pushes code; webhook triggers pipeline.
2. **Build Stage:** Code is compiled and packaged into artifacts (`.jar`, Docker image).
3. **Test Stage:** Unit, integration, and E2E tests are run; coverage analyzed.
4. **Staging Stage:** Artifact is deployed to a mirror production environment.
5. **Deployment Stage:** Promotion to production via blue-green or canary strategy.
6. **Monitor Stage:** Telemetry collected via Prometheus/Grafana for feedback.

**Step 3 — Role of automated testing (2 marks):**
- Provides **fast feedback** (< 10 min) on code quality.
- Acts as a **safety net** preventing defective code from reaching production.
- Enables **frequent, low-risk releases**, supporting the DevOps principle of "fail fast, recover faster."
- The **test pyramid** (many unit tests, fewer integration, fewest E2E) optimizes speed and reliability.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

| Type | Description | Real-World Example | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Corrective** | Fixes faults discovered after delivery. | Patching a memory leak in a banking app. | 1½ |
| **Adaptive** | Adapts system to a changed environment. | Porting an iOS app for iOS 18 compatibility. | 1½ |
| **Perfective** | Enhances performance or adds new features. | Adding a "dark mode" UI based on user feedback. | 2 |
| **Preventive** | Addresses latent faults and improves maintainability. | Refactoring legacy code to remove cyclomatic complexity. | 2 |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** What is **Software Re-engineering**? Explain its **process model** with a diagram. Compare it with **Reverse Engineering** and **Forward Engineering**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **Lehman's Laws of Software Evolution**. Explain how these laws influence **modern DevOps and CI/CD practices**. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
**Software Re-engineering** is the examination and alteration of an existing system to reconstitute it in a new form and to implement it in a new way, with the **primary goal of improving structure, maintainability, and extensibility** — typically *without* changing its external behavior.

**Step 2 — Process Model (3 marks):**
The Sommerville re-engineering model includes:
1. **Source code translation** — convert to a modern language.
2. **Reverse engineering** — derive an abstract model (UML, DFD).
3. **Program restructuring** — improve control flow and module decomposition.
4. **Data restructuring** — migrate from flat files to relational/NoSQL DB.
5. **Forward engineering** — re-implement the system in the new form.

**Step 3 — Comparison Table (2 marks):**
| Aspect | Reverse Engineering | Re-engineering | Forward Engineering |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Understand the system | Restructure for quality | Develop new system |
| **Output** | Models / documentation | Improved code | New application |
| **Behavior change** | None | Minimal / none | Yes (often) |

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Lehman's Laws (4 marks):**
- **Continuing Change:** Real-world software must change continuously or lose usefulness.
- **Increasing Complexity:** Complexity grows over time unless actively reduced.
- **Conservation of Organizational Stability:** The rate of development activity remains roughly constant.
- **Continuing Growth:** Functional content must keep growing to satisfy users.
- **Declining Quality:** Quality erodes without rigorous maintenance.
- **Feedback Law:** Major changes require organizational alignment to be effective.

**Step 2 — Influence on DevOps/CI/CD (3 marks):**
- **CI/CD directly addresses the "Continuing Change" law** by enabling low-risk, high-frequency releases.
- **Test automation and code refactoring** are responses to the "Increasing Complexity" law.
- **SRE practices and SLOs** operationalize the "Declining Quality" law by detecting regressions early.
- **Feature flags and modular architecture** satisfy the "Conservation of Familiarity" law by limiting incremental change size.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Continuous Delivery with Continuous Deployment** — the *trigger* (manual vs. automatic) is the deciding factor. Many students lose 2–3 marks by giving vague definitions.
> 2. **Mixing up maintenance types** — *Adaptive* ≠ *Perfective*. Read the trigger word: "environment" → Adaptive; "user wants improvement" → Perfective.
> 3. **Lehman's Laws are often remembered shallowly** — the **exact name** of each law is expected (e.g., *Law of Continuing Change*, not just "software must change"). Vague answers fetch ½ mark only.
> 4. **Re-engineering ≠ Forward Engineering** — re-engineering *restructures existing* code; forward engineering *creates new* code. Conflating them costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to draw a diagram** in pipeline questions — a labelled diagram is worth 2–3 marks in a 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **CI** = integrate often, build & test on every commit.
- ✅ **CD (Delivery)** = always deployable, but **manual** release trigger.
- ✅ **CD (Deployment)** = always deployable, **automatic** release trigger.
- ✅ **Test pyramid:** many unit tests, fewer integration, fewest E2E.
- ✅ **Four maintenance types:** Corrective, Adaptive, Perfective, Preventive — memorize IEEE 1219.
- ✅ **Lientz & Swanson effort split:** ~50% Perfective, ~25% Adaptive, ~20% Corrective, ~5% Preventive.
- ✅ **Lehman's Laws** apply to **E-type systems** (real-world embedded systems).
- ✅ **Re-engineering process:** Translate → Reverse → Restructure → Forward.
- ✅ **Chikofsky & Cross definitions** of Re-engineering and Reverse Engineering are canonical.
- ✅ **Re-engineering ≠ Forward Engineering** — one restructures, the other creates anew.
- ✅ **CI/CD pipeline stages:** Source → Build → Test → Stage → Deploy → Monitor.
- ✅ **Monitoring** is the *seventh* and often-forgotten stage; it closes the feedback loop.
- ✅ **Blue-Green** and **Canary** are the two most common zero-downtime deployment strategies.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Software Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Software Maintenance** is the comprehensive, post-delivery phase of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) that encompasses all modifications, enhancements, and corrective actions performed on a software system **after** it has been deployed to the production environment. According to the internationally accepted **IEEE Standard 1219 (Standard for Maintenance)** and the **ISO/IEC 14764:2022** standard, software maintenance is the totality of activities required to keep a software system operational, responsive to environmental changes, and aligned with evolving user requirements throughout its operational lifetime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Software maintenance is the modification of a software product after delivery to correct faults, to improve performance or other attributes, or to adapt the product to a modified environment.* — (Aligned with ISO/IEC 14764)

The maintenance phase is widely recognized as the **most expensive and longest phase** of the software lifecycle. Industry studies and empirical data reveal that maintenance consumes approximately **60% to 80%** of the total lifetime cost of a software system, while the development phase typically accounts for only **20% to 40%**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Car Ownership Experience"**
> Imagine you buy a brand-new car. The moment you drive it out of the showroom, your spending isn't over — in fact, a new chapter of expenses begins. You must:
> - **Take it to the mechanic** when the brakes squeak (Corrective Maintenance)
> - **Upgrade to new tires** or install better headlights for comfort (Perfective Maintenance)
> - **Switch to BS6 fuel** when the government changes emission rules (Adaptive Maintenance)
> - **Get a periodic service** every 6 months to prevent major breakdown (Preventive Maintenance)
> - **Replace the entire engine** after 10 years to extend life (Re-engineering)
>
> Software is exactly the same! A software system, once "delivered" to the customer, begins its **maintenance life**, which often spans 10–25 years — much longer than its development life.

## 1.3 Why Maintenance is the Dominant Lifecycle Phase

$$\text{Maintenance Cost Ratio} = \frac{\text{Cost of Maintenance}}{\text{Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)}} \approx 0.60 \text{ to } 0.80$$

| Lifecycle Phase | Typical % of Total Cost | Duration |
|:----------------|:-----------------------:|:--------:|
| Development (Analysis, Design, Coding, Testing) | 20% – 40% | 1 – 3 years |
| **Maintenance (Post-Delivery)** | **60% – 80%** | **10 – 25+ years** |
| Retirement/Replacement | < 5% | < 1 year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Examiners often ask *"Why does maintenance cost so much?"* The standard answer must include: **software entropy (decay)**, **staff turnover**, **changing requirements**, **environment upgrades**, and **defect latency**.

## 1.4 Categories of Maintenance (Overview)

The **Lientz & Swanson Maintenance Taxonomy** (the gold standard in software engineering) classifies maintenance into **four primary categories** (with a fifth, **emergency**, often added):

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Core Types of Maintenance:**
> 1. **Corrective Maintenance** — Fixing reported bugs/defects (≈ 20% of maintenance effort)
> 2. **Adaptive Maintenance** — Adapting to new environments (OS, DB, hardware) (≈ 25% of effort)
> 3. **Perfective Maintenance** — Adding new features/improvements requested by users (≈ 50% of effort — the LARGEST)
> 4. **Preventive Maintenance** — Code refactoring to prevent future bugs (≈ 5% of effort)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four (Plus One) Types of Maintenance — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Corrective Maintenance
Corrective maintenance involves diagnosing and **fixing errors, faults, and defects** discovered in the software after it has been delivered. These defects may be discovered by users during operational use or by the maintenance team during routine monitoring.

**Operational Steps:**
- **Step 1 — Defect Reporting:** User submits a bug report (via ticketing system like JIRA, Bugzilla)
- **Step 2 — Defect Triage:** Maintenance team prioritizes based on severity (Critical, Major, Minor)
- **Step 3 — Fault Localization:** Debugging to identify the root cause
- **Step 4 — Code Modification:** Apply the fix, preserving existing functionality
- **Step 5 — Regression Testing:** Ensure the fix did not break other modules
- **Step 6 — Re-deployment & Documentation Update**

### 2.1.2 Adaptive Maintenance
Adaptive maintenance modifies the software to **keep it compatible with changes in its external environment** — such as new operating system versions, database migrations, hardware upgrades, regulatory changes, or third-party API deprecations.

**Why and How:**
- **Why:** The external world evolves faster than the software itself
- **How:** Modifying configuration, updating interface layers, porting code, and updating dependencies

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** Migrating a banking application from **Oracle 11g to Oracle 19c**, or updating code to comply with the new **GDPR / DPDP Act 2023** privacy regulations.

### 2.1.3 Perfective Maintenance
Perfective maintenance refers to **enhancements, new features, and functional improvements** requested by users based on their evolving business needs. This is the **largest category (≈ 50%)** because user requirements are never frozen.

**Key Engineering Activities:**
- Requirement elicitation from stakeholders
- Impact analysis to understand ripple effects
- Implementation using existing architectural patterns
- User Acceptance Testing (UAT)

### 2.1.4 Preventive Maintenance
Preventive maintenance proactively improves the software's **internal structure, code quality, and maintainability** to prevent future problems. Activities include code refactoring, documentation updates, and architectural improvements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Payne's Law (Software Entropy):** *"Software systems, like buildings, tend to degrade over time unless explicitly maintained."* Preventive maintenance directly counteracts this entropy.

### 2.1.5 Emergency Maintenance
Emergency (or "hot-fix") maintenance refers to **unscheduled, high-priority corrective actions** needed when a critical system failure occurs in production (e.g., server crash, security breach, data loss). It bypasses the normal change control process.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Description** | **Unit / Notes** |
|:------------|:------------------------|:----------------|:-----------------|
| **Maintenance Cost Ratio (MCR)** | $$MCR = \frac{C_{maintenance}}{C_{TCO}} \times 100\%$$ | Percentage of lifecycle cost spent on maintenance | Expressed in % (typically 60–80%) |
| **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** | $$MTTF = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i}{N}$$ | Average operational time between failures | Hours or Days |
| **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** | $$MTTR = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i}{N}$$ | Average time taken to fix a failure | Hours or Days |
| **System Availability** | $$A = \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} \times 100\%$$ | Percentage of time system is operational | Expressed in % |
| **Defect Density (DD)** | $$DD = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$$ | Defects per thousand lines of code | Defects / KLOC |
| **Annual Maintenance Cost (AMC)** | $$AMC = \alpha \cdot C_{dev} + \beta \cdot C_{env} + \gamma \cdot C_{personnel}$$ | $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are weighting factors | Monetary units |
| **Ripple Effect Impact Factor (REIF)** | $$REIF = \frac{\text{Modules affected}}{\text{Modules changed}}$$ | Measures coupling in change impact | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Maintenance Productivity (MP)** | $$MP = \frac{\text{Function Points Modified}}{\text{Person-Months}}$$ | Output of maintenance team | FP / person-month |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever the question asks for a *numerical answer*, ensure you show **unit conversion** (KLOC vs LOC, hours vs days). Marks are often deducted for missing units.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Software maintenance is the backbone of every mission-critical industry:
- **Banking & Finance:** Continuous updates for SEBI/RBI regulatory compliance
- **Aviation:** Boeing 777's onboard software is maintained for **30+ years**
- **Healthcare:** HIS (Hospital Information Systems) must adapt to new medical protocols
- **Space:** NASA's Voyager software, launched in 1977, is still being maintained!

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: System Availability

System Availability is the cornerstone metric for any maintained software system. Let us derive it from first principles.

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Define MTTF and MTTR separately.**
- Let $T_i$ be the time from the $(i-1)^{th}$ repair to the $i^{th}$ failure
- Let $R_i$ be the time taken to repair after the $i^{th}$ failure

**Step 2 — Express total operational time.**
The total time the system is *operational* across $N$ failure-repair cycles is:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{operational} &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Express total repair (downtime).**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{downtime} &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Total time considered.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= T_{operational} + T_{downtime} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Form the average (Mean) values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
MTTF &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i}{N} \\
MTTR &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i}{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Definition of Availability.**
Availability is the fraction of total time the system is *up* (operational):

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{T_{operational}}{T_{total}} \\
  &= \frac{\sum T_i}{\sum T_i + \sum R_i}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Substituting the mean values.**
Dividing numerator and denominator by $N$:

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\frac{\sum T_i}{N}}{\frac{\sum T_i}{N} + \frac{\sum R_i}{N}} \\
  &= \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Final Percentage Form.**

$$\boxed{\;A = \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} \times 100\%\;}$$

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A software system has been observed for **5 failure cycles**. The operational times recorded are: 120 h, 150 h, 110 h, 140 h, 130 h. The corresponding repair times are: 4 h, 6 h, 5 h, 7 h, 3 h. Calculate **MTTF, MTTR, and Availability**.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute MTTF.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum T_i &= 120 + 150 + 110 + 140 + 130 = 650 \text{ hours} \\
MTTF &= \frac{650}{5} = 130 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute MTTR.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum R_i &= 4 + 6 + 5 + 7 + 3 = 25 \text{ hours} \\
MTTR &= \frac{25}{5} = 5 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Availability.**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} \times 100\% \\
  &= \frac{130}{130 + 5} \times 100\% \\
  &= \frac{130}{135} \times 100\% \\
  &\approx 96.30\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** This system is available **96.30%** of the time — equivalent to roughly **28.9 days of downtime per year**. A common KTU requirement for mission-critical systems is **99.999% ("Five Nines")** availability, which is only **5.26 minutes** of allowed downtime per year.

## 3.3 The Maintenance Cost Model (Analytical Matrix)

The **Lientz & Swanson Empirical Distribution** of maintenance effort is the standard analytical framework:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= E_{corrective} + E_{adaptive} + E_{perfective} + E_{preventive} \\
E_{total} &= 0.20 \cdot E_{total} + 0.25 \cdot E_{total} + 0.50 \cdot E_{total} + 0.05 \cdot E_{total} \\
0.20 + 0.25 + 0.50 + 0.05 &= 1.00 \;\; \text{(verified)}
\end{aligned}$$

| **Maintenance Type** | **Effort Share** | **Trigger** | **Example Activity** |
|:---------------------|:----------------:|:------------|:---------------------|
| Corrective | 20% | Reported defect | Fix login bug |
| Adaptive | 25% | Environment change | Port to new OS version |
| Perfective | 50% | User request | Add new report feature |
| Preventive | 5% | Internal quality concern | Refactor legacy module |
| **TOTAL** | **100%** | — | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Software Maintenance Process (Block-Level Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the end-to-end maintenance workflow, from issue identification to verification and re-deployment. It uses safe alphanumeric node IDs and clean uppercase labels (no markdown inside the labels).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[USER REPORTS ISSUE OR REQUEST] --> B1{TRIAGE CLASSIFICATION}
    B1 -->|BUG FOUND| C1[CORRECTIVE MAINTENANCE PATH]
    B1 -->|ENV CHANGE| C2[ADAPTIVE MAINTENANCE PATH]
    B1 -->|NEW FEATURE| C3[PERFECTIVE MAINTENANCE PATH]
    B1 -->|CODE QUALITY| C4[PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE PATH]

    C1 --> D1[FAULT LOCALIZATION AND DEBUGGING]
    C2 --> D2[ENVIRONMENT STUDY AND IMPACT ANALYSIS]
    C3 --> D3[REQUIREMENT ELICITATION AND DESIGN]
    C4 --> D4[CODE REFACTORING AND CLEANUP]

    D1 --> E1[CODE MODIFICATION]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1

    E1 --> F1[UNIT AND INTEGRATION TESTING]
    F1 --> G1[REGRESSION TEST SUITE EXECUTION]
    G1 --> H1{ALL TESTS PASS}
    H1 -->|NO| I1[RETURN TO CODE MODIFICATION]
    I1 --> E1
    H1 -->|YES| J1[CHANGE CONTROL BOARD REVIEW]
    J1 --> K1[PRODUCTION DEPLOYMENT]
    K1 --> L1[POST DEPLOYMENT MONITORING]
    L1 --> M1[UPDATE CONFIGURATION BASELINE]
    M1 --> Z1[MAINTENANCE CYCLE CLOSED]
```

## 4.2 Maintenance Type Distribution (Lientz & Swanson Model)

This diagram visualizes the **empirical distribution** of maintenance effort by category:

```mermaid
pie title Maintenance Effort Distribution
    "Perfective Maintenance" : 50
    "Adaptive Maintenance" : 25
    "Corrective Maintenance" : 20
    "Preventive Maintenance" : 5
```

## 4.3 Re-Engineering & Reverse Engineering Block Architecture

The following diagram illustrates the **forward and reverse engineering pipeline** used in legacy system modernization:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[LEGACY SYSTEM SOURCE CODE] --> Q1[REVERSE ENGINEERING PHASE]
    Q1 --> Q2[EXTRACT DESIGN AND SPECIFICATIONS]
    Q2 --> Q3[BUILD ABSTRACT SYSTEM MODEL]
    Q3 --> R1[RE ENGINEERING PHASE]
    R1 --> R2[RESTRUCTURE CODE AND DATA]
    R2 --> R3[GENERATE NEW IMPLEMENTATION]
    R3 --> R4[FORWARD ENGINEERING PHASE]
    R4 --> S1[MODERNIZED SOFTWARE SYSTEM]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently asked in KTU):**
> - **Reverse Engineering:** *Source Code → Design / Specification* (Analyzing the "what" and "how")
> - **Re-Engineering:** *Old System → New System* (Includes reverse + restructuring + forward)
> - **Forward Engineering:** *Design / Specification → Source Code* (Traditional development)

## 4.4 Maintenance Cost Composition (Hierarchical Decomposition)

```mermaid
graph TD
    X1[ANNUAL MAINTENANCE COST] --> X2[PERSONNEL COST]
    X1 --> X3[INFRASTRUCTURE COST]
    X1 --> X4[TOOLS AND LICENSE COST]
    X1 --> X5[TRAINING COST]
    X1 --> X6[OUTSOURCING COST]
    X2 --> Y1[DEVELOPER SALARY]
    X2 --> Y2[TESTER SALARY]
    X2 --> Y3[SUPERVISOR COST]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Q: Define software maintenance. List any FOUR types of maintenance with a one-line example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Software maintenance is the modification of a software product after delivery to correct faults, improve performance or other attributes, or adapt the product to a changed environment (per ISO/IEC 14764).
>
> **Types (2 Marks — ½ Mark Each):**
> 1. **Corrective** — Fixing a bug such as wrong calculation in invoice
> 2. **Adaptive** — Migrating application from Windows Server 2012 to 2019
> 3. **Perfective** — Adding a new "export to PDF" feature
> 4. **Preventive** — Refactoring a heavily coupled module to improve cohesion

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: Differentiate between re-engineering and reverse engineering.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Reverse Engineering | Re-Engineering |
|:-------|:--------------------|:---------------|
| **Direction** | Code → Design/Spec | Old System → New System |
| **Goal** | Understand existing system | Restructure for improvement |
| **Output** | Design documents | New working system |
| **Scope** | Analysis only | Reverse + Restructure + Forward |

> **Memory Aid:** Reverse = *reading* the system; Re-Engineering = *transforming* the system.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**
**Q: (a) Explain in detail the various categories of software maintenance as per the Lientz and Swanson classification. For each type, give one real-world example and justify why it is the dominant cost in the software lifecycle.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Introduction (1 Mark):** Lientz and Swanson (1980) categorized maintenance into four primary types based on empirical studies of large-scale software systems.
>
> **1. Corrective Maintenance (1.5 Marks):**
> - **Definition:** Diagnosing and fixing defects discovered in operational software
> - **Example:** Fixing a NullPointerException that crashes the payment module during checkout
> - **Typical share:** 20% of total maintenance effort
>
> **2. Adaptive Maintenance (1.5 Marks):**
> - **Definition:** Modifying software to accommodate changes in the external environment
> - **Example:** Updating a healthcare system to comply with the new DPDP Act 2023 (India's data protection law)
> - **Typical share:** 25% of total maintenance effort
>
> **3. Perfective Maintenance (1.5 Marks):**
> - **Definition:** Adding new features or improving existing functionality based on user requests
> - **Example:** Adding a "dark mode" UI to a mobile banking app after user surveys
> - **Typical share:** 50% (the LARGEST)
>
> **4. Preventive Maintenance (1.5 Marks):**
> - **Definition:** Proactive refactoring to improve maintainability and prevent future failures
> - **Example:** Restructuring a 15-year-old COBOL module into microservices
> - **Typical share:** 5%

**Q: (b) A software system was monitored for 6 failure cycles. The uptimes (in hours) are: 200, 250, 180, 220, 210, 240. The downtimes are: 8, 12, 10, 6, 9, 5 hours. Compute MTTF, MTTR, and System Availability. Comment on whether the system meets the "Five Nines" (99.999%) standard.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — MTTF Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum T_i &= 200 + 250 + 180 + 220 + 210 + 240 = 1300 \text{ hours} \\
MTTF &= \frac{1300}{6} \approx 216.67 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — MTTR Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum R_i &= 8 + 12 + 10 + 6 + 9 + 5 = 50 \text{ hours} \\
MTTR &= \frac{50}{6} \approx 8.33 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Availability Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} \times 100\% \\
  &= \frac{216.67}{216.67 + 8.33} \times 100\% \\
  &= \frac{216.67}{225.00} \times 100\% \\
  &\approx 96.30\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Five Nines (1 Mark):**
The system achieves only **96.30%** availability, which is far below the **99.999%** "Five Nines" standard. The system has approximately **318.2 hours** of annual downtime, whereas the Five Nines standard allows only **5.26 minutes** per year. Therefore, this system is suitable for general business use but **NOT** for mission-critical applications like air traffic control or financial trading.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice Option 2**

**Q: (a) What is software re-engineering? Explain its process model with a neat block diagram. How does it differ from new development?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Software re-engineering is the systematic transformation of an existing legacy system into a new, modernized form, while preserving its functional behavior. It involves understanding, restructuring, and rebuilding the system without changing its external behavior.
>
> **Process Model Steps (5 Marks):**
> 1. **Reverse Engineering** — Analyze the existing code to extract design and specification
> 2. **Program Structure Improvement** — Refactor code structure, naming, and comments
> 3. **Program Modularization** — Break monolithic code into reusable modules
> 4. **Data Re-organization** — Normalize/redesign the database schema
> 5. **Forward Engineering** — Regenerate the modernized system
>
> **Differences from New Development (1 Mark):**
> - New development starts from **scratch** with a requirements specification
> - Re-engineering starts from an **existing system** and preserves business logic

**Q: (b) Explain the key challenges and risk factors in software maintenance. Discuss how Boehm's Law of Software Evolution is relevant here.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Introduction (1 Mark):** Software maintenance, though the longest lifecycle phase, faces unique challenges that make it more complex than initial development.
>
> **Key Challenges (4 Marks):**
> 1. **Limited Understanding** — Original developers often unavailable; code is poorly documented
> 2. **Software Entropy** — Code degrades over time with each modification
> 3. **Ripple Effects** — A small change can cascade into many modules
> 4. **Staff Turnover** — Loss of domain knowledge over the years
> 5. **Test Coverage Decay** — Test suite no longer covers new modifications
> 6. **Configuration Drift** — Multiple versions become hard to track
>
> **Boehm's Law of Software Evolution (2 Marks):**
> - *"As a software system evolves, its complexity increases unless explicit efforts are made to reduce it."*
> - This law (formulated by Barry Boehm, 1981) explains why maintenance cost grows **exponentially** with each release
> - It justifies the need for **preventive maintenance** and **re-engineering** as countermeasures

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing "Preventive Maintenance" with "Corrective Maintenance." Preventive is **proactive** (refactoring), Corrective is **reactive** (bug-fixing).
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Writing *re-engineering = reverse engineering*. Re-engineering is a **complete process** (reverse + restructure + forward); reverse is only **one phase**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In numerical problems, forgetting to **multiply by 100%** for the availability answer.
> - **Mark Loss:** Do not write vague statements like *"maintenance is costly"* — always quote the **60-80%** figure and cite **Lientz & Swanson's empirical study**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Software Maintenance**

- **Definition:** Post-delivery modification (corrective, adaptive, perfective, preventive) per **ISO/IEC 14764**
- **Cost Share:** Maintenance consumes **60–80%** of total lifecycle cost
- **Lientz & Swanson Distribution:**
  - Corrective → **20%**
  - Adaptive → **25%**
  - Perfective → **50%** (largest)
  - Preventive → **5%**
- **Perfective Maintenance** is the **largest** category (often asked as a trick question)
- **Availability Formula:** $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} \times 100\%$
- **Five Nines Standard:** $99.999\%$ availability = **5.26 minutes** downtime/year
- **Re-Engineering = Reverse + Restructure + Forward** (remember this 3-step pipeline)
- **Boehm's Law:** Complexity increases unless preventive maintenance is performed
- **IEEE 1219** is the maintenance standard; **ISO/IEC 14764** defines terminology
- **Software Entropy (Payne's Law):** Unmaintained code degrades over time
- **Legacy Systems:** Old but business-critical systems often maintained via re-engineering
- **Ripple Effect:** A change in one module affecting many others — measured by **REIF** (Ripple Effect Impact Factor)
- **CMMI Maintenance Maturity:** Level 5 organizations practice preventive + perfective maintenance proactively
- **Real-World Anecdote:** NASA's Voyager software is still being **maintained** after **40+ years** — the ultimate example of long-term software maintenance
- **KLU Classic Exam Q:** *"Which type of maintenance is the most expensive?"* → Answer: **Perfective** (50% share)
- **Defect Density formula:** $DD = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$ — must be reported in **defects per KLOC**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Project Management. Risk Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Software Project Management. Risk Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Software Project Management — The Core Idea

**Software Project Management (SPM)** is the disciplined application of knowledge, skills, tools, and techniques to project activities to meet defined software requirements, delivered on time, within budget, and to the required quality level. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, SPM is treated as the **nerve centre** of all software engineering activities because every process — analysis, design, coding, testing, deployment — must be orchestrated, scheduled, and controlled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Use):**
> *Software Project Management is the art and science of planning, organizing, staffing, directing, and controlling organizational resources to achieve specific goals within time, cost, and performance constraints.*

In the **Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK)** adopted by industry, a software project is governed by the classic **Iron Triangle** (also called the **Triple Constraint**):

| Constraint | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Scope** | What must be built (features, functions) |
| **Time** | Schedule of delivery |
| **Cost** | Budget / resources spent |
| **Quality** | Implicitly tied to scope satisfaction |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Building a House

Imagine you are constructing a two-storey house in Kerala with a fixed budget of ₹50 lakh and a deadline of 12 months. You must:

- Hire a contractor (staffing)
- Buy cement, steel, and tiles (resource procurement)
- Pour the foundation → raise walls → roof → finishing (scheduling)
- Handle risks like monsoon delays, price hikes, or labour strikes (risk management)
- Track weekly progress (monitoring & control)

Software project management is **identical**, except the "bricks" are code modules, and the "monsoon" can be a change in customer requirements. Without structured management, software projects famously suffer what the **Standish Group CHAOS Report** documented as runaway failures.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The CHAOS Report categorizes projects as **Resolved (successful)**, **Challenged (over budget/time but completed)**, and **Impaired (cancelled)**. Excellent risk management shifts projects *left* on this spectrum.

### 1.3 Risk Management — The Core Idea

**Risk** in software engineering is defined as a **future adverse event** that has a non-zero probability of occurrence and, if it occurs, will have a negative impact on project objectives such as cost, schedule, or quality.
