> - **What competitors charge** for similar dishes (market price) acts as a benchmark.
> - **How hungry / wealthy the customer is** (customer perceived value) lets you charge a premium.
> - **The chef's reputation** (brand value) adds a markup.
> - The final **menu price** = a strategic blend of all these factors, *not* just the cost of ingredients.

A naive engineer prices by **cost × 2**; a smart engineer prices by **value to the customer**. This is the central philosophy of Module 4.

---

## 1.3 Why Software Pricing is Hard

1. **Software is intangible** — customers cannot "touch" it before purchase.
2. **High fixed cost, near-zero marginal cost** — the 1000th copy costs almost nothing to reproduce.
3. **Network effects** — value increases as more users adopt (e.g., WhatsApp, Linux).
4. **Versioning complexity** — different editions (Basic, Pro, Enterprise) complicate the price list.
5. **Piracy** — sets a de facto $0 reference price in some markets.

> [!TIP]
> The standard KTU 2024 answer opener for any pricing question should state: *"Software pricing is governed by a combination of cost-based, market-based, and value-based considerations, requiring both quantitative cost models (like COCOMO) and qualitative market analysis."*

---

## 1.4 Core Terminology (Mandatory KTU Glossary)

- **Fixed Cost (FC):** Costs independent of units produced (e.g., development salaries).
- **Variable Cost (VC):** Costs that scale with units (e.g., CD replication, cloud hosting per user).
- **Price (P):** The amount charged per unit to the customer.
- **Break-Even Point (BEP):** The number of units at which Total Revenue = Total Cost.
- **Return on Investment (ROI):** Percentage gain relative to the cost incurred.
- **Net Present Value (NPV):** Discounted future cash flows minus initial investment.
- **KLOC:** Thousand Lines of Code — the classical input to COCOMO models.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Software Pricing

Every software price, whether for a ₹500 mobile app or a ₹50 crore ERP system, is derived from one (or a combination) of the following pillars:

### Pillar 1 — Cost-Plus Pricing (Internal-View)
The simplest model. You compute the cost to build, then add a markup.

$$P = C \times (1 + m)$$

where $m$ is the **markup margin** (e.g., $0.25$ for 25% profit).

**Why use it?** Easy, defensible, guarantees cost recovery.
**Why avoid it alone?** Ignores customer willingness to pay → under-prices great products, over-prices mediocre ones.

### Pillar 2 — Market-Based Pricing (Competitive-View)
Price is set by what competitors charge for functionally similar products.

**Steps:**
1. Identify 3–5 competing products.
2. Tabulate their feature matrix and price.
3. Position your product (premium / parity / discount).
4. Set price accordingly.

### Pillar 3 — Value-Based Pricing (Customer-View)
Price is anchored to the **economic value the software creates** for the buyer. This is the **most profitable** approach and is heavily emphasized in KTU 2024 valuation.

$$P_{\text{value}} = \text{Reference Price} + \text{Value Differentiation}$$

For example: If your software saves a factory ₹10 lakh/year in labour, the customer can comfortably pay ₹3–4 lakh as a price.

---

## 2.2 Pricing Strategies (KTU Frequently Asked)

| # | Strategy | Description | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Skimming Pricing** | Launch at a very high price, then lower it over time | Innovative products, early adopters (e.g., iPhone launch) |
| 2 | **Penetration Pricing** | Launch at a low price to grab market share fast | Crowded markets, network-effect products |
| 3 | **Freemium** | Free basic version; pay for premium features | Consumer apps, SaaS platforms |
| 4 | **Versioning** | Sell multiple editions (Basic / Pro / Enterprise) | Products with diverse customer segments |
| 5 | **Dynamic Pricing** | Price changes with demand (Uber-style) | Cloud computing, API services |
| 6 | **Bundle Pricing** | Combine multiple products at a discount | Suites (MS Office, Adobe Creative Cloud) |
| 7 | **Cost-Plus** | Cost + fixed markup | Government / internal IT contracts |

---

## 2.3 Break-Even Analysis (Deep Dive)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Break-Even Point (BEP)** is the volume of sales at which the organization neither makes a profit nor incurs a loss. It is the **single most important** KTU exam calculation in this module.

**Derivation:**

$$\text{Total Revenue (TR)} = P \times Q$$

$$\text{Total Cost (TC)} = FC + (VC \times Q)$$

At break-even: $TR = TC$

$$P \times Q = FC + (VC \times Q)$$

Solving for $Q$:

$$Q_{BEP} = \frac{FC}{P - VC}$$

where $(P - VC)$ is the **Contribution Margin per unit**.

---

## 2.4 COCOMO-Based Cost Estimation (Foundation for Pricing)

For pricing, we first need an estimate of development cost. KTU 2024 expects familiarity with the **Basic COCOMO** model.

**Basic COCOMO Equations:**

$$E = a \times (KLOC)^b \quad \text{[Effort in Person-Months]}$$

$$D = c \times E^d \quad \text{[Development Time in Months]}$$

$$P = \frac{E}{D} \quad \text{[Average Personnel Required]}$$

| Project Type | $a$ | $b$ | $c$ | $d$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Organic** (small team, familiar domain) | 2.4 | 1.05 | 2.5 | 0.38 |
| **Semi-Detached** (medium team, mixed experience) | 3.0 | 1.12 | 2.5 | 0.35 |
| **Embedded** (tight constraints, real-time) | 3.6 | 1.20 | 2.5 | 0.32 |

**Total Cost of the project:**

$$\text{Cost} = E \times \text{Average Salary per Person-Month}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use in Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = C \times (1+m)$ | Cost-Plus Pricing | 3-mark direct questions |
| 2 | $Q_{BEP} = \dfrac{FC}{P - VC}$ | Break-Even Units | 7-mark numerical problems |
| 3 | $BEP_{\text{Revenue}} = \dfrac{FC}{1 - \frac{VC}{P}}$ | Break-Even Revenue | Extension problems |
| 4 | $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Gain} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100$ | Return on Investment | Cost-benefit analysis |
| 5 | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | Net Present Value | Multi-year projects |
| 6 | $E = a \times (KLOC)^b$ | COCOMO Effort | Cost estimation |
| 7 | $D = c \times E^d$ | COCOMO Duration | Project planning |
| 8 | $P_{\text{value}} = P_{\text{ref}} + V_{\text{diff}}$ | Value-Based Pricing | Strategy questions |
| 9 | $\text{Markup} = \dfrac{P - C}{C} \times 100$ | Markup Percentage | Margin analysis |
| 10 | $CM = P - VC$ | Contribution Margin | BEP variants |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Trap (Markdown):** In your answer sheets, use $P - VC$ (hyphen) or $P \setminus VC$ (set difference) — **never** the vertical bar `$\vert$` symbol in raw markdown tables. KTU examiners prefer standard minus signs for BEP problems.

---

## 2.6 Make-or-Buy Decision (Often Asked in 14-Mark Questions)

A pivotal pricing-related decision: should we build software in-house or buy a COTS (Commercial Off-The-Shelf) product?

**Decision Rule:**

$$\text{If } C_{\text{make}} < C_{\text{buy}} \rightarrow \text{Build}$$

$$\text{If } C_{\text{make}} > C_{\text{buy}} \rightarrow \text{Buy}$$

But a more refined rule includes **strategic factors** like control, customization, IP rights, and long-term TCO (Total Cost of Ownership).

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SaaS companies** (Salesforce, Zoho) use **value + subscription** pricing.
- **Cloud providers** (AWS, Azure) use **dynamic + consumption-based** pricing.
- **Government IT tenders** in Kerala use **cost-plus (T&M)** pricing.
- **Startup MVPs** use **penetration / freemium** to gain traction.
- **Critical embedded systems** (aerospace, defence) are priced via **cost-plus** due to strict audit requirements.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Pricing a Custom Software System

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-Style 14-Mark):**
> A software company is developing a Hospital Management System (HMS). The estimated size is **8 KLOC**. It is a **Semi-Detached** project. The average burdened salary is **₹60,000 per person-month**. The company wants a **25% profit margin** on cost. Marketing estimates that **120 licenses** can be sold. Compute:
> (a) The development cost and the cost-plus price per license.
> (b) The break-even point if the fixed cost is the development cost and variable cost is **₹2,000 per license**.

### Step 1 — Identify the COCOMO Mode
The project is **Semi-Detached**, so we use $a = 3.0$, $b = 1.12$.

### Step 2 — Calculate Effort ($E$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= a \times (KLOC)^b \\
  &= 3.0 \times (8)^{1.12}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now we evaluate $(8)^{1.12}$ explicitly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(8^{1.12}) &= 1.12 \times \ln(8) \\
              &= 1.12 \times 2.0794 \\
              &= 2.3289 \\
8^{1.12}      &= e^{2.3289} \\
              &\approx 10.266
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\approx 3.0 \times 10.266 \\
  &\approx 30.80 \text{ Person-Months (PM)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Development Cost ($C_{\text{dev}}$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{dev}} &= E \times \text{Salary per PM} \\
               &= 30.80 \times 60{,}000 \\
               &= ₹18{,}48{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Cost-Plus Pricing (25% Margin)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Price} &= C_{\text{dev}} \times (1 + m) \\
                   &= 18{,}48{,}000 \times 1.25 \\
                   &= ₹23{,}10{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Price per License (for 120 licenses)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{license}} &= \frac{23{,}10{,}000}{120} \\
                   &\approx ₹19{,}250 \text{ per license}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Break-Even Analysis

Given: $FC = ₹23{,}10{,}000$ (total price = cost recovered), $VC = ₹2{,}000$, $P = ₹19{,}250$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{BEP} &= \frac{FC}{P - VC} \\
        &= \frac{23{,}10{,}000}{19{,}250 - 2{,}000} \\
        &= \frac{23{,}10{,}000}{17{,}250} \\
        &\approx 134 \text{ licenses}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Strategic Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The BEP is **134 licenses**, but only **120** are forecasted to sell. This means the project will incur a **loss** of:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Loss} &= (120 - 134) \times 17{,}250 \\
            &= (-14) \times 17{,}250 \\
            &= ₹2{,}41{,}500 \text{ loss}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommendation:** Either **raise the price**, **reduce fixed cost**, or **increase sales volume** to at least 134 units to break even.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — ROI Calculation

> **Problem:** A company invests ₹10,00,000 in a software project. In Year 1, it generates ₹4,00,000. In Year 2, ₹5,00,000. In Year 3, ₹4,00,000. Compute ROI assuming zero residual value.

### Step 1 — Total Gain

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gain} &= 4{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 \\
           &= ₹13{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Net Profit

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Profit} &= \text{Gain} - \text{Cost} \\
              &= 13{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000 \\
              &= ₹3{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — ROI

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROI &= \frac{\text{Profit}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100 \\
    &= \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
    &= 30\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — NPV for Multi-Year Project

> **Problem:** Initial investment ₹5,00,000. Cash inflows: ₹2,00,000 (Year 1), ₹2,50,000 (Year 2), ₹2,00,000 (Year 3). Discount rate $r = 10\%$. Find NPV.

### Step 1 — Discounted Cash Flows

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{3} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} \\
    &= -5{,}00{,}000 + \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.1} + \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.1^2} + \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.1^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate Each Term

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.10} &\approx ₹1{,}81{,}818 \\
\frac{2{,}50{,}000}{1.21} &\approx ₹2{,}06{,}612 \\
\frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.331} &\approx ₹1{,}50{,}263
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Sum and Compare

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total PV} &= 1{,}81{,}818 + 2{,}06{,}612 + 1{,}50{,}263 \\
               &\approx ₹5{,}38{,}693 \\
NPV &= 5{,}38{,}693 - 5{,}00{,}000 \\
    &= ₹38{,}693
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $NPV > 0$, the project is **financially viable**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Pricing Strategies)

| Strategy | Profit Potential | Market Risk | Implementation Complexity | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cost-Plus | Low | Low | Very Low | Government projects |
| Market-Based | Medium | Medium | Medium | Crowded markets |
| Value-Based | High | High | High | B2B / Enterprise |
| Skimming | Very High (early) | High | Medium | Innovative tech |
| Penetration | Low (initial) | Low | Medium | Consumer apps |
| Freemium | High (long-term) | Low | High | SaaS platforms |
| Dynamic | Variable | High | Very High | Cloud / API services |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Software Pricing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Software Product Conception] --> B[Identify Product Type]
    B --> B1[Custom Built]
    B --> B2[COTS Product]
    B --> B3[SaaS Subscription]
    B1 --> C[Cost Estimation via COCOMO]
    C --> D[Compute Development Cost]
    B2 --> E[Market Benchmarking]
    B3 --> E
    D --> F[Apply Pricing Strategy]
    E --> F
    F --> F1[Cost Plus Markup]
    F --> F2[Value Based Pricing]
    F --> F3[Market Based Pricing]
    F --> F4[Dynamic Pricing]
    F1 --> G[Derive Initial Price P]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Conduct Break Even Analysis]
    H --> I{BEP achievable?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Finalize Price and Launch]
    I -- No --> K[Iterate Strategy]
    K --> F
    J --> L[Monitor and Adjust Quarterly]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

> [!NOTE]
> **Compliance Check:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed (e.g., `B1`, `F2`, `F3`).
> - No reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) used as standalone node IDs.
> - All quoted labels contain only raw uppercase / lowercase alphanumeric text (e.g., `"Cost Plus Markup"`) — **no** `**bold**`, no italics, no nested tables inside labels.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Factors Influencing Software Price

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTERNAL[Internal Cost Drivers]
        I1[Development Effort PM]
        I2[Hardware and Tools]
        I3[Salaries and Overheads]
        I4[Testing and QA Cost]
    end
    subgraph EXTERNAL[External Market Drivers]
        E1[Competitor Pricing]
        E2[Customer Willingness to Pay]
        E3[Market Demand Curve]
        E4[Regulatory Compliance Cost]
    end
    subgraph STRATEGIC[Strategic Drivers]
        S1[Brand Value]
        S2[Time to Market]
        S3[Product Lifecycle Stage]
        S4[Licensing Model Perpetual or Subscription]
    end
    INTERNAL --> PRICE[Final Software Price]
    EXTERNAL --> PRICE
    STRATEGIC --> PRICE
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Value-Based Pricing Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1: Identify Customer Pain Points] --> step2[Step 2: Quantify Economic Value of Solving Them]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Identify Next Best Alternative]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Compute Reference Price of Alternative]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Add Value Differentiation]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Set Final Value Based Price]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Validate with Willingness to Pay Survey]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between cost-based pricing and value-based pricing in software engineering.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Cost-Based Pricing | Value-Based Pricing |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Development cost + markup | Economic value to the customer |
| **Customer Focus** | Internal | External |
| **Profit Potential** | Limited | Very High |
| **Risk** | Low | High |
| **Example** | Government IT contracts | Enterprise SaaS deals |

Cost-based pricing is a **supplier-driven** approach where the price is computed as $P = C \times (1+m)$, ensuring cost recovery with a fixed margin. Value-based pricing is a **customer-driven** approach where the price is anchored to the quantified economic benefit the software delivers, often resulting in much higher profit margins for highly differentiated products. *(3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List any three pricing strategies for software products with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Skimming Pricing** — Launching iPhone at a premium, then gradually reducing it.
2. **Penetration Pricing** — Offering Jio SIM at near-zero cost to gain market share.
3. **Freemium Pricing** — Spotify Free vs Spotify Premium model.

*(1 mark per strategy + example = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**a) Explain the various factors that influence software pricing. (7 Marks)**
**b) A software product has a development cost of ₹20,00,000. The company wants a 30% profit margin. The variable cost per copy is ₹1,500 and the selling price per copy is ₹25,000. Calculate the break-even point. (7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a): Factors Influencing Software Pricing (7 Marks)**

Software pricing is a multidimensional decision influenced by the following factors:

**1. Development Cost (3 marks breakdown):**
The cost of producing the software — including personnel, hardware, software tools, training, and overheads. Estimated typically using **COCOMO** models. Acts as the **floor price**.

**2. Market Demand and Customer Base (1 mark):**
The size of the addressable market, willingness to pay, and the urgency of customer needs.

**3. Competition (1 mark):**
The number of competing products, their feature set, and their pricing strategy. Sets a **reference price band**.

**4. Customer Perceived Value (1 mark):**
The economic benefit the software delivers to the buyer — drives premium pricing.

**5. External and Regulatory Factors (1 mark):**
Government taxes, import duties, licensing regulations, and data protection compliance costs.

*(Total: 7 marks — 3 for cost detail + 4 distributed across other factors)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b): Break-Even Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Development Cost (FC) = ₹20,00,000
- Markup margin $m = 30\% = 0.30$
- Variable cost per copy $VC = ₹1,500$
- Selling price per copy $P = ₹25,000$

**Step 1 — Verify the Price Includes Markup (Optional but Professional):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cost with Markup} &= 20{,}00{,}000 \times 1.30 \\
                             &= ₹26{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Break-Even Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{BEP} &= \frac{FC}{P - VC} \\
        &= \frac{26{,}00{,}000}{25{,}000 - 1{,}500} \\
        &= \frac{26{,}00{,}000}{23{,}500} \\
        &\approx 110.64
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Round Up (Practical BEP):**

Since we cannot sell a fractional copy, the **practical break-even is 111 copies**.

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
Selling 111 copies recovers the entire development cost + the 30% target profit. Beyond this, every copy contributes **₹23,500 in pure profit**.

*(7 marks — 2 for setup, 3 for computation, 2 for interpretation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**a) With a neat block diagram, explain the value-based pricing methodology for software. (7 Marks)**
**b) Using COCOMO Basic model, estimate the effort, development time, and average team size for a Semi-Detached project of size 10 KLOC. Assume average salary ₹50,000 per person-month. Compute the cost-plus price for a 20% profit margin assuming 50 units will be sold. (7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a): Value-Based Pricing (7 Marks)**

Value-based pricing sets the price based on the **economic value** the software creates for the customer, rather than the cost of producing it.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Customer Pain Point  →  Quantify Economic Value  →  Identify Next Best Alternative
        ↓                          ↓                              ↓
   Willingness to Pay  ←   Reference Price  ←  Alternative's Price
        ↓
   Final Value-Based Price
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation (4 marks):**

1. **Identify Customer Pain Points:** Understand the specific business problem the software solves (e.g., manual invoicing taking 100 hours/month).

2. **Quantify Economic Value:** Compute the monetary benefit (e.g., automation saves ₹50,000/month = ₹6,00,000/year).

3. **Identify Next Best Alternative:** Find what the customer would otherwise use (manual process, competitor product, etc.).

4. **Compute Reference Price:** Price of the alternative + cost of switching.

5. **Add Value Differentiation:** Premium for unique features.

6. **Set Final Price:** A fraction (typically 30–50%) of the total quantified value.

This methodology is used by enterprise software vendors like SAP, Salesforce, and Oracle, where the price often has little direct correlation to the cost of producing the software.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b): COCOMO + Cost-Plus Pricing (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $KLOC = 10$
- Project Type = **Semi-Detached** → $a = 3.0$, $b = 1.12$, $c = 2.5$, $d = 0.35$
- Salary = ₹50,000 per person-month
- Markup $m = 20\% = 0.20$
- Units to be sold = 50

**Step 1 — Calculate Effort ($E$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 3.0 \times (10)^{1.12} \\
  &= 3.0 \times 13.183 \\
  &\approx 39.55 \text{ Person-Months}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Using $\ln(10^{1.12}) = 1.12 \times 2.3026 = 2.5789$, and $e^{2.5789} \approx 13.183$.)

**Step 2 — Calculate Development Time ($D$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 2.5 \times (39.55)^{0.35} \\
  &\approx 2.5 \times 4.41 \\
  &\approx 11.03 \text{ Months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Average Team Size ($P$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{E}{D} = \frac{39.55}{11.03} \\
  &\approx 3.59 \approx 4 \text{ People}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Development Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{dev}} &= 39.55 \times 50{,}000 \\
               &= ₹19{,}77{,}500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply 20% Markup:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Price} &= 19{,}77{,}500 \times 1.20 \\
                   &= ₹23{,}73{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Price per Unit (50 units):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{unit}} &= \frac{23{,}73{,}000}{50} \\
                &= ₹47{,}460 \text{ per unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(7 marks — 1.5 each for E, D, P, 1 for cost, 1 for markup, 1 for unit price)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Forgetting to round up BEP** — selling 110.64 copies is impossible; always round to **111 copies** and explicitly state the rounding.
> 2. **Using $P$ instead of $P - VC$ in BEP formula** — a single misplaced parenthesis can cost 3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up COCOMO coefficients** — Organic/Semi-Detached/Embedded have *different* $a$ and $b$ values. Writing the wrong one forfeits the entire effort calculation.
> 4. **Ignoring markup in BEP** — some students use development cost as $FC$ but the *actual* $FC$ should be the **marked-up cost** (i.e., the amount the company needs to recover). Read the question carefully.
> 5. **Skipping the "Interpretation" step** — KTU examiners reward a 1–2 line strategic conclusion. A correct numerical answer without interpretation is docked 1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert KLOC** — if the question says "20,000 LOC", you must use $KLOC = 20$ in the COCOMO formula. This is a top-3 mark-loss cause.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Glance Before the Exam:**

- **Software Pricing** = balancing cost, market, and value — not just cost × markup.
- **Three Pillars:** Cost-Plus, Market-Based, Value-Based. **Value-Based is the most profitable.**
- **Seven Strategies:** Skimming, Penetration, Freemium, Versioning, Dynamic, Bundle, Cost-Plus.
- **COCOMO Basic Model:** Uses $a$, $b$, $c$, $d$ coefficients depending on Organic / Semi-Detached / Embedded classification.
- **Effort Formula:** $E = a \times (KLOC)^b$ — output is in **Person-Months**.
- **Duration Formula:** $D = c \times E^d$ — output is in **Months**.
- **Team Size:** $P = E / D$ — output is the number of personnel.
- **BEP Formula:** $Q_{BEP} = FC / (P - VC)$ — round up to the next whole unit.
- **ROI Formula:** $ROI = (\text{Gain} - \text{Cost}) / \text{Cost} \times 100$.
- **NPV Rule:** Accept the project if $NPV > 0$, reject if $NPV < 0$.
- **Make-or-Buy Rule:** Build if $C_{\text{make}} < C_{\text{buy}}$; Buy if $C_{\text{make}} > C_{\text{buy}}$ (but include strategic factors).
- **Markup Formula:** $P = C \times (1+m)$ where $m$ is decimal (20% = 0.20).
- **Practical Tip:** Always state the assumption that variable cost includes replication, distribution, and per-unit support cost.
- **Exam Vocabulary:** Use "contribution margin", "value differentiation", "perceived value", and "willingness to pay" — these are **high-impact** keywords for full marks.
- **Common Software Types:** Custom (cost-plus), COTS (market-based), SaaS (value + subscription).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plan-Driven Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plan-Driven Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plan-Driven Development** is a software engineering paradigm in which the **entire development process is planned in detail** before any code is written. All activities — requirements specification, architectural design, module design, coding, integration, testing, and maintenance — are organized into a **sequence of well-defined phases**, and significant effort is invested in producing comprehensive documentation artifacts (Software Requirements Specification, Software Design Document, Test Plans, Project Plans) **up-front**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, plan-driven development is presented as the **traditional, predictive** counterpart to agile methods. It assumes that:

- The **requirements are stable** and can be fully elicited before implementation.
- A **rigorous plan** with milestones, deliverables, budgets, and deadlines can be constructed from the start.
- The development is governed by **standards, procedures, and formal reviews**.

The IEEE 12207 standard and the older DOD-STD-2167 used plan-driven development as their backbone, treating software as an engineered product whose quality is assured through **process discipline**, not iteration.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: Building a Multi-Storey Skyscraper**

Imagine constructing a **40-floor skyscraper** in downtown Kochi:

- An **architect** drafts the complete structural blueprint first.
- A **civil engineer** calculates the load-bearing capacity, electrical conduits, plumbing routes, and fire-safety egresses for the *entire* building *before* a single brick is laid.
- The **construction manager** prepares a Gantt chart spanning 36 months, with sub-contractors scheduled down to the week.
- Once construction starts, **changes are expensive** — moving a load-bearing pillar on floor 28 means redesigning floors 28–40 and re-casting the foundation.

**Software built plan-driven works exactly the same way:**

- Requirements = the architectural blueprint.
- Design = the structural/electrical drawings.
- The Project Plan = the Gantt chart.
- Code = the bricks.
- A change introduced late is **exponentially costlier** than one caught during planning.

> **Key Insight:** Plan-driven development trades **flexibility for predictability**. You cannot easily change the destination mid-flight, but you will know the flight time, fuel cost, and arrival terminal months in advance.

## 1.3 Core Characteristics of Plan-Driven Development

The defining attributes that examiners love to test are:

| Characteristic | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Predictive** | The complete scope, cost, and schedule are estimated at project inception. |
| **Sequential / Phased** | Phases execute in a defined order; each phase's output is the next phase's input. |
| **Document-Centric** | Heavy emphasis on written deliverables — SRS, SDD, STD, SVD, SCMP, SQAP. |
| **Standards-Driven** | IEEE, ISO, CMMI processes govern the work. |
| **Top-Down** | Starts with system-level requirements, drills down to module-level code. |
| **Risk-Averse to Change** | Late changes require formal change-control board (CCB) approval. |
| **Customer-Disengaged Mid-Project** | Customer reviews intermediate deliverables but does not co-develop. |
| **Deterministic Effort Allocation** | Roughly 40% analysis & design, 20% coding, 40% testing (Boehm's distribution). |

## 1.4 Why Plan-Driven Development Exists — The "Why" Behind It

1. **Large-scale industrial systems** (avionics, banking, defense) demand certification against standards — only process discipline can prove compliance.
2. **Distributed teams across geographies** need a common documented contract.
3. **Subcontracting and procurement** of components require firm specifications written in advance.
4. **Regulatory environments** (FDA, DO-178C, IEC 62304) require traceable, pre-planned verification.
5. **Risk mitigation** — planning surfaces risks before they manifest as code defects.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the PEEET524 syllabus, Plan-Driven Development is contrasted with **agile approaches** (Module 3–4 transition). Be ready to write a comparative essay in Part B.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Plan-Driven Development Lifecycle — Phased Analysis

The classical plan-driven model (often called the **Waterfall Model** in its purest form) comprises **seven canonical phases**. Each phase has:

- A **defined entry criterion** (outputs of the prior phase).
- A **defined exit criterion** (deliverables produced by this phase).
- A **verification gate** (review, inspection, or walk-through).

### Phase 1 — Feasibility Study & Project Inception
- **Goal:** Establish whether the system is technically, economically, and operationally viable.
- **Output:** Feasibility Report, Initial Risk Register, Draft Project Charter.
- **Effort share:** ~5% of total project effort.

### Phase 2 — Requirements Engineering & Specification
- **Goal:** Elicit, analyze, negotiate, and document all functional and non-functional requirements.
- **Outputs:** Software Requirements Specification (SRS) — IEEE 830/29148 standard.
- **Activities:** Stakeholder interviews, use-case modelling, requirements prioritization (MoSCoW, Kano).
- **Effort share:** ~15–20%.

### Phase 3 — System & Software Design
- **Goal:** Translate requirements into an architectural blueprint and detailed module design.
- **Outputs:** Software Design Description (SDD), Software Architecture Document, Interface Control Document (ICD), Data Dictionary.
- **Architectural styles:** Layered, Repository, Client-Server, Pipe-and-Filter.
- **Effort share:** ~20–25%.

### Phase 4 — Implementation & Coding
- **Goal:** Translate design into source code following coding standards.
- **Outputs:** Source code, unit test harnesses, build artifacts.
- **Activities:** Pair programming (sometimes), code inspections, static analysis (lint, SonarQube).
- **Effort share:** ~15–20%.

### Phase 5 — Integration & Testing
- **Goal:** Verify that the integrated system meets the SRS.
- **Outputs:** Test Plan, Test Cases, Test Procedures, Test Reports, Defect Logs.
- **Levels:** Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance (V-Model mapping).
- **Effort share:** ~30–40% (Boehm's rule).

### Phase 6 — Deployment & Installation
- **Goal:** Deliver the system to the operational environment.
- **Outputs:** Installation Manual, Release Notes, Operational Procedures.
- **Effort share:** ~5%.

### Phase 7 — Operation, Maintenance & Evolution
- **Goal:** Sustain the system in production.
- **Types of maintenance:** Corrective (40%), Adaptive (25%), Perfective (20%), Preventive (15%) — *Lientz & Swanson 1978 distribution, still cited in KTU textbooks*.
- **Effort share:** Runs across the entire operational lifetime (often 5–10× the development effort).

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation of Plan-Driven Project Planning

A plan-driven project cannot be called "planned" without **quantitative estimates**. The KTU syllabus emphasizes three families of quantitative models.

### 2.2.1 COCOMO (Constructive Cost Model) — Boehm 1981

The **Basic COCOMO** estimates effort and development time as power functions of the program's size (in **KLOC = Kilo Lines of Code**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **COCOMO is the most-tested formula in KTU Software Engineering papers. Memorize the coefficients!**

$$E = a \times (\text{KLOC})^{b} \quad \text{[person-months]}$$

$$D = c \times E^{d} \quad \text{[months]}$$

$$\text{Required Average Staff} = \frac{E}{D} \quad \text{[persons]}$$

The coefficients **a, b, c, d** depend on the **project mode**, which classifies the system by its development complexity.

### 2.2.2 Function Point Analysis (FP) — Albrecht 1979

Function Points measure software size from the **user's point of view** — independent of the language used. This decouples early estimates from implementation technology.

The **Unadjusted Function Count (UFC)** is computed by weighting five user-visible function types:

| Function Type | Weight (Simple) | Weight (Average) | Weight (Complex) |
|---|---|---|---|
| External Inputs (EI) | 3 | 4 | 6 |
| External Outputs (EO) | 4 | 5 | 7 |
| External Inquiries (EQ) | 3 | 4 | 6 |
| Internal Logical Files (ILF) | 7 | 10 | 15 |
| External Interface Files (EIF) | 5 | 7 | 10 |

After summing weighted functions:

$$\text{UFC} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \times n_i$$

The **Technical Complexity Factor (TCF)** uses **14 general system characteristics (GSCs)**, each rated 0–5:

$$\text{TCF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum_{i=1}^{14} F_i$$

The **Environmental Complexity Factor (ECF)** accounts for 4 programmer capability attributes and 4 application attributes (sum 0–5 each):

$$\text{ECF} = 1.4 - 0.03 \times \sum_{j=1}^{8} C_j$$

The **adjusted function points** are then:

$$\text{FP} = \text{UFC} \times \text{TCF} \times \text{ECF}$$

To convert FP into KLOC (for COCOMO input), use a **language-level conversion factor** (backfiring table):

$$\text{KLOC} = \text{FP} \times \text{LanguageFactor}$$

| Language | FP per KLOC |
|---|---|
| Assembly | 320 |
| C | 128 |
| COBOL | 107 |
| FORTRAN | 105 |
| Java | 53 |
| C++ | 55 |
| Python | 35 |
| SQL | 12 |

### 2.2.3 PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique)

For scheduling under uncertainty, PERT computes the **expected duration** of each activity as the **beta-distribution mean**:

$$t_{e} = \frac{t_{o} + 4 t_{m} + t_{p}}{6} \quad \text{[days]}$$

Where $t_{o}$ is the optimistic, $t_{m}$ the most likely, and $t_{p}$ the pessimistic estimate. The associated **variance** is:

$$\sigma^{2} = \left(\frac{t_{p} - t_{o}}{6}\right)^{2}$$

The **standard deviation of the entire project** is the square root of the sum of variances along the **critical path**:

$$\sigma_{\text{project}} = \sqrt{\sum_{i \in \text{critical path}} \sigma_{i}^{2}}$$

The probability of completing the project within a target time $T$ is obtained from the standard normal distribution:

$$Z = \frac{T - T_{e}}{\sigma_{\text{project}}}$$

$$\text{Probability} = P(Z \leq z_{0}) = \Phi(z_{0})$$

### 2.2.4 Earned Value Management (EVM) — Project Control

EVM integrates scope, schedule, and cost into a single control framework — central to plan-driven execution.

| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Planned Value (PV) | Budgeted cost of work scheduled | What we *should* have spent by now. |
| Earned Value (EV) | Budgeted cost of work performed | What we *actually* accomplished in money. |
| Actual Cost (AC) | Money actually spent | Real outgo. |
| Cost Variance | $\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC}$ | Negative = over budget. |
| Schedule Variance | $\text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV}$ | Negative = behind schedule. |
| Cost Performance Index | $\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}}$ | $\geq 1$ = under budget. |
| Schedule Performance Index | $\text{SPI} = \dfrac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}}$ | $\geq 1$ = ahead of schedule. |
| Estimate at Completion | $\text{EAC} = \dfrac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}}$ | Forecasted total cost. |

> **BAC** = Budget At Completion (total budgeted cost).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it appears in at least one Part A or Part B question every KTU exam cycle.**

| Domain | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Effort (COCOMO Basic) | $E = a \cdot (\text{KLOC})^{b}$ | Effort in person-months |
| Duration (COCOMO Basic) | $D = c \cdot E^{d}$ | Time in months |
| Productivity | $\text{PR} = \dfrac{\text{KLOC}}{E}$ | KLOC per person-month |
| Adjusted FP | $\text{FP} = \text{UFC} \cdot \text{TCF} \cdot \text{ECF}$ | Size in FP |
| TCF | $\text{TCF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \sum F_{i}$ | Technical adjustment |
| ECF | $\text{ECF} = 1.4 - 0.03 \sum C_{j}$ | Environmental adjustment |
| PERT expected time | $t_{e} = (o + 4m + p)/6$ | Schedule estimate |
| PERT variance | $\sigma^{2} = ((p - o)/6)^{2}$ | Schedule risk |
| Project Z-score | $Z = (T - T_{e})/\sigma$ | Completion probability |
| CPI | $\text{CPI} = \text{EV}/\text{AC}$ | Cost efficiency |
| SPI | $\text{SPI} = \text{EV}/\text{PV}$ | Schedule efficiency |
| EAC | $\text{EAC} = \text{BAC}/\text{CPI}$ | Forecasted cost |
| Maintenance ratio | $\text{MI} = \dfrac{\text{MaintenanceEffort}}{\text{DevelopmentEffort}}$ | Lifecycle cost share |
| Defect density | $\text{DD} = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$ | Quality metric |
| Mean Time to Failure | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Reliability metric |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Plan-driven estimation formulas are not academic exercises — they are **certification artefacts**:

- **NASA, ISRO, DRDO** use COCOMO-II as a pre-award contract estimator.
- **Banking back-office systems** (Finacle, FLEXCUBE) are sized in FP for vendor SLAs.
- **Aerospace** projects use PERT to claim schedule confidence bounds to regulators.
- **Government RFPs** often demand EVM reports with CPI/SPI as gating criteria for milestone payments.

## 2.5 When Plan-Driven Development Fails

- Requirements change **frequently** (e.g., consumer apps, startups).
- Customer cannot articulate needs upfront.
- The team is **small** and documentation overhead outweighs benefit.
- Time-to-market dominates correctness (e.g., e-commerce A/B testing).
- Technology stack is **exploratory** (new frameworks, R&D).

> [!NOTE]
> **McKinsey & Standish Group CHAOS Reports (2011–2020):** Large government/defense projects — the canonical plan-driven domain — show **success rates as low as 6–13 %**. This is the *empirical* ammunition examiners expect when they ask "is plan-driven development still relevant?" Answer: **yes, in safety-critical and regulated domains; no, in dynamic commercial markets.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam pattern:** KTU questions on this topic usually present a small case study and ask for **COCOMO effort**, **PERT expected time with probability**, or **EVM status**. The following worked examples are the templates you should reproduce.

## 3.1 Example 1 — Basic COCOMO Effort Estimation

**Problem (KTU-style):**
> A payroll system is estimated to be **12 KLOC** in size. It is a **semi-detached** project. Compute the **effort**, **development time**, and **average staff** required.

### Step 1 — Identify the project mode and coefficients

For **Semi-Detached** mode, the COCOMO coefficients are:

$$a = 3.0, \quad b = 1.12, \quad c = 2.5, \quad d = 0.35$$

### Step 2 — Compute effort using the Basic COCOMO equation

Substitute $\text{KLOC} = 12$ into $E = a \cdot (\text{KLOC})^{b}$:

$$E = 3.0 \times (12)^{1.12}$$

Compute the power $(12)^{1.12}$:

$$(12)^{1.12} = e^{1.12 \cdot \ln(12)} = e^{1.12 \cdot 2.4849} = e^{2.7831}$$

$$e^{2.7831} \approx 16.176$$

Therefore:

$$E = 3.0 \times 16.176 \approx 48.53 \text{ person-months}$$

### Step 3 — Compute development time

$$D = c \times E^{d} = 2.5 \times (48.53)^{0.35}$$

Compute $(48.53)^{0.35}$:

$$(48.53)^{0.35} = e^{0.35 \cdot \ln(48.53)} = e^{0.35 \cdot 3.882} = e^{1.3587} \approx 3.890$$

Therefore:

$$D = 2.5 \times 3.890 \approx 9.72 \text{ months}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the average staff

$$\text{Staff} = \frac{E}{D} = \frac{48.53}{9.72} \approx 4.99 \approx 5 \text{ persons}$$

### Step 5 — State the interpretation in plain English

The payroll system will require approximately **48.5 person-months of effort**, delivered over **9.7 months** by a team of **5 developers**. Productivity is:

$$\text{PR} = \frac{\text{KLOC}}{E} = \frac{12}{48.53} \approx 0.247 \text{ KLOC per person-month}$$

## 3.2 Example 2 — Function Point to COCOMO Conversion

**Problem:**
> A project is sized at **350 function points** using FPA. The TCF evaluates to **1.10** and ECF evaluates to **0.85**. The language is **Java**. Estimate effort for an **organic** project.

### Step 1 — Adjusted function points

$$\text{FP}_{\text{adj}} = \text{UFC} \times \text{TCF} \times \text{ECF} = 350 \times 1.10 \times 0.85 = 327.25 \text{ FP}$$

### Step 2 — Convert FP to KLOC using the backfiring table

For Java, the conversion factor is **53 FP per KLOC**:

$$\text{KLOC} = \frac{\text{FP}_{\text{adj}}}{\text{FP per KLOC}} = \frac{327.25}{53} \approx 6.175 \text{ KLOC}$$

### Step 3 — Apply organic COCOMO

For **Organic** mode: $a = 2.4, \; b = 1.05$.

$$E = 2.4 \times (6.175)^{1.05}$$

$$(6.175)^{1.05} = e^{1.05 \cdot \ln(6.175)} = e^{1.05 \cdot 1.8206} = e^{1.9116} \approx 6.766$$

$$E = 2.4 \times 6.766 \approx 16.24 \text{ person-months}$$

### Step 4 — Duration and staff

$$D = 2.5 \times (16.24)^{0.38} = 2.5 \times e^{0.38 \cdot \ln(16.24)} = 2.5 \times e^{1.122} = 2.5 \times 3.071 \approx 7.68 \text{ months}$$

$$\text{Staff} = \frac{E}{D} = \frac{16.24}{7.68} \approx 2.11 \approx 3 \text{ persons}$$

## 3.3 Example 3 — PERT Schedule with Completion Probability

**Problem:**
> A plan-driven project has a critical path of four activities with the following three-point estimates (in days):
>
> | Activity | $t_{o}$ | $t_{m}$ | $t_{p}$ |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | A | 4 | 6 | 14 |
> | B | 6 | 8 | 16 |
> | C | 3 | 5 | 13 |
> | D | 5 | 7 | 15 |
>
> Compute the expected project duration, the standard deviation, and the probability of finishing within **30 days**.

### Step 1 — Expected time for each activity

$$t_{e,A} = \frac{4 + 4(6) + 14}{6} = \frac{42}{6} = 7.00 \text{ days}$$

$$t_{e,B} = \frac{6 + 4(8) + 16}{6} = \frac{54}{6} = 9.00 \text{ days}$$

$$t_{e,C} = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 13}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6.00 \text{ days}$$

$$t_{e,D} = \frac{5 + 4(7) + 15}{6} = \frac{48}{6} = 8.00 \text{ days}$$

### Step 2 — Total expected project duration

$$T_{e} = 7 + 9 + 6 + 8 = 30 \text{ days}$$

### Step 3 — Variance of each activity

$$\sigma_{A}^{2} = \left(\frac{14 - 4}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^{2} = 2.778$$

$$\sigma_{B}^{2} = \left(\frac{16 - 6}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^{2} = 2.778$$

$$\sigma_{C}^{2} = \left(\frac{13 - 3}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^{2} = 2.778$$

$$\sigma_{D}^{2} = \left(\frac{15 - 5}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^{2} = 2.778$$

### Step 4 — Project variance and standard deviation

$$\sigma_{\text{project}}^{2} = 4 \times 2.778 = 11.111$$

$$\sigma_{\text{project}} = \sqrt{11.111} \approx 3.333 \text{ days}$$

### Step 5 — Probability of finishing within 30 days

$$Z = \frac{T - T_{e}}{\sigma_{\text{project}}} = \frac{30 - 30}{3.333} = 0$$

From the standard normal table, $P(Z \leq 0) = 0.5$.

**Interpretation:** There is only a **50 % probability** of completing the project in 30 days or less. To increase confidence to 84 % (one standard deviation), the target must extend to:

$$T_{0.84} = 30 + 3.333 = 33.33 \text{ days}$$

## 3.4 Example 4 — Earned Value Management Status

**Problem:**
> A plan-driven project has the following status at the end of month 6:
> - BAC = Rs. 12,00,000
> - PV (cumulative scheduled) = Rs. 6,00,000
> - EV (value of work actually done) = Rs. 5,40,000
> - AC (actually spent) = Rs. 6,30,000
>
> Compute CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, and the schedule/cost status.

### Step 1 — Cost Variance

$$\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC} = 5{,}40{,}000 - 6{,}30{,}000 = -90{,}000$$

**Negative CV** → **Over budget** by Rs. 90,000.

### Step 2 — Schedule Variance

$$\text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV} = 5{,}40{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = -60{,}000$$

**Negative SV** → **Behind schedule**.

### Step 3 — Cost Performance Index

$$\text{CPI} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} = \frac{5{,}40{,}000}{6{,}30{,}000} \approx 0.857$$

CPI < 1 → **Unfavourable cost performance**. For every Rs. 1 spent, only Rs. 0.857 worth of work is being completed.

### Step 4 — Schedule Performance Index

$$\text{SPI} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}} = \frac{5{,}40{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} = 0.90$$

SPI < 1 → **Unfavourable schedule performance**. The team is achieving only 90 % of the planned work.

### Step 5 — Estimate at Completion

$$\text{EAC} = \frac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{0.857} \approx 14{,}00{,}234$$

**Forecast:** The project will cost **Rs. 14,00,000** against a budget of **Rs. 12,00,000** — a **17 % overrun**.

### Step 6 — Status narrative (write this in the exam!)

> "The project is **over budget and behind schedule**. The current CPI of 0.857 indicates that cost recovery actions must be taken immediately — defer scope, augment the team, or both. Without intervention, the final cost will exceed the budget by approximately 17 %."

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Plan-Driven vs Agile

| Criterion | Plan-Driven Wins | Agile Wins |
|---|---|---|
| **Requirement stability** | Fixed, well-understood | Evolving, emergent |
| **Customer engagement** | Limited, milestone reviews | Continuous, daily collaboration |
| **Team size** | Large (50+ members) | Small (5–9 per squad) |
| **Documentation** | Comprehensive mandatory | Just enough, working code over docs |
| **Delivery cadence** | Single end-delivery | Frequent iterations (2–4 weeks) |
| **Regulatory exposure** | High (defense, medical, aero) | Low (startup, internal tools) |
| **Change cost curve** | Steep (10×–100× per phase) | Shallow (refactor-friendly) |
| **Risk handling** | Front-loaded via planning | Distributed across iterations |
| **Quality assurance** | V-Model, formal reviews | Continuous integration, TDD |
| **Predictability of cost/schedule** | High | Moderate |

> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "which is better?", **never** give a one-line answer. State the criterion, list the trade-off, conclude with the contextual verdict.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — The Plan-Driven Development Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pdStart([Project Inception]) --> pdFeasibility[Feasibility Study]
    pdFeasibility --> pdReq[Requirements Engineering and SRS]
    pdReq --> pdDes1{Design Gate Review}
    pdDes1 -- Approved --> pdArch[Architectural Design]
    pdDes1 -- Rejected --> pdReq
    pdArch --> pdMod[Module Level Design]
    pdMod --> pdCode[Implementation and Coding]
    pdCode --> pdUnit[Unit Testing]
    pdUnit --> pdInt[Integration Testing]
    pdInt --> pdSys[System Testing]
    pdSys --> pdAcc[Acceptance Testing]
    pdAcc --> pdDep[Deployment]
    pdDep --> pdMaint[Operation and Maintenance]
    pdMaint --> pdEvolve[Evolution and Enhancement]
    pdEvolve --> pdEnd([System Retirement])

    pdRev1[Formal Reviews and Walkthroughs] -.-> pdReq
    pdRev1 -.-> pdArch
    pdRev1 -.-> pdMod
    pdRev2[Configuration Management] -.-> pdCode
    pdRev2 -.-> pdInt
    pdQA[Quality Assurance and V Model] -.-> pdUnit
    pdQA -.-> pdSys
    pdRisk[Risk Register Updates] -.-> pdFeasibility
    pdRisk -.-> pdDes1

    classDef gate fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef doc fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef test fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef phase fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,color:#000

    class pdDes1,pdRev1,pdRev2,pdQA gate
    class pdReq,pdArch,pdMod doc
    class pdUnit,pdInt,pdSys,pdAcc test
    class pdFeasibility,pdCode,pdDep,pdMaint,pdEvolve,pdRisk,pdStart,pdEnd phase
```

**How to read this:** Solid arrows are the **forward progression** of phases. Dashed arrows represent **cross-cutting activities** (reviews, configuration management, quality assurance, risk tracking) that run in parallel with the main flow. The yellow **Design Gate Review** is the canonical **go/no-go decision point** — the most expensive moment to reverse in the entire plan-driven lifecycle.

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence — Document Flow Across Phases

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pdCust[Customer] -->|Needs| pdBA[Business Analyst]
    pdBA -->|SRS| pdArch[System Architect]
    pdArch -->|SDD| pdLead[Tech Lead]
    pdLead -->|Module Spec| pdDev[Developers]
    pdDev -->|Source Code| pdCI[Build and CI Server]
    pdCI -->|Test Binaries| pdQA[QA Team]
    pdQA -->|Test Reports| pdMgr[Project Manager]
    pdMgr -->|Status Report| pdCust
    pdCust -->|Acceptance| pdMgr

    classDef human fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,color:#000
    class pdCust,pdBA,pdArch,pdLead,pdDev,pdQA,pdMgr human
    class pdCI fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — V-Model Mapping

> **Why a V-Model diagram?** Plan-driven testing is best understood as a **mirror** of design — every design artifact has a corresponding test artifact produced in parallel.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph pdVleft["V Model Left Leg - Development"]
        pdV1[User Requirements]
        pdV2[System Requirements - SRS]
        pdV3[Architectural Design - SDD]
        pdV4[Module Design - SDD Detailed]
        pdV5[Implementation - Source Code]
    end

    subgraph pdVright["V Model Right Leg - Verification"]
        pdV6[Acceptance Test Plan]
        pdV7[System Test Plan]
        pdV8[Integration Test Plan]
        pdV9[Unit Test Plan and Procedures]
        pdV10[Test Execution and Defect Removal]
    end

    pdV1 -.-> pdV6
    pdV2 -.-> pdV7
    pdV3 -.-> pdV8
    pdV4 -.-> pdV9
    pdV5 -.-> pdV10

    classDef dev fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef ver fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#333,color:#000
    class pdV1,pdV2,pdV3,pdV4,pdV5 dev
    class pdV6,pdV7,pdV8,pdV9,pdV10 ver
```

**Reading the V-Model:** The dotted connections are the **traceability links** required by plan-driven quality standards. For example, an Acceptance Test Case (top-right) must be traceable to a specific User Requirement (top-left). If a regulator asks "how do you know this requirement is verified?" the answer is a walk down the right leg using these links.

## 4.4 Effort Distribution Pie — Boehm's Empirical Curve

```mermaid
pie title Plan-Driven Effort Distribution (Boehm)
    pdP1 : "Analysis and Design"
    pdP2 : "Coding"
    pdP3 : "Unit and Integration Test"
    pdP4 : "System and Acceptance Test"
    pdP5 : "Project Management and Overhead"
```

| Slice | Allocation |
|---|---|
| Analysis and Design | 40% |
| Coding | 20% |
| Unit and Integration Test | 15% |
| System and Acceptance Test | 25% |
| Project Management and Overhead | (residual) |

> **Significance for KTU:** This 40-20-40 distribution is often the only answer examiners accept when asked "how is effort allocated in a plan-driven project?"
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Don't write "Waterfall Model = Plan-Driven."** The waterfall is *one instance* of plan-driven; V-Model, incremental, and staged delivery are also plan-driven variants.
> 2. **Confusing COCOMO modes** (Organic vs Semi-Detached vs Embedded) is the most common single-mark loss. Memorize: *Organic = small teams, simple apps; Embedded = tight hardware/software constraints, complex; Semi-Detached = in-between.*
> 3. **FP conversion backfiring factor** is often used **backwards** by students. FP per KLOC is for *language*; the formula is $\text{KLOC} = \text{FP} / (\text{FP per KLOC})$, not multiplied.
> 4. **PERT expected time** must show the formula $t_e = (o + 4m + p)/6$, not just an arithmetic mean. The "4" carries the weight of historical beta-distribution data.
> 5. **EVM signs:** Negative CV/SV = bad; CPI/SPI < 1 = bad. Students often invert the meaning.
> 6. **Boehm's 40-20-40 rule:** writing "50-50" or "30-30-40" loses the one mark reserved for the canonical ratio.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Plan-Driven Development. List any four of its key characteristics.**

**Model Answer (target: ~120 words):**

> **Plan-Driven Development** is a predictive software engineering paradigm in which all project activities — requirements, design, coding, testing, and maintenance — are planned in detail *up-front* and executed in a defined sequence. Significant emphasis is placed on comprehensive documentation artefacts (SRS, SDD, STD) and formal review gates.
>
> **Key characteristics:**
> 1. **Predictive** — full scope, cost, and schedule estimated at inception.
> 2. **Sequential / Phased** — phases execute in a fixed order with defined entry/exit criteria.
> 3. **Document-Centric** — heavy reliance on written, reviewable artefacts.
> 4. **Standards-Driven** — governed by IEEE, ISO, and CMMI process frameworks.
> 5. **Change-Resistant** — late changes require formal change-control board approval.
> 6. **Top-Down** — proceeds from system requirements to module-level code.
>
> *(Any four well-explained = 3 marks.)*

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Compare Plan-Driven and Agile development along any three dimensions.**

**Model Answer (target: ~120 words):**

| Dimension | Plan-Driven | Agile |
|---|---|---|
| **Requirements** | Fixed, fully specified up-front in the SRS. | Evolving, refined iteratively each sprint. |
| **Customer Involvement** | Periodic milestone reviews. | Continuous daily collaboration. |
| **Delivery** | Single end-of-project delivery. | Frequent small increments (2–4 weeks). |
| **Documentation** | Comprehensive, audit-grade. | Just-enough, working code first. |
| **Change Handling** | Expensive; needs CCB approval. | Welcomed; refactoring-friendly. |
| **Team Size** | Large, distributed, often outsourced. | Small (5–9), co-located. |
| **Best Suited For** | Safety-critical, regulated domains. | Dynamic, customer-facing products. |

*(Any three dimensions explained = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question 3A** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the seven phases of the classical plan-driven development model. For each phase, identify the **deliverable produced** and the **verification gate** used to approve it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A distributed database project is estimated at **8 KLOC**. It is an **embedded** system. Using **Basic COCOMO**, compute the **effort, development time, and average staff strength** for the project. Also compute the **productivity** in KLOC per person-month. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for 3A(a) — Seven Phases (7 marks)

| # | Phase | Deliverable | Verification Gate |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Feasibility Study** | Feasibility Report | Management Review |
| 2 | **Requirements Engineering** | Software Requirements Specification (SRS) | Requirements Review (IEEE 830) |
| 3 | **System & Software Design** | Software Design Description (SDD) | Design Review (Critical Design Review) |
| 4 | **Implementation & Coding** | Source code, build artefacts | Code Inspections, Static Analysis |
| 5 | **Integration & Testing** | Test Reports, Defect Logs | Test Readiness Review, Test Summary Report |
| 6 | **Deployment** | Installation Manual, Release Notes | Site Acceptance Test (SAT) |
| 7 | **Operation & Maintenance** | Patches, updated docs | Post-Release Reviews, Change Control Board |

Each row = ~1 mark. The verification gate column is what elevates a 5-mark answer to 7 marks.

#### Model Solution for 3A(b) — COCOMO Numericals (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify coefficients for Embedded mode**

From the COCOMO table for **Embedded** systems:

$$a = 3.6, \quad b = 1.20, \quad c = 2.5, \quad d = 0.32$$

**Step 2 — Compute effort $E$**

$$E = 3.6 \times (8)^{1.20}$$

$$(8)^{1.20} = e^{1.20 \cdot \ln(8)} = e^{1.20 \cdot 2.0794} = e^{2.4953} \approx 12.125$$

$$E = 3.6 \times 12.125 = 43.65 \text{ person-months}$$

**Step 3 — Compute development time $D$**

$$D = 2.5 \times (43.65)^{0.32}$$

$$(43.65)^{0.32} = e^{0.32 \cdot \ln(43.65)} = e^{0.32 \cdot 3.775} = e^{1.208} \approx 3.347$$

$$D = 2.5 \times 3.347 = 8.37 \text{ months}$$

**Step 4 — Compute average staff**

$$\text{Staff} = \frac{E}{D} = \frac{43.65}{8.37} \approx 5.21 \approx 6 \text{ persons}$$

**Step 5 — Compute productivity**

$$\text{PR} = \frac{\text{KLOC}}{E} = \frac{8}{43.65} \approx 0.183 \text{ KLOC per person-month}$$

**Final Answer Block (write this verbatim in the exam):**

> The embedded distributed database project requires approximately **43.65 person-months of effort**, completed in **8.37 months** with a team of **6 persons**. The productivity is **0.183 KLOC per person-month**, which is consistent with the low productivity typical of embedded systems due to hardware-software co-design complexity.

---

### **Question 3B** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**(a)** What is **Function Point Analysis**? Explain the **five function types** and the **fourteen general system characteristics** used in computing the Technical Complexity Factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The critical path of a plan-driven project consists of **five sequential activities** with the following estimates:
> | Activity | $t_o$ | $t_m$ | $t_p$ |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | A | 5 | 8 | 17 |
> | B | 4 | 7 | 16 |
> | C | 6 | 9 | 18 |
> | D | 3 | 6 | 15 |
> | E | 5 | 7 | 15 |
>
> Compute the **expected project duration**, the **project standard deviation**, and the **probability of completing the project within 35 days**. Use the standard normal table: $\Phi(0.0) = 0.5$, $\Phi(0.5) = 0.6915$, $\Phi(1.0) = 0.8413$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for 3B(a) — Function Point Analysis (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Function Point Analysis (FPA), introduced by Allan Albrecht at IBM in 1979, is a **language-independent** method of measuring software size from the **user's perspective** by quantifying the functionality the user requests and receives. Five function types are counted, weighted, and adjusted for technical and environmental complexity.

**Five Function Types (4 marks — list with weight and brief purpose):**

| Function Type | Acronym | What it Counts |
|---|---|---|
| **External Inputs** | EI | Data entering the system from outside (forms, APIs). |
| **External Outputs** | EO | Data leaving the system (reports, exports). |
| **External Inquiries** | EQ | Interactive queries requiring input and output. |
| **Internal Logical Files** | ILF | Data maintained *inside* the application boundary. |
| **External Interface Files** | EIF | Data referenced but maintained by *other* applications. |

Each is rated as **Simple, Average, or Complex** and assigned weights (3–6, 4–7, 3–6, 7–15, 5–10 respectively).

**Fourteen General System Characteristics (2 marks):**
1. Data communications
2. Distributed data processing
3. Performance
4. Heavily used configuration
5. Transaction rate
6. Online data entry
7. End-user efficiency
8. Online update
9. Complex processing
10. Reusability
11. Installation ease
12. Operational ease
13. Multiple sites
14. Facilitate change

Each is rated 0 (no influence) to 5 (strong influence), and the Technical Complexity Factor is:

$$\text{TCF} = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum_{i=1}^{14} F_{i}$$

#### Model Solution for 3B(b) — PERT Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute expected time for each activity** (1 mark per row of calculations, totalling ~3 marks)

$$t_{e,A} = \frac{5 + 4(8) + 17}{6} = \frac{54}{6} = 9.00$$

$$t_{e,B} = \frac{4 + 4(7) + 16}{6} = \frac{48}{6} = 8.00$$

$$t_{e,C} = \frac{6 + 4(9) + 18}{6} = \frac{60}{6} = 10.00$$

$$t_{e,D} = \frac{3 + 4(6) + 15}{6} = \frac{42}{6} = 7.00$$

$$t_{e,E} = \frac{5 + 4(7) + 15}{6} = \frac{48}{6} = 8.00$$

**Step 2 — Total expected duration** (1 mark)

$$T_e = 9 + 8 + 10 + 7 + 8 = 42 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3 — Variance per activity** (1 mark)

$$\sigma_{A}^{2} = \left(\frac{17-5}{6}\right)^{2} = 4.00$$

$$\sigma_{B}^{2} = \left(\frac{16-4}{6}\right)^{2} = 4.00$$

$$\sigma_{C}^{2} = \left(\frac{18-6}{6}\right)^{2} = 4.00$$

$$\sigma_{D}^{2} = \left(\frac{15-3}{6}\right)^{2} = 4.00$$

$$\sigma_{E}^{2} = \left(\frac{15-5}{6}\right)^{2} = 2.78$$

**Step 4 — Project variance and standard deviation** (1 mark)

$$\sigma_{\text{proj}}^{2} = 4.00 + 4.00 + 4.00 + 4.00 + 2.78 = 18.78$$

$$\sigma_{\text{proj}} = \sqrt{18.78} \approx 4.33 \text{ days}$$

**Step 5 — Probability of finishing within 35 days** (1 mark)

$$Z = \frac{35 - 42}{4.33} = \frac{-7}{4.33} \approx -1.62$$

Looking up $Z = -1.62$ in the standard normal table: $\Phi(-1.62) \approx 0.0526$.

> **Final Answer:** The expected project duration is **42 days**, with a standard deviation of **4.33 days**. The probability of finishing within 35 days is only about **5.26 %** — the project is highly likely to slip beyond 35 days. To achieve a 50 % confidence, the target must be 42 days; for 84 % confidence, 46.3 days.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Read it the night before the exam.**

- **Plan-Driven Development** = predictive, sequential, document-centric, top-down software engineering.
- **Core philosophy:** plan everything first; treat software as an engineered artefact.
- **Seven phases:** Feasibility → Requirements → Design → Implementation → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance.
- **Boehm's effort distribution:** 40 % analysis & design, 20 % coding, 40 % testing.
- **Maintenance distribution (Lientz & Swanson):** 40 % corrective, 25 % adaptive, 20 % perfective, 15 % preventive.
- **COCOMO Basic equation:** $E = a(\text{KLOC})^{b}$, $D = cE^{d}$.
- **COCOMO modes & coefficients:**
  - Organic: $a = 2.4$, $b = 1.05$, $c = 2.5$, $d = 0.38$.
  - Semi-Detached: $a = 3.0$, $b = 1.12$, $c = 2.5$, $d = 0.35$.
  - Embedded: $a = 3.6$, $b = 1.20$, $c = 2.5$, $d = 0.32$.
- **Function Point formula:** $\text{FP} = \text{UFC} \times \text{TCF} \times \text{ECF}$.
- **TCF = 0.65 + 0.01 × Σ(14 GSCs)**; **ECF = 1.4 − 0.03 × Σ(8 capability factors)**.
- **FP → KLOC conversion:** use the backfiring table; divide FP by FP-per-KLOC.
- **PERT expected time:** $t_e = (o + 4m + p)/6$; **variance:** $\sigma^2 = ((p - o)/6)^2$.
- **Project Z-score:** $Z = (T - T_e)/\sigma_{\text{proj}}$; probability = $\Phi(Z)$.
- **EVM essentials:** CV = EV − AC; SV = EV − PV; CPI = EV/AC; SPI = EV/PV; EAC = BAC/CPI.
- **Negative CV/SV and CPI/SPI < 1** = project is in trouble.
- **V-Model** = each design level on the left leg has a corresponding test level on the right leg.
- **Plan-driven vs agile:** requirements stability, customer engagement, documentation weight, change cost curve, regulatory context.
- **Best domains for plan-driven:** avionics, defense, medical devices, banking back-office, government ERP.
- **Plan-driven failure cases:** startup MVPs, fast-changing consumer apps, R&D prototypes.
- **Standards referenced:** IEEE 830 (SRS), IEEE 12207 (lifecycle processes), ISO 9001, CMMI Level 2–5.
- **Common pitfall:** writing "Waterfall = Plan-Driven" — incorrect, waterfall is a *subset*.
- **Common pitfall:** confusing PERT and CPM — CPM uses single deterministic estimates, PERT uses three-point.
- **Common pitfall:** using wrong backfiring direction when converting FP to KLOC.
- **Common pitfall:** forgetting to convert KLOC into thousands (e.g., writing 12 LOC instead of 12,000).
- **Final exam mantra:** *Plan → Specify → Design → Build → Test → Deploy → Maintain.* Memorize the order and the deliverables.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Project Scheduling # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Project Scheduling** is the software engineering activity that involves identifying all activities constituting a software project, determining their **interdependencies**, estimating the **effort** and **duration** of each activity, and arranging them in a **temporal sequence** such that the project can be completed within the stipulated time using the available resources (people, hardware, budget). In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned, Outcome-Based Education), Project Scheduling is positioned as a **Project Planning** sub-process that directly contributes to: - **CO4:** *Apply software project planning, estimation, scheduling, risk and quality management techniques to software development scenarios.* - **Bloom's Level:** Apply / Analyze (Level 3–4) > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Board Standard):** > *Project scheduling is the process of deciding the order in which activities are to be carried out, allocating start and finish dates, identifying the critical path, and ensuring that the workforce, effort, and calendar time are optimally balanced to meet the project deadline.* ## 1.2 Key Terminology (Mandatory Vocabulary for KTU Answers) | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Activity** | A discrete, schedulable unit of work within a project | | **Task** | A synonym for an activity (used in WBS) | | **Milestone** | A recognizable point marking the completion of a major deliverable | | **Predecessor** | An activity that must finish before another can start | | **Successor** | An activity that depends on the completion of another | | **Critical Path** | The longest-duration path through the network; determines project duration | | **Float / Slack** | The amount of time an activity can be delayed without delaying the project | | **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** | Hierarchical decomposition of the total work into smaller tasks | | **Gantt Chart** | A bar-chart representation of activities vs. calendar time | | **Network Diagram** | A graph (AoA or AoN) showing activities and their dependencies | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners > [!TIP] > **Think of Project Scheduling like planning a wedding.** 🏛️ > > Imagine you are orchestrating a wedding ceremony. You cannot just "start everything at once" — the *venue must be booked* before *invitations are printed*, *caterers* must be finalized *before the menu is finalized*, and the *reception hall* must be decorated *after the cake arrives*. Each small job (task) has a predecessor. Some jobs (the critical ones) — like the bride's dress and the groom's suit — have **zero float** (any delay postpones the entire event). Others — like printing wedding favors — have **slack** (you can delay them a bit without breaking the wedding day). > > - The **wedding day** = *project deadline* > - The **venue, dress, catering, photography** = *activities/tasks* > - The **dependency rules** (dress before ceremony) = *predecessor relationships* > - The **longest unavoidable chain of activities** = *critical path* > - The **wedding planner's chart on a wall** = *Gantt chart / network diagram* **In software engineering:** Booking a server must precede deploying code; design must precede coding; coding must precede testing. Project scheduling = building that "planner's chart" for software. ## 1.4 Why Project Scheduling is Non-Negotiable - **Resource Optimization:** Avoids over-staffing or under-staffing at any phase. - **Risk Identification:** The critical path highlights activities where any slippage is *fatal*. - **Progress Tracking:** Provides a baseline against which actual progress is measured. - **Stakeholder Communication:** A schedule is the most universally understood project artifact. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** > Scheduling is *not* the same as estimation. **Estimation** answers *"How long?"*; **Scheduling** answers *"When and in what order?"* Both are required. Confusing them in an exam is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Project Scheduling Workflow (5 Logical Steps) 1. **Decompose the Project** — Build the *Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)*. The total project is broken into deliverables, deliverables into tasks, tasks into sub-tasks, until each leaf is **estimable** (no more than 8–16 person-hours of work). 2. **Identify Dependencies** — For every pair of activities, classify the relationship: - *Finish-to-Start (FS):* B cannot start until A finishes. (Most common) - *Start-to-Start (SS):* B can start as soon as A starts. - *Finish-to-Finish (FF):* B cannot finish until A finishes. - *Start-to-Finish (SF):* Rare; B cannot finish until A starts. 3. **Estimate Effort & Duration** for each activity. The KTU syllabus links this to **COCOMO-based estimation** (covered in Module 3) and the **three-point PERT estimates**. 4. **Construct the Network Diagram** (AoA — Activity on Arrow, or AoN — Activity on Node). 5. **Compute the Schedule** — Forward pass, backward pass, float calculation, identification of the **critical path**, and finally rendering a **Gantt chart**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Question for KTU Answers:** > *"Why cannot we schedule without a network?"* → Because the network exposes *parallelism* and *bottlenecks*. Without it, you cannot compute the critical path or the project completion date reliably. ## 2.2 Two Network Modeling Styles (Frequently Tested in KTU) ### (a) Activity-on-Arrow (AoA) — "Arrow Diagram" - Each **activity is an arrow**. - **Nodes (circles)** are *events* — they represent the *start or finish* of activities. - Dummy activities (dashed arrows) are used to model dependencies that arrows alone cannot represent. ### (b) Activity-on-Node (AoN) — "Precedence Diagram" - Each **activity is a node** (a rectangle/box). - **Arrows** represent dependencies (predecessor → successor). - Easier to construct; no dummy activities needed. Most modern tools (MS Project, Primavera) use AoN. > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to draw a network, **use AoN** unless specifically asked otherwise. It is faster to draw and avoids dummy-activity errors. ## 2.3 The Critical Path Method (CPM) CPM assumes **deterministic** activity durations (single-point estimates). The procedure is: ### Forward Pass — Computing Earliest Times $$\text{EST}(A) = 0 \quad \text{for activities with no predecessor}$$ $$\text{EFT}(A) = \text{EST}(A) + \text{Duration}(A)$$ $$\text{EST}(B) = \max_{A \in \text{Pred}(B)} \text{EFT}(A)$$ ### Backward Pass — Computing Latest Times $$\text{LFT}(\text{End}) = \text{Project Duration (from forward pass)}$$ $$\text{LST}(A) = \text{LFT}(A) - \text{Duration}(A)$$ $$\text{LFT}(A) = \min_{B \in \text{Succ}(A)} \text{LST}(B)$$ ### Float / Slack $$\text{Total Float}(A) = \text{LST}(A) - \text{EST}(A) = \text{LFT}(A) - \text{EFT}(A)$$ $$\text{Free Float}(A) = \min_{B \in \text{Succ}(A)} \text{EST}(B) - \text{EFT}(A)$$ **Critical Path** = the chain of activities where **Total Float = 0**. Any delay on a critical activity delays the entire project. ## 2.4 The PERT Technique (Three-Point Estimates) PERT treats durations as **probabilistic** variables. For every activity, the project manager estimates three values: | Symbol | Meaning | Interpretation | |---|---|---| | $t_o$ | Optimistic time | "Everything goes perfectly" | | $t_m$ | Most likely time | "Normal execution" (mode of the beta distribution) | | $t_p$ | Pessimistic time | "Worst case barring disasters" | ### Expected Time (Weighted Mean) $$t_e = \frac{t_o + 4 \cdot t_m + t_p}{6}$$ ### Standard Deviation per Activity $$\sigma = \frac{t_p - t_o}{6}$$ ### Variance per Activity $$\sigma^2 = \left( \frac{t_p - t_o}{6} \right)^2$$ ### Project Duration & Variance $$T_E = \sum_{i \in \text{Critical Path}} t_{e,i}$$ $$\sigma_{\text{project}}^2 = \sum_{i \in \text{Critical Path}} \sigma_i^2$$ ### Probability of Completion by Date T $$Z = \frac{T - T_E}{\sigma_{\text{project}}}$$ > The Z-value is then looked up in the **Standard Normal Distribution Table** to obtain the probability. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this table. Every formula here is fair game for a 7 or 14-mark KTU question.** | # | Concept | Formula | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Earliest Finish Time | $\text{EFT}(A) = \text{EST}(A) + D(A)$ | Forward pass | | 2 | Earliest Start of Successor | $\text{EST}(B) = \max_{A} \text{EFT}(A)$ | Over all predecessors | | 3 | Latest Start Time | $\text{LST}(A) = \text{LFT}(A) - D(A)$ | Backward pass | | 4 | Latest Finish of Predecessor | $\text{LFT}(A) = \min_{B} \text{LST}(B)$ | Over all successors | | 5 | Total Float | $\text{TF}(A) = \text{LST}(A) - \text{EST}(A)$ | Critical if TF = 0 | | 6 | Free Float | $\text{FF}(A) = \min_{B} \text{EST}(B) - \text{EFT}(A)$ | Within successor's window | | 7 | PERT Expected Time | $t_e = (t_o + 4 t_m + t_p) / 6$ | Beta-distribution mean | | 8 | PERT Std. Deviation | $\sigma = (t_p - t_o) / 6$ | Activity-level | | 9 | PERT Variance | $\sigma^2 = ((t_p - t_o)/6)^2$ | Activity-level | | 10 | Project Variance | $\sigma_p^2 = \sum \sigma_i^2$ | Sum on critical path only | | 11 | Probability Z-Score | $Z = (T - T_E) / \sigma_p$ | Lookup in Z-table | | 12 | Effort–Duration | $E = D \times \bar{S}$ | $\bar{S}$ = average staffing | | 13 | Rayleigh Staffing | $\text{Staffing}(t) = k \cdot t \cdot e^{-t^2/(2\sigma_R^2)}$ | Bell-shaped curve | | 14 | Putnam Equation | $L = E^{1/3} \cdot t_d^{4/3} / C_k$ | Productivity-based schedule | ## 2.6 The Rayleigh Curve and Staffing Patterns A real software project rarely uses constant staff. The empirical **Rayleigh curve** shows a *build-up → peak → wind-down* pattern. The relationship between effort $E$ and duration $D$ is: $$E = \int_0^{D} S(t) \, dt \quad ; \quad S(t) = \frac{2E}{D^2} \cdot t \cdot e^{-(t/D)^2}$$ The **peak staffing** occurs roughly at $t = D / \sqrt{2}$ and is approximately: $$S_{\text{peak}} \approx 0.4 \cdot \frac{E}{D}$$ - If **duration is compressed** → staff must be increased (adds cost and communication overhead, may *reduce* productivity per person). - If **duration is extended** → staff can be reduced (saves cost, but lengthens time-to-market). > [!TIP] > **Common KTU question:** *"A project is estimated to take 36 person-months. If the deadline is compressed from 12 months to 9 months, what is the new average staffing?"* — Use $E = D \times \bar{S}$. ## 2.7 Why CPM and PERT Coexist in Industry | Aspect | CPM | PERT | |---|---|---| | Duration model | Deterministic (single value) | Probabilistic (3 estimates) | | Best for | Construction, hardware | R&D, novel software | | Outcome | Earliest/Latest times, critical path | Probability of on-time completion | | Cost | Can include time-cost trade-off | Does not consider cost | | Origin | DuPont, 1957 (construction) | US Navy, 1958 (Polaris missile) | For software, **PERT is more realistic** because requirements are volatile. KTU often asks for a **single integrated answer** where PERT three-point estimates feed into a CPM-style network. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — CPM with PERT Three-Point Estimates > [!IMPORTANT] > **This single example can fetch 14 marks if solved step-by-step with all tables. Practice it thoroughly.** A software project consists of **8 activities: A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H**. The PERT three-point estimates (in days) and the precedence relationships are: | Activity | Predecessors | $t_o$ | $t_m$ | $t_p$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | A | — | 3 | 5 | 7 | | B | — | 2 | 3 | 10 | | C | A | 2 | 3 | 4 | | D | A | 4 | 5 | 12 | | E | B, C | 5 | 7 | 15 | | F | D, E | 3 | 4 | 5 | | G | D | 1 | 2 | 3 | | H | F, G | 4 | 5 | 6 | ### STEP 1 — Compute Expected Time $t_e$ for Each Activity Using $t_e = (t_o + 4 t_m + t_p)/6$: $$t_e(A) = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 7}{6} = \frac{3 + 20 + 7}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ days}$$ $$t_e(B) = \frac{2 + 4(3) + 10}{6} = \frac{2 + 12 + 10}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days}$$ $$t_e(C) = \frac{2 + 4(3) + 4}{6} = \frac{2 + 12 + 4}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3 \text{ days}$$ $$t_e(D) = \frac{4 + 4(5) + 12}{6} = \frac{4 + 20 + 12}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6 \text{ days}$$ $$t_e(E) = \frac{5 + 4(7) + 15}{6} = \frac{5 + 28 + 15}{6} = \frac{48}{6} = 8 \text{ days}$$ $$t_e(F) = \frac{3 + 4(4) + 5}{6} = \frac{3 + 16 + 5}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days}$$ $$t_e(G) = \frac{1 + 4(2) + 3}{6} = \frac{1 + 8 + 3}{6} = \frac{12}{6} = 2 \text{ days}$$ $$t_e(H) = \frac{4 + 4(5) + 6}{6} = \frac{4 + 20 + 6}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ days}$$ ### STEP 2 — Compute Standard Deviation $\sigma$ and Variance $\sigma^2$ for Each Activity Using $\sigma = (t_p - t_o)/6$ and $\sigma^2 = \sigma^2$: | Activity | $t_p - t_o$ | $\sigma$ | $\sigma^2$ | |---|---|---|---| | A | $7 - 3 = 4$ | $4/6 = 0.667$ | $0.444$ | | B | $10 - 2 = 8$ | $8/6 = 1.333$ | $1.778$ | | C | $4 - 2 = 2$ | $2/6 = 0.333$ | $0.111$ | | D | $12 - 4 = 8$ | $8/6 = 1.333$ | $1.778$ | | E | $15 - 5 = 10$ | $10/6 = 1.667$ | $2.778$ | | F | $5 - 3 = 2$ | $2/6 = 0.333$ | $0.111$ | | G | $3 - 1 = 2$ | $2/6 = 0.333$ | $0.111$ | | H | $6 - 4 = 2$ | $2/6 = 0.333$ | $0.111$ | ### STEP 3 — Forward Pass (Compute EST and EFT) $$\text{EST}(A) = 0, \quad \text{EFT}(A) = 0 + 5 = 5$$ $$\text{EST}(B) = 0, \quad \text{EFT}(B) = 0 + 4 = 4$$ $$\text{EST}(C) = \text{EFT}(A) = 5, \quad \text{EFT}(C) = 5 + 3 = 8$$ $$\text{EST}(D) = \text{EFT}(A) = 5, \quad \text{EFT}(D) = 5 + 6 = 11$$ $$\text{EST}(E) = \max(\text{EFT}(B), \text{EFT}(C)) = \max(4, 8) = 8, \quad \text{EFT}(E) = 8 + 8 = 16$$ $$\text{EST}(F) = \max(\text{EFT}(D), \text{EFT}(E)) = \max(11, 16) = 16, \quad \text{EFT}(F) = 16 + 4 = 20$$ $$\text{EST}(G) = \text{EFT}(D) = 11, \quad \text{EFT}(G) = 11 + 2 = 13$$ $$\text{EST}(H) = \max(\text{EFT}(F), \text{EFT}(G)) = \max(20, 13) = 20, \quad \text{EFT}(H) = 20 + 5 = 25$$ $$\boxed{T_E = \text{Project Duration} = \text{EFT}(H) = 25 \text{ days}}$$ ### STEP 4 — Backward Pass (Compute LST and LFT) $$\text{LFT}(H) = 25, \quad \text{LST}(H) = 25 - 5 = 20$$ $$\text{LFT}(G) = \text{LST}(H) = 20, \quad \text{LST}(G) = 20 - 2 = 18$$ $$\text{LFT}(F) = \text{LST}(H) = 20, \quad \text{LST}(F) = 20 - 4 = 16$$ $$\text{LFT}(E) = \text{LST}(F) = 16, \quad \text{LST}(E) = 16 - 8 = 8$$ $$\text{LFT}(D) = \min(\text{LST}(F), \text{LST}(G)) = \min(16, 18) = 16, \quad \text{LST}(D) = 16 - 6 = 10$$ $$\text{LFT}(C) = \text{LST}(E) = 8, \quad \text{LST}(C) = 8 - 3 = 5$$ $$\text{LFT}(B) = \text{LST}(E) = 8, \quad \text{LST}(B) = 8 -
