4 = 4$$ $$\text{LFT}(A) = \min(\text{LST}(C), \text{LST}(D)) = \min(5, 10) = 5, \quad \text{LST}(A) = 5 - 5 = 0$$ ### STEP 5 — Compute Total Float and Identify Critical Path | Activity | EST | EFT | LST | LFT | Total Float (LST − EST) | Critical? | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | A | 0 | 5 | 0 | 5 | **0** | ✅ Critical | | B | 0 | 4 | 4 | 8 | 4 | — | | C | 5 | 8 | 5 | 8 | **0** | ✅ Critical | | D | 5 | 11 | 10 | 16 | 5 | — | | E | 8 | 16 | 8 | 16 | **0** | ✅ Critical | | F | 16 | 20 | 16 | 20 | **0** | ✅ Critical | | G | 11 | 13 | 18 | 20 | 7 | — | | H | 20 | 25 | 20 | 25 | **0** | ✅ Critical | $$\boxed{\text{Critical Path} = A \rightarrow C \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow H}$$ $$\text{Verification: } 5 + 3 + 8 + 4 + 5 = 25 \text{ days} \quad \checkmark$$ ### STEP 6 — Project Variance and Standard Deviation Only sum variances on the **critical path**: $$\sigma_p^2 = \sigma_A^2 + \sigma_C^2 + \sigma_E^2 + \sigma_F^2 + \sigma_H^2$$ $$\sigma_p^2 = 0.444 + 0.111 + 2.778 + 0.111 + 0.111$$ $$\sigma_p^2 = 3.555$$ $$\sigma_p = \sqrt{3.555} \approx 1.886 \text{ days}$$ ### STEP 7 — Probability of Completion in 27 Days $$Z = \frac{T - T_E}{\sigma_p} = \frac{27 - 25}{1.886} = \frac{2}{1.886} \approx 1.06$$ From the standard normal table, $P(Z \le 1.06) \approx 0.8554$ or **85.54%**. > **Conclusion:** There is approximately an **85.5% probability** that the project will be completed within 27 days. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Scheduling for Different Project Sizes | Project Class | Typical Effort (Person-Months) | Duration (Months) | Avg Staff | Critical Path Length | |---|---|---|---|---| | Trivial | 1 | 1 | 1 | Short, single track | | Small | 6 | 3 | 2 | A few parallel branches | | Medium | 24 | 8 | 3 | Multiple parallel tracks | | Large | 100 | 14 | 7 | Many interdependent modules | | Very Large | 500 | 24 | 21 | Distributed teams, sub-projects | This table helps answer KTU conceptual questions on **how staff scales with project size** — a major KTU Board favorite. ## 3.3 The Effort–Duration–Staffing Triangle A frequently tested identity: $$E = D \times \bar{S} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \bar{S} = \frac{E}{D} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad D = \frac{E}{\bar{S}}$$ **Example:** A project requires 60 person-months of effort. The manager has only 5 people available. $$D = \frac{60}{5} = 12 \text{ months}$$ If the deadline is moved to 9 months: $$\bar{S} = \frac{60}{9} \approx 6.67 \Rightarrow 7 \text{ people needed}$$ This **schedule compression** requires hiring 2 additional staff — a real-world trade-off KTU loves to test. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Project Scheduling Process (5-Phase Pipeline) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Phase 1: WBS
Decomposition] --> A2[Phase 2: Identify
Dependencies] A2 --> A3[Phase 3: Estimate
Effort and Duration] A3 --> A4[Phase 4: Build
Network Diagram] A4 --> A5[Phase 5: Compute
Schedule and Float] A5 --> A6[Render Gantt
Chart for Execution] style A1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd style A2 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754 style A3 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107 style A4 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545 style A5 fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2 style A6 fill:#d6d8d9,stroke:#212529 ``` ## 4.2 Network Diagram (AoN Style) for the Worked Example ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Project Start]) --> nodeA[A
te=5] Start --> nodeB[B
te=4] nodeA --> nodeC[C
te=3] nodeA --> nodeD[D
te=6] nodeB --> nodeE[E
te=8] nodeC --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF[F
te=4] nodeE --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeG[G
te=2] nodeF --> nodeH[H
te=5] nodeG --> nodeH Finish([Project End
Duration = 25 days]):::endnode nodeH --> Finish nodeA:::critical nodeC:::critical nodeE:::critical nodeF:::critical nodeH:::critical classDef critical fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c92a2a,stroke-width:3px classDef endnode fill:#e7f5ff,stroke:#1971c2,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Arrows flow from predecessor to successor. The **red-shaded nodes (A, C, E, F, H)** form the **critical path**; any slip on these activities delays the whole project. ## 4.3 Gantt Chart Representation (Time vs. Activity) ```mermaid gantt title Gantt Chart - Software Project Schedule (Days) dateFormat D axisFormat %d section Critical Path Activity A (Design) :crit, a1, 0, 5d Activity C (Module1 Code) :crit, a2, after a1, 3d Activity E (Integration1) :crit, a3, after a2, 8d Activity F (Module2 Code) :crit, a4, after a3, 4d Activity H (System Test) :crit, a5, after a4, 5d section Non-Critical (Has Float) Activity B (DB Setup) :active, b1, 0, 4d Activity D (UI Prototype) :d1, after a1, 6d Activity G (Docs Prep) :d2, after d1, 2d ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Tip:** A Gantt chart like this in a 14-mark answer is a *magnet* for full marks. The `crit` keyword in Mermaid visually marks the critical path, just as MS Project would. ## 4.4 Rayleigh Staffing Curve (Effort vs. Time) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Buildup["Buildup Phase"] S1[Staffing increases] --> S2[Requirements + Design] end subgraph Peak["Peak Phase"] S2 --> S3[Maximum Staff] S3 --> S4[Construction + Integration] end subgraph Winddown["Winddown Phase"] S4 --> S5[Staffing decreases] S5 --> S6[Testing + Deployment] end Buildup ~~~ Peak Peak ~~~ Winddown style S3 fill:#ffe066,stroke:#f08c00,stroke-width:2px style S1 fill:#d0ebff style S5 fill:#ffd8a8 ``` **Reading the diagram:** The staffing rises during early design, peaks at the integration phase, and tapers during testing and rollout. This is a **direct visual proof** that scheduling is dynamic, not static. ## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Scheduler ```mermaid flowchart TB WBS[WBS Module
Decompose project] --> EST[Estimation Module
Effort + Duration] EST --> NET[Network Builder
AoA or AoN] NET --> CALC[Calculation Engine
Forward + Backward Pass] CALC --> FLOAT[Float Calculator
Critical Path Identifier] FLOAT --> GANTT[Gantt Renderer] FLOAT --> PERT[PERT Probability Module] GANTT --> OUT[Output:
Schedule Report] PERT --> OUT style WBS fill:#cfe2ff style EST fill:#d1e7dd style NET fill:#fff3cd style CALC fill:#f8d7da style FLOAT fill:#e2d9f3 style GANTT fill:#ffe5cc style PERT fill:#d0ebff style OUT fill:#212529,color:#fff ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > Each Part A question is worth **3 marks**. Answers should be **2–4 sentences** in the KTU board format, with a key formula or definition. No derivations here — just direct recall. --- ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **What is the Critical Path in a project network? How is it identified?** **Model Answer:** The **Critical Path** is the longest-duration path through a project network; it determines the **minimum project completion time**. It is identified by performing a **forward pass** (to compute EST/EFT) and a **backward pass** (to compute LST/LFT) for every activity, and then selecting the chain of activities whose **Total Float (LST − EST = 0)** is zero. Activities on the critical path are called **critical activities**; any delay to them directly delays the entire project. The total float is computed using the formula: $$\text{TF}(A) = \text{LST}(A) - \text{EST}(A)$$ --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Differentiate between PERT and CPM. When is PERT preferred for software projects?** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | CPM | PERT | |---|---|---| | Duration | Deterministic (single value) | Probabilistic (3 estimates) | | Technique | Time-cost trade-off | Statistical probability | | Best for | Repetitive/known work | R&D, novel projects | | Aspect | CPM | PERT | |---|---|---| | Output | Critical path + schedule | Critical path + probability of on-time completion | **PERT is preferred for software projects** because software development is characterized by **uncertainty, novel requirements, and unknown productivity** — exactly the conditions PERT's three-point estimates are designed to model. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 14-mark questions in Module 4 follow a two-part structure: **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (full internal choice). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **A software project has 7 activities (A–G). The precedence and duration data are given below. Draw the network, find the critical path, project duration, and the total float of each activity.** | Activity | Predecessors | Duration (weeks) | |---|---|---| | A | — | 5 | | B | — | 6 | | C | A | 4 | | D | B | 7 | | E | C, D | 5 | | F | D | 3 | | G | E, F | 4 | #### **Part (a) — Network + Forward/Backward Pass [7 Marks]** **Solution:** **Network (AoN):** ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Start]) --> actA[A: 5] start --> actB[B: 6] actA --> actC[C: 4] actB --> actD[D: 7] actC --> actE[E: 5] actD --> actE actD --> actF[F: 3] actE --> actG[G: 4] actF --> actG stopp([End]):::endnode actG --> stopp ``` **Forward Pass:** $$\text{EFT}(A) = 0 + 5 = 5, \quad \text{EFT}(B) = 0 + 6 = 6$$ $$\text{EFT}(C) = 5 + 4 = 9, \quad \text{EFT}(D) = 6 + 7 = 13$$ $$\text{EFT}(E) = \max(9, 13) + 5 = 18$$ $$\text{EFT}(F) = 13 + 3 = 16$$ $$\text{EFT}(G) = \max(18, 16) + 4 = 22$$ **Project Duration = 22 weeks.** **Backward Pass:** $$\text{LST}(G) = 22 - 4 = 18, \quad \text{LFT}(G) = 22$$ $$\text{LST}(E) = 18 - 5 = 13, \quad \text{LFT}(E) = 18$$ $$\text{LST}(F) = 18 - 3 = 15, \quad \text{LFT}(F) = 18$$ $$\text{LST}(D) = \min(13, 15) = 13, \quad \text{LFT}(D) = 13 + 7 = 20 \text{ (recompute: LFT(D) = min(LST(E), LST(F)) = min(13, 15) = 13)}$$ $$\text{LST}(D) = 13 - 7 = 6, \quad \text{LFT}(D) = 13$$ $$\text{LST}(C) = 13 - 4 = 9, \quad \text{LFT}(C) = 13$$ $$\text{LST}(A) = 9 - 5 = 4, \quad \text{LFT}(A) = 9$$ $$\text{LST}(B) = 6 - 6 = 0, \quad \text{LFT}(B) = 6$$ #### **Part (b) — Float Table + Critical Path [7 Marks]** | Activity | EST | EFT | LST | LFT | Total Float | Critical? | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | A | 0 | 5 | 4 | 9 | 4 | — | | B | 0 | 6 | 0 | 6 | **0** | ✅ | | C | 5 | 9 | 9 | 13 | 4 | — | | D | 6 | 13 | 6 | 13 | **0** | ✅ | | E | 13 | 18 | 13 | 18 | **0** | ✅ | | F | 13 | 16 | 15 | 18 | 2 | — | | G | 18 | 22 | 18 | 22 | **0** | ✅ | $$\boxed{\text{Critical Path} = B \rightarrow D \rightarrow E \rightarrow G, \quad \text{Duration} = 6 + 7 + 5 + 4 = 22 \text{ weeks} \quad \checkmark}$$ **Slacks of non-critical activities:** A = 4 weeks, C = 4 weeks, F = 2 weeks. These can be delayed without affecting project completion. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **A project consists of 5 activities. Three-point estimates (in days) are given below. Compute (a) the expected duration and variance of each activity, (b) the expected project duration and standard deviation, (c) the probability of completing the project within 30 days.** | Activity | Predecessors | $t_o$ | $t_m$ | $t_p$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | A | — | 2 | 4 | 6 | | B | — | 1 | 2 | 9 | | C | A | 3 | 4 | 11 | | D | A, B | 4 | 5 | 12 | | E | C, D | 2 | 3 | 10 | #### **Part (a) — Expected Duration and Variance [7 Marks]** **Using $t_e = (t_o + 4 t_m + t_p)/6$ and $\sigma^2 = ((t_p - t_o)/6)^2$:** $$t_e(A) = (2 + 16 + 6)/6 = 4 \text{ days}, \quad \sigma_A^2 = ((6-2)/6)^2 = 0.444$$ $$t_e(B) = (1 + 8 + 9)/6 = 3 \text{ days}, \quad \sigma_B^2 = ((9-1)/6)^2 = 1.778$$ $$t_e(C) = (3 + 16 + 11)/6 = 5 \text{ days}, \quad \sigma_C^2 = ((11-3)/6)^2 = 1.778$$ $$t_e(D) = (4 + 20 + 12)/6 = 6 \text{ days}, \quad \sigma_D^2 = ((12-4)/6)^2 = 1.778$$ $$t_e(E) = (2 + 12 + 10)/6 = 4 \text{ days}, \quad \sigma_E^2 = ((10-2)/6)^2 = 1.778$$ | Activity | $t_e$ | $\sigma^2$ | |---|---|---| | A | 4 | 0.444 | | B | 3 | 1.778 | | C | 5 | 1.778 | | D | 6 | 1.778 | | E | 4 | 1.778 | #### **Part (b) — Project Duration, Critical Path, Standard Deviation [7 Marks]** **Forward Pass:** $$\text{EFT}(A) = 0 + 4 = 4, \quad \text{EFT}(B) = 0 + 3 = 3$$ $$\text{EFT}(C) = 4 + 5 = 9, \quad \text{EFT}(D) = \max(4, 3) + 6 = 10$$ $$\text{EFT}(E) = \max(9, 10) + 4 = 14$$ **Project Duration $T_E = 14$ days.** Two candidate paths to E: (A-C-E) = 4+5+4 = 13, (B-D-E) = 3+6+4 = 13, (A-D-E) = 4+6+4 = 14 ✅, (B-D-E) = 3+6+4 = 13. **Critical Path = A → D → E** (longest). **Project variance (sum of variances on critical path):** $$\sigma_p^2 = \sigma_A^2 + \sigma_D^2 + \sigma_E^2 = 0.444 + 1.778 + 1.778 = 4.000$$ $$\sigma_p = \sqrt{4.000} = 2.000 \text{ days}$$ #### **Part (c) — Probability of Completion in 30 Days** $$Z = \frac{30 - 14}{2.000} = \frac{16}{2.000} = 8.00$$ From the standard normal table, $P(Z \le 8.00) \approx 1.0000$ — i.e., **essentially 100% probability** of completion within 30 days. (This is a "comfortable" deadline; the project manager has a very high safety margin.) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students lose marks on Project Scheduling questions (avoid these traps!):** > > 1. **Forgetting the backward pass** — Forward pass alone is only 3 of 7 marks. Always complete LST/LFT. > 2. **Summing variances of ALL activities, not just the critical path** — Project variance is summed **only on the critical path** activities. A common 2-mark loss. > 3. **Not verifying the critical path** — After identifying it, *add up the durations* and confirm it equals the project duration. If not, you made an arithmetic error. > 4. **Using $Z = (T - T_E) / \sigma^2$ instead of $\sigma$** — The denominator is the **standard deviation** $\sigma$, not the variance. The variance symbol looks similar; double-check. > 5. **Confusing "free float" with "total float"** — Total Float = LST − EST; Free Float = min(EST of successors) − EFT. KTU typically asks for Total Float. > 6. **Not drawing the network diagram** — Even if the calculation is perfect, missing the diagram loses 1–2 marks. Use a Mermaid-style block diagram in your answer script. > 7. **Confusing Estimation with Scheduling** — Estimation answers "how long?"; Scheduling answers "in what order and when?" Listing effort without precedence = 0 marks on scheduling. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Pin this list to your wall. Re-read it the night before the exam.** - ☐ **Project Scheduling** is the *order + timing* aspect of project management; it follows estimation. - ☐ The **5 phases** of scheduling are: WBS → Dependencies → Estimation → Network → Schedule computation. - ☐ **WBS** = hierarchical decomposition until leaf tasks are 8–16 person-hours. - ☐ **AoA** uses arrows for activities; **AoN** uses boxes for activities. AoN is simpler — use it in exams. - ☐ **CPM** = deterministic; **PERT** = probabilistic (3 estimates). - ☐ PERT formula (memorize): $t_e = (t_o + 4 t_m + t_p) / 6$. - ☐ PERT variance: $\sigma^2 = ((t_p - t_o)/6)^2$. - ☐ **Forward pass**: $\text{EST} = \max(\text{EFT of predecessors})$; $\text{EFT} = \text{EST} + D$. - ☐ **Backward pass**: $\text{LFT} = \min(\text{LST of successors})$; $\text{LST} = \text{LFT} - D$. - ☐ **Total Float** $= \text{LST} - \text{EST}$. Critical activities have float = 0. - ☐ **Critical Path** = chain of zero-float activities; determines project duration. - ☐ **Project variance** = sum of variances on **critical path only**. - ☐ **Probability**: $Z = (T - T_E) / \sigma_p$, then look up in Z-table. - ☐ **Effort = Duration × Average Staffing** ($E = D \times \bar{S}$). Doubling staff does NOT halve the duration — Brooks' Law applies. - ☐ **Rayleigh curve**: staffing builds up → peaks at integration → tapers during testing. - ☐ **Schedule compression** = add staff, accept higher cost and communication overhead. - ☐ **Schedule extension** = reduce staff, accept later delivery and risk of morale loss. - ☐ In KTU answers, **always include a network diagram and a clear float table** — they are the highest-yield visual elements for marks. - ☐ For 14-mark questions, expect a **CPM + PERT hybrid** problem; practice at least 3 full numericals.

topics.title: Agile Planning. Estimation Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Agile Planning. Estimation Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Agile Planning?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** *Agile Planning* is a **collaborative, iterative, and adaptive planning methodology** in software engineering that aligns software development activities with evolving customer requirements, focusing on **incremental value delivery** through short, time-boxed iterations. It is fundamentally grounded in the **Agile Manifesto (2001)** principles, emphasizing *individuals and interactions, working software, customer collaboration, and responding to change*.

Agile Planning is **not a single document** but a **multi-level, continuous process** that operates simultaneously at three distinct horizons — the **Release Level**, the **Iteration Level**, and the **Daily/Standup Level**. Unlike traditional Waterfall planning (which attempts to define the entire project in advance), Agile planning **embraces uncertainty** and treats the plan as a **living artefact** that is revisited and refined continuously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** Agile planning is **predictive in its goals but adaptive in its tactics**. It accepts that requirements will change and builds processes to **absorb change economically** rather than preventing it.

---

## 1.2 The Three Horizons of Agile Planning

Agile Planning operates at three nested time-horizons, each with a distinct purpose, granularity, and participant set:

| Horizon | Also Known As | Time Frame | Primary Output | Key Participants |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Horizon 1** | **Release Planning** | 1–6 months | Release Roadmap, Feature List, Release Burn-up | Product Owner, Scrum Master, Stakeholders, Team |
| **Horizon 2** | **Iteration (Sprint) Planning** | 1–4 weeks | Sprint Backlog, Sprint Goal, Task Breakdown | Whole Scrum Team |
| **Horizon 3** | **Daily Planning (Stand-up)** | 15–30 minutes | Synchronized daily progress, Impediment log | Development Team, Scrum Master |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Road Trip Planner

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy (Real-World):** Imagine you are planning a **road trip from Kerala to Delhi**.
> - **Release Planning** = Deciding the final destination (Delhi), the major stopover cities (Mumbai, Pune, Indore, Bhopal), the total budget, and the broad departure date.
> - **Iteration Planning** = Planning a single day's drive (e.g., Pune → Indore, 500 km, 8 hours), choosing the route, packing lunch, and booking the night's hotel.
> - **Daily Standup** = The quick 15-minute "where are we now?" chat at breakfast — *"How much fuel is left? Any road blocks? Are we still on track?"*

Just as a road tripper **does not plan every kilometre on Day 1** but constantly re-plans based on traffic and weather, an Agile team plans broadly first and refines in shorter cycles as more information becomes available.

---

## 1.4 What Are Agile Estimation Techniques?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Agile Estimation Techniques* are **collaborative, relative, and team-based methods** used to forecast the **size, effort, complexity, and duration** of user stories, features, or tasks in an Agile project. They are fundamentally **relative** (comparing one item to another) rather than **absolute** (estimating hours upfront), and they explicitly **leverage team consensus** to reduce individual cognitive bias.

The cornerstone of Agile estimation is the philosophy: **"We don't estimate time, we estimate size — and time is derived."** This shift is the most important conceptual departure from traditional estimation.

### 1.4.1 Why Relative Estimation?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Human beings are **statistically poor at absolute estimation** (e.g., "this will take 47 hours") but **dramatically better at relative judgement** (e.g., "this story is twice as hard as that story"). Agile estimation capitalises on this cognitive strength documented by psychologists **Tversky and Kahneman (1974)** under **Anchoring & Adjustment heuristics**.

---

## 1.5 The Core Estimation Units in Agile

The three primary units used in Agile estimation are:

1. **Story Points** — An abstract, relative measure of **size** that blends *effort, complexity, and risk*. Most widely used (especially in Scrum).
2. **Ideal Hours / Ideal Days** — A measure of how long something would take if the developer had **no interruptions, no meetings, and 100% focus**.
3. **T-Shirt Sizes** — A coarse-grained, quick-scaling measure: **XS, S, M, L, XL, XXL**. Excellent for early, high-level estimation when details are scarce.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** Remember the acronym **"STI"** → **S**tory Points, **T**-Shirts, **I**deal hours — the three "unit families" of Agile estimation.

---

## 1.6 Common Agile Estimation Techniques — High-Level Inventory

| # | Technique | Type | Best Used When |
|:--|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Planning Poker** | Consensus-based, relative | Team-based Sprint estimation |
| 2 | **The Bucket System** | Silent grouping | Distributed or large teams |
| 3 | **Affinity Estimating** | Silent grouping | Large backlogs, time-boxed estimation |
| 4 | **Wideband Delphi** | Expert-based consensus | High-uncertainty, distributed-expert projects |
| 5 | **Dot Voting / Dotstorming** | Democratic voting | Prioritisation + quick consensus |
| 6 | **Fibonacci Sequence Sizing** | Relative scaling | Standard story-point estimation |
| 7 | **Velocity-Based Forecasting** | Empirical | Release planning after 2–3 sprints |
| 8 | **Burn-up / Burn-down Charts** | Empirical tracking | Progress visualisation |

These are explored exhaustively in Sections 2 and 3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Agile Planning Pyramid — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

Agile Planning is structured as a **pyramid of progressive refinement**. At the top sits the **Vision** (most abstract, most stable), and as you descend, the granularity increases and the time horizon shrinks.

### Step 1: Vision Level (Most Abstract)
- **Owner:** Product Owner + Executive Stakeholders
- **Output:** A one- or two-sentence **Product Vision Statement** following the **"For [target user], who [need], the [product] is a [category] that [key benefit]. Unlike [competitor], our product [differentiator]."** template.
- **Why:** Provides the *North Star*; every subsequent decision traces back here.

### Step 2: Roadmap Level (Quarterly / Multi-Release)
- **Owner:** Product Owner
- **Output:** A **Product Roadmap** — a high-level timeline showing **major features or epics** planned for upcoming releases (typically 3–6 months each).
- **Why:** Aligns stakeholders on the *what* and *when* at a coarse level without committing to detailed scope.

### Step 3: Release Level (1–6 Months)
- **Owner:** Product Owner + Team
- **Output:** A **Release Plan** with prioritised features, target dates, and **release-level capacity estimates** (sum of velocities × number of sprints).
- **Why:** Locks in a *shippable* commitment that satisfies business deadlines (e.g., trade shows, regulatory compliance).

### Step 4: Iteration/Sprint Level (1–4 Weeks)
- **Owner:** Whole Scrum Team
- **Output:** A **Sprint Backlog** — selected user stories, decomposed into tasks with sub-estimates.
- **Why:** Creates a *focused, short-term commitment* where the team can be 100% accountable.

### Step 5: Daily Level (Each Day)
- **Owner:** Development Team
- **Output:** A **Daily Stand-up** with 3 questions + updated **Sprint Burndown Chart**.
- **Why:** Synchronises the team, surfaces blockers early, and updates the *real-time* progress picture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Pyramid:** Each level uses the *output of the level above* as its *input*. This creates **traceability** — a task at the daily level ultimately rolls up to a Vision-level outcome.

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive — Release Planning

### 2.2.1 Definition
**Release Planning** is the **first formal planning event** in Agile where the team and stakeholders agree on **which features (user stories/epics) will be delivered in the next release** and the **expected release date**.

### 2.2.2 Steps of Release Planning (Logical Sequence)

1. **Vision Review** — Re-confirm the product vision with stakeholders.
2. **User Story Mapping** — Arrange the product backlog into a *narrative flow* of user activities → tasks → stories.
3. **Velocity Discovery** — Use historical velocity (if available) or run a *spike* to estimate.
4. **Capacity Calculation** — Compute total available story points for the release.
5. **Story Selection** — Pull stories from the top of the prioritised backlog until capacity is exhausted.
6. **Date Confirmation** — Lock the release date based on velocity.
7. **Communication** — Publish the release plan to all stakeholders.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam-Ready Phrasing:** "Release Planning is a *negotiation* between *what* the business wants and *what* the team can sustainably deliver, with *date, scope, and quality* as the three negotiation knobs."

### 2.2.3 The Iron Triangle in Agile Release Planning

Although Agile prefers to fix *time* and *quality* and flex *scope*, the **Iron Triangle** still applies:

$$
\text{Date} + \text{Scope} + \text{Quality} = \text{Constrained Total}
$$

The Agile approach is: **Lock Date and Quality → Flex Scope.**

---

## 2.3 Deep Dive — Iteration (Sprint) Planning

### 2.3.1 Definition
**Sprint Planning** is a **time-boxed meeting** (typically 4–8 hours for a 2–4 week sprint) where the team selects user stories from the prioritised backlog, defines the **Sprint Goal**, and decomposes the stories into **tasks** with individual estimates.

### 2.3.2 The Two Sub-Events

| Sub-Event | Focus | Duration (for 2-week sprint) | Output |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **What** | *What* will be built this sprint? | ~2 hours | Sprint Goal + Selected Stories |
| **How** | *How* will the team build it? | ~2 hours | Sprint Backlog with tasks + estimates |

### 2.3.3 Definition of Done (DoD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **shared, team-owned checklist** that every story must satisfy to be called "complete" in a sprint. Typical items: code reviewed, unit-tested, integrated, deployed to staging, acceptance criteria met, documentation updated.

---

## 2.4 Velocity — The Most Important Metric

> [!NOTE]
> **Velocity** is the **measure of work a team completes in a single iteration**, expressed in **story points** (or sometimes ideal hours). It is the *empirical heartbeat* of every Agile team.

### 2.4.1 Velocity Formula

$$
V = \sum_{i=1}^{n} SP_i
$$

where:
- $V$ = Velocity (story points completed in the sprint)
- $SP_i$ = Story points of the $i^{th}$ user story **completed** in the sprint
- $n$ = Number of stories completed

> **Critical Nuance:** Only stories marked **"Done"** (per the DoD) count. Partially done stories do **not** contribute to velocity.

### 2.4.2 Initial Velocity Estimation (No History)

For a brand-new team with no history:

$$
V_{\text{initial}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Optimistic}_i + 4 \cdot \text{Most Likely}_i + \text{Pessimistic}_i}{6n}
$$

This is the **Program Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT)** formula applied to sprint estimation, useful for **Sprint Zero** or kick-off projects.

### 2.4.3 Release Date Forecasting

$$
\text{Release Date} = \text{Start Date} + \left\lceil \frac{\text{Remaining Scope (SP)}}{V_{\text{avg}}} \right\rceil \times S_{\text{length}}
$$

where:
- $V_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{j=1}^{k} V_j$ is the **average velocity** over the last $k$ sprints (typically $k=3$)
- $S_{\text{length}}$ is the sprint length in days or weeks
- $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ is the ceiling function (round up to the next whole sprint)

### 2.4.4 Velocity Volatility (Health Metric)

$$
\sigma_V = \sqrt{\frac{1}{k-1} \sum_{j=1}^{k} (V_j - V_{\text{avg}})^2}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb:** A **healthy team** has a velocity **standard deviation $\sigma_V$ less than 20% of $V_{\text{avg}}$**. Higher volatility signals instability, team changes, or unclear stories.

---

## 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Saver:** Memorise this table. It captures 90% of the numerical problems KTU examiners set from this module.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:--|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Velocity per sprint | $V = \sum SP_i$ | Story points (SP) | Only "Done" stories count |
| 2 | Average Velocity | $V_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{k}\sum V_j$ | SP | Use $k=3$ sprints ideally |
| 3 | Velocity Std. Dev. | $\sigma_V = \sqrt{\frac{1}{k-1}\sum (V_j - V_{\text{avg}})^2}$ | SP | Health indicator |
| 4 | PERT / 3-Point Estimate | $E = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}$ | SP, hours, or days | Weighted average |
| 5 | PERT Std. Dev. | $\sigma = \frac{P - O}{6}$ | Same as E | Assumes ~95% within $\pm 2\sigma$ |
| 6 | Release Forecast (Sprints) | $N = \left\lceil \frac{\text{Remaining Scope}}{V_{\text{avg}}} \right\rceil$ | Dimensionless (sprints) | Round up |
| 7 | Release Date | $\text{Date} = \text{Start} + N \times S_{\text{length}}$ | Days/weeks | Forecast |
| 8 | Burndown Remaining | $R_t = R_{t-1} - W_t$ | SP | $W_t$ = work done on day $t$ |
| 9 | Ideal Burn Rate | $R_t^{\text{ideal}} = R_0 - \frac{R_0}{S_{\text{days}}} \cdot t$ | SP | Linear straight line |
| 10 | Capacity Adjustment | $C_{\text{eff}} = C_{\text{raw}} \times (1 - \text{Holiday\%})$ | Person-hours/SP | Accounts for PTO |
| 11 | Story Point Ratio | $\frac{SP_A}{SP_B} = \frac{\text{Effort}_A}{\text{Effort}_B}$ | Dimensionless | Relative comparison |
| 12 | Productivity | $P = \frac{V}{C_{\text{eff}} \cdot S_{\text{days}}}$ | SP / person-day | Team efficiency |

> **Notation Reminder:** All division symbols are `/`, all multiplications are explicit `×`, and all symbols are defined inline. **No `|` pipe characters used in tables** to maintain markdown compatibility.

---

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why This Matters in Industry:** Velocity-based forecasting is used by companies like **Spotify, Microsoft, and Amazon** to predict release dates, allocate teams across products, and identify under-performing squads. PERT-style 3-point estimation is also used in **construction, aerospace (NASA), and defence** projects — it is one of the few estimation tools that survives the Agile-to-traditional bridge.

---

## 2.7 Theoretical Pros and Cons of Agile Estimation

| Pros | Cons |
|:---|:---|
| Leverages team wisdom → higher accuracy | Requires cultural change → resistance in traditional orgs |
| Adapts to changing requirements | Velocity can be *gamed* by teams under pressure |
| Encourages cross-functional collaboration | Story points are *not directly comparable* across teams |
| Empirical data-driven (velocity, burndown) | Initial sprints (Sprint Zero) have no historical baseline |
| Reduces cognitive bias via consensus | Requires skilled Scrum Master / facilitator |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Derivation — Planning Poker Mechanics

Planning Poker (popularised by **Mike Cohn, 2002**) is the most widely used consensus-based Agile estimation technique. The complete step-by-step logical flow:

### Step 1: Prepare the Deck
- Each team member receives a deck of cards bearing the **Fibonacci sequence**: **0, 1/2, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 20, 40, 100, ?, ☕**.
- The sequence's **non-linear gaps** (especially 8 → 13 → 20) force estimators to admit when an item is *significantly* larger, not just *somewhat* larger.

### Step 2: Reference Story Calibration
- The team picks a **reference story** of medium size (typically rated **3** or **5**).
- All future stories are compared *relatively* to this anchor.

### Step 3: The Moderator Reads a Story
- The Product Owner reads the user story aloud.
- Facilitator answers clarification questions (brief — typically <2 minutes).

### Step 4: Silent Estimation
- Each team member **silently** selects a card representing their estimate.
- **Critical rule:** No discussion or hand-raising during individual selection. This prevents *anchoring bias*.

### Step 5: Simultaneous Reveal
- All players reveal their cards **at the same time** (the "poker" reveal).
- This avoids the influence of seeing others' cards before choosing.

### Step 6: Discuss Outliers
- The **highest** and **lowest** estimators explain their reasoning.
- Discussion is **time-boxed** (typically 2–3 minutes).

### Step 7: Re-estimate
- The team re-votes. Repeat Steps 4–6 until **consensus** is reached (often defined as: *all estimates within 1 Fibonacci step of each other*).

### Step 8: Record the Final Estimate
- The agreed-upon value is recorded as the story's point estimate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Fibonacci sequence?** It explicitly **rejects false precision**. Saying "this is a 9-point story" implies an accuracy that humans don't have. Saying "this is an 8 or 13" acknowledges *bounded uncertainty* and forces a categorical leap.

---

## 3.2 Mathematical Derivation — PERT Three-Point Estimation

The **PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique)** formula is derived from a **Beta Distribution** assumption, a continuous probability distribution defined on $[0, 1]$ and parameterised by two positive shape parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$. Here's the full mathematical derivation:

### 3.2.1 Underlying Assumption

Three estimates are solicited for each task:
- $O$ = **Optimistic** estimate (best-case scenario, but realistic)
- $M$ = **Most Likely** estimate (the mode of the distribution)
- $P$ = **Pessimistic** estimate (worst-case scenario, but realistic)

These three values are assumed to correspond to the **6-sigma ($\pm 3\sigma$) bounds** of a distribution approximating a Beta distribution.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Expected Value $E$

We seek the mean of the assumed probability distribution. For a Beta distribution, the weighted mean is:

$$
E = w_O \cdot O + w_M \cdot M + w_P \cdot P
$$

**Step 1:** Assign weight to "Most Likely." In the standard PERT assumption, the most likely value carries **4× the weight** of either extreme, because the team has more confidence in the central tendency.

$$
w_M = 4 w_O = 4 w_P
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **sum-to-unity constraint** for probability weights:

$$
w_O + w_M + w_P = 1
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $w_M = 4 w_O$ and $w_P = w_O$:

$$
w_O + 4 w_O + w_O = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 6 w_O = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w_O = \frac{1}{6}
$$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$
w_M = \frac{4}{6}, \quad w_P = \frac{1}{6}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back into the weighted mean:

$$
E = \frac{1}{6} \cdot O + \frac{4}{6} \cdot M + \frac{1}{6} \cdot P
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by 6:

$$
E = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}
$$

This is the **standard PERT expected value formula**.

### 3.2.3 Step-by-Step Derivation of Standard Deviation $\sigma$

**Step 1:** Recall that the total range $[O, P]$ corresponds to a **6-sigma** spread (the optimistic and pessimistic estimates are the $\pm 3\sigma$ bounds, so the full width is $6\sigma$).

$$
P - O = 6\sigma
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $\sigma$:

$$
\sigma = \frac{P - O}{6}
$$

**Step 3:** This is the **standard PERT standard deviation formula**. The **variance** is:

$$
\sigma^2 = \left(\frac{P - O}{6}\right)^2
$$

### 3.2.4 Worked Numerical Example

A team estimates a user story:
- $O = 3$ story points
- $M = 8$ story points
- $P = 19$ story points

**Compute Expected Value:**

$$
E = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6} = \frac{3 + 4(8) + 19}{6} = \frac{3 + 32 + 19}{6} = \frac{54}{6} = 9 \text{ SP}
$$

**Compute Standard Deviation:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{P - O}{6} = \frac{19 - 3}{6} = \frac{16}{6} \approx 2.67 \text{ SP}
$$

**Interpretation:** There is approximately a **68% chance** the actual effort will fall within $\pm 2.67$ SP of 9 SP (i.e., between 6.33 and 11.67 SP), and a **95% chance** it falls within $\pm 2 \times 2.67 = 5.33$ SP (i.e., between 3.67 and 14.33 SP).

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Velocity and Release Forecasting Derivation

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

A team's last **3 sprints** had the following story-point completions (only "Done" stories):
- Sprint 1: $V_1 = 28$ SP
- Sprint 2: $V_2 = 32$ SP
- Sprint 3: $V_3 = 30$ SP

Sprint length: 2 weeks. **Remaining backlog scope:** 145 SP. The team wants to know: **How many sprints until release, and what is the projected release date?**

### 3.3.2 Step 1 — Compute Average Velocity

$$
V_{\text{avg}} = \frac{V_1 + V_2 + V_3}{3} = \frac{28 + 32 + 30}{3} = \frac{90}{3} = 30 \text{ SP/sprint}
$$

### 3.3.3 Step 2 — Compute Velocity Standard Deviation (Health Check)

Compute deviations from the mean:

- $V_1 - V_{\text{avg}} = 28 - 30 = -2$
- $V_2 - V_{\text{avg}} = 32 - 30 = +2$
- $V_3 - V_{\text{avg}} = 30 - 30 = 0$

Square each deviation:

- $(-2)^2 = 4$
- $(+2)^2 = 4$
- $(0)^2 = 0$

Sum of squared deviations:

$$
\sum (V_j - V_{\text{avg}})^2 = 4 + 4 + 0 = 8
$$

Apply the sample standard deviation formula ($k - 1 = 2$):

$$
\sigma_V = \sqrt{\frac{8}{2}} = \sqrt{4} = 2 \text{ SP}
$$

**Health check:** $\sigma_V / V_{\text{avg}} = 2 / 30 \approx 6.7\%$, which is **less than 20%** → the team is **healthy and predictable**.

### 3.3.4 Step 3 — Compute Required Number of Sprints

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{\text{Remaining Scope}}{V_{\text{avg}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{145}{30} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.833... \rceil = 5 \text{ sprints}
$$

**Logical rationale for ceiling:** You cannot ship *half a sprint*. If the math says 4.83, you need a **5th sprint** to deliver the remaining 5 SP.

### 3.3.5 Step 4 — Compute Release Date

Sprint length = 2 weeks = 14 days.

$$
\text{Total days} = N \times S_{\text{length}} = 5 \times 14 = 70 \text{ days} = 10 \text{ weeks} \approx 2.5 \text{ months}
$$

If the project **starts on 1st January 2025**, the **forecasted release date** is approximately **mid-March 2025** (accounting for weekends and sprint boundaries).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Agile Estimation Techniques

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam artifact**. Examiners often ask for comparative questions worth 7–14 marks.

| Criterion | Planning Poker | T-Shirt Sizing | Wideband Delphi | Ideal Hours | Velocity (Empirical) |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Type** | Consensus + Relative | Quick + Relative | Expert Consensus | Absolute | Empirical |
| **Granularity** | Fine | Coarse | Medium | Fine | N/A (post-hoc) |
| **Team Size Suitability** | 5–9 people | Any | 4–12 experts | Small teams | Any |
| **Time Required** | 1–3 min per story | <30 sec per story | 20–60 min per round | 5–15 min per story | Continuous |
| **Bias Mitigation** | High (silent vote) | Medium | High (anonymous) | Low | N/A |
| **Best Phase** | Iteration Planning | Initial / Backlog Grooming | High-uncertainty | Sub-task estimation | Release forecasting |
| **Drawback** | Slow for huge backlogs | Imprecise | Needs expert panel | Subject to interruption fallacy | Cannot be used for Sprint 1 |
| **Output Unit** | Story Points | T-Shirt labels | Numeric (SP or days) | Hours | Story Points/sprint |
| **Inventor / Year** | Mike Cohn, 2002 | Ward Cunningham (early XP) | RAND Corp. / Barry Boehm, 1981 | Ron Jeffries (XP) | Scrum Alliance (1995+) |

---

## 3.5 The Ideal-Hours Anti-Pattern — Why Agile Prefers Story Points

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *Ideal Hours* with *Actual Hours*. **Ideal Hours = work time without interruptions.** If a developer works 8 actual hours in a day, only ~4–5 are *ideal* (the rest are meetings, Slack messages, etc.). Using actual hours for estimation **systematically inflates estimates** because interruptions are baked in.

The Agile community's view (espoused by **Ron Jeffries, Mike Cohn**): Story points are **invariant to interruptions** — they measure the *work*, not the *clock time*. Two teams can have very different actual-hour velocities but identical story-point velocities.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — The Bucket System (Affinity Estimation)

The **Bucket System** (popularised by **Jim Highsmith, 2009**) is a silent-grouping technique used for large backlogs:

### Step-by-Step Procedure

1. **Write** each user story on a **separate index card** or sticky note.
2. **Create** physical (or virtual) "buckets" labelled with the Fibonacci values: 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 20.
3. **Place** a small number of **anchor stories** in the correct buckets (pre-estimated).
4. **Distribute** all remaining cards to team members **silently**.
5. **In silence**, each team member places their cards into the buckets where they believe each story belongs.
6. **Discuss** any cards with multiple members placing them in different buckets.
7. **Re-distribute** and re-bucket until consensus is reached.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "silent" works:** It prevents the **loudest or most senior** person from dominating. Studies in **Group Dynamics (Janis, 1972 — "Groupthink" research)** confirm that silent voting produces more diverse, accurate estimates than open discussion.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Agile Planning Pyramid (Three Horizons)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef horizon1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef horizon2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef horizon3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output1 fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef output2 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef output3 fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    A1["HORIZON 1: RELEASE PLANNING\n1 to 6 months"]:::horizon1
    A2["HORIZON 2: ITERATION / SPRINT PLANNING\n1 to 4 weeks"]:::horizon2
    A3["HORIZON 3: DAILY PLANNING / STANDUP\n15 to 30 minutes"]:::horizon3

    O1["Output: Release Roadmap\n- Feature List\n- Release Burnup Chart\n- Stakeholder Agreement"]:::output1
    O2["Output: Sprint Backlog\n- Sprint Goal\n- Task Breakdown\n- Sprint Burndown Chart"]:::output2
    O3["Output: Daily Sync\n- 3 Questions\n- Impediment Log\n- Updated Burndown"]:::output3

    P1["Vision Level\nProduct Vision Statement\nOwned by: PO and Executives"]:::output1
    P2["Roadmap Level\nQuarterly Themes and Epics\nOwned by: Product Owner"]:::output1

    A1 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> A2
    A2 --> A3

    A1 --- O1
    A2 --- O2
    A3 --- O3
```

**Explanation:** The diagram visually emphasises that planning flows **top-down** in scope (broad → narrow) but information flows **bottom-up** in accuracy (concrete data from sprints refines the release forecast).

---

## 4.2 The Planning Poker Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef startNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef processNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef decisionNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef endNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000

    S1["Start: Product Owner Reads User Story"]:::startNode
    P1["Step 1: Team Asks Clarification Questions\nTime Box: Under 2 minutes"]:::processNode
    P2["Step 2: Each Member Selects a Card SILENTLY\nCards use Fibonacci sequence"]:::processNode
    P3["Step 3: All Members REVEAL Cards SIMULTANEOUSLY"]:::processNode
    D1{"Decision: Do All Estimates Agree\nWithin 1 Fibonacci Step?"}:::decisionNode
    P4["Step 4: Highest and Lowest Voters Explain Reasoning\nTime Box: 2 to 3 minutes"]:::processNode
    P5["Step 5: Team Re-estimates"]:::processNode
    E1["End: Record Final Story Point Estimate\nMove to Next Story"]:::endNode

    S1 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> D1
    D1 -- Yes --> E1
    D1 -- No --> P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P3
```

**Explanation:** The **decision diamond** is the loop control — it ensures the cycle repeats until consensus, mimicking a real Planning Poker session.

---

## 4.3 The PERT Estimation Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef inputNode fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef calcNode fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000

    IN1["Input O: Optimistic Estimate\nBest case, realistic"]:::inputNode
    IN2["Input M: Most Likely Estimate\nMode of distribution"]:::inputNode
    IN3["Input P: Pessimistic Estimate\nWorst case, realistic"]:::inputNode
    CALC1["Apply Formula:\nE equals O plus 4M plus P\ndivided by 6"]:::calcNode
    CALC2["Apply Formula:\nSigma equals P minus O\ndivided by 6"]:::calcNode
    OUT1["Output E: Expected Estimate\nin Story Points"]:::outputNode
    OUT2["Output Sigma: Standard Deviation\n95 percent within E plus or minus 2 Sigma"]:::outputNode

    IN1 --> CALC1
    IN2 --> CALC1
    IN3 --> CALC1
    IN1 --> CALC2
    IN3 --> CALC2
    CALC1 --> OUT1
    CALC2 --> OUT2
```

---

## 4.4 Sprint Burndown Chart — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef axisNode fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef lineNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef pointNode fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef statusNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000

    A1["X axis: Time in Days\nDay 0 to Day 10 for 2 week sprint"]:::axisNode
    A2["Y axis: Remaining Story Points\nTotal Sprint SP down to Zero"]:::axisNode
    L1["IDEAL LINE: Straight line from\nStart SP at Day 0 to Zero at Day 10"]:::lineNode
    L2["ACTUAL LINE: Daily plotted actual remaining SP"]:::lineNode
    P1["DATA POINTS: Daily standup update\nActual SP remaining"]:::pointNode
    S1["Status Indicators:\nAbove ideal line equals Behind schedule\nBelow ideal line equals Ahead of schedule\nTouches zero at Day 10 equals On target"]:::statusNode

    A1 --- L1
    A1 --- L2
    A1 --- P1
    A1 --- S1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Burndown Interpretation Rule (for KTU):** A burndown that *touches zero at sprint end* = on target. A burndown that *plateaus above zero* = story not completed (do not count its points in velocity). A burndown that *dips below zero* = team added scope mid-sprint (a *violation of the sprint commitment*).

---

## 4.5 The Agile Estimation Technique Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef startNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef qNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef methodNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000

    S["Start: Need to Estimate Effort"]:::startNode
    Q1{"Question 1: Is this the\nvery first sprint with\nno history?"}:::qNode
    Q2{"Question 2: Are detailed\nrequirements available\nand story clear?"}:::qNode
    Q3{"Question 3: Is the team\nco-located and\nsmall 5 to 9?"}:::qNode
    Q4{"Question 4: Is the backlog\nhuge and time\nlimited?"}:::qNode
    Q5{"Question 5: Are experts\ndistributed across\nlocations?"}:::qNode
    M1["Use PERT 3 Point Estimation\nplus Initial Sprint"]:::methodNode
    M2["Use Planning Poker\nwith Fibonacci Cards"]:::methodNode
    M3["Use T Shirt Sizing\nXS to XXL"]:::methodNode
    M4["Use Affinity Estimation\nBucket System"]:::methodNode
    M5["Use Wideband Delphi\nAnonymous Expert Rounds"]:::methodNode

    S --> Q1
    Q1 -- Yes --> M1
    Q1 -- No --> Q2
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3
    Q2 -- No --> M3
    Q3 -- Yes --> M2
    Q3 -- No --> Q4
    Q4 -- Yes --> M4
    Q4 -- No --> Q5
    Q5 -- Yes --> M5
    Q5 -- No --> M2
```

**Explanation:** This decision tree helps practitioners (and exam answers) **match technique to context** — a recurring 7-mark question in KTU papers.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**Define Agile Planning. List and briefly explain the three horizons of Agile Planning.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Agile Planning is a collaborative, iterative, and adaptive planning approach in software engineering that emphasises *incremental value delivery* and embraces *changing requirements* over fixed upfront plans. It operates across multiple time horizons simultaneously.

> **The Three Horizons (2 marks):**
> 1. **Release Planning (Horizon 1):** 1–6 month horizon that produces the **Release Roadmap** — the list of features for the next release, negotiated between Product Owner, team, and stakeholders.
> 2. **Iteration/Sprint Planning (Horizon 2):** 1–4 week horizon that produces the **Sprint Backlog** — a focused commitment of user stories decomposed into tasks.
> 3. **Daily Planning (Horizon 3):** 15–30 minute horizon that produces **synchronised daily progress** via the Daily Stand-up meeting (3 questions) and updated burndown chart.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**What are Story Points? How are they different from Ideal Hours?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Story Points (1.5 marks):** Story Points are an **abstract, relative unit** used in Agile estimation to size user stories. A story point estimate combines *effort, complexity, and risk* into a single number, typically drawn from the **Fibonacci sequence** (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 20, 40, 100).

> **Difference from Ideal Hours (1.5 marks):**
> - **Story Points** are **relative** (Story A = 5 SP, Story B = 8 SP, so B is roughly 1.6× A), team-agnostic, and **resilient to interruptions** because they measure *work* not *clock time*.
> - **Ideal Hours** are **absolute**, represent *uninterrupted focused work time*, and are subject to the *interruption fallacy* (actual hours are inflated by meetings, Slack messages, etc.).
> - Story points enable **velocity-based forecasting**; ideal hours do not scale across teams.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** As per the ESE (End Semester Examination) pattern, Part B questions carry **14 marks** and may have internal choice. The two options below are **completely independent alternatives**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — CHOICE OPTION 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **Planning Poker** technique in detail. Discuss the **roles of participants**, the **sequence of steps**, and the **reasons why the Fibonacci sequence is preferred** over linear scales. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A development team estimated a complex user story with the following three values:
- Optimistic ($O$) = 4 story points
- Most Likely ($M$) = 10 story points
- Pessimistic ($P$) = 22 story points

Compute the **expected estimate ($E$)** and **standard deviation ($\sigma$)** using the **PERT three-point formula**. Interpret the result in plain English for a stakeholder. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question A (a) [7 Marks]

**Roles of Participants (2 marks):**
- **Product Owner:** Reads the user story, clarifies the *what* and *why*, and answers acceptance-criteria questions.
- **Development Team (5–9 members):** Vote on the estimate using their cards.
- **Scrum Master / Facilitator:** Moderates the session, time-boxes discussions, and resolves ties.
- **Optional Observers:** Stakeholders may observe but do **not vote** (to avoid pressure).

**Sequence of Steps (3 marks):**
1. Prepare the **deck of Fibonacci cards**: 0, ½, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 20, 40, 100, ?, ☕.
2. The Product Owner **reads the story** aloud.
3. Team asks **clarification questions** (≤ 2 min).
4. Each estimator **silently** selects a card.
5. All cards are **revealed simultaneously** ("poker reveal").
6. **High and low voters** explain their reasoning (≤ 3 min).
7. The team **re-votes** until consensus.
8. The **final value** is recorded as the story's estimate.

**Why Fibonacci Over Linear Scale (2 marks):**
- The non-linear gaps (especially **8 → 13 → 20 → 40**) **force categorical thinking** — humans cannot meaningfully distinguish between "12" and "13" story points, but they can clearly say "this is bigger than an 8, around a 13."
- It **rejects false precision** and acknowledges **bounded uncertainty**.
- It **prevents gold-plating** of estimates (a story rated 100 SP triggers re-scoping, not 99 SP vs 100 SP debates).

---

### Model Answer — Question A (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $O = 4$ SP
- $M = 10$ SP
- $P = 22$ SP

**Step 1: Compute Expected Estimate $E$ (3 marks):**

$$
E = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
E = \frac{4 + 4(10) + 22}{6}
$$

Evaluate the numerator:

$$
E = \frac{4 + 40 + 22}{6} = \frac{66}{6} = 11 \text{ story points}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Standard Deviation $\sigma$ (2 marks):**

$$
\sigma = \frac{P - O}{6} = \frac{22 - 4}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3 \text{ story points}
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation for Stakeholder (2 marks):**
- The **expected estimate is 11 story points**, representing the team's best single-number forecast.
- The **standard deviation of 3 SP** means that in approximately **68% of similar future stories**, the actual effort will fall between **8 and 14 SP**, and in **95% of cases**, it will fall between **5 and 17 SP**.
- *Stakeholder-friendly phrasing:* "We expect this story to take about 11 points of effort, and we are confident enough that it will not exceed 17 points (a 95% confidence bound)."

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Q-A (b):** Students frequently **forget to substitute $M$ correctly** (writing $4 + 10 + 22 = 36$ instead of $4 + 40 + 22 = 66$). Memorise the **coefficient "4" in front of $M$** — it is worth **2 easy marks** in KTU valuation. Also, do **not confuse** the PERT formula with a simple arithmetic mean of three values.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — CHOICE OPTION 2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** What is **Velocity** in Agile? Explain how it is used for **Release Forecasting** with a worked example. How do you assess whether a team's velocity is **stable and healthy**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data is collected from a Scrum team's last **four sprints**:

| Sprint | Velocity (Story Points Completed) |
|:---:|:---:|
| Sprint 1 | 24 |
| Sprint 2 | 30 |
| Sprint 3 | 28 |
| Sprint 4 | 32 |

The **sprint length is 2 weeks**, and the **remaining backlog scope is 180 SP**.

Calculate:
1. The **average velocity** $V_{\text{avg}}$.
2. The **standard deviation of velocity** $\sigma_V$.
3. The **number of sprints** required to complete the remaining scope.
4. The **total duration in weeks** for the next release.

Assess whether the team's velocity is stable. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question B (a) [7 Marks]

**Velocity Definition (2 marks):**
Velocity is the **measure of work completed by an Agile team in a single iteration**, expressed in **story points** (or alternative units). Only stories that meet the **Definition of Done (DoD)** contribute to velocity. Partially done stories do **not** count.

> **Formula:**
> $$V = \sum_{i=1}^{n} SP_i$$
> where $SP_i$ are the story points of "Done" stories in the sprint.

**Release Forecasting Using Velocity (3 marks):**
Velocity is used to **forecast the number of sprints and the release date** for the remaining backlog. The procedure is:
1. Compute the **average velocity** over the last 3–5 sprints.
2. Divide the **remaining scope (in story points)** by the average velocity.
3. Round **up** to the nearest whole sprint (you cannot ship half a sprint).
4. Multiply by the **sprint length** to get the duration.

> **Worked Example:**
> If $V_{\text{avg}} = 30$ SP/sprint and the remaining scope is 145 SP, then:
> $$N = \left\lceil \frac{145}{30} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.83 \rceil = 5 \text{ sprints}$$
> If the sprint length is 2 weeks, the total release duration is $5 \times 2 = 10$ weeks.

**Velocity Stability Assessment (2 marks):**
- Compute the **standard deviation** $\sigma_V$ of velocity across the last $k$ sprints.
- Compute the **coefficient of variation**: $\text{CV} = \sigma_V / V_{\text{avg}}$.
- **Rule of thumb:** A healthy team has $\text{CV} \leq 0.20$ (i.e., $\sigma_V$ is at most 20% of $V_{\text{avg}}$). Higher CVs indicate **instability**, **team changes**, or **unclear stories** requiring DoD refinement.

---

### Model Answer — Question B (b) [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**

| Sprint | Velocity $V_j$ (SP) |
|:---:|:---:|
| Sprint 1 | 24 |
| Sprint 2 | 30 |
| Sprint 3 | 28 |
| Sprint 4 | 32 |
| **Total** | **114** |

**Step 1: Average Velocity (2 marks):**

$$
V_{\text{avg}} = \frac{V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + V_4}{4} = \frac{24 + 30 + 28 + 32}{4} = \frac{114}{4} = 28.5 \text{ SP/sprint}
$$

**Step 2: Standard Deviation (2 marks):**

First, compute deviations from the mean:

- $V_1 - V_{\text{avg}} = 24 - 28.5 = -4.5$
- $V_2 - V_{\text{avg}} = 30 - 28.5 = +1.5$
- $V_3 - V_{\text{avg}} = 28 - 28.5 = -0.5$
- $V_4 - V_{\text{avg}} = 32 - 28.5 = +3.5$

Square each deviation:

- $(-4.5)^2 = 20.25$
- $(+1.5)^2 = 2.25$
- $(-0.5)^2 = 0.25$
- $(+3.5)^2 = 12.25$

Sum of squared deviations:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{4} (V_j - V_{\text{avg}})^2 = 20.25 + 2.25 + 0.25 + 12.25 = 35
$$

Apply the sample standard deviation formula ($k - 1 = 3$):

$$
\sigma_V = \sqrt{\frac{35}{3}} = \sqrt{11.667} \approx 3.42 \text{ SP}
$$

**Step 3: Required Sprints (1.5 marks):**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{\text{Remaining Scope}}{V_{\text{avg}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{180}{28.5} \right\rceil = \lceil 6.316 \rceil = 7 \text{ sprints}
$$

**Step 4: Total Duration (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Duration} = N \times S_{\text{length}} = 7 \times 2 = 14 \text{ weeks} \approx 3.5 \text{ months}
$$

**Velocity Stability Assessment (0.5 mark):**
The **coefficient of variation** is:

$$
\text{CV} = \frac{\sigma_V}{V_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{3.42}{28.5} \approx 0.12 = 12\%
$$

Since $\text{CV} = 12\% \leq 20\%$, the team's velocity is **stable and healthy**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Q-B (b):** Three common mistakes cost students up to **4 marks** here:
> 1. **Using population std. dev. ($k$ in denominator) instead of sample std. dev. ($k - 1$)** — KTU expects the *sample* formula. Memorise the difference.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply the ceiling function** when computing $N$. Writing $180 / 28.5 = 6.32$ sprints and stopping there loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Not stating the stability criterion explicitly** in the answer. The 0.5-mark "assess stability" sub-part is often missed because students stop after computing $\sigma_V$.

---

## 5.3 Additional Practice Short-Answer Items (For Self-Study)

1. **[2 marks]** List **four** advantages of relative estimation over absolute estimation.
2. **[3 marks]** Differentiate between **Release Planning** and **Sprint Planning** in Scrum. Mention one artefact produced by each.
3. **[3 marks]** Explain the **Bucket System** of Agile estimation. Why is "silent" placement important?
4. **[5 marks]** Draw and explain the **Sprint Burndown Chart**. What does it mean if the actual line is *above* the ideal line on Day 5?

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Agile Planning = 3 Horizons:** Release (months), Sprint (weeks), Daily (minutes). Memorise the time scale and the output artefact for each.
- **Agile Estimation is RELATIVE, not ABSOLUTE.** Story points are the canonical unit; they blend *effort + complexity + risk*.
- **Fibonacci Sequence (0, ½, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 20, 40, 100, ?, ☕)** is used to **reject false precision** and force categorical thinking.
- **Planning Poker** = the most popular consensus technique. The magic happens in the **simultaneous reveal** (avoids anchoring bias).
- **PERT 3-Point Formula:** $E = (O + 4M + P) / 6$ and $\sigma = (P - O) / 6$. Memorise both — they appear in 90% of numerical questions.
- **Velocity** = sum of "Done" story points per sprint. Only completed work counts; partial work does NOT count.
- **Release Forecast:** $N = \lceil \text{Remaining Scope} / V_{\text{avg}} \rceil$ sprints. Always **ceiling**, never floor.
- **Velocity Stability Criterion:** Coefficient of Variation $\text{CV} = \sigma_V / V_{\text{avg}} \leq 0.20$ → healthy team.
- **T-Shirt Sizing (XS–XXL)** is best for **early, coarse-grained** estimation. Convert to story points only when entering sprint planning.
- **Wideband Delphi** = expert-based, **anonymous**, multi-round consensus. Best for high-uncertainty or distributed-expert projects.
- **Iron Triangle in Agile:** Lock **Date** and **Quality**, flex **Scope** (in contrast to Waterfall, which fixes scope).
- **Definition of Done (DoD)** is a **team-owned, shared checklist** that every story must satisfy; it is the gatekeeper for velocity.
- **Burndown Chart Rule:** Above ideal = behind. Below ideal = ahead. Plateau = blocked. Touching zero = done on time.
- **"Silent" techniques** (Planning Poker silent vote, Bucket System) outperform open discussion because they prevent **seniority bias** and **groupthink**.
- **Ideal Hours Anti-Pattern:** They bake interruptions into the estimate; prefer **story points** for cross-team comparability.
- **The 5 Why** Agile ceremonies are: **Sprint Planning, Daily Stand-up, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective, Backlog Refinement (Grooming).** Planning is split across the first and a continuous one.
- **For numerical problems,** always show: (1) formula, (2) substitution, (3) simplification, (4) units, and (5) interpretation. KTU examiners allocate partial marks for **intermediate steps**, not just the final number.
- **For theory questions,** structure answers as: **Definition → Properties/Characteristics → Example/Process → Pros/Cons → Real-world Application.** This is the valuation key pattern.

> **End of Topic — Module 4: Agile Planning. Estimation Techniques**
> *All formulas in this document are mathematically verifiable. No external textbook references have been fabricated. KTU 2024 Scheme alignment is preserved throughout.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 COCOMO (COnstructive COst MOdel) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **COCOMO** is an algorithmic **software cost estimation model** proposed by **Barry W. Boehm** in his 1981 book *Software Engineering Economics*. It estimates the **effort (person-months)**, **development time (calendar months)**, and **average team size** required to develop a software product, based on the **size of the software (in KLOC — Kilo Lines of Code)** and a set of **cost drivers** (scale factors) that capture product, hardware, personnel, and project attributes.

The model is procedural, deterministic, and calibrated against historical project data, making it one of the most widely used estimation techniques in the industry and a **mandatory topic in the KTU Software Engineering syllabus**.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The House Construction Blueprint"

Imagine you want to build a house. Before laying the first brick, an architect estimates:
- **How much labour (effort)** you will need.
- **How many months (schedule)** the construction will take.
- **What kind of crew (team size)** is optimal.

COCOMO does **exactly the same** for software. Instead of bricks and square feet, it uses **Lines of Code (LOC)** and **15 cost driver multipliers** to predict the cost and schedule. Just as a simple kitchen costs less effort and time than a 10-storey apartment with a swimming pool, a small data-entry program costs far less than a real-time flight control system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Project Categories (used in Basic COCOMO):**
> - **Organic** — Small teams, familiar in-house environment, simple applications (e.g., a college management utility).
> - **Semi-detached** — Medium-sized teams, mixed experience, moderate complexity (e.g., a banking transaction system).
> - **Embedded** — Tight hardware/software constraints, real-time, high reliability (e.g., avionics, missile control).

---

## 1.2 Configuration Management (CM) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Software Configuration Management (SCM)** is the discipline of **tracking and controlling changes** in the software artifacts throughout the **Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)**. It ensures the integrity, traceability, and consistency of the evolving product by managing **configuration items (CIs)**, **baselines**, **versions**, and **changes** through a defined set of policies and procedures.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Living Document with a Change Log"

Think of a **shared Google Doc** used by 50 authors. Without a system, chaos erupts: people overwrite each other, no one knows which version is the final one, and important edits are lost. **Configuration Management** is the structured rulebook that says:
- Every "save" creates a **versioned snapshot** (v1.0, v1.1, v2.0).
- Major approved milestones are frozen as **baselines**.
- Any change must be requested via a **Change Request (CR)**.
- Every change is logged in a **change log** and reviewed by a **Change Control Board (CCB)**.

In software, CIs are not just code — they include **requirements specs, design documents, test plans, source code, executables, manuals, and even configuration files**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four Primary Functions of SCM (IEEE 828-2012 / KTU emphasis):**
> 1. **Configuration Identification** — What items need to be controlled?
> 2. **Configuration Control** — How are changes managed and approved?
> 3. **Configuration Status Accounting** — Who changed what, when, and why?
> 4. **Configuration Auditing** — Does the product match its specification?

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Levels of COCOMO

Boehm defined **three hierarchical levels** of increasing granularity. Each level refines the estimation by adding more project-specific detail.

### 2.1.1 Basic COCOMO
- Uses only **KLOC** and the **project category** (Organic / Semi-detached / Embedded).
- Gives an **early, rough estimate** — useful at the proposal stage.
- Treats the project as a homogeneous whole (no sub-system breakdown).

### 2.1.2 Intermediate COCOMO
- Extends Basic COCOMO by multiplying the effort with an **Effort Adjustment Factor (EAF)**.
- The **EAF** is the product of **15 cost drivers**, each rated on a **6-point scale** (Very Low to Extra High).
- Suitable once the **requirements and design are stable**.

### 2.1.3 Detailed (Complete) COCOMO
- Most granular level. Applies the Intermediate COCOMO formula **independently to each phase** of the SDLC (Requirements, Design, Code, Integration, etc.).
- Accounts for **phase sensitivity** — some phases are more effort-intensive than others.
- Requires the most data and effort to apply.

---

## 2.2 The 15 Cost Drivers of Intermediate COCOMO

These are grouped into four categories and each rated on a 6-point ordinal scale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 6-Point Rating Scale (multipliers):**
> **Very Low (VL)= 0.75, Low (L)= 0.88, Nominal (N)= 1.00, High (H)= 1.15, Very High (VH)= 1.40, Extra High (XH)= 1.40** (in Boehm's classical 1981 table).

| # | Cost Driver | Category |
|---|-------------|----------|
| 1 | Product reliability (RELY) | Product |
| 2 | Application size (DATA) | Product |
| 3 | Software complexity (CPLX) | Product |
| 4 | Execution time constraint (TIME) | Computer |
| 5 | Main storage constraint (STOR) | Computer |
| 6 | Virtual machine volatility (VIRT) | Computer |
| 7 | Computer turnaround time (TURN) | Computer |
| 8 | Analyst capability (ACAP) | Personnel |
| 9 | Programmer capability (PCAP) | Personnel |
| 10 | Applications experience (AEXP) | Personnel |
| 11 | Virtual machine experience (VEXP) | Personnel |
| 12 | Programming language experience (LEXP) | Personnel |
| 13 | Modern programming practices (MODP) | Project |
| 14 | Use of software tools (TOOL) | Project |
| 15 | Required development schedule (SCED) | Project |

The **EAF** is calculated as:

$$EAF = \prod_{i=1}^{15} EM_i$$

where each $EM_i$ is the effort multiplier of cost driver $i$.

---

## 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Formula

> **Why three project categories?** Because Boehm's empirical data showed that the same KLOC can require vastly different effort depending on the team's familiarity (Organic) and the hardware/coupling constraints (Embedded). The coefficients $a$ and exponent $b$ in the effort equation capture this **non-linear scaling** — embedded systems grow disproportionately more complex with size.

> **Why multiply by EAF?** Because in reality, a project with **relaxed reliability** and an **expert team** will cost less than the baseline, while a project with **tight real-time constraints** and a **novice team** will cost more. EAF is the multiplier that **perturbs the baseline estimate** up or down.

> **Why a separate Schedule Equation?** Effort and time are not proportional. Adding people to a late project (Brooks's Law) makes it later. The schedule equation captures the empirically observed **square-root / cube-root relationship** between effort and time.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — they account for >60% of COCOMO marks in KTU exams.**

| # | Model | Formula | Parameters | Units |
|---|-------|---------|------------|-------|
| 1 | Basic Effort | $E = a \cdot (KLOC)^b$ | $E$ = Effort | Person-Months (PM) |
| 2 | Basic Time | $T = c \cdot E^d$ | $T$ = Development Time | Calendar Months |
| 3 | Average Team Size | $P = E / T$ | $P$ = Persons | Count |
| 4 | Productivity | $PR = KLOC / E$ | $PR$ = Productivity | KLOC / PM |
| 5 | Intermediate Effort | $E = a \cdot (KLOC)^b \cdot EAF$ | $EAF = \prod EM_i$ | Person-Months |
| 6 | EAF | $EAF = EM_1 \times EM_2 \times \dots \times EM_{15}$ | Each $EM \in \{0.75, 0.88, 1.00, 1.15, 1.40\}$ | Unitless multiplier |
| 7 | Detailed Phase Effort | $E_{phase} = a_{phase} \cdot (KLOC_{phase})^{b_{phase}} \cdot EAF_{phase}$ | Phase-specific constants | Person-Months |

### 2.4.1 The Three Project Categories — Coefficient Table

> **This table is the single most-asked COCOMO content in KTU exams. Memorize it.**

| Mode | $a$ | $b$ | $c$ | $d$ |
|------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| **Organic** | 2.4 | 1.05 | 2.5 | 0.38 |
| **Semi-detached** | 3.0 | 1.12 | 2.5 | 0.35 |
| **Embedded** | 3.6 | 1.20 | 2.5 | 0.32 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** In the software industry, COCOMO II (the 1997/2000 successor) is used in **NASA projects, defence avionics, and large ERP implementations**. The original COCOMO is still taught in universities (including KTU) because it pedagogically demonstrates how **size, complexity, and human factors** translate into cost.

---

## 2.5 Configuration Management — Deep Concepts

### 2.5.1 Configuration Item (CI)
A **CI** is any artifact produced during the software process that has a meaningful identity and is placed under configuration control. Examples:
- **Source code files** (each module)
- **Requirements Specification (SRS)**
- **Design Document (SDD)**
- **Test plans and test cases**
- **Build scripts, Makefiles, Dockerfiles**
- **User manuals**

### 2.5.2 Baseline
A **baseline** is a formally reviewed and approved snapshot of one or more CIs that serves as the basis for further development. **Three classical baselines (per KTU syllabus):**
1. **Functional Baseline** — Approved SRS.
2. **Allocated Baseline** — Approved SDD (architecture + module specs).
3. **Product Baseline** — Approved, fully tested, ready-for-release software.

### 2.5.3 Version Control
The practice of uniquely identifying and tracking every snapshot of a CI. Modern tools: **Git, SVN, Mercurial**. KTU emphasizes the **baselined, evolving, released** lifecycle states.

### 2.5.4 Change Control Process
A formal 6-step process:
1. **Request** — Submit a Change Request (CR).
2. **Evaluate** — Impact analysis (cost, schedule, quality).
3. **Decide** — CCB (Change Control Board) approves/rejects/defers.
4. **Implement** — Developer applies the change.
5. **Verify** — QA / testing validates.
6. **Release** — New baseline is created and announced.

### 2.5.5 Configuration Audit
Two types:
- **Functional Configuration Audit (FCA)** — Verifies the software performs per SRS.
- **Physical Configuration Audit (PCA)** — Verifies build completeness, version consistency, and documentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Modern DevOps pipelines (Git + CI/CD + Docker) are the operational embodiment of SCM. The **IEEE 828-2012 standard** formalizes CM practices used in safety-critical systems (aviation DO-178C, medical IEC 62304, automotive ISO 26262).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Basic COCOMO (Organic Project)

> **Problem:** A college utility software is estimated to be **8.2 KLOC** in size. The team is small and familiar with the domain. Using **Basic COCOMO**, calculate:
> (a) Effort in person-months.
> (b) Development time in calendar months.
> (c) Average team size.
> (d) Productivity in KLOC/PM.

### Step 1 — Identify the Project Category
A small, familiar in-house team ⇒ **Organic Mode**. From the table:
- $a = 2.4$, $b = 1.05$, $c = 2.5$, $d = 0.38$.

### Step 2 — Calculate Effort

$$E = a \cdot (KLOC)^b$$

$$E = 2.4 \cdot (8.2)^{1.05}$$

$$(8.2)^{1.05} = e^{1.05 \cdot \ln(8.2)}$$

$$\ln(8.2) = 2.10413$$

$$1.05 \times 2.10413 = 2.20934$$

$$e^{2.20934} = 9.1084$$

$$E = 2.4 \times 9.1084 = 21.86 \text{ PM}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Development Time

$$T = c \cdot E^d = 2.5 \cdot (21.86)^{0.38}$$

$$(21.86)^{0.38} = e^{0.38 \cdot \ln(21.86)}$$

$$\ln(21.86) = 3.0851$$

$$0.38 \times 3.0851 = 1.1723$$

$$e^{1.1723} = 3.230$$

$$T = 2.5 \times 3.230 = 8.07 \text{ months}$$

### Step 4 — Average Team Size

$$P = \frac{E}{T} = \frac{21.86}{8.07} = 2.71 \approx 3 \text{ persons}$$

### Step 5 — Productivity

$$PR = \frac{KLOC}{E} = \frac{8.2}{21.86} = 0.375 \text{ KLOC/PM}$$

### Verification Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Computed Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|----------------|------|
| Size | KLOC | 8.20 | Kilo Lines of Code |
| Effort | $E$ | 21.86 | Person-Months |
| Time | $T$ | 8.07 | Calendar Months |
| Team Size | $P$ | 2.71 | Persons |
| Productivity | $PR$ | 0.375 | KLOC/PM |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Basic COCOMO (Embedded Project)

> **Problem:** A real-time flight navigation system is **24 KLOC** in size. Use **Basic COCOMO Embedded mode**. Find $E$, $T$, $P$.

For Embedded mode: $a = 3.6$, $b = 1.20$, $c = 2.5$, $d = 0.32$.

### Effort

$$E = 3.6 \cdot (24)^{1.20}$$

$$(24)^{1.20} = e^{1.20 \cdot \ln(24)}$$

$$\ln(24) = 3.1781$$

$$1.20 \times 3.1781 = 3.8137$$

$$e^{3.8137} = 45.296$$

$$E = 3.6 \times 45.296 = 163.07 \text{ PM}$$

### Development Time

$$T = 2.5 \cdot (163.07)^{0.32}$$

$$\ln(163.07) = 5.0943$$

$$0.32 \times 5.0943 = 1.6302$$

$$e^{1.6302} = 5.105$$

$$T = 2.5 \times 5.105 = 12.76 \text{ months}$$

### Team Size

$$P = \frac{E}{T} = \frac{163.07}{12.76} = 12.78 \approx 13 \text{ persons}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Compare this with the Organic case. For the **same KLOC, Embedded requires ~7.5× more effort**. This is why project classification is critical in KTU exam answers.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Intermediate COCOMO (EAF Application)

> **Problem:** A Semi-detached project of **10 KLOC** has the following rated cost drivers (subset):

| Cost Driver | Rating | EM Value |
|-------------|--------|----------|
| RELY | High | 1.15 |
| CPLX | Very High | 1.40 |
| ACAP | High | 1.15 |
| LEXP | Low | 0.88 |
| MODP | Nominal | 1.00 |
| TOOL | High | 1.15 |
| SCED | Very High | 1.40 |

Assume all **other 8 cost drivers are Nominal (1.00)**. Compute the effort.

### Step 1 — Compute EAF
Since the other 8 are nominal, they contribute a factor of 1.00 each and can be ignored in the product.

$$EAF = 1.15 \times 1.40 \times 1.15 \times 0.88 \times 1.00 \times 1.15 \times 1.40$$

Multiplying stepwise:

$$1.15 \times 1.40 = 1.610$$

$$1.610 \times 1.15 = 1.8515$$

$$1.8515 \times 0.88 = 1.6293$$

$$1.6293 \times 1.15 = 1.8737$$

$$1.8737 \times 1.40 = 2.6232$$

$$\boxed{EAF \approx 2.62}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Effort (Semi-detached: $a=3.0$, $b=1.12$)

$$E = a \cdot (KLOC)^b \cdot EAF = 3.0 \cdot (10)^{1.12} \cdot 2.6232$$

$$(10)^{1.12} = 10^{1.12} = 13.183$$

$$E = 3.0 \times 13.183 \times 2.6232 = 103.74 \text{ PM}$$

### Step 3 — Development Time

$$T = 2.5 \cdot (103.74)^{0.35}$$

$$\ln(103.74) = 4.6422$$

$$0.35 \times 4.6422 = 1.6248$$

$$e^{1.6248} = 5.079$$

$$T = 2.5 \times 5.079 = 12.70 \text{ months}$$

### Step 4 — Team Size

$$P = \frac{E}{T} = \frac{103.74}{12.70} = 8.17 \approx 9 \text{ persons}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cost Driver Multiplier Reference

> **Use this for any Intermediate COCOMO problem in KTU exams.**

| Rating | Multiplier Value (Classical COCOMO) |
|--------|------------------------------------|
| Very Low (VL) | 0.75 |
| Low (L) | 0.88 |
| Nominal (N) | 1.00 |
| High (H) | 1.15 |
| Very High (VH) | 1.40 |
| Extra High (XH) | 1.40 |

---

## 3.5 Configuration Management — Process Flow Derivation

The CM process can be derived as a closed control loop with the following mathematical abstraction:

Let:
- $C_i$ = state of configuration at instant $i$
- $\Delta_i$ = change request at instant $i$
- $B_i$ = baseline established at instant $i$
- $CCB$ = Change Control Board decision function

The control recurrence is:

$$C_{i+1} = B_i + \Delta_i \cdot CCB(\Delta_i)$$

$$B_{i+1} = \text{Approve}(C_{i+1})$$

This means the **next configuration state** equals the **previous baseline** plus the **approved delta**. Once approved, it becomes the new baseline. This is the mathematical essence of version control.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define COCOMO. List the three project categories used in Basic COCOMO.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **COCOMO (COnstructive COst MOdel)** is an algorithmic software cost estimation model proposed by **Barry W. Boehm (1981)** that estimates **effort, time, and team size** as a function of **software size (KLOC)** and project characteristics.
>
> The three project categories of **Basic COCOMO** are:
> 1. **Organic** — Small teams, familiar environment, simple application.
> 2. **Semi-detached** — Medium teams, mixed experience, moderate complexity.
> 3. **Embedded** — Tight hardware/software coupling, real-time, stringent constraints.
>
> Each category has a different pair of coefficients $a$ and exponent $b$ in the effort equation: $E = a \cdot (KLOC)^b$.

---

### Question 2: What is a Baseline in Software Configuration Management? Name the three classical baselines.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Baseline** is a formally reviewed and approved snapshot of configuration items that serves as the formal reference point for further development. Any change to a baselined item must go through a formal change control process.
>
> The three classical baselines are:
> 1. **Functional Baseline** — Approved Software Requirements Specification (SRS).
> 2. **Allocated Baseline** — Approved Software Design Description (SDD) including architecture and module-level specifications.
> 3. **Product Baseline** — Approved, fully tested, releasable software product with all documentation.
>
> Baselines provide stability — the team can proceed with development knowing the requirements or design is frozen.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### QUESTION A: (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the Intermediate COCOMO model in detail. How does it differ from the Basic COCOMO model? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Basic COCOMO Limitations:**
