| **Definition** | Manages the integrity of all configuration items across the project lifecycle | Manages the controlled introduction of modifications to the software product |
| **Scope** | Broader: covers versioning, baselines, audits, control | Narrower: subset of CM, focuses on change requests |
| **Primary Goal** | Ensure product completeness and traceability | Ensure changes are evaluated, approved, and implemented safely |
| **Key Activities** | Baseline creation, version control, configuration audits, status accounting | CR logging, impact analysis, CCB review, implementation |
| **Artifacts** | Baseline documents, version tags, audit reports | Change Request forms, Impact Analysis reports, CCB minutes |
| **Tools** | Git, SVN, CVS, ClearCase | JIRA, ServiceNow, Remedy, Bugzilla |
| **Relationship** | Parent discipline (contains ChM) | Sub-discipline of CM |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often write *Configuration Management* and *Change Management* as the same discipline. **This is the #1 reason for losing 3-4 marks** in ESE answers. Always emphasize that **Change Management is a sub-component of Configuration Management**, not a separate discipline.

---

#### **Question B: 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *What is a Change Control Board? Explain its composition, responsibilities, and decision-making process.*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
A Change Control Board (CCB) is a formally constituted group of stakeholders responsible for evaluating, prioritizing, and deciding on software change requests.

**Composition (3-7 members):**
- **Project Manager** (Chair)
- **Configuration Manager**
- **Lead Developer / Technical Architect**
- **QA Lead**
- **Client / Product Owner**
- **End-User Representative** (optional)

**Responsibilities:**
1. Review all incoming Change Requests
2. Evaluate impact analysis reports
3. Approve, reject, or defer CRs
4. Assign priority scores (e.g., CPS)
5. Maintain audit trails and meeting minutes
6. Communicate decisions to the development team

**Decision-Making Process:**
1. **Pre-meeting:** Configuration Manager distributes CR documents and impact reports.
2. **Meeting:** Each CR is discussed; technical and business perspectives are debated.
3. **Scoring:** CPS is calculated: $\text{CPS} = w_1 B + w_2 U + w_3 R$
4. **Voting:** Decisions may be unanimous or majority-based.
5. **Documentation:** Minutes are recorded and distributed to all stakeholders.
6. **Follow-up:** Approved CRs are tracked through the lifecycle until closure.

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A software project has received three Change Requests with the following data. Using the CPS formula with weights $w_1=0.4, w_2=0.4, w_3=0.2$, determine which CR should be approved first.*

| **CR-ID** | **Business Value (B)** | **Urgency (U)** | **Risk Reduction (R)** |
|-----------|------------------------|-----------------|------------------------|
| CR-001 | 9 | 7 | 6 |
| CR-002 | 6 | 9 | 8 |
| CR-003 | 8 | 8 | 4 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the CPS formula to each CR.

**For CR-001:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPS}_{001} &= (0.4 \times 9) + (0.4 \times 7) + (0.2 \times 6) \\
&= 3.6 + 2.8 + 1.2 \\
&= 7.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**For CR-002:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPS}_{002} &= (0.4 \times 6) + (0.4 \times 9) + (0.2 \times 8) \\
&= 2.4 + 3.6 + 1.6 \\
&= 7.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**For CR-003:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPS}_{003} &= (0.4 \times 8) + (0.4 \times 8) + (0.2 \times 4) \\
&= 3.2 + 3.2 + 0.8 \\
&= 7.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Rank in descending order.

| **Rank** | **CR-ID** | **CPS Score** |
|----------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 (Tie) | CR-001 | 7.6 |
| 1 (Tie) | CR-002 | 7.6 |
| 3 | CR-003 | 7.2 |

**Step 3: Conclusion.**
**CR-001 and CR-002 are tied** with a CPS of 7.6. The CCB should approve both first (subject to resource availability), followed by CR-003 (CPS = 7.2).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Numerical questions in ESE are graded step-by-step. **Each formula substitution MUST be shown explicitly.** A common mistake is writing only the final answer (7.6) without showing the weighted calculation. This results in a loss of **3-4 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Change Management** is a sub-discipline of **Configuration Management**, not a synonym.
- The **five-stage pipeline**: Identify → Log → Evaluate → Decide → Implement.
- **CCB** is the governing body; typically **3-7 members**.
- **Four change types** (Pressman): Corrective, Adaptive, Perfective, Preventive.
- **CPS Formula:** $\text{CPS} = w_1 B + w_2 U + w_3 R$ with $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$.
- **Risk Formula:** $R = P \times I$ (Probability × Impact).
- **Decision thresholds:** Approve ($\text{CPS} \geq 6.0$), Defer ($4.0 \leq \text{CPS} < 6.0$), Reject ($\text{CPS} < 4.0$).
- **Mandatory audit trail** for ISO 9001, CMMI, and KTU ESE grading.
- **Common Pitfalls:** Do not confuse **Configuration Management vs Change Management**; always show **explicit calculation steps** for numericals; always draw a **diagram** for process-based questions.
- **Tools:** JIRA, GitHub Issues, ServiceNow, Bugzilla, GitLab.
- **Standards:** IEEE 828-2012 (SCM), ISO/IEC 12207 (Lifecycle processes).
- **KPI Metrics to remember:** Change Rejection Rate (CRR), Change Success Rate (CSR), Mean Time to Change (MTTC).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Release Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Release Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Release Management** is the disciplined, end-to-end software engineering process that governs the planning, scheduling, versioning, building, testing, packaging, distribution, and post-deployment support of a software product or service. It forms an integral sub-discipline of **Software Configuration Management (SCM)** and **Software Project Management**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Release Management is defined as:

> *The systematic process of controlling the planning, building, testing, and deployment of software releases to designated environments (Development → Testing → Staging → Production), ensuring that the deliverable is traceable, reproducible, and aligned with stakeholder requirements while minimizing business risk.*

Mathematically expressed as a controlled transformation:

$$\text{Release} = f(\text{Approved Changes}, \text{Versioned Configuration}, \text{Build Process}, \text{Test Evidence}, \text{Environment})$$

The key governing constraints are:
- **Traceability** of every artifact from requirement to deployment
- **Reproducibility** of every release using version-controlled inputs
- **Accountability** through formal sign-off and audit logs
- **Stability** of the production environment post-release

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Release Management is frequently tested as a *part* of Software Configuration Management and *Project Management* modules. The KTU board typically expects students to explain the **release process model**, the **role of version management**, and the **relationship between release management, change management, and configuration management**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Restaurant Dish Analogy**
> 
> Imagine a restaurant kitchen. A **Release** is like serving a new dish on the menu. Before the dish reaches the customer (the *end user*), it goes through several controlled stages:
> 
> 1. **Recipe Finalization (Release Planning):** The chef decides what the dish is, what ingredients (features) go in, and what version (spicy/mild) is being released.
> 2. **Mise en Place (Configuration Identification):** All ingredients are measured, labeled, and versioned (salt batch #12, tomatoes lot #45).
> 3. **Cooking Trial (Build & Integration):** The dish is prepared in the test kitchen. Different chefs follow the same recipe to ensure consistency.
> 4. **Tasting (Acceptance Testing):** The head chef and a food critic taste the dish, sign off, and document feedback.
> 5. **Plating & Service (Deployment):** The dish is plated, photographed, and served.
> 6. **Menu Update (Release Notes):** The menu card is updated with the new dish version, allergens, and price.
> 7. **Customer Feedback (Post-Release Support):** If customers complain about saltiness, that goes back to the chef (change request) for the next version.
> 
> Release Management ensures that the *same dish*, with the *same quality*, can be served to **1,000 customers** across **10 branches** consistently — and if there's a problem, you can recall *exactly which batch of salt* caused it.

In software terms, this means: when 1 million users receive the same app update, Release Management ensures that the update behaves identically, can be rolled back if broken, and is traceable to a specific commit in version control.

### 1.3 Why Release Management Matters in Industry

| Business Driver | Impact of Poor Release Management | Impact of Good Release Management |
|---|---|---|
| Time-to-Market | Delayed rollouts due to manual errors | Automated, predictable releases |
| Customer Trust | Outages, data loss, broken features | High availability, seamless updates |
| Compliance | Failed audits (SOX, GDPR, HIPAA) | Full audit trail of all changes |
| Cost | Firefighting, hotfixes, war rooms | Reduced MTTR (Mean Time To Recovery) |
| Scalability | Manual processes break at scale | CI/CD pipelines scale effortlessly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industry Metrics** used in Release Management (memorize for KTU):
> - **MTBF** — Mean Time Between Failures
> - **MTTR** — Mean Time To Recovery
> - **Deployment Frequency** — How often releases are pushed
> - **Change Failure Rate** — % of releases causing production failures
> - **Lead Time for Changes** — Time from code commit to production
> 
> These are part of the **DORA Metrics** (DevOps Research and Assessment), often referenced in modern KTU question papers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Core Phases of Release Management

Release Management is decomposed into **five logically distinct phases**. Each phase has well-defined inputs, activities, outputs, and gate criteria (entry/exit conditions).

#### Phase 1: Release Planning & Scheduling

- **Inputs:** Approved Change Requests, Product Roadmap, Business Priorities, Resource Availability
- **Activities:** Defining release scope, setting release calendar, identifying dependencies, allocating environments, estimating effort
- **Outputs:** Release Plan Document, Release Calendar, Resource Allocation Matrix
- **Gate Criteria:** Stakeholder sign-off on scope; environment availability confirmed

#### Phase 2: Release Build & Configuration

- **Inputs:** Release Plan, Versioned Source Code, Approved Configuration Items
- **Activities:** Branching strategy, code freeze, build execution, artifact generation, version tagging
- **Outputs:** Build Artifacts (binaries, containers, installers), Build Manifest, Version Tag
- **Gate Criteria:** Successful build with reproducible checksum (e.g., SHA-256)

#### Phase 3: Release Testing & Quality Assurance

- **Inputs:** Build Artifacts, Test Plans, Acceptance Criteria
- **Activities:** Smoke testing, regression testing, UAT (User Acceptance Testing), performance testing, security scanning
- **Outputs:** Test Reports, Defect Logs, Release Approval Form
- **Gate Criteria:** Zero critical defects; all P0/P1 issues closed or accepted

#### Phase 4: Release Deployment & Distribution

- **Inputs:** Approved Release, Deployment Plan, Rollback Plan
- **Activities:** Environment promotion (Dev → Test → Stage → Prod), deployment execution, smoke tests in production, communication to stakeholders
- **Outputs:** Deployed System, Deployment Log, Release Notes
- **Gate Criteria:** Production health checks passed; rollback plan validated

#### Phase 5: Post-Release Support & Review

- **Inputs:** Production Monitoring Data, Incident Reports
- **Activities:** Hyper-care period, incident triage, retrospective, lessons learned, archival of release artifacts
- **Outputs:** Post-Release Review Report, Updated Risk Register, Improvement Backlog
- **Gate Criteria:** SLA compliance verified; no open P0 incidents

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these phases matter for KTU:** A common 14-mark question asks students to *draw and explain the release management process model*. You must be able to articulate each phase with its **inputs, activities, outputs, and responsibilities**.

### 2.2 Version Management — The Heart of Release Management

Version management is the *numerical identification system* that uniquely identifies every state of a software artifact. Without it, releases are untraceable.

#### 2.2.1 Semantic Versioning (SemVer)

The industry standard version format is **MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH** (e.g., `2.7.3`).

$$\text{Version} = \langle M, m, p \rangle$$

Where:
- **M (MAJOR):** Incremented for incompatible API changes
- **m (MINOR):** Incremented for backward-compatible new features
- **p (PATCH):** Incremented for backward-compatible bug fixes

A pre-release or build metadata is appended using a hyphen and dot-separated identifiers:

$$\text{FullVersion} = M.m.p \; (-\text{pre}[\,.\text{id}]\,)? \; (+\text{build}[\,.\text{id}]\,)?$$

Examples: `1.0.0-alpha`, `1.0.0-alpha.1`, `1.0.0-rc.2+build.456`

#### 2.2.2 Build Numbering

Build numbers are typically monotonically increasing integers that uniquely identify each build artifact:

$$\text{BuildNumber}_{n+1} = \text{BuildNumber}_{n} + 1$$

This provides chronological ordering even when version numbers repeat (e.g., hotfixes).

#### 2.2.3 Release Identifiers

Each release should have a *codename* and a *numeric identifier*:

- **Numeric ID:** `REL-2024-Q4-007`
- **Codename:** *Aurora*, *Borealis*, *Cosmos* (used in marketing)

### 2.3 Release Types and Cadence

| Release Type | Frequency | Risk Level | Typical Contents | Audience |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Major Release** | Annually / Bi-annually | High | Breaking changes, new architecture | All users |
| **Minor Release** | Quarterly | Medium | New features, enhancements | All users |
| **Patch Release** | As needed (hotfix) | Medium | Bug fixes, security patches | All users |
| **Service Pack** | Annually | Low-Medium | Cumulative patches, minor features | All users |
| **Emergency Release** | Immediate | Variable | Critical security/stability fix | All users |
| **Canary Release** | Continuous | Low | New feature to <5% of users | Selected users |
| **Blue-Green Release** | Continuous | Very Low | Parallel environment swap | All users |
| **Rolling Release** | Continuous | Low | Incremental updates | All users |

> [!NOTE]
> **Canary, Blue-Green, and Rolling** are modern DevOps release strategies. KTU 2024 includes DevOps concepts in Module 4, so be ready to contrast these deployment strategies.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Semantic Versioning | $V = \langle M, m, p \rangle$ | Major.Minor.Patch | Integers, $M \geq 0$ |
| 2 | Pre-release Identifier | $V_{\text{pre}} = V - \text{label}$ | `1.0.0-alpha.1` | Lexicographic ordering |
| 3 | Build Metadata | $V_{\text{build}} = V + \text{meta}$ | `1.0.0+sha.abc123` | Ignored in precedence |
| 4 | Version Precedence Rule | $M > m > p > \text{pre}$ | Numerical first, then pre-release | Standard SemVer 2.0 |
| 5 | Release Cadence | $T_{\text{release}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{cycle}}}{N_{\text{releases}}}$ | Avg time between releases | Days / weeks |
| 6 | Change Failure Rate | $\text{CFR} = \dfrac{F_{\text{fail}}}{R_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Failed releases / Total releases | Percentage, $\vert \text{CFR} \vert \leq 100$ |
| 7 | Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{T_{\text{operational}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | Avg uptime between crashes | Hours / days |
| 8 | Mean Time To Recovery | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum T_{\text{recovery}}}{N_{\text{incidents}}}$ | Avg time to restore service | Minutes / hours |
| 9 | Deployment Frequency | $\text{DF} = \dfrac{N_{\text{deploys}}}{T_{\text{period}}}$ | Releases per unit time | Per day / week |
| 10 | Lead Time for Changes | $\text{LT} = T_{\text{commit}} - T_{\text{deploy}}$ | Code commit to production | Hours / days |
| 11 | Release Burndown | $R_{\text{remaining}}(t) = R_0 - v \cdot t$ | Linear release progress | Items, $v$ = velocity |
| 12 | Risk Exposure | $\text{RE} = P_{\text{fail}} \times C_{\text{fail}}$ | Probability $\times$ Cost of failure | Monetary / qualitative |
| 13 | Rollback Success Rate | $\text{RSR} = \dfrac{R_{\text{success}}}{R_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Successful rollbacks / Total | Percentage |
| 14 | Artifact Reproducibility | $H(\text{artifact}) = H(\text{inputs} + \text{build})$ | Hash equality check | SHA-256 hex string |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Real-World Utility:** In CI/CD pipelines, formulas (6), (7), (8), (9), and (10) form the **DORA Four Key Metrics**, which are the de facto industry standard for measuring software delivery performance. KTU 2024 may ask you to define and interpret these.

### 2.5 Release Management vs. Change Management vs. Configuration Management

This distinction is a **favorite KTU question**. Mastering it is essential.

| Aspect | Release Management | Change Management | Configuration Management |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Delivering a verified build to an environment | Approving and tracking change requests | Controlling configuration items and baselines |
| **Scope** | Build → Deploy → Support | Request → Evaluate → Approve → Implement | Identify → Control → Status → Audit |
| **Time Horizon** | Per release cycle | Per change request | Continuous, lifecycle-wide |
| **Key Output** | Deployed release + release notes | Approved change record | Baseline + audit trail |
| **Triggered By** | Change approval, release schedule | Bug, enhancement, risk | Any change to a CI |
| **Primary Stakeholder** | Operations, DevOps team | Change Advisory Board (CAB) | Configuration Manager, QA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Relationship Diagram (in words):** Change Management decides *what* changes are allowed → Configuration Management tracks *how* those changes affect the baseline → Release Management packages and delivers the *result* of approved changes to users.

### 2.6 Release Documentation Suite

A formal release must include the following documents:

1. **Release Notes** — User-facing summary of new features, fixes, known issues
2. **Release Plan** — Schedule, scope, risks, resource plan
3. **Build Manifest** — List of artifacts, versions, checksums, dependencies
4. **Deployment Guide** — Step-by-step installation and configuration
5. **Rollback Plan** — Procedure to revert to the previous version
6. **Release Sign-off Form** — Formal approval from stakeholders
7. **Post-Release Review (PRR)** — Lessons learned, metrics, improvements

### 2.7 Risk Management in Releases

Each release carries risk. Risk is quantified as:

$$\text{Risk Exposure} = P(\text{Failure}) \times C(\text{Consequence})$$

Common release risks and mitigations:

| Risk | Probability | Consequence | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Build breaks at deploy time | Medium | High | Pre-deployment smoke tests, blue-green |
| Data loss during migration | Low | Critical | Backup + restore drill, dry-run |
| Performance regression | Medium | Medium | Load testing in staging |
| Security vulnerability | Medium | Critical | SAST/DAST scanning, signed artifacts |
| Rollback fails | Low | High | Tested rollback procedures, feature flags |
| User resistance to change | Medium | Medium | Training, phased rollout, communication |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked "How do you handle release failures?", your model answer must include: (1) Immediate rollback, (2) Incident triage, (3) Root Cause Analysis (RCA), (4) Communication to stakeholders, (5) Update of release plan to prevent recurrence.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Inputs, Activities, Outputs, and Gate Criteria Across the Five Phases

This matrix is your **go-to reference** for any 14-mark KTU question that asks you to "Explain the Release Management process model."

| Phase | Inputs | Activities | Outputs | Gate Criteria (Exit) | Responsible Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Release Planning** | Product roadmap, approved change requests, capacity plan | Define scope, set release date, identify dependencies, allocate environments, risk assessment | Release plan document, release calendar, resource matrix | Stakeholder sign-off; environment booked | Release Manager |
| **2. Build & Configuration** | Source code (versioned), dependencies, build scripts | Branching, code freeze, compile, package, version tag, sign artifacts | Build artifacts, checksum, build manifest, version tag | Reproducible build; checksum verified | Build Engineer |
| **3. Testing & QA** | Build artifacts, test plans, test data | Smoke, regression, UAT, performance, security | Test reports, defect logs, release approval | Zero P0/P1 defects; UAT sign-off | QA Lead |
| **4. Deployment** | Approved release, deployment plan, rollback plan | Environment promotion, install, configure, smoke test in prod, communicate | Deployed system, deployment log, release notes | Production health checks passed; rollback validated | DevOps Engineer |
| **5. Post-Release** | Monitoring data, incident reports, user feedback | Hyper-care, incident triage, retrospective, archive, lessons learned | Post-release review, updated risk register, improvement backlog | SLA met; no unresolved P0 incidents | Service Manager |

### 3.2 Worked Example: Calculating Release Metrics (CFR, MTBF, MTTR, DF, LT)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A software company deployed **12 releases** in **Q4 2024** (October 1 to December 31 = **92 days**). Out of these, **3 releases caused production failures**. The operational team recorded the following failure timestamps and recovery times for those 3 incidents:
> 
> - Incident 1: Crashed at 10:14, recovered at 10:52 → $T_{\text{recovery}} = 38$ min
> - Incident 2: Crashed at 15:30, recovered at 16:25 → $T_{\text{recovery}} = 55$ min
> - Incident 3: Crashed at 09:05, recovered at 09:23 → $T_{\text{recovery}} = 18$ min
> 
> The first code commit for the Q4 release cycle was made on **September 20, 2024**. The first production deployment of the cycle was on **October 1, 2024**.

**Step 1 — Change Failure Rate (CFR):**

Using the formula from the cheat sheet:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CFR} &= \frac{F_{\text{fail}}}{R_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3}{12} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
&= 25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** 25% of releases caused production failures. This is **above the elite DORA benchmark of 0–15%**, indicating a need for improved pre-release testing.

**Step 2 — Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTR} &= \frac{\sum T_{\text{recovery}}}{N_{\text{incidents}}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{38 + 55 + 18}{3} \; \text{minutes} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{111}{3} \; \text{minutes} \\[6pt]
&= 37 \; \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** On average, the team recovers from a production failure in 37 minutes. This is within the DORA elite benchmark of $<1$ hour, so the recovery process is strong.

**Step 3 — Deployment Frequency (DF):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DF} &= \frac{N_{\text{deploys}}}{T_{\text{period}}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{12 \text{ releases}}{92 \text{ days}} \\[6pt]
&= 0.1304 \; \text{releases/day} \\[6pt]
&\approx 0.76 \; \text{releases per week} \\[6pt]
&\approx 1 \; \text{release every} \; 7.67 \; \text{days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Roughly one release per week, which is in the **DORA "High" performer band** (between once per week and once per month).

**Step 4 — Lead Time for Changes (LT):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LT} &= T_{\text{deploy}} - T_{\text{commit}} \\[6pt]
&= \text{Oct 1, 2024} - \text{Sep 20, 2024} \\[6pt]
&= 11 \; \text{days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** From the first code commit to the first production deployment of the Q4 cycle took 11 days. This is in the DORA **"High"** band (between one day and one week would be elite; 11 days is acceptable but improvable).

**Step 5 — Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):**

If the total operational time in Q4 was $T_{\text{operational}} = 92 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ hours} = 2208 \text{ hours}$ and there were 3 failures:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTBF} &= \frac{T_{\text{operational}}}{N_{\text{failures}}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2208}{3} \; \text{hours} \\[6pt]
&= 736 \; \text{hours} \\[6pt]
&\approx 30.67 \; \text{days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** On average, a failure occurs once every ~30.67 days, meaning the system is highly stable between incidents.

**Summary Table of Computed Metrics:**

| Metric | Symbol | Computed Value | DORA Band |
|---|---|---|---|
| Change Failure Rate | CFR | 25% | High (needs improvement) |
| Mean Time To Recovery | MTTR | 37 minutes | Elite |
| Deployment Frequency | DF | ~1 per 7.67 days | High |
| Lead Time for Changes | LT | 11 days | High |
| Mean Time Between Failures | MTBF | ~30.67 days | Stable |

### 3.3 Version Precedence Worked Example (SemVer 2.0)

Given the following versions, rank them in **ascending order of precedence**:

- $V_1 = 1.0.0$
- $V_2 = 1.0.0-rc.1$
- $V_3 = 1.0.0-alpha$
- $V_4 = 1.0.0-alpha.1$
- $V_5 = 1.0.0-alpha.beta$
- $V_6 = 1.0.0-beta`
- $V_7 = 1.0.0-beta.2`
- $V_8 = 1.0.0-beta.11`
- $V_9 = 1.0.0-0.3.7`
- $V_{10} = 1.0.0-x.7.z.92`

**Rule:** Numerical identifiers always have lower precedence than alphanumeric identifiers. A larger numerical identifier has higher precedence. Alphanumeric identifiers are compared lexicographically (ASCII order).

**Step 1 — Group by Major.Minor.Patch:**

All have $1.0.0$ as the base, so the ordering depends solely on the pre-release identifier.

**Step 2 — Apply Precedence Rule for Pre-Releases:**

A version *with* a pre-release identifier has **lower** precedence than the same version *without* one. Therefore:

$$V_3, V_4, V_5, V_9, V_{10}, V_2, V_6, V_7, V_8 < V_1$$

**Step 3 — Within Pre-Release Group, Order Lexicographically:**

Comparing alphanumeric segments separated by dots. Numeric segments compared numerically, alphanumeric segments compared ASCII.

- $V_3 = 1.0.0\text{-alpha}$ → identifier: `alpha`
- $V_4 = 1.0.0\text{-alpha}.1$ → identifiers: `alpha`, `1`
- $V_5 = 1.0.0\text{-alpha}.beta$ → identifiers: `alpha`, `beta`
- $V_9 = 1.0.0\text{-}0.3.7$ → identifiers: `0`, `3`, `7` (all numeric)
- $V_{10} = 1.0.0\text{-}x.7.z.92$ → identifiers: `x`, `7`, `z`, `92`
- $V_2 = 1.0.0\text{-rc}.1$ → identifiers: `rc`, `1`
- $V_6 = 1.0.0\text{-beta}` → identifier: `beta`
- $V_7 = 1.0.0\text{-beta}.2` → identifiers: `beta`, `2`
- $V_8 = 1.0.0\text{-beta}.11` → identifiers: `beta`, `11`

Comparing segment-by-segment at the first level: `alpha` < `beta` < `rc` < `0` (numeric < alpha? No — numeric < alphanumeric), `x` < `rc`? Actually `rc` comes after `x` alphabetically.

**Final Ascending Precedence Order:**

$$
1.0.0\text{-alpha} \; < \; 1.0.0\text{-alpha}.1 \; < \; 1.0.0\text{-alpha}.beta \; < \; 1.0.0\text{-beta} \; < \; 1.0.0\text{-beta}.2 \; < \; 1.0.0\text{-beta}.11 \; < \; 1.0.0\text{-}0.3.7 \; < \; 1.0.0\text{-}x.7.z.92 \; < \; 1.0.0\text{-rc}.1 \; < \; 1.0.0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** Version precedence is non-trivial. KTU has asked students to identify the *correct* precedence ordering. Remember: **numeric < alphanumeric, and within numeric, compare as numbers (so `2` < `11`), but within alphanumeric, compare as strings (so `alpha` < `beta` < `rc`).**

### 3.4 Decision Matrix: When to Use Each Release Strategy

| Scenario | Recommended Strategy | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Banking app with zero tolerance for downtime | **Blue-Green** | Instant switch, instant rollback |
| Social media feature test with risk of low adoption | **Canary** | Limit blast radius to small % |
| Continuous delivery of a SaaS product | **Rolling** | No downtime, gradual replacement |
| Critical security patch on a legacy system | **Emergency Patch** | Speed > elegance |
| Quarterly business app with major UI overhaul | **Major Release** | Predictable cadence, planned migration |
| Mobile app with iOS/Android parity issues | **Phased / Staged Rollout** | Mitigate platform-specific bugs |

### 3.5 Derivation: Reproducibility Hash for Build Artifacts

Reproducibility guarantees that the same inputs always produce the same outputs. The hash of an artifact is computed as:

$$
H(\text{artifact}) = \text{SHA-256}(\text{buildScript} \Vert \text{sourceCode} \Vert \text{dependencies} \Vert \text{config})
$$

**Verification step:** If two builds produce the same hash, they are bit-for-bit identical:

$$
H_1 = H_2 \implies \text{Artifact}_1 \equiv \text{Artifact}_2
$$

**Worked example:** If source code hash is `a1b2...`, dependencies hash is `c3d4...`, build script hash is `e5f6...`, and config hash is `g7h8...`, then the combined hash input is the concatenation. Any change to *any* input changes the final hash, ensuring tamper detection.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Release Management Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following block diagram shows the **end-to-end release management process** with all five phases, decision gates, and feedback loops. Use this as your reference for any "draw and explain" KTU question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Phase 1: Release Planning] --> G1{Plan Approved?}
    G1 -- No --> A1
    G1 -- Yes --> A2[Phase 2: Build and Configuration]
    A2 --> G2{Build Successful?}
    G2 -- No --> RB1[Rollback / Re-Build]
    RB1 --> A2
    G2 -- Yes --> A3[Phase 3: Testing and QA]
    A3 --> G3{Zero P0 P1 Defects?}
    G3 -- No --> FB1[Fix and Re-Test]
    FB1 --> A3
    G3 -- Yes --> A4[Phase 4: Deployment to Production]
    A4 --> G4{Production Health OK?}
    G4 -- No --> RB2[Execute Rollback Plan]
    RB2 --> A5
    G4 -- Yes --> A5[Phase 5: Post Release Support]
    A5 --> RETRO[Lessons Learned Retrospective]
    RETRO --> IMP[Improvement Backlog]
    IMP --> A1
    A5 --> MON[Continuous Monitoring]
    MON --> A5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The diamond shapes are **gate criteria** (decision points). The dashed flow back to Phase 1 represents the *continuous improvement loop*. Any phase that fails its gate triggers a corrective loop (rebuild, retest, rollback). This is the canonical release management lifecycle.

### 4.2 Release Branching and Versioning Strategy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This diagram illustrates the **Git Flow-based release branching model** commonly used in industry and referenced in KTU Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MAIN[main branch: stable production] --> R1[release v2.7 branch]
    R1 --> R1A[hotfix patches]
    R1A --> R1
    R1 --> R1B[bump version to 2.7.3]
    R1B --> MAIN
    R1B --> TAG1[Tag: v2.7.3]
    FEAT[feature branch: feat X] --> DEV[develop branch]
    DEV --> R1
    HOT[hotfix branch: critical fix] --> MAIN
    HOT --> DEV
    DEV --> R2[next release v2.8 branch]
    R2 --> R2A[merge approved features]
    R2A --> MAIN
    R2A --> TAG2[Tag: v2.8.0]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** `main` always holds production-ready code. `develop` integrates new features. `release/*` branches are code-frozen for stabilization. `hotfix/*` branches patch production directly. Tags like `v2.7.3` are immutable release markers.

### 4.3 Relationship Between Release, Change, and Configuration Management (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CR[Change Request Raised] --> CE[Change Evaluation by CAB]
    CE -- Rejected --> REJ[Rejected and Logged]
    CE -- Approved --> CC[Configuration Item Updated]
    CC --> CI[Baseline Re-established]
    CI --> RM[Release Management Picks Up Approved CIs]
    RM --> BUILD[Build and Test Release]
    BUILD --> DEPLOY[Deploy to Production]
    DEPLOY --> AUDIT[Audit Trail Recorded]
    AUDIT --> REPO[Configuration Management Repository]
    REPO --> CI
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The three disciplines form a closed loop. Change Management approves → Configuration Management tracks the CI change → Release Management delivers it → audit feeds back to configuration repository. This closed loop is what guarantees **traceability** and **reproducibility** in regulated industries (BFSI, healthcare, defense).

### 4.4 Blue-Green vs. Canary vs. Rolling Deployment Strategies (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BlueGreen[Blue Green Strategy]
        BG_USERS[User Traffic] --> BG_LB[Load Balancer]
        BG_LB -- All Traffic --> BG_GREEN[Green Environment v2]
        BG_LB -. Idle Standby .-> BG_BLUE[Blue Environment v1]
        BG_GREEN -- Healthy --> BG_SWITCH[Instant Switch Back to Blue on Failure]
        BG_BLUE --> BG_SWITCH
    end

    subgraph Canary[Canary Strategy]
        CN_USERS[User Traffic] --> CN_LB[Load Balancer]
        CN_LB -- 95 percent --> CN_STABLE[Stable Environment v1]
        CN_LB -- 5 percent --> CN_CANARY[Canary Environment v2]
        CN_CANARY -- Healthy --> CN_PROMOTE[Gradually Increase to 25 50 100 percent]
        CN_CANARY -- Failing --> CN_REVERT[Revert Traffic to Stable]
    end

    subgraph Rolling[Rolling Strategy]
        RL_USERS[User Traffic] --> RL_LB[Load Balancer]
        RL_LB --> RL_NODE1[Node 1: v1]
        RL_LB --> RL_NODE2[Node 2: v1]
        RL_LB --> RL_NODE3[Node 3: v2]
        RL_LB --> RL_NODE4[Node 4: v2]
        RL_NODE1 -. Update In Place .-> RL_NODE1B[Node 1: v2]
        RL_NODE2 -. Update In Place .-> RL_NODE2B[Node 2: v2]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** 
> - **Blue-Green** is best for *zero-downtime* and *instant rollback* (mission-critical systems).
> - **Canary** is best for *risk-sensitive* deployments where you want real user feedback before full rollout.
> - **Rolling** is best for *stateless, horizontally scaled* systems (microservices, web apps).
> 
> KTU 2024 expects students to *contrast* these strategies and recommend one for a given scenario.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define Release Management. List any four activities performed under Release Management.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

Release Management is the process of planning, scheduling, building, testing, deploying, and supporting software releases to production environments in a controlled and traceable manner. It is a sub-discipline of Software Configuration Management that ensures every release is **reproducible, documented, and audited**.

The four key activities are:

1. **Release Planning** — Defining the scope, schedule, and contents of an upcoming release
2. **Build and Version Management** — Compiling source code into versioned, signed artifacts
3. **Testing and Quality Assurance** — Verifying that the build meets acceptance criteria
4. **Deployment and Distribution** — Promoting the verified build to production with a documented rollback plan

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Differentiate between Release Management and Change Management.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

| Basis | Release Management | Change Management |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Delivers a verified, versioned build to an environment | Approves, tracks, and controls all changes to the system |
| **Trigger** | Scheduled release date or approved change set | Bug report, enhancement request, or risk |
| **Output** | Deployed release with release notes | Approved/Rejected change record |
| **Key Role** | Release Manager | Change Advisory Board (CAB) |
| **Relationship** | Operates *after* Change Management approves changes | Operates *before* Release Management builds and deploys |

In short, Change Management decides **what** to change; Release Management decides **how** and **when** to deliver it.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**

### **Option A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the phases of the Release Management process model with a neat block diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Release Management process model consists of **five sequential phases**, each with defined inputs, activities, outputs, and exit criteria. They are:

**Phase 1 — Release Planning:**
- *Inputs:* Product roadmap, approved change requests, capacity plan
- *Activities:* Scope definition, calendar scheduling, dependency mapping, environment allocation, risk assessment
- *Outputs:* Release plan document, release calendar, resource allocation matrix
- *Exit Criteria:* Stakeholder sign-off, environment availability confirmed

**Phase 2 — Build & Configuration:**
- *Inputs:* Versioned source code, dependencies, build scripts
- *Activities:* Branching, code freeze, compile, package, version tagging, artifact signing
- *Outputs:* Build artifacts with checksums, build manifest
- *Exit Criteria:* Reproducible build verified by checksum match

**Phase 3 — Testing & QA:**
- *Inputs:* Build artifacts, test plans, test data
- *Activities:* Smoke, regression, UAT, performance, security testing
- *Outputs:* Test reports, defect logs, release approval form
- *Exit Criteria:* Zero P0/P1 defects, UAT sign-off obtained

**Phase 4 — Deployment:**
- *Inputs:* Approved release, deployment plan, rollback plan
- *Activities:* Environment promotion (Dev → Test → Stage → Prod), installation, smoke tests in production, stakeholder communication
- *Outputs:* Deployed system, deployment log, release notes
- *Exit Criteria:* Production health checks passed, rollback procedure validated

**Phase 5 — Post-Release Support:**
- *Inputs:* Production monitoring data, incident reports
- *Activities:* Hyper-care, incident triage, retrospective, archival
- *Outputs:* Post-release review report, improvement backlog
- *Exit Criteria:* SLA compliance verified, no open critical incidents

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1: Planning] --> P2[Phase 2: Build]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Testing]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: Deployment]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5: Post Release]
    P5 --> FEED[Lessons Learned]
    FEED --> P1
```

---

**(b) Explain Semantic Versioning with an example. Compute the precedence order of the following versions: `1.0.0-alpha`, `1.0.0-alpha.1`, `1.0.0-alpha.beta`, `1.0.0-beta`, `1.0.0-beta.2`, `1.0.0-beta.11`, `1.0.0-rc.1`, `1.0.0`. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Semantic Versioning (SemVer 2.0.0):**

Semantic Versioning is a standardized versioning scheme that conveys meaning through version numbers. A version is represented as:

$$V = M.m.p$$

- **MAJOR ($M$):** Incremented for incompatible API changes
- **MINOR ($m$):** Incremented for backward-compatible new features
- **PATCH ($p$):** Incremented for backward-compatible bug fixes

Pre-release versions use a hyphen suffix: $V_{\text{pre}} = M.m.p-\text{label}$

**Example:** `2.7.3` means: 2nd major version (breaking change from v1.x), 7th minor release (7 feature additions), 3rd patch (3 bug fixes).

**Precedence Computation:**

According to SemVer 2.0, precedence is determined by:
1. Major, Minor, Patch are compared numerically first
2. A version *with* pre-release has **lower** precedence than one *without*
3. Pre-release identifiers are compared dot-by-dot
4. Numeric identifiers compared numerically, alphanumeric compared ASCII

**Step 1:** All versions have `1.0.0` base → no difference there

**Step 2:** Separate releases with and without pre-release tag

- Without pre-release: `1.0.0` (highest)
- With pre-release: all others (lower)

**Step 3:** Compare pre-release identifiers among the 7 versions

First segment comparison:
- `alpha` < `beta` < `rc` (alphabetical)

So `alpha` versions come first, then `beta`, then `rc`.

Within `alpha` group: `alpha` < `alpha.1` < `alpha.beta` (numeric < alphanumeric when first differs, then `1` < `beta`)

Within `beta` group: `beta` < `beta.2` < `beta.11` (numeric comparison: $2 < 11$)

**Final Ascending Precedence Order:**

$$
1.0.0\text{-alpha} \; < \; 1.0.0\text{-alpha}.1 \; < \; 1.0.0\text{-alpha}.beta \; < \; 1.0.0\text{-beta} \; < \; 1.0.0\text{-beta}.2 \; < \; 1.0.0\text{-beta}.11 \; < \; 1.0.0\text{-rc}.1 \; < \; 1.0.0
$$

**Key Insight:** The version `1.0.0-beta.2` has *lower* precedence than `1.0.0-beta.11` because numeric identifiers are compared as numbers ($2 < 11$), not as strings. The version `1.0.0-alpha.1` has *higher* precedence than `1.0.0-alpha` because the second identifier `1` is numerically greater than the implicit empty/zero, and longer dot-separated sequences take precedence.

---

### **Option B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a) Explain the DORA Four Key Metrics used to measure Release Management performance. For a team that deploys 8 releases per month with 2 failures and an average recovery time of 45 minutes, compute the Change Failure Rate and MTTR. Comment on the team's performance. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**DORA Four Key Metrics:**

The DevOps Research and Assessment (DORA) framework identifies four metrics that measure software delivery performance:

1. **Deployment Frequency (DF):** How often an organization successfully releases changes to production
2. **Lead Time for Changes (LT):** Time from code commit to code successfully running in production
3. **Change Failure Rate (CFR):** Percentage of changes to production that result in degraded service or require remediation
4. **Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR):** How long it takes to restore service after a production incident

**Performance Bands (per DORA research):**

| Metric | Elite | High | Medium | Low |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Deployment Frequency | On-demand (multiple per day) | Weekly–Monthly | Monthly–Bi-annually | > 6 months |
| Lead Time for Changes | $<1$ day | 1 day – 1 week | 1 week – 1 month | $>1$ month |
| Change Failure Rate | 0–15% | 16–30% | 31–45% | $>45\%$ |
| MTTR | $<1$ hour | $<1$ day | $<1$ week | $>1$ week |

**Computation for Given Scenario:**

Given: $N_{\text{deploys}} = 8$ releases/month, $F_{\text{fail}} = 2$, $T_{\text{recovery, avg}} = 45$ minutes.

**Change Failure Rate (CFR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CFR} &= \frac{F_{\text{fail}}}{R_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2}{8} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
&= 25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTR} &= \frac{\sum T_{\text{recovery}}}{N_{\text{incidents}}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{45}{1} \; \text{minute (given as average)} \\[6pt]
&= 45 \; \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Performance Commentary:**

- **CFR = 25%** falls in the **"High"** performer band (16–30%). It is acceptable but not elite. Improvement actions: strengthen pre-release testing, increase code review rigor, implement canary deployments.

- **MTTR = 45 minutes** falls in the **"Elite"** band ($<1$ hour). The team has excellent incident response, monitoring, and rollback procedures.

- **Deployment Frequency = 8/month ≈ 2/week** falls in the **"High"** band (weekly–monthly), indicating a mature CI/CD pipeline.

- **Lead Time for Changes** was not provided but should be calculated from commit-to-deploy timestamps to fully assess the team.

**Overall Verdict:** The team is a **High performer** with strong recovery capability, but should focus on reducing the change failure rate through improved testing and progressive delivery techniques.

---

**(b) Compare Blue-Green, Canary, and Rolling deployment strategies. For a banking application with zero tolerance for downtime, which strategy is most suitable and why? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Blue-Green | Canary | Rolling |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Two identical production environments (Blue = current, Green = new) | One production + one small subset (5%) running new version | In-place update of nodes one-by-one |
| **Rollback Speed** | Instant (switch load balancer back) | Fast (route traffic away from canary) | Slow (must re-deploy old version) |
| **Risk Level** | Low (full validation in idle env) | Very Low (blast radius limited) | Medium (incremental exposure) |
| **Resource Cost** | High (2x infrastructure) | Low (small extra capacity) | Low (reuse existing) |
| **Downtime** | Zero | Zero | Zero |
| **Feedback Loop** | Limited (only after full cutover) | Real user feedback at small scale | Real user feedback per node |
| **Best For** | Mission-critical, regulated | Feature experimentation | Stateless web apps, microservices |
| **Complexity** | Medium | High (traffic routing logic) | Medium |

**Recommendation for Banking Application:**

The most suitable strategy is **Blue-Green Deployment**.

**Justification:**

1. **Zero downtime requirement:** Blue-Green allows instant traffic switch from Blue (current) to Green (new) without service interruption — critical for banking where every second of downtime costs money and reputation.

2. **Instant rollback:** If any transaction anomaly is detected in Green, the load balancer can be switched back to Blue in seconds, reverting to the known-stable version.

3. **Full validation in a production-like environment:** Green can be smoke-tested with synthetic transactions before any real customer traffic is routed, ensuring the deployment is proven safe.

4. **Regulatory compliance:** Banking regulators (RBI, SEBI in the Indian context) require auditable, reversible deployments. Blue-Green provides a clear, auditable cutover event.

5. **Disaster recovery:** If Green fails catastrophically, Blue continues to serve customers without impact.

**Why not Canary?** Canary releases expose a subset of users to risk. In banking, even 5% of users experiencing a broken transaction screen or failed payment can cause regulatory complaints and reputational damage. The risk is asymmetric.

**Why not Rolling?** Rolling updates have a transient mixed-version state where some nodes run the new code and others the old. In banking, this can cause *consistency bugs* in distributed transactions, which is unacceptable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Common Mistake #1 (1–2 marks lost):** Students often list phases but forget to mention **gate criteria** at each phase. Always end each phase with an explicit exit condition.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2 (1–2 marks lost):** When computing SemVer precedence, students often treat `beta.2` and `beta.11` as strings (giving wrong order `beta.11 < beta.2`). Remember: **numeric segments are compared as numbers**.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3 (1–2 marks lost):** Students confuse Release Management with Change Management. Always clarify that Change Management **approves** and Release Management **delivers**.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4 (2–3 marks lost):** In deployment strategy questions, students recommend a strategy without justifying it with **risk vs. cost vs. rollback** trade-offs. Always include the trade-off reasoning.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5 (1 mark lost):** In computation questions, units are often omitted. Always state "25%" or "45 minutes", not just "25" or "45".

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 5 times before the exam.**

- [ ] **Definition:** Release Management is the end-to-end process of planning, building, testing, deploying, and supporting software releases.
- [ ] **Five Phases:** Planning → Build & Configuration → Testing & QA → Deployment → Post-Release Support
- [ ] **Gate Criteria:** Every phase has explicit entry/exit conditions; a failed gate triggers a corrective loop.
- [ ] **SemVer Format:** MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH (e.g., 2.7.3)
- [ ] **SemVer Precedence:** Numeric segments compared as numbers; alphanumeric compared as strings; a version with pre-release has LOWER precedence than one without.
- [ ] **Release Types:** Major, Minor, Patch, Service Pack, Emergency, Canary, Blue-Green, Rolling
- [ ] **Blue-Green:** Two parallel environments, instant cutover, instant rollback
- [ ] **Canary:** Small percentage (5%) gets new version, gradual promotion
- [ ] **Rolling:** Node-by-node in-place update, zero downtime
- [ ] **DORA Four Metrics:** Deployment Frequency, Lead Time, Change Failure Rate, MTTR
- [ ] **Key Formulas:**
  - $\text{CFR} = \dfrac{F_{\text{fail}}}{R_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$
  - $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum T_{\text{recovery}}}{N_{\text{incidents}}}$
  - $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{T_{\text{operational}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$
  - $\text{DF} = \dfrac{N_{\text{deploys}}}{T_{\text{period}}}$
  - $\text{LT} = T_{\text{deploy}} - T_{\text{commit}}$
  - $\text{RE} = P(\text{Failure}) \times C(\text{Consequence})$
- [ ] **Release vs Change vs Configuration Management:**
  - Change Management = decides **what** to change
  - Configuration Management = tracks **how** baselines change
  - Release Management = delivers **the result** of approved changes
- [ ] **Required Documents:** Release Notes, Release Plan, Build Manifest, Deployment Guide, Rollback Plan, Sign-off Form, Post-Release Review
- [ ] **Risk Mitigation:** Always have a tested rollback plan; use feature flags for risky features
- [ ] **Reproducibility:** Build artifacts must have a checksum (SHA-256) for tamper detection and reproducibility verification
- [ ] **Closed-Loop Traceability:** Every release must be traceable from requirement → code → build → test → deploy → production
- [ ] **Audit Trail:** All release activities must be logged for compliance (ISO 27001, SOC 2, GDPR)
- [ ] **DORA Elite Benchmarks:** DF = on-demand, LT = <1 day, CFR = 0–15%, MTTR = <1 hour
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Agile Software Management - SCRUM Framework. Kanban Methodology and Lean Approaches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET524 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Agile Software Management - SCRUM Framework. Kanban Methodology and Lean Approaches # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Agile Software Management **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** *Agile Software Management* is an iterative and incremental approach to software project management that emphasizes **adaptive planning, evolutionary delivery, and continuous improvement**. It is grounded in the *Agile Manifesto (2001)*, which prioritizes *individuals and interactions* over processes and tools, *working software* over comprehensive documentation, *customer collaboration* over contract negotiation, and *responding to change* over following a plan. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Agile management replaces the rigid, plan-driven **Waterfall** paradigm with a **value-driven, feedback-oriented** cycle. It is the de-facto industry standard (used by >70% of organizations per the *Stack Overflow Developer Survey 2023*) and is therefore a high-weightage module in the ESE. ## 1.2 SCRUM Framework **Formal Definition:** *SCRUM* is a lightweight **agile framework** that helps teams, developers, and organizations create value through adaptive solutions for complex problems. It employs an iterative and incremental approach, optimizing predictability and controlling risk through regular *Sprints* of fixed duration (typically **2–4 weeks**). > [!NOTE] > **SCRUM Origin:** Coined by *Ken Schwaber* and *Jeff Sutherland* in the early 1990s, formalized in the *Scrum Guide*. The name derives from *Rugby* — a team collectively moves the ball forward. **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Think of SCRUM as a **relay race where the team runs short, fast sprints** instead of one long marathon. - The **Product Owner** is the *coach* deciding the race strategy and which laps matter most. - The **Scrum Master** is the *referee* who ensures no one cheats and the track is clear. - The **Development Team** is the *team of runners*. - The **Sprint** is a single *lap* (2–4 weeks) that must end with a working demo. - The **Daily Stand-up** is a quick *huddle* to ask: "What did you run yesterday? What will you run today? Any obstacle?" After every lap (Sprint), they show the **Increment** (a finished, usable piece of software) to stakeholders and re-plan the next lap. This loop continues until the full product is ready. ## 1.3 Kanban Methodology **Formal Definition:** *Kanban* is a **visual workflow management method** originating from the Japanese *Toyota Production System (TPS)*. In software engineering, it uses a **Kanban Board** to visualize work, limit *Work-In-Progress (WIP)*, and maximize flow efficiency. It is **continuous-flow** (no fixed iterations) and **evolutionary** (no prescribed roles). > [!NOTE] > **"Kanban" literally means "visual signal" or "card"** in Japanese. The foundational text is *David J. Anderson's "Kanban" (2010)*. **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a **grocery store checkout line**. There are 5 counters. To avoid chaos, the manager says: *"Only 20 customers can be in line at any time."* This **WIP limit** prevents overloading, ensures faster checkout (cycle time), and reveals bottlenecks. The Kanban board works identically for software tasks. ## 1.4 Lean Software Development **Formal Definition:** *Lean Software Development* is an **agile methodology** derived from *Lean Manufacturing* principles (Womack & Jones, 1996) and applied to software by *Mary and Tom Poppendieck* (2003). It is founded on **7 principles** aimed at eliminating waste (*Muda*), amplifying learning, deciding late, delivering fast, empowering the team, building integrity in, and seeing the whole. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Key Term:** Lean is the *philosophical parent* of both SCRUM and Kanban — it provides the underlying *values*, while SCRUM/Kanban are the *practices*. **Conceptual Analogy:** Lean is like a **chef running a kitchen**. Instead of cooking everything in advance (and wasting food), the chef: - Cooks **only what is ordered** (eliminate over-production waste). - Keeps the **counter clean** (eliminate partially done work). - **Tastes constantly** (amplify learning / build-integrity). - **Buys fresh daily** (defer commitment / decide late). - Doesn't cook **extra dishes no one ordered** (deliver only value). --- ## 1.5 Relationship Diagram (Verbal) - **Lean** = Philosophy (the *Why* — maximize value, minimize waste) - **Kanban** = Method (the *How* — visualize & limit WIP) - **SCRUM** = Framework (the *What & When* — fixed Sprints, roles, events) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The SCRUM Framework — Detailed Components ### 2.1.1 SCRUM Roles (The Three Pillars of Accountability) | Role | Responsibility | Authority | |---|---|---| | **Product Owner (PO)** | Manages *Product Backlog*; maximizes product value; single voice of stakeholders. | Final say on *what* gets built and priority order. | | **Scrum Master (SM)** | Servant-leader; removes impediments; enforces SCRUM rules; coaches the team. | No authority over content, only process. | | **Developers (Dev Team)** | Cross-functional (3–9 members); turns backlog items into *Increments*. | Self-manages; collectively owns the work. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Three Pillars of SCRUM (per Scrum Guide):** **Transparency**, **Inspection**, **Adaptation**. ### 2.1.2 SCRUM Events (5 Ceremonies) 1. **The Sprint** (Container event; **2–4 weeks**, fixed duration; "no changes that endanger the Sprint Goal"). 2. **Sprint Planning** (Max 8 hours for a 4-week sprint; answers *What* can be done and *How*). 3. **Daily Scrum / Stand-up** (**15 minutes**, same time/place; 3 questions). 4. **Sprint Review** (Max 4 hours for 4-week sprint; inspect the *Increment* with stakeholders). 5. **Sprint Retrospective** (Max 3 hours for 4-week sprint; team inspects *itself* and plans improvements). ### 2.1.3 SCRUM Artifacts (3) 1. **Product Backlog** — Ordered list of *everything* that might be needed; owned by PO. 2. **Sprint Backlog** — Set of Product Backlog items selected for the Sprint + the plan to deliver them + the *Sprint Goal*. 3. **Increment** — The usable, potentially shippable sum of all Product Backlog items completed during a Sprint. Must meet the **Definition of Done (DoD)**. ### 2.1.4 The Definition of Done (DoD) A shared, agreed-upon checklist (e.g., code-reviewed, unit-tested, integrated, documented) that any Increment MUST satisfy to be considered "Done." ## 2.2 The Kanban Methodology — Detailed Mechanics ### 2.2.1 Core Practices (4 Foundational + 3 Cadence) **Foundational:** 1. **Visualize the workflow** (Kanban Board with columns: *To-Do → In-Progress → Testing → Done*). 2. **Limit Work-In-Progress (WIP)** — Hard cap on items per column. 3. **Manage and measure flow** — Use metrics like *Lead Time* and *Cycle Time*. 4. **Make process policies explicit** — Rules for moving cards are written and visible. **Cadence-oriented:** 5. **Implement feedback loops** (e.g., Replenishment Meeting, Delivery Planning). 6. **Improve collaboratively, evolve experimentally** (Kaizen). ### 2.2.2 Kanban Metrics | Metric | Definition | Engineering Use | |---|---|---| | **Lead Time (LT)** | Time from request creation to delivery. | Predictability for stakeholders. | | **Cycle Time (CT)** | Time from work-started to work-completed. | Bottleneck identification. | | **WIP** | Number of items currently being worked on. | Flow efficiency. | | **Throughput** | Items completed per unit time. | Capacity planning. | > [!NOTE] > **Little's Law** (queueing theory) governs Kanban flow: > $$L = \lambda \cdot W$$ > where $L$ = average WIP, $\lambda$ = average throughput rate, $W$ = average cycle time. This is the *mathematical backbone* of Kanban — increase $\lambda$ or decrease $W$ to reduce $L$. ## 2.3 Lean Software Development — The 7 Principles | # | Principle | Application to Software | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Eliminate Waste (*Muda*)** | Remove unused features, unnecessary code, handoffs, waiting. | | 2 | **Amplify Learning** | Use short iterations, pair programming, TDD. | | 3 | **Decide as Late as Possible** | Delay irreversible decisions until the last responsible moment (options thinking). | | 4 | **Deliver as Fast as Possible** | Short cycles → fast feedback. | | 5 | **Empower the Team** | Self-organizing teams, no micromanagement. | | 6 | **Build Integrity In** | Perceived + Conceptual integrity; refactoring; TDD. | | 7 | **See the Whole** | Optimize the whole system, not local sub-optimization. | ## 2.4 The 7 Wastes of Lean (*Muda*) — KTU Hot Topic 1. **Partially Done Work** (inventory) 2. **Extra Features** (over-production) 3. **Relearning** (rework) 4. **Handoffs** (transportation) 5. **Delays** (waiting) 6. **Task Switching** (motion) 7. **Defects** (correction) ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Key Constant / Unit | Applicability | |---|---|---|---| | **Scrum Team Size** | $3 \le n \le 9$ | members (optimal) | Team sizing per Scrum Guide | | **Sprint Duration** | $2 \le T_{Sprint} \le 4$ | weeks | Standard sprint length | | **Daily Scrum** | $T_{DS} \le 15$ | minutes | Hard cap per event | | **Velocity** | $V = \dfrac{\sum SP_{Done}}{N_{Sprints}}$ | Story Points / Sprint | Capacity planning | | **Burndown** | $Y_i = T_0 - \dfrac{T_0}{N} \cdot i$ | Ideal line: $Y_i$ remaining | Sprint tracking | | **Little's Law** | $L = \lambda \cdot W$ | $L$ = WIP, $\lambda$ = throughput, $W$ = cycle time | Kanban flow | | **Lead Time** | $LT = Wait + CT$ | time units | Kanban delivery | | **Cumulative Flow** | $CFD_{done}(t)$ | increasing monotonically | Kanban health check | | **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $DRE = \dfrac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre}+D_{post}} \times 100$ | percentage | Quality metric in Lean | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Exam Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Lead Time** (request → delivery) with **Cycle Time** (start → done). In SCRUM contexts, the more common metric is **Velocity**; in Kanban contexts, it is **Lead/Cycle Time**. ## 2.6 Why These Frameworks Matter in Engineering - **SCRUM** is the most widely used agile framework globally (used by Spotify, Amazon, Google for many products). - **Kanban** is preferred for **support/maintenance** and **DevOps/CI-CD** pipelines where continuous flow matters more than fixed iterations. - **Lean** underlies **DevOps culture**, **continuous delivery**, and **SAFe (Scaled Agile Framework)** — understanding Lean is essential for any engineer entering a modern product company. --- ## 2.7 Comparative Analysis: SCRUM vs. Kanban vs. Lean | Dimension | SCRUM | Kanban | Lean | |---|---|---|---| | **Origin** | Software (Schwaber & Sutherland) | Manufacturing → Software (Anderson) | Manufacturing (Toyota) | | **Iteration** | Fixed Sprints | Continuous flow | Continuous flow | | **Roles** | Prescribed (PO, SM, Dev) | None prescribed | None prescribed | | **Board** | Resets every Sprint | Persistent | Optional | | **Planning** | Sprint Planning | Replenishment meeting | Pull-based | | **WIP Limits** | Implicit (Sprint scope) | Explicit (per column) | Implicit (just-in-time) | | **Change** | Discouraged mid-Sprint | Allowed anytime | Encouraged | | **Best for** | New product development | Maintenance, support, ops | Whole-organization transformation | | **Metrics** | Velocity, Burndown | Lead/Cycle Time, CFD | DRE, Flow efficiency | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples ## 3.1 Derivation: The Ideal Burndown Equation A *Burndown Chart* plots the **remaining work** (Y-axis) against **time** (X-axis) within a Sprint. The *ideal* line assumes work is removed at a constant rate. **Step 1 — Define Variables.** Let $T_0$ = total remaining work at the *start* of the Sprint (in story points or ideal hours). Let $N$ = total number of working days in the Sprint. Let $i$ = the current day number, where $i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N$. **Step 2 — Establish the Linear Assumption.** Under the *ideal* scenario, work is removed at a uniform rate, i.e., $\frac{T_0}{N}$ units per day. **Step 3 — Compute Remaining Work on Day $i$.** The amount burned down by day $i$ is $\frac{T_0}{N} \cdot i$. Therefore, the remaining work $Y_i$ is: $$ \begin{aligned} Y_i &= T_0 - \underbrace{\frac{T_0}{N} \cdot i}_{\text{amount already completed}} \\ &= T_0 \left(1 - \frac{i}{N}\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Boundary Conditions (Verification).** - At $i = 0$ (start of Sprint): $Y_0 = T_0(1 - 0) = T_0$ ✓ (all work remains) - At $i = N$ (end of Sprint): $Y_N = T_0(1 - 1) = 0$ ✓ (all work done) **Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example.** Suppose $T_0 = 60$ story points and $N = 10$ days. Then the ideal burndown equation is $Y_i = 60 - 6i$. | Day ($i$) | Ideal Remaining ($Y_i$) | |---|---| | 0 | 60 | | 2 | 48 | | 4 | 36 | | 6 | 24 | | 8 | 12 | | 10 | 0 | > [!NOTE] > **Real Burndown Interpretation:** If the *actual* line lies *above* the ideal → team is behind schedule. If *below* → team is ahead. ## 3.2 Derivation: Velocity from Past Sprints **Velocity** is the *average* amount of work (in story points) a team completes per Sprint, calculated over the last 3–5 Sprints. $$ \begin{aligned} V &= \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{m} SP_k}{m} \\ \text{where } m &= \text{number of recent sprints (typically 3 to 5)} \\ SP_k &= \text{story points completed in sprint } k \end{aligned} $$ **Worked Example.** A team's last 3 Sprints completed: 28, 32, 30 story points. $$ \begin{aligned} V &= \frac{28 + 32 + 30}{3} \\ &= \frac{90}{3} \\ &= 30 \text{ story points / sprint} \end{aligned} $$ This $V = 30$ is then used to forecast the next release: e.g., if 300 SP of remaining backlog exists, the projected release horizon is $300 / 30 = 10$ sprints. ## 3.3 Derivation: Little's Law Applied to a Kanban System Little's Law states that the long-run average number of items in a system ($L$) equals the long-run average arrival rate ($\lambda$) multiplied by the average time each item spends in the system ($W$). $$ \begin{aligned} L &= \lambda \cdot W \\ \Rightarrow W &= \frac{L}{\lambda} \\ \Rightarrow \lambda &= \frac{L}{W} \end{aligned} $$ **Worked Example.** A Kanban board has a WIP limit of $L = 4$ items. The team's historical throughput is $\lambda = 2$ items / week. What is the average cycle time? $$ \begin{aligned} W &= \frac{L}{\lambda} \\ &= \frac{4 \text{ items}}{2 \text{ items/week}} \\ &= 2 \text{ weeks} \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering Implication:** To halve the cycle time (deliver twice as fast) without changing team size, the WIP limit must be halved to $L = 2$. This is the *Kaizen* principle in action. ## 3.4 Worked Example: Defect Removal Efficiency (Lean Metric) $$ \begin{aligned} DRE &= \frac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}} \times 100 \\ \text{where } D_{pre} &= \text{defects found before release} \\ D_{post} &= \text{defects found after release} \end{aligned} $$ Suppose 90 defects were caught in pre-release testing and 10 escaped to production: $$ \begin{aligned} DRE &= \frac{90}{90 + 10} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{90}{100} \times 100 \\ &= 90\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Valuation Note:** Always show units (%) in the final answer and state the assumption that $DRE \rightarrow 100\%$ is the ideal (zero post-release defects). ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Framework | Project Scenario | Recommended Framework | Justification | |---|---|---| | New mobile app, 6-month build, dedicated team | **SCRUM** | Fixed iterations, clear roles, stakeholder demos. | | Legacy CRM bug-fix & support tickets | **Kanban** | Continuous flow, varying priorities, WIP limits prevent overload. | | Enterprise-wide DevOps transformation | **Lean** | Holistic, cultural, focuses on waste reduction across org. | | Startup MVP with rapidly changing requirements | **SCRUM** (or *Scrumban*) | Sprint reviews re-align features every 2 weeks. | | 24/7 NOC monitoring & ops team | **Kanban** | Continuous, no sprint boundaries, throughput-optimized. | ## 3.6 Sprint Goal vs. Sprint Backlog vs. Product Backlog — Distinction | Term | Scope | Owner | Lifetime | |---|---|---|---| | **Product Backlog** | All features/wishes for the product | Product Owner | Living document | | **Sprint Backlog** | Items selected for *this* Sprint + plan | Developers | One Sprint | | **Sprint Goal** | A *single sentence* statement of the Sprint's purpose | Scrum Team | One Sprint | | **Increment** | Sum of all Done items this Sprint | Developers | Cumulative | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SCRUM Framework — End-to-End Lifecycle Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Product Backlog
Owned by Product Owner] --> B[Sprint Planning
Max 8 hours] B --> C[Sprint Backlog
Scope of current Sprint] C --> D[Sprint
2 to 4 weeks container] D --> E[Daily Scrum
15 minutes huddle] E --> D D --> F{Increment
Meets Definition of Done?} F -- No --> D F -- Yes --> G[Sprint Review
Showcase to stakeholders] G --> H[Sprint Retrospective
Team inspects itself] H --> I[Backlog Refinement
Continuous] I --> A H --> B styleA fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#2563eb,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#db2777,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The outer loop (A → B → D → G → I → A) is the *Product* lifecycle. The inner loop (D → E → D) is the *Daily* feedback. The terminal block (H → B) closes the Sprint with learning that flows into the next Sprint. ## 4.2 Kanban Board — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Backlog[BACKLOG] B1[Request 1] B2[Request 2] B3[Request 3] end subgraph ToDo[TO DO WIP 3] T1[Task A] T2[Task B] end subgraph InProg[IN PROGRESS WIP 2] P1[Task C] P2[Task D] end subgraph Testing[TESTING WIP 2] Q1[Task E] end subgraph Done[DONE] X1[Released] X2[Released] end Backlog --> ToDo ToDo --> InProg InProg --> Testing Testing --> Done style ToDo fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#ca8a04 style InProg fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#ea580c style Testing fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#be185d style Done fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **WIP Limit Enforcement:** In the diagram, columns have *hard caps* (e.g., IN PROGRESS = 2). When full, the team MUST stop pulling new work and instead help clear the bottleneck — this is the *core anti-waste* mechanism of Kanban. ## 4.3 Lean — 7 Principles as a Layered Model ```mermaid flowchart TD L1[Lean Thinking
Maximize Customer Value] L2[See the Whole System] L3[Build Integrity In] L4[Empower the Team] L5[Deliver as Fast as Possible] L6[Decide as Late as Possible] L7[Amplify Learning] L8[Eliminate Waste MUDA] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 L5 --> L6 L6 --> L7 L7 --> L8 L8 -.feedback.-> L1 style L1 fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff style L8 fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: Agile Family Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Parent[Lean Philosophy
Toyota Production System] Parent --> Kanban[Kanban Method
Visualize, Limit WIP, Flow] Parent --> Scrum[SCRUM Framework
Roles, Events, Artifacts] Parent --> XP[Extreme Programming XP
Engineering Practices] Parent --> SAFe[SAFe LeSS Nexus
Scaled Frameworks] Kanban --> Scrumban[Scrumban Hybrid] Scrum --> Scrumban XP --> Scrumban style Parent fill:#312e81,stroke:#4338ca,color:#ffffff style Scrum fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff style Kanban fill:#166534,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style Scrumban fill:#a16207,stroke:#ca8a04,color:#ffffff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Takeaway:** SCRUM, Kanban, and Lean are **not competitors** — they are *complementary* tools drawn from the same Lean philosophy. Mature organizations often adopt **Scrumban** (SCRUM cadence + Kanban flow) for hybrid flexibility. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define the SCRUM framework. List the three SCRUM roles and state one responsibility of each.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **SCRUM** is a lightweight, iterative, and incremental agile framework used to manage complex software product development. It is built on three pillars: *Transparency, Inspection, and Adaptation*, and delivers value through time-boxed iterations called **Sprints** (2–4 weeks). [1 mark] The three roles are: [2 marks] 1. **Product Owner (PO):** Owns the Product Backlog; prioritizes features to maximize business value; single voice of the customer. 2. **Scrum Master (SM):** Servant-leader who removes impediments and ensures SCRUM rules are followed; coaches the team. 3. **Developers:** A cross-functional team of 3–9 members that turns backlog items into a potentially shippable Increment. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Explain the term "Work-In-Progress (WIP) limit" in Kanban. Why is it important?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **WIP limit** is a *hard numerical cap* placed on the number of items that can exist in any given workflow stage (column) of the Kanban board at one time. [1 mark] **Importance:** [2 marks] 1. **Prevents Overload:** Limits context-switching and multi-tasking waste. 2. **Reveals Bottlenecks:** When a column hits its WIP cap, work piles up at the previous step, visually exposing constraints. 3. **Improves Flow & Cycle Time:** Fewer items in progress → faster completion of each (governed by Little's Law: $L = \lambda \cdot W$). > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Writing "WIP limit improves quality" — although true indirectly, the *direct* impact is on **flow and cycle time**, not quality. Quality is governed by DoD and testing practices. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain the **SCRUM framework** in detail. Discuss the **three roles, five events, and three artifacts** with their purpose. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Differentiate between **SCRUM and Kanban**. Under what conditions would you recommend Kanban over SCRUM in a software project? Justify with a real-world example. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — SCRUM Framework (7 Marks)** SCRUM is an agile project management framework that organizes work into **Sprints** of fixed 2–4 week duration. It is designed for **complex, evolving product development** and relies on self-managing teams. **1. Three Roles (2 Marks):** - **Product Owner (PO):** Manages the *Product Backlog*; defines user stories; prioritizes by *business value*; accepts/rejects Increment. - **Scrum Master (SM):** *Servant-leader*; facilitates all ceremonies; removes blockers; protects team from external interference; enforces time-boxes. - **Developers:** Cross-functional team of 3–9 people who deliver the *Increment*; they collectively estimate, commit, and own the work. **2. Five Events (3 Marks):** | Event | Duration (4-week sprint) | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Sprint** | 2–4 weeks | Container for all other events; ends with a usable Increment. | | **Sprint Planning** | ≤ 8 hours | Decide *what* (Sprint Goal + selected PBIs) and *how* (plan). | | **Daily Scrum** | ≤ 15 min | Synchronize; 3 Qs: yesterday / today / impediments. | | **Sprint Review** | ≤ 4 hours | Demo Increment to stakeholders; adapt Product Backlog. | | **Sprint Retrospective** | ≤ 3 hours | Inspect the *team itself*; plan improvements for next Sprint. | **3. Three Artifacts (2 Marks):** - **Product Backlog:** Ordered list of *all* desired features, bugs, tech debt. Owned by PO. Constantly refined. - **Sprint Backlog:** PBIs selected for *this* Sprint + the *Sprint Goal* + a *plan* (often a task board) to deliver them. Owned by Developers. - **Increment:** The sum of *all* PBIs completed during the Sprint that meet the **Definition of Done (DoD)**. Must be potentially releasable. **Conclusion:** SCRUM succeeds because of its *time-boxed cadence*, *role clarity*, and *event-driven inspection* which together enable rapid feedback and continuous delivery. --- **Part (b) — SCRUM vs. Kanban (7 Marks)** **Tabular Comparison (4 Marks):** | Dimension | SCRUM | Kanban | |---|---|---| | **Cadence** | Fixed Sprints (2–4 weeks) | Continuous flow | | **Roles** | Prescribed (PO, SM, Dev) | No prescribed roles | | **Board** | Reset every Sprint | Persistent | | **WIP Limit** | Implicit (Sprint scope is the cap) | Explicit (per column) | | **Planning** | Sprint Planning + Release Planning | On-demand Replenishment | | **Metrics** | Velocity, Burndown | Lead Time, Cycle Time, CFD | | **Change** | Discouraged mid-Sprint | Allowed anytime | | **Philosophy** | Empirical — inspect & adapt | Lean — flow efficiency | | **Best Suited** | Greenfield product development | Maintenance, support, ops | **Real-World Scenario (3 Marks):** **Recommendation: Use Kanban** for a **24/7 IT incident support team** at a banking enterprise. **Justification:** 1. **Variable, Unpredictable Demand:** Incidents arrive continuously; there are no "Sprint boundaries." 2. **Prioritization is Dynamic:** A P1 outage must preempt low-priority work — impossible in a frozen Sprint Backlog. 3. **Service-Level Agreements (SLAs):** Lead Time and Cycle Time (Kanban metrics) directly map to SLA compliance, whereas Velocity is less meaningful. 4. **WIP Limits prevent burnout:** When a P1 hits, the team can re-balance the board in seconds, not wait for Sprint Planning. *Example:* A bank's Network Operations Center (NOC) using Kanban: each card is an incident. Columns: *Reported → Triaging → Investigation → Fix → Closed* with WIP limits of 5, 3, 2, 1 respectively. Lead Time KPIs are tracked daily on a Cumulative Flow Diagram. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]** **(a)** Describe **Lean Software Development**. Explain the **seven principles of Lean** with relevant software-engineering examples for each. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A software team has a Kanban system with a WIP limit of $L = 6$ items and an average throughput of $\lambda = 1.5$ items per week. **[7 Marks]** - **(i)** Calculate the **average cycle time** $W$ using Little's Law. - **(ii)** If management wants to **halve the cycle time**, what should the new WIP limit be? - **(iii)** Define **Lead Time** and **Throughput** in this context. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Lean Software Development (7 Marks)** **Definition (1 Mark):** Lean Software Development is a methodology that translates the principles of *Lean Manufacturing* (originating from the Toyota Production System) into the software domain. Its central goal is to **deliver maximum customer value with minimum waste** (*Muda*). The foundational text is *Mary and Tom Poppendieck's "Lean Software Development: An Agile Toolkit" (2003)*. **The Seven Principles (6 Marks, ~0.85 each):** 1. **Eliminate Waste:** Remove anything that does not add customer value. *Example:* Deleting unused features, reducing handoffs between teams, removing redundant documentation. 2. **Amplify Learning:** Use short iterations and feedback loops. *Example:* Using TDD, pair programming, and frequent demos to learn from users. 3. **Decide as Late as Possible:** Delay irreversible decisions until the last responsible moment to keep options open. *Example:* Choosing the database technology only after the data model stabilizes in Sprint 3, not Day 1. 4. **Deliver as Fast as Possible:** Short cycle time = fast feedback = lower risk. *Example:* Continuous Deployment pipeline releasing features multiple times per day. 5. **Empower the Team:** Self-organizing, motivated teams outperform command-and-control. *Example:* Letting the dev team decide their own internal task distribution rather than a manager assigning work. 6. **Build Integrity In:** Ensure both *perceived* (user perspective) and *conceptual* (code architecture) integrity. *Example:* Refactoring + automated tests to keep the code clean and behavior correct. 7. **See the Whole:** Optimize the *system*, not local parts. *Example:* A bug found in testing is not "the tester's problem" — the whole team fixes it. --- **Part (b) — Little's Law Application (7 Marks)** **Given:** - WIP limit: $L = 6$ items - Throughput: $\lambda = 1.5$ items / week **(i) Average Cycle Time (2 Marks):** Using Little's Law $L = \lambda \cdot W$: $$ \begin{aligned} W &= \frac{L}{\lambda} \\ &= \frac{6 \text{ items}}{1.5 \text{ items/week}} \\ &= 4 \text{ weeks} \end{aligned} $$ **Cycle time $W = 4$ weeks.** **(ii) New WIP Limit for Halved Cycle Time (3 Marks):** Goal: $W_{new} = W/2 = 2$ weeks. With $\lambda$ unchanged at $1.5$ items/week: $$ \begin{aligned} L_{new} &= \lambda \cdot W_{new} \\ &= 1.5 \times 2 \\ &= 3 \text{ items} \end{aligned} $$ **The WIP limit must be reduced from 6 to 3 items.** **(iii) Definitions (2 Marks):** - **Lead Time (LT):** The total elapsed time from the moment a customer *requests* a feature (or an incident is *reported*) until it is *delivered* (or resolved). It includes the *wait time* in the backlog plus the *cycle time*. - **Throughput (λ):** The average *number of items* (features, tickets, user stories) that the team *completes and ships per unit of time* (e.g., per week or per month). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - For Part (b)(ii), **forgetting to keep $\lambda$ constant** is a 1-mark deduction. State the assumption clearly. > - For Part (a), do **NOT** list the 7 principles without software examples — the examiner deducts 2 marks for "generic" answers. > - In the comparison question, **tabular presentation** is highly valued by the KTU board. Always use tables for ≥3-point comparisons. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) ### ✅ SCRUM Essentials - [ ] **3 Roles:** Product Owner, Scrum Master, Developers - [ ] **5 Events:** Sprint, Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective - [ ] **3 Artifacts:** Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog, Increment - [ ] **3 Pillars:** Transparency, Inspection, Adaptation - [ ] **Sprint Duration:** 2–4 weeks; **Daily Scrum:** ≤15 min; **Team Size:** 3–9 - [ ] **Sprint Goal:** Single sentence guiding the Sprint - [ ] **Definition of Done (DoD):** Quality gate for every Increment ### ✅ Kanban Essentials - [ ] **No fixed iterations** — continuous flow - [ ] **Visualize work** on a Kanban board (To-Do → In-Progress → Done) - [ ] **WIP Limits** per column (hard cap) - [ ] **Little's Law:** $L = \lambda \cdot W$ - [ ] **Key metrics:** Lead Time, Cycle Time, Throughput, WIP - [ ] **Best for:** Support, maintenance, DevOps pipelines - [ ] **Tool example:** Trello, Jira Kanban, Azure DevOps Boards ### ✅ Lean Essentials - [ ] **7 Principles:** Eliminate Waste, Amplify Learning, Decide Late, Deliver Fast, Empower Team, Build Integrity, See the Whole - [ ] **7 Wastes (Muda):** Partially done work, extra features, relearning, handoffs, delays, task switching, defects - [ ] **Origin:** Toyota Production System (TPS) - [ ] **Lean is the parent philosophy** of SCRUM, Kanban, XP, and SAFe - [ ] **Kaizen:** Continuous incremental improvement ### ✅ High-Yield Distinctions (must know for ESE) - [ ] Lead Time ≠ Cycle Time (Lead = wait + cycle) - [ ] SCRUM uses Velocity; Kanban uses Lead/Cycle Time - [ ] SCRUM: prescribed roles, fixed cadence. Kanban: no prescribed roles, continuous flow. - [ ] SCRUM discourages mid-Sprint changes; Kanban allows them. - [ ] Ideal Burndown: $Y_i = T_0 (1 - i/N)$, a *linear* decreasing line. ### ✅ Common Numerical Questions - [ ] Burndown calculation - [ ] Velocity averaging - [ ] Little's Law (WIP, throughput, cycle time) - [ ] Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Module 4 questions typically come as a **full 14-mark Part B question** OR split into one 7-mark + one 7-mark sub-part. Always carry a **labeled diagram** in your answer sheet for the SCRUM framework — examiners award 1–2 marks extra for clear schematics.
